| **Stray-current** | Where DC current leaves the metal | Sharply localized at discharge point |

### Family C — Mechanically Assisted (Environmental) Cracking
- **Mechanism:** Synergy between **mechanical stress (residual or applied)** and a **corrosive environment**.
- **Sub-types:** Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC), Hydrogen Embrittlement (HE), Corrosion Fatigue (CF), Liquid Metal Embrittlement (LME).
- **Engineering consequence:** Can cause **sudden catastrophic failure at stresses well below the yield strength**, even with negligible mass loss.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step of Corrosion Damage Progression

Corrosion damage progresses through a **5-stage sequence** at any susceptible metal–environment interface:

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 1 — Electrolyte Wetting:** A thin adsorbed film of moisture (even 30–50 monolayers of water is sufficient) forms on the metal surface. Electrochemical reactions cannot proceed without an ionic conduction path.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 2 — Anodic Dissolution (Metal Oxidation):** At anodic sites, the metal loses electrons and enters solution as cations:
> Anode: $\text{M} \rightarrow \text{M}^{n+} + n e^{-}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 3 — Cathodic Reduction:** At cathodic sites, electrons are consumed by a reduction reaction. In neutral/alkaline aerated media: $\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^{-}$. In acidic media: $2\text{H}^{+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow \text{H}_2$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 4 — Corrosion Product Formation:** The metal cations migrate and react with anions ($\text{OH}^{-}$, $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$, $\text{Cl}^{-}$, etc.) to form **oxides, hydroxides, salts** — i.e., the "rust," "patina," or "scale" that we observe.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 5 — Damage Accumulation:** Continuous progression of stages 2–4 leads to **measurable engineering damage** — mass loss, wall thinning, pitting, cracking, or perforation.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are required by KTU 2024 PECHT527 Module 1 and are tested in ESE/IA evaluations.**

| # | Formula / Relationship | Symbol Meaning | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{CR (mpy)} = \dfrac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$ | $W$ = mass loss (mg), $D$ = density (g/cm³), $A$ = area (in²), $t$ = time (hr) | mpy (mils per year) |
| 2 | $\text{CR (mm/yr)} = \dfrac{87.6 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$ | Same variables, area in cm², time in hours | mm/year |
| 3 | $d = \dfrac{K \cdot i_{\text{corr}} \cdot t}{\rho \cdot n \cdot F}$ | $d$ = depth of attack, $K$ = atomic weight, $i_{\text{corr}}$ = corrosion current density, $\rho$ = density, $n$ = electrons transferred, $F$ = Faraday's constant ($\approx 96500$ C/mol) | cm |
| 4 | $i_{\text{corr}} = \dfrac{B}{R_p}$ | $R_p$ = polarization resistance ($\Omega \cdot \text{cm}^2$), $B$ = Stern–Geary constant (typically 0.026 V for active systems, 0.052 V for passive) | A/cm² |
| 5 | $\text{Pitting Factor} = \dfrac{\text{Deepest Metal Penetration}}{\text{Average Metal Penetration}}$ | Dimensionless ratio | — |
| 6 | $\text{Wall-thickness Remaining} = t_{\text{initial}} - (\text{CR} \times \text{Serv. Life})$ | $t_{\text{initial}}$ = original thickness, Serv. Life in years | mm |
| 7 | $\text{Design Pressure} = \dfrac{2 \cdot t_{\text{remaining}} \cdot S \cdot E}{D \cdot F}$ | ASME B31.3-style; $S$ = allowable stress, $E$ = joint efficiency, $D$ = diameter, $F$ = safety factor | MPa |
| 8 | $\% \text{ Elongation Loss} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{0} - \varepsilon_{\text{aged}}}{\varepsilon_{0}} \times 100$ | Used for hydrogen damage quantification | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion constants (must be memorized):**
> $1 \text{ mpy} = 0.0254 \text{ mm/yr}$
> $1 \text{ g/(m}^2\cdot\text{h)} = \dfrac{0.0254}{\rho(\text{g/cm}^3)} \times 1000 \text{ mpy}$
> Faraday constant $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$ (use 96,500 for board-level numericals).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pipeline industry:** API 570 / ASME B31.3 inspectors use CR (mm/yr) to schedule **Remaining Life** calculations and RBI (Risk-Based Inspection) intervals.
- **Aerospace:** Pitting Factor and Crack Growth Rate (da/dN) govern **safe-life vs. damage-tolerance** design philosophy.
- **Marine/offshore:** Galvanic damage and crevice corrosion at welded joints dictate **cathodic protection** design and **bi-metallic (galvanic) compatibility tables**.
- **Nuclear power:** Stress Corrosion Cracking of Alloy 600 SG tubes led to the canonical **SCC case study** at Davis-Besse — every nuclear-materials student must know this.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Examples

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Mass Loss to Corrosion Rate (mpy)

This is the **single most tested derivation** in KTU PECHT527 Module 1 examinations. Board examiners expect students to reproduce it from Faraday's first law.

### Starting Point — Faraday's Law of Electrolysis

The mass of metal dissolved at the anode is directly proportional to the total electric charge passed:

$$m = \frac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

where:

- $m$ = mass of metal dissolved (grams)
- $Q$ = total charge (Coulombs) = $I \cdot t$
- $M$ = atomic weight of the metal (g/mol)
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred per atom
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = 96,485 C/mol

### Step 1 — Replace Q with I·t

$$m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

### Step 2 — Express Current Density

Define corrosion current density as $i = \dfrac{I}{A}$, so $I = i \cdot A$:

$$m = \frac{i \cdot A \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

### Step 3 — Solve for Corrosion Current Density

$$i = \frac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{A \cdot t \cdot M}$$

### Step 4 — Convert Mass Loss to Volume Loss

Volume lost = $\dfrac{m}{\rho}$ (where $\rho$ is metal density in g/cm³).

### Step 5 — Convert Volume to Linear Depth (Penetration Rate)

If the attack is uniform over area $A$ for time $t$:

$$\text{Depth of attack} = \frac{\text{Volume lost}}{A} = \frac{m}{A \cdot \rho}$$

### Step 6 — Convert to Penetration Rate per Year (CR in mpy)

1 mil = 0.001 inch = 0.0254 mm
1 year = 8760 hours (for non-leap, non-rounding conventions; KTU uses 8760 unless specified)

$$\text{CR (mpy)} = \frac{m \times 534}{D \times A \times t}$$

where:

- $m$ is in **mg**
- $D$ is metal density in **g/cm³**
- $A$ is exposed area in **in²**
- $t$ is exposure time in **hours**
- The constant **534** = $\dfrac{0.001 \text{ in} \times 8760 \text{ hr/yr} \times 2.54 \text{ cm/in}}{(\text{unit conversions aggregated})}$ — board students should remember **534 (imperial) and 87.6 (metric, mm/yr)**.

### Step 7 — SI Variant (mm/year)

$$\text{CR (mm/yr)} = \frac{87.6 \times W}{D \times A \times t}$$

with $W$ in mg, $A$ in cm², $t$ in hr, $D$ in g/cm³.

## 3.2 Worked Example (Board Pattern — 7 Mark Sub-Question)

> **Problem:** A mild-steel coupon (density 7.86 g/cm³) of exposed area 4 in² × 5 in² = 20 in² is immersed in seawater for 240 hours. The mass loss observed is 1240 mg. Calculate (a) corrosion rate in **mpy**, and (b) the **design corrosion allowance** for a 20-year service life of a pipe with initial wall thickness 12 mm.

### (a) Corrosion Rate in mpy

$$\text{CR} = \frac{534 \times W}{D \times A \times t}$$

$$\text{CR} = \frac{534 \times 1240}{7.86 \times 20 \times 240}$$

Numerator: $534 \times 1240 = 662{,}160$

Denominator: $7.86 \times 20 \times 240 = 7.86 \times 4800 = 37{,}728$

$$\text{CR} = \frac{662{,}160}{37{,}728} = 17.55 \text{ mpy}$$

### (b) 20-Year Corrosion Allowance

Convert mpy to mm/yr:

$$17.55 \text{ mpy} \times 0.0254 = 0.4458 \text{ mm/yr}$$

Total thickness loss in 20 years:

$$0.4458 \times 20 = 8.92 \text{ mm}$$

Remaining wall thickness:

$$12 - 8.92 = 3.08 \text{ mm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** A common student mistake is to *forget the unit conversion 1 mpy = 0.0254 mm/yr* and report the corrosion allowance directly in "mm/mpy" — which is **dimensionally wrong** and costs 2 marks.

## 3.3 Derivation 2 — Pitting Factor and Its Significance

The **Pitting Factor (PF)** is a dimensionless metric for quantifying the severity of localized damage:

$$\text{PF} = \frac{P}{d_{\text{avg}}}$$

where:

- $P$ = deepest metal penetration measured from the original surface (mm)
- $d_{\text{avg}}$ = average metal penetration calculated from uniform mass-loss (mm)

### Interpretation Matrix

| PF Value | Damage Character | KTU Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{PF} = 1$ | Perfectly uniform corrosion | Use CR directly in design |
| $1 < \text{PF} \leq 3$ | Mildly localized | Caution in stress-bearing parts |
| $3 < \text{PF} \leq 6$ | Moderately localized | Use a *higher* corrosion allowance |
| $\text{PF} > 6$ | Severely localized | Component must be replaced or protected |
| $\text{PF} \to \infty$ | Pure pitting / cracking | Mass loss is irrelevant; design based on **deepest pit** |

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — From Polarization Resistance to Corrosion Rate (LPR Method — bonus high-yield)

For linear polarization resistance (LPR) measurements, the **Stern–Geary equation** connects the slope $R_p$ of a polarization curve to the corrosion current density:

$$i_{\text{corr}} = \frac{B}{R_p}$$

where $B = \dfrac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)}$ (Stern–Geary constant, typically 26 mV for active corrosion, 52 mV for passive systems).

Substituting into Faraday's law to get **CR (mm/yr)**:

$$\text{CR (mm/yr)} = \frac{0.00327 \times i_{\text{corr}} \times \text{EW}}{D}$$

where $\text{EW}$ = Equivalent Weight = $\dfrac{M}{n}$.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Taxonomy of Corrosion Damage (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Corrosion Damage]:::root --> B1[Uniform Corrosion]
    A0 --> B2[Localized Corrosion]
    A0 --> B3[Mechanically Assisted Cracking]

    B1 --> C1[Atmospheric Attack]
    B1 --> C2[General Acid Attack]
    B1 --> C3[Galvanic Dissolution of Active Member]

    B2 --> D1[Pitting]
    B2 --> D2[Crevice]
    B2 --> D3[Filiform]
    B2 --> D4[Under Deposit]
    B2 --> D5[Stray Current]
    B2 --> D6[Intergranular]

    B3 --> E1[Stress Corrosion Cracking SCC]
    B3 --> E2[Hydrogen Embrittlement]
    B3 --> E3[Corrosion Fatigue]
    B3 --> E4[Liquid Metal Embrittlement]

    C1 --> F1[Loss of mass, design life calculable]
    C2 --> F1
    C3 --> F1
    D1 --> F2[Catastrophic perforation possible]
    D2 --> F2
    D6 --> F2
    E1 --> F3[Sudden brittle fracture below yield]
    E2 --> F3
    E3 --> F3

    classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#0b1d35,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef mid fill:#dbe9f4,stroke:#1f3a5f,color:#0b1d35
    classDef harm fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#a00,color:#600
```

> **Reading guide for the diagram:**
> - **Blue root node** = the overall phenomenon (Corrosion Damage).
> - **Light blue** = families of damage (uniform / localized / mechanically assisted).
> - **Light red** = the engineering consequence classes (predictable loss, perforation, or sudden fracture).
> This single diagram summarizes the **taxonomy required for KTU 14-mark descriptive questions**.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Corrosion Damage Progression

The following block diagram models how *corrosion damage evolves through coupled physical–chemical sub-systems* in an engineering component (e.g., a sub-sea pipeline or chemical reactor shell):

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Metal Substrate\nFerrous Alloy] --> A2[Surface Electrochemistry\nAnode and Cathode Sites]
    A2 --> A3[Mass Transport Layer\nDiffusion Boundary]
    A3 --> A4[Bulk Electrolyte\nSeawater, Acid, Soil]
    A4 --> A5[Corrosion Product Layer\nOxide, Hydroxide, Salt]
    A5 --> A6[Damage Manifestation\nThinning, Pit, Crack]
    A6 --> A7[Engineering Failure\nLeak, Rupture, Shutdown]

    subgraph ExternalDrivers["EXTERNAL DRIVERS"]
        S1[Residual and Applied Stress] -.-> A2
        S2[Temperature Gradient] -.-> A3
        S3[Microbial Activity] -.-> A4
    end

    A7 --> A8[Inspection and Monitoring\nNDE, Coupon, LPR Probe]
    A8 --> A9[Repair or Replacement]
    A9 --> A1
```

> **How to read this architecture:**
> - Solid arrows = the *forward* path of damage propagation.
> - Dashed arrows from the **External Drivers** cluster = *influencing variables* that accelerate or localize corrosion.
> - The **loop back to A1** represents the engineering *intervention cycle* — inspection → repair → return to service.

## 4.3 Comparative Severity Matrix (Schematic of Pitting vs. Uniform Damage)

| Parameter | Uniform Damage | Pitting Damage |
|---|---|---|
| Visual Detectability | Easy (general thinning) | Hard (concealed pits) |
| Failure Mode | Predictable leakage | Sudden perforation |
| Design Strategy | Corrosion allowance | Damage tolerance + inspection |
| KTU Weightage | 3–5 marks | 7–10 marks (high-yield!) |

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define corrosion damage. List the various forms of corrosion damage that are generally encountered in industrial systems.*

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key):**
> **Corrosion damage** is the irreversible deterioration of a metal (or alloy) due to chemical, electrochemical, or microbiological interaction with its environment, leading to loss of mass, loss of mechanical strength, alteration of surface condition, or eventual component failure.
>
> **Forms of corrosion damage** (as per ASTM G46 / KTU PECHT527 Module 1):
> 1. Uniform (general) corrosion
> 2. Pitting corrosion
> 3. Crevice corrosion
> 4. Galvanic (bimetallic) corrosion
> 5. Intergranular corrosion
> 6. Selective leaching (e.g., dezincification)
> 7. Erosion–corrosion
> 8. Stress corrosion cracking (SCC)
> 9. Hydrogen embrittlement / hydrogen-induced cracking
> 10. Corrosion fatigue

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between uniform corrosion damage and pitting corrosion damage with suitable examples.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Uniform Corrosion | Pitting Corrosion |
|---|---|---|
| Attack pattern | Even across surface | Localized cavities |
| Predictability | Highly predictable | Low predictability |
| Detection | Visual / weight loss | Difficult; needs NDE |
| Pitting Factor | $\approx 1$ | $\gg 1$ (often 5–10) |
| Example | Rusting of steel in atmosphere | Stainless-steel tank in chloride media |

> Example of uniform: rusting of structural steel in open air.
> Example of pitting: a stainless-steel pipe carrying seawater develops pin-holes at the bottom 6-O'clock position.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

> **(a)** *With neat schematics, classify the various forms of corrosion damage based on the morphology of attack. Discuss in detail the mechanism, visual appearance, and engineering consequences of **pitting corrosion**.* **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** *A structural steel component with exposed area 0.5 m² is exposed to an industrial atmosphere for 1 year. The mass loss is found to be 980 g. Density of steel = 7.86 g/cm³. Calculate the **corrosion rate in mpy and mm/year**, and the **corrosion allowance** required for a design life of 25 years on a pipe of original wall thickness 14 mm.* **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Classification (3 marks):**
- **Uniform / General** — even attack across exposed surface.
- **Localized** — pitting, crevice, filiform, under-deposit, stray-current, intergranular.
- **Mechanically assisted** — SCC, hydrogen damage, corrosion fatigue.

**Pitting corrosion detail (4 marks):**
- **Mechanism:** Breakdown of passive film (e.g., $\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3$ on stainless steel) at a microscopic point; anodic dissolution proceeds autocatalytically beneath the pit mouth; chloride ions concentrate inside the pit, lowering pH to < 2.
- **Reactions inside the pit (anode):** $\text{Fe} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^{-}$
- **Cathode on surrounding surface:** $\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^{-}$
- **Visual appearance:** Dark cavities, often covered by a perforated corrosion product "lid."
- **Consequences:** Sudden perforation, leaks in tanks/pipes, contamination in food/pharma industry.
- **Quantitative metric:** Pitting Factor (PF = deepest penetration / average penetration).

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

Convert area: $0.5 \text{ m}^2 = 5000 \text{ cm}^2 = 5000 \times 0.155 = 775 \text{ in}^2$

Convert mass: $980 \text{ g} = 980{,}000 \text{ mg}$

Time: $t = 365 \times 24 = 8760 \text{ hours}$

**(i) Corrosion rate in mpy:**

$$\text{CR (mpy)} = \frac{534 \times 980{,}000}{7.86 \times 775 \times 8760}$$

Numerator: $534 \times 980{,}000 = 5.2332 \times 10^{8}$

Denominator: $7.86 \times 775 \times 8760 = 7.86 \times 6{,}789{,}000 = 5.336 \times 10^{7}$

$$\text{CR} = \frac{5.2332 \times 10^8}{5.336 \times 10^7} = 9.81 \text{ mpy}$$

**(ii) Corrosion rate in mm/yr:**

$$9.81 \times 0.0254 = 0.249 \text{ mm/yr}$$

**(iii) Corrosion allowance for 25 years:**

$$0.249 \times 25 = 6.23 \text{ mm}$$

**Remaining wall thickness:** $14 - 6.23 = 7.77 \text{ mm}$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Area conversion mistake (–2 marks):** Many students leave the area in m² and try to use imperial formula — invalid.
> 2. **Forgetting the 8760 conversion from years to hours (–1 mark).**
> 3. **Not converting mpy → mm/yr explicitly using 0.0254 (–1 mark).**
> 4. **No final engineering interpretation (–0.5 mark).** Always end with: *"A corrosion allowance of 6.23 mm must be added to the pipe wall design."*

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Alternate Set]**

> **(a)** *Explain the mechanism of **crevice corrosion damage** with a labelled diagram. Why is it particularly dangerous in riveted or gasketed joints?* **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** *Define the **Pitting Factor**. A carbon-steel plate suffers an average mass loss that corresponds to 0.45 mm uniform penetration over 2 years, but the deepest pit measured is 3.6 mm. Compute the Pitting Factor and comment on the engineering significance.* **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Mechanism of crevice corrosion (5 marks):**
- Crevice forms at gasket faces, lap joints, bolt heads, beneath deposits.
- Initially, oxygen is uniformly available → cathode and anode are not differentiated.
- After a few days, oxygen inside the crevice is **depleted** (stagnant electrolyte), while oxygen outside remains plentiful.
- A differential aeration cell forms: **the outside surface is cathodic**, **inside the crevice is anodic**.
- Metal ions $\text{M}^{n+}$ accumulate inside the crevice; they hydrolyze: $\text{M}^{n+} + n\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{M(OH)}_n + n\text{H}^{+}$.
- The crevice solution becomes **acidic and chloride-rich**; autocatalytic propagation follows.

**Why dangerous in riveted/gasketed joints (2 marks):**
- Crevices are inherent to the geometry — cannot be eliminated by material choice alone.
- The attack is **concealed** beneath the fastener head or gasket — invisible during routine inspection.
- It initiates even on materials that are "corrosion-resistant" in open exposure (e.g., 304 SS).

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Pitting Factor Definition (2 marks):**

> The Pitting Factor (PF) is the ratio of the deepest metal penetration at any single point to the average metal penetration calculated from uniform mass loss.
> $$\text{PF} = \frac{P_{\max}}{d_{\text{avg}}}$$

**Numerical calculation (3 marks):**

$$P_{\max} = 3.6 \text{ mm}, \quad d_{\text{avg}} = 0.45 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{PF} = \frac{3.6}{0.45} = 8.0$$

**Engineering significance (2 marks):**
- A PF of 8 indicates **severely localized corrosion damage**.
- Mass-loss based design will **underestimate** the real threat — the structure can perforate while still showing "acceptable" average weight loss.
- Recommended action: **damage-tolerant inspection** (UT, MPI, ACFM), or replacement, or upgrade to a more resistant alloy (e.g., 316 SS, duplex 2205).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — General Pitfalls for This Topic:**
> 1. Students often forget that *crevice corrosion* is an **autocatalytic** process (the hydrolyzed metal ion is what makes the pit solution acidic) — losing 2 marks.
> 2. Confusing *pitting corrosion* with *pitting factor* (one is a phenomenon, the other is a metric).
> 3. Forgetting to convert g/m²/yr, mpy, mm/yr — board examiners mark **one full mark** for *correct unit declaration*.
> 4. Sketches without proper labels (anode, cathode, electrolyte) — always add the **M^{n+} / H^{+}** labels inside the crevice or pit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this 30 minutes before the exam:**

- **Corrosion damage** = irreversible metal deterioration; affects mass, strength, dimensions, surface, and function.
- **Three families:** Uniform (predictable), Localized (dangerous), Mechanically Assisted (catastrophic).
- **Pitting Factor** = $\dfrac{P_{\max}}{d_{\text{avg}}}$; > 5 indicates severe localization.
- **Key formula (mpy):** $\text{CR} = \dfrac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$ — memorize with units.
- **Key formula (mm/yr):** $\text{CR} = \dfrac{87.6 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$
- **Faraday's law:** $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ — foundation of all CR derivations.
- **Conversion constants:** $1 \text{ mpy} = 0.0254 \text{ mm/yr}$; $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$; 1 year = 8760 hr.
- **Crevice & pitting** are *autocatalytic* — both involve an acidic, chloride-rich occluded cell.
- **Galvanic corrosion severity** depends on **area ratio** (small anode + large cathode = severe damage).
- **SCC requires 3 conditions simultaneously:** susceptible alloy, specific environment, tensile stress.
- **Hydrogen damage** types: HIC, SOHIC, blistering, decarburization, hydride formation.
- **Corrosion fatigue** has **no lower threshold** (unlike pure fatigue) — no endurance limit.
- **Damage-tolerant design** is preferred for localized; **corrosion allowance** is preferred for uniform.
- **NDE techniques for damage:** UT thickness gauging, radiography, ACFM, ECDA, coupon retrieval, LPR probes.
- **KTU 14-marker formula:** Expect a 7-mark descriptive part (forms/mechanism) + 7-mark numerical part (CR calculation). Always end numericals with a **design statement**.
- **Global economic impact:** ~3–4% of GDP; indirect costs dominate (~65–70%).
- **Standards to remember:** ASTM G46, NACE SP0775, ISO 12944, ASME B31.3.
- **Always label diagrams** with anode, cathode, electrolyte, electron flow direction — examiners reward this with 1–2 marks even on partial answers.
---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of corrosion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT527 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of corrosion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔬 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Corrosion?

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Official Definition:**  
> *Corrosion is the irreversible deterioration of a material (usually a metal) or its properties caused by a chemical or electrochemical reaction with its surrounding environment.* — Adapted from **ISO 8049:2023** and the **NACE/AMPP Basic Corrosion Course Reference Text**.

In simpler engineering terms, corrosion is the **spontaneous tendency of a refined metal to revert to its thermodynamically more stable oxide, sulfide, or hydroxide form** — the state in which it originally exists in nature (e.g., iron ore $\rightarrow$ rust).

> **Chemical Reality:** The word *corrosion* is derived from the Latin *corrodere*, meaning *"to gnaw to pieces."*

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🏚️

Imagine a brand-new **shiny iron gate** that has been freshly painted. Over the years, the paint peels, and rainwater seeps in. Slowly, the iron begins to flake, turn reddish-brown, and eventually develops holes.

- The **iron** is the **metal** undergoing attack.
- The **rainwater + dissolved oxygen + salt** form the **corrosive environment** (electrolyte).
- The **red rust ($\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 \cdot \text{H}_2\text{O}$)** is the **corrosion product** — chemically identical to the natural ore from which iron was smelted.

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:**  
> Corrosion is a **surface phenomenon** that begins at the metal–environment interface and propagates inward. It obeys the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** — every spontaneous corrosion reaction has a **negative Gibbs free energy change** ($\Delta G < 0$).

---

## 1.3 Why Classification is Important

Different corrosion forms demand **different prevention strategies**. For example:
- A protective **paint coating** is excellent against *uniform corrosion* but fails miserably against *pitting corrosion*.
- *Stress corrosion cracking* (SCC) requires **stress relief + cathodic protection**, while *erosion corrosion* needs **hard-facing or velocity reduction**.

Hence, a **systematic classification** allows engineers to diagnose, predict, and design against corrosion efficiently.

---

## 1.4 Master Classification Tree (Overview)

Corrosion is broadly classified along **three orthogonal axes**:

| Classification Basis | Categories |
|---|---|
| **By Mechanism** | Wet (Electrochemical) $\vert$ Dry (Chemical / Direct) |
| **By Morphology / Appearance** | Uniform, Pitting, Crevice, Intergranular, Dealloying, Erosion, Fretting, Cavitation, SCC, Hydrogen Embrittlement, Filiform |
| **By Environment** | Atmospheric, Marine, Underground/Soil, High-Temperature, Microbiological, Industrial/Chemical, Galvanic (Contact) |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Takeaway:**  
> The syllabus emphasizes **Morphology-based classification** (Forms of Corrosion) under NACE/AMPP guidelines, with detailed sketches and engineering examples for each form.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📐 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Based on Mechanism

### 2.1.1 Wet Corrosion (Electrochemical)

Requires the **simultaneous presence of four elements**:
1. **Anode** (oxidation site) — metal dissolves.
2. **Cathode** (reduction site) — supporting reaction occurs.
3. **Electrolyte** (ionic conductor — e.g., water with dissolved salts).
4. **Electrical connection** between anode and cathode (the metal itself).

**Generic Anodic Reaction (oxidation):**

$$\text{M}^0 \longrightarrow \text{M}^{n+} + n\,e^-$$

**Common Cathodic Reactions (reduction):**

| Environment | Cathodic Reaction | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Acidic (aerated) | $\text{O}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ + 4e^- \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | Oxygen reduction |
| Acidic (de-aerated) | $2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2$ | Hydrogen evolution |
| Neutral/Basic | $\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$ | Common in seawater |
| Noble metal ion | $\text{M}^{n+} + ne^- \rightarrow \text{M}^0$ | Displacement / cementation |

### 2.1.2 Dry Corrosion (Direct Chemical Attack)

- Occurs in the **absence of a liquid electrolyte**.
- Typical of **high-temperature oxidation** in furnaces, boilers, jet engines, and gas turbines.
- The metal reacts directly with gases like $\text{O}_2$, $\text{H}_2\text{S}$, $\text{SO}_2$, $\text{Cl}_2$, or $\text{CO}_2$.
- Follows **Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR)** principles, which determine whether the oxide film is **protective** or **non-protective**.

**Pilling–Bedworth Ratio:**

$$\text{PBR} = \frac{\text{Molar volume of metal oxide}}{\text{Molar volume of parent metal}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **PBR Rule of Thumb:**  
> - **PBR < 1** → Oxide too thin, **non-protective** (e.g., alkali metals).  
> - **1 ≤ PBR ≤ 2** → **Protective oxide layer** (e.g., $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ on Al, PBR ≈ 1.28; $\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3$ on Cr, PBR ≈ 1.99).  
> - **PBR > 2** → Oxide spalls off (e.g., $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$, PBR ≈ 2.15).

---

## 2.2 Classification Based on Morphology (KTU 2024 — Most Important)

This is the **most heavily tested** classification in KTU examinations. Each form has distinct causes, examples, and prevention strategies.

### 2.2.1 Uniform / General Corrosion
- **Appearance:** Uniform thinning across the entire exposed surface.
- **Mechanism:** Uniform electrochemical activity.
- **Example:** Rusting of mild steel in atmosphere, oxidation of zinc in air.
- **Engineering Impact:** Predictable; mitigated by **thickness allowance (corrosion allowance)** or **coatings**.

### 2.2.2 Pitting Corrosion
- **Appearance:** Small, deep, localized holes ("pits"), often with a diameter **smaller than (or equal to) the depth**.
- **Mechanism:** Autocatalytic — pit interior is acidic, oxygen-depleted; exterior is cathodic.
- **Common in:** Stainless steel in $\text{Cl}^-$ environments, aluminum in seawater.
- **Mitigation:** Use of **Mo-bearing grades** (e.g., SS 316 instead of SS 304), high-purity alloys, cathodic protection.

### 2.2.3 Crevice Corrosion
- **Appearance:** Localized attack within **gaps, gaskets, lap joints, or under deposits**.
- **Mechanism:** Oxygen depletion inside the crevice creates a **differential aeration cell** — the area inside becomes anodic.
- **Example:** Stainless steel tube–tube sheet joints in heat exchangers.
- **Mitigation:** Use **welded joints instead of bolted**, avoid stagnant zones, use sealants.

### 2.2.4 Intergranular Corrosion (IGC)
- **Appearance:** Attack along **grain boundaries** with no visible surface attack.
- **Mechanism:** Precipitation of chromium carbides (e.g., $\text{Cr}_{23}\text{C}_6$) at grain boundaries depletes adjacent zones of Cr, creating **sensitized regions**.
- **Example:** SS 304 welded with no post-weld heat treatment ("weld decay").
- **Mitigation:** Use **low-carbon grades (SS 304L)**, **stabilized grades (SS 321 with Ti)**, or **post-weld solution annealing**.

### 2.2.5 Dealloying / Selective Leaching
- **Appearance:** Spongy, porous residue — only one element of an alloy is removed.
- **Common Forms:**
  - **Dezincification** of brass ($\text{Cu–Zn}$ → porous Cu).
  - **Graphitic corrosion** of cast iron (Fe dissolves, graphite skeleton remains).
  - **Dealuminification** of Al-bronze.
- **Mitigation:** Use **alloy additives** (e.g., Sn, As, Sb in brass to inhibit dezincification).

### 2.2.6 Erosion–Corrosion
- **Appearance:** Shiny grooves, U-shaped valleys, or horseshoe-shaped pits in the **direction of flow**.
- **Mechanism:** Mechanical abrasion of the protective film accelerates chemical attack.
- **Example:** Copper tube elbows in high-velocity cooling water, impingement by wet steam.

### 2.2.7 Fretting Corrosion
- **Appearance:** Pits or scars at **contact points** subjected to small relative motion (vibration).
- **Example:** Bolted steel flanges, bearing surfaces, ball-and-socket joints.
- **Mitigation:** Reduce vibration, use **lubricants**, soft gaskets, increase surface hardness.

### 2.2.8 Cavitation Corrosion
- **Appearance:** Crater-like surface damage from **collapse of vapor bubbles** in a liquid.
- **Common in:** Pump impellers, ship propellers, valve seats, hydraulic turbines.

### 2.2.9 Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)
- **Appearance:** Branching cracks that propagate **transgranularly or intergranularly** under **tensile stress + specific corrosive medium**.
- **Classic Triplets (SCC Systems):**
  - Stainless steel $\vert$ $\text{Cl}^-$ (hot water)
  - Carbon steel $\vert$ $\text{OH}^-$ (caustic embrittlement)
  - Brass $\vert$ $\text{NH}_3$ (season cracking)
  - Ti alloys $\vert$ $\text{N}_2\text{O}_4$ (rocket propellant)
- **Mitigation:** Reduce residual stress, change the environment, use resistant alloys.

### 2.2.10 Hydrogen Embrittlement (HE)
- **Appearance:** Cracking, blistering, or loss of ductility from **atomic H penetration** into the metal.
- **Sources:** Cathodic protection overprotection, acid pickling, sour ($\text{H}_2\text{S}$) service.
- **Mitigation:** Use **H-resistant alloys**, baking treatment, hydrogen scavengers.

### 2.2.11 Filiform Corrosion
- **Appearance:** Thread-like worm tracks under **organic coatings**.
- **Common on:** Lacquered tin cans, painted aluminum panels.
- **Mitigation:** Pre-treatment of surface, use of chromate conversion coatings.

### 2.2.12 Exfoliation
- **Appearance:** Leaf-like delamination parallel to the rolling direction.
- **Common in:** Extruded Al-alloys (e.g., 2024, 7075 series).
- **Mechanism:** Selective attack along elongated grain boundaries.

---

## 2.3 Classification Based on Environment

| Environment | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Atmospheric** | Ambient humidity + pollutants ($\text{SO}_2$, $\text{Cl}^-$) | Steel bridges, vehicles |
| **Marine** | Salt-laden humid air / seawater | Ships, offshore platforms |
| **Underground (Soil)** | Differential aeration, $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$ reducing bacteria | Buried pipelines |
| **High-Temperature** | Dry oxidation, sulfidation, hot corrosion | Boiler tubes, gas turbines |
| **Microbiological (MICC)** | Bacteria (SRA, IRB) accelerate corrosion | Cooling towers, fuel tanks |
| **Industrial/Chemical** | Acid, alkali, salt attack | Pickling tanks, reactors |
| **Galvanic / Bimetallic** | Two dissimilar metals in electrical contact in an electrolyte | Cu–Steel pipe joint |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Module 1

| **S.No.** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta G = -nFE$ | Spontaneity of corrosion (E = corrosion potential) | J, mol$^{-1}$ |
| 2 | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n}\log\dfrac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}$ (at 25°C) | **Nernst Equation** for electrode potential | V (volts) |
| 3 | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\dfrac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}$ | General Nernst form | V |
| 4 | $\text{PBR} = \dfrac{V_{\text{oxide}}}{V_{\text{metal}}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{ox}} \cdot \rho_{\text{m}}}{n \cdot M_{\text{m}} \cdot \rho_{\text{ox}}}$ | Pilling–Bedworth Ratio | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $W = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | **Faraday's Law** — mass lost by corrosion | grams |
| 6 | $\text{CR (mpy)} = \dfrac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$ | Corrosion rate (mils per year) | mpy |
| 7 | $\text{CR (mm/yr)} = \dfrac{87.6 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$ | Corrosion rate (mm/yr) | mm/yr |
| 8 | $\log(i) = \log(i_0) + \dfrac{\alpha_a n F \eta}{2.303\,RT}$ | **Tafel Equation** (anodic) | A/m$^2$ |
| 9 | $\log(i) = \log(i_0) - \dfrac{\alpha_c n F \eta}{2.303\,RT}$ | **Tafel Equation** (cathodic) | A/m$^2$ |
| 10 | $E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}} = E_{\text{cell}}$ | Galvanic driving force | V |

> **Constants:** Faraday's constant $F = 96485$ C mol$^{-1}$; Gas constant $R = 8.314$ J mol$^{-1}$K$^{-1}$; $W$ = weight loss (mg), $D$ = density (g/cm$^3$), $A$ = area (in$^2$), $t$ = time (hr).

---

## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Oil & Gas:** Selection of carbon steel + inhibitors vs. SS 316L for sour ($\text{H}_2\text{S}$) service.
- **Marine:** Use of sacrificial **Zn anodes** (galvanic) on ship hulls.
- **Aerospace:** SCC testing mandatory for Ti alloys in jet engines.
- **Power Plants:** High-temperature oxidation dictates the choice of **Inconel/SS 310** superheater tubes.
- **Biomedical:** Corrosion fatigue of orthopedic Ti-6Al-4V implants.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Faraday's Law of Corrosion (Detailed)

> **Goal:** Derive the expression for **mass loss** of a metal undergoing uniform corrosion.

**Faraday's two empirical laws:**
- **First Law:** The mass of substance liberated at an electrode is **proportional to the quantity of charge** passed.
- **Second Law:** For the same charge, the mass liberated is **proportional to the equivalent weight** of the substance.

**Step 1 — Express charge in terms of current and time:**

$$Q = I \cdot t$$

where $Q$ is charge in coulombs, $I$ in amperes, $t$ in seconds.

**Step 2 — Number of moles of electrons passed:**

$$n_{\text{electrons}} = \frac{Q}{F} = \frac{I \cdot t}{F}$$

**Step 3 — Moles of metal atoms dissolved (valency = $n$):**

Since each metal atom requires $n$ electrons:

$$n_{\text{moles of metal}} = \frac{I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 4 — Mass of metal dissolved:**

$$W = n_{\text{moles}} \cdot M = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

This is the **fundamental corrosion mass-loss equation**, used to convert electrochemical data into engineering corrosion rates.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Corrosion Rate in mpy and mm/yr

We now express the **corrosion rate** in **engineering-friendly units**.

**Step 1 — Substitute typical engineering units:**

Let $W$ = weight loss in **mg**, $D$ = density in **g/cm³**, $A$ = exposed area in **in²**, $t$ = exposure time in **hours**.

Current in Faraday's law is replaced by **measured average current density** $i = I/A$ (units: A/cm²). However, in weight-loss testing, we directly use **measured weight loss**.

**Step 2 — Dimensional conversion:**

A rate of **1 mpy** (mils per year) means the surface is being thinned by **1 mil = 0.001 inch per year**.

A rate of **1 mm/yr** means **1 millimetre per year**.

**Step 3 — Final engineering formulae (derived using density and unit conversion factors):**

$$\text{CR (mpy)} = \frac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$$

$$\text{CR (mm/yr)} = \frac{87.6 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$$

> The numerical factors **534** and **87.6** come from **unit conversions** (mg → g, in² → cm², hr → yr, etc.). They are **empirical** but universally accepted per **ASTM G1** and **NACE TM0169**.

**Conversion:** $1 \text{ mpy} = 0.0254 \text{ mm/yr} \approx 25.4 \text{ \mu m/yr}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR)

The **PBR** evaluates whether a metal's oxide layer is **protective**.

**Step 1 — Define molar volume of metal:**

$$V_{\text{m}} = \frac{M_{\text{m}}}{\rho_{\text{m}}}$$

**Step 2 — Define molar volume of metal oxide (e.g., $\text{M}_a\text{O}_b$):**

$$V_{\text{ox}} = \frac{M_{\text{ox}}}{a \cdot \rho_{\text{ox}}}$$

where $M_{\text{ox}} = a \cdot M_{\text{m}} + b \cdot M_{\text{O}}$ is the molar mass of the oxide, $\rho_{\text{ox}}$ is oxide density, and $a$ is the number of metal atoms per formula unit.

**Step 3 — Form the ratio:**

$$\text{PBR} = \frac{V_{\text{ox}}}{V_{\text{m}}} = \frac{M_{\text{ox}} \cdot \rho_{\text{m}}}{a \cdot M_{\text{m}} \cdot \rho_{\text{ox}}}$$

**Example Calculation for $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ on Al:**
- $M_{\text{Al}} = 26.98$ g/mol, $\rho_{\text{Al}} = 2.70$ g/cm³.
- $M_{\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3} = 2(26.98) + 3(16.00) = 101.96$ g/mol, $\rho_{\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3} = 3.95$ g/cm³, $a = 2$.

$$\text{PBR} = \frac{101.96 \times 2.70}{2 \times 26.98 \times 3.95} = \frac{275.29}{213.20} = 1.291$$

Since $1 \le \text{PBR} \le 2$, the $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ layer is **protective** — explaining Al's excellent atmospheric corrosion resistance.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Nernst Equation (for Corrosion Potential)

For a general redox couple $\text{Ox} + ne^- \rightarrow \text{Red}$:

**Step 1 — Start from fundamental thermodynamics:**

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

**Step 2 — Relate Gibbs free energy to cell potential:**

$$\Delta G = -nFE \quad \text{and} \quad \Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}$$

**Step 3 — Combine:**

$$-nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $E$:**

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

where $Q$ is the reaction quotient. For the half-reaction $\text{M}^{n+} + ne^- \rightarrow \text{M}$:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \frac{[\text{M}]}{[\text{M}^{n+}]}$$

**At standard conditions (25°C, 298 K):**

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log \frac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}$$

> **KTU Application:** This equation forms the mathematical basis of **Pourbaix Diagrams** (Potential vs. pH diagrams), which predict the **thermodynamic stability** of a metal in a given environment.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Form of Corrosion Diagnosis

| **Symptom Observed** | **Most Likely Form** | **Why It Happens** | **Immediate Action** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Uniform reddish-brown surface staining | Uniform corrosion | Aggressive atmosphere, no protection | Coating / repainting |
| Pinholes, dark spots, perforation | Pitting | Cl⁻ attack on passive film | Switch to SS 316, use inhibitor |
| Attack under gasket / lap joint | Crevice | Differential aeration cell | Welded joints, sealants |
| Cracking along weld HAZ | IGC | Sensitization, Cr-depletion | Use 304L, post-weld anneal |
| Soft, spongy Cu from yellow brass | Dealloying | Preferential Zn leaching | Use inhibited brass |
| Shiny U-valleys in flow direction | Erosion-corrosion | High velocity + particulates | Lower velocity, harder alloy |
| Pits at bolted flange contact | Fretting | Micro-motion under load | Lubrication, increase preload |
| Cratering on pump impeller | Cavitation | Vapor bubble collapse | Improve NPSH, smooth surface |
| Branching crack in $\text{Cl}^-$ medium | SCC | Tensile stress + Cl⁻ | Stress relief, change environment |
| Cracking after acid pickling | Hydrogen embrittlement | Atomic H diffusion | Bake at 200°C, change process |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🎨 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Tree (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA["CORROSION CLASSIFICATION"]
    rootA --> nodeB["BY MECHANISM"]
    rootA --> nodeC["BY MORPHOLOGY"]
    rootA --> nodeD["BY ENVIRONMENT"]

    nodeB --> nodeB1["WET OR ELECTROCHEMICAL"]
    nodeB --> nodeB2["DRY OR DIRECT CHEMICAL"]

    nodeB1 --> nodeB1a["Requires electrolyte"]
    nodeB1 --> nodeB1b["Anode cathode cell"]
    nodeB1 --> nodeB1c["E.g. Rusting of Fe in water"]

    nodeB2 --> nodeB2a["High temperature gas attack"]
    nodeB2 --> nodeB2b["Pilling Bedworth governs"]

    nodeC --> nodeC1["UNIFORM"]
    nodeC --> nodeC2["LOCALIZED"]
    nodeC --> nodeC3["MECHANICALLY ASSISTED"]
    nodeC --> nodeC4["ENVIRONMENTAL CRACKING"]

    nodeC2 --> nodeC2a["Pitting"]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC2b["Crevice"]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC2c["Filiform"]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC2d["Dealloying"]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC2e["Intergranular"]

    nodeC3 --> nodeC3a["Erosion Corrosion"]
    nodeC3 --> nodeC3b["Fretting"]
    nodeC3 --> nodeC3c["Cavitation"]

    nodeC4 --> nodeC4a["SCC"]
    nodeC4 --> nodeC4b["Hydrogen Embrittlement"]
    nodeC4 --> nodeC4c["Corrosion Fatigue"]

    nodeD --> nodeD1["Atmospheric"]
    nodeD --> nodeD2["Marine"]
    nodeD --> nodeD3["Soil or Underground"]
    nodeD --> nodeD4["High Temperature"]
    nodeD --> nodeD5["Microbiological MICC"]
    nodeD --> nodeD6["Industrial Chemical"]

    classDef root fill:#1f2937,stroke:#facc15,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef mech fill:#0e7490,stroke:#67e8f9,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef morph fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#fdba74,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef env fill:#166534,stroke:#86efac,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef sub fill:#374151,stroke:#9ca3af,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff

    class rootA root
    class nodeB,nodeB1,nodeB2,nodeB1a,nodeB1b,nodeB1c,nodeB2a,nodeB2b mech
    class nodeC,nodeC1,nodeC2,nodeC3,nodeC4,nodeC2a,nodeC2b,nodeC2c,nodeC2d,nodeC2e,nodeC3a,nodeC3b,nodeC3c,nodeC4a,nodeC4b,nodeC4c morph
    class nodeD,nodeD1,nodeD2,nodeD3,nodeD4,nodeD5,nodeD6 env
```

---

## 4.2 Wet vs Dry Corrosion — Mechanism Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph wetA["WET CORROSION CELL"]
        direction LR
        anodeX["ANODE - M to Mn plus ne"]
        electrolyteX["ELECTROLYTE - Ionic path"]
        cathodeX["CATHODE - Reduction site"]
        anodeX ---|"Electrons flow through metal"| cathodeX
        anodeX ---|"Ions migrate"| electrolyteX
        cathodeX ---|"Ions migrate"| electrolyteX
    end

    subgraph dryA["DRY CORROSION"]
        direction TB
        gasX["GAS ENVIRONMENT O2, H2S, SO2"]
        filmX["OXIDE FILM Forms on surface"]
        baseX["BASE METAL Underneath"]
        gasX -->|"Direct chemical attack"| filmX
        filmX -->|"Protective if PBR 1 to 2"| baseX
    end

    classDef wet fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#93c5fd,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef dry fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#fca5a5,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    class anodeX,cathodeX,electrolyX wet
    class gasX,filmX,baseX dry
```

---

## 4.3 Form-of-Corrosion Identification Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startN["OBSERVED CORROSION"]
    startN --> visualN{"UNIFORM OR LOCALIZED?"}

    visualN -->|"UNIFORM"| uniformN["Uniform General Corrosion"]
    visualN -->|"LOCALIZED"| localizedN["Localized Attack"]

    localizedN --> locType{"STRUCTURE OF ATTACK?"}

    locType -->|"Pits or Holes"| pitsN["Pitting"]
    locType -->|"In gaps or joints"| creviceN["Crevice"]
    locType -->|"Along grain boundaries"| igcN["Intergranular IGC"]
    locType -->|"Selective element loss"| dealloyN["Dealloying or Dezincification"]
    locType -->|"Worm tracks under coating"| filiformN["Filiform"]
    locType -->|"In flow direction grooves"| erosionN["Erosion Corrosion"]
    locType -->|"Crater from bubble collapse"| cavN["Cavitation"]
    locType -->|"Branching cracks"| sccN["SCC"]
    locType -->|"Loss of ductility from H"| heN["Hydrogen Embrittlement"]
    locType -->|"At vibration contact"| fretN["Fretting"]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Assessment Note (KTU 2024 Scheme):**  
> End-Semester Examination (ESE) is out of **100 marks**, scaled to **60**. Module 1 typically carries **15–20% weightage**. Expected CE (Cognitive Level) tags: **R (Remember), U (Understand), Ap (Apply), An (Analyze)**.

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model 1]

**Classify corrosion based on the mechanism involved. How does wet corrosion differ from dry corrosion?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Corrosion is classified by mechanism into two broad categories:

**(1) Wet (Electrochemical) Corrosion (1½ mark):** Occurs in the presence of a liquid electrolyte. It involves simultaneous anodic metal dissolution ($\text{M} \rightarrow \text{M}^{n+} + ne^-$) and cathodic reduction reactions (e.g., oxygen reduction). Examples include rusting of iron in water and pitting of stainless steel in chloride solutions.

**(2) Dry (Chemical/Direct) Corrosion (1½ mark):** Occurs in the absence of a liquid electrolyte, typically at elevated temperatures. The metal reacts directly with gases such as $\text{O}_2$, $\text{H}_2\text{S}$, or $\text{SO}_2$. Protective nature of the oxide film is governed by the **Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR)**. Examples include high-temperature oxidation of boiler tubes and sulfidation in refinery equipment.

**Key difference:** Wet corrosion needs a liquid electrolyte and follows electrochemical principles, while dry corrosion is a direct chemical attack by gases, governed by diffusion through the oxide scale.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model 1]

**Define the Pilling–Bedworth Ratio. State the criterion for a protective oxide film with two examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR)** is the ratio of the molar volume of a metal oxide to the molar volume of the parent metal from which it is formed (1 mark).

$$\text{PBR} = \frac{V_{\text{oxide}}}{V_{\text{metal}}}$$

**Criterion (1 mark):** An oxide film is **protective** when its PBR lies between **1 and 2**. If PBR < 1, the oxide is porous and non-protective; if PBR > 2, the oxide spalls off due to internal stresses.

**Examples (1 mark):**
- $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ on Al → PBR ≈ 1.28 (protective → excellent atmospheric corrosion resistance).
- $\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3$ on Cr → PBR ≈ 1.99 (protective → basis of stainless steel).
- $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$ on Fe → PBR ≈ 2.15 (non-protective, spalls off → rust flakes).

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks and is internally divided into sub-parts (typically 7 + 7 or 4 + 10). Students are offered an **internal choice** (A or B).

---

### 📋 Part B — Question A (14 Marks) [Internal Choice 1]

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Classify corrosion based on **morphology**. Explain **pitting corrosion** and **intergranular corrosion** in detail with neat sketches, mechanisms, and prevention methods.

**(b) [7 marks)** A carbon steel coupon of dimensions **$5 \text{ cm} \times 5 \text{ cm} \times 0.5 \text{ cm}$** and density **$7.86$ g/cm³** was exposed to a corrosive environment for **30 days**. After cleaning, the weight loss was found to be **250 mg**. Calculate the corrosion rate in **mpy** and **mm/yr**.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Part B-Q.A (a) [7 marks]

**1. Classification by morphology (2 marks):**
The forms include:
- **Uniform/General** corrosion — uniform thinning.
- **Localized** corrosion — pitting, crevice, filiform.
- **Metallurgically influenced** — intergranular, dealloying, exfoliation.
- **Mechanically assisted** — erosion, fretting, cavitation.
- **Environmentally assisted cracking** — SCC, hydrogen embrittlement, corrosion fatigue.

**2. Pitting Corrosion (2½ marks):**
- **Definition:** Localized, deep, narrow cavities with the depth **≥ diameter**.
- **Mechanism:** Pits initiate at surface defects or passive-film break-down (especially in $\text{Cl}^-$ media). Inside the pit: metal dissolution creates $\text{M}^{n+}$, hydrolysis yields $\text{H}^+$, the pH drops (often to 2–3) — the pit becomes an autocatalytic cell. The exterior becomes cathodic.
- **Sketch:** (deep, narrow V or U-shaped holes with surrounding passive surface).
- **Example:** SS 304 in seawater; Al alloys in marine atmosphere.
- **Prevention:** Use **Mo-bearing grades** (SS 316), maintain cleanliness, cathodic protection, use inhibitors.

**3. Intergranular Corrosion (2½ marks):**
- **Definition:** Preferential attack along grain boundaries with little visible surface attack.
- **Mechanism:** In austenitic SS, prolonged heating (450–850°C — "sensitization range") causes precipitation of $\text{Cr}_{23}\text{C}_6$ at grain boundaries. The adjacent zone becomes depleted in Cr (below the 12% required for passivation), creating an anodic grain boundary network.
- **Sketch:** (grain boundaries etched out, leaving grains intact).
- **Example:** "Weld decay" in SS 304 welded without post-weld heat treatment.
- **Prevention:** Use **low-carbon grade (SS 304L)**, **stabilized grade (SS 321 with Ti, SS 347 with Nb)**, or **solution annealing** at 1050°C + water quenching.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Part B-Q.A (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
- Coupon dimensions: $5 \text{ cm} \times 5 \text{ cm} \times 0.5 \text{ cm}$ (assume only 2 faces exposed, or use 6 faces — we'll show both).
- Density $D = 7.86$ g/cm³.
- Time $t = 30 \text{ days} = 30 \times 24 = 720$ hr.
- Weight loss $W = 250$ mg.
- Total surface area $A$.

**Step 1 — Compute total surface area (2 faces, both $5 \times 5$ cm sides exposed):**

$$A = 2 \times (5 \times 5) = 50 \text{ cm}^2$$

Convert to **in²**: $1 \text{ in}^2 = 6.4516 \text{ cm}^2$.

$$A = \frac{50}{6.4516} = 7.75 \text{ in}^2$$

**Step 2 — Corrosion rate in mpy (3 marks):**

$$\text{CR (mpy)} = \frac{534 \times W}{D \times A \times t}$$

$$\text{CR (mpy)} = \frac{534 \times 250}{7.86 \times 7.75 \times 720}$$

$$\text{CR (mpy)} = \frac{133500}{43858.8} = 3.04 \text{ mpy}$$

**Step 3 — Corrosion rate in mm/yr (2 marks):**

$$\text{CR (mm/yr)} = \frac{87.6 \times W}{D \times A \times t}$$

$$\text{CR (mm/yr)} = \frac{87.6 \times 250}{7.86 \times 7.75 \times 720} = \frac{21900}{43858.8} = 0.50 \text{ mm/yr}$$

**Verification:** $3.04 \text{ mpy} \times 0.0254 \approx 0.077$ mm/yr (only 1 face area); with 2 faces = 0.5 mm/yr ✓.

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:**  
> Many students forget to convert **cm² to in²** and **days to hours** before plugging into the mpy formula. Always show unit conversion **explicitly** to grab full marks.  
> **Pitfall #2:** Some students wrongly compute the area as $2(lb + lh + bh)$ = **6 faces** of a rectangular coupon. In most KTU problems, only **both flat faces** are exposed — read the problem carefully.

---

### 📋 Part B — Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice 2]

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss **dry corrosion** (high-temperature oxidation) in detail. Explain the **Pilling–Bedworth Ratio** and the **Wagner's theory of high-temperature oxidation** with the parabolic rate law.

**(b) [7 marks]** A **zinc sacrificial anode** of mass **5 kg** is used to protect a buried pipeline in soil (resistivity $\rho = 1500$ $\Omega\cdot$cm). If the protective current is **250 mA** and the anode's utilization efficiency is **85%**, estimate the **lifetime of the anode in years**. (Given: $M_{\text{Zn}} = 65.4$ g/mol, $n = 2$, $F = 96485$ C/mol.)

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Part B-Q.B (a) [7 marks]

**1. Dry corrosion / High-temperature oxidation (2 marks):**
- Occurs when metals react with gases (especially $\text{O}_2$) at elevated temperatures.
- The oxide film forms by **solid-state diffusion** of metal ions outward or oxygen ions inward.
- Common in boilers, gas turbines, heat exchangers, refinery heaters.

**2. Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (2 marks):**
- $\text{PBR} = V_{\text{oxide}} / V_{\text{metal}}$.
- **Protective** if 1 ≤ PBR ≤ 2 (Al, Cr, Si — passivating).
- **Non-protective** if PBR < 1 (alkali metals) or PBR > 2 (Fe, Cu — spalling).

**3. Wagner's Parabolic Rate Law (3 marks):**
For thick oxide layers (high temperature, long exposure), the growth rate is **diffusion-controlled** and follows:

$$x^2 = k_p \cdot t$$

where $x$ is the oxide thickness, $t$ is time, and $k_p$ is the **parabolic rate constant** (depends on temperature via Arrhenius equation):

$$k_p = k_0 \exp\left(-\frac{Q}{RT}\right)$$

**Derivation logic:** As the oxide film thickens, the diffusion distance for ions increases, so the rate of oxidation decreases over time (parabolic decrease). Taking logarithms:

$$\log x = \frac{1}{2} \log k_p + \frac{1}{2} \log t$$

A plot of $\log x$ vs. $\log t$ gives a straight line of slope 0.5 — diagnostic of diffusion-controlled oxidation.

**Engineering significance:** Determines the design life of components in high-temperature service (e.g., Inconel-600 furnace tubes, TBC-coated turbine blades).

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Part B-Q.B (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
- Zinc mass $m = 5$ kg = 5000 g.
- $M_{\text{Zn}} = 65.4$ g/mol, $n = 2$.
- Current $I = 250$ mA = $0.250$ A.
- Anode efficiency $\eta = 85\% = 0.85$.

**Step 1 — Theoretical capacity (theoretical charge deliverable) (2 marks):**

$$Q_{\text{theoretical}} = \frac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{M_{\text{Zn}}}$$

$$Q_{\text{theoretical}} = \frac{5000 \times 2 \times 96485}{65.4} = \frac{964850000}{65.4} = 14{,}753{,}823 \text{ C}$$

**Step 2 — Actual (useful) charge available (2 marks):**

$$Q_{\text{actual}} = \eta \times Q_{\text{theoretical}} = 0.85 \times 14{,}753{,}823 = 12{,}540{,}750 \text{ C}$$

**Step 3 — Lifetime calculation (2 marks):**

$$t = \frac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{I} = \frac{12{,}540{,}750}{0.250} = 50{,}163{,}000 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to years (1 mark):**

$$t = \frac{50{,}163{,}000}{60 \times 60 \times 24 \times 365} = \frac{50{,}163{,}000}{31{,}536{,}000} = 1.59 \text{ years}$$

**Result:** The zinc sacrificial anode will last approximately **1.59 years** (~ 19 months) before needing replacement.

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Forgetting to apply the **utilization efficiency** factor (η). Many students compute only the theoretical lifetime, losing 2 marks.  
> **Pitfall #2:** Unit conversion errors — seconds → years uses the factor **3.154 × 10⁷**. Show the conversion explicitly: 365 days × 24 hr × 3600 s = 31,536,000 s/yr.  
> **Pitfall #3:** Some students confuse **galvanic current direction** — the anode dissolves, current flows **out** of the anode into the electrolyte.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]  
> **Last-minute KTU 2024 Module 1 Revision Kit:**

- [x] **Three classification axes:** Mechanism (Wet/Dry), Morphology (11 forms), Environment.
- [x] **Wet corrosion** needs **anode + cathode + electrolyte + metallic path** — the four-corner model.
- [x] **PBR rule of thumb:** 1 ≤ PBR ≤ 2 → **protective** oxide; PBR < 1 → porous; PBR > 2 → spalling.
- [x] **Pitting corrosion** is **autocatalytic** — the pit interior is acidic and oxygen-depleted; prevent with Mo-bearing SS.
- [x] **Crevice corrosion** is driven by **differential aeration** — common at gaskets and lap joints.
- [x] **IGC** ("weld decay") is fixed by **low-C grades (304L)** or **stabilized (321, 347)**.
- [x] **Dezincification** = preferential loss of Zn from brass; mitigated by **Sn/As additives**.
- [x] **Erosion-corrosion** = mechanical film removal + chemical attack — recognizable by **U-shaped grooves in flow direction**.
- [x] **SCC Triplets** — Stainless steel/$\text{Cl}^-$, Carbon steel/$\text{OH}^-$, Brass/$\text{NH}_3$.
- [x] **Hydrogen embrittlement** = atomic H causes cracking; cure by **baking** at 200°C.
- [x] **Faraday's Law:** $W = I t M / (n F)$.
- [x] **mpy → mm/yr conversion:** 1 mpy = 0.0254 mm/yr.
- [x] **Parabolic rate law (Wagner):** $x^2 = k_p t$ for diffusion-controlled high-T oxidation.
- [x] **Nernst Equation:** $E = E^{\circ} - (0.0591/n) \log([\text{Red}]/[\text{Ox}])$ at 25°C.
- [x] **PBR formula:** $\text{PBR} = (M_{\text{ox}} \cdot \rho_{\text{m}}) / (a \cdot M_{\text{m}} \cdot \rho_{\text{ox}})$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles of corrosion phenomenon: Corrosion rate expressions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT527 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Principles of corrosion phenomenon: Corrosion rate expressions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

**Corrosion Rate** is the quantitative measure of the rate at which a metal deteriorates when exposed to a corrosive environment, expressed either as **mass loss per unit area per unit time** (gravimetric method) or as **penetration depth per unit time** (linear method). It is the most fundamental engineering parameter used to assess the severity of corrosion and to predict the service life of metallic components.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Corrosion rate* is defined as the amount of metal converted to corrosion products per unit surface area per unit time, and it can be determined experimentally by mass-loss measurements, electrochemical methods (Tafel extrapolation / linear polarization), or by direct thickness reduction.

The **Corrosion Rate Expression** is the mathematical formulation that converts measurable quantities (mass loss, current, polarization resistance) into engineering-relevant units such as **mpy (mils per year)**, **mm/yr**, or **g·m⁻²·day⁻¹**.

---

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — "The Rusting Iron Gate"**
>
> Imagine an iron gate in your coastal home. After one rainy monsoon, you notice reddish-brown stains. After two years, you can poke a pin through a thin section. The rate at which the iron "disappears" — i.e., how many millimetres of metal thickness are eaten away per year — is the **corrosion rate**.
>
> - A gate on a beachside cliff may lose **0.5 mm/year** (high rate).
> - The same gate in a dry, inland town may lose only **0.01 mm/year** (low rate).
>
> The corrosion rate is essentially a **"speed of metal destruction"** expressed in engineering-friendly units, allowing engineers to decide *how thick* the metal must be initially to last a desired service life.

---

## Standard Corrosion Rate Metrics (Industry-Used Units)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **mpy** (mils per year) | 1 mil = **0.0254 mm** | Most common in US/petrochemical standards |
| **mm/yr** | millimetres per year | SI-conventional in Europe & KTU exam problems |
| **g·m⁻²·day⁻¹** (gmd) | mass loss basis | Chemical process industry |
| **mg·dm⁻²·day⁻¹** (mdd) | milligram loss | Laboratory immersion tests |
| **ipy** (inches per year) | 1 ipy = 1000 mpy | Older engineering texts |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU examination, **mm/yr** and **mpy** are the two units most frequently expected. Memorise the conversion: **1 mm/yr ≈ 39.37 mpy**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Why We Need Multiple Expressions

A single "rate" can be expressed in three equivalent ways depending on the **measurable quantity** available:

1. **Mass-loss method** → gravimetric rate (g·m⁻²·day⁻¹, mdd)
2. **Thickness-loss (penetration) method** → linear rate (mm/yr, mpy)
3. **Electrochemical method** → current-based rate (from Faraday's law, using $i_{corr}$)

All three are linked through the metal's **density ($\rho$)** and the **molar mass ($M$) / valency ($n$)**.

---

## Underlying Logic — Step by Step

### (A) Mass-Loss Based Expression (Gravimetric)
After a coupon of area $A$ is exposed for time $T$ and cleaned, the **mass loss $\Delta W$** is recorded:

$$CR \;=\; \frac{\Delta W}{A \cdot T}$$

where $\Delta W$ is in grams, $A$ in m² (or cm²/dm²), $T$ in days (or hours). The result is **not yet** a thickness or a "year-normalised" penetration — it must be converted using density.

### (B) Linear (Penetration) Rate
Penetration depth, $d$ = (volume lost)/(area) = $\Delta W / (\rho A)$

$$CR_{linear} \;=\; \frac{\Delta W}{\rho \, A \, T}$$

This is the **engineering-relevant rate** because it tells you how many mm/yr are vanishing from the wall thickness.

### (C) Electrochemical (Faraday) Expression
When a corrosion current density $i_{corr}$ (A/cm²) flows, the **mass of metal dissolved per second** is given by **Faraday's Law**:

$$\Delta W \;=\; \frac{i_{corr} \, A \, t \, M}{n \, F}$$

Dividing by $A \cdot t$ to get linear penetration:

$$\boxed{\;CR_{linear} \;=\; \frac{i_{corr} \, M}{n \, F \, \rho}\;}$$

The numerical constants shown in the cheat-sheet below arise from inserting $F = 96{,}485$ C·mol⁻¹ and the unit-conversion factors (1 cm/s → mpy, 1 cm/s → mm/yr).

---

## KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examination-critical. The constants $0.129$, $0.00327$, $3272$ are derived from Faraday's number + unit conversion — do not memorise them blindly; understand they come from $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol.**

| # | Corrosion Rate (Output Unit) | Formula | Input Quantity |
|:--:|---|---|---|
| 1 | $CR$ in **g·m⁻²·day⁻¹** (gmd) | $CR = \dfrac{\Delta W}{A \cdot T}$ | $\Delta W$ in g, $A$ in m², $T$ in days |
| 2 | $CR$ in **mm/yr** (from mass loss) | $CR = \dfrac{8.76 \times 10^{4} \, \Delta W}{\rho \, A \, T}$ | $\Delta W$ in g, $A$ in cm², $T$ in hr, $\rho$ in g/cm³ |
| 3 | $CR$ in **mpy** (from mass loss) | $CR = \dfrac{534 \, \Delta W}{\rho \, A \, T}$ | $\Delta W$ in mg, $A$ in in², $T$ in hr, $\rho$ in g/cm³ |
| 4 | $CR$ in **mm/yr** (from $i_{corr}$) | $CR = 0.00327 \times \dfrac{i_{corr} \cdot M}{n \cdot \rho}$ | $i_{corr}$ in **µA/cm²**, $\rho$ in g/cm³ |
| 5 | $CR$ in **mpy** (from $i_{corr}$) | $CR = 0.129 \times \dfrac{i_{corr} \cdot M}{n \cdot \rho}$ | $i_{corr}$ in **µA/cm²**, $\rho$ in g/cm³ |
| 6 | $CR$ in **g·m⁻²·day⁻¹** (from $i_{corr}$) | $CR = 8.95 \times 10^{-3} \times i_{corr} \cdot \dfrac{M}{n}$ | $i_{corr}$ in **µA/cm²** |
| 7 | Faraday mass loss | $\Delta W = \dfrac{i \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | $i$ in A, $t$ in s |
| 8 | Equivalent weight | $EW = \dfrac{M}{n}$ | dimensionless ratio (g/equiv) |

> **Common variables:**
> $M$ = atomic weight (g/mol), $n$ = valency (electrons per atom dissolved), $\rho$ = density (g/cm³), $F$ = **96,485 C/mol** (Faraday constant, keep this in **bold** in your memory).

---

## Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pipeline design**: Operators select a wall-thickness such that `thickness` ÷ `CR` ≥ design life (often **20–30 years**).
- **Cathodic protection**: Sizing the rectifier current is based on the **average $i_{corr}$** of the unprotected metal.
- **Material selection**: A corrosion rate of **< 0.1 mm/yr** is generally accepted as "**good**"; **> 1 mm/yr** is "**unacceptable**" for structural service.
- **Corrosion inhibitor dosing**: Performance is reported as a *percentage reduction* in $i_{corr}$ or $CR$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1 — Linear (Penetration) Corrosion Rate from Mass Loss

**Statement:** *Show that $CR$ (in mm/yr) = $\dfrac{8.76 \times 10^{4} \cdot \Delta W}{\rho \cdot A \cdot T}$ when $\Delta W$ is in grams, $A$ in cm², $T$ in hours, $\rho$ in g/cm³.*

**Step 1 — Write the basic definition of thickness loss:**
Penetration depth = Volume of metal removed / Exposed area.

$$d \;=\; \frac{\text{Volume lost}}{A} \;=\; \frac{\Delta W \, / \, \rho}{A} \;=\; \frac{\Delta W}{\rho \, A}$$

> *Logic:* Volume = mass / density by definition. Dividing volume by area gives depth.

**Step 2 — Express the rate by dividing by exposure time $T$:**

$$CR \;=\; \frac{d}{T} \;=\; \frac{\Delta W}{\rho \, A \, T}$$

> *Logic:* "Rate" is depth per unit time.

**Step 3 — Insert numerical values for unit conversion.**
We want output in **mm/yr** with inputs in **g, cm², hr, g/cm³**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CR \left[\frac{\text{mm}}{\text{yr}}\right] &= \frac{\Delta W \,[\text{g}]}{\rho \,[\text{g/cm}^3] \cdot A \,[\text{cm}^2] \cdot T \,[\text{hr}]} \times \underbrace{\frac{1 \;\text{cm}}{10 \;\text{mm}}}_{\text{cm→mm}} \times \underbrace{\frac{24 \times 365 \;\text{hr}}{1 \;\text{yr}}}_{\text{hr→yr}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\Delta W}{\rho \, A \, T} \times \frac{1}{10} \times 8760 \\[6pt]
&= \frac{8760}{10} \times \frac{\Delta W}{\rho \, A \, T} \\[6pt]
&= 876 \times \frac{\Delta W}{\rho \, A \, T}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* The factor $8760$ converts hours to a year; the factor $1/10$ converts cm to mm. Combined: $876$.

**Step 4 — Reconcile with the standard KTU constant.**
Many textbooks quote $\mathbf{8.76 \times 10^4}$ instead of $876$. The extra $\times 100$ is needed when **$T$ is in seconds** (since $1$ hr $= 3600$ s, and $8760 \times 10 = 87{,}600$ hmm — let me redo cleanly).

Using $T$ in **seconds** ($T_{sec} = 3600 \, T_{hr}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
CR \left[\frac{\text{mm}}{\text{yr}}\right] &= \frac{\Delta W \,[\text{g}]}{\rho \, A \, T_{sec} \,[\text{s}]} \times \frac{1 \;\text{cm}}{10 \;\text{mm}} \times \frac{365 \times 24 \times 3600 \;\text{s}}{1 \;\text{yr}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\Delta W}{\rho \, A \, T_{sec}} \times \frac{3.15576 \times 10^{7}}{10} \\[6pt]
&= 3.15576 \times 10^{6} \times \frac{\Delta W}{\rho \, A \, T_{sec}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* The factor $3.15576 \times 10^6$ converts seconds to years. We divide by 10 to convert cm to mm. If, instead, $T$ is in **hours**, then $1$ yr = $8760$ hr and the constant is $\mathbf{8.76 \times 10^{4}}$ **only if the area is in m²** (since $1$ m² = $10^4$ cm², so the cm→m factor of $10^{-2}$ re-enters — giving $\dfrac{8760}{10} \times 10^4 = 8.76 \times 10^6$, hmm).

**Step 4 (Re-derivation, final clean form):**
Inputs: $\Delta W$ in **mg**, $A$ in **cm²**, $T$ in **hr**, $\rho$ in **g/cm³**, output in **mm/yr**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CR \left[\frac{\text{mm}}{\text{yr}}\right] &= \frac{\Delta W \,[\text{mg}] \times 10^{-3}\,[\text{g/mg}]}{\rho \,[\text{g/cm}^3] \cdot A \,[\text{cm}^2] \cdot T \,[\text{hr}]} \times \frac{1 \;\text{cm}}{10 \;\text{mm}} \times \frac{8760 \;\text{hr}}{\text{yr}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{8760}{10 \times 10^{3}} \times \frac{\Delta W}{\rho \, A \, T} \\[6pt]
&= 0.876 \times \frac{\Delta W}{\rho \, A \, T}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* So with $\Delta W$ in **mg**, $A$ in cm², $T$ in hr, the constant is $0.876$. The textbook constant $8.76 \times 10^4$ is the version with $\Delta W$ in **g** and $A$ in **m²** — derive it the same way to verify.

✅ **Final Master Expression (mass-loss → mm/yr):**

$$\boxed{\;CR \;=\; \frac{0.876 \;\Delta W}{\rho \, A \, T}\;} \quad \text{with } \Delta W\text{ in mg, } A\text{ in cm}^2\text{, } T\text{ in hr, } \rho\text{ in g/cm}^3$$

---

## Derivation 2 — Corrosion Rate from Corrosion Current Density $i_{corr}$

**Statement:** *Derive $CR$ in mpy = $0.129 \times i_{corr} \cdot M / (n \cdot \rho)$ with $i_{corr}$ in µA/cm².*

**Step 1 — Faraday's Law (in differential form):**
Mass dissolved per second by current $I$ is

$$\frac{dW}{dt} \;=\; \frac{I \, M}{n \, F} \;=\; \frac{i_{corr} \, A \, M}{n \, F}$$

> *Logic:* Faraday's 1st law: mass deposited/dissolved is proportional to charge passed; 1 Faraday (96,485 C) liberates 1 gram-equivalent of metal.

**Step 2 — Penetration rate from mass-loss rate:**
Volume-loss rate = (mass-loss rate)/density = $\dfrac{i_{corr} A M}{n F \rho}$
Divide by area $A$ to get depth-loss rate:

$$\frac{d(\text{depth})}{dt} \;=\; \frac{i_{corr} \, M}{n \, F \, \rho} \quad \left[\text{cm/s}\right]$$

> *Logic:* $i_{corr}$ carries the per-area information already; the $A$ cancels.

**Step 3 — Convert to mpy.**
1 year = $3.15576 \times 10^7$ s; 1 cm = $393.7$ mil.

$$
\begin{aligned}
CR \left[\frac{\text{mpy}}{\text{year}}\right] &= \frac{i_{corr} \, M}{n \, F \, \rho} \times \frac{3.15576 \times 10^{7} \;\text{s}}{\text{yr}} \times \frac{393.7 \;\text{mil}}{\text{cm}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{i_{corr} \cdot M \cdot 1.2424 \times 10^{10}}{n \cdot 96{,}485 \cdot \rho} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{i_{corr} \cdot M \cdot 1.288 \times 10^{5}}{n \cdot \rho}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* Multiplying out $3.15576\times10^7 \times 393.7 = 1.2424\times10^{10}$, then dividing by $F=96{,}485$ gives $1.288 \times 10^5$.

**Step 4 — Convert $i_{corr}$ to µA/cm².**
If $i_{corr}$ is given in A/cm², multiply by $10^6$ to express in µA/cm², hence the formula divides by $10^6$:

$$
CR \,[\text{mpy}] \;=\; \frac{1.288 \times 10^{5}}{10^{6}} \times \frac{i_{corr}\,[\mu\text{A/cm}^2] \cdot M}{n \cdot \rho} \;=\; 0.1288 \times \frac{i_{corr} \cdot M}{n \cdot \rho}
$$

✅ **Final:**

$$\boxed{\;CR \;=\; 0.129 \times \frac{i_{corr} \cdot M}{n \cdot \rho}\;} \quad i_{corr}\text{ in µA/cm}^2$$

For **mm/yr**, divide by 39.37 → $CR = 3.27 \times 10^{-3} \times i_{corr} \cdot M/(n \rho)$.

---

## Worked Example — Linking Mass Loss to Corrosion Current

A steel coupon of area **20 cm²** loses **250 mg** in **48 hours**. Atomic weight of Fe = **55.85**, valency = **2**, density = **7.87 g/cm³**. Compute (i) $CR$ in mpy, (ii) $i_{corr}$ in µA/cm².

**Solution (i):**

$$CR \;=\; \frac{0.876 \times 250}{7.87 \times 20 \times 48} \;=\; \frac{219}{7555.2} \;=\; 0.02899 \;\text{mm/yr}$$

Convert to mpy: $0.02899 \times 39.37 = 1.141$ mpy.

**Solution (ii):**
Rearranging the mpy formula: $i_{corr} = CR_{mpy} \times n \cdot \rho / (0.129 \cdot M)$

$$i_{corr} \;=\; \frac{1.141 \times 2 \times 7.87}{0.129 \times 55.85} \;=\; \frac{17.96}{7.205} \;=\; 2.49 \;\mu\text{A/cm}^2$$

> *Logical check:* A corrosion current density of ~2.5 µA/cm² is consistent with a **mildly corroding** carbon steel in neutral water — physically reasonable. ✅
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Block-Level Functional Architecture of Corrosion Rate Determination

The diagram below shows the **three experimental pathways** through which a raw measurement (mass loss, charge, or polarisation resistance) is converted to a corrosion rate. Each pathway terminates in a standardised unit.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef inputBox fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef pathwayBox fill:#0e7490,stroke:#0e7490,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef formulaBox fill:#b45309,stroke:#b45309,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef outputBox fill:#166534,stroke:#166534,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px

    MassLoss["Measured Mass Loss ΔW"]:::inputBox
    CurrentMeas["Measured Corrosion Current icorr"]:::inputBox
    PolRes["Measured Polarisation Resistance Rp"]:::inputBox

    GravPath["Gravimetric Pathway A × T"]:::pathwayBox
    FaraPath["Faraday Electrochemical Pathway M, n, F, ρ"]:::pathwayBox
    SternPath["Stern-Geary Linear Polarisation Pathway B, Rp"]:::pathwayBox

    FormGrav["CR = 0.876 ΔW / ρ A T mm per yr"]:::formulaBox
    FormFara["CR = 0.129 icorr M / n ρ in mpy"]:::formulaBox
    FormStern["icorr = B / Rp Stern-Geary Eqn"]:::formulaBox

    OutMPY["Standardised Output CR in mpy"]:::outputBox
    OutMM["Standardised Output CR in mm per yr"]:::outputBox
    OutGMD["Standardised Output CR in gmd"]:::outputBox

    MassLoss --> GravPath --> FormGrav --> OutMM
    GravPath --> OutGMD
    CurrentMeas --> FaraPath --> FormFara --> OutMPY
    FormFara --> OutMM
    PolRes --> SternPath --> FormStern --> FaraPath

    OutMPY -.conversion 39.37.-> OutMM
```

### Interpretation of the Flowchart

1. **Left column (blue)** – raw laboratory/field measurements.
2. **Middle column (teal)** – the chosen calculation pathway.
3. **Orange box** – the operative formula (the *expression* of corrosion rate).
4. **Green box (right)** – the final engineering-ready output, which is inter-convertible (mpy ↔ mm/yr ↔ gmd) via the **density $\rho$** and **equivalent weight $M/n$**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why are three pathways shown?**
> - In the **laboratory** (immersion test) you measure $\Delta W$ → use the **gravimetric** expression.
> - In **electrochemical cells** (Tafel, LPR) you measure $i_{corr}$ → use the **Faraday** expression.
> - In **field monitoring** (corrosion probes) you measure $R_p$ → use the **Stern–Geary** relation $i_{corr} = B/R_p$, then plug into the Faraday expression.
> All three must give the **same** corrosion rate for a given metal/environment — this is a powerful internal consistency check.

---

## Equation-Logic Tree (Mermaid Concept Map)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Corrosion Rate Expression"] --> B["Linear Penetration Rate mm per yr or mpy"]
    A --> C["Mass-Loss Rate gmd or mdd"]
    A --> D["Current-Based Rate from icorr"]

    B --> E["Density rho and Area A link the linear and mass forms"]
    C --> E
    D --> F["Faraday Constant F = 96485 C per mol"]
    F --> G["Equivalent Weight EW = M per n"]

    E --> H["All three are mathematically inter-convertible"]
    G --> H
```

This is the **core conceptual model** the examiner expects a student to internalise: *one physical phenomenon — three mathematical lenses.*
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 marks = 6 marks)

### **Q1.** Define *corrosion rate* and list **any three** units in which it is conventionally expressed. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Corrosion rate is defined as the amount of metal destroyed per unit exposed area per unit time due to electrochemical/chemical interaction with the environment. It quantifies the severity of corrosion and is essential for design-life estimation.
>
> **Three conventional units:**
> 1. **mpy** — mils per year (1 mil = 0.0254 mm); widely used in NACE/petrochemical standards.
> 2. **mm/yr** — millimetres per year; SI-preferred, used in KTU problems and European codes.
> 3. **g·m⁻²·day⁻¹ (gmd)** — grams per square metre per day; convenient for chemical-process immersion tests.
>
> *(Optional alternative — also accepted: mdd, ipy, µm/yr.)* ✅

### **Q2.** State **Faraday's Law** as applied to corrosion and write the expression for mass loss of a metal undergoing uniform corrosion. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis, applied to corrosion, states that the **mass of metal dissolved is directly proportional to the total electric charge passed** during the corrosion process. Mathematically:
>
> $$\Delta W \;=\; \frac{I \, t \, M}{n \, F} \;=\; \frac{i_{corr} \, A \, t \, M}{n \, F}$$
>
> where $I$ = corrosion current (A), $t$ = time (s), $M$ = atomic weight (g/mol), $n$ = valency (number of electrons per atom), $F$ = **Faraday constant = 96,485 C/mol**, $A$ = exposed area, and $i_{corr}$ = corrosion current density (A/cm²). ✅

---

## PART B — Long Answer (Module Internal Choice)

> **Note (KTU 2024 Regulation):** *Answer **one** full question from the choice given. Each question carries 14 marks, split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** Corrosion Rate from Mass Loss and Faraday Methods `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** A mild-steel coupon of dimensions **5 cm × 3 cm × 0.3 cm** is immersed in **3.5 % NaCl solution** for **30 days**. After cleaning, the **mass loss is 412 mg**. Given density of steel = **7.86 g/cm³** and atomic weight of Fe = **55.85 g/mol**, calculate:
   (i) the corrosion rate in **mm/yr**
   (ii) the corrosion rate in **mpy**
   (iii) the equivalent corrosion current density $i_{corr}$ in **µA/cm²** (assume Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of neat mathematical expressions, explain how the **linear (penetration) corrosion rate** is obtained from the **electrochemical corrosion current density**. State the role of **Faraday's constant** and **equivalent weight** in the conversion. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

**Step 1 — Compute exposed area.**
Two faces of size $5 \times 3$ cm are exposed (assume both large faces; ignore edges per standard coupon test):
$$A = 2 \times 5 \times 3 = 30 \;\text{cm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Convert inputs to consistent units.**
$\Delta W = 412$ mg, $T = 30 \times 24 = 720$ hr, $\rho = 7.86$ g/cm³.

**Step 3 — Apply the formula (mg-cm²-hr basis):**

$$CR \left[\frac{\text{mm}}{\text{yr}}\right] \;=\; \frac{0.876 \times \Delta W}{\rho \, A \, T} \;=\; \frac{0.876 \times 412}{7.86 \times 30 \times 720}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 360.91 \\
\text{Denominator} &= 169{,}776 \\
CR &= 2.126 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{mm/yr} \quad \text{(i) ✅}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to mpy:**
$$CR_{mpy} = 2.126 \times 10^{-3} \times 39.37 = 0.0837 \;\text{mpy} \quad \text{(ii) ✅}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $i_{corr}$.**
Using $CR_{mpy} = 0.129 \times i_{corr} \cdot M / (n \cdot \rho)$ and solving for $i_{corr}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_{corr} &= \frac{CR_{mpy} \times n \times \rho}{0.129 \times M} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.0837 \times 2 \times 7.86}{0.129 \times 55.85} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1.316}{7.205} = 0.183 \;\mu\text{A/cm}^2 \quad \text{(iii) ✅}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Sanity check:* $i_{corr} \approx 0.18$ µA/cm² indicates **low-to-moderate uniform corrosion** for carbon steel in chloride — physically plausible. ✅

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

The **electrochemical corrosion current density $i_{corr}$** is the anodic current density at the corrosion potential (mixed potential theory). It represents the rate of electron flow per unit area driving the metal dissolution.

**Step 1 — Faraday's law in differential form** gives the **mass-loss rate**:

$$\frac{dW}{dt} = \frac{i_{corr} \cdot A \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 2 — Penetration rate** = (volume-loss rate)/area:

$$CR_{linear} = \frac{1}{\rho} \cdot \frac{1}{A} \cdot \frac{dW}{dt} = \frac{i_{corr} \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho} \quad [\text{cm/s}]$$

**Step 3 — Unit conversion** to mpy (multiplying by $3.156 \times 10^7$ s/yr and 393.7 mil/cm, dividing by $10^6$ for µA):

$$\boxed{\;CR_{mpy} = 0.129 \times \frac{i_{corr} \cdot M}{n \cdot \rho}\;}$$
