**Role of Faraday's constant $F$:** Converts **electrical charge (coulombs)** to **moles of electrons**, which through stoichiometry yield **moles of metal dissolved** — it is the bridge between electrochemistry and mass loss.

**Role of equivalent weight $EW = M/n$:** Collapses the atomic weight and valency into a single factor that determines how much metal is lost per coulomb of charge. Higher $n$ (greater valency) → less metal mass lost per coulomb.

> *Engineering implication:* This is the basis of **Tafel extrapolation**, **linear polarisation resistance (LPR)**, and **electrochemical noise** techniques — all of which measure $i_{corr}$ and convert it directly to $CR$ using the above expression. ✅

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** A zinc coupon (**M = 65.38 g/mol, n = 2, ρ = 7.13 g/cm³**) suffers a **mass loss of 180 mg** in **100 hours** of exposure over an area of **15 cm²**. Calculate:
   (i) corrosion rate in **g·m⁻²·day⁻¹** (gmd)
   (ii) corrosion rate in **mm/yr**
   (iii) equivalent corrosion current density in **µA/cm²**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **practical significance** of expressing corrosion rate in **mm/yr** as opposed to **mpy** or **gmd**. Why is the **density $\rho$** a necessary parameter when converting from mass-loss to penetration rate? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

**Step 1 — Compute gmd.**
$T = 100/24 = 4.167$ days, $A = 15 \;\text{cm}^2 = 15 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}^2 = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}^2$
$\Delta W = 180 \;\text{mg} = 0.180 \;\text{g}$

$$
CR_{gmd} = \frac{\Delta W}{A \cdot T} = \frac{0.180}{1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 4.167} = \frac{0.180}{6.25 \times 10^{-3}} = 28.8 \;\text{g·m}^{-2}\text{·day}^{-1}
$$

**(i) ✅ 28.8 gmd**

**Step 2 — Convert gmd → mm/yr.**

For Zn: $M/n = 65.38/2 = 32.69$ g/equiv.
Using $CR_{gmd} = 8.95 \times 10^{-3} \times i_{corr} \cdot (M/n)$, solve for $i_{corr}$ first or use direct mm/yr formula:

$$CR_{mm/yr} = \frac{CR_{gmd} \times 24 \times 365}{10 \times \rho} = \frac{28.8 \times 8760}{10 \times 7.13} = \frac{252{,}288}{71.3} = 3538 \;\text{... wait, this is too high.}$$

**Recheck:** $CR$ in mm/yr from $CR$ in g·m⁻²·day⁻¹:

$$CR_{mm/yr} = \frac{CR_{gmd} \times 365}{\rho \times 10}$$

Wait, 1 g·m⁻²·day⁻¹ means 1 g per m² per day. Over 1 year, 365 g per m². Volume per m² per year = $365/\rho$ cm³ = $365 \times 10^4 / \rho$ mm³ (since 1 cm³ = 10³ mm³, and per m² = 10⁴ cm² → 1 cm³/m² = 10⁻⁴ cm = 10⁻³ mm... let me redo).

1 m² = 10⁴ cm². 1 cm depth over 1 m² = 10⁴ cm³ of metal lost.

$CR_{mm/yr} = \dfrac{CR_{gmd} \times 365}{10^4 \times \rho} \times 10 = \dfrac{CR_{gmd} \times 365 \times 10}{10^4 \times \rho} = \dfrac{CR_{gmd} \times 365}{1000 \times \rho}$

$$= \frac{28.8 \times 365}{1000 \times 7.13} = \frac{10{,}512}{7130} = 1.475 \;\text{mm/yr}$$

**(ii) ✅ 1.475 mm/yr**

This rate is **high** — zinc is an active metal corroding at > 1 mm/yr, physically reasonable for a Zn coupon in a corrosive saline medium.

**Step 3 — Compute $i_{corr}$.**
Using $CR_{mpy} = CR_{mm/yr} \times 39.37 = 58.06$ mpy.

$$i_{corr} = \frac{CR_{mpy} \times n \times \rho}{0.129 \times M} = \frac{58.06 \times 2 \times 7.13}{0.129 \times 65.38} = \frac{827.9}{8.434} = 98.2 \;\mu\text{A/cm}^2$$

**(iii) ✅ ~98 µA/cm²** — high current density consistent with active zinc corrosion. ✅

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

**Why prefer mm/yr in engineering practice?**

- **Direct thickness relevance:** A pipeline operator thinks in terms of *wall thickness lost per year*; mm/yr translates directly into **remaining life calculations** (e.g., "10 mm wall thickness at 0.5 mm/yr ⇒ 20-year life").
- **Material-comparison clarity:** A single number (mm/yr) is comparable across **all metals** regardless of density — unlike gmd, which is biased toward **low-density metals** (e.g., Mg, Al) for the same volumetric damage.
- **Compatibility with mechanical design codes** (ASME, IS 2062) which specify minimum thicknesses.

**Why mpy is also used:** entrenched in **NACE/AMPP standards** (US-origin); conversion $1\;\text{mm/yr} = 39.37\;\text{mpy}$ is trivial.

**Why gmd is used in chemistry labs:** When the corrosion product is **non-adherent or flaky**, mass loss is the only directly measurable quantity; conversion to mm/yr is a post-processing step.

**Why density $\rho$ is essential in mass-loss → penetration conversion:**

A corrosion rate in **g·m⁻²·day⁻¹** tells you the *mass* of metal vanishing, but **mechanical integrity depends on the volume** of metal vanishing — and volume = mass / density. Without $\rho$, a low-density metal (Al, $\rho = 2.7$ g/cm³) and a high-density metal (Cu, $\rho = 8.96$ g/cm³) losing the *same mass* per unit area would have *vastly different* penetration depths. For example: $1$ g/m²/day on Al corresponds to $1/(2.7 \times 10^4) = 0.135$ mm/yr, but the same on Cu is only $0.041$ mm/yr — a factor of **3.3×** difference. Hence $\rho$ is the **mandatory link** that converts "chemical loss" to "mechanical loss." ✅

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes costing students 1–3 marks in KTU valuation:**
> 1. **Unit mix-up in the constant** — using $0.876$ when $\Delta W$ is in *grams* instead of *mg* (or vice versa). Always **write the units beside every numerical value**.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert $A$** — area must be in cm² (or m²) consistently with the chosen constant. **Mixing cm² with the m²-formula** is the #1 cause of 100× errors.
> 3. **Wrong valency $n$** — students often use $n = 3$ for Fe (rust) when the actual **anodic reaction is Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻**; the dominant anodic process governs $n$, not the oxide stoichiometry.
> 4. **Misapplication of Faraday** — the formula $0.129 \cdot i \cdot M / (n\rho)$ requires $i$ in **µA/cm²**, NOT mA/cm². The $1000\times$ error is deadly.
> 5. **Ignoring edge area in coupon problems** — KTU generally *accepts* the assumption "two large faces only" but **explicitly stating the assumption** earns the full 7 marks.
> 6. **Not including the unit in the final answer** — KTU examiners **deduct 0.5 mark** for missing units in numerical answers.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Corrosion rate** is *the* engineering metric for corrosion severity — expressed in **mm/yr**, **mpy**, or **gmd**.
- **Three pathways** to determine it: (i) **Gravimetric** (mass loss), (ii) **Faraday/Electrochemical** ($i_{corr}$), (iii) **Stern–Geary** ($R_p$).
- **Faraday's constant** $F = \mathbf{96{,}485}$ C/mol is the cornerstone linking charge to mass dissolved.
- **Key formula (mpy):** $CR_{mpy} = 0.129 \times i_{corr} \cdot M / (n \cdot \rho)$, with $i_{corr}$ in µA/cm².
- **Key formula (mm/yr):** $CR = 0.876 \cdot \Delta W / (\rho \, A \, T)$, with $\Delta W$ in mg, $A$ in cm², $T$ in hr.
- **Equivalent weight** $EW = M/n$ collapses atomic weight and valency into one useful factor.
- **Density $\rho$** is the **mandatory bridge** between mass-loss and penetration-depth rates — it cannot be omitted.
- **Sanity check rule of thumb:** A "good" corrosion-resistant alloy exhibits $CR < 0.1$ mm/yr; mild steel in seawater is $0.1$–$1$ mm/yr; highly active metals (Zn, Mg) can exceed $1$ mm/yr.
- **Unit conversion constants to memorise:** 1 mm/yr = 39.37 mpy; 1 ipy = 1000 mpy; 1 year = 8760 hr = 3.156 × 10⁷ s.
- **Examiner pet topic:** Derivation of the $0.129$ constant is a frequently-asked 5-to-7-mark sub-question — be ready to derive it on paper from Faraday's law + unit conversions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrochemical aspects - electrochemical reactions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT527 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Electrochemical aspects - electrochemical reactions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Electrochemical Reactions — Formal Definition

In the context of **Corrosion Engineering**, an **electrochemical reaction** is a heterogeneous chemical process that involves the transfer of electrons (charge) across an interface between an electronic conductor (a metal) and an ionic conductor (an electrolyte). It is the fundamental reaction that drives the spontaneous degradation of metals in corrosive environments. Every corrosion event is essentially an electrochemical reaction comprising two simultaneous half-reactions: an **oxidation (anodic)** reaction that liberates electrons, and a **reduction (cathodic)** reaction that consumes those electrons.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 – Electrochemical Aspects):**
> The first module introduces the student to the *electrochemical nature of corrosion*. The examiner frequently tests the student's ability to distinguish between anodic and cathodic reactions, write balanced half-reactions, identify the role of the electrolyte, and apply **Faraday's Laws** to compute the rate of metal dissolution.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Battery That Eats Itself"

Imagine a **lemon battery**: a zinc nail and a copper coin inserted into a lemon produce enough current to light a small LED. The lemon juice (citric acid) is the electrolyte, zinc dissolves (corrodes), and copper remains protected. The zinc *sacrifices* itself by sending electrons through the wire to the copper, where hydrogen gas is evolved. This is exactly what happens during **wet corrosion** of steel in acidic water: iron is the dissolving zinc, water is the lemon, and rust is the "dead" LED. The metal is the *fuel* and the corrosion product is the *ash* — except the "fire" is electrochemical, not thermal.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants (Bolded for Recall)

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|-------|------|
| Faraday's Constant | **F** | **96,485** | C mol⁻¹ |
| Gas Constant | **R** | **8.314** | J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹ |
| Avogadro's Number | **Nₐ** | **6.022 × 10²³** | mol⁻¹ |
| Elementary Charge | **e** | **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹** | C |

> [!NOTE]
> The standard state conditions referenced in all EMF calculations are **1 M concentration**, **1 atm pressure**, and **25 °C (298.15 K)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of an Electrochemical Corrosion Cell

An electrochemical corrosion cell requires **four essential components**:

1. **Anode** — the metal surface that oxidizes and dissolves (e.g., Fe in steel). Anodic reactions *generate* electrons.
2. **Cathode** — the metal surface on which reduction occurs. Cathodic reactions *consume* electrons.
3. **Electrolyte** — an ionically conducting medium (water, soil moisture, salt spray, acid solution) that closes the ionic circuit.
4. **Metallic Path / Electronic Conductor** — the bulk metal itself (or an external contact) that closes the electronic circuit by transporting electrons from anode to cathode.

### 2.2 The Anodic (Oxidation) Reaction

At the anode, the metal lattice releases electrons and forms cations that enter the electrolyte:

$$M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^{-}$$

For the corrosion of **iron** in aerated neutral water:

$$Fe \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^{-}$$

For **zinc** in acid:

$$Zn \rightarrow Zn^{2+} + 2e^{-}$$

For **aluminium** (passive metal):

$$Al \rightarrow Al^{3+} + 3e^{-}$$

### 2.3 The Cathodic (Reduction) Reaction

The four most common cathodic reactions encountered in KTU problems are:

**Type 1 — Hydrogen evolution in acid solutions:**

$$2H^{+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow H_{2}\uparrow$$

**Type 2 — Oxygen reduction in acid solutions (aerated):**

$$O_{2} + 4H^{+} + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 2H_{2}O$$

**Type 3 — Oxygen reduction in neutral/alkaline solutions (most common in nature):**

$$O_{2} + 2H_{2}O + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 4OH^{-}$$

**Type 4 — Metal ion reduction (deposition, e.g., copper plating on iron):**

$$Cu^{2+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow Cu$$

### 2.4 Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis

**First Law (1833):** The mass of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electricity passed.

**Second Law (1834):** The mass of different substances liberated by the same quantity of electricity is proportional to their *equivalent weights*.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|----------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $m = \dfrac{Q \cdot A}{n \cdot F} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot A}{n \cdot F}$ | Faraday's mass-loss equation (m = mass dissolved) | grams |
| 2 | $E = \dfrac{W}{n}$ | Equivalent weight = Atomic weight / valency change | g/eq |
| 3 | $\Delta m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot A}{n \cdot F}$ | Corrosion mass loss from current $I$ over time $t$ | grams |
| 4 | $v = \dfrac{I}{n \cdot F}$ | Molar rate of dissolution per second | mol/s |
| 5 | $E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode}$ | Cell EMF (cathode − anode) | Volts |
| 6 | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$ | Nernst Equation (general) | Volts |
| 7 | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n}\log_{10} Q$ | Nernst Equation at **25 °C** (KTU favourite) | Volts |
| 8 | $r = \dfrac{0.00327 \cdot i \cdot A_{w}}{n \cdot \rho}$ | Corrosion rate in mmpy (millimetres per year) | mm/yr |
| 9 | $i_{corr} = \dfrac{I_{cell}}{A}$ | Current density (I = total cell current) | A/cm² |
| 10 | $\eta = E - E_{eq}$ | Polarization overpotential | Volts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is Faraday's Law critical for KTU?**
> It bridges the *electrical* and *chemical* domains of corrosion. Given a measured current, the examiner can ask you to compute the **mass of metal lost per day**, the **corrosion rate in mpy or mmpy**, or the **time to perforation of a pipe** — all using a single Faraday equation.

### 2.6 Engineering Utility

Faraday's Law is the **backbone of cathodic protection design**. Engineers at the Kerala State Electricity Board and oil companies like BPCL use it to:
- Size **sacrificial anodes** (zinc, magnesium) for buried pipelines.
- Calculate the **DC current requirement** of impressed current CP systems.
- Estimate the **service life** of storage tanks and ship hulls.
- Audit **corrosion rate** from linear polarization resistance (LPR) probes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Faraday's First Law (Mass–Charge Relationship)

**Step 1 — Define the charge $Q$ passed through the cell.**
The fundamental unit of charge in electrochemistry is the **Faraday** ($F = 96{,}485$ C/mol). One Faraday is the charge carried by **one mole of electrons** ($N_A$ electrons).

$$Q = N_A \cdot e = (6.022 \times 10^{23}) \cdot (1.602 \times 10^{-19}) = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$$

**Step 2 — Number of moles of electrons transferred.**
If total charge $Q$ flows, the moles of electrons are:

$$n_{e^-} = \dfrac{Q}{F}$$

**Step 3 — Moles of metal atoms that dissolve.**
Each metal atom losing $n$ electrons gives one metal ion. So moles of metal dissolved = moles of electrons divided by $n$:

$$n_{metal} = \dfrac{n_{e^-}}{n} = \dfrac{Q}{nF}$$

**Step 4 — Convert moles of metal to mass.**
Multiply by atomic weight $A$:

$$m = n_{metal} \cdot A = \dfrac{Q \cdot A}{nF}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $Q = I \cdot t$ for steady current.**

$$m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot A}{nF}$$

This is the **canonical Faraday mass-loss equation** used in every KTU numerical.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Corrosion Rate in mmpy

The corrosion rate expressed in **mm/year (mmpy)** is the most common industry metric. Starting from Faraday's Law, the volumetric rate of metal loss per unit area is:

$$v = \dfrac{m}{A_{surface} \cdot \rho}$$

Substituting $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot A_{w}}{nF}$ and recognizing that $\dfrac{I}{A_{surface}} = i_{corr}$ (current density in A/cm²), we get:

$$v = \dfrac{i_{corr} \cdot t \cdot A_{w}}{nF\rho}$$

Convert $t$ from seconds to years: $t = 3.156 \times 10^{7}$ s/yr. Substituting $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol:

$$r_{mmpy} = \dfrac{0.00327 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot A_{w}}{n \cdot \rho}$$

where $i_{corr}$ is in **µA/cm²** and $\rho$ is in **g/cm³**.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical — KTU Style

**Problem:** A mild steel tank is exposed to a corrosive environment. The measured corrosion current density is $i_{corr} = 25\ \mu A/cm^{2}$. Calculate (a) the mass loss per day per square metre, and (b) the corrosion rate in mmpy. Take $A_{w} = 55.85$ g/mol, $n = 2$, $\rho = 7.87$ g/cm³.

**Solution (a) — Mass loss per day:**

Step 1: Current per cm² per day. Since $i = Q/(A \cdot t)$:

$$Q = i \cdot A \cdot t = (25 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (1) \cdot (86400) = 2.16 \text{ C/cm}^2/\text{day}$$

Step 2: Mass of iron dissolved per cm² per day:

$$\Delta m = \dfrac{Q \cdot A_{w}}{nF} = \dfrac{2.16 \cdot 55.85}{2 \cdot 96485}$$

$$\Delta m = \dfrac{120.636}{192970} = 6.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ g/cm}^2/\text{day}$$

Step 3: Convert to g/m²/day (1 m² = 10,000 cm²):

$$\Delta m = 6.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 10{,}000 = 6.25 \text{ g/m}^2/\text{day}$$

**Solution (b) — Corrosion rate in mmpy:**

Step 1: Use the mmpy formula with $i_{corr} = 25\ \mu A/cm^{2}$:

$$r_{mmpy} = \dfrac{0.00327 \cdot 25 \cdot 55.85}{2 \cdot 7.87}$$

$$r_{mmpy} = \dfrac{4.566}{15.74} = 0.290 \text{ mmpy}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A rate of **0.29 mmpy** is classified as *"low corrosion"* in industrial standards. Mild steel in a benign environment typically corrodes at **0.1 – 0.5 mmpy**, while in seawater it can exceed **1.0 mmpy**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Identifying the Rate-Limiting Cathodic Reaction

| Environment | Cathodic Reaction | Typical $E^{\circ}$ (V vs SHE) | Limiting Factor |
|-------------|------------------|------------------------------|-----------------|
| Strongly acidic, deaerated (HCl) | $2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2$ | 0.00 | H⁺ diffusion |
| Acidic, aerated | $O_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^- \rightarrow 2H_2O$ | +1.229 | O₂ diffusion |
| Neutral, aerated (seawater, soil) | $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$ | +0.401 | O₂ diffusion |
| Neutral, deaerated (boiler water) | $2H_2O + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2 + 2OH^-$ | −0.828 | Water dissociation |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Electrochemical Corrosion Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Anode: Metal Surface] -->|Oxidation M to M_n_plus| B[Electrolyte]
    B -->|Ionic migration| C[Cathode: Adjacent Surface]
    C -->|Reduction of H_plus or O2| D[Reduction Product: H2 or H2O or OH_minus]
    A -->|Electron flow through metal| C
    B -->|Metal ions and aggressive ions| E[Corrosion Product Rust]
    A -->|Mass loss| F[Metal Deterioration]
    F -->|Engineering consequence| G[Structural Failure or Leakage]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Faradaic Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Current I in Amps] --> Q2[Charge Q equals I times t in Coulombs]
    Q2 --> Q3[Moles of electrons n_e equals Q divided by F]
    Q3 --> Q4[Moles of metal dissolved equals n_e divided by n]
    Q4 --> Q5[Mass loss m equals moles times Atomic Weight A]
    Q5 --> Q6[Corrosion rate in mmpy using 0.00327 factor]
    Q5 --> Q7[Service life prediction t_life equals m_total divided by mass loss rate]
    Q6 --> R1[Engineering Decision: Replace, Coat, or Cathodically Protect]
    Q7 --> R1
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Combined Anode–Cathode Reactions on a Steel Surface

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Steel_Surface[Steel Surface in Aerated Water]
        direction LR
        Anode_Region[Anode Region - Fe dissolves] -->|Electrons travel through metal| Cathode_Region[Cathode Region - O2 reduced]
    end
    Anode_Region -->|Fe2_plus into solution| Solution1[Electrolyte with Fe2_plus and dissolved O2]
    Solution1 -->|O2 diffuses to surface| Cathode_Region
    Cathode_Region -->|OH_minus produced| Solution2[Boundary layer becomes alkaline]
    Solution1 -->|Fe2_plus plus OH_minus| Rust[Fe OH 2 then Fe2O3 xH2O Rust]
    Solution2 --> Rust
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above represent the **block-level functional architecture** of the corrosion loop. The metal itself is the wire; the electrolyte is the ion highway. The whole system obeys **charge conservation** (every electron released at the anode is consumed at the cathode) and **mass conservation** (every dissolved iron atom eventually appears as a rust molecule).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Distinguish between anodic and cathodic reactions in electrochemical corrosion. Give one example of each.*

**Model Answer:**
An **anodic reaction** is the *oxidation* half-reaction in which a metal loses electrons and dissolves as cations ($M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^{-}$). Example: $Fe \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^{-}$. A **cathodic reaction** is the *reduction* half-reaction in which the electrons liberated at the anode are consumed by species in the electrolyte. Example: $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 4OH^{-}$. Anode is the site of metal *loss*; cathode is the site of *protection* (electrons flow *to* it). Both occur simultaneously and at equal electron-generation/consumption rates.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]** *State Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis and write its mathematical form.*

**Model Answer:**
**Faraday's First Law (1833):** *The mass of a substance liberated or dissolved at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electric charge passed through the electrolyte.* Mathematically:

$$m \propto Q \quad \text{or} \quad m = \dfrac{Q \cdot A_w}{n \cdot F} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot A_w}{n \cdot F}$$

where $m$ is the mass (g), $Q$ is charge (C), $I$ is current (A), $t$ is time (s), $A_w$ is atomic weight, $n$ is valency, and $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol is Faraday's constant.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *With suitable examples, describe the four common cathodic reactions encountered in corrosion. How does the nature of the electrolyte determine which cathodic reaction dominates?*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A buried cast-iron pipeline of diameter 30 cm and length 1 km is corroding in soil. The measured current density on the pipe surface is $40\ \mu A/cm^{2}$. Calculate: (i) the total corrosion current, (ii) the mass of iron lost per year, and (iii) the corrosion rate in mmpy. (Atomic weight of Fe = 55.85, n = 2, $\rho_{Fe}$ = 7.87 g/cm³).*

**Model Solution to (a):**

The four cathodic reactions commonly encountered in corrosion practice are:

1. **Hydrogen evolution in acidic solutions** (e.g., steel in HCl):
$$2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2\uparrow \quad (E^{\circ} = 0.000 \text{ V vs SHE})$$
2. **Oxygen reduction in acidic solutions** (e.g., bridge steel in acid rain):
$$O_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^- \rightarrow 2H_2O \quad (E^{\circ} = +1.229 \text{ V})$$
3. **Oxygen reduction in neutral/alkaline solutions** (e.g., marine piles, buried pipelines):
$$O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^- \quad (E^{\circ} = +0.401 \text{ V})$$
4. **Metal deposition** (e.g., Cu²⁺ reduction on galvanized steel — copper contamination of zinc coating):
$$Cu^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow Cu \quad (E^{\circ} = +0.337 \text{ V})$$

The **dominant cathodic reaction** is selected by:
- **pH of the electrolyte**: H⁺ reduction dominates in acid; O₂/H₂O reduction dominates in neutral and alkaline media.
- **Oxygen availability**: In aerated systems, O₂ reduction takes over because of its more positive potential.
- **Presence of noble metal ions**: Cu²⁺, Ag⁺, Pb²⁺ will deposit preferentially if present.

**Model Solution to (b):**

**(i) Total corrosion current:**
Surface area of pipe = $\pi D L = \pi \times 0.30 \times 1000 = 942.48\ m^2 = 9.4248 \times 10^{6}\ cm^2$.

$$I_{total} = i_{corr} \times A = 40 \times 10^{-6} \times 9.4248 \times 10^{6} = 376.99 \text{ A}$$

**(ii) Mass of iron lost per year:**
$t = 365.25 \times 24 \times 3600 = 3.156 \times 10^{7}$ s/yr.

$$m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot A_w}{nF} = \dfrac{376.99 \times 3.156 \times 10^{7} \times 55.85}{2 \times 96485}$$

$$m = \dfrac{6.647 \times 10^{11}}{192970} = 3.444 \times 10^{6} \text{ g} = 3444 \text{ kg/year}$$

**(iii) Corrosion rate in mmpy:**

$$r_{mmpy} = \dfrac{0.00327 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot A_w}{n \cdot \rho} = \dfrac{0.00327 \cdot 40 \cdot 55.85}{2 \cdot 7.87}$$

$$r_{mmpy} = \dfrac{7.305}{15.74} = 0.464 \text{ mmpy}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> A very common mistake is to **mix up the mmpy formula constants**. The factor 0.00327 is derived assuming $i_{corr}$ is in **µA/cm²** and the answer is in **mm/yr**. If you input current density in **A/cm²**, the constant becomes $3.27 \times 10^{-3}$ but you must adjust the time unit. Also, **forgetting to convert $m^2$ to $cm^2$** in the current calculation is the single most common error — students lose 2–3 marks here in KTU valuation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis. A steel coupon of area 100 cm² is exposed to a corrosive medium. A steady current of 0.05 A flows for 48 hours. Calculate the mass of iron dissolved and the corrosion rate in mmpy. (Take $A_w$ = 55.85, n = 2, $\rho$ = 7.87 g/cm³).*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Differentiate between chemical corrosion and electrochemical corrosion. Why is electrochemical corrosion far more common in engineering practice?*

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Faraday's First Law** states that the mass of metal dissolved is directly proportional to the charge passed:

$$m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot A_w}{nF}$$

**Faraday's Second Law** states that the masses of different substances liberated by the same charge are proportional to their equivalent weights ($A_w/n$).

**Numerical Solution:**
Given: $I = 0.05$ A, $t = 48 \times 3600 = 172{,}800$ s, $A = 100\ cm^2$.

Step 1: Mass dissolved.

$$m = \dfrac{0.05 \times 172{,}800 \times 55.85}{2 \times 96485} = \dfrac{482{,}544}{192{,}970} = 2.50 \text{ g}$$

Step 2: Current density.

$$i = \dfrac{0.05}{100} = 5 \times 10^{-4}\ A/cm^2 = 500\ \mu A/cm^2$$

Step 3: Corrosion rate.

$$r = \dfrac{0.00327 \times 500 \times 55.85}{2 \times 7.87} = \dfrac{91.31}{15.74} = 5.80 \text{ mmpy}$$

**Model Solution to (b):**

| Feature | Chemical Corrosion | Electrochemical Corrosion |
|---------|-------------------|---------------------------|
| Medium | Dry gas, non-electrolyte (e.g., O₂, H₂S) | Wet electrolyte (water, soil, acid) |
| Electron transfer | Direct; metal + gas → product | Indirect; mediated by ionic solution |
| Example | Oxidation of Ag in O₂ at high T | Rusting of Fe in moist air |
| Temperature | High temperature (e.g., > 500 °C) | Ambient or moderate |
| Rate control | Diffusion of gas through oxide | Diffusion of O₂ or H⁺ through electrolyte |

Electrochemical corrosion dominates engineering because **moisture is almost always present** in service environments (atmospheric humidity, rain, soil moisture, process fluids), and trace ionic contaminants (Cl⁻, SO₄²⁻) ensure adequate electrolyte conductivity. The simultaneous anodic and cathodic half-reactions are thermodynamically favourable, making wet corrosion **inevitable** unless protective measures are taken.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> In (a), the most common error is **unit conversion** — students forget $t$ must be in **seconds**, not hours. In (b), students often confuse the **direction of electron flow** in the corrosion cell. Remember: **electrons flow from anode to cathode through the metal**, while **ions migrate through the electrolyte in the opposite direction to close the circuit**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ An **electrochemical corrosion cell** has *four* components: anode, cathode, electrolyte, and metallic path.
- ✅ The **anode** is always the site of **metal loss** ($M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^{-}$). The **cathode** is always the site of **reduction** ($O_2$, $H^+$, or $H_2O$).
- ✅ The **dominant cathodic reaction** depends on **pH and aeration**:
   - Acid + deaerated → $H^+$ reduction
   - Acid + aerated → $O_2$ reduction in acid
   - Neutral/alkaline + aerated → $O_2$ reduction in neutral (most common in nature)
- ✅ **Faraday's Constant $F$ = 96,485 C/mol** is the cornerstone of all mass-loss calculations.
- ✅ The **mass loss formula** $m = \dfrac{I t A_w}{nF}$ is used in *every* KTU numerical.
- ✅ The **mmpy formula** $r = \dfrac{0.00327 \cdot i \cdot A_w}{n \rho}$ uses $i$ in **µA/cm²** and gives rate in **mm/yr**.
- ✅ **Charge conservation** demands that the *total* anodic electron generation equals the *total* cathodic electron consumption — this is what couples the two half-reactions.
- ✅ **One Faraday liberates one equivalent weight** of any substance (Faraday's Second Law).
- ✅ **Polarization and overpotential** $\eta$ cause the actual cell potential to deviate from the theoretical EMF — a key topic for Module 1's later sub-sections.
- ✅ Always **balance the half-reactions for atoms and charge** before combining them.
- ✅ **Practice 3-4 numericals per day** — the KTU paper rewards numerical fluency above all else in Module 1.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polarization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT527 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Polarization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Polarization

In the context of **Corrosion Engineering (PECHT527)**, **polarization** is formally defined as the **change in electrode potential of an electrode from its equilibrium (or corrosion) value as a result of the passage of an external current** (either applied externally or generated internally by a corrosion cell). Mathematically, it is expressed as the deviation $\eta$ (overpotential) of the working electrode potential $E$ from its equilibrium potential $E_{eq}$:

$$\eta = E - E_{eq}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Expectation):** Polarization is the **deviation of an electrode potential from its equilibrium value** when current flows through an electrochemical cell. It is the direct manifestation of irreversibility in anodic and cathodic reactions and is the single most important parameter controlling the rate of corrosion.

Polarization is universally measured in **millivolts (mV)** or **volts (V)**, and the corresponding current density is measured in **A/cm²** or **A/m²**.

## 1.2 The Three Fundamental Types of Polarization

KTU examiners expect students to identify the three primary polarization mechanisms. These are the **"Holy Trinity"** of polarization:

1. **Activation Polarization ($\eta_a$)** — Slow charge-transfer kinetics at the electrode/solution interface. The bottleneck is the **energy barrier** of the electrochemical reaction itself. Governed by the **Butler-Volmer equation** and **Tafel equation**.
2. **Concentration Polarization ($\eta_c$)** — Slow mass transport of reactive species (ions, O₂, H⁺) to or from the electrode surface through the electrolyte. Governed by **Fick's Laws of Diffusion**.
3. **Resistance (Ohmic) Polarization ($\eta_r$)** — Voltage drop across the electrolyte and surface films/oxide layers due to finite ionic/electronic conductivity. Governed by **Ohm's Law** ($IR$ drop).

The total polarization is the sum of these three:

$$\eta_{total} = \eta_a + \eta_c + \eta_r$$

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy: The Clogged Pipe System**
> Imagine water flowing through a pipe system connected to a large reservoir. The **water pressure at the source** is the equilibrium potential ($E_{eq}$). The **actual pressure you measure at the outlet** is the polarized potential ($E$). The difference is the polarization ($\eta$).
> - If the pipe has a **narrow constriction** (slow chemical reaction at the wall), that's **activation polarization**.
> - If a **debris filter** further downstream blocks dissolved oxygen from reaching the water (slow mass transport), that's **concentration polarization**.
> - If the pipe itself is made of **rusty metal** (resistive film), that's **ohmic/resistance polarization**.
> Just like in a pipe, the smallest "opening" in an electrochemical circuit controls the overall rate of flow. Polarization is the **"narrowing" of the electrochemical pathway**.

## 1.4 Why Polarization Matters in Corrosion

Without polarization, metals would corrode at their maximum thermodynamically allowed rates (orders of magnitude faster than observed). Polarization is essentially the **"brake"** that nature applies to slow down corrosion. Understanding polarization allows corrosion engineers to:

- **Predict** corrosion rates via **Stern-Geary equation** and **Tafel extrapolation**.
- **Design** cathodic protection systems.
- **Select** inhibitors that increase polarization resistance.
- **Engineer** alloys with passive films that introduce high polarization.

## 1.5 Key Standard Constants & Reference Values

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants Used Throughout Polarization Theory:**
> - **Faraday's Constant ($F$)** = **96,485 C/mol** (charge per mole of electrons)
> - **Universal Gas Constant ($R$)** = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
> - **Absolute Temperature ($T$)** = measured in **Kelvin (K)**
> - **Anodic Tafel slope ($\beta_a$)** typically **0.06 V to 0.12 V/decade**
> - **Cathodic Tafel slope ($\beta_c$)** typically **0.06 V to 0.12 V/decade**
> - **Number of electrons ($n$)** involved in the rate-determining step
> - **Exchange current density ($i_0$)** = current density at equilibrium, A/cm²

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Activation Polarization — The Heart of Electrode Kinetics

### 2.1.1 The Energy Barrier Concept
When a metal ion $M^{n+}$ is to be discharged from the solution (cathodic) or dissolved into the solution (anodic), it must overcome an **activation energy barrier** at the metal/electrolyte interface. The applied current forces the electrode potential to shift from $E_{eq}$, which effectively **lowers the energy barrier** for the forward reaction while **raising it** for the reverse reaction.

### 2.1.2 The Butler-Volmer Equation (The Master Equation)

The current density $i$ flowing through an electrode under activation control is given by the **Butler-Volmer equation**:

$$i = i_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{\alpha_a n F \eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\left(-\frac{\alpha_c n F \eta}{RT}\right) \right]$$

Where:
- $i$ = net current density (A/cm²)
- $i_0$ = **exchange current density** (A/cm²)
- $\eta$ = overpotential = $E - E_{eq}$ (V)
- $\alpha_a$ = **anodic charge-transfer coefficient** (typically 0.5)
- $\alpha_c$ = **cathodic charge-transfer coefficient** (typically 0.5)
- $n$ = number of electrons in rate-determining step
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = 96,485 C/mol
- $R$ = universal gas constant = 8.314 J/(mol·K)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The Butler-Volmer equation is valid for **small overpotentials** ($|\eta| < 10$ mV) where both exponential terms contribute. For larger overpotentials, the Tafel approximation is used.

### 2.1.3 The Tafel Equation (High Overpotential Limit)

When $\vert \eta \vert > 50$ mV, one exponential term dominates and the Butler-Volmer equation reduces to the **Tafel Equation**:

**Anodic Tafel Equation** (metal dissolution):
$$\eta_a = \beta_a \log\left(\frac{i}{i_0}\right)$$

**Cathodic Tafel Equation** (reduction reaction):
$$\eta_c = \beta_c \log\left(\frac{i}{i_0}\right)$$

Where the **Tafel slope** $\beta$ is defined as:

$$\beta = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha n F}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical value at 25°C (298 K) with $\alpha = 0.5$ and $n = 1$:**
> $$\beta = \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}{0.5 \times 1 \times 96485} \approx 0.118 \text{ V/decade} \approx 120 \text{ mV/decade}$$
> This is the **canonical Tafel slope** and frequently appears in KTU numerical problems.

## 2.2 Concentration Polarization — Mass Transport Limitation

Concentration polarization occurs when the **rate of supply** of reactants (or removal of products) to/from the electrode surface by **diffusion, convection, or migration** is slower than the rate of electrochemical reaction. At the **limiting current density** ($i_L$), the surface concentration of the reacting species drops to zero.

The overpotential for concentration polarization is given by:

$$\eta_c = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log\left(1 - \frac{i}{i_L}\right)$$

Where:
- $i_L$ = **limiting current density** (A/cm²)
- $i$ = applied current density
- $n$ = number of electrons

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Relevance:** Concentration polarization is critical in **oxygen reduction** at the cathodic sites of corroding structures (e.g., pipelines, offshore platforms). In stagnant seawater, dissolved O₂ supply is slow, and $i_L$ is very low, which significantly reduces overall corrosion rate.

## 2.3 Resistance (Ohmic) Polarization — IR Drop

This is the simplest form, governed by **Ohm's Law**:

$$\eta_r = i \cdot R_{solution} + i \cdot R_{film}$$

Where $R_{solution}$ and $R_{film}$ are the electrolyte resistance and surface film resistance respectively. Ohmic drop is significant in:
- Low-conductivity electrolytes (fresh water, humid soils)
- Painted/coated surfaces with damaged films
- Crevices and under-deposit corrosion

## 2.4 Mixed Potential Theory & Evans Diagram

When two dissimilar electrochemical reactions occur on the **same metal surface** (the actual corrosion scenario), the system is described by the **Mixed Potential Theory** (Wagner & Traud, 1938):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Evans Diagram Principles:**
> 1. The anodic dissolution curve and cathodic reduction curve are **plotted on the same E vs log(i) axes**.
> 2. They **intersect** at a unique point: the **corrosion potential $E_{corr}$** and **corrosion current density $i_{corr}$**.
> 3. The corrosion rate is determined by $i_{corr}$ via **Faraday's Law**.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula Name** | **Equation** | **Application Domain** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Overpotential Definition | $\eta = E - E_{eq}$ | All polarization types | V or mV |
| Butler-Volmer (General) | $i = i_0 \left[ \exp(\alpha_a n F \eta / RT) - \exp(-\alpha_c n F \eta / RT) \right]$ | Small overpotentials ($\vert \eta \vert < 10$ mV) | A/cm² |
| Tafel Slope Definition | $\beta = (2.303 RT) / (\alpha n F)$ | High overpotential limit | V/decade |
| Anodic Tafel Equation | $\eta_a = \beta_a \log(i/i_0)$ | Activation-controlled anodic | V |
| Cathodic Tafel Equation | $\eta_c = \beta_c \log(i/i_0)$ | Activation-controlled cathodic | V |
| Concentration Polarization | $\eta_c = (2.303 RT / nF) \log(1 - i/i_L)$ | Mass transport limited | V |
| Ohmic Polarization | $\eta_r = iR$ | Electrolyte/film resistance | V |
| Stern-Geary Equation | $i_{corr} = B / R_p$, where $B = \beta_a \beta_c / (2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c))$ | Corrosion rate from $R_p$ | A/cm² |
| Faraday's Corrosion Rate | $r = (i_{corr} \cdot M) / (n \cdot F \cdot \rho)$ | Mass loss rate | mm/yr (mpy) |
| Limiting Current Density | $i_L = nFD C_b / \delta$ | Concentration polarization | A/cm² |

> [!WARNING]
> **PITFALL:** In the KTU table, all vertical bars (for absolute value) are written as `\vert` — never as `|` — to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax during PDF rendering.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cathodic Protection Design:** Polarization curves determine the required protective current for buried pipelines, ship hulls, and offshore structures.
- **Inhibitor Selection:** Effective inhibitors shift the corrosion potential by increasing polarization at either anodic or cathodic sites.
- **Alloy Development:** Passive metals (e.g., stainless steel, Ti) exhibit a sharp rise in anodic polarization at the passivation potential, dramatically reducing $i_{corr}$.
- **Battery & Fuel Cell Industry:** The same Butler-Volmer formalism governs electrode kinetics in energy storage/conversion devices.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION_3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Butler-Volmer Equation from the Absolute Reaction Rate Theory

### 3.1.1 Starting Premises

According to **Absolute Reaction Rate Theory (Eyring, 1935)**, the rate of an electrochemical reaction depends on the number of activated complexes crossing the energy barrier per unit time. The net current density is the difference between the **forward (anodic)** and **reverse (cathodic)** current densities.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Define the equilibrium exchange current density $i_0$ as the rate at which forward and reverse reactions proceed equally at $E_{eq}$ (i.e., when $\eta = 0$).

**Step 2:** When the electrode potential is shifted by an amount $\eta$ in the anodic direction, the energy barrier for the anodic reaction is reduced by a fraction $\alpha_a$ of the total electrical work done:

$$\Delta G^{\ddagger}_{anodic} = \Delta G^{\ddagger}_0 - \alpha_a n F \eta$$

**Step 3:** The electrical work done to raise the cathodic barrier by a fraction $\alpha_c$ of the total is:

$$\Delta G^{\ddagger}_{cathodic} = \Delta G^{\ddagger}_0 + \alpha_c n F \eta$$

**Step 4:** Using the Arrhenius form, the anodic current density becomes:

$$i_a = i_0 \exp\left(\frac{\alpha_a n F \eta}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 5:** Similarly, the cathodic current density (negative sign convention) is:

$$i_c = -i_0 \exp\left(-\frac{\alpha_c n F \eta}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 6:** The net current density is the sum:

$$i_{net} = i_a + i_c = i_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{\alpha_a n F \eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\left(-\frac{\alpha_c n F \eta}{RT}\right) \right]$$

This is the **Butler-Volmer equation**. ∎

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Tafel Equation from Butler-Volmer

**Step 1:** Start with the Butler-Volmer equation for large anodic overpotential:

$$i = i_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{\alpha_a n F \eta_a}{RT}\right) - \exp\left(-\frac{\alpha_c n F \eta_a}{RT}\right) \right]$$

**Step 2:** For large positive $\eta_a$, the second exponential term becomes negligibly small ($e^{-x} \to 0$ as $x \to \infty$), so:

$$i \approx i_0 \exp\left(\frac{\alpha_a n F \eta_a}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln\left(\frac{i}{i_0}\right) = \frac{\alpha_a n F \eta_a}{RT}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $\eta_a$:

$$\eta_a = \frac{RT}{\alpha_a n F} \ln\left(\frac{i}{i_0}\right)$$

**Step 5:** Convert natural logarithm to base-10 logarithm using $\ln(x) = 2.303 \log_{10}(x)$:

$$\eta_a = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha_a n F} \log_{10}\left(\frac{i}{i_0}\right)$$

**Step 6:** Define the anodic Tafel slope $\beta_a$:

$$\beta_a = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha_a n F}$$

**Step 7:** Therefore the final Tafel equation is:

$$\eta_a = \beta_a \log\left(\frac{i}{i_0}\right) \quad \text{(Anodic Tafel Equation)} \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A plot of $\eta$ vs $\log(i)$ yields a straight line with slope $\beta_a$ and intercept at $\log(i_0)$ when $\eta = 0$. This is the basis of the **Tafel extrapolation method** for determining $i_{corr}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Stern-Geary Equation (Linear Polarization Resistance)

**Step 1:** For small overpotentials ($\vert \eta \vert < 10$ mV), use the Taylor series expansion of the exponential function:
$\exp(x) \approx 1 + x$ for small $x$.

**Step 2:** Apply this to the Butler-Volmer equation:

$$i = i_0 \left[ \left(1 + \frac{\alpha_a n F \eta}{RT}\right) - \left(1 - \frac{\alpha_c n F \eta}{RT}\right) \right]$$

**Step 3:** Simplify the expression:

$$i = i_0 \cdot \eta \cdot \frac{nF}{RT} \left(\alpha_a + \alpha_c\right)$$

**Step 4:** The **polarization resistance** $R_p$ is defined as:

$$R_p = \frac{d\eta}{di}\bigg|_{\eta \to 0} = \frac{RT}{i_0 n F (\alpha_a + \alpha_c)}$$

**Step 5:** Use the Tafel slope definitions $\beta_a = (2.303 RT)/(\alpha_a n F)$ and $\beta_c = (2.303 RT)/(\alpha_c n F)$ to eliminate $\alpha_a$ and $\alpha_c$:

$$R_p = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303 \, i_{corr} (\beta_a + \beta_c)}$$

**Step 6:** Rearranging, the **Stern-Geary equation** is:

$$\boxed{i_{corr} = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303 \, R_p (\beta_a + \beta_c)} = \frac{B}{R_p}}$$

Where the **Stern-Geary constant** $B$ is typically between **13 mV and 52 mV** for most corrosion systems. ∎

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A steel specimen in 0.5 M H₂SO₄ at 25°C has an exchange current density of $i_0 = 10^{-6}$ A/cm². The anodic Tafel slope is $\beta_a = 0.12$ V/decade and the cathodic Tafel slope is $\beta_c = 0.10$ V/decade. Calculate:
(a) The corrosion current density $i_{corr}$ (assume equal anodic and cathodic areas).
(b) The polarization resistance $R_p$.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** From the Evans diagram, at $E_{corr}$, the anodic current equals the cathodic current. For equal anodic/cathodic areas, $i_{corr}$ can be found by equating the two Tafel expressions (when plotted on a $\log i$ axis they intersect):

$$\eta_a = \beta_a \log(i_{corr}/i_{0,a}) \quad \text{and} \quad \eta_c = \beta_c \log(i_{corr}/i_{0,c})$$

For identical anodic and cathodic $i_0$ values ($i_0 = 10^{-6}$ A/cm² for both reactions on the same surface):

$$i_{corr} = i_0 \times 10^{\Delta E / (\beta_a + \beta_c)}$$

Assuming a typical potential difference of 0.3 V between the anodic and cathodic equilibrium reactions in this system:

$$i_{corr} = 10^{-6} \times 10^{0.3 / (0.12 + 0.10)} = 10^{-6} \times 10^{1.364} \approx 2.31 \times 10^{-5} \text{ A/cm}^2$$

**Part (b):** Applying the Stern-Geary equation:

$$B = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)} = \frac{0.12 \times 0.10}{2.303 \times 0.22} = \frac{0.012}{0.5067} \approx 0.0237 \text{ V} = 23.7 \text{ mV}$$

$$R_p = \frac{B}{i_{corr}} = \frac{0.0237 \text{ V}}{2.31 \times 10^{-5} \text{ A/cm}^2} \approx 1026 \, \Omega \cdot \text{cm}^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $i_{corr} \approx 2.31 \times 10^{-5}$ A/cm², $R_p \approx 1026 \, \Omega \cdot \text{cm}^2$

---

## 3.5 The Polarization Curve Analysis Matrix

| **Region of Curve** | **Controlling Process** | **Equation Used** | **Slope Characteristics** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $E < E_{corr}$ (cathodic branch) | Cathodic activation | $\eta_c = \beta_c \log(i/i_0)$ | Linear in $\log(i)$ |
| $E \approx E_{corr}$ | Mixed control | Butler-Volmer (full form) | Curved transition |
| $E > E_{corr}$ (anodic branch) | Anodic activation | $\eta_a = \beta_a \log(i/i_0)$ | Linear in $\log(i)$ |
| Anodic region, $i \to i_L$ | Concentration polarization | $\eta_c = (2.303RT/nF)\log(1-i/i_L)$ | Vertical asymptote at $i_L$ |
| Passive region (alloys) | Film formation | Steep rise in $\eta$ | Near-vertical slope |
| Transpassive region | Film breakdown | Slope decreases | Re-dissolution occurs |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the term 'polarization' in corrosion engineering. List the three main types of polarization with a one-line description of each.**

**Model Answer:**

Polarization is defined as the **deviation of an electrode potential from its equilibrium value** when current flows through an electrochemical cell. It represents the irreversible energy losses associated with the corrosion reactions.

The three main types are:
1. **Activation Polarization** — Energy loss due to slow charge-transfer kinetics at the electrode/electrolyte interface.
2. **Concentration Polarization** — Energy loss due to slow mass transport of reacting species through the electrolyte.
3. **Resistance (Ohmic) Polarization** — Energy loss due to the IR drop across the electrolyte and surface films.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is the Tafel slope? State its mathematical expression and the standard numerical value at 25°C for a single-electron transfer with $\alpha = 0.5$.**

**Model Answer:**

The Tafel slope $\beta$ is defined as the **slope of the linear region of an electrode's polarization curve** when overpotential $\eta$ is plotted against $\log_{10}$ of current density $i$. It has units of **V/decade** (or mV/decade).

Mathematically:

$$\beta = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha \, n F}$$

At standard temperature 25°C (T = 298 K) with $n = 1$ and $\alpha = 0.5$:

$$\beta = \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}{0.5 \times 1 \times 96485} \approx 0.118 \text{ V/decade} \approx 118 \text{ mV/decade}$$

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]

**(a) Derive the Butler-Volmer equation starting from the absolute reaction rate theory. Clearly define all symbols used.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Refer to the detailed derivation given in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The step-by-step derivation is:

1. Define the equilibrium exchange current density $i_0$ at $\eta = 0$.
2. The applied overpotential $\eta$ modifies the activation energy barrier: anodic barrier decreases by $\alpha_a n F \eta$, cathodic barrier increases by $\alpha_c n F \eta$.
3. Apply the Arrhenius equation to the modified barrier:

$$i_a = i_0 \exp\left(\frac{\alpha_a n F \eta}{RT}\right)$$

$$i_c = i_0 \exp\left(-\frac{\alpha_c n F \eta}{RT}\right)$$

4. Net current density is the algebraic sum:

$$i = i_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{\alpha_a n F \eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\left(-\frac{\alpha_c n F \eta}{RT}\right) \right]$$

This is the **Butler-Volmer equation**. ∎

---

**(b) Explain the construction and interpretation of an Evans diagram. Show how the corrosion current density $i_{corr}$ and corrosion potential $E_{corr}$ are determined from it.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

An **Evans diagram** is a combined plot of the anodic and cathodic polarization curves on the same axes of **electrode potential $E$ (y-axis)** versus **logarithm of current density $\log i$ (x-axis)**.

**Construction Steps:**
1. The anodic polarization curve (metal dissolution) is plotted as a straight line in the Tafel region with slope $\beta_a$ and a positive trend.
2. The cathodic polarization curve (reduction reaction, e.g., H₂ evolution or O₂ reduction) is plotted as a straight line with slope $\beta_c$ and a negative trend.
3. Both lines are extended into the Tafel region.

**Interpretation:**
- The **intersection point** of the two Tafel lines gives the **corrosion potential $E_{corr}$** (y-coordinate) and the **corrosion current density $i_{corr}$** (x-coordinate).
- The corrosion rate is then calculated from $i_{corr}$ using Faraday's Law:

$$CR = \frac{0.00327 \times i_{corr} \times EW}{\rho} \quad \text{(mm/yr)}$$

- The **slope of the polarization curve at $E_{corr}$** gives the **polarization resistance $R_p$**, which is inversely proportional to $i_{corr}$ via the Stern-Geary equation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse the *exchange current density* $i_0$ (which refers to a single half-reaction at its own equilibrium) with the *corrosion current density* $i_{corr}$ (which is a mixed kinetic parameter of the entire corroding system). Always distinguish these clearly in your answer.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) State and explain the Stern-Geary equation. How is it used to determine the corrosion rate from linear polarization resistance (LPR) measurements?** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Stern-Geary equation** relates the corrosion current density $i_{corr}$ to the **polarization resistance $R_p$** of the corroding system:

$$i_{corr} = \frac{B}{R_p} \quad \text{where} \quad B = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)}$$

**Derivation Outline (Full derivation in Section 3.3):**
1. Begin with the Butler-Volmer equation.
2. Apply Taylor series expansion for small $\eta$ ($|\eta| < 10$ mV).
3. The polarization resistance $R_p = (d\eta/di)|_{\eta \to 0}$ is obtained.
4. Substitute the Tafel slope definitions to express $R_p$ in terms of $\beta_a$, $\beta_c$, and $i_{corr}$.
5. Rearrange to obtain the Stern-Geary form.

**Application in LPR Measurement:**
- A small perturbation $\Delta E$ (typically $\pm 10$ mV) is applied around $E_{corr}$ using a potentiostat.
- The resulting current $\Delta i$ is measured.
- $R_p = \Delta E / \Delta i$.
- $i_{corr}$ is calculated using the Stern-Geary equation (with $B \approx 26$ mV for many active corrosion systems).
- Corrosion rate is then calculated via Faraday's Law.

---

**(b) Differentiate between activation polarization and concentration polarization. Under what conditions does each become the rate-determining step?** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| **Aspect** | **Activation Polarization** | **Concentration Polarization** |
|---|---|---|
| Cause | Slow charge transfer at the interface | Slow mass transport in the electrolyte |
| Governing equation | Butler-Volmer / Tafel | $\eta_c = (2.303RT/nF)\log(1 - i/i_L)$ |
| Controlling parameter | Exchange current density $i_0$ | Limiting current density $i_L$ |
| When dominant | At low current densities, high $i_0$, well-stirred solutions | At high current densities, low $i_L$, stagnant solutions |
| Typical shape on E-log i plot | Linear Tafel region | Curves toward vertical at $i = i_L$ |
| Engineering control | Catalysis, increase temperature, surface activation | Increase convection, raise reactant concentration, use rotating electrodes |

**Rate-Determining Conditions:**
- **Activation polarization is rate-determining** when $i \ll i_L$, i.e., the reaction rate is slow compared to the supply of reactants. Typical of well-stirred, aerated acidic solutions.
- **Concentration polarization is rate-determining** when $i$ approaches $i_L$, i.e., the reactant supply cannot keep up with the electron-transfer rate. Typical of oxygen reduction in stagnant seawater, or hydrogen evolution at high current densities.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students fail to mention that **both polarizations act simultaneously** in real systems. The total polarization $\eta_{total} = \eta_a + \eta_c + \eta_r$. The slowest of the three controls the overall rate. Always state which is dominant in your answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rapid-Revision Checklist for Polarization (PECHT527 Module 1):**
>
> - **Definition:** Polarization = deviation of electrode potential from equilibrium value ($\eta = E - E_{eq}$).
> - **Three types:** Activation ($\eta_a$), Concentration ($\eta_c$), Ohmic ($\eta_r$). Total: $\eta_{total} = \eta_a + \eta_c + \eta_r$.
> - **Butler-Volmer Equation:** Master equation valid for all overpotentials. Memorize the form.
> - **Tafel Equation:** Valid only for $|\eta| > 50$ mV. Plot of $\eta$ vs $\log i$ is linear with slope $\beta$.
> - **Tafel Slope:** $\beta = (2.303 RT)/(\alpha n F) \approx 0.118$ V/decade at 25°C for $n=1$, $\alpha = 0.5$.
> - **Exchange current density $i_0$:** Inherent kinetic property; higher $i_0$ → faster reaction → less polarization.
> - **Limiting current density $i_L$:** Maximum rate under mass-transport control; reaction cannot exceed $i_L$.
> - **Evans Diagram:** Intersection of anodic and cathodic Tafel lines → $(E_{corr}, i_{corr})$.
> - **Stern-Geary Equation:** $i_{corr} = B/R_p$ where $B$ is a constant (~13-52 mV); foundation of LPR technique.
> - **Linear Polarization Resistance (LPR):** Used to rapidly estimate corrosion rate using $\pm 10$ mV perturbation.
> - **Concentration Polarization Formula:** $\eta_c = (2.303RT/nF)\log(1 - i/i_L)$.
> - **Ohmic Polarization:** $\eta_r = iR$ (Ohm's Law).
> - **Standard Constants:** $F = 96485$ C/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $T$ in Kelvin.
> - **Numerical Conversion:** $1$ A/cm² $\times$ EW $\approx 0.00327$ mm/yr corrosion rate for steels.
> - **Real-World Applications:** Cathodic protection, anodic protection, inhibitor selection, alloy design, battery/fuel cell electrode design.
> - **Common Mistake:** Confusing $i_0$ (single half-reaction) with $i_{corr}$ (mixed corrosion system). Always clarify.
> - **Examiner's Tip:** Always draw an Evans diagram in numerical problems; it is worth 2-3 marks by itself.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Passivity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT527 - CORROSION ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Passivity # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Passivity ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Passivity** is defined as the loss of chemical reactivity exhibited by certain metals and alloys (e.g., chromium, nickel, aluminum, titanium, and their alloys) under specific environmental conditions, where the metal, although thermodynamically unstable, behaves as if it were chemically inert due to the formation of a thin, adherent, and protective oxide (or oxy-hydroxide) film on its surface. In electrochemical terms, passivity is the state in which a metal exhibits a corrosion rate orders of magnitude lower than what would be predicted from its standard electrode potential, due to kinetic hindrance imposed by a surface film of the order of **2–10 nm** thickness. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECHT 527 - Module 1):** > Passivity is the foundational concept that distinguishes *kinetically-controlled* corrosion resistance from *thermodynamically-controlled* corrosion resistance. The passivation phenomenon is the operating principle behind stainless steels, titanium implants, and aluminum aerospace structures. The phenomenon was first observed by **Wetzlar (1826)** and was systematically studied by **Schönbein and Faraday (1836)**. The term *"passive"* originates from the Latin *passivus* (suffering), reflecting how the metal "suffers" a change in its surface state that renders it unreactive. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy Imagine a freshly painted iron gate left in the rain. The wet paint film acts like a **raincoat** — water touches the paint, not the iron. The iron beneath is *thermodynamically* unstable in water (it *wants* to rust), but the paint film *kinetically* prevents water from reaching the iron. **Passivity works the EXACT same way**, except the "raincoat" is a film that the metal creates **for itself** when exposed to certain environments (oxidizing conditions). Chromium, for instance, instantly forms an invisible Cr₂O₃ layer that is only a few atoms thick, yet blocks further corrosion almost completely. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction:** > - **Noble metals** (Au, Pt): thermodynamically inert — they *don't want* to corrode. > - **Passive metals** (Cr, Al, Ti): thermodynamically unstable — they *want* to corrode but are *prevented* by a self-generated oxide film. ## 1.3 The Four Signature Features of a Passive Metal 1. **Low current density** (typically 10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁸ A/cm²) despite the metal being far from its reversible potential. 2. **Abrupt current drop** at the **critical passivation potential (E_crit / E_F)**. 3. **Wide passive range** of potential where the metal remains passive. 4. **Film recovery capability** — if the passive film is mechanically damaged (in an oxidizing environment), it spontaneously re-forms. ## 1.4 Standard Metrics to Remember | Metric | Typical Value | Significance | |---|---|---| | Passive film thickness | **2 – 10 nm** | A few atomic layers only | | Passive current density (i_pass) | **10⁻⁶ – 10⁻⁸ A/cm²** | Corrosion rate ≪ active state | | Critical current density (i_crit) | **10⁻⁴ – 10⁻² A/cm²** | Peak current before passivation | | Passive range (ΔE_passive) | **0.5 – 1.5 V** | Stability window of passivity | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Anodic Polarization Curve of a Passivating Metal The single most important graphical tool for understanding passivity is the **anodic polarization curve** of a metal in a suitable electrolyte. When an active-passive metal (e.g., Fe in H₂SO₄) is anodically polarized, four distinct regions appear: ### Step-by-Step Logic of the Four Regions **Region 1 – Active Region (AB):** The metal dissolves according to its normal anodic dissolution reaction, e.g., Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻. Current density rises exponentially with potential following Tafel kinetics. The metal is corroding actively. **Region 2 – Active-to-Passive Transition (BC):** As potential increases, the surface coverage by adsorbed oxygen or oxide nuclei grows. The current density reaches a **maximum called the Critical Current Density (i_crit)** at point C. This is the *peak dissolution rate* — the metal is at its most destructive here. **Point C – The Critical/Flade Point:** - E_crit = critical passivation potential (also called **Flade potential**, E_F, in honor of Flade who first reported it in 1911). - i_crit = critical current density. - Beyond this point, the oxide film becomes continuous and protective. **Region 3 – Passive Region (DE):** A sudden, dramatic drop in current density by 4–6 orders of magnitude occurs. The metal surface is now covered by a thin, adherent oxide film. Current density remains nearly constant at the low **passive current density (i_pass)** over a wide potential range. This is the **engineering zone** where we want our metal to operate. **Region 4 – Transpassive Region (EF):** At sufficiently high potentials, the passive film breaks down due to: - **Oxygen evolution** (in neutral/acidic solutions) at E > 1.23 V(SHE). - **Oxidation of the oxide itself** (e.g., CrO₃ formation from Cr₂O₃ in stainless steels). - **Pitting or transpassive dissolution** — the metal resumes active dissolution. > [!NOTE] > **The Three "Magic" Current Densities to Memorize for KTU:** > 1. **i_corr** — corrosion current density (mixed potential) > 2. **i_crit** — critical passivation current density (peak of active region) > 3. **i_pass** — passive current density (plateau of passive region) ## 2.2 The Flade Potential (E_F) The **Flade potential** is the most negative potential at which the passive film is *just stable*. Below E_F, the film dissolves; above E_F, the film is stable. Flade first measured it for iron in H₂SO₄ and obtained E_F ≈ +0.58 V (SHE). The Flade potential is **pH-dependent** and follows the **Nernstian relationship**: $$E_F = E_F^{\circ} - 0.059 \cdot pH \quad \text{(at 25 °C)}$$ where E_F° is the standard Flade potential (characteristic of the metal-oxide system). For example, for iron: **E_F° ≈ +0.58 V (SHE)**, giving: $$E_F = 0.58 - 0.059 \cdot pH \;\; (V vs SHE)$$ ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Equation | Physical Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E_F = E_F^{\circ} - 0.059\,pH$ | Flade potential vs pH (Nernst) | V (SHE) | | 2 | $\eta = E - E_{eq}$ | Overpotential driving dissolution | V | | 3 | $\eta_a = \beta_a \log\frac{i}{i_0}$ | Anodic Tafel equation (active region) | V | | 4 | $\eta_c = \beta_c \log\frac{i_0}{i}$ | Cathodic Tafel equation | V | | 5 | $i_{crit} = nFk_c\,C_{H^+}^a \exp\!\left(\frac{\alpha F E_F}{RT}\right)$ | Critical current density (kinetic) | A/cm² | | 6 | $i_{pass} \approx 10^{-6}\ \text{to}\ 10^{-8}$ | Passive current density range | A/cm² | | 7 | $r_{mpy} = \frac{0.129 \cdot i_{pass} \cdot M}{n \cdot \rho}$ | Corrosion rate from passive i | mpy | | 8 | $\Delta E_{passive} = E_{trans} - E_F$ | Width of passive range | V | | 9 | $M\cdot t = \frac{i_{pass} \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Mass loss during passivity | g | | 10 | $\log i_{pass} = \log i_0 + \frac{\alpha F(E - E_F)}{2.303\,RT}$ | Passive-region current dependence | A/cm² | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Pipe-Free Notation:** > All absolute values and Nernst pH terms in the formulas above use the standard $\vert$ symbol OUTSIDE the markdown table cells. This prevents the markdown parser from breaking the table grid. ## 2.4 The Two Modern Theories of Passivity ### Theory 1: Oxide Film Theory (Evans – 1927) A continuous, three-dimensional oxide layer (3–10 nm thick) physically separates the metal from the electrolyte. The film is an electronic insulator or poor conductor, so ion transfer across it is the rate-limiting step. **Evidence for the Film Theory:** - Passive films can be detected by ellipsometry, XPS, AES, and TEM. - Film thickness correlates with applied potential (linear growth of ~2 nm/V). - Films are stoichiometric compounds (e.g., Cr₂O₃, Al₂O₃, TiO₂, Fe₂O₃/Fe₃O₄). ### Theory 2: Adsorption Theory (Uhlig – 1940s) Passivity arises from a chemisorbed monolayer (or sub-monolayer) of oxygen, OH⁻, or water molecules on the metal surface. No bulk 3D oxide is required — the chemisorbed species blocks active sites. **Evidence for the Adsorption Theory:** - Some metals (e.g., Ni in H₂SO₄) passivate with films only **1 atom thick**. - The amount of oxygen required for passivation of iron is ~0.01 C/cm², corresponding to ~0.5 monolayer. - The transition from active to passive occurs sharply, more consistent with surface coverage saturation than with bulk film growth. > [!NOTE] > **Modern Consensus:** For most engineering alloys (Fe-Cr, Ni-Cr, stainless steels), passivity is a **hybrid** phenomenon — a chemisorbed oxygen monolayer at the metal/film interface, covered by a thicker (3–10 nm) defective oxide. Both theories are not mutually exclusive. ## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Passivity | Industry | Application | Passive System | |---|---|---| | Aerospace | Aircraft skin, rivets | Al 2024, Ti-6Al-4V | | Chemical plants | Storage tanks, reactors | SS 304, SS 316, Hastelloy | | Biomedical | Orthopedic implants | Ti, Ti-6Al-4V | | Marine | Propeller shafts, valves | Monel 400, SS 316 | | Nuclear | Fuel cladding | Zircaloy-4 (ZrO₂ film) | | Architecture | Building facades | Anodized aluminum, SS 304 | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Flade Potential as a Nernst Equation We derive the pH-dependence of the Flade potential for the iron passivation reaction: $$\text{Fe} + \tfrac{3}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 3\,\text{H}^+ + 3\,e^-$$ **Step 1 — Identify the half-reaction and its Nernst form.** For the redox couple with $n = 3$ electrons transferred: $$E_F = E_F^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\frac{a_{\text{H}^+}^3 \cdot a_{\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3}^{1/2}}{a_{\text{Fe}} \cdot a_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}^{3/2}}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the standard states.** For pure solid Fe and Fe₂O₃, activities = 1. For dilute aqueous solution, $a_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} \approx 1$: $$E_F = E_F^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{3F}\ln a_{\text{H}^+}^3$$ **Step 3 — Simplify the logarithm.** Using $\ln a_{\text{H}^+}^3 = 3 \ln a_{\text{H}^+} = -3 \cdot 2.303 \cdot \text{pH}$: $$E_F = E_F^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{3F}\bigl(-3 \cdot 2.303 \cdot \text{pH}\bigr) = E_F^{\circ} + \frac{2.303\,RT}{F}\cdot \text{pH}$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate at T = 298 K (25 °C).** $\dfrac{2.303 \cdot RT}{F} = 0.05916\ \text{V}$: $$\boxed{E_F = E_F^{\circ} - 0.059\,\text{pH} \quad (V\ \text{vs SHE})}$$ **Engineering Insight:** The negative sign on the pH term means that **as pH increases (more alkaline solution), the Flade potential shifts in the active (more negative) direction**. This is why iron passivates more easily in alkaline solutions (e.g., passivation of iron in concrete pore water, pH ≈ 12.5, is the basis of the *Bauer-Kleinlogel equation* for rebar). --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Corrosion Rate from the Passive Current Density Given a passive current density $i_{pass}$, derive the corrosion penetration rate in **mils per year (mpy)**. **Step 1 — Start from Faraday's law of mass loss.** Mass dissolved per unit area per unit time: $$\dot{m} = \frac{i_{pass} \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$ where $M$ = molar mass (g/mol), $n$ = electrons per atom, $F$ = 96,485 C/mol. **Step 2 — Convert mass loss to thickness loss.** Divide mass flux by density $\rho$ (g/cm³): $$\dot{x} = \frac{i_{pass} \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}\quad \text{[cm/s]}$$ **Step 3 — Convert cm/s → mpy.** 1 cm/s = $3.154 \times 10^{9}$ mpy × 1 cm (since 1 year = 3.154×10⁷ s, and 1 cm = 1000 mil): $$\dot{x}_{mpy} = \frac{i_{pass} \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho} \times 3.154 \times 10^9 \times \frac{1}{2.54}$$ **Step 4 — Simplify the constant.** $\dfrac{3.154 \times 10^9}{2.54} = 1.288 \times 10^9 \approx 0.129$ when using $i_{pass}$ in $\mu A/cm^2$: $$\boxed{r_{mpy} = \frac{0.129 \cdot i_{pass}\,[\mu A/cm^2] \cdot M}{n \cdot \rho}}$$ **Numerical Example — Stainless Steel 304 in H₂SO₄:** - $i_{pass} = 1\ \mu A/cm^2$ - M(Fe) ≈ 55.85, with Cr/Ni averaging ≈ 56 g/mol - n = 2 (Fe²⁺, Cr³⁺ averaging), ρ ≈ 7.9 g/cm³ $$r_{mpy} = \frac{0.129 \times 1 \times 56}{2 \times 7.9} \approx 0.46\ \text{mpy}$$ Compare to **carbon steel** in the same solution (active) where r ≈ 100–500 mpy. The passive state reduces corrosion by **~3 orders of magnitude**. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: The Mixed-Potential Model for an Active-Passive Metal Coupled to a Cathode **Setup:** An active-passive metal (e.g., Fe in H₂SO₄) is coupled with a cathodic reaction (e.g., H⁺ reduction). The system operating point (E_corr, i_corr) is found at the intersection of the anodic (with passivation) and cathodic polarization curves. Let the cathodic Tafel line be: $$E = E_{eq,c} - \beta_c \log\frac{i}{i_{0,c}}$$ Let the anodic curve be **piecewise**: **Active Branch (E < E_F):** $$E = E_{eq,a} + \beta_a \log\frac{i}{i_{0,a}}$$ **Passive Branch (E > E_F, i = i_pass):** $$i = i_{pass} = \text{const.}$$ **System Behavior (Three Cases):** **Case 1 — Cathode line intersects ACTIVE branch (Q₁ < i_crit):** The metal corrodes actively. The intersection point gives E_corr and i_corr in the active region. Common for low-chromium steels. **Case 2 — Cathode line intersects PASSIVE branch (i_crit < Q₁ < i_pass):** The metal spontaneously passivates because the cathodic limiting current exceeds the critical current. The corrosion rate drops to i_pass. **This is the goal of alloying — adding Cr to Fe.** **Case 3 — Cathode line intersects at i_crit:** The system is at the boundary. The slightest environmental change (more oxidizer, less pH) can shift the metal into the passive region. Stainless steel in aerated H₂SO₄ behaves this way. **Mathematical Condition for Spontaneous Passivation:** $$\boxed{i_{cathodic\ limiting} \geq i_{crit}}$$ This is the fundamental criterion for *alloy design for passivity* — reduce i_crit (through Cr, Ni additions) AND increase the cathodic reaction rate (through dissolved O₂, Fe³⁺, or H⁺). --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Summary of Passivity Parameters for Common Metals | Metal | Electrolyte | i_crit (A/cm²) | i_pass (A/cm²) | E_F (V vs SHE) | Passive Range (V) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Fe | 1 N H₂SO₄ | 1 × 10⁻³ | 7 × 10⁻⁶ | +0.58 | 0.4 | | Cr | 1 N H₂SO₄ | 3 × 10⁻⁴ | 1 × 10⁻⁷ | −0.20 | 0.9 | | Ni | 1 N H₂SO₄ | 1 × 10⁻⁴ | 1 × 10⁻⁶ | +0.20 | 0.5 | | Al | 0.1 N NaCl | 1 × 10⁻³ | 3 × 10⁻⁶ | −1.20 | ~1.0 | | Ti | 1 N H₂SO₄ | 1 × 10⁻⁵ | 5 × 10⁻⁶ | −0.05 | 1.5 | | 18-8 SS | 1 N H₂SO₄ | 1 × 10⁻⁴ | 1 × 10⁻⁶ | −0.10 | 0.8 | > [!NOTE] > **Observation:** Chromium has the most negative E_F (−0.20 V) and the lowest i_pass (10⁻⁷ A/cm²) — which is exactly why **adding Cr to Fe is the single most effective passivity strategy** in metallurgy. --- ## 3.5 Derivation 4: Effect of Alloying on Critical Current Density (Sensitization Criterion) For an Fe–Cr alloy in 1 N H₂SO₄, the critical current density decreases exponentially with Cr content (Brusic-style empirical relation): $$\log i_{crit} = \log i_{crit}^{Fe} - k \cdot x_{Cr}$$ where $x_{Cr}$ = atomic fraction of Cr, and $k$ ≈ 0.06 (at 25 °C). For SS 304 (18% Cr, atomic ≈ 0.18): $$\log i_{crit}^{304} \approx \log(10^{-3}) - 0.06 \times 18 = -3 - 1.08 = -4.08$$ $$i_{crit}^{304} \approx 8.3 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{A/cm}^2$$ This represents roughly a **12× reduction** compared to pure iron, which is the metallurgical basis for the *18-8 stainless steel* standard. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: The Active-to-Passive Transition in Anodic Polarization This Mermaid diagram maps the energy/kinetic states of a metal surface as potential increases, replacing the standard textbook polarization curve with a clean block-flow representation. ```mermaid graph TD stateA["REGION 1: ACTIVE DISSOLUTION

What is the role of chromium in imparting passivity to stainless steels?** **Model Answer:** Passivity is the loss of chemical reactivity of a metal or alloy in a specific environment, where the metal becomes kinetically inert due to the formation of a thin, adherent, and protective oxide film on its surface, even though thermodynamically it is still unstable. Chromium plays a **decisive role** in imparting passivity to stainless steels because: 1. **Chromium oxide (Cr₂O₃) film formation:** When Cr content exceeds ~10.5%, a continuous and self-healing Cr₂O₃ film forms spontaneously on the steel surface in oxidizing environments. 2. **Critical current density reduction:** Chromium alloying exponentially reduces the critical passivation current density ($i_{crit}$), making it easier for the alloy to enter the passive state. For Fe–Cr alloys, $\log i_{crit} = \log i_{crit}^{Fe} - 0.06 \cdot \%Cr$. 3. **Lower Flade potential:** Chromium has a more negative Flade potential (−0.20 V SHE) than iron (+0.58 V SHE), shifting the passive region of the alloy to more negative potentials. 4. **Wide passive range:** The Cr₂O₃ film remains stable over a wide range of potentials (~0.9 V), providing robust corrosion resistance. This is why 18-8 stainless steel (18% Cr, 8% Ni) is the workhorse of the chemical industry. ### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, PECHT 527]** **What is the Flade potential? Derive its Nernstian dependence on pH.** **Model Answer:** The **Flade potential (E_F)** is defined as the critical potential below which the passive film on a metal is unstable and the metal reverts to the active state. It marks the boundary between the passive region and the active-to-passive transition on the anodic polarization curve. Flade (1911) first identified this potential for iron in sulfuric acid. **Derivation:** Consider the equilibrium of the passive film formation reaction for iron: $$Fe + \tfrac{3}{2}H_2O \rightleftharpoons \tfrac{1}{2}Fe_2O_3 + 3H^+ + 3e^-$$ Applying the Nernst equation with $n = 3$: $$E_F = E_F^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{3F}\ln a_{H^+}^3$$ Simplifying using $\ln a_{H^+} = -2.303 \cdot pH$: $$E_F = E_F^{\circ} - \frac{2.303 \cdot RT}{F} \cdot pH$$ At $T = 298$ K: $$\boxed{E_F = E_F^{\circ} - 0.059\,pH \quad (V\ \text{vs SHE})}$$ For iron, $E_F^{\circ} = +0.58$ V, so $E_F = 0.58 - 0.059 \cdot pH$. The negative pH slope means the Flade potential shifts to more active (negative) values as the solution becomes more alkaline — which is why iron passivates readily in concrete pore solution (pH ≈ 12.5). --- ## 5.2 PART B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **Question A: 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PECHT 527]** **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the anodic polarization behavior of an active-passive metal. Label all four regions clearly. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Discuss the **oxide film theory** and the **adsorption theory** of passivity. Compare their relative merits. **[7 Marks]** --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(a) The anodic polarization curve of an active-passive metal (e.g., Fe in 1 N H₂SO₄) is obtained by plotting log(i) versus E as the potential is swept in the noble (positive) direction. The curve exhibits **four distinct regions** plus two transition points. **Region 1 – Active Region (AB):** The metal dissolves actively via the reaction M → M^(n+) + n e⁻. The current follows the anodic Tafel law, $\eta_a = \beta_a \log(i/i_0)$. The metal is in its freely corroding state. **Region 2 – Active-to-Passive Transition (BC):** As potential approaches the Flade potential, oxide nuclei begin forming on the surface. Current continues to rise but begins to deviate from Tafel behavior. At **point C (the critical point)**, the current reaches its **maximum value, the critical current density i_crit**. The corresponding potential is the **critical passivation potential (E_crit) or Flade potential (E_F)**. **Region 3 – Passive Region (DE):** Just past E_F, the oxide film becomes **continuous and protective**, covering the entire metal surface. The current density **collapses by 4 to 6 orders of magnitude** — from i_crit (≈ 10⁻³ A/cm²) to the **passive current density i_pass** (≈ 10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁸ A/cm²). The metal behaves as if it were noble. This region spans a wide potential range (the **passive range**), and is the **engineering zone of stability**. **Region 4 – Transpassive Region (EF):** At sufficiently positive potentials, the passive film is destroyed by: - Anodic oxidation of the oxide itself (e.g., Cr₂O₃ → CrO₃ + O²⁻, soluble). - Oxygen evolution (in acidic solution, E > 1.23 V SHE). - Pitting (especially in chloride-containing media). Current density rises again. The metal is no longer protected. ``` log i │ AB C D E F │ ╲ ╱│─────────│╱ │ ╲ ╱ │ │ │ ╳ │ PASSIVE │ TRANSPASSIVE │ ╱ ╲ │ RANGE │ + O2 EVOLUTION │ ╱ ╲│─────────│ │ ACTIVE i_pass i_rise └──────────────────────────────────────► E E_corr E_F E_trans ``` **Key parameters identified on the curve:** - **i_crit** — critical current density (at point C) - **E_F** — Flade potential (at point C) - **i_pass** — passive current density (plateau DE) - **ΔE_passive = E_trans − E_F** — width of the passive range --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(b) **The Oxide Film Theory (Evans – 1927):** This theory proposes that passivity arises from a **continuous, three-dimensional oxide layer** (3–10 nm thick) that physically separates the metal from the electrolyte. The film is essentially an electronic insulator or poor conductor, and ion transfer across it is the rate-limiting step of any further corrosion. Classic examples: Cr₂O₃ on Cr, Al₂O₃ on Al, TiO₂ on Ti, Fe₂O₃/Fe₃O₄ on Fe. **Evidence supporting the film theory:** 1. Films are detectable by ellipsometry, XPS, AES, TEM, and Mössbauer spectroscopy. 2. Film thickness grows linearly with applied potential (~2 nm/V). 3. Films are stoichiometric compounds with measurable stoichiometry. 4. Removal of the film (e.g., by cathodic reduction) instantly destroys passivity. **The Adsorption Theory (Uhlig – 1940s):** This theory proposes that passivity arises from a **chemisorbed monolayer (or sub-monolayer)** of oxygen, OH⁻ ions, or water molecules on the metal surface. No bulk 3D oxide is required — the chemisorbed species **blocks the active surface sites** required for metal dissolution. The film is essentially one atom thick. **Evidence supporting the adsorption theory:** 1. Some metals (e.g., Ni in H₂SO₄) passivate with films only **1 atom thick**. 2. The amount of oxygen required for iron passivation is ~0.01 C/cm², corresponding to ~0.5 monolayer coverage. 3.
