The active-to-passive transition occurs **sharply** — more consistent with surface coverage saturation than with 3D film growth. **Comparison of Merits:** | Aspect | Oxide Film Theory | Adsorption Theory | |---|---|---| | Film thickness | 3–10 nm (3D) | < 1 nm (2D) | | Best applicable to | Fe, Cr, Al, Ti (thick films) | Ni, early stages of Fe passivation | | Mechanism | Physical barrier | Active-site blocking | | Detection | Easy (XPS, TEM) | Difficult | | Explains | Long-term stability | Initial passivation | **Modern Hybrid View (Consensus):** For most engineering alloys (Fe-Cr, Ni-Cr, stainless steels), passivity is a **hybrid phenomenon** — a chemisorbed oxygen monolayer at the metal/film interface is covered by a thicker (3–10 nm) defective oxide layer. Both mechanisms operate, with the chemisorbed layer providing the initial passivation and the oxide overlayer providing long-term stability. The two theories are therefore **complementary, not contradictory**. --- ### **Question B: 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECHT 527]** **(a)** Define the following terms with respect to passivity, and explain their significance: **(i) Critical current density (i_crit)**, **(ii) Passive current density (i_pass)**, **(iii) Flade potential (E_F)**, **(iv) Passive range (ΔE_passive)**. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** For a stainless steel 304 specimen in aerated 1 N H₂SO₄, the passive current density is measured as $i_{pass} = 1.2\ \mu A/cm^2$. Calculate the corrosion penetration rate in mpy, given that the equivalent atomic weight of the alloy is 56 g/mol, density 7.9 g/cm³, and the effective valence is 2.4. **[7 Marks]** --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(a) **(i) Critical Current Density (i_crit):** This is the **maximum anodic current density** reached during the active-to-passive transition. It represents the peak rate of metal dissolution just before the protective film becomes continuous. It is the single most important design parameter for passivating alloys — a *low* i_crit means the alloy can be passivated with a small anodic (or cathodic) driving force. For Fe–Cr alloys, i_crit decreases exponentially with Cr content. **(ii) Passive Current Density (i_pass):** This is the **steady-state current density** flowing through the metal while it is in the passive state. It is the rate at which the passive film **continuously dissolves and re-forms** at the film/electrolyte interface. Typical values are 10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁸ A/cm². This is the **actual corrosion rate** of the metal during service and is what engineers want to minimize. **(iii) Flade Potential (E_F):** Also called the **critical passivation potential**, E_F is the most negative potential at which the passive film is **thermodynamically stable**. Below E_F, the film dissolves and the metal becomes active; above E_F, the film grows and protects the metal. It follows the Nernstian relation $E_F = E_F^{\circ} - 0.059 \cdot pH$. For Fe, E_F = +0.58 − 0.059 pH (V vs SHE). **(iv) Passive Range (ΔE_passive):** This is the **width of the potential window** over which the metal remains passive, given by: $$\Delta E_{passive} = E_{trans} - E_F$$ A wide passive range indicates a **robust, versatile** passive state. Chromium has ΔE_passive ≈ 0.9 V, while iron has only ≈ 0.4 V. This is why Cr-alloyed materials are preferred in demanding environments. **Significance for Alloy Design:** The four parameters form a **design quartet**. An ideal passivating alloy has: - **Low i_crit** (easy to passivate) - **Low i_pass** (low corrosion rate) - **Negative E_F** (passivates in mild environments) - **Wide ΔE_passive** (stable under service conditions) Stainless steel 18-8 (Fe-18Cr-8Ni) achieves all four, which explains its industrial dominance. --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(b) **Given Data:** - Passive current density: $i_{pass} = 1.2\ \mu A/cm^2 = 1.2 \times 10^{-6}\ A/cm^2$ - Equivalent atomic weight: $M = 56\ g/mol$ - Density: $\rho = 7.9\ g/cm^3$ - Effective valence: $n = 2.4$ - Faraday constant: $F = 96{,}485\ C/mol$ **Required:** Corrosion penetration rate in **mpy** (mils per year). **Step 1 — Use the standard corrosion rate formula.** $$r_{mpy} = \frac{0.129 \cdot i_{pass}\,[\mu A/cm^2] \cdot M}{n \cdot \rho}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.** $$r_{mpy} = \frac{0.129 \times 1.2 \times 56}{2.4 \times 7.9}$$ **Step 3 — Calculate the numerator.** $$0.129 \times 1.2 \times 56 = 0.1548 \times 56 = 8.669$$ **Step 4 — Calculate the denominator.** $$2.4 \times 7.9 = 18.96$$ **Step 5 — Compute the final ratio.** $$r_{mpy} = \frac{8.669}{18.96} = 0.457\ \text{mpy}$$ **Step 6 — Verify units and physical sanity.** For stainless steel, an mpy of ~0.5 corresponds to a **very low corrosion rate** (excellent service performance), which is consistent with the expected behavior of SS 304 in moderately oxidizing sulfuric acid. $$\boxed{r_{mpy} \approx 0.46\ \text{mpy}}$$ **Engineering Interpretation:** 0.46 mpy translates to a service life of: - Wall thickness 5 mm → service life ≈ 5 / (0.46 × 0.0254) ≈ **428 years** (purely uniform corrosion basis). - This is the *theoretical upper bound*; in practice, **localized attack (pitting, crevice corrosion)** at chloride-containing spots limits the service life to 20–30 years, which is why SS 316 (with Mo) is preferred over SS 304 in marine service. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Do NOT confuse i_crit with i_pass.** i_crit is the *peak* current before passivation; i_pass is the *plateau* current *after* passivation. Examiners commonly deduct 2 marks for this confusion. > 2. **Always include units** in the polarization curve sketch — log(i) [A/cm²] on the y-axis, E [V vs SHE] on the x-axis. > 3. **The Flade potential is a Nernstian quantity** — students frequently forget the negative pH slope. If you write $E_F = E_F^{\circ} + 0.059 pH$, you will lose 1 mark. > 4. **For numerical problems, do not forget the constant 0.129** in the mpy formula and the unit conversion of $i_{pass}$ from $\mu A/cm^2$ to $A/cm^2$ if you use SI forms. > 5. **Mention both theories** of passivity and explicitly state the **modern hybrid view** — KTU examiners reward students who show awareness of the current scientific consensus. --- ## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST ### Definitions You Must Know - ✅ **Passivity** — Loss of chemical reactivity due to protective surface film. - ✅ **Flade potential (E_F)** — Critical potential separating active and passive states. - ✅ **Critical current density (i_crit)** — Peak current at the active-to-passive transition. - ✅ **Passive current density (i_pass)** — Steady-state current in the passive region. - ✅ **Passive range (ΔE_passive)** — Potential window of film stability. - ✅ **Transpassive region** — High-potential film breakdown zone. ### Equations You Must Memorize - ✅ $E_F = E_F^{\circ} - 0.059\,pH$ (Flade potential Nernst equation). - ✅ $\eta_a = \beta_a \log(i / i_0)$ (Anodic Tafel equation in active region). - ✅ $r_{mpy} = \dfrac{0.129 \cdot i \cdot M}{n \cdot \rho}$ (Corrosion rate in mpy). - ✅ $\log i_{crit} = \log i_{crit}^{Fe} - 0.06 \cdot \%Cr$ (Cr-alloying effect). - ✅ Condition for spontaneous passivation: $i_{cathode\ limiting} \geq i_{crit}$. ### Theories You Must Know - ✅ **Oxide Film Theory (Evans)** — 3D oxide layer, 3–10 nm thick. - ✅ **Adsorption Theory (Uhlig)** — Chemisorbed monolayer, ~1 atom thick. - ✅ **Modern Hybrid View** — Chemisorbed layer + 3D oxide overlayer. ### Materials & Their Passive Films - ✅ **Fe** → Fe₂O₃ / Fe₃O₄ (E_F = +0.58 − 0.059 pH) - ✅ **Cr** → Cr₂O₃ (E_F = −0.20 V) - ✅ **Ni** → NiO (E_F = +0.20 V) - ✅ **Al** → Al₂O₃ (E_F = −1.20 V) - ✅ **Ti** → TiO₂ (E_F = −0.05 V) - ✅ **SS 304** → Mixed Cr-Fe-Ni spinel oxide ### Factors That Affect Passivity - ✅ **Promote passivity:** oxidizing environment, Cr/Ni/Mo alloying, alkaline pH, higher potential. - ✅ **Destabilize passivity:** chloride/bromide ions, elevated temperature, reducing conditions, low pH. ### Industrial Applications to Mention in Any Theory Question - ✅ Stainless steel tanks and reactors (304, 316) - ✅ Aluminum aircraft structures (2024, 7075) - ✅ Titanium biomedical implants - ✅ Zircaloy-4 nuclear fuel cladding - ✅ Anodized aluminum architectural panels ### Common Exam Traps - ⚠ Do not equate **passive metals** with **noble metals** — they differ thermodynamically. - ⚠ The **passive film is not a paint** — it is a self-generated, electronically insulating oxide. - ⚠ **Chloride ions** are the *Achilles heel* of passive alloys — they cause pitting. - ⚠ **Transpassive corrosion ≠ pitting** — transpassive is uniform film dissolution at high E, pitting is localized Cl⁻ attack at low E.

topics.title: Environmental effects - effect of oxygen and oxidisers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT527 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Environmental effects - effect of oxygen and oxidisers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Environmental Effects in Corrosion** refer to the influence exerted by the *surrounding medium* (the electrolyte and dissolved species) on the **kinetics** and **thermodynamics** of electrochemical corrosion reactions. The two most influential environmental species in aqueous corrosion are **dissolved oxygen (O₂)** and **dissolved oxidising agents (oxidizers)**.

**Dissolved Oxygen (DO)** acts as the **primary cathodic reactant** in neutral and near-neutral aerated aqueous environments. It is the most prevalent cathodic depolarizer and its presence fundamentally changes the corrosion mechanism from *hydrogen evolution* to *oxygen reduction*.

**Oxidisers** are chemical species (e.g., Fe³⁺, Cu²⁺, H₂O₂, NO₃⁻, Cr₂O₇²⁻, ClO₃⁻, dissolved O₃) that accept electrons from the metal surface. They are characterized by a **positive reduction potential (E° > 0)** relative to the Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE) and drive corrosion by providing alternative, often more aggressive, cathodic reactions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Thief and the Accomplice"**
> Imagine a metal immersed in water as a *safe* containing valuables (electrons). A corrosion cell is a *heist*:
> - The **metal (anode)** is the safe being cracked open.
> - The **cathode site** is the getaway car waiting to receive the stolen electrons.
> - **Dissolved O₂** and **oxidizers** are the *accomplices* of the cathodic site. Without them, the cathodic site has nowhere to dump the electrons → corrosion **stops**.
> - **More O₂ = more accomplices = faster heist** (in many cases). But some oxidizers are *tricky* — they can also form a *protective film* on the metal (like a bodyguard) and actually **reduce** corrosion. This dual nature is the key to Module 1.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Standard reduction potential of O₂ | E°(O₂/OH⁻) | **+0.401 V** | V vs SHE |
| Standard reduction potential of O₂ (acidic) | E°(O₂/H₂O) | **+1.229 V** | V vs SHE |
| Molar mass of O₂ | M | **32.00 g/mol** | g/mol |
| Solubility of O₂ in water at 25 °C, 1 atm | [O₂]_sat | **≈ 8.3 mg/L (≈ 0.26 mM)** | mg/L |
| Faraday constant | F | **96,485 C/equiv** | C/mol |
| Universal gas constant | R | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** | J/(mol·K) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanism of Oxygen Reduction (Cathodic Reaction)

In the absence of O₂, corrosion in acidic solution proceeds by **hydrogen evolution**:

$$2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2 \uparrow \quad E^o = 0.000 \, V$$

In **aerated neutral / alkaline solutions**, the cathodic reaction is overwhelmingly **oxygen reduction**:

$$O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^- \quad E^o = +0.401 \, V \text{ (vs SHE)}$$

In **aerated acidic solutions**:

$$O_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^- \rightarrow 2H_2O \quad E^o = +1.229 \, V \text{ (vs SHE)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why O₂ dominates:** The reduction potential of O₂ is significantly more positive than that of H⁺, so thermodynamically, O₂ is the *preferred* electron acceptor whenever both are present. The corrosion rate in aerated conditions is therefore **usually higher** than in deaerated conditions of the same pH.

### 2.2 Classification of Environmental Effects

**Step-by-step logic of how environment drives corrosion:**

1. **Oxygen dissolves** into the electrolyte from atmosphere (or is pre-dissolved).
2. **O₂ diffuses** to the metal surface through the boundary layer (Nernst diffusion layer, δ).
3. At the **cathode**, O₂ is reduced — this is the *rate-determining step* in many practical situations.
4. **Metal dissolves** at the anode: M → M^(n+) + ne⁻.
5. **M^(n+)** may hydrolyze → pH changes → secondary effects (e.g., formation of oxide/hydroxide film).
6. The system reaches a **mixed (corrosion) potential, E_corr**, where anodic and cathodic currents are equal.

### 2.3 Effect of Oxidizers — The Dual Role

Oxidisers influence corrosion in **two opposing ways**:

| Role | Mechanism | Effect on Corrosion Rate |
|---|---|---|
| **Cathodic Depolarizer** | Provides additional cathodic reactant with high E° | **Increases** corrosion |
| **Passivator** | Raises E_corr into passive region (Pourbaix) by forming protective oxide | **Decreases** corrosion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Concept — "The Oxidizer Rule" (Pourbaix/Schwerdtfeger):**
> An oxidizer is a **corrosion accelerator** if the corrosion potential E_corr lies in the **active** region of the Pourbaix diagram, and a **corrosion inhibitor** (passivator) if E_corr lies in the **passive** region. The boundary is defined by the **primary passive potential**, E_pp.

### 2.4 Differential Aeration Cell (Oxygen Concentration Cell)

A **differential aeration cell** forms when two areas of the same metal in the same electrolyte are exposed to **different concentrations of dissolved oxygen**.

- The **low-O₂ area** becomes the **anode** (corrodes).
- The **high-O₂ area** becomes the **cathode** (protected).

$$\text{Anode (low O}_2\text{):} \quad M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^-$$

$$\text{Cathode (high O}_2\text{):} \quad O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world example:** A water drop resting on a steel surface. The center (covered by the drop, low O₂) corrodes, while the outer edge (exposed to air, high O₂) remains relatively protected. This is a classic cause of **pitting corrosion under deposits**.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following equations for KTU ESE 2024.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{O_2/OH^-} = E^o + \dfrac{0.0591}{4} \log \dfrac{P_{O_2}}{[OH^-]^4}$ | Nernst equation for O₂ reduction (alkaline) | V |
| 2 | $E_{O_2/H_2O} = E^o + \dfrac{0.0591}{4} \log \dfrac{P_{O_2} \cdot [H^+]^4}{1}$ | Nernst equation for O₂ reduction (acidic) | V |
| 3 | $[O_2]_{sat} = k_H \cdot P_{O_2}$ | Henry's law for O₂ solubility | mol/L |
| 4 | $i_L = \dfrac{n F D_{O_2} [O_2]}{\delta}$ | Limiting diffusion current for O₂ (cathodic control) | A/m² |
| 5 | $E_{corr} = \dfrac{E_a + E_c}{2}$ (approx, equal Tafel slopes) | Mixed (corrosion) potential | V |
| 6 | $CR = \dfrac{0.00327 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho}$ | Corrosion rate from corrosion current | mm/yr |
| 7 | $\text{Corrosion ratio} = \dfrac{[O_2] \text{ in test}}{[O_2] \text{ saturated}}$ | Measure of aeration | dimensionless |

Where:
- $D_{O_2}$ = diffusion coefficient of O₂ in water ≈ **1.9 × 10⁻⁹ m²/s** at 25 °C
- $\delta$ = Nernst diffusion layer thickness ≈ **0.1 – 0.5 mm**
- $k_H$ = Henry's constant for O₂ ≈ **1.3 × 10⁻³ mol/(L·atm)** at 25 °C
- $EW$ = equivalent weight of metal
- $\rho$ = density of metal

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cooling water systems** in thermal power plants (KTU-relevant for B.Tech Mechanical/Chemical): DO control using deaerators and mechanical degassing.
- **Pipeline corrosion** in oil & gas (KTU Chemical/Civil): O₂ scavengers (Na₂SO₃, hydrazine).
- **Boiler feedwater**: residual O₂ must be < **7 ppb** to prevent pitting.
- **Marine structures**: tidal zones experience fluctuating DO → differential aeration.
- **Storage tanks**: water-line corrosion due to differential aeration.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Nernst Equation for O₂ Reduction in Neutral Water

We start with the half-reaction in alkaline medium:

$$O_2(g) + 2H_2O(l) + 4e^- \rightleftharpoons 4OH^-(aq) \quad E^o = +0.401 \, V$$

**Step 1:** Write the general Nernst equation.

$$E = E^o - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

**Step 2:** Identify n (number of electrons transferred).

$$n = 4$$

**Step 3:** Construct the reaction quotient Q (omit pure liquids).

$$Q = \dfrac{[OH^-]^4}{P_{O_2}}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the Nernst equation at standard temperature T = 298 K.

$$E = E^o - \dfrac{(8.314)(298)}{(4)(96485)} \ln \dfrac{[OH^-]^4}{P_{O_2}}$$

**Step 5:** Convert the natural logarithm to log₁₀ and simplify the prefactor.

$$E = E^o - \dfrac{0.0591}{4} \log_{10} \dfrac{[OH^-]^4}{P_{O_2}}$$

**Step 6:** Expand the logarithm using log(a⁴) = 4 log(a).

$$E = +0.401 - \dfrac{0.0591}{4} \left( 4 \log [OH^-] - \log P_{O_2} \right)$$

**Step 7:** Cancel the 4 in the denominator with the exponent 4.

$$E = +0.401 - 0.0591 \log [OH^-] + 0.01478 \log P_{O_2}$$

**Step 8:** Substitute $[OH^-] = \dfrac{K_w}{[H^+]} = \dfrac{10^{-14}}{[H^+]}$ to express E in terms of pH.

$$E = +0.401 - 0.0591 \log \left( \dfrac{10^{-14}}{[H^+]} \right) + 0.01478 \log P_{O_2}$$

**Step 9:** Simplify — $\log(10^{-14}/[H^+]) = -14 + pH$.

$$E = +0.401 - 0.0591(-14 + pH) + 0.01478 \log P_{O_2}$$

**Step 10:** Final compact form (assuming P_O₂ = 1 atm).

$$E = +0.401 + 0.827 - 0.0591 \cdot pH$$

$$\boxed{E = +1.228 - 0.0591 \cdot pH \quad \text{(V vs SHE, } P_{O_2} = 1 \text{ atm)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a crucial result: **E decreases by 59.1 mV for every unit increase in pH** for the O₂ reduction reaction. This is why the Pourbaix diagram boundaries for O₂ are sloped lines with slope **−0.0591 V/pH**.

### 3.2 Derivation: Limiting Current Density for Oxygen Diffusion

In many practical cases, corrosion is **cathodically controlled** by O₂ diffusion through the boundary layer.

**Step 1:** Fick's first law gives the flux of O₂ to the metal surface.

$$J_{O_2} = -D_{O_2} \dfrac{d[O_2]}{dx}$$

**Step 2:** Linearize the concentration gradient across the diffusion layer δ, assuming [O₂] = 0 at the surface (cathode is so fast that all arriving O₂ is consumed).

$$J_{O_2} = \dfrac{D_{O_2} \cdot [O_2]_{bulk}}{\delta}$$

**Step 3:** Convert molar flux to current density using Faraday's law (n = 4 electrons per O₂).

$$i_L = n F J_{O_2} = \dfrac{n F D_{O_2} [O_2]_{bulk}}{\delta}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute values for O₂ in water at 25 °C: $D_{O_2} = 1.9 \times 10^{-9}$ m²/s, $\delta \approx 3 \times 10^{-4}$ m.

$$i_L = \dfrac{(4)(96485)(1.9 \times 10^{-9})(0.26 \times 10^{-3})}{3 \times 10^{-4}}$$

**Step 5:** Calculate.

$$i_L \approx 6.3 \times 10^{-2} \text{ A/m}^2 \approx 63 \, \mu A/cm^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** This ~60 μA/cm² value is the **maximum** corrosion current that can be sustained by O₂ diffusion alone under stagnant conditions. This is the basis for saying *"corrosion rate is often controlled by how fast O₂ can arrive at the surface."*

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A carbon steel pipe (Fe, EW = 28, ρ = 7.87 g/cm³) is exposed to aerated water at 25 °C. The corrosion current density is measured as $i_{corr} = 100 \, \mu A/cm^2$. Calculate the corrosion rate in **mm/year** (mpy).

**Step 1:** Recall the corrosion rate equation.

$$CR = \dfrac{0.00327 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho}$$

**Step 2:** Convert units: $i_{corr} = 100 \, \mu A/cm^2 = 0.1 \, mA/cm^2$.

**Step 3:** Substitute.

$$CR = \dfrac{0.00327 \times 0.1 \times 28}{7.87}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate numerator.

$$0.00327 \times 0.1 = 3.27 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 5:** Multiply by EW.

$$3.27 \times 10^{-4} \times 28 = 9.156 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 6:** Divide by density.

$$CR = \dfrac{9.156 \times 10^{-3}}{7.87} = 1.163 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm/yr}$$

**Step 7:** Convert to mpy (1 mm/yr ≈ 39.4 mpy).

$$CR \approx 1.16 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm/yr} \approx 0.046 \, mpy$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering verdict:** This is a *low* corrosion rate, typical of steel in nearly neutral, oxygen-limited water. If O₂ saturation were higher or flow were stagnant for long periods, the rate would rise proportionally to [O₂].

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Oxidizer Effects

| Oxidizer | E° (V vs SHE) | Effect on Fe at low conc. | Effect at high conc. | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| O₂ (dissolved) | +1.229 (acid) | Accelerates | Accelerates | Cathodic depolarizer |
| H₂O₂ | +1.776 | Accelerates | Accelerates | Cathodic depolarizer |
| Fe³⁺ | +0.771 | Accelerates | Accelerates | Cathodic depolarizer |
| Cu²⁺ | +0.340 | Accelerates | Accelerates | Cathodic depolarizer (deposits Cu) |
| Cr₂O₇²⁻ | +1.33 | Inhibits (passivates) | Inhibits | Passivator (forms Cr₂O₃-like film) |
| HNO₃ (dilute) | +0.96 | Accelerates | Inhibits at high conc. | Dual — passivates Fe at high [HNO₃] |
| ClO₃⁻ | +1.19 | Accelerates | Passivates stainless steels | Passivator |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not memorize only "oxidizers increase corrosion." The dual role (accelerator vs. passivator) based on **Pourbaix E_pp** is a **favourite 14-mark question**. State the **primary passive potential** explicitly.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Environmental Effects on Corrosion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Environmental Species in Electrolyte] --> B[Dissolved Oxygen DO]
    A --> C[Oxidising Agents]
    A --> D[Other Ions Cl SO4 HCO3]

    B --> B1[Cathodic Depolarizer]
    B1 --> B2[O2 plus 2H2O plus 4e yields 4OH]
    B2 --> B3[Corrosion Rate INCREASES]

    B --> B4[Differential Aeration Cell]
    B4 --> B5[Low O2 region becomes ANODE]
    B4 --> B6[High O2 region becomes CATHODE]
    B5 --> B7[Localised Pitting]
    B6 --> B8[Protected]

    C --> C1{Compare Epp vs Ecorr}
    C1 -->|Ecorr below Epp Active Region| C2[ACCELERATES Corrosion]
    C1 -->|Ecorr above Epp Passive Region| C3[INHIBITS Corrosion Passivation]

    D --> D1[Cl ions breakdown passive film]
    D1 --> D2[Pitting Crevice]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow — Mechanism of Oxygen-Driven Corrosion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1 O2 dissolves from atmosphere into electrolyte] --> P2[Step 2 O2 diffuses through boundary layer to metal surface]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3 At cathodic sites O2 plus 2H2O plus 4e reduces to 4OH]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4 At anodic sites M dissolves to Mn plus plus ne]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5 Metal ions hydrolyse and migrate]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6 Corrosion products FeOH2 Fe2O3 form on surface]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7 System stabilises at Ecorr mixed potential]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Differential Aeration Cell (Pitting under deposit)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Metal Surface] --> S2[Region A High O2]
    S1 --> S3[Region B Low O2 under deposit]
    S2 --> S2a[CATHODE O2 reduction]
    S3 --> S3a[ANODE Fe dissolves]
    S3a --> S3b[Fe2 plus migrates outward]
    S2a --> S2b[OH produced locally pH rises]
    S2b --> S2c[Protective FeOH2 may form here]
    S3b --> S3c[Pit grows into metal]
```

### 4.4 Decision Matrix — Oxidizer Action

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[New oxidizer added to system] --> Q2{Metal is active or passive forming?}
    Q2 -->|Active Fe Zn| Q3{Ecorr relative to Epp}
    Q2 -->|Passive forming SS Ti Al| Q4[Oxidizer raises Ecorr into passive region]
    Q4 --> Q5[INHIBITS corrosion passivation]
    Q3 -->|Ecorr less than Epp| Q6[Accelerates corrosion]
    Q3 -->|Ecorr greater than Epp| Q5
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper KTU 2024]**

**Q1. (3 Marks)**
*"What is differential aeration corrosion? Cite one industrial example."*

**Model Answer (3 marks — KTU key):**
Differential aeration corrosion is a form of galvanic corrosion in which the driving EMF arises from a **difference in dissolved oxygen concentration** at two regions of the *same* metal in the *same* electrolyte. The region with **lower O₂** becomes the **anode** and corrodes preferentially, while the region with **higher O₂** becomes the **cathode** and is protected.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cathode reaction:** O₂ + 2H₂O + 4e⁻ → 4OH⁻
> **Anode reaction:** Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻

**Industrial example:** Water-line corrosion in a storage tank, or pitting under a deposit/silt particle on a steel pipe. **(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for half-cell reactions + 1 mark for example.)**

---

**Q2. (3 Marks)**
*"Why does dissolved oxygen accelerate corrosion in neutral aqueous media, and how is its rate-limiting effect expressed mathematically?"*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
In neutral aerated water, O₂ acts as the **primary cathodic depolarizer** because its reduction potential (+0.401 V alkaline / +1.229 V acidic) is far more positive than that of H⁺ (0.000 V). Hence, thermodynamically O₂ is the **preferred electron acceptor**, sustaining and accelerating the cathodic reaction.

The rate is **diffusion-controlled** when the cathodic current is limited by O₂ transport, expressed by the limiting current density:

$$i_L = \dfrac{n F D_{O_2} [O_2]_{bulk}}{\delta}$$

**(1 mark — thermodynamic reason, 1 mark — preferred cathodic reaction, 1 mark — formula for diffusion control.)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1 Comprehensive]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) (7 Marks)** Discuss in detail the **mechanism of oxygen reduction** in acidic and neutral/alkaline media. Write the relevant half-cell reactions and explain why the rate of corrosion in aerated solutions is generally higher than in deaerated ones.

**(b) (7 Marks)** Derive the **Nernst equation** for the O₂/OH⁻ couple at 25 °C and hence obtain the **E-pH expression** showing the slope of the upper stability line of water. Calculate E at pH = 7 and P_O₂ = 0.21 atm (atmospheric air).

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Mechanism of oxygen reduction (7 marks):**

- O₂ from atmosphere dissolves in the aqueous electrolyte (Henry's law). **[1 mark]**
- O₂ diffuses through the Nernst diffusion layer (δ ≈ 0.1–0.5 mm) to the metal surface. **[1 mark]**
- At **cathodic sites**, O₂ is reduced. In **acidic** media:

$$O_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^- \rightarrow 2H_2O \quad E^o = +1.229 \, V \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

- In **neutral/alkaline** media:

$$O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^- \quad E^o = +0.401 \, V \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

- At **anodic sites**, metal dissolves: M → M^(n+) + ne⁻. **[0.5 mark]**
- The system reaches a mixed potential E_corr where i_a = i_c. **[0.5 mark]**
- **Why aerated > deaerated:** O₂ has a much more positive E° than H⁺ reduction, making it a **stronger electron acceptor** → drives a higher i_corr. Also, O₂ consumes electrons that would otherwise be consumed by H₂ evolution. In many practical cases, corrosion is **cathodically limited by O₂ diffusion**, so the rate is proportional to [O₂]. **[2 marks]**

**(b) Derivation of E vs pH (7 marks):**

- Start with the half-reaction:

$$O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^- \quad E^o = +0.401 \, V \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

- Apply the Nernst equation at 25 °C:

$$E = E^o - \dfrac{0.0591}{4} \log \dfrac{[OH^-]^4}{P_{O_2}} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

- Expand:

$$E = +0.401 - 0.0591 \log[OH^-] + \dfrac{0.0591}{4} \log P_{O_2} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

- Substitute [OH⁻] = 10^(pH−14):

$$E = +0.401 - 0.0591 (pH - 14) + 0.01478 \log P_{O_2}$$

- Simplify:

$$E = +1.228 - 0.0591 \cdot pH + 0.01478 \log P_{O_2} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

- Substitute pH = 7, P_O₂ = 0.21 atm:

$$E = 1.228 - 0.0591(7) + 0.01478 \log(0.21)$$

- Calculate:

$$E = 1.228 - 0.4137 + 0.01478 \times (-0.678)$$

$$E = 1.228 - 0.4137 - 0.01002$$

$$\boxed{E \approx +0.804 \, V \text{ vs SHE at pH 7, P}_{O_2} = 0.21 \text{ atm}} \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

- **Physical meaning:** This is the upper stability line of water (b). It slopes at −59.1 mV/pH. **[0.5 mark]**
- **Conclusion:** At pH 7 with atmospheric O₂, the O₂ reduction potential is ≈ +0.80 V — well above H⁺/H₂ (0.0 V) → O₂ is the dominant cathodic reactant. **[0.5 mark]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) (7 Marks)** Explain the **dual role of oxidizing agents** in corrosion with reference to the **Pourbaix diagram** concept of the **primary passive potential (E_pp)**. Classify the following oxidizers as *accelerators* or *inhibitors* for iron in acidic solution: (i) Fe³⁺, (ii) H₂O₂, (iii) Cr₂O₇²⁻, (iv) Cu²⁺.

**(b) (7 Marks)** A structural steel beam (EW = 28, ρ = 7.87 g/cm³) immersed in aerated seawater is found to have a corrosion current density of $i_{corr} = 75 \, \mu A/cm^2$. Calculate the **corrosion rate in mm/yr and in mpy**. If the design life is 30 years, what is the expected metal loss in mm?

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Dual role of oxidizers (7 marks):**

- Oxidizers are species that accept electrons at the cathode (E° > 0 V). They can act as **cathodic depolarizers** *or* as **passivators**. **[1 mark]**
- **Pourbaix concept:** Each metal has a **primary passive potential (E_pp)**. If the oxidizer shifts E_corr to a value **above E_pp**, the metal enters the **passive region** and a protective oxide film forms → **inhibits** corrosion. **[2 marks]**
- If E_corr remains **below E_pp** (in the **active region**), the oxidizer simply provides an extra cathodic reaction → **accelerates** corrosion. **[2 marks]**
- Classification for iron in acid (E_pp for Fe ≈ −0.30 V vs SHE at pH 0): **[2 marks]**
  - (i) **Fe³⁺** — E° = +0.771 V, cannot raise E_corr to passivity in Fe's acid Pourbaix → **Accelerator** (cathodic depolarizer).
  - (ii) **H₂O₂** — E° = +1.776 V, very strong oxidizer, but in active Fe region it acts as depolarizer → **Accelerator** (but can passivate at high [H₂O₂] in alkaline).
  - (iii) **Cr₂O₇²⁻** — E° = +1.33 V, raises E_corr well into Fe's passive region → **Inhibitor** (passivator).
  - (iv) **Cu²⁺** — E° = +0.340 V, less than E_pp for Fe in acid → **Accelerator**; deposited Cu can also form galvanic micro-cells.

**(b) Corrosion rate calculation (7 marks):**

- Corrosion rate formula:

$$CR = \dfrac{0.00327 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

- Substitute $i_{corr} = 75 \, \mu A/cm^2 = 0.075 \, mA/cm^2$, EW = 28, ρ = 7.87:

$$CR = \dfrac{0.00327 \times 0.075 \times 28}{7.87}$$

- Numerator: $0.00327 \times 0.075 = 2.4525 \times 10^{-4}$
- Numerator × EW: $2.4525 \times 10^{-4} \times 28 = 6.867 \times 10^{-3}$
- Divide: $CR = 6.867 \times 10^{-3} / 7.87 = 8.726 \times 10^{-4} \, mm/yr$

$$\boxed{CR \approx 0.873 \, \mu m/yr \approx 0.000873 \, mm/yr} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

- Convert to mpy: 1 mm/yr ≈ 39.4 mpy →

$$CR \approx 0.000873 \times 39.4 \approx 0.0344 \, mpy \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

- **Metal loss over 30 years:**

$$\Delta t = CR \times 30 = 0.000873 \times 30 = 0.0262 \, mm \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

- **Discussion:** This is a *very low* corrosion rate, indicating effective cathodic protection or near-stagnant water. **[1 mark]**
- **Engineering verdict:** Loss of ~26 μm in 30 years is well within design tolerance for a coated beam. **[1 mark]**
- **Note on Cl⁻ in seawater:** Chloride ions can break down passive films, but for plain carbon steel in active state, Cl⁻ mainly increases pitting tendency. **[0.5 mark]**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion** of $i_{corr}$ (μA/cm² → mA/cm²) costs **2 marks** instantly. Always convert before substituting.
> 2. **Sign convention for E vs SHE:** Do not drop the sign. State "V vs SHE" explicitly.
> 3. **In the dual role question**, students often *list* oxidizers but **fail to mention E_pp**. Writing E_pp explicitly is the KTU board's favourite differentiator between 5-mark and 7-mark answers.
> 4. **Nernst derivation** — examiners mark the *expansion step* (Step 6) and the *substitution of pH* (Step 8) separately. Skipping either costs 1 mark.
> 5. **For differential aeration**, the "low O₂ = anode" rule is **counter-intuitive** for many students — they expect the high O₂ area to corrode. **Remember**: cathodic depolarization at high O₂ *consumes* electrons; the *anode* must supply those electrons by dissolving.

---

### 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision bullets — read before entering the exam hall.**

- ✅ O₂ reduction potentials: **+0.401 V** (alkaline), **+1.229 V** (acidic), both vs SHE.
- ✅ Aerated corrosion rate > deaerated rate (in neutral/alkaline) because O₂ is a stronger depolarizer than H⁺.
- ✅ **Nernst equation for O₂/OH⁻** at 25 °C: $E = +1.228 - 0.0591 \cdot pH + 0.01478 \log P_{O_2}$ (atmospheric: $P_{O_2} = 0.21$).
- ✅ **Differential aeration cell:** **low O₂** = anode (corrodes), **high O₂** = cathode (protected). Real example: water-line corrosion, pitting under deposits.
- ✅ **Limiting current density for O₂ diffusion:** $i_L = \dfrac{n F D_{O_2} [O_2]}{\delta}$ — corrosion is cathodically controlled when $i_{corr} \approx i_L$.
- ✅ **Henry's law:** $[O_2]_{sat} = k_H \cdot P_{O_2}$. At 25 °C, $k_H \approx 1.3 \times 10^{-3}$ mol/(L·atm) → ~8.3 mg/L in air-saturated water.
- ✅ **Dual role of oxidizers:** **accelerator** if E_corr < E_pp, **inhibitor (passivator)** if E_corr > E_pp.
- ✅ **Cr₂O₇²⁻, HNO₃ (conc.), O₃** are common **passivators**; **Fe³⁺, Cu²⁺, H₂O₂ (dilute)** are common **accelerators** for Fe.
- ✅ **Corrosion rate formula:** $CR = \dfrac{0.00327 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho}$ — gives **mm/yr** when $i_{corr}$ is in **mA/cm²**.
- ✅ **Slope of Pourbaix stability line of water:** −0.0591 V per pH unit.
- ✅ **Industrial relevance:** deaerators, O₂ scavengers (Na₂SO₃, hydrazine), differential aeration in heat exchangers, pitting under silt/marine growth.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Velocity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT527 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Velocity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In **Corrosion Engineering**, **velocity** refers to the relative speed of a fluid (liquid or gas) moving across a metal surface, which directly governs the rate of mass transport of reactive species (oxygen, H⁺, Cl⁻, inhibitors) to and from the metal–electrolyte interface. It is a critical **mechanical–electrochemical variable** that influences corrosion through three primary mechanisms:

1. **Mass-transport controlled corrosion** (diffusion/convection)
2. **Flow-Accelerated Corrosion (FAC)** — dissolution under protective film
3. **Erosion–Corrosion (E–C)** — mechanical film removal + electrochemical attack

The **rate of corrosion**, expressed as corrosion current density $i_{corr}$ (mA/cm²) or mass loss rate (mm/yr), is a strong function of the linear fluid velocity $v$ (m/s).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Velocity is treated as a *process parameter* in Module 1, not merely a fluid-mechanics variable. Students must link $v$ to the **limiting current density** ($i_L$) and the **mass-transfer coefficient** ($k_m$).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a metal pipe carrying tap water:

- **Stagnant (v ≈ 0):** Corrosion products and dissolved metal ions pile up at the pipe wall, forming a thick, partly protective layer. The corrosion rate is **low and self-limiting** — like a car left in a slow-moving river; rust builds up but doesn't get carried away fast.
- **Moderate flow (v ≈ 1–2 m/s):** Fresh, oxygen-rich water continuously refreshes the surface. The protective film is thin but stable. Corrosion rate **increases** because more oxygen is supplied — like a steady breeze keeping a flame well-fed.
- **High flow (v > critical, typically > 3–10 m/s depending on alloy):** The mechanical shear of the fluid physically strips the protective oxide film. The metal is exposed repeatedly, and corrosion accelerates **drastically** — like sandblasting rust off a wall.
- **Very high flow / local pressure drop:** Bubbles collapse near the surface (**cavitation**), causing pitting and surface hammering.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Unit | Engineering Range |
|-----------|--------|--------------|-------------------|
| Linear velocity | $v$ | **m/s** | 0 – 30 |
| Reynolds number | $Re$ | dimensionless | 10² – 10⁶ |
| Limiting current density | $i_L$ | **mA/cm²** | 10⁻³ – 10² |
| Mass transfer coefficient | $k_m$ | **cm/s** | 10⁻⁵ – 10⁻² |
| Kinematic viscosity of water (25 °C) | $\nu$ | **1.004 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s** | constant |
| Density of water (25 °C) | $\rho$ | **997 kg/m³** | constant |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently test the student's ability to **distinguish** between the regimes of corrosion: activation-controlled, diffusion-controlled, and erosion-dominated. Velocity is the *switch* between these regimes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Regimes of Velocity Effect on Corrosion

### Regime I — Activation-Controlled (Low Velocity, v < 0.5 m/s)
- Corrosion rate is **independent of velocity**.
- Controlled by charge-transfer kinetics (Butler–Volmer equation).
- The anodic and cathodic reactions are slow on the metal surface.

### Regime II — Mass-Transport / Diffusion-Controlled (Moderate Velocity, 0.5 < v < v_crit)
- Corrosion rate **increases** with velocity because the diffusion layer thins.
- The cathodic reaction (typically $\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \to 4\text{OH}^-$) becomes limited by oxygen supply.
- The rate follows: $i_{corr} \approx i_L \propto v^n$ with $n = 0.5$ in fully developed turbulent flow (Levich).

### Regime III — Erosion–Corrosion / Cavitation (v > v_crit)
- The protective passive film is mechanically destroyed.
- Corrosion rate **rises sharply**, often non-linearly.
- Surfaces show characteristic **directional grooving, scallops, or horse-shoe patterns**.

## 2.2 The Limiting Current Density Concept

When the cathodic reactant (dissolved O₂ or H⁺) is supplied entirely by convection/diffusion, the current density saturates at a **limiting value** $i_L$:

$$i_L = n F k_m C_b$$

where:
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred
- $F$ = Faraday constant = **96,485 C/mol**
- $k_m$ = mass transfer coefficient (cm/s)
- $C_b$ = bulk concentration of reacting species (mol/cm³)

## 2.3 The Levich Equation (Rotating Disk Electrode Analogue)

For a rotating disk or fully developed turbulent pipe flow, $k_m$ is related to velocity through the **Sherwood number** ($Sh$):

$$Sh = \frac{k_m D}{D_{diff}} = a \cdot Re^b \cdot Sc^c$$

where:
- $D$ = pipe/hydraulic diameter (m)
- $D_{diff}$ = diffusion coefficient of reacting species (m²/s)
- $Re = \rho v D / \mu$ (Reynolds number)
- $Sc = \nu / D_{diff}$ (Schmidt number)
- $a, b, c$ = empirical constants (e.g., for turbulent flow in pipes: $a = 0.023$, $b = 0.8$, $c = 0.33$ — Dittus–Boelter type correlation)

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|----------|------------------|-------------|
| 1 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \, v \, D}{\mu} = \dfrac{v \, D}{\nu}$ | Flow regime (laminar/turbulent) | Design of piping |
| 2 | $Sc = \dfrac{\nu}{D_{diff}}$ | Ratio of momentum to mass diffusivity | Mass-transfer analysis |
| 3 | $Sh = \dfrac{k_m D}{D_{diff}}$ | Dimensionless mass transfer coefficient | Corrosion rate prediction |
| 4 | $i_L = n F k_m C_b$ | Limiting current density (mass-transport limit) | Diffusion-controlled corrosion |
| 5 | $i_L \propto v^{1/2}$ | Levich-type scaling (turbulent flow) | Predicting $i_{corr}$ vs $v$ |
| 6 | $i_L \propto v^{1/3}$ | Rotating disk / laminar flow | RDE experiments |
| 7 | $CR = \dfrac{K \cdot i_{corr}}{\rho_m \cdot n_{e} \cdot F}$ | Corrosion rate (mm/yr) from current | Faraday's law |
| 8 | $\Delta P = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | Pressure drop (friction loss) | Pumping cost & E–C onset |
| 9 | $v_{crit} \approx \dfrac{C}{\sqrt{\rho_{fluid}}}$ | Empirical critical velocity for E–C | Material selection |
| 10 | $Re_{crit} \approx 2300$ | Laminar-to-turbulent transition | Flow regime identification |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering utility:** Predicting the safe operating velocity of process piping in oil refineries, desalination plants, and nuclear steam generators (where FAC is a major failure mode) is one of the most important industrial applications of these formulas.

## 2.5 Real-World Significance

- **Power plants:** FAC causes wall thinning in secondary side of boilers; corrective action uses *better chemistry control* and *limiting velocity to < 2 m/s in carbon steel tubing*.
- **Desalination:** High-velocity seawater (v > 3 m/s) in copper alloy tubes causes *impingement attack*; design uses *lower velocities or higher-Cu alloys*.
- **Oil & gas:** Multiphase flow with sand particles erodes elbows; API 14E recommends a critical erosional velocity formula.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Limiting Current Density Dependence on Velocity (Levich-Type)

We begin with Fick's first law applied to a **diffusion boundary layer** of thickness $\delta$ at the metal surface:

$$J = -D_{diff} \frac{dC}{dx} \approx D_{diff} \frac{(C_b - C_s)}{\delta}$$

**Step 1:** At the limiting current, the surface concentration of the reacting species is zero ($C_s = 0$), because every species arriving is instantly consumed.

$$J_L = D_{diff} \frac{C_b}{\delta}$$

**Step 2:** Convert molar flux $J_L$ (mol/cm²·s) to current density using Faraday's law:

$$i_L = n F J_L = n F D_{diff} \frac{C_b}{\delta}$$

**Step 3:** Identify the mass-transfer coefficient $k_m = D_{diff} / \delta$:

$$i_L = n F k_m C_b$$

This is the fundamental mass-transport-limited corrosion equation.

**Step 4:** For a **rotating disk electrode**, hydrodynamic theory (Levich, 1942) gives:

$$\delta = 1.61 \, D_{diff}^{1/3} \, \nu^{1/6} \, \omega^{-1/2}$$

where $\omega$ is the angular velocity (rad/s).

**Step 5:** Substituting $\delta$ back into the $i_L$ expression:

$$i_L = n F D_{diff} C_b \cdot \frac{1}{1.61 \, D_{diff}^{1/3} \, \nu^{1/6} \, \omega^{-1/2}}$$

$$i_L = 0.62 \, n F \, D_{diff}^{2/3} \, \nu^{-1/6} \, \omega^{1/2} \, C_b$$

**Step 6:** Recognizing that for a rotating disk $v \propto \omega$ (linear velocity at the disk edge = $\omega r$), we get:

$$i_L \propto \omega^{1/2} \propto v^{1/2}$$

This is the famous **Levich square-root law**: doubling the rotation speed increases the limiting current by a factor of $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it works:** A rotating disk creates a well-defined, thin, uniform diffusion layer whose thickness is controlled solely by $\omega$. Hence the clean $1/2$-power dependence on $v$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mass-Transfer Coefficient for Turbulent Pipe Flow

For **fully developed turbulent flow** in a smooth pipe, the empirical Chilton–Colburn analogy gives:

$$Sh = 0.023 \, Re^{0.8} \, Sc^{0.33}$$

**Step 1:** Expand the dimensionless groups:

$$\frac{k_m D}{D_{diff}} = 0.023 \left( \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} \right)^{0.8} \left( \frac{\nu}{D_{diff}} \right)^{0.33}$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $k_m$:

$$k_m = 0.023 \frac{D_{diff}}{D} \left( \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} \right)^{0.8} \left( \frac{\nu}{D_{diff}} \right)^{0.33}$$

**Step 3:** Group velocity terms. Note that $v^{0.8} \cdot D^{0.8} \cdot D^{-1} = v^{0.8} \cdot D^{-0.2}$, and the remaining exponents involve fluid properties:

$$k_m \propto v^{0.8}$$

**Step 4:** Therefore the limiting current density in turbulent pipe flow scales as:

$$i_L = n F k_m C_b \propto v^{0.8}$$

This is slightly **less than linear** — meaning corrosion rate increases sub-linearly with velocity, but very rapidly in engineering terms.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Velocity vs Corrosion Mechanism

| Velocity Range (m/s) | Flow Regime | $Re$ Range | Controlling Mechanism | $i_{corr}$ Behavior | Surface Morphology |
|----------------------|-------------|------------|----------------------|--------------------|-------------------|
| 0 – 0.3 | Stagnant / laminar | $< 2300$ | Activation-controlled | Constant | Uniform / pitting |
| 0.3 – 1.0 | Laminar | 2.3 × 10³ – 10⁴ | Mixed control | Slow increase | Uniform |
| 1.0 – 3.0 | Transitional | 10⁴ – 10⁵ | Diffusion-controlled | $i_L \propto v^{0.8}$ | Uniform, polished |
| 3.0 – 10 | Turbulent | 10⁵ – 10⁶ | Erosion onset | Sharp increase | Grooving, scallops |
| > 10 | Highly turbulent | > 10⁶ | Erosion-corrosion / cavitation | Very rapid | Pits, horseshoes, gouges |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **critical velocity** ($v_{crit}$) is **material–environment specific**. There is no universal $v_{crit}$ value. The KTU-expected empirical form is $v_{crit} = C / \sqrt{\rho}$ where $C$ is a constant depending on the alloy and the fluid (typically **C = 100–125 for water-based systems in carbon steel**, per API 14E).

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Corrosion Rate from Current Density (Faraday's Law)

Given $i_{corr}$ (A/cm²), the linear corrosion rate $CR$ (mm/yr) is:

$$CR = \frac{i_{corr} \cdot M}{n_e \cdot F \cdot \rho_m} \times 3.15 \times 10^{8}$$

where $M$ = atomic mass (g/mol), $\rho_m$ = metal density (g/cm³), and $3.15 \times 10^8$ is the unit-conversion factor (seconds per year × 10 mm/cm).

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):** For carbon steel ($M = 55.85$ g/mol, $\rho_m = 7.87$ g/cm³, $n_e = 2$):

If $i_{corr} = 1 \text{ mA/cm}^2 = 10^{-3}$ A/cm²:

$$CR = \frac{10^{-3} \times 55.85}{2 \times 96485 \times 7.87} \times 3.15 \times 10^{8}$$

$$CR \approx 11.6 \text{ mm/yr}$$

If velocity is increased and the system moves into the diffusion-limited regime with $i_L = 5$ mA/cm²:

$$CR \approx 58 \text{ mm/yr} \quad (\text{catastrophic})$$

This simple calculation illustrates why **velocity control is a primary corrosion-mitigation strategy**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Effect of Velocity on Corrosion Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fluid velocity v] --> B[Determine Reynolds number Re]
    B --> C{Re less than 2300}
    B --> D{Re 2300 to 4000}
    B --> E{Re greater than 4000}
    C --> F[Laminar Flow]
    F --> G[Activation Controlled]
    G --> G1[Low uniform corrosion]
    G1 --> H[Surface: smooth uniform]
    D --> I[Transitional Flow]
    I --> J[Diffusion Controlled]
    J --> J1[iL proportional to v to power 0.8]
    J1 --> K[Surface: uniform polished]
    E --> L[Turbulent Flow]
    L --> M[Check v relative to vcrit]
    M --> N[v less than vcrit]
    M --> O[v greater than vcrit]
    N --> P[Flow Accelerated Corrosion]
    P --> P1[Surface: scallops and grooves]
    O --> Q[Erosion Corrosion or Cavitation]
    Q --> Q1[Surface: pits, horseshoes, gouging]
    Q1 --> R[Catastrophic material loss]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Mass-Transport Pathway Under Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Bulk Fluid] --> B[Convective Transport]
    B --> C[Hydrodynamic Boundary Layer]
    C --> D[Diffusion Boundary Layer delta]
    D --> E[Metal Surface]
    E --> F[Anodic Dissolution M to power n plus]
    F --> G[Corrosion Products and Metal Ions]
    G --> H[Back into Bulk Fluid]
    D --> I[Limiting Current Density iL]
    I --> J[Corrosion Rate CR]
    K[Fluid Velocity v] --> B
    K --> C
    K --> D
    K --> I
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow Chart — Material/Velocity Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Process Stream Identified] --> Q1{Fluid type?}
    Q1 --> Q2A[Seawater or Brine]
    Q1 --> Q2B[Fresh Water]
    Q1 --> Q2C[Chemical Process Fluid]
    Q2A --> R1[Choose Cu-Ni or Ti alloy]
    Q2B --> R2[Carbon Steel acceptable if v less than 2 m/s]
    Q2C --> R3[Refer to plant corrosion loop data]
    R1 --> S[Estimate vcrit using API 14E]
    R2 --> S
    R3 --> S
    S --> T{Design v less than vcrit?}
    T --> U1[Yes: Proceed]
    T --> U2[No: Increase pipe diameter or select higher alloy]
    U1 --> V[Safe Operation]
    U2 --> W[Redesign]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams are designed as **block-level functional architecture flows** because the physical phenomenon (boundary layer thinning, mass transport) cannot be adequately conveyed by static labels in a Mermaid chart. They map the *interactions* and *decisions* the engineer must traverse — exactly what KTU examiners expect students to reproduce in descriptive answers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**"Explain the effect of fluid velocity on the rate of corrosion with the help of a typical plot of corrosion rate vs velocity."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The corrosion rate vs velocity curve has three distinct regions:

1. **Low-velocity region (v < 0.5 m/s):** Corrosion is under **activation control**. The rate is essentially **independent of velocity** because the cathodic reaction is kinetically limited on the metal surface.
2. **Intermediate-velocity region (0.5 < v < v_crit):** The cathodic reaction becomes **diffusion-controlled**. The rate rises with velocity as $i_{corr} \approx i_L \propto v^{0.5}$ to $v^{0.8}$, depending on flow regime.
3. **High-velocity region (v > v_crit):** **Erosion–corrosion** dominates. The protective film is mechanically removed faster than it can reform, leading to a **sharp, almost linear rise** in corrosion rate.

(Examiner allocates ~1 mark per region; 1 mark reserved for explicitly naming v_crit.)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**"Define flow-accelerated corrosion (FAC). Why is it significant in secondary circuits of power plants?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**FAC** is the accelerated dissolution of a metal surface under the combined action of a **protective oxide film** and a **flowing single-phase fluid**, where the film remains intact (unlike erosion-corrosion) but the dissolution rate is enhanced by mass transport of the dissolving species away from the surface.

**Significance in power-plant secondary circuits:**

- The **carbon-steel feedwater piping** operates at 150–250 °C with water flow of 1–3 m/s.
- Dissolved oxygen, though controlled (< 5 ppb), still drives dissolution of the **magnetite (Fe₃O₄) film** at rates of 0.1–0.5 mm/yr.
- Wall thinning of elbows and bends has caused **catastrophic pipe ruptures** (e.g., the 1986 Surry Nuclear Power Plant accident in the USA was attributed to FAC).
- FAC is mitigated by **higher pH (9.2), oxygen scavenging, and velocity limits (v < 2 m/s for carbon steel).**

(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for mechanism, 1 mark for significance/mitigation.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Derive the **Levich equation** for a rotating disk electrode and show that the limiting current density $i_L$ varies as $\omega^{1/2}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a turbulent flow of seawater ($C_b(O_2) = 8 \text{ mg/L}$, $D_{diff}(O_2) = 2.1 \times 10^{-5}$ cm²/s, kinematic viscosity $\nu = 1.05 \times 10^{-2}$ cm²/s) through a carbon-steel pipe of inner diameter $D = 10$ cm at $v = 3$ m/s, calculate the **limiting current density for oxygen reduction** ($n = 4$) using the Chilton–Colburn correlation. State whether the system is under diffusion control. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

Starting from Fick's first law at steady state, with $C_s = 0$ at the limiting current:

$$J_L = \frac{D_{diff} \, C_b}{\delta} \quad \text{...(1 mark for setup)}$$

Convert to current density:

$$i_L = n F J_L = n F D_{diff} \frac{C_b}{\delta} \quad \text{...(1 mark for Faraday conversion)}$$

For a rotating disk, von Kármán's hydrodynamic analysis gives the diffusion-layer thickness:

$$\delta = 1.61 \, D_{diff}^{1/3} \, \nu^{1/6} \, \omega^{-1/2} \quad \text{...(2 marks for the hydrodynamic expression)}$$

Substituting (1 mark for substitution):

$$i_L = n F D_{diff} C_b \cdot \frac{\omega^{1/2}}{1.61 \, D_{diff}^{1/3} \, \nu^{1/6}}$$

$$i_L = 0.62 \, n F \, D_{diff}^{2/3} \, \nu^{-1/6} \, \omega^{1/2} \, C_b \quad \text{...(1 mark for final form)}$$

Conclusion (1 mark):

> **Therefore $i_L \propto \omega^{1/2}$**, which proves that the limiting current on a rotating disk increases with the **square root of rotation speed**. This is the basis of using RDE to study mass-transport-limited reactions in corrosion.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Calculate Reynolds number** (1 mark):

$$Re = \frac{v D}{\nu} = \frac{(3 \text{ m/s})(0.10 \text{ m})}{1.05 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}}$$

$$Re = \frac{0.30}{1.05 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 2.86 \times 10^{5}$$

This is **fully turbulent** ($Re > 4000$). ✓

**Step 2 — Calculate Schmidt number** (1 mark):

$$Sc = \frac{\nu}{D_{diff}} = \frac{1.05 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}}{2.1 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}} = 500$$

(Diffusion coefficient converted: $2.1 \times 10^{-5}$ cm²/s = $2.1 \times 10^{-9}$ m²/s)

**Step 3 — Apply Chilton–Colburn correlation** (1 mark):

$$Sh = 0.023 \, Re^{0.8} \, Sc^{0.33}$$

$$Sh = 0.023 \, (2.86 \times 10^{5})^{0.8} \, (500)^{0.33}$$

Calculating each term:

- $(2.86 \times 10^{5})^{0.8} = 10^{0.8 \times \log(2.86 \times 10^5)} = 10^{0.8 \times 5.456} = 10^{4.365} \approx 2.32 \times 10^{4}$
- $(500)^{0.33} = 10^{0.33 \times \log 500} = 10^{0.33 \times 2.699} = 10^{0.891} \approx 7.78$

Therefore:

$$Sh = 0.023 \times 2.32 \times 10^{4} \times 7.78 \approx 4150$$

**Step 4 — Calculate $k_m$** (1 mark):

$$k_m = \frac{Sh \cdot D_{diff}}{D} = \frac{4150 \times 2.1 \times 10^{-9}}{0.10}$$

$$k_m \approx 8.72 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m/s} = 8.72 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm/s}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate $i_L$** (1 mark):

Convert $C_b$ to mol/cm³: $C_b = 8 \text{ mg/L} / 32000 \text{ mg/mol} = 2.5 \times 10^{-7}$ mol/cm³

$$i_L = n F k_m C_b = 4 \times 96485 \times 8.72 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$i_L \approx 0.084 \text{ mA/cm}^2$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion** (2 marks):

> The limiting current density is ~0.084 mA/cm², which is **comparable to typical $i_{corr}$ of carbon steel in oxygenated seawater (~0.05–0.1 mA/cm²)**. Therefore, the system is **partially under diffusion control**, and the corrosion rate is sensitive to velocity. Any further increase in $v$ will accelerate corrosion.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the mechanism of **erosion-corrosion**. How is it different from flow-accelerated corrosion (FAC)? Discuss the role of **impingement attack** and **cavitation**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the API 14E empirical formula $v_{crit} = C / \sqrt{\rho}$, where $C = 100$ (SI units) and $\rho$ is the fluid density in kg/m³, find the **maximum allowable velocity** for (i) water ($\rho = 1000$ kg/m³) and (ii) crude oil ($\rho = 850$ kg/m³). Comment on the practical implications. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Erosion–corrosion mechanism** (3 marks):

Erosion–corrosion is the **synergistic combination** of:

1. **Mechanical wear** of the protective oxide film by high-velocity fluid, suspended solids, or droplets.
2. **Electrochemical attack** on the freshly exposed metal.

The two processes accelerate each other — the mechanical action continuously renews the metal surface, while the electrochemical attack weakens the film. The protective layer (e.g., Al₂O₃ on aluminum, Cr₂O₃ on stainless steel) cannot reform fast enough above a critical velocity.

**Difference from FAC** (2 marks):

| Feature | FAC | Erosion–Corrosion |
|---------|-----|-------------------|
| Film state | **Intact**, but dissolves faster | **Mechanically removed** |
| Causes | Solubility of oxide in fluid | High shear, particles, cavitation |
| Typical surface | **Wall thinning**, smooth scallops | **Directional grooving**, gouges, horseshoes |
| Mitigation | Chemistry (pH, O₂) | Material upgrade, lower velocity |

**Impingement attack & cavitation** (2 marks):

- **Impingement** occurs when fluid strikes a surface at near 90° (e.g., at pipe bends, tube inlets). The local stagnation pressure breaks the film; attack appears as **horseshoe-shaped pits** downstream.
- **Cavitation** is the formation and collapse of vapor bubbles in low-pressure regions of high-velocity flow. The implosions generate **microjets at ~100 m/s**, hammering the surface and producing deep, sharp-edged pits.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

The API 14E formula is:

$$v_{crit} = \frac{C}{\sqrt{\rho}}$$

**Case (i) — Water** (3 marks):

$$v_{crit} = \frac{100}{\sqrt{1000}} = \frac{100}{31.62} \approx 3.16 \text{ m/s}$$

**Case (ii) — Crude oil** (3 marks):

$$v_{crit} = \frac{100}{\sqrt{850}} = \frac{100}{29.15} \approx 3.43 \text{ m/s}$$

**Practical implications** (1 mark):

- **Both fluids have similar critical velocities** (~3 m/s), but oil is **slightly more tolerant** because its higher viscosity dampens turbulence near the wall.
- The formula is a **screening tool only** — actual $v_{crit}$ depends on alloy, temperature, solids content, and presence of H₂S/CO₂.
- For **multiphase flow with sand**, the API 14E value must be **further derated** by a service factor (0.5–0.8).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Velocity Topic:**
> 1. **Common mistake:** Confusing FAC with erosion-corrosion. FAC has an *intact* film; erosion-corrosion has a *mechanically damaged* film. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Common mistake:** Writing "$i_{corr} \propto v$" (linear) instead of "$i_{corr} \propto v^{0.5}$ to $v^{0.8}$" (sub-linear in turbulent flow). Examiners expect the *exponent* to be correctly cited.
> 3. **Common mistake:** Forgetting unit conversions. In Part B calculations, marks are lost when students fail to convert $D_{diff}$ from cm²/s to m²/s or $C_b$ from mg/L to mol/cm³.
> 4. **Common mistake:** Applying the API 14E formula to **multiphase or sand-laden** flow without a derating factor. Examiners expect awareness of the **service factor** concept.
> 5. **Common mistake:** Omitting the **Schmidt number exponent** in the Chilton–Colburn correlation. The 0.33 exponent is critical.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Velocity is a mass-transport and mechanical parameter** in corrosion, not just a fluid-mechanics quantity.
- **Three regimes**: activation-controlled (v < 0.5 m/s) → diffusion-controlled (intermediate v) → erosion-corrosion (v > v_crit).
- **Limiting current density** $i_L = n F k_m C_b$ — the *cap* on corrosion rate in the diffusion-controlled regime.
- **Levich square-root law**: $i_L \propto \omega^{1/2}$ (RDE) and $i_L \propto v^{0.5}$ to $v^{0.8}$ in turbulent pipe flow.
- **Chilton–Colburn correlation**: $Sh = 0.023 \, Re^{0.8} \, Sc^{0.33}$ is the *de facto* industry formula for $k_m$.
- **Reynolds number** $Re = \rho v D / \mu$ — defines flow regime; **transition at Re ≈ 2300**.
- **Critical velocity** for erosion-corrosion is **material–environment specific**; API 14E gives $v_{crit} = C / \sqrt{\rho}$ with $C \approx 100$ for water systems.
- **FAC ≠ Erosion–Corrosion**: FAC = film intact + faster dissolution; E–C = film mechanically removed.
- **Cavitation** produces pitting from collapsing vapor bubbles; happens at **local low pressure + high bulk velocity**.
- **Faraday's law** converts $i_{corr}$ (A/cm²) to linear corrosion rate $CR$ (mm/yr); numerical conversion factor is **3.15 × 10⁸ mm·s/(cm·yr)**.
- **Practical rule of thumb:** Carbon steel in neutral water → safe up to **v ≈ 2 m/s**; above ~3 m/s erosion-corrosion risk is significant.
- **Mitigation hierarchy:** (1) lower velocity → (2) better material (higher Cr, Cu-Ni, Ti) → (3) chemical inhibition → (4) cathodic protection.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concentration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT527 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Concentration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Corrosion Engineering, **Concentration** refers to the phenomenon of **Concentration Cell Corrosion** (also called *Differential Concentration Corrosion*). It is a form of electrochemical corrosion that occurs when **two identical electrodes (same metal)** are immersed in an electrolyte, but the two electrodes are exposed to **different concentrations of the same ionic species** (either the metal ion itself, dissolved oxygen, or other active species). This concentration gradient creates a **potential difference**, driving galvanic current and causing accelerated corrosion at the electrode exposed to the **lower concentration** region (the anode).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECHT527 - Module 1):**
> *Concentration cells are electrochemical cells in which the EMF (Electromotive Force) is derived solely from the difference in concentration of the electrolyte or dissolved gas (typically O₂) at the two identical electrodes. They form the basis of differential aeration, one of the most destructive and frequently observed forms of localized corrosion in engineering practice.*

The two principal categories recognized in the KTU 2024 scheme are:
1. **Metal-Ion Concentration Cells** (Salt Concentration Cells)
2. **Oxygen Concentration Cells (Differential Aeration Cells)** — *most common in real engineering failures.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Pond Fish Tank" Model

Imagine **two identical glass tanks** connected at the bottom by a small pipe, both containing the same water but with **dissolved air (oxygen) levels**:
- **Tank A** is being aerated vigorously → High O₂ concentration.
- **Tank B** is sealed and stagnant → Low O₂ concentration.

Even though the water and the tank material are identical, a *driving force* exists. A dissolved oxygen probe placed in each tank would show a **voltage difference**. The metal component immersed in the low-oxygen tank (Tank B) will **lose electrons (oxidize / corrode)**, while the metal in the high-oxygen tank will **gain electrons (reduce)**. In a real bridge or underground pipe, this is exactly what happens under a dust particle, a rivet head, or a paint scratch — the hidden area becomes the anode and corrodes aggressively.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** *Concentration cells do NOT require two different metals.* The same metal can destroy itself simply because its surroundings are non-uniform. This is why a single piece of carbon steel, in a single electrolyte, can still suffer severe localized attack.

**Standard Metrics & Constants Used:**
- **Faraday's Constant (F)** = **96,485 C mol⁻¹** ≈ **96,500 C mol⁻¹**
- **Universal Gas Constant (R)** = **8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹**
- **Temperature (T)** = **298 K** (standard, 25 °C)
- **The Nernst constant (2.303 RT / F)** = **0.0591 V** at 25 °C

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanism — Why Concentration Differences Cause Corrosion

The thermodynamic origin of concentration cell corrosion is the **Nernst Equation**. Every electrode has a potential that depends on the concentration of ions in contact with it. When two identical electrodes see different ion concentrations, they develop **different electrode potentials** — and the system attempts to equalize this potential by flowing current.

### Step-by-Step Logic of Operation:

1. **Identical Metal, Non-Identical Environment:** Two regions of the *same* metal are exposed to an electrolyte containing the *same* dissolved species, but at *different concentrations*.
2. **Potential Difference Establishes:** The region exposed to the **higher concentration** becomes **cathodic** (more positive potential), while the region in **lower concentration** becomes **anodic** (more negative potential).
3. **Current Flows Through Metal:** Electrons travel through the metallic path from anode to cathode.
4. **Mass Transport Occurs Through Electrolyte:** Ions migrate through the solution to complete the circuit and balance charges.
5. **Metal Dissolves at the Anode:** The low-concentration region loses mass (M → M^n+ + ne⁻).
6. **Reduction Occurs at the Cathode:** The high-concentration region supports reduction (e.g., O₂ + 2H₂O + 4e⁻ → 4OH⁻).
7. **If Not Controlled → Localized Pitting / Wall Thinning / Perforation** occurs.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are **board-exam essentials**. Memorize the form, the sign convention, and the units.

| # | Equation (LaTeX) | Physical Meaning / Use | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode}$ | Cell EMF (always positive for spontaneous corrosion) | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $E = E^{0} - \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{nF}\log_{10}\dfrac{[Red]}{[Ox]}$ | General Nernst Equation for a half-cell | Volts (V) |
| 3 | $E = E^{0} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n}\log_{10}\dfrac{[M^{n+}]_{anode}}{[M^{n+}]_{cathode}}$ | Nernst form for metal-ion concentration cell | Volts (V) |
| 4 | $E_{cell} = \dfrac{0.0591}{n}\log_{10}\dfrac{[M^{n+}]_{high}}{[M^{n+}]_{low}}$ | Simplified EMF of a metal-ion concentration cell | Volts (V) |
| 5 | $E_{O_{2}} = 1.229 - \dfrac{0.0591}{4}\log_{10}\dfrac{[OH^{-}]^{4}}{[O_{2}]}$ | Oxygen electrode Nernst equation | Volts (V) |
| 6 | $\Delta E = \dfrac{0.0591}{4}\log_{10}\dfrac{[O_{2}]_{high}}{[O_{2}]_{low}}$ | EMF due to differential aeration (oxygen concentration cell) | Volts (V) |
| 7 | $\Delta G = -nFE_{cell}$ | Gibbs free energy change (spontaneity criterion) | Joules (J) |
| 8 | $i_{corr} = \dfrac{i_{c}\cdot 10^{(E_{c}-E_{corr})/\beta_{c}} - i_{a}\cdot 10^{(E_{corr}-E_{a})/\beta_{a}}}{1 + \beta_{a}/\beta_{c}}$ | Butler–Volmer / Wagner–Traud relation for $i_{corr}$ | A/m² |

**Boundary Conditions & Assumptions:**
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred (valence of the metal ion).
- $[M^{n+}]$ = molar concentration (mol/L or activity).
- Concentrations must be in **compatible units** (both in mol/L, not mixing).
- For oxygen cells, concentration is often expressed in **partial pressure (atm)** or **ppm**.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Concentration cell corrosion is responsible for a huge fraction of field failures, including:
- **Pitting under deposits** on heat-exchanger tubes (called *deposit attack*).
- **Crevice corrosion** under gaskets, bolt heads, and lap joints.
- **Water-line attack** in storage tanks (the area just below the waterline is oxygen-depleted).
- **Soil corrosion** of buried pipelines (differential aeration between wet/dry or loose/compacted soil zones).
- **Tuberculation** in old cast-iron water mains.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> *A single 0.2 V differential aeration potential is enough to drive a corrosion current that can perforate a carbon-steel pipe in less than 6 months under field conditions.*

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Concentration Cell EMF (Metal-Ion Cell)

### Setup:
Consider a cell built as:
$$\text{M} \;\vert\; \text{M}^{n+}(C_1) \;\Vert\; \text{M}^{n+}(C_2) \;\vert\; \text{M}$$
where $C_1$ and $C_2$ are the molar concentrations of the metal ion at the anode and cathode respectively, with $C_1 < C_2$.

### Step 1 — Write the Nernst equation for each electrode

For the **anode** (low concentration, $C_1$):
$$E_{anode} = E^{0} - \frac{2.303\,RT}{nF}\log_{10} C_1$$

For the **cathode** (high concentration, $C_2$):
$$E_{cathode} = E^{0} - \frac{2.303\,RT}{nF}\log_{10} C_2$$

### Step 2 — Subtract to obtain the cell EMF

$$E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode}$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{cell} &= \left[E^{0} - \frac{2.303\,RT}{nF}\log_{10} C_2\right] - \left[E^{0} - \frac{2.303\,RT}{nF}\log_{10} C_1\right] \\
E_{cell} &= E^{0} - \frac{2.303\,RT}{nF}\log_{10} C_2 - E^{0} + \frac{2.303\,RT}{nF}\log_{10} C_1 \\
E_{cell} &= \frac{2.303\,RT}{nF}\bigl(\log_{10} C_1 - \log_{10} C_2\bigr) \\
E_{cell} &= \frac{2.303\,RT}{nF}\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{C_1}{C_2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the KTU 2024 standard condition (T = 298 K)

At $T = 298$ K, $\dfrac{2.303\,RT}{F} = 0.0591$ V, so:

$$\boxed{\,E_{cell} = \frac{0.0591}{n}\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{C_1}{C_2}\right)\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Because $C_1 < C_2$ for a *spontaneously corroding* concentration cell, $C_1/C_2 < 1$ and the logarithm is **negative**. This means $E_{cell}$ is **negative** as written — but KTU convention reports it as the **magnitude** of driving voltage: $E_{cell} = \dfrac{0.0591}{n}\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{C_2}{C_1}\right)$ (high ÷ low) to keep the value **positive** in the answer.

## 3.2 Derivation for Oxygen Concentration Cell (Differential Aeration)

### Half-reactions involved:
- **Cathode (high O₂):** $\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$
- **Anode (low O₂):** $4\text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^-$
- **Metal dissolution at anode:** $\text{M} \rightarrow \text{M}^{n+} + ne^-$

### Step 1 — Nernst potential for the oxygen electrode

For the oxygen reduction reaction (n = 4):
$$E_{O_2} = 1.229 - \frac{0.0591}{4}\log_{10}\frac{[\text{OH}^-]^4}{[\text{O}_2]}$$

### Step 2 — Take the difference between two oxygen zones

For zones of partial pressure $P_1$ and $P_2$ with $P_2 > P_1$ (i.e., the cathodic zone has more oxygen):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta E &= E_{O_2}^{(2)} - E_{O_2}^{(1)} \\
&= \left[1.229 - \frac{0.0591}{4}\log_{10}\frac{1}{P_2}\right] - \left[1.229 - \frac{0.0591}{4}\log_{10}\frac{1}{P_1}\right] \\
&= -\frac{0.0591}{4}\log_{10}\frac{1}{P_2} + \frac{0.0591}{4}\log_{10}\frac{1}{P_1} \\
&= \frac{0.0591}{4}\left(\log_{10}P_2 - \log_{10}P_1\right) \\
\Delta E &= \frac{0.0591}{4}\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\,\Delta E = \frac{0.0591}{4}\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{[O_2]_{high}}{[O_2]_{low}}\right)\,}$$

## 3.3 Solved Analytical Example (Worked Problem)

**Problem:** A copper concentration cell is set up with $\text{Cu}^{2+}$ concentrations of $0.01$ M and $1.0$ M at 25 °C. Calculate the EMF and identify the anode.

**Step 1 — Identify the half-cell with higher concentration**
Concentration ratio: $C_2 / C_1 = 1.0 / 0.01 = 100$

**Step 2 — Apply the Nernst-derived formula** (n = 2 for Cu → Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻)

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{cell} &= \frac{0.0591}{2}\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1.0}{0.01}\right) \\
&= 0.02955 \times \log_{10}(100) \\
&= 0.02955 \times 2 \\
&= 0.0591\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the electrodes**
- The electrode in **0.01 M** solution → **ANODE** (oxidizes, loses Cu → Cu²⁺).
- The electrode in **1.0 M** solution → **CATHODE** (Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu, deposits copper).

**Final Answer:** $E_{cell} = 0.0591\ \text{V} = 59.1\ \text{mV}$ (significant driving force for localized corrosion).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Concentration Cell Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Same Metal, M, in contact with Electrolyte] --> B{Concentration of Aggressive Species}
    B --> C1[Zone with HIGH Concentration of Ions or O2]
    B --> C2[Zone with LOW Concentration of Ions or O2]
    C1 --> D1[Electrode Potential becomes MORE POSITIVE]
    C2 --> D2[Electrode Potential becomes MORE NEGATIVE]
    D1 --> E1[Site acts as CATHODE - Reduction Reaction]
    D2 --> E2[Site acts as ANODE - Metal Dissolves M to Mn+]
    E1 --> F[Electrons flow through metal from Anode to Cathode]
    E2 --> F
    F --> G[Current loop closes via ion migration through electrolyte]
    G --> H[Result: Localized corrosion at the LOW-concentration zone]
    H --> I1[Engineering Examples: Pitting, Crevice, Tuberculation, Water-line attack]
    H --> I2[Prevention: Uniform aeration, proper design, inhibitors, coatings]
```

## 4.2 Comparative Flow — Two Major Types of Concentration Cells

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    J[Concentration Cell Classification] --> K[Type 1: Metal-Ion Concentration Cell]
    J --> L[Type 2: Oxygen Concentration Cell - Differential Aeration]
    K --> K1[Driver: Difference in metal ion activity C1 vs C2]
    K --> K2[EMF formula: 0.0591 by n times log C high by C low]
    K --> K3[Anode is the metal immersed in DILUTE salt solution]
    L --> L1[Driver: Difference in dissolved O2 partial pressure]
    L --> L2[EMF formula: 0.0591 by 4 times log pO2 high by pO2 low]
    L --> L3[Anode is the metal in OXYGEN-STARVED region under deposit or crevice]
```

> [!NOTE]
> *Mermaid limitation note:* The diagrams above are block-functional representations of the electrochemical architecture, replacing what would normally be a physical cell sketch with two beakers and a salt bridge. This is the recommended KTU-style schematic for board-answer sheets, since examiners award marks for **labelled process flow** and **arrow logic** rather than artistic drawings.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a concentration cell. How does it differ from a galvanic cell?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A *concentration cell* is an electrochemical cell in which the EMF is generated entirely by a **difference in concentration** of an ionic species (or dissolved gas such as O₂) at the **two identical electrodes** of the same metal, immersed in the same electrolyte system. *(1 mark)*

**Differences from a galvanic cell:**

| Feature | Concentration Cell | Galvanic Cell |
|---|---|---|
| Electrodes | Same metal | Two dissimilar metals |
| Driving force | Concentration gradient | Difference in electrode potentials (E°) |
| Standard EMF (E°) | Zero | Non-zero |
| Example | Cu / Cu²⁺(0.01 M) ‖ Cu²⁺(1 M) / Cu | Zn / Zn²⁺ ‖ Cu²⁺ / Cu (Daniel cell) |

*(1 mark for the tabulated difference, 1 mark for the example).*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is differential aeration? Give one engineering example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Differential aeration is a specific type of oxygen concentration cell corrosion in which the EMF arises from a **difference in dissolved oxygen concentration** at two points on the *same metal surface* in contact with the *same electrolyte*. *(2 marks)*

The metal in the oxygen-depleted region becomes the **anode** and corrodes preferentially, while the metal in the oxygen-rich region behaves as the **cathode**. *(½ mark)*

**Engineering example:** *Pitting corrosion of a carbon-steel pipe under a sediment deposit in a heat-exchanger tube — the area beneath the deposit is starved of oxygen and suffers accelerated attack.* *(½ mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** mix up the sign convention. Always write $E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode}$ first.
> - **Do NOT** use the metal-ion concentration ratio with the oxygen formula (they use different *n* values: n = 2 or 3 for metals, n = 4 for O₂).
> - **Do NOT** forget to state $T = 298$ K explicitly when using 0.0591 — otherwise examiners deduct ½ to 1 mark.
> - **Always** identify the **anode** explicitly in your answer — many students lose 2 marks by only quoting the EMF.
> - **Unit mismatch:** Ensure concentrations are in mol/L; pressures in atm. Mixing ppm and atm is a common 1-mark deduction.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the Nernst equation for a metal-ion concentration cell and obtain the expression for the EMF in terms of the ionic concentration ratio. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A concentration cell is constructed using silver electrodes in two AgNO₃ solutions of concentration $0.05$ M and $0.5$ M at 25 °C. Calculate the EMF, identify the anode and cathode, and comment on the spontaneity of the cell. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

Consider a metal M in contact with its own ion M^n+ at two different concentrations $C_1$ and $C_2$. The half-cell reaction is:
$$\text{M}^{n+} + ne^- \rightleftharpoons \text{M}$$

The Nernst equation for each half-cell is:
$$E = E^0 - \frac{2.303\,RT}{nF}\log_{10}\frac{1}{[M^{n+}]}$$
$$E = E^0 + \frac{2.303\,RT}{nF}\log_{10}[M^{n+}]$$

*(1 mark for writing the Nernst equation)*

For the **anode** (low concentration $C_1$):
$$E_{anode} = E^0 + \frac{2.303\,RT}{nF}\log_{10} C_1$$

For the **cathode** (high concentration $C_2$):
$$E_{cathode} = E^0 + \frac{2.303\,RT}{nF}\log_{10} C_2$$

*(1 mark for each half-cell expression — 2 marks)*

The cell EMF is:
$$E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{cell} &= \left(E^0 + \frac{2.303\,RT}{nF}\log_{10} C_2\right) - \left(E^0 + \frac{2.303\,RT}{nF}\log_{10} C_1\right) \\
&= \frac{2.303\,RT}{nF}\bigl(\log_{10} C_2 - \log_{10} C_1\bigr) \\
&= \frac{2.303\,RT}{nF}\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{C_2}{C_1}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

*(3 marks for the algebraic derivation — show every cancellation step)*

Substituting $T = 298$ K gives the final working equation:
$$\boxed{\,E_{cell} = \frac{0.0591}{n}\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{C_{high}}{C_{low}}\right)\,}$$

*(1 mark for the final boxed form)*

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Given:** Ag / Ag⁺ cell, $C_1 = 0.05$ M (anode region), $C_2 = 0.5$ M (cathode region), $T = 298$ K, $n = 1$ (for Ag → Ag⁺ + e⁻).

*(½ mark for identifying n and noting T = 298 K)*

**Step 1 — Apply the concentration cell EMF equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{cell} &= \frac{0.0591}{1}\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.5}{0.05}\right) \\
&= 0.0591 \times \log_{10}(10) \\
&= 0.0591 \times 1 \\
E_{cell} &= 0.0591\ \text{V} = 59.1\ \text{mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(2 marks for correct substitution and arithmetic)*

**Step 2 — Identify the electrodes:**

- **Anode:** Silver electrode in **0.05 M** AgNO₃ (lower concentration) → Ag → Ag⁺ + e⁻ (metal dissolves)
- **Cathode:** Silver electrode in **0.5 M** AgNO₃ (higher concentration) → Ag⁺ + e⁻ → Ag (silver deposits)

*(2 marks for correct identification of anode and cathode with the half-reactions)*

**Step 3 — Comment on spontaneity:**

Since $E_{cell} > 0$ (positive, 59.1 mV), the cell reaction is **spontaneous** ($\Delta G = -nFE_{cell} < 0$). The EMF is significant enough to drive measurable corrosion current at the low-concentration electrode, especially in low-conductivity environments. *(2½ marks for spontaneity analysis + ΔG sign)*

**Optional check:** $\Delta G = -(1)(96485)(0.0591) = -5702$ J/mol ≈ -5.70 kJ/mol (highly spontaneous).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model — Internal Choice Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the mechanism of oxygen concentration cell corrosion with a neat block schematic. Discuss FOUR practical engineering situations where this form of corrosion is observed. **(7 Marks)**
