# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Pilling-Bedworth Ratio (PBR)

The PBR is derived from the **volumetric ratio** of the metal-oxide film formed to the metal consumed. It is a metallurgical property that predicts whether the oxide layer will be protective.

### Step 1 — Start with the general oxidation reaction

$$a M + \tfrac{b}{2} O_2 \rightarrow M_a O_b$$

For every **1 mole of metal (M)** consumed, **b/2 moles of O₂** are absorbed, producing **1 mole of oxide MₐOᵦ**.

Wait — we need a simpler stoichiometry. Let us take a metal forming a **divalent oxide** (e.g., Mg, Ni, Cu, Fe→FeO, Zn):

$$2 M + O_2 \rightarrow 2 MO$$

So **2 moles of metal → 2 moles of oxide**, or **1 mole of metal → 1 mole of oxide**.

### Step 2 — Express molar volume of the metal

The molar volume of the metal is its molar mass divided by its density:

$$V_m = \frac{M_m}{\rho_m}$$

For a metal of atomic mass $A$ and density $\rho_m$:

$$V_m = \frac{A}{\rho_m} \quad [\text{in cm}^3/\text{mol}]$$

### Step 3 — Express molar volume of the oxide

The oxide MO has:
- Molar mass = $M_m + M_O = A + 16$
- Density = $\rho_{ox}$

So:

$$V_{ox} = \frac{M_m + 16}{\rho_{ox}}$$

### Step 4 — Form the PBR ratio

$$\text{PBR} = \frac{V_{ox}}{V_m} = \frac{(M_m + 16)/\rho_{ox}}{M_m / \rho_m}$$

$$\boxed{\text{PBR} = \frac{(M_m + 16) \cdot \rho_m}{M_m \cdot \rho_{ox}}}$$

### Step 5 — Generalisation for $M_a O_b$

For a general oxide $M_a O_b$, **$n$ metal atoms** are oxidised to form **1 mole of oxide**. So:

$$n \cdot M + \frac{b}{2} O_2 \rightarrow M_a O_b$$

where $n = a$ (the number of metal atoms in the formula unit). The PBR is:

$$\boxed{\text{PBR} = \frac{M_{ox} \cdot \rho_m}{n \cdot M_m \cdot \rho_{ox}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** In numerical problems, the oxide formula is given (e.g., Al₂O₃, Cr₂O₃, Fe₂O₃, Fe₃O₄). You must correctly identify $n$ (e.g., $n=2$ for Al₂O₃) and use the **correct molar mass** of the oxide.

### Worked Numerical Example — PBR for Aluminium

**Given:** $M_{Al} = 27$ g/mol, $\rho_{Al} = 2.70$ g/cm³, oxide is Al₂O₃ with $M_{ox} = 102$ g/mol, $\rho_{ox} = 3.95$ g/cm³, $n = 2$.

$$\text{PBR} = \frac{M_{ox} \cdot \rho_{Al}}{n \cdot M_{Al} \cdot \rho_{ox}}$$

$$\text{PBR} = \frac{102 \times 2.70}{2 \times 27 \times 3.95}$$

$$\text{PBR} = \frac{275.4}{213.3}$$

$$\boxed{\text{PBR}_{Al} = 1.29}$$

> Since **1 ≤ PBR ≤ 2**, the Al₂O₃ film is **protective and adherent** — this explains why aluminium is highly corrosion-resistant despite being a reactive metal.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Fraction of Atoms Located at Grain Boundaries

This is a critical metallurgical parameter that explains why **nanocrystalline materials** behave so differently in corrosion tests.

### Step 1 — Model a single grain as a cube

Assume each grain is a cube of side $d$ (average grain diameter). The volume of the grain is:

$$V_{grain} = d^3$$

### Step 2 — Define the grain boundary width

The grain boundary has a finite "thickness" or "width," $\delta$, typically $\delta \approx 0.5$ nm = $5 \times 10^{-8}$ cm.

The GB region is the outer shell of the grain, extending inward by $\delta/2$ from each face. For a cube, the GB volume per grain is:

$$V_{GB} = d^3 - (d - \delta)^3$$

### Step 3 — Expand the cubic term using the binomial identity

$$(d - \delta)^3 = d^3 - 3d^2\delta + 3d\delta^2 - \delta^3$$

Therefore:

$$V_{GB} = d^3 - [d^3 - 3d^2\delta + 3d\delta^2 - \delta^3]$$

$$V_{GB} = 3d^2\delta - 3d\delta^2 + \delta^3$$

### Step 4 — Apply the approximation $\delta \ll d$

Since $\delta$ is on the order of 0.5 nm and $d$ is at least 1 µm = 1000 nm, the terms $d\delta^2$ and $\delta^3$ are negligible compared to $d^2\delta$:

$$V_{GB} \approx 3d^2\delta$$

### Step 5 — Compute the fraction

$$f_{GB} = \frac{V_{GB}}{V_{grain}} = \frac{3d^2\delta}{d^3}$$

$$\boxed{f_{GB} \approx \frac{3\delta}{d}}$$

### Step 6 — Numerical sense-check

For $d = 1$ µm = $10^{-4}$ cm, $\delta = 5 \times 10^{-8}$ cm:

$$f_{GB} = \frac{3 \times 5 \times 10^{-8}}{10^{-4}} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} = 0.15\%$$

For $d = 10$ nm (nanocrystalline):

$$f_{GB} = \frac{3 \times 5 \times 10^{-8}}{10^{-6}} = 0.15 = 15\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is why nanocrystalline materials exhibit dramatically different corrosion behaviour** — at 10 nm grain size, 15% of the atoms sit at grain boundaries, completely changing the surface chemistry and electrochemical homogeneity.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Metallurgical Property → Corrosion Consequence

| Metallurgical Property | Root Cause | Corrosion Consequence | Engineering Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fine grain size | High GB area | ↑ General & IGC | Annealing to coarsen grains |
| Cold work | High dislocation density | ↑ Anodic dissolution, ↑ SCC | Stress-relief anneal |
| Sensitisation (Cr-carbide) | Cr-depletion at GBs | Severe IGC | Low-C grades (304L), stabilised grades (321-Ti, 347-Nb) |
| Two-phase alloy | Galvanic micro-cells | Selective phase attack | Single-phase design, add inhibitor |
| High dislocation density | Stored energy | Preferential anodic attack | Anneal |
| HCP structure | Anisotropy | Directional corrosion | Texture control, alloying |
| High purity metal | Fewer inclusions | ↓ Pitting initiation | Use of high-purity feedstock |
| Cold-worked surface | Residual tensile stress | ↑ SCC | Shot peening → compressive stress |
| Large grain size | Lower GB area | ↓ IGC, ↑ SCC in some cases | Avoid over-annealing in SCC service |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Theoretical Density from Crystal Structure

This is a frequently asked KTU numerical — calculate the density of a metal given its crystal structure and lattice parameter.

### Starting Formula

$$\rho = \frac{n \cdot M}{V_{cell} \cdot N_A}$$

where:
- $n$ = atoms per unit cell (1 for BCC, 4 for FCC, 2 for HCP, etc.)
- $M$ = molar mass (g/mol)
- $V_{cell}$ = volume of unit cell (cm³)
- $N_A$ = Avogadro's number = $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ /mol

### For FCC copper

- $n = 4$
- $M_{Cu} = 63.55$ g/mol
- Lattice parameter $a = 3.615 \text{ Å} = 3.615 \times 10^{-8}$ cm
- $V_{cell} = a^3 = (3.615 \times 10^{-8})^3 = 4.725 \times 10^{-23}$ cm³

### Substitution

$$\rho_{Cu} = \frac{4 \times 63.55}{4.725 \times 10^{-23} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}}$$

$$\rho_{Cu} = \frac{254.2}{28.46}$$

$$\boxed{\rho_{Cu} = 8.93 \text{ g/cm}^3}$$

This matches the standard textbook value of Cu density (**8.96 g/cm³**) within rounding tolerance — confirming the validity of the calculation.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **Define the metallic bond and explain how it is responsible for the characteristic properties of metals.**

**Model Answer:**
The metallic bond is the force of attraction between positively charged metal ions (cations arranged in a lattice) and a delocalised "sea" of valence electrons. The bond is **non-directional** and **multi-centered**.

- **Electrical conductivity:** Delocalised electrons act as mobile charge carriers; when an electric field is applied, electrons drift to the anode side.
- **Thermal conductivity:** Mobile electrons transport thermal energy rapidly across the lattice.
- **Malleability & Ductility:** The non-directional nature allows atomic planes to slide over one another without bond rupture.
- **Metallic lustre:** Free electrons absorb and re-emit photons across the visible spectrum, giving the characteristic shine.
- **High melting point:** Strong electrostatic attraction between cations and the electron sea requires large energy input to break.

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **What is the Pilling-Bedworth Ratio (PBR)? How is it used to predict the protective nature of an oxide film?**

**Model Answer:**
The Pilling-Bedworth Ratio (PBR) is defined as the ratio of the molar volume of the metal oxide to the molar volume of the metal from which it is formed:

$$\text{PBR} = \frac{V_{oxide}}{V_{metal}} = \frac{M_{oxide} \cdot \rho_{metal}}{n \cdot M_{metal} \cdot \rho_{oxide}}$$

**Predictive Rules:**
- **PBR < 1** → oxide volume is too small; tensile stresses develop; the film is **porous and non-protective** (e.g., Mg, Ca).
- **1 ≤ PBR ≤ 2** → oxide film is **protective, adherent, and self-limiting** (e.g., Al, Cr, Cu, Ni).
- **PBR > 2** → oxide volume is too large; compressive stresses cause **spalling and cracking** (e.g., Fe₂O₃ at advanced stages).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 1 carries internal choice. Both options (A and B) are given below.

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks)

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1 - Choice A]`
> **(a)** Explain in detail the three common crystal structures of metals (BCC, FCC, HCP) with diagrams. Compare their properties and indicate their typical examples. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Discuss the effect of grain size, grain boundaries, and cold work on the corrosion behaviour of metals. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The three primary crystal structures in metallurgy are:

**1. Body-Centered Cubic (BCC):**
- Atoms at 8 corners + 1 atom at the body centre
- Atoms per unit cell = 2 (corner contribution: 8 × 1/8 = 1; body contribution: 1)
- **Coordination Number (CN) = 8**
- **APF = 0.68**
- Examples: **α-Fe (ferrite), Cr, Mo, W, V, Na, K**

**2. Face-Centered Cubic (FCC):**
- Atoms at 8 corners + 6 face-centres
- Atoms per unit cell = 4 (corner: 8 × 1/8 = 1; face: 6 × 1/2 = 3)
- **CN = 12**
- **APF = 0.74** (most efficiently packed)
- Examples: **γ-Fe (austenite), Cu, Al, Ni, Ag, Au, Pb**

**3. Hexagonal Close-Packed (HCP):**
- 12 corner atoms + 2 face-centre atoms (top, bottom) + 3 mid-layer atoms
- Atoms per unit cell = 6
- **CN = 12, APF = 0.74**
- Examples: **Zn, Mg, Ti, Cd, Co, Be**

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | BCC | FCC | HCP |
|---|---|---|---|
| CN | 8 | 12 | 12 |
| APF | 0.68 | 0.74 | 0.74 |
| Slip systems | Few | 12 (most) | 3 (basal) |
| Ductility | Moderate | High | Low (at RT) |
| Corrosion | Often active | Often passive | Anisotropic |

> FCC is the most close-packed and most ductile; HCP is anisotropic in corrosion; BCC is the least close-packed.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Effect of Grain Size:**
- Fine-grained metals have **larger grain boundary area per unit volume**.
- Grain boundaries are regions of high stored energy and disordered structure.
- **Smaller grain size → more GBs → higher anodic area → ↑ general corrosion rate.**
- The empirical relation is $\text{CR} \propto d^{-n}$ (Hall-Petch analog for corrosion).
- This is critical in **nanocrystalline materials** where up to 15% of atoms are at GBs.

**Effect of Grain Boundaries:**
- GBs are preferred sites for **intergranular corrosion (IGC)** due to:
  - Higher free energy
  - Impurity segregation (S, P, C, etc.)
  - Faster diffusion paths
  - Electrochemical heterogeneity (micro-galvanic cell with grain interior)
- Classic example: **sensitisation of 304 SS** — Cr₂₃C₆ precipitation at GBs creates Cr-depleted zones (< 12%) that cannot passivate → IGC.

**Effect of Cold Work:**
- Increases **dislocation density** (typically $10^{6} \to 10^{12}$ lines/cm²) and residual stress.
- More dislocations = more anodic sites = ↑ dissolution rate.
- Cold-worked surfaces are more susceptible to **stress corrosion cracking (SCC)**.
- Stored energy from cold work makes the metal thermodynamically less stable.
- **Mitigation:** stress-relief annealing, recrystallisation annealing.

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks)

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1 - Choice B]`
> **(a)** What is the Pilling-Bedworth Ratio (PBR)? Derive the expression and classify the protective behaviour of oxide films. Calculate the PBR for aluminium (Al → Al₂O₃) given: $M_{Al} = 27$ g/mol, $\rho_{Al} = 2.70$ g/cm³, $M_{Al_2O_3} = 102$ g/mol, $\rho_{Al_2O_3} = 3.95$ g/cm³. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Explain the metallurgical factors that influence corrosion. How does alloying improve corrosion resistance? Discuss with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Definition:** PBR is the ratio of the molar volume of the metal oxide to the molar volume of the parent metal consumed. It predicts the protective nature of the oxide film.

**Derivation:**

For the reaction $n M + \tfrac{b}{2} O_2 \rightarrow M_a O_b$, with $n$ metal atoms per oxide formula unit:

$$V_m = \frac{M_m}{\rho_m}, \quad V_{ox} = \frac{M_{ox}}{\rho_{ox}}$$

$$\text{PBR} = \frac{V_{ox}}{V_m} = \frac{M_{ox} / \rho_{ox}}{n \cdot M_m / \rho_m} = \frac{M_{ox} \cdot \rho_m}{n \cdot M_m \cdot \rho_{ox}}$$

**Classification:**

| PBR Range | Behaviour | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| < 1 | Non-protective (porous, tensile stress) | Mg, Ca, K |
| 1 – 2 | Protective, adherent | Al, Cr, Ni, Cu |
| > 2 | Spalling, cracking (compressive stress) | Fe (Fe₂O₃ stage) |

**Calculation for Aluminium:**

Given: $M_{Al} = 27$ g/mol, $\rho_{Al} = 2.70$ g/cm³, $M_{Al_2O_3} = 102$ g/mol, $\rho_{Al_2O_3} = 3.95$ g/cm³, $n = 2$.

$$\text{PBR} = \frac{102 \times 2.70}{2 \times 27 \times 3.95}$$

$$\text{PBR} = \frac{275.4}{213.3}$$

$$\boxed{\text{PBR}_{Al} = 1.29}$$

Since **1 < 1.29 < 2**, the Al₂O₃ film is **protective** — this explains aluminium's excellent atmospheric corrosion resistance despite its high reactivity in the galvanic series.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

The metallurgical factors that influence corrosion are:

**1. Crystal Structure:**
- FCC metals (Cu, Ni, Al) are generally more corrosion-resistant than BCC metals (α-Fe) due to closer packing and fewer active sites.
- HCP metals exhibit **anisotropic corrosion** — different crystallographic planes corrode at different rates.

**2. Grain Size and Boundaries:**
- Fine grains → high GB area → preferential IGC attack.
- Coarse grains → lower GB area → better general corrosion resistance.

**3. Phase Composition:**
- Single-phase alloys are more electrochemically homogeneous → less micro-galvanic attack.
- Two-phase alloys (e.g., α+β brass) suffer **selective leaching** (dezincification).

**4. Cold Work and Residual Stress:**
- Cold work increases dislocation density → ↑ anodic sites → ↑ corrosion rate.
- Residual tensile stress → ↑ SCC susceptibility.

**5. Sensitisation (Chromium Carbide Precipitation):**
- In 304 SS, heating at 500–850 °C causes Cr₂₃C₆ at GBs → Cr-depleted zones → severe IGC.

**6. Impurity and Inclusion Content:**
- Sulfide inclusions (MnS) in steel act as **pitting initiation sites** in chloride environments.

**How Alloying Improves Corrosion Resistance:**

**(i) Solid Solution Strengthening with Noble Element:**
- Adding Ni to Cu (e.g., 70-30 Cu-Ni alloy) increases strength and corrosion resistance in seawater.

**(ii) Passivation by Chromium:**
- Adding **≥ 12% Cr** to Fe creates a stable, self-healing Cr₂O₃ passive film → **stainless steel**.
- Higher Cr, plus Mo (e.g., 316 SS, 2–3% Mo) improves pitting resistance.

**(iii) Cathodic Alloying:**
- Adding small amounts of **cathodic elements** (Pd, Pt) to Ti or Cr enhances passivation by lowering the critical current density for passivation.

**(iv) Anodic Inhibitor Alloying:**
- Adding **Sn or Sb to brass** prevents dezincification by forming a continuous protective film.

**(v) Grain Refinement:**
- Adding Ti, Nb, V to steel forms stable carbides that pin grain boundaries, refining grain size, and in some cases (e.g., 321 SS), preventing sensitisation.

**Examples:**
- **Stainless steel (304, 316):** Cr + Ni alloying for passivation.
- **Monel (Ni-30% Cu):** Excellent in HF and seawater.
- **Inconel 625:** Ni-Cr-Mo for sour service (H₂S environments).
- **Al-2024:** Cu alloying for strength, but requires protection to prevent IGC.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in This Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor $n$ in PBR** — Students often write $\text{PBR} = M_{ox} \rho_m / (M_m \rho_{ox})$ without the $n$. For Al₂O₃, $n=2$ and this error gives PBR = 2.58, which would falsely predict "spalling" instead of "protective."
> 2. **Confusing the Hall-Petch relation** with corrosion — it is a *mechanical* relation; the corrosion analog is empirical ($\text{CR} \propto d^{-n}$).
> 3. **Mixing up CN values** — FCC and HCP both have CN = 12; the difference is in slip systems and APF.
> 4. **Forgetting to state "per unit cell"** when listing atomic contributions in BCC/FCC — corner atoms contribute 1/8, face-centre atoms 1/2.
> 5. **Ignoring the band theory explanation** — Examiner expects at least a 1-line mention of overlapping valence/conduction bands for electrical conductivity.
> 6. **Writing "more grain boundary = better corrosion"** — it is the OPPOSITE; fine grains = more GB area = MORE corrosion. This is a 2–3 mark trap.
> 7. **Sensitisation mechanism skipping Cr-depletion threshold of 12%** — the 12% passivation threshold is THE key fact; omitting it loses 2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must Memorise):**

- [x] **Metallic bond** = cations + delocalised electron sea; non-directional.
- [x] **3 crystal structures:** BCC (CN 8, APF 0.68), FCC (CN 12, APF 0.74), HCP (CN 12, APF 0.74).
- [x] **Atoms per cell:** BCC = 2, FCC = 4, HCP = 6.
- [x] **FCC** has 12 slip systems → most ductile; **HCP** has 3 → brittle.
- [x] **PBR formula** — must include the $n$ factor.
- [x] **PBR 1–2 = protective**; outside this range = non-protective or spalling.
- [x] **Al PBR = 1.29** → Al₂O₃ is protective.
- [x] **Grain boundaries** are the preferred sites for IGC.
- [x] **Fine grain size → more GB → ↑ corrosion** (Hall-Petch analog: $\text{CR} \propto d^{-n}$).
- [x] **Cold work** → ↑ dislocations → ↑ corrosion rate; mitigate by annealing.
- [x] **Sensitisation of 304 SS:** Cr₂₃C₆ at GB → Cr depletion below 12% → IGC.
- [x] **12% Cr threshold** for passivation in stainless steel.
- [x] **Dezincification** = selective leaching of Zn from brass.
- [x] **MnS inclusions** in steel = pitting initiation sites in Cl⁻.
- [x] **Electron sea model** explains lustre, ductility, conductivity.
- [x] **Band theory:** overlap of valence + conduction band → metallic conduction.
- [x] **Nanocrystalline metal:** $f_{GB} = 3\delta / d$; at 10 nm grain size, ~15% atoms at GB.
- [x] **Theoretical density** formula: $\rho = nM / (V_{cell} N_A)$.
- [x] **5 categories of metallic properties:** physical, mechanical, chemical, electrochemical, structural.
- [x] **Alloying mechanisms:** solid solution, passivation (Cr, Al), cathodic (Pd, Pt), grain refinement (Ti, Nb, V).

> **Bonus One-Liners for the Examiner's Eye:**
- *"FCC has APF of 0.74, the maximum theoretical packing for spheres."*
- *"Grain boundary diffusion is 10³–10⁶× faster than lattice diffusion."*
- *"Cold work increases stored energy, making the metal more anodic."*
- *"Sensitisation is reversible by re-solution annealing above 1050 °C followed by rapid quenching."*
- *"Stabilised grades (321-Ti, 347-Nb) prevent IGC by forming TiC/NbC in preference to Cr₂₃C₆."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Economic considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT527 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Economic considerations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Economic considerations in corrosion engineering** refer to the systematic financial evaluation of the costs associated with corrosion damage and the cost-effectiveness of various corrosion prevention, control, and mitigation strategies over the service life of an asset, structure, or component.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Adaptation):**
> Economic considerations encompass the evaluation of direct and indirect losses due to corrosion, the cost of preventive measures (materials selection, coatings, cathodic protection, inhibitors), and the application of engineering economic principles—such as **Present Worth (PW)**, **Capitalized Cost (CC)**, and **Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost (EUAC)**—to select the most economically optimal corrosion control strategy.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine buying a car. You have two choices:

1. **Cheap Car (₹5 Lakh):** Will rust within 5 years; needs frequent repairs.
2. **Premium Car (₹10 Lakh):** Stainless body, ceramic coating, anti-rust warranty for 15 years.

A purely short-sighted person picks the cheap one. But an **engineer thinks in life-cycle terms**:
- Cheap car: ₹5 Lakh + ₹4 Lakh in 10 years of rust repairs + resale loss = **₹12 Lakh**
- Premium car: ₹10 Lakh + ₹0 in maintenance + better resale = **₹11 Lakh**

> The premium car is **cheaper in the long run**! This is the *exact* logic applied in corrosion economics — the **cheapest initial material is rarely the cheapest total solution**.

**Corrosion economics = "Pay now to save later, OR pay much more later if you don't pay now."**

---

### 1.3 Why Economic Considerations Matter (KTU High-Impact Context)

> [!NOTE]
> **NACE IMPACT Study (Industry Standard Benchmark):**
> The global cost of corrosion is estimated at **US $2.5 Trillion annually**, equivalent to roughly **3–4% of global GDP**. Of this, approximately **15–35% can be saved** by applying existing cost-effective corrosion control technologies. *This single statistic is a KTU-favorite 3-mark question starter.*

| Loss Type | Industry Impact |
|---|---|
| Direct Loss | Plant shutdown, replacement of parts |
| Indirect Loss | Loss of product, contamination, efficiency loss, safety hazards |
| Strategic Loss | Environmental liability, legal penalties, brand reputation |

---

### 1.4 Standard Economic Metrics in Corrosion Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following economic parameters are universally used in corrosion cost analysis and MUST be memorized for the KTU exam:
>
> - **Initial Cost (P)** – Capital expenditure (CAPEX) at year 0
> - **Salvage Value (SV)** – Resale or scrap value at end of life
> - **Annual Maintenance Cost (AMC)** – Yearly recurring corrosion-related expenses
> - **Useful Life (n)** – Service life in years
> - **Interest Rate (i)** – Discount rate, expressed as decimal (e.g., 8% = 0.08)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Corrosion Costs

Corrosion-related expenditures are broadly classified into two categories. KTU examiners *frequently* ask students to differentiate between these with real-world examples.

#### A) Direct Costs (Tangible Losses)
These are immediately visible, measurable, and appear on the balance sheet:
- **Replacement Cost:** Buying new pipes, tanks, structural steel.
- **Repair & Maintenance Cost:** Welding, patching, recaulking.
- **Corrosion Protection Cost:** Paints, coatings, cathodic protection systems, inhibitors, alloy upgrades.
- **Inspection & Monitoring Cost:** Non-destructive testing (NDT), corrosion coupons, probes.

#### B) Indirect Costs (Hidden Losses – Often > 50% of total cost)
These are *not* recorded as corrosion-specific but are caused by corrosion:
- **Plant Shutdown Losses:** Loss of production (a refinery losing $500,000/day).
- **Product Loss:** Leakage of oil/gas/chemicals through corroded pipelines.
- **Product Contamination:** Corrosion products (e.g., rust in food/pharma industries) cause rejection.
- **Loss of Efficiency:** Internal scaling/tuberculation in pipes increases pumping costs.
- **Safety & Environmental Damage:** Explosions, toxic releases, lawsuits, EPA fines.
- **Insurance Premium Hikes:** Higher premiums for facilities with poor corrosion management.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** Indirect costs are often **4 to 10 times the direct cost**. So a $1M direct corrosion loss may translate to a $5M–$10M real economic loss. This justifies investing in prevention.

---

### 2.2 Engineering Economic Methods for Corrosion Control

To compare two corrosion control options (e.g., **Option A**: Carbon steel + coating, **Option B**: Stainless steel), engineers use the following time-value-of-money tools:

#### (a) Present Worth Analysis (PWA)
All future costs are converted to a single equivalent value **today** (Year 0) and compared. The option with the **lowest PW** wins.

#### (b) Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost (EUAC)
All costs are converted to a uniform **yearly** payment. Useful when alternatives have different service lives.

#### (c) Capitalized Cost (CC)
Used for **perpetual/indefinite-life** structures (e.g., buried pipelines, monuments). It is the present worth of a **perpetual** series of costs.

#### (d) Benefit-Cost Ratio (B/C)
Ratio of *benefits* (corrosion loss averted) to *costs* (prevention). If **B/C > 1**, the project is economically justified.

#### (e) Rate of Return (ROR) Method
The discount rate at which the present worth of costs equals present worth of benefits (i.e., NPV = 0).

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table lists **all KTU-grade formulas** for economic analysis. Note the use of `\mid` (not `|` pipe character) to keep table syntax safe.

| # | Parameter / Method | Formula | Meaning of Terms |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Single Payment Compound Amount | $F = P(1+i)^n$ | F = Future value, P = Present value, i = interest rate, n = years |
| 2 | Single Payment Present Worth | $P = F(1+i)^{-n}$ | Converts future cost F to today's value |
| 3 | Capital Recovery Factor (CRF) | $CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | Converts P to annual A |
| 4 | Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost | $EUAC = P \cdot CRF - SV \cdot \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1} + AMC$ | A = Annual equivalent cost, AMC = Annual maintenance |
| 5 | Sinking Fund Factor (SFF) | $SFF = \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | Converts F to annual A |
| 6 | Capitalized Cost (Perpetual Life) | $CC = P + \dfrac{A}{i}$ | For n → ∞ ; A = annual recurring cost |
| 7 | Net Present Value (NPV) | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{B_t - C_t}{(1+i)^t}$ | B = benefits, C = costs, t = year |
| 8 | Benefit-Cost Ratio | $B/C = \dfrac{PW\ of\ Benefits}{PW\ of\ Costs}$ | If B/C > 1 → Accept |
| 9 | Minimum Cost of Protection | $\dfrac{d(Total\ Cost)}{dt} = 0$ | t = thickness / protection level; optimal point where cost is minimum |
| 10 | Corrosion Rate Conversion | $mpy = \dfrac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$ | W = weight loss (mg), D = density (g/cm³), A = area (in²), T = time (hr) |

---

### 2.4 The "Optimum Protection" Concept (KTU Hot Topic)

A higher thickness of coating/alloy-upgrading always increases the **initial cost**, but reduces **maintenance/replacement cost**. The **Total Cost** has a **U-shaped curve**:

$$
Total\ Cost(t) = Initial\ Cost(t) + \dfrac{Maintenance\ Cost(t)}{i}
$$

The **optimum protection level** (thickness $t^*$) is found where total cost is minimum:
$$
\dfrac{d(Total\ Cost)}{dt} = 0
$$

> Above $t^*$: You are **over-engineering** (paying extra for negligible gain).
> Below $t^*$: You are **under-protecting** (paying more in repairs than the savings).

---

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Pipeline Industry:** Choosing between carbon steel with cathodic protection vs. stainless steel.
- **Marine Structures:** Cost-benefit of using corrosion-resistant aluminum alloys vs. regular steel with marine coatings.
- **Chemical Plants:** Selection of cost-effective inhibitor concentration.
- **Refineries:** Justifying investment in corrosion monitoring systems using B/C ratio.
- **Power Plants:** Economic thickness of boiler tubes to prevent hot corrosion.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)

The CRF is the workhorse of KTU economic problems. Let us derive it rigorously.

**Starting Point:** A present value $P$ invested today yields interest $i$ per year. We want to find a uniform annual withdrawal $A$ for $n$ years that **depletes the fund to zero at year n**.

The fund balance after year 1 = $P(1+i) - A$.
The fund balance after year 2 = $P(1+i)^2 - A(1+i) - A$.
Continuing, after $n$ years:

$$
0 = P(1+i)^n - A\left[(1+i)^{n-1} + (1+i)^{n-2} + \dots + 1\right]
$$

The bracket is a geometric series. With first term 1, common ratio $(1+i)$, and $n$ terms, its sum is:

$$
S = \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}
$$

Substituting:

$$
0 = P(1+i)^n - A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}
$$

Solving for $A$:

$$
A = P \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}}
$$

> **Memory Trick:** "**i(1+i)ⁿ over (1+i)ⁿ minus 1**" — a KTU examiner's favorite.

---

### 3.2 Derivative Derivation: Optimum Coating Thickness

Consider total cost $C_T$ of corrosion protection as a function of coating thickness $t$:

$$
C_T(t) = C_0 + C_1 t + \dfrac{C_2}{t}
$$

Where:
- $C_0$ = Fixed cost (preparation, labor) — independent of thickness
- $C_1 t$ = Material cost (proportional to thickness $t$)
- $C_2 / t$ = Failure/repair cost (inversely proportional to thickness)

To find minimum, differentiate w.r.t. $t$ and set to zero:

$$
\dfrac{d C_T}{dt} = C_1 - \dfrac{C_2}{t^2} = 0
$$

$$
C_1 = \dfrac{C_2}{t^2}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{C_2}{C_1}}}
$$

**Substituting back to find minimum total cost:**

$$
C_{T,min} = C_0 + C_1 \sqrt{\dfrac{C_2}{C_1}} + \dfrac{C_2}{\sqrt{C_2/C_1}}
$$

$$
C_{T,min} = C_0 + 2\sqrt{C_1 C_2}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** The **minimum total cost is twice the geometric mean of the variable costs**, plus the fixed cost. This is a beautiful KTU-style result.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Carbon Steel vs. Stainless Steel

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Style Numerical Problem (Full Step-by-Step Solution):**

A chemical company must choose between two materials for a process pipe carrying a corrosive fluid:

| Parameter | Material A: Carbon Steel | Material B: Stainless Steel |
|---|---|---|
| Initial Cost (P) | ₹ 1,00,000 | ₹ 3,00,000 |
| Useful Life (n) | 5 years | 10 years |
| Annual Maintenance (AMC) | ₹ 30,000 | ₹ 5,000 |
| Salvage Value (SV) | ₹ 10,000 | ₹ 30,000 |
| Interest Rate (i) | 10% | 10% |

**Decision Rule:** Use **EUAC** (Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost). Lower EUAC = better choice.

**Step 1:** Calculate the Capital Recovery Factor (CRF) for each.

For Material A (n = 5, i = 0.10):
$$
CRF_A = \dfrac{0.10(1.10)^5}{(1.10)^5 - 1} = \dfrac{0.10 \times 1.61051}{1.61051 - 1} = \dfrac{0.161051}{0.61051} = 0.26380
$$

For Material B (n = 10, i = 0.10):
$$
CRF_B = \dfrac{0.10(1.10)^{10}}{(1.10)^{10} - 1} = \dfrac{0.10 \times 2.59374}{2.59374 - 1} = \dfrac{0.259374}{1.59374} = 0.16275
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the EUAC for each material.

General formula:
$$
EUAC = P \cdot CRF + AMC - SV \cdot SFF
$$

For Material A (SFF = i/[(1+i)ⁿ − 1] = 0.10/0.61051 = 0.16380):
$$
EUAC_A = (1{,}00{,}000)(0.26380) + 30{,}000 - (10{,}000)(0.16380)
$$
$$
EUAC_A = 26{,}380 + 30{,}000 - 1{,}638 = \mathbf{₹\ 54{,}742\ per\ year}
$$

For Material B (SFF = 0.10/1.59374 = 0.06275):
$$
EUAC_B = (3{,}00{,}000)(0.16275) + 5{,}000 - (30{,}000)(0.06275)
$$
$$
EUAC_B = 48{,}825 + 5{,}000 - 1{,}883 = \mathbf{₹\ 51{,}942\ per\ year}
$$

**Step 3:** Compare.
- $EUAC_A = ₹\ 54{,}742$
- $EUAC_B = ₹\ 51{,}942$

**Decision:** **Material B (Stainless Steel)** is economically superior by **₹ 2,800/year**, despite a 3× higher initial cost.

> **Lesson:** This is the textbook KTU example. Initial cost alone is a misleading metric — **life-cycle cost always wins**.

---

### 3.4 Capitalized Cost Derivation (Perpetual Life)

For a structure with infinite life (n → ∞):

$$
CC = P + \dfrac{A}{i}
$$

**Derivation:** The present worth of an infinite series of annual payments $A$ starting from year 1:

$$
PW = \dfrac{A}{1+i} + \dfrac{A}{(1+i)^2} + \dots = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{A}{(1+i)^k}
$$

This infinite geometric series has first term $\dfrac{A}{1+i}$ and common ratio $\dfrac{1}{1+i}$. Its sum is:

$$
PW = \dfrac{A/(1+i)}{1 - 1/(1+i)} = \dfrac{A/(1+i)}{(1+i-1)/(1+i)} = \dfrac{A/(1+i)}{i/(1+i)} = \dfrac{A}{i}
$$

Hence:
$$
\boxed{CC = P + \dfrac{A}{i}}
$$

**Example:** A buried pipeline costs ₹10 Lakh to install, requires ₹50,000/year inspection forever. At i = 8%:
$$
CC = 10{,}00{,}000 + \dfrac{50{,}000}{0.08} = 10{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}25{,}000 = ₹\ 16{,}25{,}000
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Framework of Corrosion Economic Decision-Making

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Asset Requiring Corrosion Protection] --> A1[Identify Alternatives]
    A1 --> B1[Alt 1: Carbon Steel + Coating]
    A1 --> B2[Alt 2: Alloy Upgrade e.g. SS]
    A1 --> B3[Alt 3: Cathodic Protection]
    A1 --> B4[Alt 4: Inhibitor Dosing]
    B1 --> C1[Estimate Initial Cost P]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Estimate Annual Maintenance AMC]
    D1 --> E1[Estimate Service Life n]
    E1 --> F1[Apply Economic Method PW or EUAC or CC]
    F1 --> G1[Compute Total Cost for each Alt]
    G1 --> H1[Select Lowest Cost Option]
    H1 --> I1[Verify B/C Ratio greater than 1]
    I1 --> J1[Final Corrosion Control Decision]
```

> **Reading the Flow:** The diagram maps the complete decision flow starting from the asset, branching into 4 alternative protection methods, converging into a single cost computation, and finally selecting the **lowest life-cycle cost** alternative.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Total Cost vs. Protection Thickness (U-Curve)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A2[Protection Level t: Low] --> B2[Low Initial Cost]
    A2 --> C2[High Maintenance Cost]
    C2 --> D2[High Total Cost]
    E2[Protection Level t: Optimum] --> F2[Balanced Initial Cost]
    E2 --> G2[Balanced Maintenance]
    G2 --> H2[MINIMUM Total Cost]
    I2[Protection Level t: Excessive] --> J2[Very High Initial Cost]
    I2 --> K2[Low Maintenance]
    K2 --> L2[High Total Cost]
    D2 --> M2[U-Shaped Cost Curve]
    H2 --> M2
    L2 --> M2
    M2 --> N2[Engineer Selects Optimum t star]
```

> **Interpretation:** The U-curve clearly shows that **both under-protection and over-protection are economically wasteful**. The optimal thickness $t^*$ sits at the bottom of the U.

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Classification of Corrosion Costs

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Corrosion Costs] --> DIR[Direct Costs]
    ROOT --> IND[Indirect Costs]
    DIR --> D1[Replacement]
    DIR --> D2[Repair and Maintenance]
    DIR --> D3[Coatings and Inhibitors]
    DIR --> D4[Inspection and Monitoring]
    IND --> I1[Plant Shutdown Loss]
    IND --> I2[Product Contamination]
    IND --> I3[Efficiency Loss]
    IND --> I4[Safety and Environmental Penalty]
    IND --> I5[Insurance Premium Hike]
    I4 --> WARN[Safety Hazard Note]
    WARN[Indirect costs often 4x to 10x direct costs]
```

---

### 4.4 Functional Flow: Steps in a KTU Exam Numerical Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Read given data] --> S2[Step 2: Identify n and i]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Calculate CRF]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Calculate SFF]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Compute EUAC for each alternative]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Compare and select minimum]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Conclude with engineering recommendation]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A: 2-Mark / 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper Style]**

#### **Q1. Differentiate between direct and indirect costs of corrosion. Give two examples of each. [3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

> **Direct costs** are tangible, immediately visible, and appear on the company's balance sheet.
> - *Examples:* (i) Cost of replacing a corroded pipe (ii) Cost of repainting a structure.
>
> **Indirect costs** are hidden, intangible, and not directly recorded as corrosion-related losses.
> - *Examples:* (i) Loss of production due to plant shutdown (ii) Product contamination leading to rejection.

---

#### **Q2. State and explain the NACE IMPACT estimate of global corrosion cost. [3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The **NACE International IMPACT Study** (2016) estimated the global annual cost of corrosion at approximately **US $2.5 Trillion**, equivalent to **3.4% of the global GDP**. The study further revealed that applying **best available corrosion control practices** can save **15% to 35%** of this cost (≈ $375–$875 billion annually). This highlights the immense economic value of investing in proper corrosion engineering.

---

---

### PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each 14-mark question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Internal choice between Question A OR Question B.

---

#### **Question A: (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the various **methods of economic analysis** used in the selection of corrosion control options. [7 Marks]

**(b)** A heat exchanger tube has two material options:
- **Option 1:** Carbon steel – Initial cost ₹ 2,00,000, life 4 years, annual maintenance ₹ 40,000, salvage ₹ 20,000.
- **Option 2:** Stainless steel – Initial cost ₹ 5,00,000, life 8 years, annual maintenance ₹ 10,000, salvage ₹ 50,000.
- Interest rate = 12% per year.

Determine the **most economical option** using the **EUAC method**. [7 Marks]

---

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

**(a) Methods of Economic Analysis in Corrosion Control:**

The following methods are used to compare corrosion control alternatives that have different initial costs, service lives, and maintenance costs:

1. **Present Worth Analysis (PWA):** All future costs (maintenance, replacement) are discounted to a single equivalent **present value** at year 0. The option with the lowest present worth is selected.
2. **Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost (EUAC):** All costs are converted into a uniform annual amount using the **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)**. This is the most widely used method in corrosion engineering.
3. **Capitalized Cost (CC):** Used for structures with **perpetual/indefinite life** (e.g., underground pipelines, monuments). $CC = P + A/i$.
4. **Benefit-Cost (B/C) Ratio:** Compares the present worth of **benefits** (corrosion loss averted) to the present worth of **costs** (prevention). Accept if B/C > 1.
5. **Rate of Return (ROR) Method:** The discount rate at which NPV = 0; if the computed ROR exceeds the minimum attractive rate of return (MARR), the project is accepted.
6. **Optimum Protection Analysis:** Determines the **thickness** or protection level at which the sum of initial and maintenance costs is minimum.

> **Conclusion:** These methods enable engineers to make **rational, quantitative decisions** about corrosion control, balancing **CAPEX (capital expenditure) vs. OPEX (operating expense)**.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1:** Given Data Summary
| Parameter | Option 1 (CS) | Option 2 (SS) |
|---|---|---|
| P | ₹ 2,00,000 | ₹ 5,00,000 |
| n | 4 | 8 |
| AMC | ₹ 40,000 | ₹ 10,000 |
| SV | ₹ 20,000 | ₹ 50,000 |
| i | 12% = 0.12 | 12% = 0.12 |

**Step 2:** Compute CRF and SFF for Option 1 (n=4, i=0.12):
$$
CRF_1 = \dfrac{0.12(1.12)^4}{(1.12)^4 - 1} = \dfrac{0.12 \times 1.5735}{0.5735} = 0.32923
$$
$$
SFF_1 = \dfrac{0.12}{0.5735} = 0.20923
$$

**Step 3:** Compute CRF and SFF for Option 2 (n=8, i=0.12):
$$
CRF_2 = \dfrac{0.12(1.12)^8}{(1.12)^8 - 1} = \dfrac{0.12 \times 2.4760}{1.4760} = 0.20130
$$
$$
SFF_2 = \dfrac{0.12}{1.4760} = 0.08130
$$

**Step 4:** Compute EUAC.
$$
EUAC = P \cdot CRF + AMC - SV \cdot SFF
$$

For **Option 1 (Carbon Steel):**
$$
EUAC_1 = (2{,}00{,}000)(0.32923) + 40{,}000 - (20{,}000)(0.20923)
$$
$$
EUAC_1 = 65{,}846 + 40{,}000 - 4{,}185 = \mathbf{₹\ 1{,}01{,}661\ per\ year}
$$

For **Option 2 (Stainless Steel):**
$$
EUAC_2 = (5{,}00{,}000)(0.20130) + 10{,}000 - (50{,}000)(0.08130)
$$
$$
EUAC_2 = 1{,}00{,}650 + 10{,}000 - 4{,}065 = \mathbf{₹\ 1{,}06{,}585\ per\ year}
$$

**Step 5:** Decision.
- $EUAC_1 = ₹\ 1{,}01{,}661$ ← **Lower**
- $EUAC_2 = ₹\ 1{,}06{,}585$

**Conclusion:** **Option 1 (Carbon Steel)** is the more economical choice, saving **₹ 4,924/year** compared to stainless steel. Although stainless steel has a longer life, its high initial cost makes carbon steel the better economic choice for this scenario.

---

#### **Question B (ALTERNATIVE): (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Style]**

**(a)** What are the **direct and indirect costs of corrosion**? Explain with **five real-world examples** for each category. [7 Marks]

**(b)** A buried pipeline has two corrosion protection alternatives:
- **Alternative X:** Coal tar coating — Initial cost ₹ 8,00,000, annual maintenance ₹ 60,000.
- **Alternative Y:** 3-Layer PE coating — Initial cost ₹ 12,00,000, annual maintenance ₹ 25,000.
- Interest rate = 10% per year, **indefinite life** (perpetual).

Determine the **most economical alternative** using the **Capitalized Cost method**. [7 Marks]

---

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

**(a) Direct and Indirect Costs of Corrosion:**

> **Direct Costs:** These are visible, tangible, and directly attributable to corrosion.
> 1. Cost of replacing a corroded boiler tube in a power plant.
> 2. Cost of repainting a bridge every 3 years.
> 3. Cost of applying cathodic protection to a pipeline.
> 4. Cost of corrosion inhibitor dosing in a cooling water system.
> 5. Cost of routine inspection using ultrasonic thickness gauging.

> **Indirect Costs:** These are hidden but often exceed direct costs.
> 1. Shutdown of an oil refinery for 7 days due to a corroded heat exchanger (production loss).
> 2. Contamination of food products by rust particles, leading to a product recall.
> 3. Reduced flow rate in water pipelines due to internal tuberculation, increasing pumping cost.
> 4. Legal compensation paid to workers injured by a corrosion-induced explosion.
> 5. Increased insurance premium for a chemical plant with poor corrosion management.

---

**(b) Capitalized Cost Calculation:**

For perpetual life, the formula is:
$$
CC = P + \dfrac{A}{i}
$$

**Alternative X (Coal Tar Coating):**
$$
CC_X = 8{,}00{,}000 + \dfrac{60{,}000}{0.10} = 8{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 = ₹\ 14{,}00{,}000
$$

**Alternative Y (3-Layer PE Coating):**
$$
CC_Y = 12{,}00{,}000 + \dfrac{25{,}000}{0.10} = 12{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}50{,}000 = ₹\ 14{,}50{,}000
$$

**Decision:**
- $CC_X = ₹\ 14{,}00{,}000$ ← **Lower (Preferred)**
- $CC_Y = ₹\ 14{,}50{,}000$

**Conclusion:** **Alternative X (Coal Tar Coating)** is the more economical choice, with a capitalized cost saving of **₹ 50,000** over the lifetime of the pipeline. Although it has higher annual maintenance, the lower initial investment dominates.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Salvage Value term in EUAC:** Many students compute $EUAC = P \cdot CRF + AMC$ and **omit the $-SV \cdot SFF$** term. This loses **2 marks** consistently.
> 2. **Using Present Worth (PW) when service lives differ:** If two alternatives have different $n$, you **must** use EUAC, not PW. PW comparison is only valid for **equal life** alternatives.
> 3. **Confusing CRF and SFF:** CRF converts $P \to A$; SFF converts $F \to A$. Mixing them is a guaranteed **3-mark penalty**.
> 4. **Not converting interest rate to decimal:** $i = 10\%$ must be entered as **0.10**, not 10. A common silly mistake worth **1 mark**.
> 5. **Omitting units in the final answer:** Always state "₹ per year" for EUAC. KTU examiners are strict on units.
> 6. **Ignoring indirect costs in real-world discussion:** In theory questions, always mention that **indirect costs are 4×–10× direct costs**. This is a high-value sentence that examiners actively look for.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Memorize This Block:**

- [x] **Two Categories of Cost:** Direct (visible) vs Indirect (hidden, often 4×–10× direct cost).
- [x] **NACE Benchmark:** Global corrosion cost = **$2.5 Trillion/year** = **3.4% of global GDP**.
- [x] **Savable Amount:** **15%–35%** of corrosion cost can be saved with proper control.
- [x] **Three Core Methods:** PW, EUAC, CC — know when to use each.
- [x] **PW vs EUAC:** PW for **equal life**; EUAC for **unequal life** alternatives.
- [x] **Capitalized Cost:** $CC = P + A/i$ — used for **perpetual life** (n → ∞).
- [x] **CRF Formula:** $CRF = i(1+i)^n / [(1+i)^n - 1]$ — most important formula in this module.
- [x] **SFF Formula:** $SFF = i / [(1+i)^n - 1]$
- [x] **EUAC Formula:** $EUAC = P \cdot CRF + AMC - SV \cdot SFF$
- [x] **Optimum Thickness:** $t^* = \sqrt{C_2/C_1}$; minimum cost $= C_0 + 2\sqrt{C_1 C_2}$.
- [x] **B/C Rule:** Accept the project if **B/C > 1**; reject if B/C < 1.
- [x] **Decision Rule:** Always select the alternative with the **LOWEST** PW, EUAC, or CC.
- [x] **Always convert i to decimal** (10% = 0.10) before calculation.
- [x] **Always mention indirect costs** in any descriptive answer — it's a KTU scoring line.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Importance of corrosion inspection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT527 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Importance of corrosion inspection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Corrosion Inspection** is the systematic, planned, and documented process of examining, monitoring, measuring, and evaluating the integrity of metallic (and sometimes non-metallic) components, structures, and systems to detect, characterize, and quantify the extent of corrosion damage, predict remaining service life, and ensure continued safe, reliable, and economical operation.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECHT527 – Corrosion Engineering, Module 1)**, corrosion inspection forms the **third pillar of corrosion control** (alongside material selection and protective methods). It bridges the gap between theoretical corrosion prediction and real-world asset integrity management. Inspection converts the *invisible deterioration* caused by electrochemical reactions into *measurable engineering data* that drives maintenance, replacement, and safety decisions.

The three pillars of corrosion control are:

| Pillar | Role |
|---|---|
| **Material Selection** | Choosing inherently corrosion-resistant alloys |
| **Corrosion Protection** | Applying coatings, inhibitors, cathodic/anodic protection |
| **Corrosion Inspection** | Verifying that the first two pillars remain effective in service |

> [!NOTE]
> The **NACE/AMPP International Standard TM0101** and **API 571** define corrosion inspection as a *closed-loop feedback mechanism* — the data acquired during inspection feeds back into the design, materials, and protection strategy of the next operational cycle.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Human Body Health Check-Up"

Imagine your car is a living human body, and corrosion is a silent, invisible disease (like high blood pressure or internal arterial decay). **Corrosion inspection is exactly equivalent to a periodic medical check-up.**

- **Routine Blood Test** ↔ **Corrosion Coupons & ER Probes** (continuous monitoring of "health indicators")
- **MRI / X-Ray Scan** ↔ **Ultrasonic Thickness Gauging (UT)** and **Radiography (RT)** (detecting hidden structural damage)
- **Annual Master Health Check-Up** ↔ **Scheduled Visual + NDT Inspection (Turnaround / Shutdown Inspection)**
- **Doctor's Prescription** ↔ **Maintenance, Repair, or Replacement Recommendation**

Just as a patient ignoring routine health check-ups risks a sudden heart attack, an industrial plant ignoring corrosion inspection risks **catastrophic failure** — explosions, fires, toxic releases, and multi-million-dollar losses.

## 1.3 Core Reasons — Why Corrosion Inspection is *Non-Negotiable*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme explicitly mandates that students must be able to **justify the economic, safety, environmental, and regulatory necessity** of corrosion inspection programmes.

The importance of corrosion inspection can be grouped into **five engineering drivers**:

1. **Safety of Life (Personnel & Public)**
2. **Asset Integrity & Reliability**
3. **Economics (Direct & Indirect Cost Avoidance)**
4. **Environmental Protection**
5. **Regulatory & Legal Compliance**

A landmark study (NACE International, *Impacts of Corrosion*, 2016 — methodology reproduced in KTU recommended reading) estimates the **global direct cost of corrosion at approximately 3–4% of Gross Domestic Product (GDP)**, with a further **~6% of GDP in indirect costs**. Inspection is the single most cost-effective intervention to recover a large portion of this loss.

> [!NOTE]
> The famous NACE estimate — that **15–35% of annual corrosion cost could be saved by applying existing corrosion-control technology, primarily inspection** — is one of the most frequently asked 3-mark questions in KTU university examinations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic of Corrosion Inspection — Five-Step Framework

Inspection is not a random act of looking at metal. It is a **structured engineering process** built on the following five logical steps:

- **Step 1 — Baseline Establishment (Pre-Service / Commissioning Inspection)**
  - Records the *as-built* wall thickness, surface finish, coating thickness, and hardness.
  - This baseline is the **zero-reference** against which *all future* thickness-loss measurements are compared.
  - Without a baseline, an inspector cannot answer the question: *"How much metal has been lost since the plant started?"*

- **Step 2 — Periodic In-Service Monitoring**
  - Scheduled measurements (e.g., ultrasonic thickness surveys every turnaround) using *reproducible locations* (often called "TMLs — Thickness Monitoring Locations").
  - Determines the **actual corrosion rate** under real operating conditions.

- **Step 3 — Data Recording, Trending & Analysis**
  - Inspection data is plotted as a **Wall Thickness vs. Time** graph.
  - The slope of the trend line gives the *measured* corrosion rate, which is then compared with the *design* corrosion rate.

- **Step 4 — Fitness-for-Service (FFS) Assessment**
  - Using codes such as **API 579-1/ASME FFS-1**, the remaining strength of the corroded component is evaluated.
  - Decision: *Continue / Repair / Replace*.

- **Step 5 — Feedback to Design & Operation**
  - Lessons learned are fed back to the materials, process, and protection teams — closing the **Plan–Do–Check–Act (PDCA) loop**.

## 2.2 The Five Engineering Drivers of Corrosion Inspection — Detailed Breakdown

### A. Safety of Life

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** A corroded pressure vessel, pipeline, or storage tank can rupture with little warning. Past industrial disasters (e.g., the 1984 Bhopal disaster, the 1992 Guadalajara sewer explosions, the 2010 San Bruno pipeline rupture) had **undetected corrosion** as a major contributing root cause.

Inspection prevents:
- Sudden brittle fracture of pressure equipment
- Pipeline leak / rupture → fire, explosion, vapour cloud
- Collapse of structural members (offshore platforms, bridges)
- Failures of critical rotating equipment (shafts, impellers)

### B. Asset Integrity & Reliability

Unplanned downtime of a refinery, power plant, or petrochemical unit can cost **₹ 1–10 crore per day** in lost production. Inspection converts *unplanned* failures into *planned* shutdowns, enabling:
- Just-in-time procurement of spares
- Bundling of maintenance activities
- Predictable plant availability factors (target > 95%)

### C. Economics — Direct and Indirect Cost

- **Direct cost avoidance:** Replacement of pipes, vessels, structural members.
- **Indirect cost avoidance:** Production loss, demurrage, customer-claim penalties, insurance premium hikes.
- Inspection cost is typically **0.5–2% of replacement cost**, whereas unplanned failure can be **10–100×** the inspection cost.

### D. Environmental Protection

A corroded tank bottom can release hydrocarbons into soil and groundwater, leading to:
- Cleanup liabilities under **EPA / Indian CPCB / OISD** norms
- Brand and reputational damage
- Criminal prosecution of plant managers

### E. Regulatory & Legal Compliance

Inspection is mandated by:
- **Indian Boiler Regulations (IBR)**
- **OISD (Oil Industry Safety Directorate) Standards**
- **PESO (Petroleum & Explosives Safety Organisation)**
- **Factory Act 1948 (Section 6, 7, 28)**
- **API 510, 570, 653** (Pressure Vessel, Piping, Tankage)
- **ASME Section V** (Nondestructive Examination)
- **ISO 17039** (Corrosion monitoring)

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All quantities below are **non-negotiable for KTU Part-B 14-mark answers**. Memorise the units.

| # | Concept / Quantity | Formula / Expression | Units / Typical Value | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Corrosion rate (from mass loss) | $CR = \dfrac{K \cdot W}{A \cdot T \cdot D}$ | mm/yr (K = 87.6 for mm/yr) | Most-tested formula in Module 1 |
| 2 | Corrosion rate (from thickness loss) | $CR = \dfrac{\Delta t}{T}$ | mm/yr | Used directly with UT inspection data |
| 3 | Remaining life (from inspection) | $RL = \dfrac{t_{current} - t_{min}}{CR_{actual}}$ | years | Core of RBI decisions |
| 4 | Minimum allowable thickness | $t_{min}$ from ASME / API code | mm | Compared with measured thickness |
| 5 | Plant availability | $A = \dfrac{T_{up}}{T_{up} + T_{down}} \times 100$ | % | Quantifies reliability benefit |
| 6 | Optimal inspection interval | $T^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 C_{insp}}{C_{fail} \cdot \lambda^2}}$ | years | **See derivation in Section 3** |
| 7 | RBI risk priority | $Risk = POF \times COF$ | dimensionless 1–5 scale | Inspection planning tool |
| 8 | Direct corrosion cost of GDP | $\approx 3 - 4\%$ of GDP | % | Famous NACE figure — 3-mark Q |
| 9 | Indirect : Direct cost ratio | $\approx 1.5 : 1$ to $2 : 1$ | ratio | Used in cost-of-corrosion essays |
| 10 | Inspection cost vs. replacement | $C_{insp} \approx 0.5 - 2\%\, C_{replace}$ | % | Justifies inspection budgets |
| 11 | Thickness loss per cycle | $\Delta t = t_{baseline} - t_{measured}$ | mm | Direct output of UT inspection |
| 12 | Safety factor (FFS) | $SF = \dfrac{\sigma_{allow}}{\sigma_{actual}}$ | dimensionless | FFS assessment |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic — "5 E's" for KTU viva:** **E**conomics, **E**nvironment, **E**nergy-loss avoidance, **E**ngineering reliability, **E**nforceable compliance.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Corrosion inspection is the **backbone** of the following industries and is therefore a high-recruitment skill area for KTU graduates:

- **Oil & Gas (upstream, midstream, downstream)** — RBI of subsea pipelines, risers, downhole tubing.
- **Petrochemical & Refinery** — Corrosion under insulation (CUI) on overheads, overhead condensers, hydroprocessing reactors.
- **Power Generation** — Boiler water-wall tubes, condenser tubes, steam turbine blades, cooling water systems.
- **Marine & Offshore** — Ballast tanks, splash zones, hulls, offshore platform legs.
- **Chemical Process** — Storage tanks (floor plates), agitator shafts, reactor jackets.
- **Aerospace & Defence** — Aircraft fuselage lap joints, fuel tanks, missile storage.
- **Civil Infrastructure** — Reinforced concrete (rebar corrosion), bridges, water pipelines.
- **Automotive & EV** — Battery enclosures, body panels, chassis.

> [!NOTE]
> For an Indian context, **IOCL, BPCL, HPCL, ONGC, BHEL, NTPC, L&T, Tata Steel, and SAIL** are the largest employers of corrosion engineers with inspection expertise.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although the topic "Importance of corrosion inspection" is largely qualitative, the KTU 2024 Scheme (Outcome-Based Education) requires students to **quantify the economic and risk-based rationale**. The derivations below are mandatory for the 14-mark Part-B questions.

## 3.1 Derivation — Remaining Service Life from Inspection Data

> This is the single most important derivation for the *Importance of Corrosion Inspection* topic.

The **remaining life** of a corroded component is the time available before the wall thickness falls to the **minimum allowable thickness** ($t_{min}$) prescribed by the design code (e.g., ASME Section VIII, API 650, API 570).

### Step 1 — Define the baseline wall thickness

The **baseline thickness** ($t_0$) is the as-built thickness measured during the pre-commissioning or first-turnaround inspection.

$$t_0 = \text{Thickness at start of service (mm)}$$

### Step 2 — Measure current thickness during inspection

The **current thickness** ($t_c$) is the value recorded during the most recent inspection using UT, RT, or any qualified method.

$$t_c = \text{Thickness at time of inspection (mm)}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the actual corrosion rate from two successive inspections

If we have *two* inspection data points, taken at times $T_1$ and $T_2$ (years), the **actual measured corrosion rate** is:

$$\begin{aligned}
CR_{actual} &= \dfrac{t_1 - t_2}{T_2 - T_1} \quad \text{(in mm/yr)} \\[6pt]
\text{where} \quad t_1 &= \text{thickness at first inspection (mm)} \\
t_2 &= \text{thickness at second inspection (mm)}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Explanation of logic:** The difference in thickness readings, divided by the time elapsed between the two inspections, gives the *real-world* corrosion rate — this is far more reliable than the *design* corrosion rate because it captures actual operating conditions (temperature, pressure, fluid chemistry, flow regime).

### Step 4 — Determine the minimum allowable thickness

The minimum allowable thickness ($t_{min}$) is obtained from the relevant design code by the equation:

$$t_{min} = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6 P} + CA$$

where $P$ is internal design pressure, $R$ is inside radius, $S$ is allowable stress, $E$ is joint efficiency, and $CA$ is corrosion allowance.

### Step 5 — Compute the Remaining Life

The **remaining service life** is the time at which the current thickness will drop to the minimum allowable thickness, assuming the *actual* corrosion rate continues linearly:

$$\begin{aligned}
RL &= \dfrac{t_c - t_{min}}{CR_{actual}} \quad \text{(years)} \\[6pt]
\text{where} \quad t_c &= \text{current measured thickness (mm)} \\
t_{min} &= \text{minimum allowable thickness (mm)} \\
CR_{actual} &= \text{actual measured corrosion rate (mm/yr)}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6 — Decision Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU valuation tip:** Always end a remaining-life problem with an *engineering decision* — not just a number.

| Condition | Recommended Action |
|---|---|
| $RL > 2 \times$ (next inspection interval) | **Continue in service**, schedule next inspection |
| $RL \approx$ next inspection interval | **Increase inspection frequency** |
| $RL <$ next inspection interval | **Replace or repair immediately** |
| $t_c \le t_{min}$ | **Shut down — unfit for service** |

## 3.2 Derivation — Optimal Inspection Interval (Total Cost Minimisation)

> This derivation is a *favourite 14-mark question* in KTU Module-1 university exams.

Assume:
- A corroding component fails randomly with **Poisson-process** failure rate $\lambda$ (failures/year).
- Each planned inspection costs $C_{insp}$ (₹ per inspection).
- Each failure costs $C_{fail}$ (₹ — including repair, lost production, safety penalty).
- Inspection interval is $T$ years (uniform).

### Step 1 — Expected number of failures in interval $T$

For a low failure rate, the expected number of failures in time $T$ is approximately:

$$N_{fail} = \lambda T$$

### Step 2 — Expected cost of failures per unit time

$$\dot{C}_{fail} = \dfrac{\lambda T \cdot C_{fail}}{T} = \lambda \cdot C_{fail}$$

(Per unit time, this is a constant — independent of $T$.)

### Step 3 — Expected cost of inspection per unit time

$$\dot{C}_{insp} = \dfrac{C_{insp}}{T}$$

### Step 4 — Total expected cost per unit time

$$C_{total}(T) = \dfrac{C_{insp}}{T} + \lambda \cdot C_{fail}$$

> **Logic:** The total cost has two terms: (i) inspection cost (decreases as interval $T$ grows longer) and (ii) failure cost (constant). To minimise total cost, we *differentiate* with respect to $T$.

### Step 5 — Minimise by calculus

$$\dfrac{d C_{total}}{dT} = -\dfrac{C_{insp}}{T^{2}} + 0 = 0$$

This naive form is minimised as $T \to \infty$ — which is incorrect because failures are *catastrophic* if inspection is skipped. The realistic formulation assumes **failure probability grows with $T$**:

$$P(\text{fail in }T) \approx 1 - e^{-\lambda T} \approx \lambda T \quad \text{for small } \lambda T$$

Including this and adding a quadratic growth term, the canonical **optimal inspection interval** (used in reliability engineering textbooks) is:

$$\boxed{\,T^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, C_{insp}}{C_{fail} \cdot \lambda^{2}}}\,}$$

### Step 6 — Engineering interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **T\* is proportional to √(C_insp)** and **inversely proportional to √(C_fail)**. This means:
> - If failure cost is **10× higher**, inspection interval should be **√10 ≈ 3.16× shorter**.
> - This single equation *mathematically justifies* the existence of inspection programmes.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Inspection Method vs. Application

| Inspection Method | What it Detects | Typical Accuracy | Limitation | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Visual (VT)** | Surface corrosion, pitting, blistering, coating failure | ± 1 mm (visual) | Cannot see subsurface | General walk-down, external tanks |
| **Ultrasonic Thickness (UTT)** | Wall-thickness loss | ± 0.1 mm | Requires couplant, surface prep | Piping, pressure vessels, storage tanks |
| **Radiography (RT)** | Wall thinning, pitting, weld defects | ± 1% of thickness | Radiation hazard, slow | Piping circumferential scans |
| **Eddy Current (ECT)** | Surface & near-surface cracks, pitting | ± 0.05 mm | Limited depth of penetration | Heat-exchanger tubes |
| **Magnetic Particle (MT)** | Surface & sub-surface cracks | Qualitative | Ferromagnetic only | Welds, forgings |
| **Dye Penetrant (PT)** | Surface cracks | Qualitative | Surface-breaking only | Non-magnetic alloys (SS, Cu) |
| **Acoustic Emission (AE)** | Active cracks, leaks | Qualitative — real-time | Needs active loading | Pressure testing, in-service monitoring |
| **Corrosion Coupons** | Average corrosion rate, deposit analysis | ± 5% | Retrospective (weeks delay) | Process lines, water systems |
| **ER (Electrical Resistance) Probes** | Real-time cumulative metal loss | ± 0.5 µm | Localised, intrusive | Cooling water, overheads |
| **LPR (Linear Polarisation Resistance)** | Instantaneous corrosion rate | ± 0.001 mpy | Needs conductive electrolyte | Cooling water, oil-field brines |
| **Hydrogen Probes** | Hydrogen permeation → sour corrosion | Qualitative | Slow response | Sour-service (H₂S) equipment |
| **Thermography (IRT)** | Insulation-corrosion, refractory loss | ± 1 °C | Surface-only | CUI surveys, furnace tubes |
| **Borescope / Video Probe** | Internal visual of hidden cavities | Visual only | Restricted access | Turbines, reactors, column trays |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic for inspection methods — "VULTURE-MCH":** **V**isual, **U**ltrasonic, **L**PR, **T**hermography, **U**ltrasonic-thickness, **R**adiography, **E**ddy-current, **M**agnetic-particle, **C**oupon, **H**ydrogen-probe. Practise writing this in the 14-mark answer.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

> **Problem (Model KTU 14-mark Part-B):** A new carbon-steel overhead condenser tube is commissioned with wall thickness 4.0 mm. After 2 years, ultrasonic thickness measurement gives 3.6 mm at the most-corroded location. The minimum allowable thickness from ASME code is 2.0 mm. Design corrosion rate is 0.4 mm/yr. Compute the (a) actual corrosion rate, (b) remaining life, (c) recommended next-inspection interval.

### Solution

**(a) Actual corrosion rate**

$$\begin{aligned}
CR_{actual} &= \dfrac{t_0 - t_c}{\Delta T} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{4.0 - 3.6}{2} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{0.4}{2} \\[6pt]
&= 0.2 \; \text{mm/yr}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Interpretation:** The actual rate is **half** the design rate. The designer was conservative (which is good engineering practice).

**(b) Remaining life**

$$\begin{aligned}
RL &= \dfrac{t_c - t_{min}}{CR_{actual}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{3.6 - 2.0}{0.2} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{1.6}{0.2} \\[6pt]
&= 8.0 \; \text{years}
\end{aligned}$$

**(c) Recommended next-inspection interval**

Per the FFS convention, next inspection interval $\le RL / 2$:

$$T_{next} = \dfrac{8.0}{2} = 4 \; \text{years}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The next inspection should be scheduled **within 4 years** — well before the calculated remaining life of 8 years — to provide a safety margin of 100%.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Corrosion Inspection Closed-Loop (PDCA Cycle)

This block-level architecture shows how inspection data **closes the feedback loop** in corrosion control — a high-yield 14-mark diagram.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Design and Material Selection] --> B[Fabrication and Construction]
    B --> C[Baseline Inspection pre-service]
    C --> D[In-Service Operation]
    D --> E[Periodic Inspection NDT and Monitoring]
    E --> F[Data Analysis and Trending]
    F --> G{Fitness for Service FFS Decision}
    G -->|Continue| H[Record and Schedule Next Inspection]
    G -->|Repair| I[Maintenance and Repair]
    G -->|Replace| J[Procure and Replace]
    I --> D
    J --> D
    H --> E
    F --> K[Feedback to Design Team]
    K --> A

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#b45309,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#b45309,color:#ffffff
    style K fill:#6d28d9,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — The 5 E's — Drivers of Corrosion Inspection

A concept map that visually links each of the five engineering drivers to its underlying sub-factors. This is the *most-impressive* diagram to draw in a KTU 14-mark answer.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Importance of Corrosion Inspection]
    P --> E1[Economics]
    P --> E2[Environment]
    P --> E3[Energy and Resource]
    P --> E4[Engineering Reliability]
    P --> E5[Enforceable Compliance]

    E1 --> E1a[Avoid unplanned shutdown]
    E1 --> E1b[Reduce replacement cost]
    E1 --> E1c[Lower insurance premium]

    E2 --> E2a[Prevent soil contamination]
    E2 --> E2b[Prevent water pollution]
    E2 --> E2c[Prevent atmospheric release]

    E3 --> E3a[Conserve raw material]
    E3 --> E3b[Extend asset life]
    E3 --> E3c[Reduce energy for rework]

    E4 --> E4a[Maintain design margin]
    E4 --> E4b[Detect pitting early]
    E4 --> E4c[Predict remaining life]

    E5 --> E5a[Comply with IBR]
    E5 --> E5b[Comply with OISD]
    E5 --> E5c[Comply with API 510 570 653]

    style P fill:#0f172a,color:#ffffff
    style E1 fill:#0369a1,color:#ffffff
    style E2 fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff
    style E3 fill:#a16207,color:#ffffff
    style E4 fill:#9d174d,color:#ffffff
    style E5 fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Inspection-Planning Decision Tree

This tree shows *when* to choose a particular inspection method — useful for an inspector's first-year decision-making and for viva questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Equipment under Inspection] --> Q1{What failure mode is suspected?}
    Q1 -->|General wall thinning| UT[Ultrasonic Thickness Survey]
    Q1 -->|Pitting or localised attack| UT2[UT with high-resolution scanning]
    Q1 -->|Cracking| Q2{Is the material ferromagnetic?}
    Q1 -->|Under-deposit corrosion| CP[Coupon Retrieval plus ER Probe]
    Q1 -->|CUI under insulation| IRT[Infrared Thermography]
    Q2 -->|Yes| MT[Magnetic Particle Testing]
    Q2 -->|No| PT[Dye Penetrant Test]
    Q1 -->|Real-time rate needed| LPR[Linear Polarisation Resistance Probe]
    Q1 -->|Active crack growth| AE[Acoustic Emission Monitoring]
    Q1 -->|Internal hidden surface| BS[Borescope Inspection]

    style Start fill:#1e293b,color:#ffffff
    style Q1 fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
    style Q2 fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff

    style UT2 fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff

    style PT fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff

```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Cost-vs-Time Trade-off (Block Architecture)

Because a physical Cartesian plot is hard to render in Mermaid, this block-level functional architecture conveys the *information content* of the standard cost-vs-time graph used in inspection-economics essays.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph X[Cost Components]
        X1[Inspection Cost per unit time]
        X2[Failure Cost per unit time]
    end

    X1 --> X1a[Decreases as interval T increases]
    X2 --> X2a[Increases as interval T increases]

    X1a --> Sum[Total Cost Ctotal equals Cinsp by T plus lambda times Cfail]
    X2a --> Sum

    Sum --> Min[Minimum at T star equals sqrt 2 Cinsp over Cfail lambda squared]

    style X1 fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff
    style X2 fill:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
```
