# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part-A questions are **direct, definition-oriented, two-to-three-sentence answers**. Length ≈ 80–120 words. Memorise the key facts.

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Question]**
*State any three reasons why corrosion inspection is important in an oil refinery.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Corrosion inspection is critical in an oil refinery for the following reasons:

1. **Safety of life and property:** Hydrocarbon-carrying equipment such as overheads, distillation columns, and reactors operate at high temperature and pressure. Undetected corrosion thinning can lead to rupture, fire, and explosion, endangering personnel and surrounding communities.

2. **Economics — avoidance of unplanned shutdown:** Unplanned refinery shutdowns cost approximately ₹ 1–10 crore per day in lost production. Periodic inspection converts unpredictable failures into *planned* maintenance, drastically reducing downtime cost.

3. **Regulatory compliance:** Indian regulations (OISD-118, OISD-150, IBR, Factory Act) mandate periodic inspection of pressure vessels, piping, and storage tanks as per API 510, 570, and 653. Non-compliance can lead to legal penalties, licence cancellation, and insurance-claim rejection.

> **Valuation tip:** Always number the points. Each correctly-explained point = **1 mark**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Question]**
*List the commonly used non-destructive techniques employed for corrosion inspection of process equipment.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The commonly used non-destructive testing (NDT) techniques for corrosion inspection are:

1. **Visual Testing (VT)** — Preliminary method for detecting surface corrosion, coating failure, and leak stains.

2. **Ultrasonic Thickness Testing (UTT)** — Most widely used method for measuring remaining wall thickness; gives quantitative metal-loss data with ± 0.1 mm accuracy.

3. **Radiographic Testing (RT)** — Uses X-rays or gamma rays to detect internal wall-thinning, pitting, and weld defects, particularly in piping circumferential scans.

4. **Eddy Current Testing (ECT)** — Used for non-magnetic tubes (e.g., heat-exchanger condenser tubes) to detect pitting and wall loss.

5. **Magnetic Particle Testing (MT)** and **Dye Penetrant Testing (PT)** — Used for detecting surface and sub-surface cracks in ferromagnetic and non-ferromagnetic materials respectively.

6. **Acoustic Emission (AE)** — Real-time monitoring of active crack growth and leaks during hydrostatic testing.

> **Valuation tip:** Naming a technique = 0.5 mark, briefly stating its use = 0.5 mark. Maximum 3 techniques with uses for full marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme, every Part-B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Both Question A and Question B are independent alternatives; the student answers *one*.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the **five engineering drivers** that justify the importance of corrosion inspection in any chemical process industry. Give at least one industry example for each driver.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A carbon-steel heat-exchanger shell is commissioned with wall thickness **8.0 mm**. After **3 years** of service, ultrasonic inspection gives a current thickness of **7.4 mm** at the thinnest location. The minimum allowable thickness per ASME code is **5.6 mm**. The design corrosion rate is **0.5 mm/yr**. Compute the (i) actual corrosion rate, (ii) remaining service life, and (iii) recommend the next inspection interval. Justify your decision.

---

### ✅ Model Answer — Question A

**(a) The Five Engineering Drivers of Corrosion Inspection (7 marks):**

1. **Safety of life (2 marks)**
   - Corrosion can lead to sudden rupture of pressure vessels, pipelines, and storage tanks. Catastrophic failures endanger plant personnel, firefighters, and the public.
   - *Industry example:* The 2010 San Bruno (USA) gas-pipeline rupture — undetected corrosion caused an explosion killing 8 people.

2. **Asset integrity and reliability (1 mark)**
   - Inspection ensures equipment continues to perform its design function without unexpected failure, maintaining high plant availability (> 95%).

3. **Economics (1.5 marks)**
   - Direct replacement costs (steel, fabrication, downtime) and indirect costs (lost production, demurrage, customer claims) are minimised. Inspection cost is typically 0.5–2% of replacement cost.
   - *Industry example:* IOCL Panipat refinery saved approximately ₹ 50 crore per year through RBI implementation.

4. **Environmental protection (1 mark)**
   - Inspection prevents leaks that can contaminate soil, groundwater, and air, avoiding cleanup liabilities under Indian CPCB and EPA norms.

5. **Regulatory and legal compliance (1.5 marks)**
   - Statutory codes — Indian Boiler Regulations (IBR), OISD-118, API 510/570/653, ASME Section V, ISO 17039 — mandate periodic inspection. Non-compliance leads to prosecution and licence cancellation.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Actual corrosion rate (2 marks)**

$$\begin{aligned}
CR_{actual} &= \dfrac{t_0 - t_c}{T} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{8.0 - 7.4}{3} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{0.6}{3} \\[6pt]
&= 0.2 \; \text{mm/yr}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Engineering comment (1 mark):** The actual rate is **0.2 mm/yr**, which is **less than half** the design rate of 0.5 mm/yr. The designer was conservatively safe.

**(ii) Remaining service life (2 marks)**

$$\begin{aligned}
RL &= \dfrac{t_c - t_{min}}{CR_{actual}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{7.4 - 5.6}{0.2} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{1.8}{0.2} \\[6pt]
&= 9.0 \; \text{years}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Recommended next inspection interval (2 marks)**

Per the standard FFS rule, the next inspection interval should be **at most half the remaining life** to provide a 100% safety margin:

$$T_{next} \le \dfrac{RL}{2} = \dfrac{9.0}{2} = 4.5 \; \text{years}$$

**Recommendation:** Schedule the next ultrasonic-thickness survey within **4 years** (round down for conservatism). Additionally:
- Increase to **annual UT scanning** in the area of the thinnest reading.
- Consider **on-stream corrosion-rate monitoring** using an ER or LPR probe to capture any transient upset.
- Document the location as a "TML — Thickness Monitoring Location" and trend future readings against this baseline.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **Role of Inspection in the Corrosion-Control Loop** (PDCA cycle). Explain how inspection data is used in fitness-for-service (FFS) assessment per **API 579-1 / ASME FFS-1**.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Discuss the **economic importance** of corrosion inspection. Show mathematically how the **optimal inspection interval** $T^*$ depends on inspection cost, failure cost, and failure-rate. Use numerical values: $C_{insp} = ₹ 50{,}000$ per inspection, $C_{fail} = ₹ 2{,}00{,}00{,}000$ per failure, $\lambda = 0.05$ failures/yr, to find $T^*$ in years.

---

### ✅ Model Answer — Question B

**(a) Role of Inspection in the Corrosion-Control Loop (7 marks):**

> **Diagram (3 marks):** Draw the PDCA cycle:

```
   PLAN (Material/Design)  →  DO (Fabrication/Operation)
       ↑                              ↓
       ↑                         CHECK (Inspection)
       ↑                              ↓
       ACT (Feedback to Design)  ←  ACT (Maintenance/Repair)
```

- **Plan (1 mark):** Material selection, corrosion allowance, protective coatings, cathodic protection design.
- **Do (1 mark):** Fabrication, construction, commissioning, and pre-service baseline inspection.
- **Check (1 mark):** Periodic in-service inspection using UT, RT, ER probes, etc. Data is plotted as thickness-vs-time.
- **Act (1 mark):** Decisions per **API 579-1/ASME FFS-1** — *Continue / Repair / Replace*. FFS calculates remaining strength factor (RSF), which compares actual measured thickness with the minimum required for the design pressure and temperature. The result is fed back to Plan.

> **Connection to API 579-1 (extra 1 mark for completeness):** The FFS assessment involves three levels: Level 1 (screening), Level 2 (detailed), Level 3 (advanced with FEA). Inspection data (localised metal loss, dimensions, locations) is the *primary input* to all three levels.

**(b) Economic Importance and Optimal Inspection Interval (7 marks):**

**Economic importance (3 marks):**

1. NACE estimate: Direct cost of corrosion ≈ **3–4% of GDP**; indirect cost is *equal to or greater than* direct cost.
2. Up to **15–35% of corrosion cost** is preventable by applying existing inspection-based corrosion-control technology.
3. Inspection cost ≈ **0.5–2%** of replacement cost. Failing to inspect risks **10–100×** higher cost through unplanned shutdowns, environmental cleanup, and safety incidents.

**Optimal interval derivation (3 marks):**

$$\boxed{\,T^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, C_{insp}}{C_{fail} \cdot \lambda^{2}}}\,}$$

**Numerical calculation (1 mark):**

$$\begin{aligned}
T^{*} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 50{,}000}{2 \times 10^{7} \times (0.05)^{2}}} \\[6pt]
&= \sqrt{\dfrac{1 \times 10^{5}}{2 \times 10^{7} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3}}} \\[6pt]
&= \sqrt{\dfrac{1 \times 10^{5}}{5 \times 10^{4}}} \\[6pt]
&= \sqrt{2.0} \\[6pt]
&\approx 1.414 \; \text{years} \\[6pt]
&\approx 17 \; \text{months}
\end{aligned}$$

**Engineering decision (extra 1 mark for rounding & safety):** Schedule inspection every **17 months**, with a **safety reduction to 12 months** for conservatism.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top five places where students LOSE marks on "Importance of Corrosion Inspection" answers:**
>
> 1. **Listing without explaining** — Naming "Safety, Economics, Environment" without a one-line engineering explanation for each (loses 1.5–2 marks per point).
> 2. **Missing the NACE 3–4% of GDP statistic** — Examiners *expect* this number; not stating it loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** — Writing 8.0/0.2 = 40 without the unit "years" is marked wrong.
> 4. **No engineering decision in numericals** — Computing remaining life but not stating *what to do next* loses 1–2 marks. Always end with "**schedule next inspection in X years, recommend additional monitoring**".
> 5. **Conflating inspection with monitoring** — Inspection is *periodic discrete* (e.g., turnaround UT survey); monitoring is *continuous* (e.g., ER/LPR probes). Examiners specifically look for this distinction.
> 6. **Omitting the diagram** — A 14-mark question *demands* a labelled diagram (PDCA cycle, inspection-method matrix, or wall-thickness-vs-time plot). Skipping it loses 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember — High-Density Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> Use this as a **last-hour rapid-revision** card before the KTU examination.

- **Definition:** Corrosion inspection is the *systematic, planned, documented examination, monitoring, measurement, and evaluation* of equipment to detect, characterise, and quantify corrosion damage and predict remaining life.
- **The "5 E's" of importance:** **E**conomics, **E**nvironment, **E**nergy/resource conservation, **E**ngineering reliability, **E**nforceable compliance. Always state all five in a 7-mark answer.
- **NACE statistic (must-memorise):** Direct cost of corrosion ≈ **3–4% of GDP**; 15–35% of this is preventable by inspection.
- **Inspection cost vs replacement cost:** Inspection ≈ 0.5–2% of replacement; unplanned failure ≈ 10–100× inspection cost.
- **Inspection cycle:** **Plan → Do → Check → Act (PDCA)** — inspection is the "Check" stage.
- **Five most important inspection methods (must-name):** Visual (VT), Ultrasonic (UTT), Radiography (RT), Eddy Current (ECT), Magnetic Particle (MT).
- **Three real-time monitoring methods (must-name):** Corrosion coupon, Electrical Resistance (ER) probe, Linear Polarisation Resistance (LPR) probe.
- **Key formula (must-derive):** Actual corrosion rate $CR = \Delta t / T$ in mm/yr.
- **Key formula (must-derive):** Remaining life $RL = (t_c - t_{min}) / CR_{actual}$ in years.
- **Key formula (must-derive):** Optimal interval $T^* = \sqrt{2 C_{insp} / (C_{fail} \lambda^2)}$.
- **Decision rule (must-memorise):** Schedule next inspection at **≤ RL / 2** for a 100% safety margin.
- **Baseline importance (must-state):** Without a *pre-service baseline thickness*, you cannot compute corrosion rate from later inspections.
- **FFS codes (must-name):** API 579-1 / ASME FFS-1 for fitness-for-service assessment.
- **Indian regulatory codes (must-name):** IBR, OISD-118/150, API 510/570/653, Factory Act 1948.
- **Three levels of FFS (bonus point):** Level 1 (screening) → Level 2 (detailed) → Level 3 (FEA-based).
- **Inspection vs monitoring distinction (bonus):** Inspection is *periodic* and *quantitative*; monitoring is *continuous* and *real-time*.
- **"CUI" (bonus):** Corrosion Under Insulation is a *hidden* corrosion mode; thermography is the inspection method.
- **Real-world Indian example (bonus):** IOCL, BPCL, ONGC, BHEL refineries and platforms use RBI programmes.

> **Final Exam Mantra:** *State the 5 E's. Quote the NACE statistic. Show the PDCA diagram. Derive the remaining-life equation. End with a clear engineering decision.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Different forms of corrosion: Galvanic or two metal corrosion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT527 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Different forms of corrosion: Galvanic or two metal corrosion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Galvanic corrosion** (also called *bimetallic corrosion* or *two-metal corrosion*) is an electrochemical form of corrosion that occurs when **two dissimilar metals are placed in electrical contact in the presence of a common electrolyte (corrosive medium)**, resulting in the preferential accelerated attack of the more *electrochemically active* (less noble) metal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme – PECHT527, Module 2):**
> Galvanic corrosion is one of the **eight classical forms of corrosion** and is classified as a *wet / electrochemical* form. It is the most frequently encountered form in real engineering assemblies (riveted, bolted, welded or soldered joints between dissimilar metals).

The basic requirements for galvanic corrosion to occur are:

1. **Two metals with a measurable difference in electrode potential.**
2. **Metallic (electrical) continuity** between them (direct contact or via a wire).
3. **A common electrolyte** (e.g., salt water, moisture, acid, soil moisture) bridging the two metals.
4. A continuous **path for ionic conduction** through the electrolyte.

> [!NOTE]
> **Terminology Note:** The term "galvanic" is named after Luigi Galvani, who in 1780 discovered the bio-electric effect, and Alessandro Volta, who in 1800 built the first chemical battery (the Voltaic pile) — the foundation of modern electrochemistry.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **"The Two Friends in a Bathtub" Analogy**
> Imagine two friends — one *active* and always donating, the other *lazy* and always receiving — sharing a common pool of money (the electrolyte). The active friend (anode) keeps giving away his money, becomes poorer and eventually "dissolves," while the lazy friend (cathode) just collects. **The greater the difference in their natures, the faster the active one depletes.** In galvanic corrosion, electrons flow from the active metal (anode) to the noble metal (cathode), and metal ions dissolve into the electrolyte from the anode.

A simpler engineering analogy: **A bolted joint between a copper pipe and a steel pipe under a leaky rooftop.** The copper remains intact while the steel bolt or nearby steel flange rusts away rapidly, because copper is *nobler* and steel becomes the sacrificial anode.

## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Faraday's constant | $F$ | **96,485 C mol⁻¹** (≈ 96,500 C mol⁻¹) |
| Universal gas constant | $R$ | **8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹** |
| Standard temperature (Nernst) | $T$ | **298.15 K (25 °C)** |
| Nernst factor at 25 °C | $RT/F$ | **0.02569 V** |
| $\ln 10$ at 25 °C | — | **2.303** |
| Molar volume of gas (STP) | — | **22.414 L mol⁻¹** |

> [!NOTE]
> The two principal references used in the study of galvanic corrosion are the **Standard EMF (Electromotive Force) Series** (based on pure metals in 1 M solutions of their ions at 25 °C) and the **Practical Galvanic Series** (based on actual metals/alloys in a real electrolyte such as seawater). The latter is **more useful in engineering practice** because it accounts for passivation, oxide films, and real surface conditions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of Galvanic Corrosion — Step-by-Step Logic

The galvanic corrosion process can be decomposed into four coupled half-reactions that occur *simultaneously*:

**Step 1 — Anodic dissolution (oxidation)** at the *less noble* metal (anode):

$$M \longrightarrow M^{n+} + n e^{-}$$

> *Why:* Atoms of the more active metal lose electrons and enter the electrolyte as cations. This is the metal that physically corrodes.

**Step 2 — Cathodic reduction** at the *more noble* metal (cathode). Depending on the electrolyte, the cathodic reaction is typically one of:

- **Hydrogen evolution** (in acidic, oxygen-free media):

$$2H^{+} + 2e^{-} \longrightarrow H_{2}\uparrow$$

- **Oxygen reduction (acidic):**

$$O_{2} + 4H^{+} + 4e^{-} \longrightarrow 2H_{2}O$$

- **Oxygen reduction (neutral / alkaline — most common in practice):**

$$O_{2} + 2H_{2}O + 4e^{-} \longrightarrow 4OH^{-}$$

- **Metal-ion reduction** (e.g., Cu²⁺/Cu, Fe³⁺/Fe²⁺ — less common).

> *How:* Electrons released at the anode travel through the metallic path to the cathode, where they are consumed by reducible species in the electrolyte.

**Step 3 — Ionic conduction in the electrolyte.** Cations (e.g., $M^{n+}$) migrate from the anode to the cathode side; anions (e.g., $OH^{-}$, $Cl^{-}$) migrate from cathode to anode, completing the circuit.

**Step 4 — Formation of corrosion products.** Dissolved metal ions often react with electrolyte species to form insoluble compounds (oxides, hydroxides, carbonates) that deposit as rust, patina, or scale.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core 'Why' behind galvanic corrosion:**
> When two dissimilar metals are short-circuited in an electrolyte, the resulting composite electrode behaves like a **galvanic cell (primary battery)**. The mixed (corrosion) potential of the couple lies between the two individual open-circuit potentials. This mixed potential is *closer to the noble metal's potential* (because the cathodic area usually dominates kinetics), forcing the anodic metal to dissolve at a rate far higher than it would in isolation.

## 2.2 Galvanic Series vs. EMF Series

| Feature | EMF Series | Galvanic Series |
|---|---|---|
| Basis | Pure metals in 1 M ion activity | Actual metals/alloys in real electrolyte (e.g., seawater) |
| Conditions | Ideal, theoretical | Practical, observed |
| Includes alloys? | No (mostly) | Yes (brass, bronze, stainless steel, etc.) |
| Includes passive metals? | No | Yes (e.g., active vs. passive SS) |
| Use in design | Thermodynamic limit | **Engineering decisions** |
| Ranking changes with environment? | No | **Yes** |

### Simplified Practical Galvanic Series (in seawater, most active at top)

| Group | Metal / Alloy | Tendency |
|---|---|---|
| Most Anodic (active) | Magnesium | Sacrificial |
| ↑ | Zinc | Sacrificial |
| ↑ | Aluminum alloys | — |
| ↑ | Carbon steel, Cast iron | — |
| ↑ | Stainless steel (active) | — |
| ↑ | Lead, Tin, Lead–Tin solder | — |
| ↑ | Brass, Copper | — |
| ↑ | Bronze, Copper–nickel | — |
| ↑ | Nickel (passive) | — |
| ↑ | Stainless steel (passive) | — |
| Most Cathodic (noble) | Silver, Gold, Platinum | — |

> *How to use the table:* The further apart two metals are in the series, the greater the driving voltage and the higher the galvanic current. The metal listed higher (more active) becomes the **anode** and corrodes.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **directly testable** in KTU Part A and Part B questions.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode}$ | Net driving EMF of galvanic couple | V (volt) |
| 2 | $E = E^{\circ} + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{a_{ox}}{a_{red}}\right)$ | Nernst equation (general) | V |
| 3 | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n}\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{a_{red}}{a_{ox}}\right)$ | Nernst equation (25 °C, base-10) | V |
| 4 | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n}\log_{10}Q$ | Nernst for any redox reaction at 25 °C | V |
| 5 | $W = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Faraday's first law (mass dissolved) | g |
| 6 | $CR = \dfrac{I \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot A \cdot t}$ | Corrosion rate (mass loss basis) | g cm⁻² s⁻¹ |
| 7 | $CR_{mm/yr} = \dfrac{0.00327 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho}$ | Corrosion rate in mm/yr from $i_{corr}$ in µA/cm² | mm yr⁻¹ |
| 8 | $EW = \dfrac{M}{n}$ | Equivalent weight of metal | g eq⁻¹ |
| 9 | $i_{a}\cdot A_{a} = \vert i_{c}\vert\cdot A_{c}$ | Charge balance (mixed potential) | A |
| 10 | $\dfrac{r_{a}^{\text{galv}}}{r_{a}^{\text{free}}} \approx \dfrac{A_{c}}{A_{a}}$ | Area effect amplification factor | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\Delta E \uparrow \Rightarrow I_{galv}\uparrow$ | Greater EMF → higher galvanic current | qualitative |
| 12 | $V = \dfrac{RT}{F} = 0.0257\,\text{V (at 25°C)}$ | Thermal voltage | V |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world engineering utility:**
> - **Cathodic protection design** (impressed current and sacrificial anode systems) directly uses the galvanic series.
> - **Materials selection in plant design** (e.g., tube–tube-sheet joints in heat exchangers) uses galvanic compatibility charts.
> - **Failure analysis** of pipe flanges, riveted joints, marine fasteners, and bimetallic heat exchangers.

## 2.4 Factors Affecting Galvanic Corrosion

1. **Position in the galvanic series** (EMF difference $\Delta E$).
2. **Area ratio** ($A_{c}/A_{a}$) — most critical design factor.
3. **Distance between the two metals** (close contact → severe).
4. **Nature of electrolyte** (conductivity, $pH$, dissolved oxygen).
5. **Temperature** (higher $T$ increases kinetics).
6. **Surface films and coatings** (can polarize either electrode).
7. **Relative motion / flow velocity** of the electrolyte.
8. **Initial corrosion product behaviour** (porous vs. protective).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Cell EMF for a Dissimilar-Metal Couple

Consider two metals $M_1$ and $M_2$ in electrical contact through an electrolyte, where $M_1$ is the **anode** (more active) and $M_2$ is the **cathode** (more noble).

**Step 1 — Anodic half-reaction:**

$$M_1 \longrightarrow M_1^{n_1} + n_1 e^{-}, \quad E^{\circ}_{M_1^{n+}/M_1}$$

**Step 2 — Cathodic half-reaction:**

$$M_2^{n_2+} + n_2 e^{-} \longrightarrow M_2, \quad E^{\circ}_{M_2^{n+}/M_2}$$

**Step 3 — Overall cell reaction (balanced):** Multiply to get equal electrons $L = \text{lcm}(n_1, n_2)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
L \cdot M_1 + L \cdot M_2^{n_2+} &\longrightarrow L \cdot M_1^{n_1} + L \cdot M_2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Net cell EMF (thermodynamic driving force):**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{cell}^{\circ} &= E_{cathode}^{\circ} - E_{anode}^{\circ} \\
&= E_{M_2^{n_2+}/M_2}^{\circ} - E_{M_1^{n_1+}/M_1}^{\circ}
\end{aligned}$$

> *Conversion logic:* By IUPAC convention, a spontaneous galvanic cell has $E_{cell} > 0$. The metal with the **lower (more negative) standard reduction potential** becomes the anode.

**Step 5 — Adjusting for non-standard activities using the Nernst equation.** For each half-cell:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0591}{n}\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{a_{red}}{a_{ox}}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Mixed (corrosion) potential** $E_{corr}$ of the coupled system:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{corr} \quad &\text{satisfies: } \quad I_{a,total}(E_{corr}) = \vert I_{c,total}(E_{corr})\vert \\
&\text{i.e.,} \quad \sum_{a} i_{a,k}\cdot A_{a,k} = \sum_{c} \vert i_{c,j}\vert\cdot A_{c,j}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Corrosion current density at the anode** (Evans / Wagner–Traud mixed potential theory):

$$\begin{aligned}
i_{corr,a} &= \dfrac{i_{0,a}}{A_{a}}\exp\!\left[\dfrac{\alpha_{a} F (E_{corr} - E_{eq,a})}{RT}\right] \\
i_{corr,c} &= \dfrac{i_{0,c}}{A_{c}}\exp\!\left[\dfrac{-\alpha_{c} F (E_{corr} - E_{eq,c})}{RT}\right]
\end{aligned}$$

> *Conversion logic:* At $E_{corr}$, the anodic current from $M_1$ exactly equals the cathodic current consumed at $M_2$. This balance determines both the corrosion rate of $M_1$ and the rate of the cathodic reaction (e.g., $O_2$ reduction) on $M_2$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Quantitative Area Effect

In a galvanic couple, the **same total current** flows through both electrodes (series circuit). Let the corrosion current be $I_{galv}$ (A).

**Step 1 — Current density at the anode:**

$$i_{a} = \frac{I_{galv}}{A_{a}}$$

**Step 2 — Penetration rate of the anode (linear corrosion rate, m/s):**

$$\begin{aligned}
CR_{a} &= \frac{i_{a}\cdot M_{a}}{n_{a}\cdot F \cdot \rho_{a}} \quad \text{(m s}^{-1}\text{)} \\
&\text{or, in mm/yr using $i_{corr}$ in µA/cm}^{2}\text{:} \\
CR_{a}\ (\text{mm/yr}) &= \frac{0.00327 \cdot i_{a}\cdot M_{a}}{n_{a}\cdot \rho_{a}} \\
&= \frac{0.00327 \cdot i_{a} \cdot EW_{a}}{\rho_{a}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Substituting $i_{a} = I_{galv}/A_{a}$:**

$$CR_{a} = \frac{0.00327 \cdot I_{galv} \cdot EW_{a}}{A_{a} \cdot \rho_{a}}$$

> *Key insight:* $CR_{a} \propto \dfrac{1}{A_{a}}$ for a fixed $I_{galv}$. So **a small anode + large cathode = catastrophically fast corrosion of the anode.**

**Step 4 — Defining the amplification factor** relative to the freely corroding anode:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{CR_{a}^{galv}}{CR_{a}^{free}} \;\approx\; \frac{A_{c}}{A_{a}}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **Area Effect Equation** — one of the highest-yield derivations in KTU Module 2.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem (Model):** A steel plate ($E^{\circ}_{Fe^{2+}/Fe} = -0.44$ V) is riveted with copper rivets ($E^{\circ}_{Cu^{2+}/Cu} = +0.34$ V) and exposed to seawater (assume $E_{corr,cathode} \approx 0.00$ V SHE due to $O_2$ reduction). The total galvanic current measured is $I_{galv} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ A, the anode area is $A_a = 5\,\text{cm}^2$ and the cathode area is $A_c = 50\,\text{cm}^2$. Calculate the **corrosion rate of the steel** in mm/yr.

**Given:** $M_{Fe} = 55.85$ g/mol, $n = 2$, $\rho_{Fe} = 7.87$ g/cm³, $EW = 27.92$ g/eq, $i_a = I_{galv}/A_a = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}/5 = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ A/cm² $= 500\,\mu\text{A/cm}^2$.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
CR_{a} &= \frac{0.00327 \times i_{a} \times EW}{\rho} \\
&= \frac{0.00327 \times 500 \times 27.92}{7.87} \\
&= \frac{45.649}{7.87} \\
&\approx 5.80\ \text{mm/yr}
\end{aligned}$$

> *Conversion logic:* Convert each unit carefully — $i_a$ in µA/cm², $EW$ in g/eq, $\rho$ in g/cm³ → final $CR$ in mm/yr. This value is **severe service-level corrosion** and confirms the catastrophic effect of $A_c/A_a = 10$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Rate-Influencing Factors

| Factor | Mechanism | Severity Direction |
|---|---|---|
| Greater $\Delta E$ between metals | Higher driving voltage, higher $I_{galv}$ | ↑ |
| Larger $A_c/A_a$ ratio | Higher $i_a$ on small anode | ↑↑ (very strong) |
| Higher electrolyte conductivity (e.g., seawater) | Lower IR drop, larger $I_{galv}$ | ↑ |
| Lower electrolyte $pH$ (more acidic) | More $H^+$ for cathodic reduction | ↑ |
| Higher dissolved $O_2$ | More $O_2$ reduction at cathode | ↑ |
| Higher temperature | Faster kinetics, lower $\eta$ | ↑ |
| Larger gap between coupled metals | More IR drop in electrolyte, smaller local cell | ↓ |
| Protective coating on cathode | Reduces cathodic area → reduces $I_{galv}$ | ↓↓ |
| Protective coating on anode (only) | **Dangerous** — pinholes create $A_c/A_a \to \infty$ | ↑↑↑ |
| Formation of insoluble corrosion products | Polarizes anode, slows reaction | ↓ |
| Flowing electrolyte (high velocity) | Removes protective films, increases $O_2$ transport | ↑ |
| Stagnant electrolyte | Allows buildup of films, depletion of $O_2$ | ↓ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Galvanic Corrosion Cell — Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ANODE_BLOCK["ANODE METAL M1 - ACTIVE"]
        A1["Metal Atoms M1"]
        A2["Oxidation M1 to M1 n+"]
        A3["Release of n electrons"]
    end

    subgraph ELECTRON_PATH["METALLIC PATH - WIRE OR DIRECT CONTACT"]
        E1["Electron Flow e-"]
    end

    subgraph CATHODE_BLOCK["CATHODE METAL M2 - NOBLE"]
        C1["Electron Accumulation on Surface"]
        C2["Cathodic Reduction"]
        C3["H+ or O2 Reduced to H2 or OH-"]
    end

    subgraph ELECTROLYTE_BLOCK["ELECTROLYTE - IONIC CONDUCTION LOOP"]
        L1["M1 n+ migrates from anode"]
        L2["OH- or Cl- migrates to anode"]
        L3["Cations and anions complete circuit"]
    end

    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> E1 --> C1 --> C2 --> C3
    C3 --> L1
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> A1

    style ANODE_BLOCK fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#c82333,color:#000
    style CATHODE_BLOCK fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,color:#000
    style ELECTROLYTE_BLOCK fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000
    style ELECTRON_PATH fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
```

> *How to read this:* Electrons travel **externally** (through the metal) from anode to cathode; ions travel **internally** (through the electrolyte) in the opposite direction, completing the circuit. The anode is the **loser** (it dissolves); the cathode is the **gainer** (often physically unaffected, though it may accumulate scale).

## 4.2 Area-Effect Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BAD_DESIGN["BAD DESIGN - DANGER"]
        B1["Small Anode Area Aa"]
        B2["Large Cathode Area Ac"]
        B3["Ac to Aa ratio very large"]
        B4["Very High Current Density on Anode"]
        B5["Catastrophic Localized Attack"]

        B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4 --> B5
    end

    subgraph GOOD_DESIGN["GOOD DESIGN - SAFE"]
        G1["Large Anode Area Aa"]
        G2["Small Cathode Area Ac"]
        G3["Ac to Aa ratio small"]
        G4["Low Current Density on Anode"]
        G5["Manageable Uniform Attack"]

        G1 --> G2 --> G3 --> G4 --> G5
    end

    style BAD_DESIGN fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#c82333,color:#000
    style GOOD_DESIGN fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,color:#000
```

> *Take-away:* The "small anode, large cathode" trap is one of the most frequent exam pitfalls — e.g., **steel rivets in a copper plate** (bad) vs. **copper rivets in a steel plate** (acceptable for many applications).

## 4.3 Galvanic-Corrosion Prevention Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1["Identify Dissimilar Metal Couple in Design"]
    P2["Check Galvanic Series Position Difference"]
    P3{"Is delta E large?"}

    P3 -- No --> P4["No Special Action Needed"]
    P3 -- Yes --> P5["Check Area Ratio Ac to Aa"]
    P6{"Is Ac to Aa unfavorable?"}

    P6 -- Yes --> P7["REVERSE Components or Use Insulating Gasket"]
    P6 -- No --> P8["Choose Compatible Materials"]

    P9["Apply Additional Protections"]
    P10["Option 1: Coat the Cathode Only"]
    P11["Option 2: Use Sacrificial Anode CP"]
    P12["Option 3: Add Inhibitor to Electrolyte"]
    P13["Option 4: Control Electrolyte Flow and Drainage"]

    P8 --> P9
    P9 --> P10
    P9 --> P11
    P9 --> P12
    P9 --> P13

    style P1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000
    style P7 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#c82333,color:#000
    style P10 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,color:#000
    style P11 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,color:#000
    style P12 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,color:#000
    style P13 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Design Rule:** When coatings are used to break galvanic contact, **always coat the *cathode* (noble metal)**, never just the anode. A pinhole in an anode-only coating makes the situation dramatically worse, because the bare anode spot is now a *tiny* anode against a *huge* coated cathode.
