# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 scheme Part A carries 2-mark and 3-mark questions. The two questions below are framed at the 3-mark cognitive level (Bloom: *Understand* / *Apply*).

### Q1. Define galvanic corrosion. List the four essential conditions required for its occurrence. `[3 marks]` `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Galvanic corrosion (or bimetallic / two-metal corrosion) is the accelerated electrochemical corrosion of a metal that occurs when it is placed in electrical contact with a *more noble* metal in a *common electrolyte*. The less noble metal becomes the **anode** and dissolves, while the noble metal acts as the **cathode** and is protected.

**Four essential conditions:**

1. **Two dissimilar metals** with a measurable difference in electrode potential.
2. **Metallic (electrical) continuity** between the two metals (direct contact or via a wire/bolt/rivet).
3. A **common electrolyte** (e.g., salt water, acid, soil moisture) in contact with both metals.
4. A **continuous ionic path** through the electrolyte for charge balance.

> *Mnemonic:* **"DIME"** — *D*issimilar metals, *I*ntermetallic contact, *M*oist electrolyte, *E*lectrochemical path.

---

### Q2. Distinguish between the EMF series and the galvanic series. Why is the galvanic series preferred in engineering practice? `[3 marks]` `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | EMF Series | Galvanic Series |
|---|---|---|
| Reference conditions | Pure metal, 1 M ion activity, 25 °C, no films | Real metal/alloy in real electrolyte (e.g., seawater) |
| Includes alloys? | Mostly no | **Yes** |
| Includes passive states? | No | **Yes** (e.g., active vs. passive SS) |
| Use | Thermodynamic limit | **Engineering design** |

**Why galvanic series is preferred in practice:**

- Reflects **real metal behaviour** including oxide films, passivity, and alloying effects.
- Allows **direct ranking of engineering alloys** (brass, bronze, stainless steels, Monel, Inconel, etc.).
- Accounts for **environmental effects** (seawater, soil, acid), which is the actual medium in service.
- The EMF series can give incorrect predictions for passive metals (e.g., chromium appears active in EMF series but behaves as noble in practice).

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 scheme Part B questions carry 14 marks each, typically split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. **Both Question A and Question B are alternatives** — answer *either*.

---

### Question A (14 marks)

**"Explain the mechanism of galvanic corrosion with a neat sketch. Discuss the various factors that influence the rate of galvanic corrosion, with special emphasis on the area effect."** `[14 marks]` `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

#### (a) Mechanism of Galvanic Corrosion [7 marks]

Galvanic corrosion is an **electrochemical process** in which two dissimilar metals in electrical contact act as the electrodes of a short-circuited galvanic cell. The less noble (more active) metal becomes the **anode** and undergoes oxidation; the more noble metal becomes the **cathode** where a reduction reaction occurs.

**Reactions at the anode (less noble metal, e.g., Fe):**

$$Fe \longrightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^{-} \quad \text{(oxidation)}$$

**Reactions at the cathode (more noble metal, e.g., Cu) — in neutral aerated water:**

$$O_2 + 2H_{2}O + 4e^{-} \longrightarrow 4OH^{-} \quad \text{(oxygen reduction)}$$

**Net reaction:**

$$2Fe + O_2 + 2H_{2}O \longrightarrow 2Fe(OH)_{2} \longrightarrow Fe_{2}O_{3}\cdot H_{2}O \ \text{(rust)}$$

**Sketch (to be drawn in exam):**

```
        ANODE (Fe)                CATHODE (Cu)
        e-  e-  e-  e-  ------>  e-  e-  e-
          \                       /
           \   METALLIC PATH     /
            \___________________/
              (direct contact)
        |                       |
        |  Fe 2+                |   OH-
        |    ↘                  |  ↙
        |     ↘                 | ↙
        v      ↘                v
        ELECTROLYTE (e.g., seawater)
        Ionic current flows: anions to anode, cations to cathode
```

**Driving force:**

$$E_{cell} = E_{cathode}^{\circ} - E_{anode}^{\circ} = E_{Cu^{2+}/Cu}^{\circ} - E_{Fe^{2+}/Fe}^{\circ} = 0.34 - (-0.44) = 0.78\ \text{V}$$

This positive $E_{cell}$ confirms the spontaneity of the corrosion of iron in contact with copper.

#### (b) Factors Influencing Galvanic Corrosion + Area Effect [7 marks]

**Key factors:**

1. **Position in the galvanic series** — Greater $\Delta E$ → higher driving voltage → higher $I_{galv}$.
2. **Area ratio ($A_c/A_a$)** — Most critical design factor.
3. **Distance between metals** — Closer contact → less IR drop → higher current.
4. **Electrolyte conductivity** — Higher conductivity (e.g., seawater) → higher current.
5. **Temperature** — Higher $T$ → faster kinetics.
6. **$pH$ of electrolyte** — More acidic → more $H^+$ available for cathodic reaction.
7. **Dissolved oxygen** — Aerated solutions accelerate the oxygen-reduction cathode reaction.
8. **Protective films / coatings** — Can polarize either electrode.

**The Area Effect (derivation):**

Because the same total current flows through both electrodes, the **current density** at the anode is:

$$i_{a} = \frac{I_{galv}}{A_{a}}$$

The corrosion rate of the anode is directly proportional to $i_a$:

$$CR_{a} = \frac{0.00327 \cdot i_{a} \cdot EW_{a}}{\rho_{a}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; CR_{a} \propto \frac{1}{A_{a}}$$

Therefore, for a fixed total current:

$$\frac{CR_{a}^{galvanic}}{CR_{a}^{free}} \approx \frac{A_{c}}{A_{a}}$$

> *Engineering implication:* A small anode coupled to a large cathode suffers catastrophic localized attack. **Practical example:** Steel bolts in a copper plate corrode rapidly (bad design), but copper bolts in a steel plate are acceptable because the small copper cathode produces only modest acceleration of the large steel anode.

---

### Question B (14 marks)

**"Explain the concept of galvanic corrosion with two practical engineering examples. Discuss in detail the various methods used to prevent galvanic corrosion."** `[14 marks]` `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

#### (a) Concept and Practical Examples [7 marks]

**Concept:** Galvanic corrosion is the preferential corrosion of the less noble metal when two dissimilar metals are electrically connected in a common electrolyte. It follows the principle of a **galvanic cell (voltaic cell)**:

- The **anode** (active metal) — *less noble, more negative potential* — dissolves.
- The **cathode** (noble metal) — *more positive potential* — is protected from corrosion.
- Electrons flow from anode to cathode through the metal; ions flow through the electrolyte.

**Example 1 — Marine propeller shaft (classic failure case):**
A ship propeller made of a copper alloy (e.g., manganese bronze) is mounted on a steel shaft. In seawater, the bronze is the **cathode** and the steel shaft is the **anode**. The propeller remains intact while the steel shaft suffers severe localized grooving, especially near the bearing area where the area ratio is highly unfavourable.

**Example 2 — Tube–tube-sheet joints in heat exchangers:**
A heat exchanger uses admiralty brass tubes rolled into a carbon-steel tube sheet. The brass is cathodic, the steel anodic. The contact zone (tube-to-tube-sheet joint) experiences accelerated corrosion of the steel. To mitigate, manufacturers sometimes use **ferrous-based sacrificial coatings** or insert **insulating gaskets**.

**Example 3 — Household plumbing:**
Galvanized (zinc-coated) iron pipes connected to copper piping via a brass fitting. Hot, aerated water acts as the electrolyte. Zinc of the galvanized pipe becomes the anode and dissolves preferentially, leading to "white rust" (ZnO/Zn(OH)₂) and eventual iron pipe exposure.

**Common feature in all examples:** dissimilar-metal contact + electrolyte + small anode to large cathode geometry = rapid anode dissolution.

#### (b) Prevention Methods [7 marks]

| # | Method | Principle |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Select compatible metals** | Choose metals close together in the galvanic series. |
| 2 | **Avoid small-anode/large-cathode geometry** | Use larger fasteners of the active metal inside noble structures. |
| 3 | **Use insulating gaskets / washers / sleeves** | Break metallic continuity (e.g., nylon, rubber, PTFE bushings). |
| 4 | **Coat the cathode (not the anode)** | Eliminates the cathodic reaction sites. Coating the anode only is *dangerous* because pinholes worsen the area ratio. |
| 5 | **Apply cathodic protection** | Use sacrificial anodes (Zn, Mg, Al) or impressed current CP. |
| 6 | **Add corrosion inhibitors** | In the electrolyte (e.g., chromates, phosphates, nitrites — historically). |
| 7 | **Control the electrolyte** | Drain pockets, reduce humidity, use desiccants, avoid chloride exposure. |
| 8 | **Use third-metal transition pieces** | Insert a metallurgically intermediate alloy (e.g., Monel) between the two extremes. |
| 9 | **Weld with compatible filler** | Avoid using noble filler on active base metals. |
| 10 | **Design for easy replacement** | Place the expected-to-corrode component where it is easy to inspect/replace. |

> *Key practical example:* In aircraft construction, aluminium rivets are used *in aluminium sheets* (compatible). When steel fasteners are unavoidable, they are **cadmium-plated** (cadmium is slightly more active than aluminium and acts as a sacrificial coating) and **isolated with fibre washers** to break the electrical path.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks in galvanic-corrosion questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing anode and cathode.** Examiner's note: *Always state the anode first and identify it as the more active / less noble metal.* Drawing arrows of electron flow *from anode to cathode through the metal* and *ion flow through the electrolyte* — many students reverse these.
> 2. **Quoting the EMF series for alloys.** Stainless steel in the EMF series shows chromium as active, but in practice (galvanic series) it can be passive and noble. Examiner penalises use of EMF series for engineering decisions.
> 3. **Forgetting the area effect derivation.** Simply stating "small anode + large cathode = bad" gets only partial credit. The $CR_a \propto 1/A_a$ proportionality and the $A_c/A_a$ amplification factor are expected.
> 4. **Recommending "coat the anode" as prevention.** This is the classic wrong answer. The correct answer is **"coat the cathode"** — a coated anode with pinholes makes the corrosion *worse*.
> 5. **Writing the half-reactions incorrectly.** Examiner expects balanced half-reactions and the correct stoichiometric electron count.
> 6. **Forgetting units in numerical problems.** Nernst equation calculations must specify the temperature and the base used (natural log vs. log₁₀).
> 7. **Skipping the sketch.** Even in part-(a) theory questions, a labelled diagram is worth 2–3 marks on its own.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Galvanic Corrosion — 1-page mental model**

- **Definition:** Accelerated corrosion of the *less noble* metal in a dissimilar-metal couple immersed in a common electrolyte.
- **Conditions (DIME):** Dissimilar metals, Intermetallic contact, Moist electrolyte, Electrochemical path.
- **Anode** = more active (less noble) metal — *dissolves*; **Cathode** = more noble metal — *protected*.
- **Galvanic series (seawater):** Mg → Zn → Al → Fe, Steel → Pb, Sn → Brass, Cu → Ni, SS (passive) → Ag, Au, Pt.
- **Driving EMF:** $E_{cell} = E_{cathode}^{\circ} - E_{anode}^{\circ}$. Positive $E_{cell}$ → spontaneous.
- **Nernst equation (25 °C):** $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n}\log_{10}Q$.
- **Corrosion rate:** $CR = \dfrac{0.00327 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho}$ in mm/yr (with $i_{corr}$ in µA/cm²).
- **Area effect (the most-tested formula):** $\dfrac{CR_{a}^{galv}}{CR_{a}^{free}} \approx \dfrac{A_{c}}{A_{a}}$. Small anode + large cathode = **catastrophic attack**.
- **Coatings rule:** *Coat the cathode, never just the anode.*
- **Distance effect:** corrosion severity decreases as the distance between dissimilar metals increases.
- **Prevention toolbox:** compatible materials, area-ratio design, insulating gaskets, sacrificial anodes, impressed-current CP, inhibitors, drainage design, transition alloys.
- **Key engineering examples:** Cu rivets in steel sheet (good) vs. steel rivets in Cu sheet (bad); marine propeller on steel shaft; heat-exchanger tube-to-tube-sheet joints; galvanized pipe connected to copper pipe.
- **High-frequency KTU keywords to memorise:** *galvanic couple, EMF series, galvanic series, mixed potential, charge balance, area effect, sacrificial anode, cathodic protection, polarization, Evans diagram*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Crevice corrosion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT527 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Crevice corrosion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Crevice corrosion** is a highly localized form of attack that occurs within narrow gaps, slots, seams, or crevices where a stagnant electrolyte solution is trapped and shielded from the bulk environment. The attack is driven by differences in oxygen concentration, metal ion concentration, and pH between the occluded (crevice) interior and the external (exposed) surface, producing an electrochemical concentration cell (differential aeration cell).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Crevice corrosion is classified under *Forms of Localized Corrosion* (Module 2) and is considered a high-frequency examination topic because it tests a student's grasp of differential aeration cells, anodic/cathodic area ratios, and the autocatalytic nature of localized attack.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard IUPAC / NACE Terminology:** The narrow space that initiates the attack is called the **crevice** (typically gap width **< 0.1 mm to 0.5 mm**). Below this range, capillary action is too strong and ionic diffusion may be partially restricted; above it, bulk convection prevents the necessary stagnation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Blocked Drain" Picture)

Imagine two connected water tanks:
- **Tank A (the bulk/outside)**: A large, open swimming pool. Oxygen from the atmosphere dissolves freely into the water, and any disturbance (a swimmer, a wave) instantly replenishes the dissolved O₂.
- **Tank B (the crevice)**: A small, narrow pipe connected to Tank A, with a dead-end. Once the water in this pipe is consumed (oxygen is depleted by cathodic reaction), no new oxygen can flow in because the pipe is too narrow for convection and diffusion is extremely slow.

Now place an iron plate in both tanks and short-circuit them electrically (as metals are). The plate in Tank A (oxygen-rich) becomes the **cathode**, and the plate in Tank B (oxygen-starved) becomes the **anode** that dissolves aggressively. The narrow pipe also traps the dissolved metal ions and chloride ions, acidifying the crevice. The pool stays clean, but the pipe eats through the metal from the inside.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Hook for Examiners:** Always explain crevice corrosion as a **"small-anode / large-cathode" geometry** — a deadly combination because a tiny area must supply all the electrons demanded by a vast cathodic surface, leading to extremely high anodic current densities (iA) and rapid penetration.

## 1.3 Common Engineering Locations

| Location | Typical Gap Width | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|
| Gasket surfaces | 0.05 – 0.2 mm | Flanged pipe joints in chemical plants |
| Bolt / rivet heads | < 0.1 mm | Lap joints in ship hulls |
| Threaded connections | 0.1 – 0.3 mm | Heat-exchanger tube sheets |
| Tube-to-tube-sheet | < 0.1 mm | Condenser tubes in power plants |
| Sediment / debris under | 0.1 – 1.0 mm | Stainless steel tanks with scale deposits |
| Weld spatter zones | variable | Poorly cleaned welded stainless pipes |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Stage Mechanism (Initiation + Propagation)

### **Stage I — Initiation (Induction Period)**

1. The crevice is filled with aerated electrolyte (e.g., NaCl solution) initially in equilibrium with the bulk solution.
2. **Oxygen reduction** (the only available cathodic reaction at near-neutral pH) consumes dissolved O₂ both inside and outside the crevice:
   - $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$
3. Inside the crevice, geometry restricts O₂ replenishment by convection → **O₂ concentration falls to near zero** within minutes to hours.
4. Outside, O₂ remains at saturation. A **differential aeration cell** is established.

### **Stage II — Propagation (Autocatalytic Loop)**

Once Stage I is complete, four coupled sub-processes accelerate the attack:

| Step | Process | Reaction / Physical Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **A** | Anodic metal dissolution inside crevice | $Fe \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^-$ (or M → M^n+ + ne^-) |
| **B** | Cathodic O₂ reduction at the mouth (exterior) | $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$ |
| **C** | Cation hydrolysis → acidification | $Fe^{2+} + 2H_2O \rightarrow Fe(OH)_2 + 2H^+$ |
| **D** | Electroneutrality-driven Cl⁻ migration | Cl⁻ ions diffuse into the crevice to balance the positive charge of dissolved metal ions |

The result is a **self-sustaining (autocatalytic)** aggressive microenvironment with:
- **Low pH (often 1–3)**,
- **High [Cl⁻]** (can be 3–10× bulk concentration),
- **High [M^n+]**,
- **Zero dissolved oxygen**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" of Autocatalysis:** The more metal that dissolves, the more H⁺ and Cl⁻ are produced/trapped, which further accelerates dissolution. The system is its own catalyst.

## 2.2 Critical Design Equations

While crevice corrosion is largely descriptive, certain quantitative relations govern the rate and threshold:

### (a) Nernst Equation for the Dissolved-Oxygen Half-Cell

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{O_2/OH^-} &= E^0_{O_2/OH^-} - \frac{0.0591}{n}\log\frac{[OH^-]^4}{p_{O_2}[H_2O]^2} \\
&\approx 1.23 - 0.0591 \cdot pH + 0.0148 \cdot \log p_{O_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $E^0 = +1.23\ \text{V (SHE)}$, $n = 4$, and $p_{O_2}$ is the partial pressure of O₂. This shows that **lowering pH raises E** (more cathodic / more aggressive) and **lowering pO₂ lowers E** (more anodic / less cathodic) — directly establishing the differential aeration driving force.

### (b) Charge-Conservation / Evans Diagram Coupling

For the dissimilar aeration cell, Evans mixed-potential theory gives:

$$
i_a \cdot A_a = i_c \cdot A_c
$$

Where $i_a$ and $i_c$ are the anodic and cathodic current densities, and $A_a$, $A_c$ are their respective areas. Because $A_c \gg A_a$ in crevice geometry, $i_a \gg i_c$, leading to very high local penetration.

### (c) Penetration Rate (Faraday's Law)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CR} &= \frac{i_a \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho} \quad \text{(mm/year)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

For Fe: M = **55.85 g/mol**, n = 2, F = **96 485 C/mol**, ρ = **7.87 g/cm³** — yielding a constant $K_{Fe} = 0.0116\ \text{mm·g/(A·cm·yr)}$.

### (d) Critical Crevice Temperature (CCT)

A practical KTU-favorite parameter for stainless steels:

$$
\text{CCT} = \text{CPT} - \text{(a constant offset, typically 15–25 °C)}
$$

Where **CPT** = Critical Pitting Temperature (ASTM G48). The CCT is the lowest temperature at which crevice corrosion initiates in a standardized test.

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Parameter | Expression | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Aeration cell EMF | $\Delta E = E_{out} - E_{in} = \dfrac{0.0591}{4}\log\dfrac{p_{O_2}^{out}}{p_{O_2}^{in}}$ | Driving force for the corrosion cell |
| 2 | Anodic current density | $i_a = \dfrac{i_c \cdot A_c}{A_a}$ | Shows why small anode → high attack |
| 3 | Corrosion rate | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{K \cdot i_a \cdot M}{n \cdot \rho}$ | mm/yr penetration prediction |
| 4 | Hydrolysis acidification | $M^{n+} + nH_2O \rightarrow M(OH)_n + nH^+$ | Quantifies $\Delta pH$ inside crevice |
| 5 | Mass-action Cl⁻ enrichment | $\dfrac{[Cl^-]_{crevice}}{[Cl^-]_{bulk}} \approx 1 + \dfrac{[M^{n+}]_{crevice}}{[M^{n+}]_{bulk}}$ | Predicts salt-concentration gradient |
| 6 | CCT vs CPT | $\text{CCT} = \text{CPT} - 15\ \text{to}\ 25\ °C$ | Stainless-steel selection in seawater |
| 7 | Passivity breakdown | $E > E_{pit/crevice}\ (\text{vs. SCE})$ | Threshold for stable propagation |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineer's Rule of Thumb:** If a KTU numerical gives a current density of $i_a = 10\ \text{mA/cm}^2$ in a crevice of area ratio $A_c/A_a = 1000$, expect a corrosion rate of order **0.1–0.5 mm/year locally** — enough to perforate a 3 mm sheet in 6–30 years, often faster with chloride assistance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: The Differential Aeration EMF

We derive the open-circuit potential difference between the exterior (oxygen-rich) and the interior (oxygen-depleted) of a crevice, assuming both surfaces are covered with a thin film of near-neutral NaCl electrolyte.

### Step 1 — Write the Nernst expression at the exterior

For the cathodic reaction at the mouth of the crevice, with bulk pH ≈ 7 and $p_{O_2}^{out} \approx 0.21$ atm (atmospheric):

$$
E_{out} = E^0 - \frac{0.0591}{4}\log\frac{[OH^-]^4}{p_{O_2}^{out}} = 1.23 - 0.0591(7) + \frac{0.0591}{4}\log(0.21)
$$

### Step 2 — Numerical evaluation of the first two terms

$$
E_{out} = 1.23 - 0.4137 + (0.01478)\log(0.21) = 1.23 - 0.4137 + 0.01478 \times (-0.6778)
$$

$$
E_{out} = 1.23 - 0.4137 - 0.01002 = 0.8063\ \text{V (SHE)}
$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate $E_{in}$ assuming O₂ is fully depleted

For a fully deaerated crevice, set $p_{O_2}^{in} \approx 10^{-4}$ atm (typical dissolved-O₂ limit under stagnant conditions):

$$
E_{in} = 1.23 - 0.4137 + 0.01478 \times \log(10^{-4}) = 0.8163 + 0.01478 \times (-4) = 0.8163 - 0.0591 = 0.7572\ \text{V (SHE)}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the cell EMF

$$
\Delta E = E_{out} - E_{in} = 0.8063 - 0.7572 = 0.0491\ \text{V}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A small EMF of only ~50 mV is sufficient to drive a vigorous localized cell, because the *internal resistance* of the thin electrolyte film in the crevice is extremely low, allowing large currents to circulate.

### Step 5 — Resulting anodic current density (by area-ratio)

Suppose the cathodic area $A_c = 100\ \text{cm}^2$ and the anodic area inside the crevice $A_a = 0.1\ \text{cm}^2$. The cathodic current density on bare metal is typically $i_c = 10\ \mu A/cm^2$:

$$
i_a = \frac{i_c \cdot A_c}{A_a} = \frac{10 \times 10^{-6} \times 100}{0.1} = 1.0 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{A/cm}^2 = 10\ \text{mA/cm}^2
$$

### Step 6 — Convert to penetration rate (Fe substrate)

Using Faraday's law with the iron constant $K_{Fe} = 0.0116\ \text{mm·g/(A·cm·yr)}$:

$$
\text{CR} = \frac{0.0116 \times i_a \times M_{Fe}}{n \cdot \rho_{Fe}} = \frac{0.0116 \times 10 \times 10^{-3} \times 55.85}{2 \times 7.87}
$$

$$
\text{CR} = \frac{6.479 \times 10^{-3}}{15.74} = 4.12 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{mm/yr} \times 1000 = 0.412\ \text{mm/yr (Fe-equivalent)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Note:** If the question states "stainless steel" you must apply the *passive* corrosion current (orders of magnitude lower, $\mu A/cm^2$), giving a CR of $10^{-3}$–$10^{-2}$ mm/yr, **but** with the breakdown (pitting) penetration factored in (use PREN: %Cr + 3.3×%Mo + 16×%N).

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Crevice Acidification via Hydrolysis

Given a crevice that has produced $[Fe^{2+}] = 0.01$ M locally, compute the resulting pH.

### Step 1 — Hydrolysis equilibrium

$$
Fe^{2+} + 2H_2O \rightleftharpoons Fe(OH)_2 + 2H^+ \quad K_{h,1} \approx 10^{-13.5}
$$

For the simplified mononuclear hydrolysis: $K_h = \dfrac{[H^+]^2}{[Fe^{2+}][H_2O]^2} \approx \dfrac{[H^+]^2}{[Fe^{2+}]}$

### Step 2 — Solve for $[H^+]$

$$
[H^+]^2 = K_h \cdot [Fe^{2+}] = 10^{-13.5} \times 10^{-2} = 10^{-15.5}
$$

$$
[H^+] = 10^{-7.75}\ \text{M} \Rightarrow pH \approx 7.75
$$

### Step 3 — Consider the chloride-promoted second-stage acidification

With Cl⁻ co-migration, $Fe^{2+}$ is partially stabilized as $FeCl^+$ complexes, suppressing back-hydrolysis. Empirically, crevice pH in active stainless-steel crevices drops to **1–3**, an additional factor of 10⁴–10⁶ in $[H^+]$.

### Step 4 — Final expression for pH drop

$$
\Delta pH = pH_{bulk} - pH_{crevice} \approx \frac{1}{2}\left(\log[Fe^{2+}]_{crevice} - \log K_h\right) - \log f_{Cl^-}
$$

Where $f_{Cl^-}$ is a chloride-stabilization factor (typically 10²–10⁴ for seawater).

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this Rule of Thumb for KTU:** "Every 10-fold increase in dissolved metal ion concentration inside the crevice lowers the local pH by approximately 0.5 units, until Cl⁻ stabilizes the metal chloride complexes and the pH plummets to 1–3."

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Crevice vs. Pitting

| Property | Crevice Corrosion | Pitting Corrosion |
|---|---|---|
| Initiator | Geometry (gap) | Surface film breakdown |
| Autocatalytic? | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| Anode location | Hidden, inside gap | At film defect (visible) |
| Cathode location | External, around mouth | Surrounding passive film |
| $A_c/A_a$ ratio | **Very high (100–10 000)** | Moderate (10–100) |
| Initiating electrolyte | Stagnant bulk | Often requires Cl⁻ to break film |
| Detection | Difficult (concealed) | Easier (visible pits) |
| Prevention | Eliminate gaps; weld instead of bolt; use solid gaskets | Raise CPT; raise Mo/Cr content |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Crevice Corrosion Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 0: Aerated Electrolyte Fills Crevice] --> B[Stage 1: O2 Reduction Consumes Dissolved O2]
    B --> C[Stage 1b: Convection Cannot Replenish O2 Inside]
    C --> D[Stage 1c: Differential Aeration Cell Established]
    D --> E[Stage 2a: Anode Inside Crevice: M dissolves to Mn+]
    E --> F[Stage 2b: Cathode Outside: O2 + 2H2O + 4e- to 4OH-]
    F --> G[Stage 2c: Hydrolysis: Mn+ + nH2O to M OHn + nH+]
    G --> H[Stage 2d: pH inside drops to 1 to 3]
    H --> I[Stage 2e: Electroneutrality forces Cl- migration inward]
    I --> J[Stage 2f: Cl- + Mn+ form soluble chloro-complexes]
    J --> K[Stage 2g: Aggressive Local Environment Sustains Dissolution]
    K --> L[Stage 3: Autocatalytic Propagation and Penetration]
    L --> M[Final: Perforation or Component Failure]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of the Crevice Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Outside[BULK EXTERIOR - O2 rich, near-neutral pH]
        Cathode[External Cathode Surface] --> CathodicRxn[O2 Reduction: 2H2O + O2 + 4e- to 4 OH-]
    end
    subgraph Inside[CREVICE INTERIOR - O2 starved, low pH, high Cl-]
        Anode[Internal Anode Surface] --> AnodicRxn[Metal Dissolution: M to Mn+ + ne-]
        HydrolysisBlock[Hydrolysis Step: Mn+ + nH2O to M OHn + nH+] --> AcidEnv[Acidified Microenvironment]
        ClMigration[Cl- Migration for Charge Balance] --> AcidEnv
        AcidEnv --> Anode
    end
    CathodicRxn -. electrons flow through metal .-> Anode
    Anode -. Mn+ ions diffuse outward .-> Cathode
```

## 4.3 Mermaid — Prevention Strategy Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Design Stage] --> Q1{Can the gap be eliminated?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> A1[Use full-penetration welds or butt joints]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is the electrolyte stagnant?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> A2[Provide continuous drainage and ventilation]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Can alloy be upgraded?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> A3[Use Mo-bearing austenitic SS e.g. 316 or 6Mo super-austenitic]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Cathodic protection feasible?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> A4[Apply sacrificial anode or impressed current CP]
    Q4 -- No --> A5[Use non-absorbent gaskets and seal crevices]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define crevice corrosion. List any four common locations where it occurs in engineering service.**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Crevice corrosion is a localized form of electrochemical attack occurring within narrow gaps or occluded regions where a stagnant electrolyte is trapped, creating a differential aeration cell between the aerated exterior and oxygen-starved interior. The driving force is the difference in dissolved-oxygen concentration.

**Four common locations:**
1. Gasket faces of flanged joints in chemical pipelines.
2. Tube-to-tube-sheet rolled joints in heat exchangers and condensers.
3. Beneath deposits of sand, scale, or biofilm on stainless-steel tank bottoms.
4. Riveted or bolted lap joints in ship hulls and structural members.

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Allocation Pattern (3 marks):** Definition (1 mark) + 4 locations (0.5 each = 2 marks). Examiners deduct 0.5 marks if the differential-aeration aspect is omitted from the definition.

---

### **Q2. Why is the small-anode/large-cathode geometry so destructive in crevice corrosion?**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
In a crevice, the anodic area is restricted to the small interior surface while the cathodic area is the entire exposed exterior surface. By charge conservation $i_a A_a = i_c A_c$, the anodic current density $i_a$ becomes amplified by the ratio $A_c/A_a$ (often 100–10 000). This means the limited anodic area must supply electrons to a vast cathodic area, producing a very high local dissolution rate and rapid penetration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Comment (3 marks):** A complete answer must include (i) the charge-balance equation, (ii) the meaning of the area ratio, and (iii) a numerical or qualitative comment on penetration rate.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> KTU 2024 Scheme Note: Each Part B question offers an internal choice. Question A and Question B below are **independent alternatives** the examiner may print on the question paper.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the mechanism of crevice corrosion in a stainless-steel flange assembly exposed to seawater. Highlight the differential aeration cell and the autocatalytic nature of the attack. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the EMF developed across a differential aeration cell with exterior $p_{O_2} = 0.21$ atm and a fully deaerated crevice with $p_{O_2} = 10^{-4}$ atm at pH 7 and 25 °C. Comment on whether this EMF alone is sufficient to cause significant corrosion. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Sketch description (2 marks):**
A flange pair is shown with a soft gasket compressed between the faces, leaving a micro-gap of order 0.05–0.2 mm. The outside is bathed in flowing aerated seawater; the crevice is filled with stagnant seawater.

**Step 2 — Initiation (1.5 marks):**
Oxygen is reduced at all surfaces initially: $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$. Inside the crevice, restricted diffusion causes [O₂] to fall to near zero within hours, while outside [O₂] remains at saturation. The cell EMF established is:

$$
\Delta E = \frac{0.0591}{4}\log\dfrac{0.21}{10^{-4}} \approx 0.06\ \text{V}
$$

**Step 3 — Propagation / Autocatalysis (2.5 marks):**
- **Anode (inside):** $Fe \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^-$ (or $Cr \rightarrow Cr^{3+} + 3e^-$ for SS).
- **Cathode (outside):** Continued O₂ reduction.
- **Hydrolysis:** $Fe^{2+} + 2H_2O \rightarrow Fe(OH)_2 + 2H^+$, dropping pH to 1–3.
- **Cl⁻ migration:** Charge balance pulls Cl⁻ into the crevice; combined with H⁺, this forms a highly aggressive HCl-like environment that depassivates the stainless steel.
- The reactions produce *more* aggressive conditions, which in turn accelerate the reactions — hence **autocatalytic**.

**Step 4 — Conclusion (1 mark):**
Once initiated, the crevice becomes self-sustaining; perforation typically occurs within months for 3 mm SS 304 in warm seawater.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Nernst for aerated exterior (2 marks):**
$$
E_{out} = 1.23 - 0.0591(7) + \frac{0.0591}{4}\log(0.21) = +0.806\ \text{V (SHE)}
$$

**Step 2 — Nernst for deaerated crevice (2 marks):**
$$
E_{in} = 1.23 - 0.0591(7) + \frac{0.0591}{4}\log(10^{-4}) = +0.757\ \text{V (SHE)}
$$

**Step 3 — Cell EMF (1 mark):**
$$
\Delta E = 0.806 - 0.757 = 0.049\ \text{V}
$$

**Step 4 — Sufficiency of EMF (2 marks):**
A mere 50 mV EMF is sufficient because (i) the internal resistance $R$ of the thin electrolyte film in the crevice is very low (ohm-level or less), so the current $I = \Delta E / R$ can be substantial, and (ii) the cathodic-to-anodic area ratio amplifies the anodic current density. Therefore, even a small driving force produces rapid local penetration.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss the factors that influence the initiation and propagation of crevice corrosion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A stainless-steel flange joint in a chemical plant has a crevice area ratio $A_c/A_a = 500$. The cathodic current density on the outer surface is $i_c = 12\ \mu A/cm^2$. Calculate (i) the anodic current density inside the crevice, and (ii) the resulting localized penetration rate in mm/yr, assuming the substrate is iron (Fe) and using the Faraday constant $K_{Fe} = 0.0116\ \text{mm·g/(A·cm·yr)}$ with M = 55.85 g/mol, n = 2, ρ = 7.87 g/cm³. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**Metallurgical factors (2.5 marks):**
1. **Alloy composition:** Higher Cr, Mo, and N raise the Critical Crevice Temperature (CCT). The PREN (Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number) is given by:
   $$\text{PREN} = \%Cr + 3.3 \times \%Mo + 16 \times \%N$$
   A PREN > 32 is recommended for seawater service.
2. **Surface finish:** Smoother surfaces reduce crevice initiation.
3. **Cold work / sensitization:** Sensitization (Cr-carbide precipitation at grain boundaries) depletes Cr at the boundary, drastically reducing crevice resistance.

**Environmental factors (2.5 marks):**
1. **Chloride concentration:** Threshold typically 100–1000 ppm Cl⁻ for austenitic SS.
2. **Temperature:** Initiation rate follows Arrhenius behavior until CCT.
3. **pH of bulk solution:** Lower bulk pH accelerates initiation.
4. **Dissolved oxygen:** Higher [O₂] outside increases the differential-aeration driving force.

**Geometric factors (2 marks):**
1. **Gap width:** Critical range is 0.025–0.4 mm; outside this, attack does not initiate.
2. **Crevice depth:** Deeper crevices are more aggressive because diffusion paths are longer.
3. **Area ratio $A_c/A_a$:** Larger ratios give higher anodic current density.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Anodic current density (2 marks):**

Using $i_a A_a = i_c A_c$:

$$
i_a = i_c \cdot \frac{A_c}{A_a} = 12 \times 10^{-6} \times 500 = 6.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{A/cm}^2 = 6.0\ \text{mA/cm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Penetration rate via Faraday (3 marks):**

$$
\text{CR} = \frac{K \cdot i_a \cdot M}{n \cdot \rho}
$$

Plug in $K = 0.0116$, $i_a = 6.0 \times 10^{-3}$, $M = 55.85$, $n = 2$, $\rho = 7.87$:

$$
\text{CR} = \frac{0.0116 \times 6.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 55.85}{2 \times 7.87} = \frac{3.887 \times 10^{-3}}{15.74} = 2.47 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{mm/yr}
$$

Wait — this is too low for iron because $i_a$ was given in $\mu A$ scale. Recheck unit conversion: $i_a = 6.0 \times 10^{-3}\ A/cm^2$ corresponds to a CR consistent with active iron. Multiplying by the dimensional constant:

$$
\text{CR} = 0.0116 \times (6.0 \times 10^{-3}) \times \frac{55.85}{2 \times 7.87} = 2.47 \times 10^{-4} \times 3.55 = 8.77 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{mm/yr}
$$

**Step 3 — Corrected unified form (1 mark):**

Using the standard Fe constant $1.16 \times 10^{-3}$ mm/yr per mA/cm² (already includes M, n, ρ):

$$
\text{CR} = 1.16 \times 10^{-3} \times 6.0 = 6.96 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{mm/yr} \approx 0.007\ \text{mm/yr}
$$

**Step 4 — Comment (1 mark):**
For a structural 3 mm-thick flange, this would still take ~430 years — but in practice, once passivity is lost and Cl⁻ concentrates, $i_a$ rises by 1–3 orders of magnitude, reducing the perforation time to a few years. Always state the assumption clearly.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Error:** $i_a$ must be in **A/cm²** before substituting into Faraday's equation; using mA/cm² without scaling by $10^{-3}$ is the #1 reason students lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Missing area-ratio justification:** Marks are awarded for explicitly invoking the charge-conservation equation $i_a A_a = i_c A_c$. Skipping this step costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Ignoring Cl⁻ amplification:** A perfect Faraday calculation giving 0.007 mm/yr is *physically misleading* unless you add a comment about how chloride complexation and depassivation raise $i_a$ by 10²–10³×. Examiners reward the engineering commentary.
> 4. **Confusion of CPT vs CCT:** CCT is **always lower** than CPT for the same alloy. Reversing this relationship loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Forgetting to label diagram axes/labels** in Part A of Question A: the sketch is worth 2 marks — clearly mark the anode, cathode, gap width, and oxygen gradient.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Crevice Corrosion**

- **Definition:** Localized attack in narrow gaps due to differential aeration between interior (anode, O₂-starved) and exterior (cathode, O₂-rich).
- **Critical gap width:** ~0.025 mm to 0.4 mm; below this, capillary blockage prevents electrolyte entry; above it, bulk convection eliminates the gradient.
- **Required geometry:** Stagnant electrolyte + restricted O₂ access + small anode / large cathode.
- **Initiation stage:** O₂ depletion inside the crevice; duration minutes to hours.
- **Propagation stage:** Metal dissolution + hydrolysis (H⁺ generation) + Cl⁻ migration → pH drops to 1–3, [Cl⁻] rises 3–10× → autocatalytic.
- **Driving force EMF:** $\Delta E \approx 0.04$–$0.06$ V, but huge area ratio makes this sufficient.
- **Key formula:** $i_a = i_c \cdot (A_c / A_a)$ — small anode, large cathode.
- **Faraday penetration rate:** $\text{CR} = \dfrac{0.0116 \cdot i_a \cdot M}{n \cdot \rho}$ (mm/yr).
- **CCT = CPT − 15 to 25 °C** for stainless steels.
- **PREN ≥ 32** recommended for seawater service (e.g., 6Mo super-austenitic grades).
- **Common sites:** Flange gaskets, tube-to-tube-sheet joints, bolt/rivet heads, beneath deposits, threaded fittings.
- **Prevention toolkit:** (1) Eliminate gaps by welding or using solid gaskets, (2) Drain and ventilate to prevent stagnation, (3) Use Mo-bearing alloys, (4) Apply cathodic protection, (5) Smooth surface finish, (6) Avoid sensitization by proper heat treatment (solution annealing).
- **Comparison anchors:** Crevice is *concealed* and *geometry-driven*; pitting is *visible* and *film-breakdown-driven*. Both are autocatalytic.
- **Examiner's favorites:** (1) Mechanism diagram with reactions, (2) EMF derivation, (3) Faraday penetration calculation with area-ratio argument, (4) Comparison table crevice vs. pitting, (5) CCT vs. CPT distinction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pitting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT527 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pitting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Pitting Corrosion** is an extremely *localized* form of attack on a metal surface, confined to a small area, which results in the formation of **cavities or "pits"** that penetrate from the surface downward into the bulk of the metal. The bulk of the surface remains largely unattacked, while a small volume of metal is lost at accelerating rates due to the establishment of an **autocatalytic (self-sustaining) electrochemical cell** within the pit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board-Definition Format:** Pitting is a *localized* corrosion phenomenon in which a passive film (typically on stainless steels, Al, Ti, Ni alloys) undergoes localized breakdown, creating anodic sites surrounded by large cathodic areas. The depth of attack is **greater than the lateral spread**, making it the most destructive form of corrosion per unit mass lost.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **freshly tarred road** in Kerala during the monsoon. Rainwater accumulates in a tiny *pin-hole* crack. Over weeks, that water seeps under the tar, dissolves the underlying bitumen, and creates a deep **pothole** — while the rest of the road still *looks* perfectly fine. 

That pothole is a **pit**. The hidden water is the **aggressive electrolyte** (often Cl⁻ ions). The surrounding tar is the **passive film** that broke down at one weak spot. The deeper the pothole grows, the more it traps water and accelerates. This is exactly how pitting corrosion behaves — *insidious, hidden, and catastrophic relative to its size.*

### 1.3 Key Metrics Used in Pitting Engineering

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Range / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Pitting Potential** | $E_{pit}$ | mV vs SCE (typically +200 to +1000 mV) |
| **Protection / Repassivation Potential** | $E_{prot}$ | mV vs SCE (always $<$ $E_{pit}$) |
| **Critical Pitting Temperature** | **CPT** | °C (ASTM G48 test) |
| **Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number** | **PREN** | Dimensionless (typically 18–60) |
| **Pitting Factor** | $P_f$ | Ratio (always $\geq 1$) |

> [!NOTE]
> A **Pitting Factor of 1** means uniform corrosion. Values **> 5** indicate a strong tendency toward localized pitting. KTU 2024 board answers should always quote the *numerical value* of $P_f$ along with the equation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanism — Step-by-Step Breakdown

Pitting corrosion does NOT happen in one step. It is a **four-stage process**, and KTU examiners LOVE asking you to enumerate these stages for full marks.

### Stage 1 — **Passivity Breakdown (Initiation)**
- The metal is normally protected by a thin, adherent **passive oxide film** (e.g., Cr₂O₃ on stainless steel, Al₂O₃ on aluminum).
- **Aggressive anions** — most commonly **chloride ions (Cl⁻)** — adsorb onto the passive film at local "weak spots" (inclusions, grain boundaries, scratches).
- Adsorbed Cl⁻ displaces oxygen in the film, locally thinning it until the film *ruptures* and exposes the bare metal.

### Stage 2 — **Pit Nucleation (Metastable Pitting)**
- The exposed metal becomes a microscopic **anode**.
- Electrons released flow to the surrounding **passive surface**, which now acts as the **cathode** (oxygen reduction).
- A **very small cathode : anode area ratio** is created — this is the *deadly* geometric condition that drives pitting.

### Stage 3 — **Pit Propagation (Autocatalytic Growth)**
Inside the pit:
- Metal dissolves: $\text{M} \rightarrow \text{M}^{n+} + n e^-$
- Hydrolysis of metal cations generates H⁺: $\text{M}^{n+} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{M(OH)}^{n-1} + \text{H}^+$
- The pit interior becomes **acidic** (pH may drop to **0–2**), and **Cl⁻ migrates in** to maintain charge neutrality.
- Oxygen is depleted inside the pit → differential aeration cell.
- This is **autocatalytic** because the products of dissolution *accelerate further dissolution*.

### Stage 4 — **Pit Termination (Repassivation or Perforation)**
- If conditions change (potential drops below $E_{prot}$, or the pit is exposed to aerated solution), the pit may **repassivate** and stop.
- If propagation continues, the pit may grow **completely through** the wall → **catastrophic failure** (e.g., tube leakage in a heat exchanger).

> [!TIP]
> KTU valuation tip: When asked "Explain pitting mechanism", always draw a *labeled pit cross-section* showing the anode, cathode, hydrolysis reaction, Cl⁻ migration, and oxygen gradient. Examiners award full 7 marks only when the **autocatalytic nature** is explicitly stated.

## 2.2 Pitting Morphology (KTU Diagram-Question Favorite)

| Type | Description | Typical Alloy |
|---|---|---|
| **Deep, narrow pits** | High aspect ratio; most dangerous | Austenitic SS in Cl⁻ |
| **Shallow, wide pits** | Low aspect ratio; less penetrative | Aluminum in mild Cl⁻ |
| **Subsurface pits** | Grow *sideways* under the surface | Aluminum alloys |
| **Etch pits** | Crystallographic orientation visible | Nickel, Ti |

## 2.3 Pitting Potential & Hysteresis Loop

The **cyclic polarization curve** is the single most-tested graph in this module.

- **Forward scan** (anodic): current rises sharply at $E_{pit}$.
- **Reverse scan** (cathodic): current drops to the passive value at $E_{prot}$ (repassivation potential).
- The **hysteresis loop** between $E_{pit}$ and $E_{prot}$ indicates pitting susceptibility.
  - **Wide loop** = high susceptibility.
  - **No loop** = material resists pitting.

**Key rule:** $E_{pit} > E_{prot}$ *always* holds for a pitting-susceptible system.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{pit} > E_{prot}$ | Susceptibility criterion | mV (SCE) |
| 2 | $P_f = \dfrac{d_{max}}{d_{avg}}$ | Pitting factor (depth) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \cdot \%\text{Mo} + 16 \cdot \%\text{N}$ | Pitting Resistance Equiv. No. | dimensionless |
| 4 | $d = k \cdot t^{n}$ | Pit depth vs time (logarithmic) | mm, years |
| 5 | $d = k \cdot \log(t)$ | Alternative pit-growth law | mm, years |
| 6 | $\Delta E = E_{pit} - E_{prot}$ | Hysteresis loop width | mV |
| 7 | $\text{CPT} = f(\%Cr, \%Mo, \%N)$ | Critical Pitting Temperature (ASTM G48) | °C |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pipeline note:** $n$ in the power law $d = k t^n$ is **experimentally observed** to lie between **0.3 and 0.5** for most stainless steels. This is *not* a parabolic ($\sqrt{t}$) law like high-temperature oxidation — this distinction is a common KTU one-mark trap.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pitting studies are **critical** in:
- **Offshore oil & gas** platforms (Cl⁻ in seawater — Arabian Gulf, Bay of Bengal)
- **Chemical process industry** — heat exchangers, evaporator tubes
- **Marine propellers and pumps** (Kerala's fishing-fleet industry)
- **Nuclear power plants** — sensitized stainless steel piping
- **Biomedical implants** — Ti-6Al-4V in physiological saline

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Pitting Factor $P_f$

The Pitting Factor is defined as the ratio of the *deepest* metal penetration to the *average* penetration calculated from mass loss.

**Step 1 — Average Penetration**

Average penetration is obtained from mass-loss data using Faraday's law:

$$d_{avg} = \frac{W \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A}$$

where:
- $W$ = mass loss in grams
- $M$ = atomic mass of metal (g/mol)
- $n$ = valence of metal ions
- $F$ = Faraday constant = **96500 C/mol**
- $\rho$ = density of metal (g/cm³)
- $A$ = exposed surface area (cm²)

**Step 2 — Deepest Pit Penetration**

The deepest pit is measured *physically* using a calibrated microscope or needle-point depth gauge (ASTM G46 standard).

$$d_{max} = \text{measured maximum pit depth (mm)}$$

**Step 3 — Combine**

$$\boxed{P_f = \frac{d_{max}}{d_{avg}}}$$

**Interpretation scale:**

$$P_f = 1 \Rightarrow \text{uniform corrosion}$$

$$P_f \approx 1\text{ to }2 \Rightarrow \text{mild pitting}$$

$$P_f \approx 2\text{ to }5 \Rightarrow \text{moderate pitting}$$

$$P_f > 5 \Rightarrow \text{severe pitting}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation — PREN Formula

The **Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number (PREN)** is an empirical formula widely used to rank stainless steels. It arises from the observation that **Cr, Mo, and N all improve pitting resistance**, with Mo and N being far more potent than Cr.

**Step 1 — Empirical weighting of alloying elements**

From extensive cyclic-potentiodynamic-polarization data on austenitic stainless steels, the following weighting factors were established:

- Cr: coefficient = 1
- Mo: coefficient = 3.3
- N: coefficient = 16

**Step 2 — Form the linear combination**

$$\text{PREN} = (\%\text{Cr}) + 3.3 \cdot (\%\text{Mo}) + 16 \cdot (\%\text{N})$$

For **duplex stainless steels**, the modified formula includes a +1 bonus (since duplexes perform better than predicted):

$$\text{PREN}_{duplex} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \cdot \%\text{Mo} + 16 \cdot \%\text{N} + 1$$

**Step 3 — Engineering interpretation**

$$\text{PREN} < 18 \Rightarrow \text{Not seawater resistant}$$

$$18 \leq \text{PREN} \leq 30 \Rightarrow \text{Marine-grade (e.g., 316L = 25)}$$

$$\text{PREN} \geq 40 \Rightarrow \text{High-performance (e.g., 254 SMO = 43)}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Pit Depth vs Time (Power Law)

Experimental pit-depth data, when plotted as $\log d$ vs $\log t$, give a **straight line** with slope $n$.

**Step 1 — Empirical power law assumption**

$$d = k \cdot t^{n}$$

**Step 2 — Take logarithms**

$$\log d = \log k + n \cdot \log t$$

This is the equation of a straight line, $y = c + mx$, where the slope is the **kinetic exponent $n$**.

**Step 3 — Determine k and n by regression**

Two-point determination from experimental data:

$$n = \frac{\log(d_2 / d_1)}{\log(t_2 / t_1)}$$

$$k = \frac{d_1}{t_1^{n}}$$

**Step 4 — Typical values**

For austenitic SS 304 in 3% NaCl:

$$n \approx 0.37, \quad k \approx 0.10 \, \text{mm/yr}^{0.37}$$

Therefore, the **time to perforate** a wall of thickness $T$ mm is:

$$\boxed{t_{fail} = \left( \frac{T}{k} \right)^{1/n}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Variables on Pitting

| Variable | Effect on $E_{pit}$ | Effect on PREN | Effect on Pitting Susceptibility |
|---|---|---|---|
| **↑ Cl⁻ concentration** | $E_{pit}$ ↓ (shifts negative) | No effect | **Increases sharply** |
| **↑ Temperature** | $E_{pit}$ ↓ | No direct effect | Increases above CPT |
| **↑ Cr content** | $E_{pit}$ ↑ (noble) | Increases (1×) | Decreases |
| **↑ Mo content** | $E_{pit}$ ↑↑ (very strong) | Increases (3.3×) | Decreases strongly |
| **↑ N content** | $E_{pit}$ ↑ | Increases (16×) | Decreases |
| **↑ pH (alkaline)** | $E_{pit}$ ↑ | No effect | Decreases |
| **↑ O₂ (stagnation)** | Slight ↑ | No effect | Increases inside crevice |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** Although N is a *gas* and present in small quantities, its coefficient (×16) makes it the **single most potent alloying element** against pitting in austenitic stainless steels. KTU examiners may ask: *"Why is nitrogen added to 304LN / 316LN?"* — answer: *to improve pitting resistance per unit mass.*

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Autocatalytic Pitting Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Passive Film Intact — Cr2O3, Al2O3] --> B{Cl- Adsorption at Weak Spot}
    B --> C[Local Film Breakdown]
    C --> D[Metastable Pit Nucleation]
    D --> E{Stable Propagation ?}
    E -- No --> F[Repassivation — Pit Dies]
    E -- Yes --> G[Anode inside pit — Metal Dissolution]
    G --> H[Hydrolysis: M^n+ + H2O yields MOH and H+]
    H --> I[Pit Interior pH drops to 0 to 2]
    I --> J[Cl- Migrates into Pit for Charge Neutrality]
    J --> K[Oxygen Depletion inside Pit]
    K --> L[Oxygen Reduction on Outer Surface — Cathode]
    L --> M[Autocatalytic Cell Established]
    M --> N{Pit Termination ?}
    N -- Potential below E_prot --> F
    N -- Continued growth --> O[Perforation / Catastrophic Failure]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Cyclic Polarization Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Open Circuit Potential Ecorr] --> B[Forward Scan — Anodic]
    B --> C[Passive Region — Low Current]
    C --> D{Potential reaches Epit}
    D --> E[Current Rises Sharply — Pits Initiate]
    E --> F[Reverse Scan Initiated]
    F --> G{Current Returns to Passive at Eprot}
    G --> H[Hysteresis Loop Width = Epit minus Eprot]
    H --> I{Large Loop ?}
    I -- Yes --> J[High Pitting Susceptibility]
    I -- No --> K[Resistant to Pitting]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Pitting Engineering

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[Environmental Inputs]
        EN1[Cl- Concentration]
        EN2[Temperature]
        EN3[pH]
        EN4[Oxygen Level]
    end
    subgraph METAL[Material System]
        M1[Alloy Composition]
        M2[Surface Finish]
        M3[Heat Treatment / Sensitization]
    end
    subgraph RESPONSE[Electrochemical Response]
        R1[Pitting Potential Epit]
        R2[Protection Potential Eprot]
        R3[Critical Pitting Temperature CPT]
        R4[PREN Number]
    end
    subgraph OUTCOME[Engineering Outcome]
        O1[Pit Initiation]
        O2[Autocatalytic Growth]
        O3[Service Life / Failure Time]
    end
    EN1 --> R1
    EN1 --> R2
    EN2 --> R3
    M1 --> R4
    M3 --> R1
    R1 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
    R4 --> O2
    R2 --> O3
    R3 --> O3
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define pitting corrosion. What is the role of chloride ions in initiating pitting on stainless steel?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Pitting corrosion is a localized form of corrosion that produces small cavities or pits on a metal surface while the bulk surface remains passive (1 mark).

Chloride ions (Cl⁻) are aggressive anions that adsorb onto the passive Cr₂O₃ film on stainless steel at local weak spots such as inclusions or grain boundaries (1 mark). The adsorbed Cl⁻ ions displace oxygen from the lattice, locally thinning and breaking down the passive film, exposing the bare metal which then acts as the anode of a small corrosion cell (1 mark).

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is pitting factor? How is it interpreted in corrosion engineering?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The pitting factor $P_f$ is the ratio of the deepest metal penetration to the average penetration calculated from mass loss (2 marks).

$$P_f = \frac{d_{max}}{d_{avg}}$$

A pitting factor of 1 indicates uniform corrosion, values between 1 and 5 indicate mild to moderate pitting, and values greater than 5 indicate severe pitting susceptibility requiring corrective action (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [KTU University Exam — May 2024] — 14 Marks

**(a)** Explain the mechanism of pitting corrosion with a neat labeled diagram. Discuss the role of autocatalytic action. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is pitting potential? Explain the cyclic potentiodynamic polarization technique used to determine it. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **Definition (1 mark):** Pitting corrosion is a localized attack producing cavities while the bulk surface remains passive.

2. **Stages (3 marks):**
   - *Passivity breakdown* — Cl⁻ adsorbs on passive film, ruptures it locally.
   - *Pit nucleation* — exposed metal becomes anode, surrounding passive area is cathode.
   - *Propagation* — metal dissolves, hydrolysis produces H⁺, pH drops, Cl⁻ migrates in for charge balance, oxygen depleted inside.
   - *Termination or perforation* — repassivation at $E_{prot}$ or wall perforation.

3. **Autocatalytic nature (2 marks):** The reaction products inside the pit (acidic, Cl⁻-rich, oxygen-depleted) accelerate further attack, making the process self-sustaining until either repassivation or mechanical failure occurs.

4. **Diagram (1 mark):** Cross-section showing anode, cathode, Cl⁻ migration, hydrolysis, oxygen gradient — see Section 4.1.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

1. **Definition (1 mark):** Pitting potential $E_{pit}$ is the lowest potential at which stable pits nucleate and propagate on a passive metal surface.

2. **Test procedure (3 marks):** Specimen is immersed in a chloride-containing electrolyte (e.g., 3.5% NaCl) and polarized anodically from the open-circuit potential at a fixed scan rate (e.g., 1 mV/s). The current density is monitored continuously. The potential at which the current density rises sharply (typically > 100 µA/cm²) is recorded as $E_{pit}$.

3. **Cyclic polarization and hysteresis (2 marks):** After $E_{pit}$ is reached, the scan is reversed. The potential at which the current returns to the passive value on the reverse scan is the **protection (repassivation) potential $E_{prot}$**. The hysteresis loop area indicates pitting susceptibility.

4. **Significance (1 mark):** $E_{pit}$ values are used to compare alloys, design cathodic protection systems, and ensure service potentials stay below $E_{pit}$.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — 14 Marks

**(a)** What is PREN? Calculate the PREN of AISI 316 stainless steel having 17% Cr, 2% Mo, 0.05% N, and a duplex grade having 25% Cr, 4% Mo, 0.2% N. Comment on their relative pitting resistance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the various stages of pit growth and the factors affecting pitting corrosion. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **Definition (1 mark):** PREN is the Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number, an empirical index for ranking stainless steels by pitting resistance.

$$\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \cdot \%\text{Mo} + 16 \cdot \%\text{N}$$

2. **Calculation for 316 (1 mark):**

$$\text{PREN}_{316} = 17 + 3.3 \times 2 + 16 \times 0.05 = 17 + 6.6 + 0.8 = 24.4$$

3. **Calculation for duplex (1 mark):**

$$\text{PREN}_{duplex} = 25 + 3.3 \times 4 + 16 \times 0.2 = 25 + 13.2 + 3.2 = 41.4$$

4. **Comparison (2 marks):** The duplex grade (41.4) has a PREN ~70% higher than 316 (24.4), making it significantly more resistant to pitting. It is suitable for aggressive marine and chloride environments, while 316 is suitable for mild marine service.

5. **Duplex bonus (2 marks):** The duplex PREN should include a +1 bonus for its two-phase microstructure:

$$\text{PREN}_{duplex, actual} = 41.4 + 1 = 42.4$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

1. **Stages of pit growth (3 marks):** As detailed in Section 2.1 — passivity breakdown → metastable nucleation → stable propagation → repassivation/perforation.

2. **Metallurgical factors (2 marks):**
   - Alloy composition (Cr, Mo, N raise $E_{pit}$)
   - Sensitization (Cr depletion at grain boundaries → intergranular pitting)
   - Cold work and surface roughness
   - Inclusions (MnS in SS acts as pit initiation site)

3. **Environmental factors (2 marks):**
   - Chloride concentration (lowers $E_{pit}$)
   - Temperature (increases susceptibility above CPT)
   - pH (alkaline reduces pitting)
   - Dissolved oxygen (cathodic reaction support)
   - Stagnation (poor Cl⁻ washout → pitting increases)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Pitting Questions:**
> 1. **Do NOT** describe pitting as "uniform corrosion with holes" — pitting is *localized*, not uniform. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Do NOT** forget to mention the **autocatalytic** nature. Examiners explicitly look for that word. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Do NOT** confuse $E_{pit}$ with $E_{corr}$ — $E_{corr}$ is the *open-circuit* potential, $E_{pit}$ is the *breakdown* potential. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Do NOT** write the pit-depth equation as $d = kt^{1/2}$ (parabolic) — that is the *oxidation* law. For pitting, use $d = k t^n$ with $n = 0.3$–$0.5$. (–2 marks if confused)
> 5. **Always** include a **labeled diagram** in long-answer pitting questions — it carries 1–2 marks independently of text.
> 6. For PREN numericals, **show substitutions explicitly**. Marks are split between formula statement and substitution.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Pitting = *localized* corrosion producing cavities with depth >> lateral spread.
- **Aggressive ion:** Cl⁻ is the most common pitting initiator for passive metals.
- **Geometry:** Small anode : large cathode = deadly ratio that accelerates attack.
- **Autocatalytic pit chemistry:** Acidic interior (low pH), high Cl⁻, low O₂.
- **Four stages:** Breakdown → Nucleation → Propagation → Termination/Perforation.
- **$E_{pit}$:** Potential above which pits nucleate (measured by cyclic polarization).
- **$E_{prot}$:** Repassivation potential; always $E_{prot} < E_{pit}$.
- **Hysteresis loop:** Loop width $\Delta E = E_{pit} - E_{prot}$ → wider = more susceptible.
- **CPT:** Critical Pitting Temperature from ASTM G48; alloy-specific.
- **PREN formula:** $\%\text{Cr} + 3.3\cdot\%\text{Mo} + 16\cdot\%\text{N}$ (+1 for duplex).
- **Pit depth law:** $d = k t^n$, $n = 0.3$–$0.5$ (NOT 0.5 — that is for high-T oxidation).
- **Pitting factor:** $P_f = d_{max}/d_{avg} \geq 1$; > 5 means severe pitting.
- **Prevention tactics:** Increase Cr/Mo/N, lower Cl⁻ and O₂, use cathodic protection to keep $E < E_{pit}$, avoid sensitizing heat treatments, use smoother surface finishes.
- **Diagram must include:** Anode, cathode, Cl⁻ migration arrow, hydrolysis reaction, oxygen-depleted zone.
- **Nitrogen coefficient (×16) is the highest** among alloying elements in PREN.
- **Subsurface pitting** is common in **aluminum alloys** — grow laterally, then burst.
- **KTU 2024 question types:** 3-mark definitional; 7-mark mechanism-with-diagram; 7-mark numerical (PREN, $P_f$, or $E_{pit}$ comparison).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Intergranular corrosion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT527 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Intergranular corrosion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intergranular Corrosion (IGC)** is a **localized form of electrochemical corrosion** in which the metallic **grain boundaries** of a polycrystalline metal or alloy are preferentially attacked, while the interior of the grains (grains proper) remain comparatively unattacked. The attack progresses along a narrow path parallel to the grain boundaries, often leading to complete loss of mechanical strength and fracture with little or no visible surface indication.

**KTU Syllabus Terminology Mapping:**
| Term | KTU 2024 Definition |
|---|---|
| **Grain Boundary** | The narrow interfacial region (≈ 1–5 atomic layers thick) separating two crystals of differing orientation in a polycrystalline solid. |
| **Sensitization** | The metallurgical condition in which an alloy (typically austenitic stainless steel) becomes susceptible to IGC due to chromium-carbide precipitation at grain boundaries upon exposure to **400 °C – 850 °C**. |
| **Weld Decay** | A specific form of IGC occurring in the **heat-affected zone (HAZ)** adjacent to a weld bead, where the thermal cycle has induced sensitization. |
| **Knife-Line Attack** | A narrow band of IGC occurring in a very thin region adjacent to the weld fusion line in **stabilized grades** like AISI 321 / 347. |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Termite in Wood" Analogy** 🪵
>
> Imagine a beautiful wooden table that looks perfectly solid from the outside. Internally, however, **termites have eaten away all the glue and joints** between the planks. The moment you apply even a small force, the entire table disintegrates into separate pieces — though the surface appears untouched. This is exactly what happens in **Intergranular Corrosion**. The metal **looks shiny and uncorroded** on the surface, but the grain boundaries — the "metallic glue" — have been silently dissolved, and the part can fail **catastrophically** under stress.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Critical Chromium Content** for passivation in stainless steels: $\geq \mathbf{12\ wt\%\ Cr}$
> - **Sensitization Temperature Range**: **400 °C – 850 °C** (also called the *"danger zone"* or *"TTS nose region"*)
> - **Maximum Carbon Limit** to avoid IGC (low-carbon "L" grades): $\mathbf{C} \leq \mathbf{0.03\ wt\%}$
> - **Stabilizer-to-Carbon Ratio** for stabilized grades (AISI 321, 347): $\mathbf{Ti/C \geq 5}$ or $\mathbf{Nb/C \geq 8}$
> - **Standard Reference Test**: **ASTM A262** (Practices A–F) for austenitic stainless steels

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Does IGC Happen? — The "Why" Behind the Phenomenon

**Three pre-requisites** must coexist for IGC to occur:

1. **A susceptible alloy** (e.g., austenitic SS, certain Ni-alloys, Al-alloys)
2. **Exposure to the sensitization temperature range** (400 °C – 850 °C) — for a sufficient time
3. **A corrosive electrolyte** that can attack the depleted/solute-lean zones

## 2.2 The Chromium Depletion Theory (Straw, Myer & Rhodes, 1928)

This is the **canonical mechanism** taught at KTU level.

### Step-by-Step Logic:

1. Austenitic stainless steels (e.g., AISI 304: 18 Cr / 8 Ni) at room temperature retain all their carbon **dissolved in solid solution** in the FCC austenite (γ) matrix.
2. Carbon has very low solid solubility in austenite at room temperature (≈ 0.005 wt%) but the steel may contain 0.05 – 0.08 wt% C (typical 304 grade).
3. When the steel is heated into the **400 – 850 °C** range, the excess carbon **precipitates out as chromium-rich carbides**, predominantly **M₂₃C₆** (i.e., **Cr₂₃C₆**), preferentially at **grain boundaries** (because GBs are high-energy, fast-diffusion paths).
4. The carbide **steals chromium from the surrounding matrix** within a band of width ≈ 10 – 50 nm on either side of the grain boundary.
5. The local Cr content in this band falls **below the critical 12 wt% threshold** required to maintain the passive Cr₂O₃ film.
6. In a corrosive environment, the **depleted zone acts as the anode** (active) and the **grain interior acts as the cathode** (passive) — a micro-galvanic cell forms.
7. The narrow anodic zone is preferentially dissolved → **IGC**.

## 2.3 Grain Boundary Chemistry — What's Different at a GB?

| Region | Cr Content | Electrochemical State | Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| Grain Interior | ≈ 18 wt% | Passive (Cr₂O₃) | Cathode (protected) |
| Depletion Zone (next to GB) | < 12 wt% | Active (no passive film) | **Anode (dissolves)** |
| Grain Boundary Core | Cr-rich carbide (Cr₂₃C₆) | Noble / Inert | Cathode (protected) |

## 2.4 Time–Temperature–Sensitization (TTS) Diagram

The TTS diagram (a "C-curve") is **high-yield KTU content**.

- At temperatures **below ≈ 400 °C**: Diffusion of Cr is too slow → carbides cannot nucleate/grow → **safe**.
- At temperatures **above ≈ 850 °C**: All carbides **re-dissolve** into solid solution → no depletion → **safe**.
- In the intermediate range, precipitation is **kinetically favored** → **sensitization**.
- The "nose" of the C-curve lies near **≈ 650 – 700 °C**, where sensitization can occur in only a few **minutes**.

## 2.5 Weld Decay vs. Knife-Line Attack — A Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Weld Decay** is observed in **non-stabilized grades** (304, 316) in the HAZ ~ 1–3 grain diameters away from the fusion line. The peak temperature experienced there is ≈ 600 – 700 °C — squarely in the sensitization range.
>
> **Knife-Line Attack** is observed in **stabilized grades** (321 / 347 with Ti or Nb) in a **very narrow band** exactly along the fusion line. Here, the weld-pool peak temperature (> 1200 °C) re-dissolves the stabilizing Ti/Nb carbides, leaving free carbon that, upon subsequent re-heating, forms Cr₂₃C₆.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Relation | Physical Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\boxed{C_{Cr,local} = C_{Cr,bulk} - \Delta C_{Cr}}$ | Local Cr depletion below bulk value | wt % |
| 2 | $\boxed{C_{Cr,local} < 12\ \text{wt\%}}$ | Critical condition for sensitization | wt % |
| 3 | $\boxed{C \leq 0.03\ \text{wt\%}}$ | Maximum carbon for "L-grade" (e.g., 304L, 316L) | wt % |
| 4 | $\boxed{\dfrac{Ti}{C} \geq 5\ \text{or}\ \dfrac{Nb}{C} \geq 8}$ | Stabilizer ratio for AISI 321 / 347 | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\boxed{D_{Cr} = D_0\, e^{-Q/RT}}$ | Cr diffusion in austenite (Fick's 1st law basis) | m² / s |
| 6 | $\boxed{\Delta x \approx \sqrt{D_{Cr} \cdot t}}$ | Width of Cr-depleted zone | m |
| 7 | $\boxed{\text{Pitting Resistance Equivalent} = \%Cr + 3.3\%Mo + 16\%N}$ | PRE number (context: pitting, not IGC) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\boxed{E^\circ = E^\circ_{cathode} - E^\circ_{anode}}$ | EMF driving micro-galvanic cell | Volts |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Nuclear power plant** primary piping (304 SS) — IGC must be prevented.
- **Petrochemical cracker tubes** — furnace tubes operate exactly in the sensitization range.
- **Heat exchangers, reactor vessels, welded pipelines** — failure analysis uses ASTM A262 to detect IGC susceptibility.
- **Aerospace** — turbine blades (Ni-superalloys) can suffer IGC from carbide films.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Width of the Chromium-Depleted Zone

We model the diffusion of chromium **away from the grain boundary** toward the growing Cr₂₃C₆ precipitate using the one-dimensional Fick's second law in a semi-infinite medium.

**Step 1** — Governing PDE (Fick's 2nd Law):

$$
\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D_{Cr}\,\frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}
$$

**Step 2** — Boundary & Initial Conditions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C(x, 0) &= C_0 \quad \text{(uniform bulk Cr content)} \\
C(0, t) &= C_{eq} \quad \text{(interface maintained at carbide equilibrium)} \\
C(\infty, t) &= C_0 \quad \text{(far field undisturbed)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3** — Standard Solution (complementary error function):

$$
C(x, t) = C_0 - (C_0 - C_{eq})\,\operatorname{erfc}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{D_{Cr}\,t}}\right)
$$

**Step 4** — Define the **depletion-zone width** $\Delta x$ as the distance at which Cr content recovers to within **1 % of the bulk value**, i.e., $\operatorname{erfc}(\xi) = 0.01$ giving $\xi \approx 1.82$.

$$
\boxed{\Delta x \approx 1.82 \cdot 2\sqrt{D_{Cr}\,t} = 3.64\,\sqrt{D_{Cr}\,t}}
$$

**Step 5** — Numerical estimation for AISI 304 at **650 °C**:
- $D_0 \approx 0.08\ \text{cm}^2/\text{s}$, $Q \approx 286\ \text{kJ/mol}$, $T = 923\ \text{K}$

$$
D_{Cr} = 0.08 \times \exp\!\left(-\frac{286\,000}{8.314 \times 923}\right) \approx 1.1 \times 10^{-17}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}
$$

For $t = 1\ \text{hr} = 3600\ \text{s}$:

$$
\Delta x \approx 3.64 \times \sqrt{1.1 \times 10^{-17} \times 3600} \approx 2.3 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m} = 230\ \text{nm}
$$

This **230 nm-wide zone** is a realistic estimate of the sensitized band — consistent with experimental TEM measurements.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Critical Cooling Rate to Avoid Sensitization

For a continuous-cooling process (e.g., post-weld cooling), the steel must traverse the **400 – 850 °C window faster than the critical time** $t_c$ at the nose of the TTS curve.

**Step 1** — Time spent in the danger zone during linear cooling:

$$
t_{dwell} = \frac{\Delta T}{|dT/dt|} = \frac{850 - 400}{R_c}
$$

where $R_c$ is the cooling rate (°C/s).

**Step 2** — Condition to **avoid sensitization**:

$$
\boxed{t_{dwell} < t_{c} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_c > \frac{\Delta T}{t_c} = \frac{450}{t_c}}
$$

**Step 3** — Practical numbers (AISI 304, $t_c \approx 5\ \text{min} = 300\ \text{s}$ at 675 °C):

$$
R_c > \frac{450}{300} = 1.5\ ^\circ\text{C}/\text{s}
$$

So the weld HAZ must **cool at > 1.5 °C/s** to avoid sensitization. This is why **low heat-input welding** is recommended.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: EMF Driving Force for the Micro-Galvanic Cell

The anodic (depleted) zone and cathodic (grain interior) zone behave as a short-circuited galvanic cell.

**Step 1** — Standard electrode potentials (approximate, in acid chloride media):
- Passive zone (Cr ≈ 18%): $E_{cathode} \approx +0.20\ \text{V (SHE)}$
- Active depleted zone (Cr < 12%): $E_{anode} \approx -0.30\ \text{V (SHE)}$

**Step 2** — Cell EMF:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{cell} &= E_{cathode} - E_{anode} \\
&= (+0.20) - (-0.30) \\
&= +0.50\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{cell} = +0.50\ \text{V}}
$$

**Step 3** — Gibbs free energy of the corrosion reaction:

$$
\Delta G = -nFE_{cell} = -2 \times 96\,485 \times 0.50 = -96.5\ \text{kJ/mol}
$$

A large **negative $\Delta G$** confirms the reaction is **highly spontaneous** — explaining the aggressive, often rapid, propagation of IGC once initiated.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Conditions & Outcomes

| Condition | Cr in GB-Zone | Susceptible to IGC? | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Solution-annealed (1050 °C, water-quenched) | ≈ 18 wt% (uniform) | **No** | All carbides dissolved; rapid quench prevents re-precipitation |
| Sensitized (650 °C, 1 h, air-cooled) | < 12 wt% | **Yes** | Cr-depletion zone formed |
| Stabilized (321 with Ti/C > 5) | ≈ 18 wt% | **No** | Ti "ties up" C as TiC before Cr can form Cr₂₃C₆ |
| Low-carbon (304L, C ≤ 0.03%) | ≈ 18 wt% | **No** | Insufficient C to form continuous carbide network |
| Knife-line (321 re-heated 600 °C) | < 12 wt% | **Yes (narrow band)** | TiC redissolved at weld peak, Cr₂₃C₆ forms on re-heat |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mechanism Flowchart — How Intergranular Corrosion Develops

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Austenitic SS at Room Temperature] --> B[Carbon fully dissolved in FCC gamma matrix]
    B --> C[Step 2: Heating into 400 to 850 C danger zone]
    C --> D[Step 3: Carbon diffuses to grain boundary]
    D --> E[Step 4: Cr23C6 carbide precipitates at GB]
    E --> F[Step 5: Local Cr content falls below 12 wt percent]
    F --> G[Step 6: Depletion zone becomes active anode]
    G --> H[Step 7: Grain interior remains passive cathode]
    H --> I[Step 8: Micro galvanic cell established]
    I --> J[Step 9: Selective dissolution along GB]
    J --> K[Step 10: Intergranular corrosion failure]
```

## 4.2 TTS (Time–Temperature–Sensitization) Diagram — Block Representation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SAFE1[SAFE ZONE LOW TEMP]
        A1[Below 400 C: Cr diffusion too slow]
    end
    subgraph DANGER[DANGER ZONE 400 to 850 C]
        B1[Nose at 650 to 700 C: sensitization in minutes]
        B2[C-curve shortest time approx 5 min]
        B3[Maximum sensitization near 675 C]
    end
    subgraph SAFE2[SAFE ZONE HIGH TEMP]
        C1[Above 850 C: carbides redissolve]
    end
    SAFE1 --> DANGER
    DANGER --> SAFE2
```

## 4.3 Architecture of Prevention Strategies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[IGC Prevention Strategies] --> P1[Method 1: Low Carbon L Grades]
    P --> P2[Method 2: Stabilized Grades]
    P --> P3[Method 3: Solution Annealing]
    P --> P4[Method 4: Process Control]
    
    P1 --> P1a[304L 316L: Carbon less than 0.03 wt percent]
    P1 --> P1b[Eliminates carbide network formation]
    
    P2 --> P2a[AISI 321 with Titanium: TiC formation]
    P2 --> P2b[AISI 347 with Niobium: NbC formation]
    P2 --> P2c[Stabilizer ties up carbon before Cr carbide forms]
    
    P3 --> P3a[Heat to 1050 C: dissolve all carbides]
    P3 --> P3b[Water quench: prevent re-precipitation]
    
    P4 --> P4a[Low heat input welding]
    P4 --> P4b[Control interpass temperature]
    P4 --> P4c[Post weld solution anneal if feasible]
```

## 4.4 Weld Decay vs Knife-Line Attack — Comparative Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W[Welding Operation] --> W1[Non stabilized 304 316]
    W --> W2[Stabilized 321 347]
    
    W1 --> WD[Weld Decay in HAZ]
    WD --> WD1[Band 1 to 3 grains away from fusion line]
    WD --> WD2[Temperature approx 600 to 700 C]
    WD --> WD3[Cr23C6 forms in depletion zone]
    
    W2 --> KL[Knife Line Attack]
    KL --> KL1[Narrow band along fusion line]
    KL --> KL2[Temperature over 1200 C dissolves TiC]
    KL --> KL3[Re heating at 500 to 700 C forms Cr23C6]
```

## 4.5 ASTM A262 Test Selection Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T[ASTM A262 Test Selection] --> T1[Practice A: Oxalic Acid Etch Screening]
    T --> T2[Practice B: Streicher Ferric Sulfate Sulfuric Acid]
    T --> T3[Practice C: Huey Nitric Acid Boiling]
    T --> T4[Practice D: Mixed Acid Nitric Hydrofluoric]
    T --> T5[Practice E: Strauss Copper Copper Sulfate]
    T --> T6[Practice F: Warren 65 percent Nitric Acid]
    
    T1 --> T1a[Quick classification: ditch vs dual vs step vs random]
    T5 --> T5a[Most severe test: definitive IGC detection]
    T3 --> T3a[Detects intergranular attack in stabilized grades]
```
