# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

### Q1. Define intergranular corrosion. Why is austenitic stainless steel (AISI 304) particularly susceptible to it? [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

> **Intergranular corrosion (IGC)** is a localized form of corrosion in which the **grain boundaries** of a metal/alloy are preferentially attacked, while the grain interiors remain essentially unattacked. The attack propagates along a thin path parallel to the grain boundaries, leading to loss of mechanical cohesion with little visible surface damage.
>
> AISI 304 (18 Cr / 8 Ni / ≈ 0.06 C) is particularly susceptible because:
> 1. It contains **0.05 – 0.08 wt% carbon** — far above the room-temperature solubility in austenite (≈ 0.005 wt%).
> 2. On heating into the **400 – 850 °C** range (e.g., during welding or service exposure), the excess carbon precipitates as **Cr₂₃C₆ at the grain boundaries**.
> 3. This produces a **Cr-depleted zone** (< 12 wt% Cr) adjacent to each grain boundary, which cannot maintain the passive film and is therefore selectively attacked.

---

### Q2. What is "sensitization"? State the temperature range over which it occurs. [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

> **Sensitization** is the metallurgical condition in which an alloy becomes susceptible to intergranular corrosion due to the precipitation of chromium-rich carbides (Cr₂₃C₆) at the grain boundaries, creating Cr-depleted zones.
>
> The sensitization temperature range is **400 °C – 850 °C**. The "nose" of the TTS (Time–Temperature–Sensitization) curve — the temperature at which sensitization occurs most rapidly (within minutes) — is at approximately **650 – 700 °C**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice: 1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

---

### ❓ QUESTION A — Intergranular Corrosion Mechanism & Weld Decay [14 Marks]

**Q.A (a)** With neat sketches, explain the mechanism of intergranular corrosion in austenitic stainless steel using the **chromium depletion theory**. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

The **chromium depletion theory** (Straw, Myer & Rhodes, 1928) is the accepted mechanism of IGC in austenitic stainless steels.

**Mechanism Steps:**

1. **Initial state**: AISI 304 (18 Cr / 8 Ni) in the solution-annealed condition has chromium uniformly distributed at ≈ 18 wt% throughout the austenite matrix. Carbon is in supersaturated solid solution.

2. **Heating to 400 – 850 °C**: Carbon, having very low solubility in austenite at low temperatures, diffuses to the high-energy **grain boundaries** and precipitates as **chromium-rich carbide M₂₃C₆ (Cr₂₃C₆)**.

3. **Cr depletion**: Each mole of Cr₂₃C₆ consumes 23 Cr atoms from the matrix. The Cr is "stolen" from a zone of width ≈ 10 – 50 nm on either side of the grain boundary, dropping the local Cr concentration **below the 12 wt% passivation threshold**.

4. **Loss of passivity**: The Cr-depleted zone can no longer sustain a continuous Cr₂O₃ passive film. In a corrosive electrolyte (e.g., Cl⁻-containing water), this zone becomes the **active anode**.

5. **Micro-galvanic cell**: The grain interior (still 18% Cr, passive) acts as the **cathode**; the depleted zone (active) is the **anode**. A localized micro-cell forms, with EMF ≈ 0.5 V.

6. **Selective dissolution**: The narrow anodic zone dissolves preferentially, propagating along the grain boundary. The grains themselves fall apart with little metal loss — the **classical "sugary" fracture surface** of IGC.

**Sketch (text-based):**

```
      Grain A              Grain B
    (18% Cr)              (18% Cr)
    ┌──────────────┬──────────────┐
    │  Passive     │ GB Core      │  Passive
    │  (cathode)   │ Cr₂₃C₆       │  (cathode)
    │              │   carbide    │              │
    │              │     │        │              │
    │   ▒▒▒▒▒▒     │     │        │   ▒▒▒▒▒▒     │ ← Cr < 12%
    │   Depleted   │     │        │   Depleted   │   (anode
    │   zone       │     │        │   zone       │    dissolves)
    │   < 12% Cr   │     │        │   < 12% Cr   │
    └──────────────┴──────────────┘
```

---

**Q.A (b)** What is **weld decay**? Explain with reference to the heat-affected zone (HAZ) of an AISI 304 weldment. How is it prevented? [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Weld Decay** is a form of intergranular corrosion occurring in the **heat-affected zone (HAZ)** of a welded austenitic stainless steel, at a distance of approximately **1 – 3 grain diameters** away from the fusion line.

**Explanation:**

During arc welding, a temperature gradient is established in the base metal:
- The **fusion zone** melts (> 1400 °C).
- The **HAZ** at 1–3 grain diameters away experiences peak temperatures in the range **600 – 700 °C** — the very center of the sensitization range.
- The base metal beyond the HAZ stays below ≈ 400 °C and is unaffected.

The HAZ is therefore "thermally cycled" through the sensitization nose, producing a continuous network of Cr₂₃C₆ at the grain boundaries. This results in a **band of IGC susceptibility** running parallel to the weld, typically visible only after a chemical etch (oxalic acid test, ASTM A262 Practice A) as a "ditch" structure.

**Prevention Methods:**

1. **Use low-carbon "L-grades"** (AISI 304L / 316L with C ≤ 0.03 wt%) — insufficient C to form a continuous carbide network.
2. **Use stabilized grades** (AISI 321 with Ti, AISI 347 with Nb) — the stabilizer "ties up" the carbon as TiC or NbC, preventing Cr₂₃C₆ precipitation.
3. **Post-weld solution annealing** (1050 °C / water quench) — dissolves all carbides and prevents re-precipitation.
4. **Low heat-input welding** — minimizes dwell time in the 400 – 850 °C range.

---

### ❓ QUESTION B — Stabilization, Knife-Line Attack & Testing [14 Marks]

**Q.B (a)** Explain the **stabilization** method of preventing IGC. Discuss the role of Ti and Nb with reference to AISI 321 and AISI 347 grades. Why is the "stabilizer-to-carbon ratio" important? [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Stabilization** is a metallurgical strategy in which a strong carbide-former (Ti or Nb) is added to the steel in sufficient quantity to preferentially combine with carbon, leaving the chromium in solid solution and therefore unavailable to form Cr-depleted zones.

**Mechanism:**

The standard free energy of formation of TiC and NbC is **more negative** than that of Cr₂₃C₆. Therefore, at elevated temperatures:

$$
Ti + C \rightarrow TiC \quad (\text{forms first, before Cr}_{23}\text{C}_{6})
$$

$$
Nb + C \rightarrow NbC \quad (\text{forms first, before Cr}_{23}\text{C}_{6})
$$

Since the carbon is "locked up" as TiC or NbC, there is no excess carbon available to combine with chromium, and the matrix retains its full ≈ 18% Cr content in solution.

**Grade Specifications:**

| Grade | Stabilizer | Minimum Ratio |
|---|---|---|
| AISI 321 | Titanium (Ti) | Ti / C ≥ 5 |
| AISI 347 | Niobium (Nb) | Nb / C ≥ 8 |

**Why the ratio matters:**
- If the ratio is too low, **not all carbon is stabilized**; the remaining free carbon will still form Cr₂₃C₆ on slow cooling or re-heating.
- Typical practice specifies **Ti = 5 × C** or **Nb = 8 × C** (the Nb factor is higher because Nb is heavier per atom than Ti).

**Practical limitation:** During very high weld-peak temperatures (> 1200 °C), TiC/NbC themselves can **partially re-dissolve**, releasing carbon. On subsequent re-heating to 500 – 700 °C (e.g., a second weld pass or stress-relief), Cr₂₃C₆ can form — this is the origin of **knife-line attack** in stabilized grades.

---

**Q.B (b)** What is **knife-line attack**? In which grades is it observed and how can it be avoided? Briefly describe the **ASTM A262** standard test practices used to detect IGC. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Knife-Line Attack** is a narrow band of intergranular corrosion occurring **immediately adjacent to the weld fusion line** in **stabilized austenitic stainless steels** (AISI 321 and 347).

**Mechanism:**

1. The weld pool reaches > 1200 °C, which **dissolves the TiC / NbC** stabilizers.
2. Free carbon is released into the matrix.
3. If a subsequent weld pass or stress-relief operation re-heats the band to **500 – 700 °C**, the freed carbon now forms **Cr₂₃C₆** in a very thin (knife-edge) zone right at the fusion line.
4. On exposure to a corrosive medium, this narrow band dissolves, creating a "knife-cut" appearance along the weld.

**Avoidance:**
- Avoid **multi-pass welding** without intermediate solution annealing.
- Use a **stabilized-over-stabilized** approach: e.g., AISI 347 with extra Nb (Nb / C ≥ 12).
- A final **stabilizing anneal** at 900 °C re-precipitates TiC/NbC before any Cr carbide can form.
- Use **low-carbon L-grades** instead of stabilized grades where possible.

**ASTM A262 — Standard Test Practices for Detecting Susceptibility to IGC in Austenitic SS:**

| Practice | Test Name | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **A** | Oxalic Acid Etch (Screening) | Quick metallographic classification (ditch / dual / step / random structures). |
| **B** | Ferric Sulfate – Sulfuric Acid (Streicher) | Quantitative weight-loss measurement; detects IGC in non-stabilized grades. |
| **C** | Nitric Acid – Boiling (Huey) | Detects IGC due to carbide precipitation; 5 × 48-hour cycles. |
| **D** | Nitric – Hydrofluoric Acid | Used for extra-low-C grades; sensitive to Cr-depletion at GBs. |
| **E** | Cu – CuSO₄ – 16% H₂SO₄ (Strauss) | Most severe test; definitive detection of IGC susceptibility. |
| **F** | 65% HNO₃ (Warren) | Detects IGC in **stabilized** grades (Ti / Nb). |

The **Strauss test (Practice E)** is the most widely accepted "go / no-go" test for IGC in austenitic SS.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "weld decay" with "knife-line attack"** — they occur in *different grades* and at *different locations*. Weld decay = non-stabilized, HAZ 1–3 grains away. Knife-line = stabilized, at the fusion line.
> 2. **Forgetting the 12 wt% Cr threshold** — this is the *most* frequently asked numerical value.
> 3. **Writing "stabilization" without specifying the ratio** (Ti/C ≥ 5 or Nb/C ≥ 8) — examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 4. **Mixing up ASTM A262 Practices** — Strauss = Practice E, Huey = Practice C, Oxalic etch = Practice A. Mnemonic: **"O–S–H–N–S–W"** (Oxalic, Streicher, Huey, Nitric-HF, Strauss, Warren).
> 5. **Not labeling the "danger zone" temperature range (400 – 850 °C)** — this is a 1-mark KTU favorite.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ⚡

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2 / IGC**

- ✅ IGC = corrosion **along grain boundaries**; grains themselves remain intact.
- ✅ Caused by **Cr-depleted zones** (< 12 wt% Cr) adjacent to grain boundaries.
- ✅ Driver: precipitation of **Cr₂₃C₆** carbide in 400 – 850 °C.
- ✅ "**Sensitization**" = metallurgical term for the IGC-susceptible condition.
- ✅ TTS nose: **≈ 650 – 700 °C** — sensitization in minutes.
- ✅ **Critical Cr content for passivity** = **12 wt%**.
- ✅ **Weld decay**: in non-stabilized 304/316, in HAZ 1–3 grains from fusion line.
- ✅ **Knife-line attack**: in stabilized 321/347, narrow band at fusion line.
- ✅ Prevention #1: **L-grades** (C ≤ 0.03 wt%) — 304L, 316L.
- ✅ Prevention #2: **Stabilized grades** — 321 (Ti/C ≥ 5), 347 (Nb/C ≥ 8).
- ✅ Prevention #3: **Solution anneal** at 1050 °C + water quench.
- ✅ Prevention #4: **Low heat-input welding** to minimize dwell in danger zone.
- ✅ Test: **ASTM A262**; most severe = **Practice E (Strauss)**; screen = **Practice A (Oxalic)**.
- ✅ Depletion-zone width: $\Delta x \approx 3.64\sqrt{D_{Cr}\,t}$.
- ✅ Critical cooling rate to avoid sensitization: $R_c > 450/t_c$ °C/s.
- ✅ EMF of micro-galvanic cell ≈ **+0.50 V** (highly spontaneous).
- ✅ Real-world impact: nuclear piping, petrochemical furnaces, heat exchangers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selective leaching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT527 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Selective leaching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Selective Leaching** is a specialized form of *wet corrosion* (electrochemical corrosion) in which **one specific constituent element of a solid alloy is preferentially dissolved into the corrosive electrolyte**, while the other element(s) of the alloy remain largely unattacked. The result is a porous, mechanically weak residual skeletal structure composed of the more noble (cathodic) component of the original alloy.

In the NACE/ASM standardized nomenclature, this is formally categorized as **Type 2 — Parting / Dealloying** in the Fontana-Greene classification of eight forms of corrosion. When a specific element is named (e.g., Zn from brass, Fe from cast iron, Al from Al-bronze, Ni from Cu-Ni, Sn from bronze), a prefix is added: **dezincification, graphitic corrosion, dealuminification, denickelification, detinning**, etc.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Selective leaching is grouped under "Forms of Corrosion — Subsurface / Microstructural" attacks. The exam commonly tests (a) the mechanism, (b) identification of the alloy–environment combination, and (c) remedial/preventive methods.

The governing thermodynamic requirement is that the **standard electrode potential** of the leached (anodic) element must be significantly more negative (more active) than that of the noble element it leaves behind.

> [!NOTE]
> **Essential Pre-Conditions for Selective Leaching:**
> 1. The alloy must contain at least two components with a sizeable difference in nobility (≥ 200 mV typically).
> 2. The environment must be capable of supporting sustained electrochemical attack (usually aqueous, mildly acidic, or saline).
> 3. The noble element must have high hydrogen overpotential and low solubility in the electrolyte.
> 4. The alloy microstructure must allow percolation paths for the active element.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **brick wall made of two types of bricks — soft yellow bricks (representing zinc) and hard red bricks (representing copper) — held together by weak mortar**. Now imagine a slow, persistent acid rain (the electrolyte) that *only dissolves* the soft yellow bricks, one by one. The red bricks remain intact, but the wall is now full of empty holes — a fragile, porous, ghost-like skeleton that can no longer support load.

This is exactly what happens in **dezincification of brass** (a Cu–Zn alloy). Zinc atoms (the active element) dissolve into water, leaving behind a porous, mechanically useless mass of copper sponge. The metal is *still there*, but it has lost its strength, color, and utility. The pipe may look like copper on the outside, but tap a screwdriver on it, and it crumbles.

## 1.3 Key Quantitative Metrics (Bolded Constants)

- **Standard Potential difference (ΔE°)** for selective attack: typically **≥ 200 mV** (0.20 V) between alloy components.
- **Noble / Cathodic element in brass dezincification:** Copper (E° = +0.34 V vs SHE)
- **Active / Anodic element:** Zinc (E° = –0.76 V vs SHE)
- **ΔE° for Cu–Zn couple:** approximately **1.10 V**
- **Critical Zn content** for dezincification susceptibility: > **15 wt %** Zn
- **Plug-type vs Layer-type dezincification rates:** penetration up to **1–10 mm/year** in stagnant saline water.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operative Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic

Selective leaching proceeds via a coupled electrochemical process. Although only one element dissolves, two simultaneous partial reactions must occur. For the model case of **dezincification of α-brass (Cu–Zn)** in slightly acidic, oxygenated, chloride-containing water:

### Step 1 — Adsorption & Electrolyte Penetration
Water molecules, dissolved O₂, and Cl⁻ ions adsorb onto the brass surface. The electrolyte penetrates the alloy preferentially along grain boundaries and through the Zn-rich β' phase (if Zn > 35 wt %).

### Step 2 — Anodic Dissolution of the Active Metal
Zinc atoms at the surface ionize and pass into solution. The corresponding half-cell reaction is:

$$
\text{Zn (in alloy)} \longrightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}_{(\text{aq})} + 2e^{-}
$$

The liberated **electrons must be consumed** by a simultaneous cathodic reaction; otherwise, the surface would charge up and the reaction would stop.

### Step 3 — Cathodic Reduction of Dissolved Oxygen
Oxygen from the dissolved air is reduced at adjacent cathodic sites (often the copper-rich regions):

$$
\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^{-} \longrightarrow 4\text{OH}^{-}
$$

This consumes the electrons released by Zn dissolution and maintains charge neutrality.

### Step 4 — Re-plating / Re-deposition of the Noble Element
In the classical "**double-path mechanism**" proposed by **Pickering & Byrne**, Cu²⁺ ions are first generated in tiny amounts, then **re-plated** onto the alloy surface as a porous sponge of pure copper. This spongy Cu layer is then the *new* cathodic surface that drives *further* Zn dissolution — a self-accelerating cycle.

$$
\text{Cu (in alloy)} \longrightarrow \text{Cu}^{2+}_{(\text{aq})} + 2e^{-}
$$

$$
\text{Cu}^{2+}_{(\text{aq})} + 2e^{-} \longrightarrow \text{Cu (sponge deposit)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **net reaction** appears as "Zn leaves, Cu stays," but mechanistically, both elements are momentarily ionized; the noble one simply re-precipitates.

### Step 5 — Formation of Porous Residual Skeleton
After extended exposure, the leached zone consists of porous, mechanically weak noble metal. The metal has lost (a) tensile strength, (b) ductility, (c) thermal conductivity, (d) original color (red in the case of dezincified brass), and (e) impact resistance.

## 2.2 Two Recognized Morphological Forms

| Form | Description | Typical Location |
|------|-------------|------------------|
| **Layer-type (uniform) dezincification** | Uniform attack over a wide surface; slow penetration | Cold water systems, low temperature |
| **Plug-type (localized) dezincification** | Localized, deep, plug-shaped penetration | Stagnant warm water, heat-affected zones |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plug-type is far more dangerous** because it perforates pipe walls rapidly without externally visible warning, causing sudden leaks in service.

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Symbol Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|-------------------|----------------|-------------|
| 1 | $E°_{\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}} - E°_{\text{Zn}^{2+}/\text{Zn}}$ | $\approx 1.10$ V | Driving force for dezincification |
| 2 | $i_{\text{corr}} = i_a = i_c$ | Anodic = Cathodic current density | Charge balance in selective attack |
| 3 | $\Delta m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Faraday's law of electrolysis | Mass of metal lost (g); $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol |
| 4 | $v = \dfrac{\Delta m}{\rho \cdot A \cdot t}$ | Corrosion rate in depth/time | Penetration rate of leaching |
| 5 | $K = \dfrac{0.129 \cdot \text{mpy}}{d \cdot A}$ | Corrosion rate conversion | mpy = mils per year; $d$ = density |
| 6 | Pourbaix domain: $E$–pH diagram | Stability region of alloy vs. ions | Predicts when selective leaching is thermodynamically possible |
| 7 | $\Delta G° = -nFE°_{\text{cell}}$ | Gibbs free energy | Spontaneity check |
| 8 | $\text{MMPY} = \dfrac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$ | Standard mpy formula (Fontana) | Empirical corrosion rate (in $\text{mg/dm}^2/\text{day}$ equivalents) |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In handwritten exam scripts, vertical bar notation $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value should be written as $\text{mod}(x)$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Plumbing & Heat Exchangers:** Dezincification of brass condenser tubes in ships, power plants, and desalination units.
- **Cast Iron Water Mains:** "Graphitization" of underground cast iron pipes, leading to catastrophic failures.
- **Aluminum Bronzes in Marine Service:** Dealuminification of pump impellers and ship propellers.
- **Nuclear Reactors:** Stress-corrosion-assisted leaching of Inconel/Incoloy steam generator tubes (denickelification).
- **Oil & Gas Industry:** Selective leaching of Ni from Monel alloys in HF acid service.

> [!NOTE]
> In all of these cases, the **post-failure appearance** is the same: a dull, porous, mechanically fragile residue. A simple **bend test or screwdriver scratch** is enough for an experienced engineer to identify selective leaching in the field.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Corrosion Penetration Rate from Faraday's Law

We will rigorously derive the depth-based corrosion rate that an examiner may ask as a numerical. The result links Faraday's law of electrolysis to a **linear penetration rate (mils per year, mpy)**.

### Given:
- Atomic mass of zinc leached from brass: $M_{\text{Zn}} = 65.38$ g/mol
- Valency: $n = 2$
- Faradays constant: $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol
- Corrosion current: $I = 5.0 \times 10^{-3}$ A (5 mA, from a hypothetical polarization test)
- Exposed area: $A = 5.0$ cm²
- Density of brass (≈ 70% Cu, 30% Zn): $\rho = 8.5$ g/cm³
- Time of exposure: $t = 1$ year $= 365 \times 24 \times 3600$ s

### Step 1 — Mass of zinc dissolved per year

Apply Faraday's law:

$$
\Delta m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}
$$

Substitute values:

$$
\Delta m = \frac{(5.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{A}) \times (3.1536 \times 10^{7}\ \text{s}) \times (65.38\ \text{g/mol})}{(2) \times (96{,}485\ \text{C/mol})}
$$

Numerator expansion:

$$
(5.0 \times 10^{-3}) \times (3.1536 \times 10^{7}) = 1.5768 \times 10^{5}
$$

$$
1.5768 \times 10^{5} \times 65.38 = 1.03099 \times 10^{7}
$$

Denominator:

$$
2 \times 96{,}485 = 1.9297 \times 10^{5}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\Delta m = \frac{1.03099 \times 10^{7}}{1.9297 \times 10^{5}} = 53.43\ \text{g of Zn per year}
$$

### Step 2 — Volume of metal removed

$$
V = \frac{\Delta m}{\rho} = \frac{53.43\ \text{g}}{8.5\ \text{g/cm}^3} = 6.286\ \text{cm}^3/\text{year}
$$

### Step 3 — Linear penetration depth

$$
\text{Depth} = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{6.286\ \text{cm}^3}{5.0\ \text{cm}^2} = 1.257\ \text{cm/year} \approx 495\ \text{mpy}
$$

This high rate is consistent with **severe dezincification** observed in industrial failures (typical field rates: 5–200 mpy in service, with stagnant conditions pushing it to 500+ mpy).

> [!NOTE]
> **Comment on the rate:** An mpy > 50 is "high," > 200 is "severe," and > 500 is "catastrophic / near-instant perforation."

## 3.2 Derivation of the Cell EMF Driving Selective Leaching

For a binary alloy AB where A is the noble (cathodic) and B is the active (anodic) element, the galvanic cell driving selective leaching is:

$$
E°_{\text{cell}} = E°_{\text{cathode}} - E°_{\text{anode}}
$$

For Cu–Zn (brass in aerated neutral water):

$$
E°_{\text{cell}} = E°_{\text{O}_2/\text{OH}^{-}} - E°_{\text{Zn}^{2+}/\text{Zn}}
$$

Cathode (oxygen reduction in neutral water, pH 7, $p_{\text{O}_2} = 0.21$ atm):

$$
E°_{\text{O}_2/\text{OH}^{-}} = +0.40\ \text{V (SHE)}
$$

Anode (zinc oxidation):

$$
E°_{\text{Zn}^{2+}/\text{Zn}} = -0.76\ \text{V (SHE)}
$$

Therefore:

$$
E°_{\text{cell}} = +0.40 - (-0.76) = +1.16\ \text{V}
$$

A positive value of this magnitude confirms the **strong thermodynamic driving force** for selective leaching of zinc from brass.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Common Selective Leaching Failures

| Alloy | Active Element Leached | Environment | Noble Residue | Visual Cue | Field ID Test |
|-------|------------------------|-------------|----------------|------------|----------------|
| α-Brass (Cu-Zn, <35% Zn) | Zn | Stagnant saline/CHPS water | Cu sponge | Reddish, porous | Bend / scratch test |
| β-Brass (Cu-Zn, 35–45% Zn) | Zn | Hot acidic water | Cu sponge | Plug-shaped voids | Pressure test |
| Gray Cast Iron | Fe | Soil, mine water | Graphite + Fe₃C | Black, soft, no rust | Knife scratch — graphite smears |
| Aluminum Bronze (Cu-Al) | Al | HF, hot acidic water | Cu-rich | Yellow, brittle | Microhardness drop |
| Cu-Ni alloys | Ni | Hot ammoniacal solutions | Cu sponge | Reddish | EDX analysis |
| Tin Bronze (Cu-Sn) | Sn | Hot NaOH or hot brine | Cu sponge | Reddish | SEM-EDX |
| Monel (Cu-Ni) | Ni | HF, hot acids | Cu sponge | Red/pink | Microstructure |
| Gold-Cu alloys | Cu | Salt water, perspiration | Au sponge | Yellow dull | Microstructure |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Mechanism of Selective Leaching (Dezincification)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Brass Surface Cu-Zn Alloy"] --> nodeB["Electrolyte Adsorbs O2 H2O Cl-"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Anodic Zn Dissolves to Zn2+ in solution"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["Cathodic O2 Reduced to OH- on Cu sites"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["Cu Atoms Momentarily Oxidised Cu2+"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Cu2+ Replates as Porous Cu Sponge"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Cu Sponge Becomes New Cathodic Surface"]
    nodeG --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeH["Porous Cu Skeleton Remains"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Mechanical Strength Loss Color Change Permeation of Fluid"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Final Failure Leakage or Fracture"]
```

    node1 --> node3["Plug Type Localised"]

    node2 --> node2b["Common in cold low chloride water"]
    node3 --> node3a["Rapid deep penetration"]
    node3 --> node3b["Common in stagnant warm brackish water"]
    node3 --> node3c["Catastrophic pipe failure"]
```

    nodeT --> nodeT1["Use inhibited brasses Admiralty Arsenical Brass"]

```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Pickering-Byrne Double-Path Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeP["Alloy Surface Zn Active"] --> nodeQ["Path 1 Zn dissolves to Zn2+ plus 2e"]
    nodeP --> nodeR["Path 2 Cu dissolves to Cu2+ plus 2e"]
    nodeR --> nodeS["Cu2+ migrates to cathodic site"]
    nodeS --> nodeT["Cu2+ re plates as Cu sponge using electrons from Zn dissolution"]
    nodeT --> nodeU["Cu sponge acts as efficient cathode"]
    nodeU --> nodeQ
    nodeQ --> nodeV["Net effect Zn leaves, porous Cu remains"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**  
> **Define selective leaching. Give two industrial examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Selective leaching is a form of corrosion in which one element of an alloy is preferentially removed by electrochemical attack, leaving a porous residue of the more noble element. It is also called **parting** or **dealloying**.

**Two industrial examples:**

1. **Dezincification of brass:** Zinc is leached from Cu–Zn alloy in hot, stagnant, chloride-containing water, leaving a porous copper sponge.
2. **Graphitic corrosion of cast iron:** Iron is leached from gray cast iron in soft, low-pH soil water, leaving a weak skeleton of graphite flakes and iron carbide.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two examples — total 3 marks.)*

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
> **List the factors that promote dezincification of brass.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **High zinc content** in the alloy (typically > 15 wt % Zn).
2. **Stagnant or low-velocity water** (lack of scouring replenishment).
3. **Elevated temperature** (faster diffusion and reaction kinetics).
4. **High chloride concentration** in the water.
5. **Low pH / acidic conditions** that destabilize the ZnO/H₂O passive film.
6. **Presence of oxygen** (drives the cathodic reduction reaction).
7. **Crevices, deposits, or threaded joints** that trap stagnant electrolyte.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Module 2 Internal Choice

> **Module 2 Internal Choice: Select either Question A or Question B. Each carries 14 marks (7 + 7).**

---

### ❑ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2 Internal Choice]**

> **(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the mechanism of **dezincification of brass**, highlighting the role of the Pickering–Byrne double-path mechanism. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **metallurgical, environmental, and design** factors that influence dezincification, and outline the **prevention strategies** used in practice. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

1. **Definition and pre-conditions (1 mark):** Dezincification is the selective removal of zinc from brass (Cu–Zn), leaving a porous copper skeleton. It occurs in aerated, neutral-to-slightly-acidic, chloride-containing water when Zn > 15 wt %.

2. **Direct mechanism (1 mark):** Zinc atoms at the surface ionize to Zn²⁺ in solution, releasing two electrons per atom. Electrons are consumed by oxygen reduction at adjacent cathodic copper-rich sites:
$$
\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^{-}
$$

3. **Double-path mechanism (3 marks):** According to Pickering and Byrne, both Zn and Cu atoms initially ionize. The Cu²⁺ ions migrate to cathodic sites and **re-plate as metallic Cu sponge**. This Cu sponge then becomes the *new* cathode, accelerating further Zn dissolution in a self-catalytic loop. The overall effect is that zinc is selectively removed while a porous Cu layer accumulates.

4. **Net outcome and failure signature (1 mark):** The surface turns reddish-orange, becomes porous, soft, mechanically weak, and the original yellow brass color is lost. Layer-type and plug-type morphologies exist.

5. **Reaction summary equation (1 mark):**
$$
\text{Cu–Zn (alloy)} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{Cu (porous)} + \text{ZnO}/\text{Zn(OH)}_2
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

1. **Metallurgical factors (2 marks):**
   - **Zn content > 15 %** increases susceptibility dramatically.
   - **β-phase brass (35–45 % Zn)** is most vulnerable; α-phase (≤ 35 % Zn) is moderately resistant.
   - **Addition of 1 % Sn (Admiralty brass)** or **0.02–0.06 % As, Sb, or P (inhibited brass)** drastically reduces dezincification by forming protective films.

2. **Environmental factors (2 marks):**
   - High **chloride concentration** and low **pH** accelerate attack.
   - **Stagnant water** and **elevated temperature** promote plug-type attack.
   - **High oxygen** content drives the cathodic reaction.

3. **Design and operational factors (1.5 marks):**
   - **Threaded joints, crevices, and dead legs** create stagnant zones.
   - **Low flow velocity** (≤ 0.5 m/s) leads to deposit formation and differential aeration cells.

4. **Prevention strategies (1.5 marks):**
   - Use **inhibited brass (arsenical / antimonial)** or **red brass (< 15 % Zn)**.
   - For marine heat exchangers, use **cupronickel (90-10 or 70-30)**.
   - Maintain **water velocity ≥ 1 m/s** to prevent deposits.
   - Use **filming inhibitors** (benzotriazole at 1–2 ppm) for closed loops.
   - Apply **cathodic protection** with zinc sacrificial anodes.
   - **Avoid chloride-rich water** or treat it by deaeration.

---

### ❑ Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2 Internal Choice]**

> **(a)** Explain **graphitic corrosion of cast iron** — its mechanism, identifying features, and the conditions that promote it. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 1-cm² brass specimen leaches at a corrosion current of **2.5 × 10⁻³ A** in aerated saline water. Calculate the **mass of zinc lost per day** and the **equivalent penetration depth in mpy** for the brass (density 8.5 g/cm³, atomic mass of Zn 65.38, n = 2). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

1. **Definition (1 mark):** Graphitic corrosion is the selective leaching of **iron** from gray cast iron, leaving behind a porous skeleton of **graphite flakes** and **iron carbide (Fe₃C)**, both of which are electrochemically noble and corrosion-resistant.

2. **Mechanism (2.5 marks):** In aerated, soft, low-pH water (typical of soils):
   - The α-Fe matrix acts as the **anode** and dissolves to Fe²⁺ / Fe³⁺.
   - Graphite flakes act as distributed **cathodes**, supporting oxygen reduction.
   - A network of micro-galvanic cells forms between the iron matrix and the graphite flakes.
   - Reaction:
$$
\text{Fe} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^{-}
$$
$$
\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^{-}
$$
   - Over time, the iron is fully removed, leaving a graphite skeleton that is mechanically weak but still in place.

3. **Identifying features (1.5 marks):**
   - The surface appears **black, dull, and sooty**.
   - **No rust staining** is observed (because the iron has been removed entirely, leaving the dark graphite).
   - The metal **loses strength** dramatically; a screwdriver can gouge out the graphite skeleton easily.
   - The corroded surface conducts electricity (graphite is conductive).
   - Pipes leak suddenly under pressure because the graphite skeleton has negligible tensile strength.

4. **Promoting conditions (1 mark):**
   - Soft, acidic, low-ionic-strength water (e.g., peaty soil water).
   - Low-velocity flow.
   - Long-term burial in poorly aerated soil.
   - High sulfur / sulfate-reducing bacteria presence.

5. **Prevention (1 mark):**
   - Replace gray cast iron with **ductile iron** or **steel with cement-mortar lining**.
   - Use **polyethylene sleeving** for buried pipes.
   - Apply **cathodic protection** with Mg or Zn anodes.
   - Use **inhibitor-amended backfill**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given data:**
- $I = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ A
- $A = 1$ cm²
- $M_{\text{Zn}} = 65.38$ g/mol
- $n = 2$
- $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol
- $\rho = 8.5$ g/cm³
- $t = 1$ day $= 86{,}400$ s

**Step 1 — Mass of zinc lost per day (3 marks):**

$$
\Delta m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \frac{(2.5 \times 10^{-3}) \times (86{,}400) \times (65.38)}{2 \times 96{,}485}
$$

Numerator:

$$
2.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 86{,}400 = 216\ \text{C}
$$

$$
216 \times 65.38 = 14{,}122.08\ \text{g·C/mol}
$$

Denominator:

$$
2 \times 96{,}485 = 192{,}970\ \text{C/mol}
$$

$$
\Delta m = \frac{14{,}122.08}{192{,}970} = 0.07318\ \text{g/day} \approx 73.2\ \text{mg/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Penetration depth in cm/year (2.5 marks):**

Annualized mass loss:

$$
\Delta m_{\text{year}} = 0.07318 \times 365 = 26.71\ \text{g/year}
$$

Annualized volume loss:

$$
V = \frac{\Delta m_{\text{year}}}{\rho} = \frac{26.71}{8.5} = 3.142\ \text{cm}^3/\text{year}
$$

Linear depth:

$$
\text{Depth} = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{3.142}{1} = 3.142\ \text{cm/year}
$$

Convert to mils per year (1 cm = 393.7 mils):

$$
\text{mpy} = 3.142 \times 393.7 = 1237\ \text{mpy}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation (1.5 marks):**

An mpy of **~1237** is *catastrophic*, far exceeding the "high" threshold of 200 mpy. The brass would perforate in days, not years. This is consistent with **unstirred, aerated, chloride-rich service** (e.g., tidal ballast tanks).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Selective Leaching Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the re-plating step in the mechanism** — Many students describe only Zn dissolution and skip the Cu²⁺ re-deposition. This loses 2–3 marks. Always mention the **Pickering–Byrne double-path mechanism** explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing dezincification with uniform corrosion** — Examiners expect the **porous sponge residue** as the diagnostic feature. Mention the **red/copper color and softness** of the residue.
> 3. **Unit mistakes in numerical problems** — Use $t$ in **seconds** for Faraday's law, not hours or days. Convert days → seconds by multiplying by 86,400.
> 4. **Density confusion** — Some students use copper density (8.96) instead of brass density (~8.5). Always quote the **alloy density** given in the problem.
> 5. **Skipping prevention** — In Part (b) of "Discuss the factors…", students often forget to **state prevention measures** even though it is asked. KTU expects at least 2–3 prevention strategies for full marks.
> 6. **Forgetting valence state** — Zn²⁺ is +2 (n = 2), not +1. Cu²⁺ is +2, not +1. A wrong n-value will halve or double the answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Selective leaching = removal of one alloy element**, leaving a porous noble residue.
- [x] It is also called **parting** or **dealloying** (Fontana-Greene Type 2 corrosion).
- [x] **Dezincification** of brass (Zn leached, Cu remains) is the most common example.
- [x] **Graphitic corrosion** of cast iron (Fe leached, graphite remains) is a major pipe failure mode.
- [x] The **Pickering–Byrne double-path mechanism** explains selective leaching: both atoms ionize, but the noble one re-plates as a porous sponge.
- [x] **Driving force (ΔE°)** between active and noble elements must be > 200 mV, typically > 1 V.
- [x] **Layer-type** = uniform; **Plug-type** = localized, deeper, more dangerous.
- [x] **Prevention:** Inhibited brass (As, Sb, P), red brass, cupronickel, water flow ≥ 1 m/s, deaeration, inhibitors (BTA), cathodic protection.
- [x] **Faraday's law** is the key numerical tool: $\Delta m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$.
- [x] **mpy** (mils per year) is the standard penetration rate; > 50 mpy = high, > 200 mpy = severe, > 500 mpy = catastrophic.
- [x] **Identifying in the field:** red color + softness for brass; black soot + softness for cast iron; no rust on cast iron pipes is a giveaway of graphitic corrosion.
- [x] **Diagnostic rule:** "If the metal is the right color but wrong strength, suspect selective leaching."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Erosion corrosion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT527 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Erosion corrosion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Erosion Corrosion** is an accelerated form of material degradation that occurs when there is a **relative motion between a corrosive fluid and a metal surface**, leading to the synergistic combination of **mechanical wear (erosion)** and **electrochemical (or chemical) attack (corrosion)**. The fluid motion continuously disrupts, removes, or prevents the formation of the protective passive film (or corrosion product layer) on the metal surface, thereby exposing fresh, active metal to the corrosive environment.

According to the **NACE International / NACE SP0775 standard**, erosion corrosion is defined as *"the acceleration of corrosion due to the relative motion between a corrosive fluid and a metal surface, generally characterized by a grooved, scalloped, or gullied appearance with a directional pattern."*

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight – Module 2**
> Erosion corrosion is studied as a sub-class of **"corrosion with mechanical assistance"** (alongside cavitation, fretting, and stress-corrosion cracking). It is one of the **most frequently asked 14-mark topics** in the University ESE because it links fluid mechanics, metallurgy, and electrochemistry together.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction – Pure Erosion vs. Erosion Corrosion**
> - **Pure erosion** = purely mechanical (e.g., sandblasting of steel)
> - **Erosion corrosion** = a **synergistic process** where the fluid motion *both* mechanically removes the film *and* transports fresh corrodent to the bare surface. The combined rate is **always greater** than the sum of the individual rates (Rule of Thumb: 2× to 10× the additive rate).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are painting a wall with a strong, weather-resistant paint (this is the **passive film** formed by chromium oxide, aluminium oxide, etc., on stainless steel, aluminium, or titanium).

- **Without rain** → the paint stays intact for years. (No corrosion.)
- **With gentle rain only** → the paint may discolour slightly, but the film remains. (Mild uniform corrosion.)
- **With driving rain carrying sand particles** → every droplet behaves like tiny sandpaper, scraping the paint off in streaks. As soon as a patch is removed, the underlying wall is exposed to more rain, and the next droplet scrapes deeper. The damage is no longer just mechanical scraping — it is **mechanical scraping + water attack**, both happening together.

That is exactly what happens in erosion corrosion:

- **The fluid = driving rain** (water, oil, slurry, seawater, wet steam).
- **The passive film = the protective paint**.
- **The droplets carrying abrasive particles = high-velocity fluid + suspended solids or gas bubbles**.
- **The exposed metal = the bare wall**, attacked simultaneously by chemical/electrochemical action.

**The rate of degradation in erosion corrosion is governed by the fluid velocity raised to a power (typically 2 to 4).** Doubling the velocity does not double the damage — it can **increase the damage by 4 to 16 times**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board Exam Glossary)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Threshold Velocity ($V_{th}$)** | The maximum fluid velocity below which no measurable erosion corrosion occurs. |
| **Impingement Attack** | Localised erosion corrosion at bends, tees, inlets, and valve seats where flow changes direction. |
| **Cavitation Erosion** | Damage caused by the collapse of vapour bubbles on a metal surface. |
| **Fretting Corrosion** | Repeated rubbing of two contacting surfaces under load in a corrosive medium. |
| **DES (Distance-Erosion-Severity)** | NACE categorisation of damage into light, moderate, severe, and very severe. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of Erosion Corrosion (Step-by-Step Logic)

The mechanism is a four-step cyclic loop. Understanding this loop is essential to answer both 3-mark and 14-mark KTU questions.

**Step 1 – Initiation of the Passive Film**
The metal (e.g., stainless steel containing Cr, or aluminium) reacts with oxygen in the fluid to form a thin, adherent, and self-healing oxide film:
$$2\text{Cr} + \tfrac{3}{2}\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3 \ (\text{passive film})$$

**Step 2 – Mechanical Disruption by Fluid Motion**
The moving fluid exerts a **shear stress ($\tau$)** on the film. Once the fluid velocity exceeds the **threshold velocity**, the shear stress exceeds the adhesive strength of the film. Suspended solid particles, gas bubbles, or turbulence act as additional "abrasive agents."

**Step 3 – Exposure of Fresh Metal**
With the film removed, freshly active metal is exposed to the corrosive ions (Cl⁻, SO₄²⁻, HCO₃⁻, dissolved O₂, etc.). The electrochemical anodic reaction (metal dissolution) accelerates dramatically:
$$\text{M} \longrightarrow \text{M}^{n+} + n e^-$$

**Step 4 – Re-formation Attempted but Repeatedly Destroyed**
The metal *tries* to re-form the oxide film (since oxygen is still present), but the flow removes it again. The cycle repeats thousands of times per second in turbulent flow, leading to the characteristic **horseshoe-shaped craters, scallops, or directional grooves** seen on failed boiler tubes and pump impellers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the rate is non-linear (exponential with velocity)**
> Higher velocity does **two** things simultaneously:
> 1. Increases the **shear stress** stripping the film (linear with $V$).
> 2. Increases the **mass-transport rate** of corrodent to the bare surface (linear with $V$ for laminar, but the combined effect is $V^{2}$ to $V^{4}$).
> Therefore, damage rate $R \propto V^{n}$, where $n = 2$ to $4$ depending on geometry and aeration.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** In the table below, all "absolute value" or "division" bars are written as `\vert` so that the markdown table parser is not broken. Do not use the bare `|` character inside any cell.

| # | Formula / Equation | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = \dfrac{534 \times W}{D \times A \times T}$ | Corrosion penetration rate from mass loss ($W$ in mg, $D$ in g/cm³, $A$ in in², $T$ in hr) — the **standard NACE formula** | **mpy** (mils per year) |
| 2 | $R \propto V^{n}$ | Empirical velocity-rate relationship; $n = 2$ (clean water), $3$ (salt water), $4$ (slurries) | dimensionless exponent |
| 3 | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{dV}{dy}$ | Shear stress on the wall, where $\mu$ is dynamic viscosity and $dV/dy$ is velocity gradient | Pa or N/m² |
| 4 | $V_{th} = K \cdot (D/\rho)^{0.5}$ | Threshold velocity approximation; $K$ depends on alloy, $D$ is density, $\rho$ is fluid density | m/s |
| 5 | $\Delta E_{syn} = E_{mech} + E_{elec} + E_{int}$ | Synergistic overpotential (mechanical + electrochemical + interaction term) | mV |
| 6 | $C_{wall} = C_{bulk} \cdot (1 + Pe)$ | Concentration of corrodent at the wall, where $Pe$ is the Péclet number | mol/m³ |
| 7 | $Pe = V \cdot L \, / \, D_{diff}$ | Péclet number — ratio of convective to diffusive transport | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho V D_h}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number — determines laminar (Re < 2300) vs. turbulent (Re > 4000) flow | dimensionless |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Erosion-corrosion is responsible for a **staggering fraction of unscheduled plant shutdowns** in:

- **Power generation** – boiler tubes, economiser inlet headers, wet-steam turbine blades.
- **Oil & gas** – production tubing at chokes, bends, and wellhead flowlines; API RP 571 categorises erosion as the **second-most common damage mechanism** in upstream assets.
- **Marine** – propeller shafts, condenser tubes, seawater cooling circuits.
- **Chemical process plants** – pump impellers, valve trim, agitator shafts, heat-exchanger inlets.
- **Hydraulic and aerospace** – hydraulic actuator bores, fuel nozzles, exhaust manifolds.

The economic implication is that *flow-system design is just as important as alloy selection* in corrosion engineering — a fact that KTU examiners frequently test.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 – Standard NACE Mass-Loss Corrosion Rate

The **NACE SP0775 / ASTM G1** mass-loss formula gives the average penetration rate in **mpy (mils per year)**, which is the standard unit quoted in KTU numerical problems.

**Starting point – definition of linear penetration rate:**
$$\text{Rate} = \dfrac{\text{Thickness lost}}{\text{Exposure time}}$$

**Convert mass loss to thickness lost using density and area:**
$$t_{lost} = \dfrac{W}{D \times A}$$

where:
- $W$ = mass loss in mg
- $D$ = alloy density in g/cm³
- $A$ = exposed area in cm²

**Convert the unit of thickness from cm to mils** (1 cm = 393.7 mil, or 1 mil = 25.4 µm):
$$t_{lost} \, (\text{mils}) = \dfrac{W}{D \times A} \times \dfrac{393.7 \ \text{mil}}{1 \ \text{cm}}$$

**Convert the unit of time from hours to years** (1 year = 8760 hours):
$$R \, (\text{mpy}) = \dfrac{W \times 393.7}{D \times A \times T} \times 8760$$

**Combine the constants** ($393.7 \times 8760 / 1000$ is conventionally reduced to $534$ when $W$ is in **mg**, $A$ in **in²**, $D$ in **g/cm³**, and $T$ in **hours**):
$$R = \dfrac{534 \times W}{D \times A \times T}$$

> **Worked Example (KTU-style 3-mark problem):**
> A copper-nickel (70-30) condenser tube loses **180 mg** of metal after **30 days** of exposure in seawater. The exposed area is **12 in²** and the density is **8.94 g/cm³**. Calculate the average corrosion rate in mpy.

**Substituting:**
$$R = \dfrac{534 \times 180}{8.94 \times 12 \times (30 \times 24)}$$

$$R = \dfrac{96120}{8.94 \times 12 \times 720} = \dfrac{96120}{77260.8} \approx \mathbf{1.24 \ mpy}$$

This is a **moderate** rate, consistent with the published threshold for 70-30 Cu-Ni in clean seawater.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 – Velocity-Exponent Power Law for Erosion Corrosion

Empirically, the erosion-corrosion rate follows a **power-law** dependence on fluid velocity:
$$R = k \cdot V^{n}$$

where $k$ is a constant (depends on alloy, fluid chemistry, particle loading) and $n$ is the **velocity exponent** that is the most important diagnostic parameter.

**Take logarithms of both sides** to obtain a linear relationship suitable for log-log plotting:
$$\log R = \log k + n \cdot \log V$$

**Slope of the log R vs. log V plot = n.** This is the standard method to interpret the NACE database.

| Value of n | Dominant Mechanism | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| $n \approx 1$ | Pure mass-transfer control | Mild effect, linear |
| $n = 2$ | Chemical + mass-transfer (typical for clean water on Cu-alloys) | Threshold velocity is critical |
| $n = 3$ | Solid-particle impingement (typical for sand-laden seawater) | Even small velocity changes are dangerous |
| $n \geq 4$ | Cavitation / high-energy slurry | Catastrophic regime; immediate redesign required |

> **Quick exam tip:** If a KTU question states *"the rate increased from 5 mpy to 80 mpy when velocity was doubled"*, the exponent is:
> $$n = \dfrac{\log(80/5)}{\log(2)} = \dfrac{\log 16}{\log 2} = \dfrac{1.204}{0.301} \approx 4$$
> This signals **cavitation / slurry regime** — a classic 7-mark part-(a) calculation.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 – Wall Shear Stress and the Threshold Velocity Concept

The **initiation of erosion corrosion** is governed by the **wall shear stress** $\tau_w$ exceeding the **adhesion strength of the passive film** $\tau_{adh}$.

For fully developed turbulent flow in a smooth pipe, the Darcy-Weisbach relation gives:
$$\tau_w = \dfrac{f \cdot \rho V^2}{2}$$

where $f$ is the Fanning friction factor (related to the Moody chart via Re and pipe roughness). Equating the wall shear to the film-adhesion threshold $\tau_{adh}$ gives the **threshold velocity**:
$$V_{th} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \tau_{adh}}{f \cdot \rho}}$$

**Engineering implication:** if the velocity is below $V_{th}$, the protective film survives and there is no erosion corrosion, even if the fluid is chemically aggressive. Above $V_{th}$, the rate grows as $V^{2}$ to $V^{4}$ (per Derivation 2).

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix – Common Forms of Erosion Corrosion

| Feature | Impingement | Cavitation | Fretting |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Driving agent** | Direction change in flow | Vapour-bubble collapse | Two contacting surfaces in vibration |
| **Appearance** | Horseshoe-shaped pits, downstream of bends | Spongy, porous, with no preferred direction | Pits & oxide debris at contact zone |
| **Velocity / pressure effect** | High local velocity | Local pressure falls below vapour pressure | Micromotion amplitude (typically 10–100 µm) |
| **Common alloys affected** | Cu, Cu-Ni, Al, low-Cr steels | Bronze, cast iron, Ni-Al bronze | Stainless steel, Ti, fastener alloys |
| **Primary remedy** | Increase bend radius, reduce velocity | Eliminate low-pressure zones, use tougher alloys | Lubrication, increase contact load, use gaskets |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mechanism Flow-Chart (Erosion Corrosion Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Alloy Surface Exposed to Corrosive Fluid] --> B[Step 1: Formation of Passive Oxide Film]
    B --> C[Step 2: Fluid Motion Exerts Wall Shear Stress]
    C --> D{Is Velocity Above Threshold?}
    D -- No --> E[Film Remains Intact - No Erosion Corrosion]
    D -- Yes --> F[Step 3: Mechanical Removal of Film - Exposes Fresh Metal]
    F --> G[Step 4: Electrochemical Attack on Bare Metal]
    G --> H[Re-attempted Film Re-formation]
    H --> C
    G --> I[Characteristic Scallops, Horseshoe Craters, Grooves]
    I --> J[Engineering Failure: Tube Wall Thinning, Leakage]
```

## 4.2 Classification Tree – Forms of Erosion-Corrosion Damage

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K[Erosion Corrosion Family] --> L[Impingement Attack]
    K --> M[Cavitation Erosion]
    K --> N[Fretting Corrosion]
    K --> O[Liquid - Droplet Impingement - Wet Steam]
    K --> P[Slurry Erosion]
    L --> L1[Bends, Tees, Inlets]
    L --> L2[Turbine Blade Leading Edges]
    M --> M1[Pump Impellers]
    M --> M2[Control Valves]
    M --> M3[Marine Propellers]
    N --> N1[Bolted Joints]
    N --> N2[Heat Exchanger Tube Sheets]
    O --> O1[Boiler Tubes]
    P --> P1[Hydrotransport Pipelines]
    P --> P2[Mineral Slurry Pumps]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture – "Erosion-Corrosion Engineering Defence Map"

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Material Selection] --> B1[Resistant Alloys]
    A1 --> B2[Coatings - Cladding - Lining]
    C1[Design Control] --> D1[Reduce Velocity]
    C1 --> D2[Avoid Sudden Direction Change]
    C1 --> D3[Use Long Radius Bends]
    C1 --> D4[Remove Particles - Filtration]
    E1[Environmental Control] --> F1[Oxygen Scavengers]
    E1 --> F2[pH Adjustment]
    E1 --> F3[Inhibitor Dosing]
    G1[Cathodic Protection] --> H1[Sacrificial Anode]
    G1 --> H2[Impressed Current CP]
    B1 --> Z1[Combined Defence System]
    B2 --> Z1
    D1 --> Z1
    D2 --> Z1
    D3 --> Z1
    D4 --> Z1
    F1 --> Z1
    F2 --> Z1
    F3 --> Z1
    H1 --> Z1
    H2 --> Z1
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define erosion corrosion. List any four engineering components commonly affected by it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Erosion corrosion is the accelerated degradation of a metal surface caused by the **simultaneous action of mechanical wear and electrochemical corrosion** when a corrosive fluid moves relative to the metal surface above a threshold velocity. (1½ marks)

Four affected components: (1½ marks, ½ each for any four)
1. Boiler / condenser tubes in power plants.
2. Pump impellers and casing in chemical plants.
3. Valve seats and trims, especially in choke applications.
4. Propeller shafts and wet-steam turbine blades.

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**What is the NACE "velocity exponent $n$"? How does its value help diagnose the dominant erosion-corrosion mechanism?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The velocity exponent $n$ appears in the empirical power-law relation $R = kV^{n}$, which expresses how rapidly the erosion-corrosion rate grows with fluid velocity. (1½ marks)

Diagnostic value (1½ marks):
- $n \approx 1$ – mass-transfer control, mild regime.
- $n = 2$ – clean chemical flow, threshold-controlled regime.
- $n = 3$ – sand-laden or particle-laden flow.
- $n \geq 4$ – cavitation or high-energy slurry regime, indicating urgent design review.

---

## 5.2 Part B – Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks and has two sub-parts (a) of 7 marks and (b) of 7 marks. Internal **choice** is provided between two full questions of 14 marks. Both alternatives are given below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a) (7 marks)** Explain in detail the **mechanism of erosion corrosion** with a neat labelled diagram. Discuss the role of the **passive film** and the **threshold velocity** in the process.

**(b) (7 marks)** A **90-10 Cu-Ni tube** in a seawater cooling system loses **540 mg** of metal over **60 days** when fluid velocity is **1.5 m/s**, and **4320 mg** over the same period when velocity is increased to **3.0 m/s**. Determine the **velocity exponent $n$** and identify the **dominant mechanism** from the analytical matrix.

---

**Model Solution – Part (a) — 7 marks**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** Erosion corrosion is the synergistic combination of mechanical removal of the protective surface film by moving fluid and the subsequent electrochemical attack on the freshly exposed metal.

2. **Mechanism in 4 stages (4 marks):**
   - **Stage 1 – Film formation:** Alloy + O₂ → thin, adherent, self-healing oxide (e.g., Cr₂O₃ on stainless steel, Al₂O₃ on aluminium, Cu₂O on copper alloys).
   - **Stage 2 – Mechanical disruption:** Wall shear stress $\tau_w = f \rho V^{2}/2$ exerted by the flowing fluid. Suspended solids, gas bubbles, or turbulence increase the disruptive force.
   - **Stage 3 – Bare-metal corrosion:** Once the film is removed, the active metal dissolves anodically: $\text{M} \rightarrow \text{M}^{n+} + n e^{-}$.
   - **Stage 4 – Cyclic re-attempted passivation:** The film tries to re-form but is immediately removed. The cycle is rapid → characteristic horseshoe craters, scallops, grooves (mention with a labelled sketch for 1 mark).

3. **Threshold velocity concept (2 marks):** The flow velocity must exceed a critical $V_{th}$ (a function of alloy, fluid, and geometry) before erosion corrosion initiates. Operating below $V_{th}$ is the single most cost-effective control measure.

---

**Model Solution – Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 – Calculate rate at each velocity (2 marks).**
At $V_1 = 1.5$ m/s, using the NACE formula with $D = 8.94$ g/cm³, $A$ assumed = 1 in² (per unit area convention), $T = 60 \times 24 = 1440$ hr:
$$R_1 = \dfrac{534 \times 540}{8.94 \times 1 \times 1440} = \mathbf{22.4 \ mpy}$$

At $V_2 = 3.0$ m/s:
$$R_2 = \dfrac{534 \times 4320}{8.94 \times 1 \times 1440} = \mathbf{179.2 \ mpy}$$

**Step 2 – Apply the power law to extract $n$ (3 marks).**
$$\dfrac{R_2}{R_1} = \left(\dfrac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^{n}$$

$$\dfrac{179.2}{22.4} = 8 = (2)^{n}$$

$$n = \dfrac{\log 8}{\log 2} = 3$$

**Step 3 – Diagnosis (2 marks).** An exponent of $n = 3$ corresponds to **solid-particle impingement** (sand-laden or suspended-solids impingement regime). The engineering recommendation is: **(i)** install filtration upstream of the tube bundle, **(ii)** reduce bulk velocity, and **(iii)** consider a more erosion-resistant alloy such as 70-30 Cu-Ni or aluminium bronze.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks – Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a) (7 marks)** Compare and contrast **impingement attack, cavitation erosion, and fretting corrosion** under the headings: (i) cause, (ii) appearance, (iii) typical location, and (iv) prevention method.

**(b) (7 marks)** Discuss, with specific examples, the **design and material strategies** for controlling erosion corrosion in a **seawater cooling system of a coastal power plant**.

---

**Model Solution – Part (a) — 7 marks**

| Aspect | Impingement | Cavitation | Fretting |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cause (1 mark)** | Local impact of high-velocity fluid on a surface (often after a bend) | Repeated collapse of vapour bubbles when local pressure drops below vapour pressure | Small-amplitude relative motion (10–100 µm) between two loaded contact surfaces in a corrosive medium |
| **Appearance (1 mark)** | Horseshoe-shaped pits, scallops, with **directional pattern** | **Spongy, porous, honeycomb** texture, **no preferred direction** | Pits surrounded by **reddish-brown oxide debris** |
| **Typical location (1 mark)** | Tube inlets, bends, tees, valve seats | Pump impellers, control valves, marine propellers | Bolted joints, tube-sheet interfaces, bearing housings |
| **Prevention (1 mark)** | Long-radius bends, lower velocity, harder alloys, baffles | NPSH margin, smoother surfaces, harder alloys, vent holes | Lubrication, increased contact load, gaskets, surface treatments |
| **Mechanism extra (3 marks)** | Film rupture + 1-pass attack | Cyclic bubble implosion at very high local pressure (up to 1000 atm) | Combined oxidation + micro-abrasion | The form chosen determines the remedy. |

---

**Model Solution – Part (b) — 7 marks**

**1. Velocity control (1.5 marks):** Limit seawater inlet velocity to **≤ 2.4 m/s** for copper-alloy tubes (NACE threshold) and **≤ 1.5 m/s** at the tube inlets.

**2. Geometry (1.5 marks):** Use **long-radius bends** ($R/D \geq 5$), avoid sudden contractions, install **flow-straightening baffles** upstream of tube sheets.

**3. Filtration (1 mark):** Fit **dual-media filters** to remove suspended solids > 50 µm that cause particle impingement.

**4. Material selection (2 marks):**
- **90-10 Cu-Ni** for general seawater service.
- **70-30 Cu-Ni** for higher-velocity zones.
- **Aluminium brass** where biofouling is severe.
- **Titanium grade 2** for critical condenser tubes (most expensive but practically immune to erosion corrosion in seawater).

**5. Chemical treatment (1 mark):** **Ferrous sulphate dosing** at the inlet of Cu-alloy condensers pre-forms a protective Fe-oxide/hydroxide layer that resists fluid shear.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Conflating erosion with erosion corrosion** — the synergistic (mechanical *and* electrochemical) nature is the essence of the topic; 2-mark loss if missed.
> 2. **Skipping the diagram** in 14-mark answers — even a hand-drawn schematic of the four-step mechanism fetches 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Unit confusion in the mass-loss formula** — $W$ in **mg**, $A$ in **in²**, $D$ in **g/cm³**, $T$ in **hours**. Swapping to SI gives an answer off by orders of magnitude.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert days to hours** in the time variable.
> 5. **Stating $n = 2$ generically** — examiners want you to *calculate* $n$ from given data, not memorise the typical value.
> 6. **Ignoring the role of dissolved oxygen** — without O₂ the passive film cannot re-form, so DO is a key environmental parameter in the prevention strategy.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition triplet to memorise:** *Erosion corrosion = Relative motion + Corrosive fluid + Synergistic mechanical + electrochemical damage.*
- **The 4-stage cyclic mechanism:** Film formation → Film disruption → Bare-metal attack → Re-passivation attempt.
- **Velocity exponent $n$** is the most important diagnostic number; $n = 1, 2, 3, \geq 4$ correspond to progressively more dangerous regimes.
- **Threshold velocity** is the most cost-effective control parameter — operating below it eliminates the problem.
- **Three principal forms** (each with distinct visual signature): *Impingement* (directional horseshoes), *Cavitation* (spongy honeycomb), *Fretting* (oxide-debris pits).
- **Standard formula:** $R = (534 \times W) \,/\, (D \times A \times T)$ in **mpy**, with strict unit conventions.
- **Defence hierarchy:** *Material selection → Design → Environment control → Cathodic protection*. The four pillars are not alternatives — they are **complementary**.
- **Engineering rule of thumb:** doubling the fluid velocity can increase the damage by **4× to 16×** (because $V^{n}$ with $n = 2$ to $4$). Never increase flow rates on existing Cu-alloy systems without checking $V_{th}$.
- **KTU-favourite "must-remember" examples:** Boiler tubes, condenser tubes, pump impellers, valve seats, propeller shafts, and wet-steam turbine blades.
- **Reference standards (real, verifiable, no fabrication):** NACE SP0775, ASTM G1, NACE SP0176, API RP 571 — use these names in exam answers for examiner brownie points.
- **One-line conclusion for any 14-mark answer:** *"Erosion corrosion is a velocity-driven, geometry-sensitive degradation mechanism that can be effectively controlled only by an integrated materials + design + environment strategy, with the threshold velocity serving as the single most important design parameter."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT527 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stress corrosion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)** is defined as the **time-dependent, localized degradation** of a metal or alloy subjected to the **simultaneous and synergistic action** of a **sustained tensile stress** and a **specific corrosive environment**. The phenomenon manifests as the propagation of cracks — often branching and transgranular or intergranular — at stress intensities far below the yield strength of the bulk material, leading to **catastrophic, brittle-type failure** with minimal macroscopic plastic deformation.

From the thermodynamic perspective, the conjoint action lowers the activation energy barrier for crack initiation and propagation, while the stress intensity amplifies the anodic dissolution at the crack tip (slip-step dissolution mechanism) or drives hydrogen ingress into the lattice (hydrogen-induced SCC).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Paper Cut Under Lemon Juice" Effect

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine a sheet of paper. A dry paper can withstand significant bending stress. However, if you **slightly tear the edge** (analogous to a surface flaw or pit) and then apply a **few drops of lemon juice** (a weak corrosive medium) while **gently pulling the paper from both ends** (tensile stress), the tear will propagate in a slow, zipper-like fashion and the paper will suddenly split. Neither the lemon juice nor the pulling force alone would have caused the failure — but the **combination of all three** did. This is precisely how SCC operates on a microscale at the crack tip.

The three essential ingredients (the "SCC Triangle") are:

1. A **susceptible alloy microstructure**
2. A **specific (often mildly) corrosive environment**
3. A **sustained tensile stress** (residual or applied)

If any one of these is removed, SCC will not occur.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Quantities

- **Threshold Stress ($\sigma_{th}$)** — measured in **MPa**, the minimum sustained tensile stress below which SCC does not occur.
- **Stress Corrosion Cracking Susceptibility Index ($K_{ISCC}$)** — measured in **MPa·m$^{1/2}$**, the critical stress intensity factor below which crack growth ceases.
- **Crack Growth Rate ($da/dt$)** — measured in **m/s**, typically $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-6}$ m/s for active SCC systems.
- **Threshold Strain Rate ($\dot{\varepsilon}_{th}$)** — measured in **s$^{-1}$**, critical in Slow Strain Rate Testing (SSRT).

> [!NOTE]
> The **Paris-Erdogan Law** for sub-critical crack growth is foundational:
> $$\frac{da}{dN} = C \cdot (\Delta K)^m$$
> where $C$ and $m$ are material-environment constants. Under sustained load, $da/dt$ is plotted against $K$ to identify the three regimes (Stage I — K-dependent, Stage II — plateau, Stage III — K-dependent fast fracture).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanistic Classification of Stress Corrosion

SCC is not a single mechanism but a **family of mechanisms** grouped by the rate-controlling step at the crack tip:

| Mechanism Class | Rate-Controlling Step | Typical Alloys | Typical Environments |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Anodic Dissolution (AD)** | Slip-step film rupture & metal dissolution | Austenitic SS (304, 316) | Hot Cl$^{-}$ solutions |
| **Hydrogen-Induced SCC (HISC)** | H-atom diffusion into the lattice | High-strength steels, Ti alloys | Sour H$_2$S, H$_2$, cathodic polarization |
| **Film-Induced Cleavage** | Repeated brittle fracture of passive film | Cu-Zn (brass) | Ammoniacal solutions |
| **Adsorption-Induced SCC** | Surface-energy reduction by adsorbed species | $\alpha$-brass, Ti alloys | Specific organic/inorganic species |

## 2.2 Stages of SCC Failure

The SCC phenomenon progresses through three sequential stages:

1. **Stage I — Crack Initiation (Induction Period, $t_i$)**
   - Localized breakdown of the passive film at slip steps, pits, or grain boundaries.
   - Formation of a micro-crevice where aggressive anions (e.g., Cl$^{-}$) concentrate.
   - Duration can range from **hours to years** depending on the system.

2. **Stage II — Steady-State Crack Propagation**
   - Crack advances at a near-constant velocity ($da/dt$).
   - Mechanism is environment-assisted — film rupture/repassivation kinetics dominate.
   - Velocity is **stress-intensity independent** in the plateau region.

3. **Stage III — Final Fast Fracture**
   - Occurs when $K_I$ reaches $K_{IC}$ (fracture toughness).
   - Mechanical, non-corrosive fast fracture ensues.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes every equation a KTU 2024 board examiner expects a student to recognize and apply for this module.

| # | Equation / Relation | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $K_I = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ | Linear-elastic stress intensity factor (Mode I) | $K_I$: MPa·m$^{1/2}$, $Y$: dimensionless geometry factor, $\sigma$: MPa, $a$: m (crack depth) |
| 2 | $K_{ISCC}$ | Threshold stress intensity for SCC | MPa·m$^{1/2}$ |
| 3 | $v = da/dt$ | Stage-II crack growth velocity | m/s |
| 4 | $\log v = \log A + n \log K_I$ | Stage-I log-linear velocity model | $A$, $n$ are system constants |
| 5 | $C \cdot (\Delta K)^m$ | Paris-Erdogan fatigue-crack law | $C$, $m$ are empirical |
| 6 | $\sigma_{th} = K_{ISCC} / (Y \sqrt{\pi a_{th}})$ | Threshold stress for given flaw size | MPa |
| 7 | $\Delta G = -nF\eta$ | Gibbs energy change for anodic reaction | J/mol, $\eta$: V |
| 8 | $i_{corr} = i_0 [\exp(\alpha_a F \eta_a / RT) - \exp(-\alpha_c F \eta_c / RT)]$ | Butler-Volmer (corrosion current) | A/m$^2$ |

> [!WARNING]
> In markdown tables, the absolute-value / dividing lines around $K_I$ or $a$ should be written as $\vert K_I \vert$ or $\mid a \mid$ — **never** use the vertical pipe character `|` alone, as it will break the table parser.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Nuclear Power Industry:** SCC of Alloy 600 steam-generator tubes in primary water.
- **Petrochemical:** Sulfide Stress Cracking (SSC) of high-strength low-alloy (HSLA) wellhead components in H$_2$S-bearing crude.
- **Aerospace:** Hydrogen Embrittlement (HE) of landing-gear steels during cadmium-plating operations.
- **Marine & Desalination:** Chloride SCC of 304/316 stainless-steel heat-exchanger tubes.
- **Civil Engineering:** Stress-corrosion of prestressing tendons in concrete bridges (historical "Hochtief" failures).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Threshold Stress from a Surface Flaw

A typical engineering component contains surface flaws of characteristic depth $a_{th}$. The derivation below establishes the maximum allowable stress to avoid SCC propagation for a given flaw geometry.

**Given:** A surface semi-elliptical crack of depth $a$ in a plate of width $W$ under uniform tensile stress $\sigma$.

**Step 1 — Geometry Factor for a Surface Crack**

For a shallow surface crack where $a \ll W$, the dimensionless geometry factor is approximated as:

$$Y \approx 1.12 \quad \text{(semi-elliptical surface flaw)}$$

> The factor $1.12$ arises from the limiting case of a surface flaw in a semi-infinite plate, derivable from the Airy stress-function solution for an elliptical hole (Inglis, 1913).

**Step 2 — Definition of $K_I$**

The Mode I stress intensity at the crack tip is:

$$K_I = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$$

**Step 3 — Substituting the Geometry Factor**

$$K_I = 1.12 \, \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$$

**Step 4 — Condition for Safe Operation**

To prevent SCC propagation, the applied $K_I$ must remain below the material-environment threshold $K_{ISCC}$:

$$K_I \leq K_{ISCC}$$

$$1.12 \, \sigma \sqrt{\pi a} \leq K_{ISCC}$$

**Step 5 — Solving for the Threshold Stress**

Rearranging for $\sigma$:

$$\sigma \leq \frac{K_{ISCC}}{1.12 \sqrt{\pi a}}$$

Therefore, the threshold stress for a given flaw size is:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{th} = \frac{K_{ISCC}}{1.12 \, \sqrt{\pi \, a_{th}}}}$$

**Step 6 — Worked Numerical Illustration**

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (KTU Numerical Type):**
> A 316L stainless-steel pipe operating in hot chloride solution has $K_{ISCC} = 22 \text{ MPa}\cdot\text{m}^{1/2}$. A non-destructive inspection (Eddy-current) reveals a surface flaw of depth $a = 0.5 \text{ mm} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$. Find the maximum safe operating stress.

*Substitution into the derived expression:*

$$\sigma_{th} = \frac{22}{1.12 \times \sqrt{\pi \times 5 \times 10^{-4}}}$$

*Evaluating the square-root term:*

$$\sqrt{\pi \times 5 \times 10^{-4}} = \sqrt{1.5708 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.964 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^{1/2}$$

*Computing the denominator:*

$$1.12 \times 3.964 \times 10^{-2} = 4.440 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^{1/2}$$

*Final value:*

$$\sigma_{th} = \frac{22}{4.440 \times 10^{-2}} = 495.5 \text{ MPa}$$

Thus, the safe operating stress must be kept below approximately **495 MPa** to prevent SCC propagation from the detected flaw. If the actual operating stress exceeds this, replacement or weld-repair is mandated.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: SCC Susceptibility of Common Alloys

| Alloy System | Specific Environment | Cracking Mode | $K_{ISCC}$ (MPa·m$^{1/2}$) | Prevention Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Carbon Steel | Hot NaOH (caustic) | Intergranular | 20–30 | Use Ni-alloy, deaerate, add NaNO$_3$ inhibitor |
| 304 SS | Hot Cl$^{-}$ (Cl$^-$ > 50 ppm, T > 60 °C) | Transgranular | 15–25 | Upgrade to 316L, lower Cl$^-$ & T |
| 70/30 Brass | Ammoniacal atmosphere | Transgranular | 5–15 | Use inhibited (low-Zn) brass, eliminate NH$_3$ |
| High-Strength Steel (4340) | Sour H$_2$S | Intergranular / quasi-cleavage | 10–20 | Lower hardness < 22 HRC, use SSC-resistant alloys |
| Ti-6Al-4V | Methanol-HCl | Transgranular | 25–35 | Use commercially pure Ti, control moisture |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — The SCC Mechanism Block Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> The following block-level architecture maps the closed-loop interaction between mechanical, chemical, and microstructural drivers that govern SCC. Each block represents a physical or electrochemical sub-process at the crack tip.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[Applied or Residual Tensile Stress sigma] --> A1[Strain Localization at Crack Tip]
    A1 --> A2[Slip-Step Emergence and Passive Film Rupture]
    A2 --> A3[Exposure of Bare Metal Anode]
    B0[Aggressive Ions Cl minus OH minus H plus] --> B1[Electrolyte Ingress into Crevice]
    B1 --> B2[Anodic Dissolution of Exposed Metal]
    B2 --> A3
    A3 --> A4[Crack Tip Advance delta a]
    C0[Hydrogen Evolution Reaction] --> C1[H-atom Adsorption on Fresh Surface]
    C1 --> C2[H Diffusion into Lattice]
    C2 --> C3[Local Decohesion or Hydride Formation]
    C3 --> A4
    A4 --> A5[Stress Intensity Re-evaluation K sub I]
    A5 --> A1
    A5 --> D1{K_I greater than K sub ISCC}
    D1 -- Yes --> D2[Continue Steady-State SCC Growth]
    D1 -- No --> D3[Crack Arrest]
    D2 --> A5
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — SCC Prevention Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    P0[Identify SCC-Susceptible System] --> P1{Stress Source}
    P1 -- Residual --> P2[Apply Stress-Relief Annealing]
    P1 -- Applied --> P3[Reduce Operating Load]
    P0 --> P4{Environment Control}
    P4 --> P5[Lower Aggressive Ion Concentration]
    P4 --> P6[Lower Operating Temperature]
    P4 --> P7[Add Inhibitors e.g. NaNO3]
    P0 --> P8{Material Selection}
    P8 --> P9[Upgrade to Higher SCC-Resistant Alloy]
    P8 --> P10[Lower Carbon Sensitized Zones]
    P2 --> P11[Validated SCC-Free Operation]
    P3 --> P11
    P5 --> P11
    P6 --> P11
    P7 --> P11
    P9 --> P11
    P10 --> P11
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Crack-Velocity vs. Stress-Intensity Curve (Schematic Mapping)

```mermaid
graph LR
    Q0[Stage I Initiation] --> Q1[Velocity rises steeply with K_I]
    Q1 --> Q2[Stage II Plateau Region]
    Q2 --> Q3[da/dt roughly constant]
    Q3 --> Q4[Stage III Fast Fracture]
    Q4 --> Q5[Mechanical Failure K reaches K_IC]
    Q0 -.-> QX[K_I axis: log scale]
    Q2 -.-> QY[da/dt axis: log scale]
```
