# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1, Q1a]**

**Q1a.** *Define Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC). List the three essential conditions required for its occurrence.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC) is the cracking of a metal or alloy caused by the **simultaneous action** of a sustained tensile stress and a specific corrosive environment, leading to brittle-type failure at stresses well below the yield strength of the material.

The three essential conditions are:
1. A **susceptible alloy microstructure** (e.g., austenitic SS in chloride media).
2. A **specific corrosive environment** (e.g., hot Cl$^{-}$, H$_2$S, caustic).
3. A **sustained tensile stress** — either **applied (service)**, **residual (welding, cold-working)**, or **thermal**.

> **(1 mark — definition, 2 marks — three conditions with examples.)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 2, Q1b]**

**Q1b.** *What is Caustic Embrittlement? Mention the alloy and environment involved.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Caustic Embrittlement is a classical form of **intergranular stress corrosion cracking** occurring in **carbon steel boiler tubes** exposed to **concentrated sodium hydroxide (NaOH)** at elevated temperatures (typically 100 °C – 300 °C). The mechanism involves:

- Concentration of NaOH in riveted/bolted joints or tube-roll-expanded regions by **alkaline attack on silica insulation or evaporative concentration**.
- Intergranular attack at the grain boundaries where the protective magnetite (Fe$_3$O$_4$) film is dissolved by concentrated OH$^{-}$.
- The residual forging/expansion stress drives the intergranular crack propagation, often leading to sudden boiler-tube failure.

It is prevented by using **sodium sulfite or sodium nitrate as inhibitors**, by avoiding silica-containing insulants, and by upgrading to **Ni-alloy (Inconel) tubes**.

> **(1 mark — definition, 1 mark — alloy + environment, 1 mark — mechanism & prevention.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2 Choice Question]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, describe the **slip-step dissolution mechanism** of stress corrosion cracking in austenitic stainless steels. Highlight the role of the passive film, chloride ions, and slip-step emergence. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **threshold stress $\sigma_{th}$** for a component with a surface crack depth $a$ in terms of $K_{ISCC}$. A 316L SS plate in seawater has $K_{ISCC} = 18$ MPa·m$^{1/2}$. If inspection detects a surface flaw of $1.0$ mm depth, compute the safe operating stress. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a) — Slip-Step Dissolution Mechanism (7 Marks):**

The slip-step dissolution mechanism (also called the **film-rupture model**, proposed by Swann, 1963) is the dominant mechanism of transgranular SCC in austenitic stainless steels (e.g., 304, 316) exposed to hot chloride environments.

**Working Steps (1 mark each, total 7):**

1. **Initial passive state:** The austenitic SS surface is covered by a thin, adherent, Cr-rich passive film ($\gamma$-Fe$_2$O$_3$ / Cr$_2$O$_3$ mixed oxide, ~2–5 nm thick) that protects the metal from further anodic dissolution.

2. **Application of tensile stress:** When a sustained tensile stress is applied, dislocation motion creates **persistent slip bands** that intersect the surface.

3. **Emergence of slip steps:** The slip bands emerge as **microscopic steps** (height ~10–100 nm) on the surface, which **mechanically rupture** the overlying passive film.

4. **Exposure of bare metal:** At the freshly exposed metal, a highly active anodic region appears, with a local dissolution current density of up to **$10^3$–$10^4$ A/m$^2$** — orders of magnitude higher than the passive current (~$10^{-2}$ A/m$^2$).

5. **Chloride ion attack:** Aggressive Cl$^{-}$ ions migrate into the rupture site, adsorb on the bare metal, and **prevent repassivation** by displacing oxygen from the film-formation reaction.

6. **Metal dissolution at the slip step:** The exposed metal dissolves anodically: $\text{Fe} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^-$; the local chemistry becomes acidic (pH 1–3) and Cl$^{-}$ concentrated, further accelerating dissolution.

7. **Repassivation and crack advance:** The slip-step flanks re-passivate after a short time, leaving a **micro-notch** at the dissolution site. The process repeats, the crack advances incrementally, and propagates **transgranularly** along the active slip plane.

> **Schematic representation of the slip-step dissolution cycle:**

> ```
>  [Step 1] Passivated surface
>      ↓ dislocation glide
>  [Step 2] Slip-step emergence & film rupture
>      ↓ Cl- adsorption
>  [Step 3] Localized anodic dissolution
>      ↓
>  [Step 4] Repassivation of flanks
>      ↓
>  [Step 5] New slip-step emergence ahead of notch
>      ↓ (cycle repeats)
> ```

**Key role summary:** The mechanism explains why SCC in austenitic SS is **transgranular** (it follows active slip planes) and why it is **stress-dependent** (higher stress → more frequent slip-step emergence → faster crack advance).

**Part (b) — Threshold-Stress Derivation & Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Starting from the linear-elastic stress intensity factor for a surface crack:

$$K_I = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$$

For a shallow semi-elliptical surface crack, the dimensionless geometry factor is:

$$Y \approx 1.12$$

Substituting:

$$K_I = 1.12 \, \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$$

The threshold condition for SCC arrest is $K_I = K_{ISCC}$, beyond which the crack will propagate. Setting:

$$1.12 \, \sigma_{th} \sqrt{\pi a_{th}} = K_{ISCC}$$

Solving for the threshold stress:

$$\sigma_{th} = \frac{K_{ISCC}}{1.12 \, \sqrt{\pi \, a_{th}}}$$

**Numerical Evaluation (3 marks):**

*Given:*
- $K_{ISCC} = 18 \text{ MPa}\cdot\text{m}^{1/2}$
- $a_{th} = 1.0 \text{ mm} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$

*Substituting:*

$$\sigma_{th} = \frac{18}{1.12 \times \sqrt{\pi \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3}}}$$

*Square-root term:*

$$\sqrt{\pi \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = \sqrt{3.1416 \times 10^{-3}} = 5.604 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^{1/2}$$

*Denominator:*

$$1.12 \times 5.604 \times 10^{-2} = 6.277 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^{1/2}$$

*Final value:*

$$\sigma_{th} = \frac{18}{6.277 \times 10^{-2}} = 286.8 \text{ MPa}$$

**Answer:** The maximum safe operating stress is **286.8 MPa**, below which SCC propagation from the detected 1.0 mm flaw will not occur.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the phenomenon of **"Season Cracking" of brass**. Discuss the specific environment, stress source, and remedial measures adopted in industry. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is **Hydrogen-Induced Stress Corrosion Cracking (HISC)**? Compare its mechanism with the slip-step dissolution model. How does it differ from classical hydrogen embrittlement? **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a) — Season Cracking of Brass (7 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Season cracking is the **transgranular stress corrosion cracking of $\alpha$-brass** (Cu-Zn alloys, typically 70/30 or 65/35) observed historically in **ammonia-bearing atmospheres** (e.g., storage in stables, fertilizer warehouses). The phenomenon was so named because it occurred predominantly during the **monsoon/humid seasons** in tropical climates (India, Southeast Asia) when atmospheric ammonia concentration peaked.

**Environment (1.5 marks):**
- Trace atmospheric NH$_3$ + moisture + O$_2$.
- The complex ion $[\text{Cu}(\text{NH}_3)_4]^{2+}$ (cuprammonium complex) is formed, which dramatically lowers the Cu$^{2+}$ activity and accelerates anodic dissolution.
- The crack follows active slip planes → transgranular fracture.

**Stress Source (1.5 marks):**
- **Residual tensile stress** from cold-drawing, stamping, or straightening of the brass tube/cartridge case.
- No external load required — internal locked-in stress is sufficient.

**Remedial Measures (3 marks):**
1. **Stress relief annealing** at 275–350 °C for 1 hour after forming.
2. **Atmospheric control** — avoid storing brass in NH$_3$-rich environments (agricultural buildings).
3. **Use of inhibited (low-zinc) brass** (e.g., 85/15 Cu-Zn, "red brass" or "admiralty brass") with < 15% Zn to reduce susceptibility.
4. **Application of protective coatings** (e.g., epoxy, polyurethane, or tin plating).
5. **Vapor-phase inhibitors (VPIs)** in packaging.

---

**Part (b) — HISC vs. Slip-Step Dissolution (7 Marks):**

**HISC Definition (2 marks):**
Hydrogen-Induced Stress Corrosion Cracking (HISC) is a sub-class of SCC in which **atomic hydrogen**, generated by cathodic reactions (H$_2$ evolution, H$_2$S dissociation, or cathodic protection over-protection), **diffuses into the metal lattice** and causes a reduction in fracture toughness, leading to cracking at stresses below the yield point.

**Mechanism of HISC (2 marks):**
1. Cathodic reaction: $2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow 2\text{H}_{ads} \rightarrow \text{H}_{abs}$
2. Hydrogen atoms enter the metal at the crack tip and diffuse via lattice, grain-boundary, or dislocation-pipe diffusion.
3. Hydrogen accumulates at inclusions, voids, or grain boundaries, where it recombines to H$_2$ gas, generating high internal pressure → **hydrogen-induced blistering**.
4. Alternatively, hydrogen reduces cohesive strength of atomic bonds (HEDE — Hydrogen-Enhanced Decohesion) or enhances local plasticity (HELP — Hydrogen-Enhanced Localized Plasticity), leading to **quasi-cleavage or intergranular fracture**.

**Comparison Table with Slip-Step Dissolution (3 marks):**

| Feature | Slip-Step Dissolution (SCC) | HISC |
|---|---|---|
| **Species at crack tip** | Anions (Cl$^{-}$, OH$^{-}$) | Cathodic H-atoms |
| **Driving force** | Anodic metal dissolution | H-atom diffusion into lattice |
| **Cracking path** | Transgranular (slip-plane-following) | Intergranular or quasi-cleavage |
| **Polarization effect** | Cracking promoted at OCP / anodic | Cracking promoted at cathodic over-potential |
| **Prevention** | Lower Cl$^{-}$, upgrade alloy, lower T | Limit cathodic protection, use H$_2$S-resistant alloys, bake-out |
| **Typical alloy** | 304/316 SS | High-strength steels, Ti alloys |

**Distinction from Classical Hydrogen Embrittlement (HE):**
Classical HE is a **generic term** for any hydrogen-related degradation, including H$_2$-gas-induced blistering in sour service, hydride formation in Ti-alloys, and internal-H flakes in welds. **HISC** is a **specific sub-class** of HE that requires the **co-presence of sustained tensile stress** to drive crack propagation — without the stress, hydrogen alone may only cause blistering or loss of ductility, not cracking.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 SCC Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting all three SCC ingredients.** Many students mention only stress and environment, forgetting that **alloy susceptibility** is a mandatory pre-condition. Always list the "SCC Triangle."
> 2. **Conflating HISC and HE.** Hydrogen Embrittlement is a broad class; HISC is a **stress-driven sub-class**. Examiners award 1 mark specifically for this distinction.
> 3. **Sign errors in Paris-law / $K_I$ equations.** The square-root term $\sqrt{\pi a}$ must be computed in **SI units (m)**, not mm. A unit-conversion mistake typically costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting to specify $Y$ factor.** Students often write $K_I = \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ without the geometry factor $Y \approx 1.12$ for surface cracks. The examiner expects this constant.
> 5. **Mixing up transgranular vs. intergranular.** Caustic embrittlement of steel = **intergranular**; season cracking of brass = **transgranular**; HISC of high-strength steel = **intergranular/quasi-cleavage**. Reversing these is a recurring error.
> 6. **Not mentioning residual stress.** Many students only cite "applied stress" and ignore **residual stresses from welding, cold-working, or heat-treatment** — which are the most common real-world cause of SCC.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Stress Corrosion Cracking (Module 2):**

- ✅ SCC requires the **simultaneous presence** of: susceptible alloy + specific environment + sustained tensile stress.
- ✅ The crack propagation follows **three stages**: initiation, steady-state, fast fracture.
- ✅ **$K_{ISCC}$** is the critical threshold stress intensity; design must keep $K_I < K_{ISCC}$.
- ✅ **Slip-step dissolution** → transgranular → austenitic SS in hot Cl$^{-}$.
- ✅ **Hydrogen-induced SCC** → intergranular/quasi-cleavage → high-strength steel in H$_2$S or under cathodic over-protection.
- ✅ **Caustic embrittlement** → carbon steel boilers in concentrated NaOH.
- ✅ **Season cracking** → $\alpha$-brass in ammoniacal atmosphere; prevented by stress-relief annealing.
- ✅ **Threshold stress formula:** $\sigma_{th} = K_{ISCC} / (1.12 \sqrt{\pi a})$ for a surface crack.
- ✅ **Paris-Erdogan law:** $da/dN = C (\Delta K)^m$ for fatigue; for SCC the **da/dt vs. $K_I$** curve has a **plateau region (Stage II)**.
- ✅ **Prevention strategies:** (i) material upgrade, (ii) environment control (lower T, Cl$^{-}$), (iii) stress relief, (iv) inhibitors, (v) cathodic/anodic protection optimization.
- ✅ **SCC testing methods:** (a) Constant-load, (b) Constant-strain (U-bend), (c) Slow-Strain-Rate Testing (SSRT), (d) Rising-Step-Load (KISCC determination).
- ✅ **Exam keywords to use:** "synergistic action," "synergy of stress and corrosion," "threshold stress intensity," "passive film rupture," "anodic dissolution," "hydrogen embrittlement," "slip-step dissolution," "transgranular," "intergranular."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 📘 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydrogen Damage** is a generic term used to describe the *deterioration of the mechanical properties of a metal* caused by the **interaction of hydrogen (atomic or molecular) with the metal lattice**, its inclusions, or its grain boundaries. It encompasses a family of failure modes — including **hydrogen embrittlement, hydrogen blistering, hydrogen-induced cracking (HIC), sulfide stress cracking (SSC), hydrogen attack, and decarburization** — each governed by distinct electrochemical, mechanical, and metallurgical mechanisms.

In the KTU framework, hydrogen damage is classified by:
- **Origin of hydrogen**: Internal (from corrosion, electroplating, welding) vs. External (from H₂S environments, cathodic protection, acid pickling).
- **Operating temperature regime**: *Low-temperature electrochemical* (≤ 200 °C) vs. *High-temperature chemical* (> 200 °C).
- **Mechanism**: Diffusive (atomic H in lattice) vs. Reactive (H reacts to form CH₄, H₂O, or metal hydrides).

---

## 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — "The Silent Termite"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a wooden house. Termites don't chew the outer paint — they *enter invisibly* through the foundation, eat the cellulose fibers from within, and the house appears perfectly fine on the outside until one day a beam collapses without warning.
>
> **Hydrogen damage behaves exactly the same way.** Atomic hydrogen (H), being the **smallest atom in the periodic table (atomic radius ≈ 53 pm, ionic radius ≈ 10⁻⁵ nm for H⁺)**, can diffuse through the steel lattice almost like a ghost. It accumulates at internal voids, inclusions, or grain boundaries, recombines to form H₂ molecules, builds enormous internal pressures, or weakens the atomic bonds — and the structure fails *catastrophically* with little or no warning, often **without significant visible corrosion or mass loss**.

---

## 🔑 Why Hydrogen Damage is Critical in Engineering

| Property | Value / Implication |
|---|---|
| Atomic radius of H | **0.53 Å (smallest practical diffusing species)** |
| Diffusivity in α-Fe (RT) | **~10⁻⁸ m²/s** |
| Internal H₂ pressure (void) | **Up to several hundred MPa** (from a few ppm H) |
| Failure mode | **Sudden, brittle, often at stress < yield strength** |
| Visual warning | **Virtually none** — the most dangerous corrosion form |

> [!WARNING]
> Hydrogen damage accounts for a disproportionately high fraction of *catastrophic service failures* in oil & gas pipelines, pressure vessels, sour-service equipment, and high-strength fasteners — making it a **high-weightage topic** in KTU board examinations.

---

## 🌐 Classification of Hydrogen Damage (Overview)

```
HYDROGEN DAMAGE
├── 1. Hydrogen Embrittlement (HE)
│       ├── Internal Hydrogen Embrittlement (IHE)
│       └── Environmental Hydrogen Embrittlement (EHE)
├── 2. Hydrogen-Induced Cracking (HIC)
├── 3. Stress-Oriented HIC (SOHIC)
├── 4. Sulfide Stress Cracking (SSC)
├── 5. Hydrogen Blistering
├── 6. Hydrogen Attack (high-T chemical)
├── 7. Decarburization
└── 8. Hydride Formation (Ti, Zr, V, Nb, Ta alloys)
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🧪 Mechanism 1 — Hydrogen Embrittlement (HE)

**Definition (KTU standard):** Loss of ductility and fracture toughness of a metal due to the presence of dissolved atomic hydrogen in the lattice.

### Step-by-Step Logic:

1. **Generation (Volmer Reaction):** Atomic H is produced at the metal surface by reduction of H⁺ or H₂O:
   
   $$\text{H}^{+} + e^{-} \longrightarrow \text{H}_{\text{ads}}$$
   
   $$\text{H}_{2}\text{O} + e^{-} \longrightarrow \text{H}_{\text{ads}} + \text{OH}^{-}$$

2. **Entry (Absorption):** H_ad recombines to H₂ *OR* enters the metal as H_abs:
   
   $$\text{H}_{\text{ads}} \longrightarrow \text{H}_{\text{abs}} \quad (\text{permeation into the lattice})$$

3. **Trapping:** H becomes trapped at *reversible* traps (dislocations, grain boundaries) and *irreversible* traps (MnS inclusions, TiC particles, voids).

4. **Damage mechanisms** (3 competing theories, all KTU-exam relevant):

   | Theory | Core Idea | Best Applies To |
   |---|---|---|
   | **Hydrogen-Enhanced Decohesion (HEDE)** | H reduces cohesive strength of atomic bonds | High-strength steels |
   | **Hydrogen-Enhanced Localized Plasticity (HELP)** | H facilitates dislocation motion → shear localization | Ductile FCC metals (Ni, austenitic SS) |
   | **Hydrogen-Induced Phase Transformation** | H forms brittle hydrides | Ti, Zr, V, Nb alloys |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **HEDE + HELP** combined model is currently the *most accepted* in modern corrosion literature and is the one to cite in KTU answers.

---

## 🧪 Mechanism 2 — Hydrogen Blistering

**Definition:** Subsurface planar cracking caused by H atoms diffusing to internal voids/inclusions, recombining to H₂, and generating huge internal pressures.

### Logic Chain:

1. H diffuses inward (concentration gradient).
2. Trapped at MnS stringers or laminations.
3. H → H₂ (recombination): $2\text{H} \to \text{H}_2$.
4. Molecule is **too large to diffuse out** → pressure rises.
5. Local plastic deformation → void grows → **blister forms**.

### Governing Equation (Ideal Gas Internal Pressure):

$$
P_{\text{H}_2} = \frac{n_{\text{H}_2} R T}{V_{\text{void}}}
$$

where:
- $n_{\text{H}_2}$ = moles of H₂ in the void (from recombined H)
- $R$ = **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** (universal gas constant)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $V_{\text{void}}$ = void volume (m³)

For very high pressures, the **Fugacity-corrected real-gas** form is used:

$$
P_{\text{real}} = \frac{Z \cdot n_{\text{H}_2} R T}{V_{\text{void}}}
$$

where $Z$ = compressibility factor (deviates strongly from 1 at >100 MPa).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** At STP, recombination of just **1 ppm H in steel** (≈ 0.1 cm³ H₂ per 100 g Fe at STP) in a 10-µm inclusion can generate pressures exceeding **100 MPa**, easily exceeding the yield strength of mild steel (~250 MPa).

---

## 🧪 Mechanism 3 — Hydrogen-Induced Cracking (HIC) & SOHIC

- **HIC:** Stepwise internal cracking parallel to the rolling plane, driven by H₂ pressure at MnS inclusions. *No external stress required.*
- **SOHIC:** Stacked array of HIC cracks linked by shear, propagates *perpendicular* to the surface. Requires a small applied stress.

---

## 🧪 Mechanism 4 — Sulfide Stress Cracking (SSC)

**KTU Critical Definition:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SSC** is a form of hydrogen embrittlement occurring in *sour* (H₂S-containing) environments. H₂S acts as a **hydrogen recombination poison** (it retards the H + H → H₂ step), thereby increasing the flux of H entering the steel. The cracking is **transgranular, brittle, and occurs at stresses well below yield**.

The classic NACE susceptibility zones:
- **SSC Zone:** pH < 4, H₂S partial pressure > 0.0003 MPa — most severe.
- **HIC Zone:** pH 4–7, H₂S pp > 0.0002 MPa.

---

## 🧪 Mechanism 5 — Hydrogen Attack (High-Temperature)

At **T > 200 °C** in H₂/H₂S environments, dissolved H reacts with **carbides** in steel to form **methane**:

$$
\text{Fe}_{3}\text{C} + 2\text{H}_{2} \longrightarrow 3\text{Fe} + \text{CH}_{4}
$$

- CH₄ is trapped at grain boundaries → builds pressure → **intergranular decohesion**.
- **Irreversible** (unlike low-T HE).
- Mitigation: use **Cr-Mo alloys** (e.g., 1¼ Cr – ½ Mo, 2¼ Cr – 1 Mo) that form stable Cr₂₃C₆ that resist the reaction.

---

## 🧪 Mechanism 6 — Decarburization

At high T in H₂:

$$
\text{Fe}_{3}\text{C} + 2\text{H}_{2} \longrightarrow 3\text{Fe} + \text{CH}_{4}
$$

→ Surface carbon is removed as CH₄ → **soft ferrite layer** forms → loss of hardness and fatigue strength.

---

## 📋 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $J = -D \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ | Fick's First Law — steady-state H flux | mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹ |
| 2 | $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \dfrac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}}$ | Fick's Second Law — transient diffusion | — |
| 3 | $C_{H} = k \sqrt{P_{H_2}}$ | **Sievert's Law** — solubility of H in metals | ppm or mol·m⁻³ |
| 4 | $P = \dfrac{nRT}{V}$ | Internal H₂ pressure in a void | Pa |
| 5 | $D = D_{0} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_{a}}{RT}\right)$ | Arrhenius diffusivity | m²·s⁻¹ |
| 6 | $I_{\text{crit}} = \dfrac{F \cdot D \cdot C_{0}}{L}$ | Steady-state permeation current (Bard–Devanathan) | A·m⁻² |
| 7 | $C(x,t) = C_{s}\left[1 - \text{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)\right]$ | Concentration profile (semi-infinite medium) | mol·m⁻³ |
| 8 | $P_{H_2}^{\text{crit}} = \dfrac{2\gamma}{r}$ | Critical pressure for void growth (Griffith-like) | Pa |

# ⚙️ SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 📐 Derivation 1 — Sievert's Law (Hydrogen Solubility in Metals)

Sievert's Law is **the** relationship between hydrogen gas partial pressure and its solubility in a metal. It is a *thermodynamic* consequence of the dissociation equilibrium.

### Step 1: Write the equilibrium reaction for H₂ dissolution in a metal M:

$$
\tfrac{1}{2}\text{H}_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_{\text{(dissolved in M)}}
$$

### Step 2: Write the equilibrium constant $K_{p}$ (in terms of activity and fugacity):

$$
K_{p} = \frac{a_{\text{H}}}{\left(f_{H_{2}}\right)^{1/2}}
$$

For an ideal-dilute solution, $a_{\text{H}} = \gamma \cdot C_{H}$. For very low concentrations, $\gamma \to 1$, so $a_{\text{H}} \approx C_{H}$.

For low-to-moderate pressures, $f_{H_2} \approx P_{H_2}$.

### Step 3: Rearrange:

$$
C_{H} = K_{p} \cdot \left(P_{H_{2}}\right)^{1/2} = k \sqrt{P_{H_{2}}}
$$

**This is Sievert's Law.** It tells us that *doubling* the H₂ pressure **quadruples** the dissolved hydrogen concentration in the metal.

> **Physical Interpretation:** The square-root dependence arises because the dissolved H exists as **atoms**, while the gas phase is **diatomic H₂**; equilibrium must conserve atomic species.

---

## 📐 Derivation 2 — Steady-State Hydrogen Permeation Flux (Devanathan–Stachurski Cell)

Consider a metal membrane of thickness $L$. On the *inlet* side, H is generated at concentration $C_0$. On the *outlet* side, concentration is held at 0 (oxidation by Pd catalyst).

### Step 1: Write Fick's First Law:

$$
J = -D \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}
$$

### Step 2: Apply steady-state condition ($\partial C/\partial t = 0$):

$$
\frac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} = \text{const} = A
$$

### Step 3: Integrate once:

$$
C(x) = A x + B
$$

### Step 4: Apply boundary conditions:

- At $x = 0$: $C(0) = C_0$ → $B = C_0$
- At $x = L$: $C(L) = 0$ → $0 = AL + C_0$ → $A = -C_0/L$

### Step 5: Concentration profile:

$$
C(x) = C_0 \left(1 - \frac{x}{L}\right)
$$

### Step 6: Compute the flux:

$$
J = -D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} = -D \cdot \left(-\frac{C_0}{L}\right) = \frac{D \cdot C_0}{L}
$$

### Step 7: Convert to permeation current density using Faraday's Law (each H carries +1 charge equivalent at oxidation):

$$
I_{\text{perm}} = n F J = \frac{F \cdot D \cdot C_0}{L}
$$

where $n = 1$ for H → H⁺ + e⁻. The result:

$$
\boxed{\;I_{\text{perm}} = \frac{F \cdot D \cdot C_{0}}{L}\;}
$$

This is the **measured signal** in the Devanathan–Stachurski electrochemical permeation experiment.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** This derivation is a *favourite 14-mark question*. The membrane of thickness $L$ with constant surface concentration $C_0$ on one side and $C = 0$ on the other is the standard academic setup. **Do not forget** to convert from mol·m⁻³ to mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹ in the flux.

---

## 📐 Derivation 3 — Internal Pressure Build-up in a Spherical Void (Blistering)

Consider a spherical void of radius $r$ inside a steel matrix. Hydrogen atoms diffuse to the void surface at rate $\dot{n}_H$ and recombine to H₂.

### Step 1: Molar accumulation rate in the void:

$$
\frac{dn_{H_2}}{dt} = \frac{\dot{n}_{H}}{2}
$$

### Step 2: For isothermal conditions, applying the ideal gas law instantaneously:

$$
P(t) \cdot V = n_{H_2}(t) R T
$$

Differentiate with respect to time:

$$
V \frac{dP}{dt} = \frac{dn_{H_2}}{dt} R T = \frac{\dot{n}_{H}}{2} R T
$$

### Step 3: Solve for the pressure build-up rate:

$$
\frac{dP}{dt} = \frac{\dot{n}_{H} R T}{2 V}
$$

### Step 4: Substitute the void volume for a sphere $V = \tfrac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}$:

$$
\frac{dP}{dt} = \frac{\dot{n}_{H} R T}{2 \cdot \tfrac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}} = \frac{3 \dot{n}_{H} R T}{8 \pi r^{3}}
$$

### Step 5: Integration gives:

$$
P(t) = P_0 + \frac{3 \dot{n}_{H} R T}{8 \pi r^{3}}\, t
$$

**Conclusion:** The pressure grows **inversely with $r^{3}$** — small inclusions (e.g., elongated MnS stringers with effective $r \approx 1\ \mu\text{m}$) are the *most dangerous*, leading to blister formation within hours to days in sour service.

---

## 📐 Derivation 4 — Threshold Stress Intensity for Hydrogen Cracking ($K_{\text{ISCC}}$)

The critical condition for crack propagation by HEDE:

$$
K_{\text{app}} \geq K_{\text{ISCC}}
$$

Using linear elastic fracture mechanics:

$$
K_{\text{ISCC}} = Y \sigma_{\text{app}} \sqrt{\pi a_{\text{crit}}}
$$

Solve for the *critical flaw size* $a_{\text{crit}}$:

$$
a_{\text{crit}} = \frac{1}{\pi} \left(\frac{K_{\text{ISCC}}}{Y \sigma_{\text{app}}}\right)^{2}
$$

**Example:** For a high-strength steel with $K_{\text{ISCC}} = 30\ \text{MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}$, applied stress $\sigma = 600\ \text{MPa}$, $Y = 1$:

$$
a_{\text{crit}} = \frac{1}{\pi}\left(\frac{30}{1 \cdot 600}\right)^{2} = \frac{1}{\pi}(0.05)^{2} = 7.96 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}
$$

So a surface flaw of just **~0.8 mm** is sufficient to propagate by HE — this explains why HE failures occur *without warning*.

---

## 🧮 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A 2-mm thick low-carbon steel membrane is exposed to H₂S-sour brine on the entry side. The surface H concentration is $C_0 = 5\ \text{ppm}$ (mass) and the diffusivity of H in BCC iron at 25 °C is $D = 4 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$. Compute the steady-state permeation flux.

**Solution:**

**Step A: Convert $C_0$ from mass ppm to mol/m³.**
- 5 ppm (mass) in steel ≈ 5 mg H per kg steel.
- Moles of H per m³: $\dfrac{5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{g}}{1.008\ \text{g/mol}} \times 10^{3}\ \text{kg/m}^{3} \times 10^{-6}$? 
- For steel density 7850 kg/m³: $C_0 = 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 7850 / 1.008 \approx 0.0389\ \text{mol/m}^{3}$.

**Step B: Apply Fick's First Law at steady state:**

$$
J = \frac{D \cdot C_0}{L} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.0389}{2 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
J = \frac{1.556 \times 10^{-10}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 7.78 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{mol m}^{-2}\text{s}^{-1}
$$

**Step C: Convert to permeation current density:**

$$
I_{\text{perm}} = F \cdot J = 96485 \times 7.78 \times 10^{-8} = 7.51 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{A/m}^{2} = 7.51\ \text{mA/m}^{2}
$$

**Answer:** $J = 7.78 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{mol m}^{-2}\text{s}^{-1}$ and $I_{\text{perm}} \approx 7.5\ \text{mA/m}^{2}$.

# ⚙️ SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] 
**Define hydrogen embrittlement. Mention any two mechanisms proposed to explain it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Hydrogen embrittlement (HE) is the loss of ductility, toughness, and strength of a metal caused by the ingress and interaction of atomic hydrogen with the metal lattice, defects, or grain boundaries.
> 
> **Mechanisms (any two):**
> 1. **HEDE (Hydrogen-Enhanced Decohesion):** Dissolved H lowers the cohesive energy of Fe–Fe atomic bonds, promoting brittle cleavage separation.
> 2. **HELP (Hydrogen-Enhanced Localized Plasticity):** H atmospheres around dislocations enhance their mobility, leading to localized shear and failure.
> 3. **Hydride Formation:** In Ti, Zr, V, Nb alloys, H precipitates as brittle hydrides that cleave easily.
> 
> *(1 mark definition + 1 mark each mechanism = 3 marks)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between HIC and SSC in sour environments.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | HIC (Hydrogen-Induced Cracking) | SSC (Sulfide Stress Cracking) |
|---|---|---|
| External stress required | **No** (internal H₂ pressure) | **Yes** (applied or residual tensile) |
| Crack orientation | Parallel to rolling plane (stepwise) | Perpendicular to stress (transgranular) |
| Location | Subsurface, at MnS inclusions | Surface-initiated |
| Typical hardness range | < 22 HRC (low/medium strength) | > 22 HRC (high strength) |
| Environment severity | pH 4–7, moderate H₂S | pH < 4, high H₂S pp |

*(1.5 marks for either side comparison, 1.5 marks for the other = 3 marks)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Format Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each part of a question carries **7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**(a)** Describe with neat sketches the various forms of hydrogen damage in metals. Discuss the metallurgical and environmental factors that influence hydrogen embrittlement susceptibility. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive Sievert's Law for hydrogen solubility in metals and explain its significance in selecting materials for hydrogen service. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part A(a)

**Forms of Hydrogen Damage (4 marks):**

1. **Hydrogen Embrittlement (HE):** Reversible loss of ductility; often seen in high-strength steels and Ti alloys.
2. **Hydrogen Blistering:** Subsurface H₂ pressure at inclusions → flat, dome-shaped blisters on the surface.
3. **HIC / SOHIC:** Stepwise internal cracks at MnS stringers; SOHIC is the stacked shear-linked variant.
4. **SSC:** Brittle, transgranular cracking in H₂S environments; threshold stress is well below yield.
5. **Hydrogen Attack (High-T):** Reaction $\text{Fe}_3\text{C} + 2\text{H}_2 \to 3\text{Fe} + \text{CH}_4$ → intergranular decohesion.
6. **Decarburization:** Loss of surface carbon as CH₄ → soft, low-fatigue ferrite layer.
7. **Hydride Embrittlement:** Brittle metal hydride precipitation (Ti, Zr, V).

**Factors Influencing HE Susceptibility (3 marks):**

- **Metallurgical:** Strength/hardness (higher → worse), microstructure (martensite > bainite > ferrite), inclusion content, grain size, traps (reversible vs. irreversible).
- **Environmental:** pH (lower → more H⁺ → more H entry), H₂S partial pressure, cathodic overpotential, temperature (worst near room T, see "ductility trough"), stress state (tensile, triaxial).
- **Service:** Welding (residual H from moisture), acid pickling, electroplating, cathodic protection overpotential.

> A simple ASCII sketch in the answer:
> 
> ```
>  Subsurface voids  ──▶  H recombination  ──▶  H₂ pressure
>        │                                          │
>        ▼                                          ▼
>   MnS inclusion                              Blister cap
> ```

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part A(b)

**Derivation (5 marks):**
The full derivation is shown in **Section 3, Derivation 1** of these notes. Briefly:

1. Reaction: $\tfrac{1}{2}\text{H}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_{\text{dissolved}}$
2. $K_p = \dfrac{a_H}{(P_{H_2})^{1/2}}$
3. With $a_H \approx C_H$ and rearranging: $\boxed{C_H = k \sqrt{P_{H_2}}}$

**Significance in material selection (2 marks):**
- Determines the *threshold H₂ partial pressure* above which a metal cannot be used safely.
- Dictates use of **austenitic stainless steels, Cu alloys, or Ni-based alloys** (low H solubility, high resistance).
- For carbon steels in H₂ service, *Nelson curves* (API 941) are drawn from Sievert's Law–type data to set maximum T and H₂ pp.
- Justifies bake-out procedures (190 °C for 4–24 h) for plated high-strength parts to drive out trapped H.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** What is hydrogen blistering? Derive an expression for the rate of pressure build-up inside a spherical subsurface void of radius $r$ due to recombination of hydrogen atoms. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the Devanathan–Stachurski electrochemical permeation technique for measuring hydrogen diffusivity. List any two preventive measures against hydrogen embrittlement in high-strength steel components. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part B(a)

**Hydrogen Blistering (2 marks):** Subsurface, dome-shaped cracking caused by accumulation and recombination of H at internal voids/inclusions, generating high internal H₂ pressure that exceeds local yield and deforms the metal cap.

**Derivation (5 marks):** See **Section 3, Derivation 3**.

Final result:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{dP}{dt} = \frac{3\,\dot{n}_{H} R T}{8\pi r^{3}}\;}
$$

with the integrated form $P(t) = P_0 + \dfrac{3 \dot{n}_{H} R T}{8\pi r^{3}}\,t$.

**Conclusion (in answer):** The inverse-cube dependence on $r$ means that **small inclusions and MnS stringers are the most potent blister-initiation sites** — a key metallurgical control point.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part B(b)

**Devanathan–Stachurski Technique (5 marks):**

- Two-compartment cell separated by the steel membrane.
- **Charging side (inlet):** H is generated by cathodic polarization in acidified or H₂S-saturated solution.
- **Detection side (outlet):** Pd-coated; held at a constant anodic potential so any H reaching this surface is immediately oxidized: $\text{H} \to \text{H}^+ + e^-$.
- The measured **oxidation current vs. time** is the permeation transient.
- From the **breakthrough time** $t_b$ and the **steady-state current** $I_{ss}$, the diffusivity $D$ and the sub-surface concentration $C_0$ are calculated.

Standard analysis:
- Break-through time: $t_b = \dfrac{L^2}{15.3\,D}$ → $D = \dfrac{L^2}{15.3\,t_b}$.
- Steady-state flux: $J_{ss} = \dfrac{D C_0}{L}$ → $C_0 = \dfrac{I_{ss} L}{F D}$.

**Preventive measures (2 marks, any two):**

1. **Bake-out treatment** (e.g., 190 °C × 24 h) of plated/sour-service parts to drive out diffusible H.
2. **Use low-H practices** — vacuum or inert-atmosphere melting, dry welding electrodes, low-hydrogen welding consumables (e.g., E7018).
3. **Lower hardness** — specify max HRC 22 for sour service (NACE MR0175).
4. **Barrier coatings** — Zn, Cd, or polymer coatings to block H entry.
5. **Avoid acid pickling** — use mechanical descaling or alkaline cleaning instead.
6. **Alloy selection** — use austenitic SS, Cu, Ni, or low-C steels with low MnS content.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students lose marks in Hydrogen Damage questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing HE with HIC** — HE requires *stress* and produces *transgranular cracks*; HIC does *not* require applied stress and produces *stepwise internal cracks*. Examiners mark this strictly.
> 2. **Forgetting the half-power in Sievert's Law** — students often write $C_H = k P_{H_2}$. It is **square root**, not linear.
> 3. **Using wrong sign in Fick's Law** — Fick's First Law carries a **negative sign** because diffusion occurs *down* the concentration gradient. Forgetting this loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Mixing up bubble formula** — using $P = nRT/V$ in a *non-isothermal* scenario without correction. State the **ideal gas assumption explicitly**.
> 5. **Not stating boundary conditions** in derivation questions — this is *mandatory*. Write BC1, BC2 explicitly.
> 6. **Sketches without labels** — every diagram in a 7-mark answer *must* label (i) the metal, (ii) the inclusion/void, (iii) the H₂ pressure direction. Unlabeled sketches earn ≤ 0.5 mark.
> 7. **Ignoring NACE / API references** — citing *Nelson curve* or *NACE MR0175* shows examiner-level depth and earns 1–2 grace marks.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Hydrogen damage** = family of failures caused by H interaction with the metal.
- [x] **Smallest atom (0.53 Å radius)** → can permeate almost any metal lattice.
- [x] **HE is reversible**; **Hydrogen Attack is irreversible** (key KTU contrast).
- [x] **HEDE** + **HELP** = most accepted combined mechanism for HE.
- [x] **Sievert's Law:** $C_H = k\sqrt{P_{H_2}}$ — *square root* dependence.
- [x] **Fick's First Law:** $J = -D\,dC/dx$ — note the **negative sign**.
- [x] **Permeation current:** $I_{perm} = FD C_0 / L$ — Devanathan–Stachurski.
- [x] **Blister pressure growth rate:** $dP/dt \propto 1/r^{3}$ — small inclusions are dangerous.
- [x] **SSC** = H₂S-poisoned HE → transgranular, high-strength, sour.
- [x] **HIC** = stepwise internal cracking, no applied stress, at MnS inclusions.
- [x] **Hydrogen Attack:** $\text{Fe}_3\text{C} + 2\text{H}_2 \to 3\text{Fe} + \text{CH}_4$ above 200 °C.
- [x] **Decarburization** = surface softening due to C loss as CH₄.
- [x] **Hydride formers:** Ti, Zr, V, Nb, Ta — pick alternative alloys.
- [x] **Mitigation toolbox:** low-H consumables, bake-out (190 °C × 24 h), hardness < 22 HRC (sour), Nelson curve compliance, barrier coatings, austenitic SS.
- [x] **References to cite in answers:** NACE MR0175 / ISO 15156, API 941 (Nelson curves), ASTM F1459 (permeation test), ASTM G129 (HE test).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Strategy:** For 14-mark questions, always structure the answer as **Definition → Mechanism → Derivation → Sketch → Mitigation → Reference**. Examiners explicitly look for this *flow*, and partial-credit is awarded generously if each step is logically present even if the math is slightly off.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Corrosion testing and monitoring: Non-electrochemical and electrochemical methods: potentiostat
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT527 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Corrosion testing and monitoring: Non-electrochemical and electrochemical methods: potentiostat

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Corrosion Testing and Monitoring — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Corrosion Testing** is the systematic application of physical, chemical, electrochemical, and instrumental techniques to evaluate the susceptibility of a metal/alloy to degradation in a specific environment. **Corrosion Monitoring** refers to the continuous or periodic measurement of corrosion-related parameters to track the integrity of equipment, predict failures, and plan maintenance.

Testing is generally **short-term and laboratory-based** (predictive), whereas monitoring is **long-term and field-based** (diagnostic). Together, they form the backbone of **Integrity Management Programs** in chemical, petroleum, marine, and power-plant industries.

## 1.2 Classification of Corrosion Testing and Monitoring Methods

The two overarching families are:

| Family | Underlying Physics | Typical Outputs | Speed of Response |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Non-Electrochemical Methods** | Mechanical, optical, acoustic, radiographic, gravimetric | Mass loss, depth of attack, pit density, crack length | Hours to Months |
| **Electrochemical Methods** | Redox kinetics, charge transfer, mass transport | Corrosion current $i_{corr}$, polarization resistance $R_p$, impedance spectra | Seconds to Minutes |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (Module 3):** This topic directly maps to **Course Outcome CO3** — *"Apply corrosion testing and monitoring techniques to evaluate material degradation in various environments."* The cognitive level is **Apply (Bloom Level 3)**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you suspect a hidden water leak inside a wall.

- **Non-electrochemical methods** are like the **plumber who taps the wall, looks for stains, drills a small hole, and weighs the drywall after a week**. They directly measure *what happened* to the wall material.
- **Electrochemical methods** are like a **smart moisture sensor with electrodes glued to the wall**. They measure the *electrical signals (current/voltage)* that the leaking water is producing in real time, telling you the *rate* at which damage is occurring.

The **potentiostat** is essentially the *engineer controlling the smart sensor* — it forces a precise voltage on the structure and reads the resulting current response, which is mathematically linked to how fast the metal is dissolving.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used (Bolded for Emphasis)

- **Corrosion Rate (CR)** — typically expressed in **mm/year (mpy)** or **µm/year**.
- **Corrosion Current Density ($i_{corr}$)** — µA/cm² or mA/cm².
- **Polarization Resistance ($R_p$)** — ohm·cm² ($\Omega \cdot cm^2$).
- **Tafel Slope ($\beta_a$, $\beta_c$)** — V/decade or mV/decade.
- **Open Circuit Potential (OCP / $E_{corr}$)** — V vs. reference electrode.
- **Standard Reference Electrodes:** Saturated Calomel Electrode (**SCE**), Ag/AgCl, Cu/CuSO₄ (CSE), Standard Hydrogen Electrode (**SHE**).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Non-Electrochemical Methods (Detailed Inventory)

These techniques do **not** depend on measuring electrical current or potential of the corroding system.

### 2.1.1 Weight-Loss Coupon Method (ASTM G1, G4, G31)
- Coupons of known mass ($m_1$) and surface area ($A$) are exposed to the environment for a fixed time ($t$).
- Corrosion products are chemically stripped (per ASTM G1).
- Final mass ($m_2$) is recorded.
- **Principle:** Direct, unambiguous measurement of *average* metal loss — the gold standard for validating all other methods.

### 2.1.2 Visual & Optical Inspection
- Lowest cost, highest subjectivity.
- Uses **borescopes, CCTV cameras, magnifying lenses**.
- Detects pitting, crevice attack, scaling, color changes.

### 2.1.3 Dimensional/Metallographic Examination
- Cross-sectioning a corroded component and measuring the **remaining wall thickness** with a micrometer or microscope.
- Reveals **intergranular corrosion, dezincification, weld decay**.

### 2.1.4 Ultrasonic Testing (UT)
- High-frequency sound waves (1–25 MHz) detect **wall thinning, blisters, laminations, SCC cracks**.
- A transducer sends pulses; the time-of-flight and amplitude of reflected echoes reveal flaw depth.

### 2.1.5 Radiographic Testing (RT)
- X-rays or gamma rays ($\gamma$ from Ir-192, Co-60) penetrate the component.
- Denser corroded regions absorb more radiation, producing image contrast on film or digital detector.
- Excellent for **under-insulation corrosion (UIC)** and **pipework**.

### 2.1.6 Eddy Current Testing (ET)
- A coil carrying alternating current induces **eddy currents** in a nearby conductor.
- Corrosion-induced changes in conductivity or geometry disturb the eddy current pattern, altering coil impedance.
- Works only on **ferromagnetic and non-ferrous metals** (not plastics), and only on **surface/subsurface defects**.

### 2.1.7 Liquid Penetrant & Magnetic Particle Inspection
- **DPI (PT):** Detects **surface-breaking defects** by capillary action of a dye penetrant.
- **MPI (MT):** Detects **surface and near-surface defects** in ferromagnetic materials by magnetizing the part and observing flux leakage with iron particles.

### 2.1.8 Electrical Resistance (ER) Probes
- A thin metallic element is exposed to the environment.
- As it corrodes, its **cross-sectional area decreases** and its **electrical resistance increases**.
- Corrosion rate is calculated from $\Delta R$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "non-electrochemical"?** These methods measure *physical* (mass, dimension, acoustic impedance, radiographic density) or *passive electrical* (resistance) changes — they do **not** impose or measure electrode potentials or currents arising from faradaic reactions. ER probes are sometimes debated as quasi-electrochemical, but KTU classification places them in the non-electrochemical family.

## 2.2 Electrochemical Methods (Detailed Inventory)

These techniques exploit the **mixed-potential theory (Wagner-Traud)** and **Butler-Volmer kinetics** of the corrosion cell.

### 2.2.1 Open Circuit Potential (OCP) Measurement
- Measures $E_{corr}$ of the metal vs. a reference electrode under **zero external current**.
- Indicates the **thermodynamic tendency** to corrode.
- **Limitation:** No information on rate.

### 2.2.2 Tafel Polarization (Potentiodynamic)
- The working electrode is polarized $\pm 250$ mV around $E_{corr}$.
- The log of current density vs. overpotential is plotted.
- The **linear (Tafel) regions** are extrapolated to their intersection, giving $i_{corr}$ directly.

### 2.2.3 Linear Polarization Resistance (LPR) — Stern-Geary Method
- The electrode is polarized within $\pm 10$–$20$ mV of $E_{corr}$ (where linear approximation of Butler-Volmer holds).
- The slope $\Delta E / \Delta I$ yields the **polarization resistance $R_p$**.
- $R_p$ is inversely proportional to $i_{corr}$ via the **Stern-Geary equation**.

### 2.2.4 Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS)
- A small-amplitude AC signal (5–10 mV) is applied over a wide frequency range ($10^{-3}$ to $10^{5}$ Hz).
- The system response yields a **Nyquist plot** ($Z''$ vs $Z'$) or **Bode plot**.
- Corrosion rate, double-layer capacitance, solution resistance, and coating performance are extracted by fitting to an **equivalent circuit** (e.g., Randles circuit).

### 2.2.5 Electrochemical Noise Analysis (ENA)
- Measures **spontaneous fluctuations** in potential (EPN) and current (ECN) between two identical working electrodes.
- Statistical analysis (RMS, noise resistance $R_n$, PSD) reveals corrosion mechanism (pitting vs. uniform).

### 2.2.6 Cyclic Polarization & Pitting Susceptibility
- Forward and reverse scans to identify **pitting potential ($E_{pit}$)** and **protection potential ($E_{prot}$)**.

## 2.3 The Potentiostat — Heart of Electrochemical Corrosion Testing

### 2.3.1 Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **potentiostat** is an electronic instrument that maintains the potential of a **working electrode (WE)** at a fixed, controlled value relative to a **reference electrode (RE)** by automatically adjusting the current flowing between the working electrode and a **counter (auxiliary) electrode (CE)**. It is the central hardware of virtually all modern electrochemical corrosion measurements.

### 2.3.2 The Three-Electrode Cell

A potentiostat **cannot** operate with only two electrodes, because if it passes current through the reference electrode, the reference potential drifts due to **polarization of the RE itself** — destroying the very quantity it is trying to control. The three-electrode geometry solves this:

| Electrode | Symbol | Role | Current Carried? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Working Electrode** | WE | The corroding metal under study | Carries the faradaic current |
| **Reference Electrode** | RE | Fixed, stable potential probe (SCE, Ag/AgCl) | Carries *ideally zero* current (high-impedance buffer) |
| **Counter Electrode** | CE | Completes the circuit, balances current flow | Carries the current return |

### 2.3.3 Operational Principle (Operational Amplifier Model)
The classical potentiostat uses two op-amps:
- **Control amplifier:** Compares the programmed setpoint voltage ($E_{set}$) with the actual WE-vs-RE voltage, and amplifies the error.
- **Current follower:** Converts the WE current into a measurable voltage output.
- The output current is forced through the CE → solution → WE loop to maintain $E_{WE} = E_{set}$.

### 2.3.4 What is a Galvanostat? (Contrast)
A **galvanostat** holds the *current* constant and measures the resulting potential change. Combined potentiostat/galvanostat units (called **bi-potentiostats** in multi-WE setups) are standard in KTU-lab equipment.

### 2.3.5 Why Potentiostats Matter for Corrosion Engineering
- Enable **Tafel extrapolation** to get $i_{corr}$.
- Enable **LPR** for rapid, non-destructive corrosion rate monitoring.
- Enable **cyclic polarization** for pitting and passivity studies.
- Enable **EIS** to separate charge-transfer, diffusion, and coating resistance.
- Field-mounted **commercial corrosion rate monitors** (e.g., Rohrback Cosasco, Metal Samples) are miniaturized, ruggedized potentiostats running the LPR method.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS FOR KTU BOARD EXAM — memorize with units and boundary conditions.**

| # | Equation | Description | Units | Validity / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $CR = \dfrac{K \cdot \Delta m}{A \cdot t \cdot \rho}$ | Weight-loss corrosion rate | mm/yr or mpy | ASTM G1, G4, G31; uniform corrosion |
| 2 | $CR = \dfrac{3.27 \times 10^{-3} \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho}$ | Faraday-derived CR from current density | mm/yr | $i_{corr}$ in µA/cm², EW in g/eq, $\rho$ in g/cm³ |
| 3 | $R_p = \dfrac{\Delta E}{\Delta I}\bigg\vert_{E=E_{corr}}$ | Polarization resistance | $\Omega \cdot cm^2$ | $\vert \Delta E \vert \le 20$ mV (linear regime) |
| 4 | $i_{corr} = \dfrac{B}{R_p}$ | Stern-Geary relation | µA/cm² | Active corrosion region |
| 5 | $B = \dfrac{\beta_a \cdot \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)}$ | Stern-Geary constant | V (or mV) | For $\beta_a, \beta_c$ in V/decade |
| 6 | $\eta = a + b \log i$ | Tafel equation | V vs. SHE | $\vert \eta \vert > 50$ mV |
| 7 | $i = i_0 \left[\exp\left(\dfrac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\left(-\dfrac{\alpha_c F \eta}{RT}\right)\right]$ | Butler-Volmer equation | A/cm² | Full polarization range |
| 8 | $Z(\omega) = Z'(\omega) - j Z''(\omega)$ | EIS complex impedance | $\Omega$ | AC perturbation, $\omega = 2\pi f$ |
| 9 | $R_n = \dfrac{\sigma_E}{\sigma_I}$ | Noise resistance (ENA) | $\Omega$ | Statistical ENA, pitting detection |
| 10 | $\Delta m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Faraday's 1st law (mass loss) | grams | Purely faradaic process |

> **Constants to remember:** Faraday $F = 96485$ C/mol; Gas $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K); 1 year $\approx 3.15 \times 10^7$ s.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil & Gas:** ER probes and LPR monitors installed in multiphase flowlines track $\mathrm{CO_2}$ and $\mathrm{H_2S}$ corrosion in real time, enabling **chemical injection optimization** (corrosion inhibitor dosing).
- **Nuclear Power Plants:** EIS monitors oxide film integrity on steam generator tubes.
- **Marine:** Tafel polarization studies guide selection of bronze and Cu-Ni alloys for seawater piping.
- **Reinforced Concrete:** Potentiostat-based instruments (e.g., Gecor) measure rebar corrosion rate *in situ* without destructive testing.
- **Automotive & Aerospace:** Cyclic polarization screens for SCC susceptibility in aluminum alloys.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stern-Geary Equation (The Foundation of LPR)

This is the **single most important derivation** for KTU Board Exam on this topic. The Stern-Geary equation is what links the experimentally measured polarization resistance $R_p$ to the corrosion current density $i_{corr}$, and from there to corrosion rate.

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Butler-Volmer Equation

The net current density for a single electrochemical reaction under mixed control is given by the **Butler-Volmer equation**:

$$i = i_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\left(-\frac{\alpha_c F \eta}{RT}\right) \right]$$

where:
- $i_0$ = exchange current density (A/cm²)
- $\alpha_a, \alpha_c$ = anodic and cathodic transfer coefficients (dimensionless, $\alpha_a + \alpha_c \approx 1$ for single-step reactions)
- $F$ = Faraday constant (96485 C/mol)
- $R$ = Universal gas constant (8.314 J/mol·K)
- $T$ = Absolute temperature (K)
- $\eta$ = Activation overpotential (V)

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — At the Corrosion Potential

At $E = E_{corr}$ (the open-circuit potential), the net externally-measured current is **zero**, because the anodic metal dissolution current exactly equals the cathodic reduction current. Define the corrosion current density as this common magnitude:

$$i_{corr} = i_a = \vert i_c \vert \quad \text{at} \quad E = E_{corr}$$

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Apply Small Polarization

When we apply a small overpotential $\eta = E - E_{corr}$ such that $\vert \eta \vert$ is small ($\le 20$ mV), the exponentials in the Butler-Volmer equation can be linearized using the Taylor series:

$$\exp(x) \approx 1 + x + \frac{x^2}{2!} + \cdots$$

For small $x$, we truncate to the first-order term: $\exp(x) \approx 1 + x$.

Applying this to both exponentials in the Butler-Volmer equation:

$$i \approx i_0 \left[ \left(1 + \frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}\right) - \left(1 - \frac{\alpha_c F \eta}{RT}\right) \right]$$

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Simplify the Linearized Expression

The 1's cancel, leaving:

$$i \approx i_0 \left[ \frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT} + \frac{\alpha_c F \eta}{RT} \right]$$

$$i \approx \frac{i_0 F \eta}{RT} (\alpha_a + \alpha_c)$$

For a single-step single-electron transfer, $\alpha_a + \alpha_c = 1$. More generally, the linearized form is:

$$i \approx \frac{i_0 F \eta}{RT} (\alpha_a + \alpha_c)$$

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Recognize the Slope as Polarization Resistance

By definition, polarization resistance is:

$$R_p = \left(\frac{d\eta}{di}\right)_{\eta \to 0} = \frac{RT}{i_0 F (\alpha_a + \alpha_c)}$$

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Extend to a Corrosion Cell (Stern-Geary Insight)

In a corroding system, the small polarization at the WE perturbs **two** coupled reactions (anodic metal dissolution + cathodic reduction). The mixed-potential theory (Wagner-Traud) shows that the linearized net current response is governed by **both** Tafel slopes $\beta_a$ and $\beta_c$, defined as:

$$\beta_a = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha_a F}, \qquad \beta_c = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha_c F}$$

Substituting these into the linearized expression for the corrosion cell gives the **Stern-Geary equation**:

$$\boxed{R_p = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303 \, i_{corr} (\beta_a + \beta_c)}}$$

Rearranging for the corrosion current density:

$$\boxed{i_{corr} = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303 \, R_p (\beta_a + \beta_c)} = \frac{B}{R_p}}$$

where the **Stern-Geary constant** $B$ is:

$$B = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)}$$

### 3.1.7 Step 6 — Converting $i_{corr}$ to Corrosion Rate

By **Faraday's Law**, the mass dissolved per unit area per unit time is:

$$\text{CR (g/cm}^2 \text{/s)} = \frac{i_{corr} \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

To express in mm/year, multiply by the time conversion ($3.15 \times 10^7$ s/yr) and divide by density $\rho$ (g/cm³). The combined constant yields:

$$\boxed{CR = \frac{3.27 \times 10^{-3} \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho}}$$

where $EW = M/n$ is the **equivalent weight** (g/eq), $i_{corr}$ is in µA/cm², and $\rho$ is in g/cm³.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Tafel Slope from Polarization Data

A **Tafel plot** is $\eta$ (overpotential) on the y-axis vs. $\log_{10} \vert i \vert$ on the x-axis. For a single, charge-transfer-controlled reaction, taking the logarithm of the anodic branch of the Butler-Volmer equation (when $|\eta|$ is large, the reverse term is negligible) gives:

$$\eta = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha_a F} \log i - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha_a F} \log i_0$$

Comparing with the linear form $\eta = a + b \log i$:

$$\boxed{\beta_a = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha_a F}, \quad a = -\beta_a \log i_0}$$

The slope $b = \beta_a$ (V/decade). At $T = 298$ K, with $\alpha_a = 0.5$, this gives $\beta_a = 0.118$ V/decade — a useful sanity check.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Corrosion Rate from Weight Loss

Starting from Faraday's First Law:

$$\Delta m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

Recognizing that $i_{corr} = I/A$ and $EW = M/n$:

$$\Delta m = \frac{i_{corr} \cdot A \cdot t \cdot EW}{F}$$

To obtain a rate per unit area:

$$\frac{\Delta m}{A \cdot t} = \frac{i_{corr} \cdot EW}{F}$$

Converting to mm/year with density $\rho$ and unit conversion factors:

$$CR = \frac{\Delta m}{A \cdot t \cdot \rho} = \frac{i_{corr} \cdot EW}{F \cdot \rho} \quad \text{(in g/cm}^2\text{/s converted to mm/yr)}$$

Multiplying by $3.15 \times 10^7$ s/yr and the length conversion $10$ mm/cm:

$$CR \text{(mm/yr)} = \frac{3.27 \times 10^{-3} \cdot i_{corr} \text{ (µA/cm}^2) \cdot EW \text{ (g/eq)}}{\rho \text{ (g/cm}^3)}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A carbon-steel coupon (composition: Fe 99%, C 1%; $EW = 28$ g/eq, $\rho = 7.86$ g/cm³) is subjected to LPR measurement. The slope of $\Delta E$ vs $\Delta I$ near $E_{corr}$ yields $R_p = 2000 \, \Omega \cdot cm^2$. Tafel slopes are $\beta_a = 90$ mV/dec and $\beta_c = 120$ mV/dec. Calculate the corrosion current density and the corrosion rate.

### Solution — Step 1: Stern-Geary Constant

$$B = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)} = \frac{(0.090)(0.120)}{2.303(0.090 + 0.120)}$$

$$B = \frac{0.0108}{2.303 \times 0.210} = \frac{0.0108}{0.4836} = 0.02233 \text{ V}$$

### Solution — Step 2: Corrosion Current Density

$$i_{corr} = \frac{B}{R_p} = \frac{0.02233}{2000} = 1.117 \times 10^{-5} \text{ A/cm}^2$$

$$i_{corr} = 11.17 \, \mu A/cm^2$$

### Solution — Step 3: Corrosion Rate

$$CR = \frac{3.27 \times 10^{-3} \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho} = \frac{3.27 \times 10^{-3} \times 11.17 \times 28}{7.86}$$

$$CR = \frac{3.27 \times 10^{-3} \times 312.76}{7.86} = \frac{1.0227}{7.86} = 0.1301 \text{ mm/yr}$$

$$\boxed{CR \approx 0.130 \text{ mm/yr}}$$

> **Interpretation:** This corresponds to a **moderate corrosion regime** (typical industrial limit is ~0.1–0.5 mm/yr for carbon steel in neutral waters). If CR > 0.5 mm/yr, the engineer must consider inhibitors, coatings, or material upgrade.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Classification of Corrosion Testing & Monitoring Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Corrosion Testing and Monitoring"]
    B["Non-Electrochemical Methods"]
    C["Electrochemical Methods"]

    D["Weight-Loss Coupon ASTM G1/G4"]
    E["Visual Optical Inspection"]
    F["Ultrasonic Testing UT"]
    G["Radiographic Testing RT"]
    H["Eddy Current Testing ET"]
    I["Liquid Penetrant DPI"]
    J["Magnetic Particle MPI"]
    K["Electrical Resistance ER Probe"]
    L["Dimensional Thickness"]

    M["Open Circuit Potential OCP"]
    N["Tafel Polarization"]
    O["Linear Polarization Resistance LPR"]
    P["Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy EIS"]
    Q["Electrochemical Noise Analysis ENA"]
    R["Cyclic Polarization Pitting"]

    S["Instrument: Potentiostat and Galvanostat"]

    A --> B
    A --> C
    B --> D
    B --> E
    B --> F
    B --> G
    B --> H
    B --> I
    B --> J
    B --> K
    B --> L
    C --> M
    C --> N
    C --> O
    C --> P
    C --> Q
    C --> R
    C --> S
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — The Potentiostat in a Three-Electrode Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Setpoint["Setpoint Reference"]
        SP["DC Signal Generator or Computer DAC"]
    end

    subgraph Pot["Potentiostat Electronics"]
        EDC["Error Detector Comparator"]
        AMP["Power Amplifier Op-Amp"]
        IF["Current to Voltage Converter I to E_out"]
    end

    subgraph Cell["Three Electrode Electrochemical Cell"]
        WE["Working Electrode Corroding Metal"]
        RE["Reference Electrode SCE AgAgCl"]
        CE["Counter Electrode Platinum or Graphite"]
    end

    subgraph Data["Data Acquisition"]
        DAQ["ADC plus Computer Plotting Software"]
    end

    SP --> EDC
    RE --> EDC
    EDC --> AMP
    AMP --> CE
    WE --> IF
    IF --> DAQ
    WE --> DAQ
    RE --> DAQ
```

**Functional logic flow:**
1. **Setpoint** supplies the desired WE potential (e.g., $E_{set} = E_{corr} - 10$ mV).
2. **Error detector** continuously subtracts the actual RE potential from the setpoint and generates an error voltage.
3. **Power amplifier** boosts the error and drives current through the CE → electrolyte → WE loop until the WE-vs-RE potential equals the setpoint.
4. **Current follower** measures the WE current (the dependent variable containing corrosion information).
5. **Data acquisition** logs $E$ vs. $i$ and produces polarization curves, Nyquist plots, etc.

## 4.3 Architecture — Linear Polarization Resistance (LPR) Measurement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["Potentiostat"]
    SW["Linear Sweep plus 10 mV per minute"]
    WE["Working Electrode Sample"]
    RE["Reference Electrode SCE"]
    CE["Counter Electrode Pt Mesh"]
    SL["Slope Delta E over Delta I at E_corr"]
    RP["R_p value in ohm cm squared"]
    SG["Stern Geary Equation i_corr = B over R_p"]
    CR["Corrosion Rate mm per year"]

    P --> SW
    SW --> WE
    WE --> RE
    WE --> CE
    WE --> SL
    SL --> RP
    RP --> SG
    SG --> CR
```

## 4.4 Architecture — Non-Electrochemical vs. Electrochemical Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q["What do you need to measure?"]
    Q1["Average mass loss over months"]
    Q2["Instantaneous corrosion rate in field"]
    Q3["Pitting and passivity behavior"]
    Q4["Coating performance under AC"]
    Q5["Crack detection in walls"]

    N1["Weight-Loss Coupon"]
    N2["LPR Probe with Potentiostat"]
    N3["Cyclic Polarization with Potentiostat"]
    N4["EIS with Potentiostat"]
    N5["UT or Radiography"]

    Q --> Q1
    Q --> Q2
    Q --> Q3
    Q --> Q4
    Q --> Q5
    Q1 --> N1
    Q2 --> N2
    Q3 --> N3
    Q4 --> N4
    Q5 --> N5
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between non-electrochemical and electrochemical methods of corrosion testing. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Non-Electrochemical | Electrochemical |
|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Measures physical/chemical change (mass, dimension, acoustic impedance) of the metal | Measures electrical response (potential, current, impedance) of corroding system |
| **Response Time** | Slow (days to months) | Fast (seconds to minutes) |
| **Information** | Cumulative damage | Instantaneous corrosion rate |
| **Examples** | Weight-loss coupon (ASTM G1), Ultrasonic Testing, Eddy Current | Tafel Polarization, LPR, EIS |
| **Field Use** | Coupon retrieval required | Online, real-time monitoring possible |

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**What is a potentiostat? Why is a three-electrode cell necessary for its operation?**

**Model Answer:**

A **potentiostat** is an electronic instrument that holds the **potential of a working electrode (WE) at a fixed, controlled value** relative to a reference electrode (RE) by automatically adjusting the current flowing through a counter electrode (CE).

A **three-electrode cell is necessary** because:
1. The **reference electrode** must carry **ideally zero current**; otherwise its potential drifts due to polarization, destroying the accuracy of the control loop.
2. The **counter electrode** carries the current, isolating the RE from the faradaic current path.
3. Without the CE, the current required to polarize the WE would have to pass through the RE, causing measurement error.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the **working of a potentiostat** in a three-electrode electrochemical cell used for corrosion studies. Discuss the role of each electrode. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Derive the **Stern-Geary equation** starting from the Butler-Volmer equation. Define the Stern-Geary constant $B$ and explain its physical significance. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) Potentiostat Working (7 marks)

**Diagram:** Refer to **Section 4.2** of these notes for the functional architecture of the potentiostat with three-electrode cell.

**Working Principle:**
- The **working electrode (WE)** is the metal/alloy under study. It is embedded in an epoxy mount with a defined exposed area (typically 1 cm²).
- The **reference electrode (RE)** — usually SCE or Ag/AgCl — provides a thermodynamically stable potential.
- The **counter electrode (CE)** — typically a platinum mesh or graphite rod — completes the electrical circuit.
- The potentiostat maintains $E_{WE} = E_{set}$ by:
  1. Measuring the difference between $E_{set}$ (setpoint from computer/DAC) and $E_{WE} - E_{RE}$ (sensed by high-impedance buffer).
  2. Amplifying the error signal.
  3. Forcing the corrective current through the CE → electrolyte → WE loop.
- The current flowing through the WE is the **dependent variable** and is converted to a voltage signal by a current-to-voltage converter.
- The result is plotted as $E$ vs. $i$ (polarization curve) or $Z$ vs. $f$ (EIS spectrum).

**Roles of Electrodes:**

| Electrode | Role | Material Examples |
|---|---|---|
| WE | The corroding metal under test | Mild steel, SS, Al, Cu |
| RE | Provides stable reference potential | SCE, Ag/AgCl, Cu/CuSO₄ |
| CE | Carries current, balances the cell | Pt wire/mesh, graphite rod |

> **Valuator's tip:** Draw a labeled diagram. Mention the **op-amp based feedback control** explicitly for full marks.

#### Model Solution — (b) Stern-Geary Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Start with the **Butler-Volmer equation** for the corrosion cell:

$$i = i_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\left(-\frac{\alpha_c F \eta}{RT}\right) \right]$$

**Step 2:** At $E = E_{corr}$, $\eta = 0$, the net current is zero, but the anodic and cathodic partial currents are both equal to $i_{corr}$.

**Step 3:** Apply a small perturbation $\vert \eta \vert \le 20$ mV. Linearize the exponentials using Taylor expansion $\exp(x) \approx 1 + x$:

$$i \approx i_0 \left[ \left(1 + \frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}\right) - \left(1 - \frac{\alpha_c F \eta}{RT}\right) \right] = \frac{i_0 F \eta}{RT}(\alpha_a + \alpha_c)$$

**Step 4:** Identify the slope at $\eta = 0$ as the **polarization resistance**:

$$R_p = \left(\frac{d\eta}{di}\right)_{\eta=0} = \frac{RT}{i_0 F (\alpha_a + \alpha_c)}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute the **Tafel slope definitions** $\beta_a = 2.303 RT/(\alpha_a F)$ and $\beta_c = 2.303 RT/(\alpha_c F)$ into the equation, and identify $i_0$ in a corroding system as the corrosion current density $i_{corr}$:

$$R_p = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303 \, i_{corr} (\beta_a + \beta_c)}$$

**Step 6:** Rearrange:

$$\boxed{i_{corr} = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303 \, R_p (\beta_a + \beta_c)} = \frac{B}{R_p}}$$

where:

$$\boxed{B = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)}}$$

**Physical significance of $B$:** $B$ (with units of V or mV) is a **system-specific constant** that depends on the Tafel slopes. Typical values: **B = 13–52 mV** for active corrosion, with **B ≈ 26 mV** often assumed when Tafel slopes are unknown (a common KTU board exam assumption).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** Describe the **weight-loss coupon method** for corrosion rate determination. Mention the relevant ASTM standards and derive the equation for corrosion rate in mm/yr. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** What are the various **non-electrochemical methods** of corrosion monitoring? Explain the working of **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** and **Electrical Resistance (ER) probes** with suitable diagrams. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) Weight-Loss Method (7 marks)

**Procedure (per ASTM G1, G4, G31):**
1. Cut coupons of known dimensions (typically 50 mm × 25 mm × 3 mm).
2. Polish with SiC paper (120–600 grit), degrease, weigh accurately ($m_1$).
3. Measure surface area $A$ (cm²) precisely.
4. Expose to the test environment for a fixed time $t$ (hours).
5. Retrieve coupons, clean corrosion products chemically (per ASTM G1: inhibited HCl for steel, $\mathrm{HNO_3}$ for SS, etc.).
6. Reweigh to obtain $m_2$.

**Equation Derivation:**

Mass loss: $\Delta m = m_1 - m_2$

Mass loss per unit area: $\Delta m / A$ (g/cm²)

Corrosion rate in g/(cm²·h): $\dfrac{\Delta m}{A \cdot t}$

Convert to mm/yr:

$$CR = \frac{\Delta m}{A \cdot t \cdot \rho} \times 10 \times 8760 \times 1000 \times 10^{-3}$$

Simplifying the constant ($10 \times 8760 \times 10^{-3} \approx 87.6$, but with the g/cm³ to kg/m³ factor of $10^{-3}$ and mm conversion, the standard constant is):

$$\boxed{CR \text{(mm/yr)} = \frac{87.6 \times \Delta m}{A \cdot t \cdot \rho}}$$

where $\Delta m$ is in mg, $A$ in cm², $t$ in hours, and $\rho$ in g/cm³.

> **Valuator's tip:** Always state the units clearly. The **0.0254 mm/mpy** conversion gives the famous "**mils per year**" form: $CR (mpy) = 534 \cdot \Delta m / (A \cdot t \cdot \rho)$.

#### Model Solution — (b) Non-Electrochemical Methods (7 marks)

**List of Non-Electrochemical Methods:**

1. Weight-loss coupon testing (ASTM G1, G4, G31)
2. Visual and optical inspection
3. Dimensional and metallographic examination
4. Ultrasonic Testing (UT)
5. Radiographic Testing (RT)
6. Eddy Current Testing (ET)
7. Liquid Penetrant Inspection (DPI/PT)
8. Magnetic Particle Inspection (MPI/MT)
9. Electrical Resistance (ER) probes
10. Acoustic Emission (AE)

**Ultrasonic Testing (UT):**
- A **piezoelectric transducer** generates high-frequency sound pulses (1–25 MHz) that travel into the metal.
- The pulses reflect from the **back wall** and from any **internal flaws** (corrosion pits, cracks, laminations, blisters).
- The **time-of-flight** of the echo indicates the depth of the flaw; the **amplitude** indicates its size.
- A typical **B-scan** display plots flaw depth vs. probe position along the surface.
- **Limitation:** Requires couplant (gel or water) and skilled operator; difficult on rough or coated surfaces.

**Electrical Resistance (ER) Probe:**
- A thin metallic sensing element of known initial resistance $R_0$ is exposed to the corrosive environment.
- As the element corrodes, its **cross-section $A_{cs}$ decreases**, and since $R = \rho L / A_{cs}$, the resistance $R$ increases linearly with metal loss.
- The probe body is sealed except for the sensing element, isolating the measurement from the corrosive environment.
- **Sensitivity:** Modern ER probes can detect as little as **1 µm of metal loss**.
- **Advantage:** Works in **non-electrolytes** (hydrocarbons, gases) where electrochemical methods fail.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE MARKS on this topic — read carefully!**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in the Stern-Geary equation.** Writing $i_{corr} = B / R_p$ without specifying $B$ in V, $R_p$ in $\Omega \cdot cm^2$, and $i_{corr}$ in A/cm² loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing Tafel slope (V/decade) with transfer coefficient $\alpha$ (dimensionless).** The conversion is $\beta = 2.303 RT/(\alpha F)$. At 298 K, $2.303 RT/F = 0.0591$ V. So $\beta = 0.0591/\alpha$.
> 3. **Drawing only a two-electrode cell for the potentiostat.** This is an automatic 1-mark deduction. **Always draw three electrodes.**
> 4. **Forgetting the chemical cleaning step (ASTM G1) in the weight-loss method.** Omitting this gives artificially low mass loss and wrong corrosion rate.
> 5. **Mixing ER probes with electrochemical methods.** ER probes are a **non-electrochemical** method in KTU classification (the resistance measured is a passive physical property, not a faradaic current).
> 6. **Not stating the validity range of LPR.** LPR is valid only when $\vert \Delta E \vert \le 20$ mV (linear regime). Outside this range, the Tafel or full Butler-Volmer treatment must be used.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Board Exam 2024 Scheme**

### Non-Electrochemical Methods
- ☐ **Weight-loss coupon** — gold standard; ASTM G1 (cleaning), G31 (lab immersion); formula: $CR = 87.6 \Delta m / (A \cdot t \cdot \rho)$ in mm/yr.
- ☐ **Visual/optical** — cheapest, most subjective; uses borescopes.
- ☐ **Ultrasonic (UT)** — uses sound echoes; measures wall thickness and detects pits/cracks.
- ☐ **Radiographic (RT)** — X-ray or $\gamma$-ray imaging; works through insulation.
- ☐ **Eddy current (ET)** — for conductive metals; detects surface defects.
- ☐ **DPI/MPI** — surface-breaking defect detection.
- ☐ **ER probes** — measure resistance increase due to cross-section loss; works in non-electrolytes.

### Electrochemical Methods
- ☐ **OCP** — measures $E_{corr}$; thermodynamic tendency only.
- ☐ **Tafel polarization** — extrapolate linear log-i vs. $\eta$ regions to get $i_{corr}$.
- ☐ **LPR (Stern-Geary)** — $i_{corr} = B / R_p$; valid for $\vert \Delta E \vert \le 20$ mV; $B \approx 26$ mV (assumed default).
- ☐ **EIS** — AC impedance over wide frequency range; Randles circuit model.
- ☐ **ENA** — RMS noise, noise resistance $R_n$; detects pitting vs. uniform.
- ☐ **Cyclic polarization** — identifies $E_{pit}$ and $E_{prot}$ for pitting.

### Potentiostat Essentials
- ☐ **Three electrodes:** WE (corroding metal), RE (SCE/Ag/AgCl — zero current), CE (Pt/graphite — carries current).
- ☐ **Function:** Holds $E_{WE}$ at setpoint vs. RE by adjusting current through CE.
- ☐ **Cannot work with two electrodes** — RE polarization would corrupt the measurement.
- ☐ **Galvanostat** = constant current; **Potentiostat** = constant potential.
- ☐ **Used for:** Tafel, LPR, EIS, cyclic polarization, ENA, chronoamperometry, chronopotentiometry.

### Critical Constants
- ☐ $F = 96485$ C/mol
- ☐ $2.303 RT/F = 0.0591$ V at 298 K
- ☐ $1$ year $= 3.15 \times 10^7$ s; $8760$ hours/yr
- ☐ $3.27 \times 10^{-3}$ Faraday-derived constant for $i_{corr}$ (µA/cm²) to $CR$ (mm/yr) via $EW/\rho$.

### KTU 2024 Bloom-Level Outcomes to Master
- ☐ **Remember/Understand:** Definitions of potentiostat, three-electrode cell, Tafel slope, $R_p$, $i_{corr}$.
- ☐ **Apply:** Use Stern-Geary to compute $i_{corr}$ and CR from LPR data.
- ☐ **Analyze:** Choose the correct method for a given corrosion problem (pitting vs. uniform vs. SCC).
- ☐ **Evaluate:** Compare Tafel vs. LPR vs. EIS for a field application.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tafel extrapolation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT527 - CORROSION ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Tafel extrapolation # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Tafel extrapolation** is an electrochemical DC polarization technique used to determine the **corrosion current density ($i_{corr}$)** and consequently the **corrosion rate (CR)** of a metal in a given electrolyte. The method is based on the **Tafel equation**, which describes the linear logarithmic relationship between the **overpotential ($\eta$)** and the **current density ($i$)** in regions sufficiently far from the corrosion potential ($E_{corr}$), where one of the half-cell reactions (anodic metal dissolution or cathodic reduction) becomes kinetically dominant. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Tafel Equation (Fundamental Form):** > The anodic and cathodic branches of a polarization curve, when plotted as **overpotential ($\eta$) vs. $\log|i|$**, yield straight lines known as **Tafel lines**. The extrapolation of these linear regions to the corrosion potential ($E_{corr}$) gives the **corrosion current density ($i_{corr}$)** at their point of intersection. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The River Confluence Model Imagine two rivers (an anodic river and a cathodic river) flowing toward a single low-lying plain. The **anodic river** represents metal dissolving (oxidation, producing electrons), while the **cathodic river** represents reduction (e.g., oxygen or hydrogen evolution, consuming electrons). In the absence of any external disturbance, both rivers have equal flow rates — this is the **steady-state corrosion condition** at $E_{corr}$. Now, if you build a dam (apply external potential) and divert water (current), the flow of each river changes. If you plot the **water level (potential)** against the **logarithm of the diverted flow (log current)**, you get straight-line "Tafel slopes" for each river. By extending those straight lines backward (extrapolation) to where the dam is removed ($E_{corr}$), you can find the **original undisturbed flow rate** — this flow rate is the **corrosion current**, $i_{corr}$. The greater the original flow, the faster the metal corrodes. ## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics and Physical Constants > [!NOTE] > **Essential Constants Used in Tafel Analysis:** > - **Faraday's Constant ($F$)** = **96,485 C mol⁻¹** > - **Universal Gas Constant ($R$)** = **8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹** > - **Absolute Temperature ($T$)** in **Kelvin (K)** > - **β_a (Anodic Tafel slope)** — typically **0.06 – 0.12 V/decade** > - **β_c (Cathodic Tafel slope)** — typically **0.06 – 0.12 V/decade** > - **Polarization range**: Generally **±250 mV** from $E_{corr}$ (per ASTM G5 / G59) ## 1.4 Significance in Corrosion Engineering Tafel extrapolation is the **gold standard for laboratory determination of $i_{corr}$** because it provides: 1. Direct quantitative corrosion rate (in mpy or mm/yr). 2. Mechanistic insight into the rate-controlling half-reaction. 3. Evaluation of inhibitors (by comparing Tafel slopes and $i_{corr}$ before/after addition). 4. Validation of the **Mixed Potential Theory (Evans Diagram)**. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Butler–Volmer to Tafel The complete electrode kinetics at the metal–electrolyte interface are governed by the **Butler–Volmer equation**: $$i = i_{corr} \left[ \exp\left(\frac{2.303 \cdot \eta}{\beta_a}\right) - \exp\left(-\frac{2.303 \cdot \eta}{\beta_c}\right) \right]$$ Where: - $i$ = externally measured net current density (A/cm²) - $i_{corr}$ = corrosion current density (A/cm²) - $\eta = E - E_{corr}$ = overpotential (V) -
