$\beta_a$ = anodic Tafel slope (V/decade) - $\beta_c$ = cathodic Tafel slope (V/decade) ### Step-by-Step Logic Reduction: 1. **At $E = E_{corr}$** → $\eta = 0$ → both exponentials equal 1 → net current $i = 0$ (equilibrium condition of mixed potential). 2. **For large anodic polarization** ($\eta \gg 0$, typically $\eta > 50$ mV): - The cathodic exponential term becomes negligible. - Equation reduces to: $i \approx i_{corr} \cdot \exp\left(\frac{2.303 \cdot \eta}{\beta_a}\right)$ 3. **Taking logarithm of both sides** gives the **anodic Tafel equation**: - $\eta_a = \beta_a \log\left(\frac{i}{i_{corr}}\right)$ 4. **For large cathodic polarization** ($\eta \ll 0$): - $i \approx -i_{corr} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{2.303 \cdot \eta}{\beta_c}\right)$ 5. **Cathodic Tafel equation**: - $-\eta_c = \beta_c \log\left(\frac{|i|}{i_{corr}}\right)$ ## 2.2 Tafel Slopes — Physical Meaning > [!IMPORTANT] > The Tafel slope $\beta$ is **inversely proportional to the charge-transfer coefficient ($\alpha$)**: > - $\beta_a = \dfrac{2.303 \cdot RT}{\alpha_a \cdot n \cdot F}$ (for anodic) > - $\beta_c = \dfrac{2.303 \cdot RT}{\alpha_c \cdot n \cdot F}$ (for cathodic) > > A **steep Tafel slope** = a sluggish electrochemical reaction (kinetically hindered). > A **shallow Tafel slope** = a fast electrochemical reaction (kinetically facile). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Parameter / Formula** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Units** | **Engineering Use** | |---|---|---|---| | Overpotential | $\eta = E_{applied} - E_{corr}$ | V (Volt) | Driving force for current | | Anodic Tafel Eq. | $\eta_a = \beta_a \cdot \log\left(\dfrac{i_a}{i_{corr}}\right)$ | V | Metal dissolution kinetics | | Cathodic Tafel Eq. | $\eta_c = \beta_c \cdot \log\left(\dfrac{\vert i_c \vert}{i_{corr}}\right)$ | V | Reduction reaction kinetics | | Anodic Tafel slope | $\beta_a = \dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha_a \, n F}$ | V/decade | Reaction mechanism indicator | | Cathodic Tafel slope | $\beta_c = \dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha_c \, n F}$ | V/decade | Reaction mechanism indicator | | Stern–Geary Coefficient | $B = \dfrac{\beta_a \cdot \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)}$ | V | Polarization resistance calc. | | Corrosion Rate (mpy) | $CR = \dfrac{0.129 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho}$ | mpy (mils per year) | Engineering corrosion loss | | Corrosion Rate (mm/yr) | $CR = \dfrac{0.00327 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho}$ | mm/yr | SI corrosion loss | | Equivalent Weight | $EW = \dfrac{\text{Atomic Weight}}{n}$ | g/eq | Faraday calculation | | Faraday Mass Loss | $W = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | grams | Total metal dissolved | > [!NOTE] > **Where in the formula sheet:** > - $i_{corr}$ in **µA/cm²** for the mpy equation > - $\rho$ = density of metal in **g/cm³** > - $EW$ = equivalent weight in **g/eq** > - $n$ = number of electrons transferred in dissolution reaction ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Tafel extrapolation is the **backbone of laboratory corrosion science** and is routinely applied for: - **Inhibitor screening**: Comparing $i_{corr}$ and Tafel slopes in inhibited vs. uninhibited electrolytes. - **Coating evaluation**: Polarization behavior of coated vs. bare substrates. - **Alloy development**: Ranking new alloys in aggressive media (HCl, H₂SO₄, NaCl, seawater). - **Failure analysis**: Reconstructing in-service corrosion behavior from laboratory polarization data. - **Cathodic protection design**: Validating the required protective current. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Butler–Volmer to Tafel Equation ### Starting Point — Butler–Volmer Equation The current density at a polarized electrode (no mass-transport limitation) is: $$i = i_{corr} \left[ \exp\left(\frac{2.303 \cdot \eta}{\beta_a}\right) - \exp\left(-\frac{2.303 \cdot \eta}{\beta_c}\right) \right]$$ ### Step 1: Apply Large Anodic Polarization ($\eta \gg 0$) When $\eta$ is large and positive (typically $\eta > 50$ mV), the second exponential term becomes very small compared to the first: $$i \approx i_{corr} \cdot \exp\left(\frac{2.303 \cdot \eta}{\beta_a}\right)$$ ### Step 2: Take Common Logarithm (base 10) $$\log\left(\frac{i}{i_{corr}}\right) = \frac{\eta}{\beta_a}$$ ### Step 3: Solve for Overpotential $$\eta = \beta_a \cdot \log\left(\frac{i}{i_{corr}}\right)$$ This is the **Anodic Tafel Equation** in its standard form. It predicts a **straight line** when $\eta$ is plotted against $\log(i)$, with slope $\beta_a$. ### Step 4: Similarly Derive the Cathodic Branch For large cathodic polarization ($\eta \ll 0$): $$i \approx -i_{corr} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{2.303 \cdot \eta}{\beta_c}\right)$$ $$\log\left(\frac{|i|}{i_{corr}}\right) = -\frac{\eta}{\beta_c}$$ $$\eta = -\beta_c \cdot \log\left(\frac{|i|}{i_{corr}}\right)$$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Tafel Slope from Charge-Transfer Kinetics ### Step 1: Start with the Single-Electron Charge-Transfer Reaction The anodic current density from transition-state theory is: $$i_a = i_0 \cdot \exp\left(\frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}\right)$$ ### Step 2: Take the Logarithm $$\log(i_a) = \log(i_0) + \frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{2.303 \, RT}$$ ### Step 3: Compare with the Anodic Tafel Equation Comparing the coefficient of $\eta$ with $\eta = \beta_a \log(i/i_{corr})$: $$\frac{1}{\beta_a} = \frac{\alpha_a F}{2.303 \, RT}$$ ### Step 4: Final Expression $$\beta_a = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha_a F}$$ This derivation shows that the Tafel slope is **thermodynamically linked** to the **charge-transfer coefficient ($\alpha$)** — a microscopic descriptor of the energy barrier symmetry. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Corrosion Rate from $i_{corr}$ (Faraday's Law) ### Step 1: Mass Loss per Unit Time from Faraday's Law $$\dot{m} = \frac{i_{corr} \cdot A \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$ Where: - $\dot{m}$ = mass loss rate (g/s) - $A$ = exposed area (cm²) - $t$ = time (s) - $M$ = atomic mass (g/mol) ### Step 2: Penetration Depth The linear corrosion penetration $CR$ (in cm/s) is: $$CR = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho \cdot A}$$ ### Step 3: Substitute and Simplify $$CR = \frac{i_{corr} \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho} = \frac{i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho \cdot F}$$ ### Step 4: Convert to Engineering Units (mpy) Using $1 \text{ mpy} = 0.00254 \text{ cm/yr}$ and unit conversions: $$CR (\text{mpy}) = \frac{0.129 \cdot i_{corr}(\mu A/cm^2) \cdot EW}{\rho (g/cm^3)}$$ $$CR (\text{mm/yr}) = \frac{0.00327 \cdot i_{corr}(\mu A/cm^2) \cdot EW}{\rho (g/cm^3)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU-style):** > Given: Mild steel in 1 M H₂SO₄, $i_{corr} = 100 \, \mu A/cm^2$, Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻, $M_{Fe} = 55.85$, $\rho_{Fe} = 7.87 \, g/cm^3$, $n = 2$. > > $EW = 55.85/2 = 27.93 \, g/eq$ > > $$CR = \frac{0.129 \times 100 \times 27.93}{7.87} \approx 45.8 \, \text{mpy}$$ > > This corresponds to **≈ 1.16 mm/yr** — a moderately aggressive corrosion rate. ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Stern–Geary Equation (Link to LPR) For small polarizations ($\vert \eta \vert < 10$ mV), the Butler–Volmer equation linearizes to: $$i \approx i_{corr} \cdot \frac{2.303 \cdot \eta}{\beta_a} + i_{corr} \cdot \frac{2.303 \cdot \eta}{\beta_c}$$ $$\frac{\eta}{i} = R_p = \frac{B}{i_{corr}}$$ $$\boxed{B = \frac{\beta_a \cdot \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)}}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **Stern–Geary equation**, the theoretical bridge between **Tafel extrapolation** (high $\eta$) and **Linear Polarization Resistance (LPR)** (low $\eta$). Knowing $\beta_a$ and $\beta_c$ from a Tafel experiment allows determination of $i_{corr}$ from a single LPR measurement — the principle behind commercial corrosion rate meters. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Tafel Extrapolation Procedure (Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Prepare Working Electrode] --> B[Immerse in Electrolyte
Wait for Stable OCP] B --> C[Measure Ecorr Open Circuit Potential] C --> D[Apply Linear Polarization
from minus 250 mV to plus 250 mV vs Ecorr] D --> E[Record E versus log i Data] E --> F{Identify Linear Tafel Regions
typically at least one decade of current} F -->|Anodic branch| G[Determine Slope beta_a
Intercept at Ecorr] F -->|Cathodic branch| H[Determine Slope beta_c
Intercept at Ecorr] G --> I[Extrapolate Both Lines to Ecorr] H --> I I --> J[Intersection Point gives icorr] J --> K[Calculate Corrosion Rate
CR from Faraday Law] K --> L[End: Report icorr and CR] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Evans Diagram and Tafel Extrapolation Concept ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Anodic Reaction
Metal to Mn plus plus ne
Slope beta_a] -->|Extrapolate to Ecorr| C[Intersection Point
Ecorr, icorr] B[Cathodic Reaction
2H plus plus 2e to H2
Slope beta_c] -->|Extrapolate to Ecorr| C C --> D[Corrosion Current Density icorr] D --> E[Apply Faraday Law
CR equals K times icorr times EW over rho] E --> F[Engineering Corrosion Rate
mpy or mm per yr] ``` ## 4.3 Schematic of a Three-Electrode Potentiostatic Tafel Setup ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Cell[Electrochemical Cell] WE[Working Electrode
Metal Specimen] CE[Counter Electrode
Platinum or Graphite] RE[Reference Electrode
SCE or Ag or AgCl] end PS[Potentiostat
Gamry or PAR or Autolab] -->|Senses E between WE and RE| WE PS -->|Applies I between WE and CE| CE WE -->|Current i| PS RE -->|Potential feedback| PS PS -->|Logs E versus log i Curve| PC[Data Acquisition Computer] PC -->|Plot and Extrapolate| Result[Tafel Extrapolation
icorr, beta_a, beta_c] ``` ## 4.4 Conceptual Map — Tafel Extrapolation and Related Methods ```mermaid flowchart TD DC[DC Polarization Techniques] --> A1[Linear Polarization Resistance LPR
Small eta less than 10 mV] DC --> A2[Tafel Extrapolation
Large eta plus or minus 250 mV] DC --> A3[Cyclic Polarization
For Pitting and Passivity] A2 --> B1[Provides beta_a and beta_c] A2 --> B2[Provides icorr] B1 --> C1[Stern Geary B factor] C1 --> A1 B2 --> D1[Faraday Law] D1 --> E1[Corrosion Rate CR] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: Define the Tafel equation. What are Tafel slopes?** **Model Answer:** The Tafel equation expresses the linear logarithmic dependence of the **overpotential ($\eta$)** on the **current density ($i$)** when a single electrochemical reaction dominates the kinetics. It is written as: $$\eta = a \pm b \log(i)$$ The constants $\beta_a$ (anodic) and $\beta_c$ (cathodic) are called **Tafel slopes**, with units of **V/decade** (volts per decade of current). They represent the rate at which the overpotential must change to produce a tenfold increase in current. Typical values lie in the range of **0.06 – 0.12 V/decade**. The Tafel slopes are inversely related to the charge-transfer coefficient $\alpha$ and are diagnostic of the rate-controlling reaction mechanism. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: What is meant by Tafel extrapolation? Why is it performed?** **Model Answer:** Tafel extrapolation is a graphical procedure applied to a **potentiodynamic polarization curve** in which the **linear anodic and cathodic Tafel regions** (at sufficient overpotential from $E_{corr}$, typically $|\eta| > 50$ mV) are extrapolated back to the **corrosion potential ($E_{corr}$)**. The **point of intersection** of the two extrapolated Tafel lines gives the **corrosion current density ($i_{corr}$)**. It is performed to: 1. Quantify the **kinetics of corrosion** through $i_{corr}$. 2. Determine the **Tafel slopes** $\beta_a$ and $\beta_c$ for mechanistic analysis. 3. Calculate the **corrosion rate (CR)** using Faraday's law. 4. Evaluate the **efficiency of corrosion inhibitors** by comparing $i_{corr}$ before and after addition. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: (a)** Derive the Tafel equation from the Butler–Volmer equation, clearly stating the assumptions used. **(7 marks)** **Q: (b)** Explain the experimental procedure to obtain Tafel parameters ($i_{corr}$, $\beta_a$, $\beta_c$) using a three-electrode potentiostat. Discuss the role of ASTM standards G5 and G59. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a) — Derivation (7 Marks) **Step 1: Write the Butler–Volmer equation** as the general rate expression for a corroding electrode: $$i = i_{corr} \left[ \exp\left(\frac{2.303 \, \eta}{\beta_a}\right) - \exp\left(-\frac{2.303 \, \eta}{\beta_c}\right) \right]$$ **Step 2: State the assumption** — For sufficiently large anodic polarization ($\eta \gg 0$, typically $\eta > 50$ mV), the **cathodic exponential term becomes negligible** because the reverse reaction is suppressed by the applied potential. $$i \approx i_{corr} \cdot \exp\left(\frac{2.303 \, \eta}{\beta_a}\right)$$ **Step 3: Rearrange to isolate the exponential:** $$\frac{i}{i_{corr}} = \exp\left(\frac{2.303 \, \eta}{\beta_a}\right)$$ **Step 4: Take the natural logarithm on both sides:** $$\ln\left(\frac{i}{i_{corr}}\right) = \frac{2.303 \, \eta}{\beta_a}$$ **Step 5: Convert to base-10 logarithm using $\ln(x) = 2.303 \log(x)$:** $$\eta = \beta_a \cdot \log\left(\frac{i}{i_{corr}}\right)$$ This is the **Anodic Tafel Equation** — a straight-line equation in the $\eta$ vs. $\log(i)$ plane with slope $\beta_a$ and intercept $\beta_a \log(i_{corr})$. **Step 6: Similarly for the cathodic branch** with $\eta \ll 0$: $$\eta = -\beta_c \cdot \log\left(\frac{|i|}{i_{corr}}\right)$$ --- #### Model Solution for (b) — Experimental Procedure (7 Marks) **Step 1: Specimen Preparation** The metal specimen (working electrode) is mounted in epoxy or a holder exposing a defined area (typically 1 cm²). It is ground with SiC papers (up to 600 or 1200 grit), polished, degreased with acetone, rinsed with distilled water, and dried. **Step 2: Electrochemical Cell Setup** A standard **three-electrode cell** is used: - **Working Electrode (WE):** metal specimen - **Reference Electrode (RE):** Saturated Calomel Electrode (SCE) or Ag/AgCl - **Counter Electrode (CE):** Platinum or graphite **Step 3: Measure Open Circuit Potential (OCP)** The specimen is immersed in the electrolyte and the **stable rest potential $E_{corr}$** is recorded (typically after 30–60 minutes). **Step 4: Potentiodynamic Polarization** Using a **potentiostat**, the potential is scanned from **−250 mV** to **+250 mV** (or more) relative to $E_{corr}$ at a typical scan rate of **0.1 – 1 mV/s**. The resulting current is logged continuously. **Step 5: Data Plotting and Extrapolation** The data is plotted as **E (V) vs. $\log|i|$ (A/cm²)**. Linear regions in the anodic and cathodic branches (at least one decade of current) are identified. Their linear fits are extrapolated back to $E_{corr}$. The intersection gives $i_{corr}$, and the slopes give $\beta_a$ and $\beta_c$. **Step 6: Role of ASTM Standards** - **ASTM G5**: Standard reference test method for making potentiostatic and potentiodynamic anodic polarization measurements on stainless steels. - **ASTM G59**: Standard practice for conducting potentiodynamic polarization resistance measurements. These standards prescribe cell geometry, electrode preparation, scan rate, electrolyte composition (e.g., 1 N H₂SO₄), and reporting format — ensuring **reproducibility and comparability** of Tafel data across laboratories. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: (a)** With the help of a labeled **Evans diagram**, explain the principle of Tafel extrapolation for determining corrosion current density. **(7 marks)** **Q: (b)** For mild steel corroding in aerated 1 M H₂SO₄, the following Tafel parameters were measured: $\beta_a = 0.060$ V/decade, $\beta_c = 0.120$ V/decade, and $i_{corr} = 100 \, \mu A/cm^2$. Calculate: (i) The Stern–Geary coefficient $B$. (ii) The linear polarization resistance $R_p$ of the system. (iii) The corrosion rate in **mpy** (atomic weight of Fe = 55.85, $n = 2$, $\rho = 7.87 \, g/cm^3$). **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a) — Evans Diagram (7 Marks) **Evans Diagram Construction:** An **Evans diagram** is a semi-logarithmic plot showing the **partial anodic current density** and the **partial cathodic current density** as functions of electrode potential on the same axes. - **X-axis:** Electrode potential $E$ (V vs. RE) - **Y-axis:** $\log|i|$ (A/cm²) - **Anodic line:** Represents metal dissolution (M → M^(n+) + ne⁻), with positive slope $\beta_a$ (typically moving up to the right). - **Cathodic line:** Represents the cathodic reduction (e.g., 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂ or O₂ + 2H₂O + 4e⁻ → 4OH⁻), with negative slope $-\beta_c$. **Point of Intersection:** At the **corrosion potential ($E_{corr}$)**, the anodic and cathodic currents are equal in magnitude and opposite in sign — the net external current is zero. This intersection defines $i_{corr}$, the rate of metal dissolution at the open-circuit condition. **Tafel Extrapolation Principle:** Far from $E_{corr}$, the measured net current is dominated by one of the half-reactions. In these **Tafel regions**, $E$ vs. $\log|i|$ becomes linear. By drawing the **best-fit straight line** through the anodic Tafel region (slope $\beta_a$) and through the cathodic Tafel region (slope $-\beta_c$), and extrapolating them to $E_{corr}$, their intersection gives $i_{corr}$ geometrically. The Tafel slopes themselves are obtained from the slopes of these linear fits. --- #### Model Solution for (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks) **Given:** - $\beta_a = 0.060$ V/decade - $\beta_c = 0.120$ V/decade - $i_{corr} = 100 \, \mu A/cm^2 = 100 \times 10^{-6}$ A/cm² - Atomic weight of Fe = 55.85, $n = 2$, $\rho = 7.87 \, g/cm^3$ **(i) Stern–Geary Coefficient B:** $$B = \frac{\beta_a \cdot \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)}$$ $$B = \frac{0.060 \times 0.120}{2.303 \times (0.060 + 0.120)} = \frac{0.0072}{2.303 \times 0.180}$$ $$B = \frac{0.0072}{0.41454} = 0.01737 \, V$$ **Result:** $B \approx 0.0174 \, V$ (or **17.4 mV**). **(ii) Linear Polarization Resistance $R_p$:** By the Stern–Geary equation: $$R_p = \frac{B}{i_{corr}} = \frac{0.01737 \, V}{100 \times 10^{-6} \, A/cm^2}$$ $$R_p = 173.7 \, \Omega \cdot cm^2$$ **Result:** $R_p \approx 173.7 \, \Omega \cdot cm^2$. **(iii) Corrosion Rate in mpy:** First, calculate **Equivalent Weight**: $$EW = \frac{\text{Atomic Weight}}{n} = \frac{55.85}{2} = 27.93 \, g/eq$$ Apply the corrosion rate formula: $$CR (\text{mpy}) = \frac{0.129 \cdot i_{corr}(\mu A/cm^2) \cdot EW}{\rho}$$ $$CR = \frac{0.129 \times 100 \times 27.93}{7.87} = \frac{360.30}{7.87}$$ $$CR \approx 45.78 \, \text{mpy}$$ **Result:** **Corrosion Rate ≈ 45.8 mpy** (which corresponds to **≈ 1.16 mm/yr**). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Sign convention confusion**: Students often lose marks by mixing anodic and cathodic Tafel signs. Always remember — anodic branch has **positive** slope; cathodic branch has **negative** slope on an E vs. log|i| plot. > 2. **Unit mismatch in $i_{corr}$**: The mpy formula requires $i_{corr}$ in **µA/cm²**, NOT A/cm². Forgetting the $10^6$ factor leads to a wrong answer by six orders of magnitude. > 3. **Forgetting the factor 2.303**: This is the conversion from natural to base-10 logarithm. It MUST appear in the Stern–Geary denominator. > 4. **Insufficient Tafel region**: Examiners penalize extrapolation through curved (non-linear) data. A valid Tafel line must extend over at least **one decade** of current. > 5. **Ignoring scan rate effects**: A scan rate that is too high distorts the polarization curve. Always state a typical value (0.1 – 1 mV/s) and justify it. > 6. **Confusing OCP and $E_{corr}$**: Although numerically equal in theory, OCP includes IR drop. Always confirm $E_{corr}$ from the actual polarization curve inflection. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER - **Tafel equation** is a **logarithmic** form of the Butler–Volmer equation, valid at **high overpotentials** ($|\eta| > 50$ mV). - **Tafel slopes** $\beta_a$ and $\beta_c$ typically range from **0.06 to 0.12 V/decade** and are inversely related to charge-transfer coefficients. - **Tafel extrapolation** = extending the linear anodic and cathodic branches of a polarization curve back to $E_{corr}$ to obtain $i_{corr}$ from their intersection. - The **standard polarization range** is **±250 mV** from $E_{corr}$, as per **ASTM G5 / G59**. - A **three-electrode cell** (Working, Reference, Counter) and a **potentiostat** are mandatory for Tafel experiments. - $i_{corr}$ is converted to **corrosion rate** using **Faraday's law**: $CR = \dfrac{0.129 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho}$ (mpy). - The **Stern–Geary equation** $B = \dfrac{\beta_a \cdot \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)}$ connects Tafel parameters to LPR measurements. - **Equivalent Weight** $EW = \text{Atomic Weight}/n$ — must be calculated correctly for the actual oxidation state of the metal. - Tafel extrapolation is a **destructive technique** (specimen is permanently polarized) — avoid in field applications. - Tafel slopes are **mechanistic fingerprints** — changes in slope indicate changes in the rate-controlling step. - The intersection of anodic and cathodic Tafel lines is the **Mixed Potential Theory** of Evans (1929) in graphical form. - The method **assumes** activation-controlled kinetics (no concentration polarization / mass-transport limitation).

topics.title: Linear polarization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT527 - CORROSION ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Linear polarization # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Linear Polarization Resistance (LPR)** is a rapid, non-destructive electrochemical technique used to measure the instantaneous corrosion rate of a metal in an electrolyte by applying a small DC perturbation (typically **±10 mV to ±30 mV**) around the open-circuit potential (OCP or $E_{corr}$). The slope of the resulting linear potential–current relationship at $E_{corr}$ yields the **polarization resistance ($R_p$)**, which is inversely proportional to the corrosion current density ($i_{corr}$) through the celebrated **Stern–Geary equation**. In the exact KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the Linear Polarization method is described as a "low-signal perturbation technique based on the linearization of the Butler–Volmer equation in the vicinity of the corrosion potential, where the overpotential $\eta$ is small enough ($\eta \ll RT/F$) for the exponential terms to be approximated by their first-order Taylor series expansion." > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (PECHT527 / Module 3):** > Linear Polarization is one of the *four* mandatory electrochemical methods listed in Module 3. It carries the highest direct numerical weight in Part B questions because it explicitly links a measurable quantity ($R_p$) to the kinetic parameters ($i_{corr}$, $\beta_a$, $\beta_c$) of a corroding system. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pedal Feel" of a Car Imagine you are driving a car and you want to know — *without looking at the speedometer* — how fast the engine is being pushed. You press the accelerator pedal **just a tiny bit** (a few millimeters) and feel the car's response: - A **stiff** pedal feel (you press hard, car barely accelerates) → the engine is **highly loaded** (high friction) → **high resistance** to motion. - A **soft** pedal feel (you press lightly, car shoots forward) → the engine is **lightly loaded** (low friction) → **low resistance** to motion. In **linear polarization**, the "pedal" is the **small overpotential $\Delta E$** (typically 10–30 mV) we apply around $E_{corr}$, and the "car's response" is the resulting **current $\Delta i$**. The ratio $\Delta E / \Delta i$ at $E_{corr}$ is the **polarization resistance $R_p$**. A *small* $R_p$ means the metal "gives in" easily to the perturbation → it is corroding **fast**. A *large* $R_p$ means the metal "resists" the perturbation → it is corroding **slowly**. > [!NOTE] > **Why "Linear"?** > The method is called "linear" because, for very small perturbations, the Butler–Volmer equation's exponential curves appear as a **straight line** when plotted as $E$ vs. $i$. The slope of this line is $R_p$. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value / Unit | Standard Reference | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $F$ | Faraday constant | **96,485 C mol⁻¹** | NIST Fundamental Constant | | $R$ | Universal gas constant | **8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹** | NIST Fundamental Constant | | $T$ | Absolute temperature | **298 K** (standard) | IUPAC STP | | $i_{corr}$ | Corrosion current density | **µA cm⁻²** | ASTM G59 | | $R_p$ | Polarization resistance | **Ω cm²** | ASTM G59 | | $B$ | Stern–Geary constant | **0.026 V (active)**, **0.052 V (passive)** | Stern & Geary (1957) | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — From Butler–Volmer to Stern–Geary The complete current–overpotential relationship for a single electrochemical reaction is given by the **Butler–Volmer equation**: $$i = i_0 \left[ \exp\left( \frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT} \right) - \exp\left( -\frac{\alpha_c F \eta}{RT} \right) \right]$$ For a corroding system (mixed anodic and cathodic reactions at $E_{corr}$), the corrosion engineer uses the **empirical form** with Tafel slopes: $$i = i_{corr} \left[ \exp\left( \frac{2.303\,\eta}{\beta_a} \right) - \exp\left( -\frac{2.303\,\eta}{\beta_c} \right) \right]$$ where: - $i_{corr}$ = corrosion current density (A cm⁻²) - $\eta = E - E_{corr}$ = applied overpotential (V) - $\beta_a$ = anodic Tafel slope (V decade⁻¹) - $\beta_c$ = cathodic Tafel slope (V decade⁻¹) ### 2.1.1 The "Small $\eta$" Approximation (The Heart of LPR) When $|\eta| \ll \beta/2.303$ (i.e., $|\eta| < 10$–30 mV), we can apply the **first-order Taylor series**: $$\exp(x) \approx 1 + x$$ Substituting into the Butler–Volmer equation: $$i = i_{corr} \left[ \left( 1 + \frac{2.303\,\eta}{\beta_a} \right) - \left( 1 - \frac{2.303\,\eta}{\beta_c} \right) \right]$$ The "1"s cancel out, leaving: $$i = i_{corr} \cdot 2.303\,\eta \left( \frac{1}{\beta_a} + \frac{1}{\beta_c} \right)$$ ### 2.1.2 Definition of Polarization Resistance By definition, $R_p$ is the slope of the $E$–$i$ curve **at the origin** (i.e., at $E_{corr}$): $$R_p = \left( \frac{d\eta}{di} \right)_{\eta = 0}$$ Taking the reciprocal derivative from the linearized expression: $$R_p = \frac{1}{i_{corr} \cdot 2.303 \left( \frac{1}{\beta_a} + \frac{1}{\beta_c} \right)}$$ ## 2.2 The Stern–Geary Equation (Master Equation) Rearranging the expression above yields the **Stern–Geary equation**, the cornerstone of LPR: $$i_{corr} = \frac{B}{R_p} = \frac{1}{2.303\,R_p} \cdot \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{\beta_a + \beta_c}$$ where the **Stern–Geary constant** $B$ is defined as: $$B = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Typical $B$ values used in industry when Tafel slopes are unknown:** > - $B \approx 0.026$ V → for **active** corroding systems (e.g., carbon steel in HCl) > - $B \approx 0.052$ V → for **passive** systems (e.g., stainless steel in aerated water) > These were proposed by Stern and Weisert (1959) and are accepted ASTM defaults. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Field | Application | | :--- | :--- | | **Oil & Gas** | On-line monitoring of pipeline internal corrosion using LPR probes (e.g., Metal Samples®, Rohrback Cosasco®). | | **Chemical Process Industry** | Real-time inhibitor dosage optimization in cooling water circuits. | | **Reinforced Concrete** | Embedded LPR sensors for assessing rebar corrosion in bridges and marine structures. | | **Coatings Evaluation** | Accelerated corrosion testing of protective paints and polymer coatings. | | **Pharmaceutical / Food** | Corrosion monitoring of stainless steel storage tanks for regulatory compliance. | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorize the following table. Every Part B question in PECHT527 on LPR will require at least 2 of these formulas. | # | Formula | Description | Units | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $i_{corr} = B / R_p$ | **Master Stern–Geary equation** | A cm⁻² | | 2 | $B = \beta_a \beta_c \,/\, [2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)]$ | Stern–Geary constant from Tafel slopes | V | | 3 | $B = 0.026$ V (active) or $0.052$ V (passive) | Default $B$ when Tafel slopes unknown | V | | 4 | $R_p = (\Delta E / \Delta i)_{E \to E_{corr}}$ | Definition of polarization resistance | Ω cm² | | 5 | $CR = 0.00327 \times i_{corr} \times EW / \rho$ | Corrosion rate from $i_{corr}$ | mm yr⁻¹ | | 6 | $\eta = E_{applied} - E_{corr}$ | Definition of overpotential | V | | 7 | Valid range: $\vert \eta \vert \le 30$ mV | LPR validity criterion | mV | | 8 | $2.303 = \ln(10)$ | Logarithm base conversion constant | dimensionless | > **Note on $CR$ formula (5):** Where $EW$ = equivalent weight of metal (g eq⁻¹) and $\rho$ = density (g cm⁻³). This converts $i_{corr}$ from electrochemical units to engineering corrosion rate units. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Stern–Geary Equation **Starting Point:** The two-exponential Butler–Volmer equation for a corroding metal $$i = i_{corr} \left[ \exp\left( \frac{2.303\,\eta}{\beta_a} \right) - \exp\left( -\frac{2.303\,\eta}{\beta_c} \right) \right]$$ **Step 1 — Recognize the limiting condition.** For Linear Polarization, we operate in the regime $\vert \eta \vert \ll \beta/2.303$, which is mathematically equivalent to $\vert 2.303\,\eta / \beta \vert \ll 1$. > *Conversion logic:* The Taylor series for $\exp(x)$ converges rapidly when $\vert x \vert \ll 1$. Keeping only first-order terms is justified when the overpotential is less than ~10 mV for typical $\beta$ values of 0.06–0.12 V. **Step 2 — Apply the Taylor Series expansion.** The first-order Taylor expansion of $\exp(x)$ is: $$\exp(x) \approx 1 + x \quad \text{for } \vert x \vert \ll 1$$ Substitute into the anodic exponential term with $x = 2.303\,\eta / \beta_a$: $$\exp\left( \frac{2.303\,\eta}{\beta_a} \right) \approx 1 + \frac{2.303\,\eta}{\beta_a}$$ Substitute into the cathodic exponential term with $x = -2.303\,\eta / \beta_c$: $$\exp\left( -\frac{2.303\,\eta}{\beta_c} \right) \approx 1 - \frac{2.303\,\eta}{\beta_c}$$ **Step 3 — Insert into the Butler–Volmer equation.** $$i = i_{corr} \left[ \left( 1 + \frac{2.303\,\eta}{\beta_a} \right) - \left( 1 - \frac{2.303\,\eta}{\beta_c} \right) \right]$$ **Step 4 — Simplify algebraically.** The two "1" terms cancel by subtraction: $$i = i_{corr} \left[ \frac{2.303\,\eta}{\beta_a} + \frac{2.303\,\eta}{\beta_c} \right]$$ Factor out $2.303\,\eta$: $$i = i_{corr} \cdot 2.303\,\eta \left( \frac{1}{\beta_a} + \frac{1}{\beta_c} \right)$$ **Step 5 — Combine the fractions inside the parenthesis.** $$\frac{1}{\beta_a} + \frac{1}{\beta_c} = \frac{\beta_c + \beta_a}{\beta_a \beta_c}$$ Therefore: $$i = i_{corr} \cdot 2.303\,\eta \cdot \frac{\beta_a + \beta_c}{\beta_a \beta_c}$$ **Step 6 — Define Polarization Resistance.** $R_p$ is, by definition, the inverse slope of the $\eta$–$i$ curve evaluated at the origin ($\eta = 0$): $$R_p = \left( \frac{d\eta}{di} \right)_{\eta = 0}$$ From the linear equation $i = \eta / R_p$, the slope $di/d\eta = 1/R_p$ is constant. Equating: $$\frac{1}{R_p} = i_{corr} \cdot 2.303 \cdot \frac{\beta_a + \beta_c}{\beta_a \beta_c}$$ **Step 7 — Invert to get the Stern–Geary master equation.** $$\boxed{\,i_{corr} = \frac{1}{2.303\,R_p} \cdot \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{\beta_a + \beta_c} = \frac{B}{R_p}\,}$$ where $$\boxed{\,B = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)}\,}$$ **Derivation complete.** Q.E.D. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style) ### **Problem Statement:** A carbon steel specimen is immersed in 0.5 M H₂SO₄. A linear polarization sweep from −20 mV to +20 mV around $E_{corr}$ yields a polarization resistance $R_p = 220 \;\Omega\cdot\text{cm}^2$. The Tafel slopes (from a separate Tafel plot) are $\beta_a = 0.060$ V/dec and $\beta_c = 0.120$ V/dec. Calculate: 1. The Stern–Geary constant $B$. 2. The corrosion current density $i_{corr}$. 3. The corrosion rate in mm/yr (given $EW = 27.92$ g/eq for Fe, $\rho = 7.87$ g/cm³). ### **Solution (Step-by-Step):** **Part (a): Stern–Geary Constant $B$** Using the Tafel-slope form: $$B = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)}$$ Substitute the given values: $$B = \frac{(0.060)(0.120)}{2.303 \times (0.060 + 0.120)}$$ $$B = \frac{0.00720}{2.303 \times 0.180}$$ $$B = \frac{0.00720}{0.41454}$$ $$\boxed{B = 0.01737 \text{ V}}$$ > *Conversion logic:* Multiply denominator first ($2.303 \times 0.180 = 0.41454$), then divide numerator by denominator. **Part (b): Corrosion Current Density $i_{corr}$** Apply the Stern–Geary master equation: $$i_{corr} = \frac{B}{R_p} = \frac{0.01737 \text{ V}}{220 \;\Omega\cdot\text{cm}^2}$$ $$\boxed{i_{corr} = 7.896 \times 10^{-5} \text{ A cm}^{-2} = 78.96 \;\mu\text{A cm}^{-2}}$$ > *Unit check:* V / (Ω·cm²) = (A·Ω) / (Ω·cm²) = A/cm². ✓ **Part (c): Corrosion Rate in mm/yr** Use the standard conversion formula: $$CR = \frac{0.00327 \times i_{corr} \times EW}{\rho}$$ The constant 0.00327 has units of (mm·g) / (cm·µA·yr·eq) so that the final answer comes out in mm/yr when $i_{corr}$ is in µA/cm². $$CR = \frac{0.00327 \times 78.96 \times 27.92}{7.87}$$ Numerator: $0.00327 \times 78.96 = 0.25820$; then $0.25820 \times 27.92 = 7.209$. $$CR = \frac{7.209}{7.87} = 0.9160 \text{ mm/yr}$$ $$\boxed{CR \approx 0.92 \text{ mm/yr}}$$ > *Engineering interpretation:* This is a high corrosion rate, consistent with carbon steel in an aggressive acid environment (H₂SO₄) without inhibitor. Such a rate would be unacceptable for industrial service. ## 3.3 Validity Conditions Checklist (Examiner-Favored) | Condition | Required Value | Consequence if Violated | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $\vert \eta \vert$ range | $\le 30$ mV (ideally $\le 10$ mV) | Butler–Volmer becomes non-linear; Tafel behavior dominates. | | Steady-state | $i$ must stabilize before reading | Capacitive currents distort $R_p$. | | Uniform corrosion | Single $E_{corr}$ well-defined | Localized corrosion (pitting) gives misleading $R_p$. | | Solution resistance | $R_{soln} \ll R_p$ | Ohmic drop adds error. Use Luggin capillary or iR-compensation. | | No concentration polarization | Adequate stirring / fresh electrolyte | Mass-transport effects shift Tafel slopes. | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — LPR Experimental & Data Flow Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([Start: Clean metal sample in electrolyte]) --> prepA[Mechanical polishing
up to 600 grit SiC] prepA --> mountA[Encapsulate in epoxy
leaving 1 cm² exposed] mountA --> cellA[Mount 3-electrode cell:
Working Electrode WE
Reference Electrode RE
Counter Electrode CE] cellA --> ocpA[Measure Open Circuit Potential
until E stabilizes = E_corr] ocpA --> sweepA[Apply small DC sweep
eta = minus 20 mV to plus 20 mV
step = 0.5 mV per second] sweepA --> rawA[Record current i
at each eta] rawA --> plotA[Plot E versus i
linear region appears near origin] plotA --> slopeA[Compute slope dE/di at E_corr
this equals R_p in Ohm cm squared] slopeA --> branchCheck{Tafel slopes
known?} branchCheck -- Yes --> calcB[Compute B from beta_a and beta_c] branchCheck -- No --> defaultB[Use default B equals 0.026 V active
or 0.052 V passive] calcB --> icorrA[Apply Stern Geary
i_corr equals B divided by R_p] defaultB --> icorrA icorrA --> crA[Convert i_corr to CR in mm per yr
using EW and density] crA --> reportA([Report:
i_corr in microampere per cm squared
CR in mm per year
R_p in Ohm cm squared]) ``` ## 4.2 Three-Electrode Cell Schematic (Text Representation) ``` ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ ELECTROLYTE │ │ │ │ [WE]═══════════╗ │ │ ↑ ║ │ │ │ ║ (current path) │ │ │ ║ │ │ │ ▼ │ │ [CE]═══════════╝ │ │ │ │ [RE]───── (Luggin capillary tip) │ │ ↑ │ │ │ (high-impedance voltmeter) │ └─────┼───────────────────────────────────┘ │ ┌─────┴───────┐ │ POTENTIOSTAT│ │ / GPES │ └─────────────┘ WE = Working Electrode (corroding metal under study) RE = Reference Electrode (SCE / Ag-AgCl / SHE) CE = Counter Electrode (Pt or graphite) ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map — Linearization Logic ```mermaid flowchart LR bvEq((Butler-Volmer
2 exponentials)) --> smallEta{Small eta
less than 30 mV} smallEta --> taylorA[Taylor expand
exp x approx 1 plus x] taylorA --> linEq((Linear i vs eta
single straight line)) linEq --> slopeDef[Slope dE/di
at eta equals 0] slopeDef --> rpDef((R_p defined)) rpDef --> sgEq((Stern-Geary
i_corr equals B over R_p)) tafelA[Tafel slopes
beta_a and beta_c] --> bDef((B constant)) bDef --> sgEq sgEq --> crOutput[Corrosion rate CR
in mm per year] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** The first diagram (4.1) is a *procedural* flowchart showing the order of operations in a real LPR experiment. The second (4.2) is the *physical* electrochemical cell layout. The third (4.3) is the *theoretical* logic chain connecting the Butler–Volmer equation to the final corrosion rate output. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECHT527]` **Define Linear Polarization Resistance. Why is the applied potential range restricted to ±20 mV around $E_{corr}$?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Linear Polarization Resistance (LPR) is an electrochemical technique in which a small DC potential perturbation (typically ±10 mV to ±30 mV) is applied around the open-circuit corrosion potential $E_{corr}$, and the resulting current is measured. The slope $\Delta E / \Delta i$ evaluated at $E_{corr}$ gives the **polarization resistance $R_p$**, which is then related to the corrosion current density via the **Stern–Geary equation**: $$i_{corr} = \frac{B}{R_p}$$ The potential range is restricted to ±20 mV because **the Butler–Volmer equation is only linear when the overpotential is small** ($\vert \eta \vert \ll \beta/2.303$, i.e., $\vert \eta \vert \le 10$–30 mV). Beyond this range, the exponential terms in the Butler–Volmer equation dominate, the $E$–$i$ plot becomes non-linear, and the simple Stern–Geary equation is no longer valid — Tafel analysis must then be used instead. (3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for equation, 1 for justification of ±20 mV limit) --- ### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PECHT527]` **What is the Stern–Geary constant $B$? State its typical values for active and passive corroding systems.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Stern–Geary constant $B$ is the proportionality constant in the equation $i_{corr} = B / R_p$ and is defined as: $$B = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)}$$ where $\beta_a$ and $\beta_c$ are the anodic and cathodic Tafel slopes (V/decade). **Typical values:** - For **active** corroding systems (e.g., carbon steel in acid): $B \approx 0.026$ V - For **passive** systems (e.g., stainless steel in aerated neutral solution): $B \approx 0.052$ V These default values are used when the Tafel slopes have not been independently determined. (3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for typical values, 1 for engineering context) --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in LPR Problems:** > 1. **Forgetting the 2.303 factor** in the Stern–Geary constant — this is the most common error and costs ~2 marks. The 2.303 = ln(10) comes from the base conversion of Tafel slopes (decadic vs natural). > 2. **Unit confusion between $R_p$ and resistance $R$** — $R_p$ must be in Ω·cm² (normalized to electrode area). Bare Ohms gives a 10² or 10⁴ error. > 3. **Using $B = 0.026$ when Tafel slopes are explicitly given** — if slopes are given, you MUST compute $B$ from them. Using a default $B$ here costs full marks for that part. > 4. **Forgetting to convert $i_{corr}$ from A/cm² to µA/cm²** in the corrosion-rate formula — the 0.00327 constant requires $i_{corr}$ in **µA/cm²**. > 5. **Not stating the validity condition** (η ≤ 30 mV) when asked to justify the technique — examiners specifically award marks for this. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, PECHT527]` **(a)** With the help of the Butler–Volmer equation, derive the Stern–Geary equation for a corroding metal. State clearly the assumptions used. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A mild steel electrode of area 1 cm² in 1 M H₂SO₄ gives a polarization resistance $R_p = 150$ Ω at 298 K. The Tafel slopes are $\beta_a = 0.060$ V/dec and $\beta_c = 0.120$ V/dec. Calculate the corrosion current density and the corrosion rate in mm/yr. Take $EW = 27.92$ g/eq and $\rho = 7.87$ g/cm³. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution — Part (a):** **Step 1: Write the Butler–Volmer equation for a corroding system.** For a metal undergoing corrosion, the net current density is the difference between the anodic dissolution current and the cathodic reduction current. Using the empirical Tafel form: $$i = i_{corr} \left[ \exp\left( \frac{2.303\,\eta}{\beta_a} \right) - \exp\left( -\frac{2.303\,\eta}{\beta_c} \right) \right]$$ **Step 2: State the assumption clearly.** For Linear Polarization, we assume that the overpotential $\eta$ is small: $$\vert \eta \vert \ll \frac{\beta}{2.303} \quad \text{(i.e., } \vert \eta \vert \le 30 \text{ mV)}$$ This justifies applying the first-order Taylor expansion $\exp(x) \approx 1 + x$ for $\vert x \vert \ll 1$. **Step 3: Apply the Taylor series.** $$\exp\left( \frac{2.303\,\eta}{\beta_a} \right) \approx 1 + \frac{2.303\,\eta}{\beta_a}$$ $$\exp\left( -\frac{2.303\,\eta}{\beta_c} \right) \approx 1 - \frac{2.303\,\eta}{\beta_c}$$ **Step 4: Substitute into Butler–Volmer and simplify.** $$i = i_{corr} \left[ \left( 1 + \frac{2.303\,\eta}{\beta_a} \right) - \left( 1 - \frac{2.303\,\eta}{\beta_c} \right) \right]$$ $$i = i_{corr} \cdot 2.303\,\eta \left( \frac{1}{\beta_a} + \frac{1}{\beta_c} \right) = i_{corr} \cdot 2.303\,\eta \cdot \frac{\beta_a + \beta_c}{\beta_a \beta_c}$$ **Step 5: Define $R_p$ and rearrange.** $$R_p = \frac{d\eta}{di}\bigg|_{\eta = 0} = \frac{1}{2.303\,i_{corr}} \cdot \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{\beta_a + \beta_c}$$ **Step 6: Final Stern–Geary equation.** $$\boxed{i_{corr} = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303\,R_p(\beta_a + \beta_c)} = \frac{B}{R_p}}$$ > **Assumptions summary (1 mark):** small $\eta$, single uniform corrosion potential, no solution resistance, steady-state, no concentration polarization. --- #### **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Step 1: Compute $B$ from the given Tafel slopes.** $$B = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)} = \frac{(0.060)(0.120)}{2.303 \times (0.060 + 0.120)} = \frac{0.00720}{0.41454}$$ $$B = 0.01737 \text{ V}$$ **Step 2: Compute $i_{corr}$.** $$i_{corr} = \frac{B}{R_p} = \frac{0.01737}{150} = 1.158 \times 10^{-4} \text{ A/cm}^2$$ $$i_{corr} = 115.8 \;\mu\text{A/cm}^2$$ **Step 3: Convert to corrosion rate in mm/yr.** $$CR = \frac{0.00327 \times i_{corr}(\mu\text{A/cm}^2) \times EW}{\rho}$$ $$CR = \frac{0.00327 \times 115.8 \times 27.92}{7.87}$$ Numerator: $0.00327 \times 115.8 = 0.37867$; $\times 27.92 = 10.572$. $$CR = \frac{10.572}{7.87} = 1.343 \text{ mm/yr}$$ $$\boxed{CR \approx 1.34 \text{ mm/yr}}$$ > **Engineering comment (1 mark):** This is an unacceptably high rate — typical inhibitor treatment would be required for industrial service in H₂SO₄ pickling or acid-cleaning operations. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, PECHT527]` **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the experimental setup for measuring polarization resistance. List the validity conditions for the LPR technique. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A stainless steel (SS304) probe in a cooling water circuit is monitored by LPR. The measured $R_p = 4.5 \times 10^4$ Ω·cm² at 298 K. Using the default Stern–Geary constant for passive systems, calculate the corrosion current density and the corrosion rate in mm/yr. Take $EW = 25.12$ g/eq and $\rho = 7.94$ g/cm³. Comment on the corrosion susceptibility. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution — Part (a):** **Experimental Setup (4 marks):** The LPR measurement uses a **three-electrode electrochemical cell** connected to a **potentiostat/Galvanostat** (e.g., Gamry, Autolab, CH Instruments): 1. **Working Electrode (WE):** The corroding metal sample under study, mounted in epoxy with a defined exposed area (typically 1 cm²). 2. **Reference Electrode (RE):** A stable potential reference — usually a Saturated Calomel Electrode (SCE), Ag/AgCl, or a Luggin capillary connected to an external reference. The RE carries negligible current. 3. **Counter Electrode (CE):** An inert conductor (Pt wire/mesh or graphite rod) that completes the circuit and carries the current. 4. **Potentiostat:** Applies the small DC potential $\Delta E = \pm 20$ mV around $E_{corr}$ and measures the resulting current $\Delta i$. 5. **Procedure:** First, monitor the open-circuit potential (OCP) until stable (≤ 1 mV drift per minute). This value is $E_{corr}$. Then apply a small linear potential sweep (e.g., from −20 mV to +20 mV relative to $E_{corr}$) at a slow scan rate (~0.1 mV/s). Plot $E$ vs. $i$. The slope at the origin is $R_p$. ``` Three-Electrode Cell ┌─────────────────────┐ │ Electrolyte │ │ │ │ (WE) (RE) (CE) │ │ │ │ │ │ └────┼─────┼─────┼────┘ │ │ │ └─────┴─────┘ │ ┌─────────────┐ │ Potentiostat│ └─────────────┘ ``` **Validity Conditions (3 marks):** 1. $\vert \eta \vert \le 30$ mV (linear region of Butler–Volmer). 2. Steady-state must be reached (no capacitive currents). 3. Solution resistance $R_{soln}$ must be negligible or compensated. 4. Corrosion must be uniform (no localized pitting, no crevice corrosion). 5. The system must be in electrochemical equilibrium before perturbation. --- #### **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Step 1: Identify the correct $B$ value.** For SS304 in a passive condition (cooling water), use the default: $$B = 0.052 \text{ V}$$ **Step 2: Compute $i_{corr}$.** $$i_{corr} = \frac{B}{R_p} = \frac{0.052}{4.5 \times 10^4} = 1.156 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A/cm}^2$$ $$i_{corr} = 1.156 \;\mu\text{A/cm}^2$$ **Step 3: Compute corrosion rate.** $$CR = \frac{0.00327 \times i_{corr}(\mu\text{A/cm}^2) \times EW}{\rho}$$ $$CR = \frac{0.00327 \times 1.156 \times 25.12}{7.94}$$ Numerator: $0.00327 \times 1.156 = 0.003780$; $\times 25.12 = 0.09497$. $$CR = \frac{0.09497}{7.94} = 0.01196 \text{ mm/yr}$$ $$\boxed{CR \approx 0.012 \text{ mm/yr}}$$ **Step 4: Engineering comment.** A corrosion rate of ~0.012 mm/yr is **very low**, confirming that the SS304 is in a **passive state** in the cooling water. This is well below the typical industrial acceptance limit of 0.1 mm/yr. No corrective action is required, but continued LPR monitoring is recommended to detect any passivity breakdown. (1 mark) --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Linear Polarization (PECHT527 / Module 3)** - [ ] **Definition:** LPR measures the slope of the linear $E$–$i$ plot at $E_{corr}$ to obtain $R_p$ in Ω·cm². - [ ] **Stern–Geary Master Equation:** $i_{corr} = B / R_p$ — memorize this verbatim. - [ ] **$B$ Formula:** $B = \beta_a \beta_c / [2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)]$ — the **2.303 factor is critical** (do not drop it). - [ ] **Default $B$ values:** $0.026$ V (active) and $0.052$ V (passive) — used when Tafel slopes are unknown. - [ ] **Validity window:** $\vert \eta \vert \le 30$ mV (ideally $\le 10$ mV) — outside this range, the linearization fails. - [ ] **Three-electrode setup:** WE (corroding metal), RE (SCE/Ag-AgCl), CE (Pt/inert). - [ ] **Step 1 of any LPR problem:** Compute $B$ from Tafel slopes (or pick the default). - [ ] **Step 2:** Compute $i_{corr} = B / R_p$. Report in µA/cm² (multiply A/cm² by $10^6$). - [ ] **Step 3 (if asked):** Convert to mm/yr using $CR = 0.00327 \times i_{corr}(\mu A/cm^2) \times EW / \rho$. - [ ] **Assumption list:** uniform corrosion, no $iR$ drop, steady-state, single $E_{corr}$. - [ ] **Advantage of LPR:** fast, non-destructive, suitable for on-line industrial monitoring. - [ ] **Disadvantage of LPR:** requires $B$ value; gives *uniform* corrosion rate, misses localized pitting. - [ ] **Key derivation step:** $1 + \beta_c/\beta_a + \beta_a/\beta_c$ — the 1's cancel, leaving only the overpotential-dependent terms. - [ ] **Common examiner trap:** confusing $R_p$ (Ω·cm²) with raw resistance $R$ (Ω) — always normalize to electrode area. - [ ] **Industry standard:** ASTM G59-97 is the standard test method for LPR — mention in viva answers for bonus marks.

topics.title: Galvanostat
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT527 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Galvanostat

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Galvanostat** (also called a *current-control potentiostat* or *constant-current source*) is a precision electronic instrument used in **electrochemistry and corrosion science** that actively forces a user-defined, **constant direct current (DC)** to flow through an electrochemical cell between the **working electrode (WE)** and the **counter electrode (CE)**, while simultaneously monitoring the resulting potential difference between the working electrode and a **reference electrode (RE)**.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context (PECHT527 – Corrosion Engineering), the galvanostat is classified as one of the two principal instrumental techniques of **potentiostatic and galvanostatic polarization**, the other being the **potentiostat**. Whereas a potentiostat fixes the *potential* and measures the resulting *current*, a galvanostat fixes the *current* and measures the resulting *potential*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECHT527 / Module 3):** Galvanostats are essential for conducting **Galvanostatic Polarization Experiments**, determining **corrosion rates from current measurements**, generating **E vs. log i (Tafel) plots**, and performing **cathodic/anodic protection current-density calibrations**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Origin of the Term:** The name *galvanostat* is derived from **Luigi Galvani** (1737–1798), the Italian physician whose experiments on "animal electricity" laid the groundwork for modern electrochemistry, combined with the Greek *statos* (στατός) meaning "standing still" — i.e., a device that holds the current steady.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **domestic water tap connected to a municipal pipeline**. The municipal pressure (analogous to the *electrode potential*, $E$) keeps changing throughout the day — sometimes high, sometimes low. A **galvanostat is like a flow-restrictor or a flow-controller** that you attach to the tap: no matter what the upstream pressure does, exactly **1.5 litres per minute** of water (the *current*, $I$) comes out of the pipe. To verify this, you install a pressure gauge downstream of the controller (the *reference electrode* reading the *potential*). As the upstream pressure changes, your downstream gauge needle moves — and that movement is the **data** you record.

Translating back to electrochemistry:

| Water Analogy | Electrochemical Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Water flow rate (L/min) | Current, $I$ (A) |
| Pressure gauge reading | Potential of working electrode, $E_{WE}$ (V) |
| Adjustable flow-controller knob | Galvanostat set-point (constant $I_{set}$) |
| Pipe resistance variation | Polarization resistance, $R_p$ (Ω) |

Another useful mental model: think of the galvanostat as an **electronic "current pump"** — it is a *current source* (in contrast to a potentiostat, which is a *voltage source*). The simplest implementation uses an **operational amplifier (op-amp)** with feedback to dynamically adjust the compliance voltage applied across the cell, ensuring the current through the standard resistor (sense resistor) — and hence through the cell — remains constant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a constant current?** In corrosion studies, many faradaic processes (metal dissolution, oxygen reduction, hydrogen evolution) are **activation-controlled** and follow exponential (Tafel) kinetics. Holding the current constant and observing how the potential drifts with time gives a direct, sensitive measurement of the **anodic/cathodic Tafel slope**, $\beta_a$ or $\beta_c$.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants Referenced

- **Faraday's constant:** $F = 96{,}485\ \text{C mol}^{-1}$ (**bold**; exact value defined since 2019 SI redefinition)
- **Universal gas constant:** $R = 8.314\ \text{J mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$
- **Typical cell currents in lab corrosion tests:** $i = 10^{-6}\ \text{A cm}^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}\ \text{A cm}^{-2}$
- **Reference electrode potential (SHE):** $E^\circ = 0.000\ \text{V}$ at 25 °C
- **Sense resistor (current-measuring shunt) precision:** typically **0.1 %** tolerance, low temperature coefficient
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step

A modern galvanostat operates as a **closed-loop feedback control system** built around one or more operational amplifiers. The fundamental control logic is as follows:

1. **User sets a reference current** $I_{set}$ via a precision voltage source $V_{ref}$ across a known sense resistor $R_s$, producing $I_{set} = V_{ref}/R_s$.
2. The op-amp continuously monitors the actual current through the cell using a **current-to-voltage (I-V) transducer** (which may simply be $R_s$).
3. The op-amp output (compliance voltage) is dynamically increased or decreased so that the **difference (error signal)** between $I_{set}$ and $I_{actual}$ is driven to zero.
4. The cell potential $E_{cell}$ is monitored using a high-impedance differential amplifier connected to the reference electrode.
5. Data acquisition (analog-to-digital converter, ADC) records $E_{cell}$ vs. time, producing the characteristic **galvanostatic polarization curve**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **three-electrode configuration** (WE, CE, RE) is mandatory for accurate measurement. The current flows between WE and CE, while the RE is used purely for potential sensing (no current flows through it). This is what distinguishes a true galvanostat from a simple two-terminal constant-current supply.

## 2.2 Circuit Topology — Op-Amp Realisation

The classic implementation uses an **inverting summing amplifier** topology:

- The reference voltage $V_{ref}$ is applied to the non-inverting input.
- The voltage across the sense resistor $R_s$ (which is in series with the cell) is fed back to the inverting input.
- The op-amp adjusts its output until $V_{feedback} = V_{ref}$, enforcing $I_{cell} = V_{ref}/R_s$.

For high-current applications (e.g., cathodic protection of pipelines where currents exceed **10 A**), a **booster stage** using power transistors or MOSFETs is added between the op-amp and the cell.

## 2.3 Governing Equations in Galvanostatic Polarization

When a constant current density $i$ is imposed on a corroding metal, the electrode potential $E$ drifts according to the **Butler-Volmer kinetics**:

$$i = i_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{E - E_{corr}}{\beta_a}\right) - \exp\left(-\frac{E - E_{corr}}{\beta_c}\right) \right]$$

In the **Tafel regions** (high anodic or cathodic overpotentials), one exponential term dominates, and we obtain:

$$\eta = E - E_{corr} = \pm \beta \log_{10}\left(\frac{i}{i_0}\right)$$

where the **Tafel slope** $\beta$ has units of **V/decade** (typically $0.03$ to $0.12$ V/dec for most metal/electrolyte systems).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_{set} = V_{ref} / R_s$ | Set current | A | Galvanostat set-point |
| 2 | $i = I / A$ | Current density | A cm⁻² | Normalising to electrode area |
| 3 | $\eta = E - E_{corr}$ | Overpotential | V (or mV) | Polarization shift from $E_{corr}$ |
| 4 | $\eta_a = \beta_a \log_{10}(i / i_0)$ | Anodic Tafel | V | Anodic branch of Tafel plot |
| 5 | $\eta_c = -\beta_c \log_{10}(\vert i \vert / i_0)$ | Cathodic Tafel | V | Cathodic branch of Tafel plot |
| 6 | $i_{corr} = (i_a \cdot i_c)^{1/2}$ at $E_{corr}$ | Corrosion current density | A cm⁻² | Extrapolation of Tafel lines |
| 7 | $CR = (i_{corr} \cdot M) / (n \cdot F \cdot \rho)$ | Corrosion rate | mm yr⁻¹ | Practical corrosion rate |
| 8 | $R_p = \Delta E / \Delta i$ | Polarisation resistance | Ω cm² | Linear polarisation resistance method |
| 9 | $B = \beta_a \beta_c / (2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c))$ | Stern-Geary constant | V | Used in $i_{corr} = B / R_p$ |
| 10 | $E_{cell} = E_c - E_{ref} \pm iR_{soln}$ | Cell potential | V | Ohmic (IR) drop correction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** Galvanostats are indispensable in determining $i_{corr}$ (and hence corrosion rate) using the **Tafel extrapolation method**, in calibrating **cathodic protection rectifier outputs**, in performing **chronopotentiometry** (potential vs. time at constant $i$), and in conducting **galvanostatic pulse experiments** for evaluating protective coatings.

## 2.5 Why Galvanostatic? — Engineering Justification

- **Simplicity of control:** Current is easier to measure and regulate precisely than potential at very low values.
- **Compliance with high-impedance electrolytes:** The op-amp can drive the cell voltage up to ±**10 V to ± 30 V** ("compliance voltage") to maintain the set current.
- **Direct Tafel data:** Constant $i$ produces a stable overpotential, ideal for steady-state measurements.
- **Coatings and inhibitors:** Galvanostatic step tests rapidly identify breakdown potentials of protective films.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Op-Amp Action in a Galvanostat

We model the op-amp with finite open-loop gain $A_{OL}$ and analyse how accurately it forces the cell current to match $I_{set}$.

Let:
- $V_{ref}$ = user-set reference voltage (input to the non-inverting terminal)
- $V_{fb}$ = feedback voltage at the inverting terminal (proportional to actual cell current)
- $V_{out}$ = op-amp output (the compliance voltage applied across the cell + sense resistor)
- $A_{OL}$ = open-loop DC gain (typically $10^5$ to $10^6$ for precision op-amps)

The op-amp equation is:

$$V_{out} = A_{OL} \left( V_{ref} - V_{fb} \right)$$

In the ideal limit, $A_{OL} \to \infty$, so to keep $V_{out}$ finite, the term in parentheses must tend to zero:

$$V_{ref} - V_{fb} \to 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad V_{fb} \to V_{ref}$$

But $V_{fb} = I_{cell} \cdot R_s$, therefore:

$$I_{cell} \cdot R_s = V_{ref} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad I_{cell} = \frac{V_{ref}}{R_s}$$

This is the **fundamental governing equation of the galvanostat**: the cell current is locked to the ratio of the reference voltage to the sense resistance.

For a **finite** $A_{OL}$, the residual error is:

$$I_{cell} = \frac{V_{ref}}{R_s} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{1}{A_{OL}\beta}}$$

where $\beta$ is the feedback network factor. For large $A_{OL}\beta$, the error is negligible (parts per million).

> **Conversion logic:** The derivation rests on the **virtual short** principle of an op-amp with negative feedback: the two input terminals are held at the same potential by the action of high gain. This is the textbook analysis expected in the KTU board exam.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — From Butler-Volmer to Tafel Equation (Tafel Approximation)

Starting from the Butler-Volmer equation written in terms of current density $i$:

$$i = i_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\left(-\frac{\alpha_c F \eta}{RT}\right) \right]$$

where $\alpha_a$ and $\alpha_c$ are the **charge-transfer coefficients** (anodic and cathodic), and $\eta = E - E_{corr}$ is the overpotential.

**Step 1:** Define the Tafel slopes:

$$\beta_a = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha_a F} \quad \text{and} \quad \beta_c = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha_c F}$$

**Step 2:** At large positive $\eta$ (anodic Tafel region), the second exponential becomes negligible, so:

$$i \approx i_0 \exp\left(\frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Take the base-10 logarithm of both sides:

$$\log_{10} i = \log_{10} i_0 + \frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{2.303 \, RT}$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange for $\eta$:

$$\eta = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha_a F} \log_{10}\left(\frac{i}{i_0}\right) = \beta_a \log_{10}\left(\frac{i}{i_0}\right)$$

This is the **anodic Tafel equation**, and by symmetry the cathodic form is:

$$\eta = -\beta_c \log_{10}\left(\frac{\vert i \vert}{i_0}\right)$$

> **Conversion logic:** This derivation is **high-yield** for KTU 14-mark questions on Tafel plots. Board examiners specifically look for the $2.303$ conversion between natural and base-10 logarithms.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Tafel Extrapolation to Find $i_{corr}$

A galvanostat is used to step the current density through several decades, and $E$ is recorded at each step. The data are plotted as $E$ (y-axis, linear) vs. $\log_{10} i$ (x-axis).

**Anodic branch** (at $i \gg i_{corr}$):

$$E = E_{corr} + \beta_a \log_{10}\left(\frac{i}{i_0}\right)$$

**Cathodic branch** (at $\vert i \vert \gg i_{corr}$):

$$E = E_{corr} - \beta_c \log_{10}\left(\frac{\vert i \vert}{i_0}\right)$$

Both branches are **straight lines** in the semi-log plot. Extrapolate the anodic line backwards (decreasing $i$) and the cathodic line forwards until they intersect. The intersection lies at the **corrosion point**:

$$E = E_{corr}, \quad i = i_{corr}$$

**Step 1:** Set the two expressions for $E$ equal:

$$E_{corr} + \beta_a \log_{10}\left(\frac{i_{corr}}{i_0}\right) = E_{corr} - \beta_c \log_{10}\left(\frac{i_{corr}}{i_0}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Cancel $E_{corr}$:

$$\beta_a \log_{10}\left(\frac{i_{corr}}{i_0}\right) = -\beta_c \log_{10}\left(\frac{i_{corr}}{i_0}\right)$$

**Step 3:** The only way for $\beta_a \neq \beta_c$ to satisfy this is:

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{i_{corr}}{i_0}\right) = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad i_{corr} = i_0$$

> **Conversion logic:** The exchange current density $i_0$ is geometrically defined as the current density at which the anodic and cathodic branches of the polarisation curve extrapolate to $E_{corr}$. In practice, board examiners accept the graphical intersection as $i_{corr}$.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Stern–Geary Equation (Linear Polarisation Resistance Bridge)

Although not exclusively a galvanostat result, the **Stern–Geary equation** is the most commonly used outcome of low-current-density polarisation, where the galvanostat is operated in the **± 10 mV regime** about $E_{corr}$.

Starting from Butler-Volmer and Taylor-expanding for small $\eta$:

$$i = i_0 \left[ \left(1 + \frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}\right) - \left(1 - \frac{\alpha_c F \eta}{RT}\right) \right] = i_0 \frac{(\alpha_a + \alpha_c) F \eta}{RT}$$

Define:

$$B = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)}$$

Then:

$$i_{corr} = \frac{B}{R_p}, \quad \text{where} \quad R_p = \left(\frac{dE}{di}\right)_{E=E_{corr}}$$

This is the **Stern–Geary equation**, and the galvanostat (or potentiostat) is used to measure $R_p$ as the slope of the linear $E$–$i$ plot near $E_{corr}$.

> **Conversion logic:** $R_p$ has units of $\Omega \cdot \text{cm}^2$ when $i$ is in A cm⁻² and $E$ is in V. The constant $B$ typically lies in the range **0.013 V to 0.052 V** for most metal/electrolyte systems.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A galvanostat is set to deliver $I = 500\ \mu\text{A}$ through a mild-steel working electrode of area $A = 1\ \text{cm}^2$ immersed in aerated 3 % NaCl. The measured steady-state potential shifts from $E_{corr} = -0.450\ \text{V (SCE)}$ to $E = -0.392\ \text{V (SCE)}$ (anodic). The anodic Tafel slope is $\beta_a = 0.060\ \text{V/dec}$. Compute the **exchange current density** $i_0$.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Current density:

$$i = \frac{I}{A} = \frac{500 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{A}}{1\ \text{cm}^2} = 5.0 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{A cm}^{-2}$$

Step 2: Overpotential:

$$\eta = E - E_{corr} = -0.392 - (-0.450) = +0.058\ \text{V}$$

Step 3: Apply anodic Tafel:

$$\eta = \beta_a \log_{10}\left(\frac{i}{i_0}\right) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad 0.058 = 0.060 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{5.0 \times 10^{-4}}{i_0}\right)$$

Step 4: Isolate the logarithm:

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{5.0 \times 10^{-4}}{i_0}\right) = \frac{0.058}{0.060} = 0.9667$$

Step 5: Antilog:

$$\frac{5.0 \times 10^{-4}}{i_0} = 10^{0.9667} = 9.27$$

Step 6: Solve for $i_0$:

$$i_0 = \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-4}}{9.27} = 5.39 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{A cm}^{-2}$$

> **Answer:** $i_0 \approx 5.4 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{A cm}^{-2}$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of a Galvanostat

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CTRL[Control Section]
        VREF[Precision Vref Source]
        OPAMP[Operational Amplifier]
        BOOST[Power Booster Stage]
    end
    subgraph SENSE[Current Sense]
        RSENSE[Sense Resistor Rs]
        IVCONV[I to V Transducer]
    end
    subgraph CELL[Three Electrode Cell]
        WE[Working Electrode]
        RE[Reference Electrode]
        CE[Counter Electrode]
        ELEC[Electrolyte]
    end
    subgraph DAQ[Data Acquisition]
        DIFFAMP[High Z Differential Amplifier]
        ADC[Analog to Digital Converter]
        REC[Recorder and PC Display]
    end

    VREF -- reference voltage --> OPAMP
    OPAMP -- error amplified --> BOOST
    BOOST -- compliance voltage --> CE
    CE -- counter electrode terminal --> CELL
    CELL -- through electrolyte --> WE
    WE -- returns to sense --> RSENSE
    RSENSE -- Vfb feedback --> OPAMP
    RSENSE -- measured drop --> IVCONV
    RE -- potential sensing only --> DIFFAMP
    WE -- potential sensing only --> DIFFAMP
    DIFFAMP -- cell potential --> ADC
    ADC -- digital E vs t --> REC
    IVCONV -- logged current --> REC
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Galvanostatic Polarization Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start at Ecorr and i equals 0] --> B[Apply small cathodic current density i1]
    B --> C[Record steady state potential E1]
    C --> D[Increase i to i2, i3, ... decade by decade]
    D --> E[Record E2, E3, ...]
    E --> F[Reverse polarity, apply anodic i1, i2, i3]
    F --> G[Record anodic E1, E2, E3]
    G --> H[Plot E vs log10 of i on semi log axes]
    H --> I[Identify anodic Tafel linear region]
    H --> J[Identify cathodic Tafel linear region]
    I --> K[Extrapolate anodic line to Ecorr]
    J --> L[Extrapolate cathodic line to Ecorr]
    K --> M[Intersection gives icorr and Ecorr]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Compute corrosion rate using Faraday law]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Comparison: Galvanostat vs. Potentiostat

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GALV[Galvanostat] -- controls --> ICUR[Current I = constant]
    GALV -- measures --> EPOT[Potential E = variable]
    POT[Potentiostat] -- controls --> EPOT2[Potential E = constant]
    POT -- measures --> ICUR2[Current I = variable]
    ICUR -.-> APPA[Application: Tafel extrapolation]
    EPOT -.-> APPB[Application: Polarization resistance]
    EPOT2 -.-> APPC[Application: Passive film breakdown potential]
    ICUR2 -.-> APPD[Application: Coating porosity evaluation]
```

## 4.4 ASCII Schematic — Equivalent Circuit

```
                 +Vcc
                  |
                  |
            +-----+-----+
            |  BOOST   |
  Vref --->|+ \        |
            |  >---+----+---- to COUNTER electrode (CE)
   Vfb <---|+ /   |    |
            +-----+    |
                  |    |
                  R    |
                  s    |
                  |    |
                  +----+---- to WORKING electrode (WE)
                  |
                  |    (Reference electrode, RE, is connected
                  +---- only to the differential voltmeter,
                       no current flows)
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a galvanostat. Mention its primary role in corrosion studies.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A galvanostat is an electronic instrument that maintains a **constant direct current** through an electrochemical cell between the working and counter electrodes, while the resulting potential of the working electrode is measured against a reference electrode.
**Primary role in corrosion studies (1 mark):** It is used to perform **galvanostatic polarization experiments** for determining corrosion current density ($i_{corr}$), Tafel slopes ($\beta_a, \beta_c$), and corrosion rates by the Tafel extrapolation method.
**Working principle (1 mark):** The instrument uses an op-amp feedback loop that forces the voltage across a sense resistor to equal a reference voltage, locking the cell current to $I = V_{ref}/R_s$.

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between a galvanostat and a potentiostat.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Galvanostat | Potentiostat |
|---|---|---|
| **Controlled variable** | Current ($I$ = constant) | Potential ($E$ = constant) |
| **Measured variable** | Potential ($E$ vs. time) | Current ($I$ vs. time) |
| **Output** | Variable compliance voltage | Variable cell current |
| **Typical use** | Tafel extrapolation, $i_{corr}$ | Polarization resistance, cyclic voltammetry |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of an **operational-amplifier-based galvanostat**. Discuss the role of the sense resistor and the reference voltage.

**(b) [7 marks]** A galvanostatic polarization experiment on **aluminium in 3.5 % NaCl** gives the following anodic data:

| Current density $i$ (A cm⁻²) | Potential $E$ (V vs. SCE) |
|---|---|
| $1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ | $-0.742$ |
| $1.0 \times 10^{-5}$ | $-0.715$ |
| $1.0 \times 10^{-4}$ | $-0.683$ |
| $1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ | $-0.650$ |

$E_{corr} = -0.760\ \text{V (SCE)}$. Determine the **anodic Tafel slope** $\beta_a$ and the **exchange current density** $i_0$.

#### Model Solution for (a)

1. **Block diagram and description (3 marks):** A galvanostat consists of (i) a precision reference-voltage source, (ii) an op-amp with feedback, (iii) a sense resistor $R_s$ in series with the cell, (iv) a power booster for high-current applications, and (v) a high-impedance differential amplifier connected to the reference electrode for potential measurement.
2. **Op-amp action (2 marks):** The op-amp adjusts its output voltage (compliance voltage) so that the voltage across the sense resistor equals $V_{ref}$. By Ohm's law, this locks the current: $I_{cell} = V_{ref}/R_s$.
3. **Role of $R_s$ and $V_{ref}$ (2 marks):** $R_s$ converts the cell current to a measurable feedback voltage. The pair $(V_{ref}, R_s)$ defines the set-point current. Precision of $R_s$ directly determines accuracy of $I_{set}$.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1:** Compute $\eta = E - E_{corr}$ for each point:

| $i$ (A cm⁻²) | $\log_{10} i$ | $E$ (V) | $\eta$ (V) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ | $-6.0$ | $-0.742$ | $+0.018$ |
| $1.0 \times 10^{-5}$ | $-5.0$ | $-0.715$ | $+0.045$ |
| $1.0 \times 10^{-4}$ | $-4.0$ | $-0.683$ | $+0.077$ |
| $1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ | $-3.0$ | $-0.650$ | $+0.110$ |

**Step 2:** Apply the Tafel equation: $\eta = \beta_a (\log_{10} i - \log_{10} i_0)$.

**Step 3:** Slope of $\eta$ vs. $\log_{10} i$ (using extreme points):

$$\beta_a = \frac{\Delta \eta}{\Delta \log_{10} i} = \frac{0.110 - 0.018}{-3.0 - (-6.0)} = \frac{0.092}{3.0} = 0.0307\ \text{V/dec}$$

**Step 4:** Use any data point to find $i_0$:

$$0.110 = 0.0307 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{i_0}\right)$$

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{i_0}\right) = \frac{0.110}{0.0307} = 3.583$$

$$\frac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{i_0} = 10^{3.583} = 3.83 \times 10^{3}$$

$$i_0 = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{3.83 \times 10^{3}} = 2.61 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{A cm}^{-2}$$

**Answer:** $\beta_a \approx 0.031\ \text{V/dec}$ and $i_0 \approx 2.6 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{A cm}^{-2}$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the **Tafel equation** from the Butler-Volmer equation, stating clearly the conditions under which the Tafel approximation is valid.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **Tafel extrapolation method** for determining corrosion current density using a galvanostat. A specimen of mild steel (atomic mass $M = 55.85\ \text{g mol}^{-1}$, density $\rho = 7.85\ \text{g cm}^{-3}$, $n = 2$) shows $i_{corr} = 8.5\ \mu\text{A cm}^{-2}$. Calculate the corrosion rate in **mm yr⁻¹**.

#### Model Solution for (a)

1. State the Butler-Volmer equation (2 marks):

$$i = i_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\left(-\frac{\alpha_c F \eta}{RT}\right) \right]$$

2. State the Tafel condition (1 mark): at $|\eta| \gg RT/F$ (typically $|\eta| > 50$ mV), one exponential term dominates.
3. Show the simplification (2 marks): drop the smaller term and solve for $\eta$, leading to:

$$\eta = \pm \beta \log_{10}\left(\frac{i}{i_0}\right), \quad \beta = \frac{2.303 RT}{\alpha F}$$

4. Physical meaning (2 marks): $\beta$ is the **decadic Tafel slope** in V/dec; a smaller $\beta$ implies a sharper polarisation response, characteristic of systems with low charge-transfer resistance.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Method description (3 marks):** The galvanostat is used to step the current density $i$ in decade increments, and the corresponding steady-state potential $E$ is recorded. The plot of $E$ vs. $\log_{10} i$ exhibits two linear regions — the anodic Tafel branch (above $E_{corr}$) and the cathodic Tafel branch (below $E_{corr}$). Extrapolation of these straight lines to the corrosion potential $E_{corr}$ gives $i_{corr}$ at the intersection.

**Numerical calculation (4 marks):** Faraday's law for corrosion rate:

$$CR = \frac{i_{corr} \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

Convert units carefully:

- $i_{corr} = 8.5 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{A cm}^{-2} = 8.5 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{C s}^{-1}\text{cm}^{-2}$
- Seconds per year: $365.25 \times 24 \times 3600 = 3.156 \times 10^{7}\ \text{s}$

Compute the numerator:

$$i_{corr} \cdot M = (8.5 \times 10^{-6}) \times 55.85 = 4.747 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{g C}^{-1}\text{cm}^{-2}$$

Compute the denominator:

$$n \cdot F \cdot \rho = 2 \times 96485 \times 7.85 = 1.515 \times 10^{6}\ \text{C g cm}^{-3}$$

Rate in cm s⁻¹:

$$CR = \frac{4.747 \times 10^{-4}}{1.515 \times 10^{6}} = 3.13 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{cm s}^{-1}$$

Convert to mm yr⁻¹:

$$CR = 3.13 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{cm s}^{-1} \times 3.156 \times 10^{7}\ \text{s yr}^{-1} \times 10\ \text{mm cm}^{-1}$$

$$CR = 0.0988\ \text{mm yr}^{-1} \approx 0.10\ \text{mm yr}^{-1}$$

**Answer:** Corrosion rate $\approx 0.10\ \text{mm yr}^{-1}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversion traps:** Students frequently lose 2–3 marks by writing $i_{corr}$ in $\mu\text{A cm}^{-2}$ without converting to $\text{C s}^{-1}\text{cm}^{-2}$. Remember $1\ \mu\text{A} = 10^{-6}\ \text{C/s}$.
> 2. **Confusing $E$ with $\eta$:** $E$ is the absolute measured potential; $\eta$ is the deviation from $E_{corr}$. The Tafel equation uses $\eta$, not $E$.
> 3. **Sign conventions:** Cathodic current is negative; overpotential is positive for anodic, negative for cathodic.
> 4. **The $2.303$ factor:** Failing to convert from $\ln$ to $\log_{10}$ is the single most common error in Tafel derivations — examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 5. **Forgetting $\rho$:** The corrosion rate formula requires density $\rho$, not just molar mass $M$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision checklist for "Galvanostat" (PECHT527 / Module 3):**

- ✅ A **galvanostat** is a *current-source* instrument; a **potentiostat** is a *voltage-source* instrument.
- ✅ Core equation: $I_{cell} = V_{ref}/R_s$, enforced by op-amp feedback.
- ✅ The **three-electrode** configuration (WE, CE, RE) is mandatory; current flows between WE and CE; RE is a *voltage probe only*.
- ✅ **Tafel equation** (anodic): $\eta_a = \beta_a \log_{10}(i/i_0)$, with $\beta_a = 2.303 RT / (\alpha_a F)$.
- ✅ **Tafel approximation** is valid for $|\eta| > 50$ mV (one exponential term dominates).
- ✅ **Tafel extrapolation** gives $i_{corr}$ at the intersection of anodic and cathodic Tafel lines; the corresponding potential is $E_{corr}$.
- ✅ **Stern–Geary equation:** $i_{corr} = B/R_p$ with $B = \beta_a \beta_c / [2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)]$ — used with **linear polarisation resistance (LPR)**.
- ✅ **Corrosion rate (mm/yr)** from Faraday: $CR = i_{corr} M / (n F \rho)$. Convert $i_{corr}$ to $\text{C s}^{-1}$ and time to seconds per year ($3.156 \times 10^7$).
- ✅ **Compliance voltage** is the maximum cell voltage the galvanostat can deliver — typically ±10 to ±30 V; high-resistance electrolytes demand higher compliance.
- ✅ **Sense resistor** tolerance (typically **0.1 %**) directly determines set-current accuracy.
- ✅ Typical Tafel slope range: **0.030 to 0.120 V/dec** for most metal/electrolyte systems.
- ✅ **Galvanostatic** techniques include chronopotentiometry, Tafel extrapolation, and constant-current cathodic-protection calibration.
- ✅ **Source of error:** Ohmic (IR) drop in solution — minimise by Luggin capillary, or compensate electronically.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Impedance spectroscopy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT527 - CORROSION ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Impedance spectroscopy # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS)** — also called **AC Impedance Spectroscopy** — is a non-destructive, perturbative analytical technique used to characterize the electrical properties of electrochemical systems (corroding metals, coatings, electrolytes) by applying a small-amplitude sinusoidal voltage (or current) perturbation over a wide range of frequencies and measuring the resulting current (or voltage) response. The **impedance** $Z(\omega)$ is the frequency-dependent complex analogue of resistance, defined by **Ohm's Law for AC circuits**: $$Z(\omega) = \frac{V(t)}{I(t)} = \frac{V_0 \sin(\omega t)}{I_0 \sin(\omega t + \phi)}$$ where $V_0$ and $I_0$ are the **perturbation amplitudes**, $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the **angular frequency** (in **rad/s**), $t$ is time, and $\phi$ is the **phase shift** between the voltage and current signals. The measurement is repeated at typically **5 to 10 frequency points per decade** across a span of $10^{-3}$ Hz to $10^{6}$ Hz, yielding a complete **impedance spectrum** that is then fitted to an **equivalent electrical circuit** to extract physicochemical parameters of the corroding interface. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 3 of *Corrosion Engineering*, EIS is treated as an **electrochemical monitoring technique** alongside Tafel extrapolation and Linear Polarization Resistance (LPR). Students must be able to interpret a **Nyquist plot** and a **Bode plot**, and explain how the **Randles equivalent circuit** models a corroding metal–electrolyte interface. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are **pushing a child on a swing**: - If you push *in time* with the swing's motion (low frequency), energy transfers efficiently — the swing goes high. This is like a **resistor** in DC, where voltage and current are in phase. - If you push *out of sync* (high frequency), the swing resists and absorbs energy sluggishly.
