This is like a **capacitor**, which stores charge and causes a phase lag. - The swing itself is a combination of the **rope (inductance)**, **air drag (resistance)**, and **gravity (capacitance)**. An EIS instrument does the same thing electrically — it probes a corroding metal by sending in AC signals of many different frequencies and watches how the interface "swings back." > [!TIP] > **The Three Intuitions to Lock In:** > 1. **Resistance (R)** → real, in-phase, energy-dissipating element (think: friction). > 2. **Capacitance (C)** → imaginary, out-of-phase by $-90°$, energy-storing element (think: spring). > 3. **Inductance (L)** → imaginary, out-of-phase by $+90°$, energy-storing element (think: inertia). ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Faraday's Constant**: $F = 96485$ **C mol⁻¹** - **Universal Gas Constant**: $R = 8.314$ **J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹** - **Double-Layer Capacitance** of a typical metal/aqueous interface: **10 – 50 µF cm⁻²** - **Standard AC perturbation amplitude**: **5 – 10 mV (rms)** around the open-circuit potential — small enough to keep the system linear, per the **Kramers-Kronig causality test**. - **Frequency range**: **10⁵ Hz → 10⁻³ Hz** (covering both the electrolyte response and the slow faradaic corrosion processes). --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Physical Origin of Impedance at a Corroding Interface When a metal M corrodes in an acid, the interface is best described as a **parallel combination** of: 1. A **faradaic pathway** — electrons crossing the metal/electrolyte boundary (the actual corrosion reaction). Modeled as a **charge-transfer resistance** $R_{ct}$ in series with a **Warburg diffusion impedance** $Z_W$. 2. A **non-faradaic pathway** — charging and discharging of the **electrical double layer** at the interface, modeled as a **double-layer capacitance** $C_{dl}$. Between the working electrode surface and the bulk electrolyte lies the **uncompensated solution resistance** $R_s$, which is in **series** with the parallel combination above. ## 2.2 The Three Passive Elements under AC Excitation For each ideal circuit element subjected to $V(t) = V_0 e^{j\omega t}$: | Element | Governing Law | Complex Impedance $Z$ | Phase Angle $\phi$ | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---|---|---| | Resistor (R) | $V = IR$ | $Z_R = R$ (purely real) | $0°$ | Ohmic drop in solution / film | | Capacitor (C) | $I = C\,\dfrac{dV}{dt}$ | $Z_C = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C} = -\dfrac{j}{\omega C}$ | $-90°$ | Double-layer charging | | Inductor (L) | $V = L\,\dfrac{dI}{dt}$ | $Z_L = j\omega L$ | $+90°$ | Adsorption pseudo-inductance (rare in corrosion) | > [!NOTE] > **Why $j$?** Engineers use $j = \sqrt{-1}$ (instead of $i$) to avoid confusion with the symbol for current. The complex plane is therefore split into: > - **Real axis ($Z'$)** → resistive component. > - **Imaginary axis ($Z''$)** → reactive (capacitive/inductive) component. ## 2.3 Deconstructing a Complex Impedance Any measured impedance is written in **Cartesian** form as: $$Z(\omega) = Z'(\omega) - j Z''(\omega)$$ and equivalently in **polar** form as: $$Z(\omega) = \vert Z(\omega) \vert \, e^{-j\phi(\omega)}$$ where the **modulus** is: $$\vert Z(\omega) \vert = \sqrt{\,Z'(\omega)^{2} + Z''(\omega)^{2}\,}$$ and the **phase angle** is: $$\phi(\omega) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{Z''(\omega)}{Z'(\omega)}\right)$$ ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations for Part A and Part B. They are the only ones you will be asked to manipulate.** | # | Equation | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Z = V/I = V_0 e^{j\omega t} / I_0 e^{j(\omega t - \phi)}$ | $V$ in V, $I$ in A, $\phi$ in rad | Definition of AC impedance | | 2 | $Z_C = 1/(j\omega C)$ | $C$ in F, $\omega$ in rad/s | Capacitor's frequency response | | 3 | $Z_W = \sigma (1-j)/\sqrt{\omega} = \sigma\omega^{-1/2}(1-j)$ | $\sigma$ = Warburg coeff. ($\Omega\,\text{s}^{-1/2}$) | Semi-infinite diffusion (Randles) | | 4 | $R_{ct} = RT / (n F i_{corr})$ | $R_{ct}$ in $\Omega$, $i_{corr}$ in A/cm² | **Stern–Geary link** — corrosion rate from EIS | | 5 | $C_{dl} = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A / d$ | $A$ in m², $d$ in m | Helmholtz double-layer capacitance | | 6 | $\tau = R_{ct}\,C_{dl}$ | Time constant in s | Characteristic relaxation time | | 7 | $\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{Z'^2 + Z''^2}$ | Dimensionless ratio | Modulus from Cartesian | | 8 | $\phi = \arctan(Z''/Z')$ | Phase in degrees or rad | Phase from Cartesian | | 9 | CPE: $Z_{CPE} = 1/[Q(j\omega)^n]$ | $Q$ in F·s$^{n-1}$, $0 \le n \le 1$ | Non-ideal capacitance (rough electrodes) | | 10 | $f_{top} = 1/(2\pi R_{ct} C_{dl})$ | $f_{top}$ in Hz | Frequency at semicircle apex on Nyquist | ## 2.5 The Randles Equivalent Circuit — The Heart of Corrosion-EIS > [!NOTE] > **KTU examiners expect you to draw and label the Randles circuit and identify what each component represents physically.** A typical drawing is shown in Section 4. The **Randles cell** models a corroding metal undergoing activation-controlled dissolution plus diffusion of the reactive species: - $R_s$ — **Electrolyte (solution) resistance** between the reference electrode probe tip and the working electrode. - $C_{dl}$ — **Double-layer capacitance** at the metal/electrolyte interface. - $R_{ct}$ — **Charge-transfer resistance** — the direct measure of corrosion resistance (larger $R_{ct}$ → slower corrosion rate). - $Z_W$ — **Warburg impedance** — accounts for diffusion-limited mass transport of reactants/products. The total impedance of the Randles circuit is: $$Z_{Randles}(\omega) = R_s + \frac{R_{ct}}{1 + (j\omega R_{ct} C_{dl})}$$ > [!TIP] > **For KTU derivations**: When the Warburg element is **omitted** (pure activation control, no diffusion limit), the Nyquist plot becomes a **perfect semicircle** of diameter $R_{ct}$ centered on the real axis at $R_s + R_{ct}/2$. ## 2.6 From $R_{ct}$ to Corrosion Rate — The Stern–Geary Bridge The single most useful outcome of an EIS experiment in corrosion engineering is the **polarization resistance** $R_p$, obtained from the low-frequency limit of the impedance: $$R_p = \lim_{\omega \to 0} \left[ Z'(\omega) - R_s \right] \approx R_{ct}$$ The **Stern–Geary equation** then converts this to a corrosion current density: $$i_{corr} = \frac{B}{R_p} = \frac{1}{R_p}\,\frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)}$$ where $B$ is the **Stern–Geary constant** (typically **25 – 52 mV** for actively corroding systems). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** Unlike weight-loss tests (which take days), EIS gives $i_{corr}$ in **minutes**, and because it is non-perturbative, the same sample can be monitored over time to study **coating degradation**, **inhibitor screening**, and **pitting initiation**. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > Every algebraic transition is shown in full. A **textual logic row** is given below each equation to explain the underlying physical or mathematical reasoning — this is the style that scores full marks in KTU valuation. ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Impedance of a Pure Capacitor under Sinusoidal Excitation **Starting premise:** A capacitor obeys the constitutive relation $q = C V$. Differentiating both sides with respect to time gives the current: $$I(t) = \frac{dq}{dt} = C \frac{dV}{dt}$$ **Step 1 — Apply the sinusoidal voltage perturbation.** Let $V(t) = V_0 e^{j\omega t}$ (using the complex exponential convention, which is mathematically equivalent to $V_0 \cos(\omega t)$ for the real part). $$I(t) = C \frac{d}{dt}\left(V_0 e^{j\omega t}\right) = C \cdot j\omega V_0 e^{j\omega t} = j\omega C \cdot V(t)$$ > *Logic: The derivative of an exponential reproduces the function with an extra factor of $j\omega$ — this is the heart of why AC analysis is so clean.* **Step 2 — Form the ratio $V/I$ to obtain the impedance.** $$Z_C = \frac{V(t)}{I(t)} = \frac{V(t)}{j\omega C \cdot V(t)} = \frac{1}{j\omega C}$$ > *Logic: We cancel the common $V(t)$, leaving the inverse of $j\omega C$.* **Step 3 — Rationalize the denominator** (engineering preference: avoid $j$ in the denominator). $$Z_C = \frac{1}{j\omega C} \cdot \frac{-j}{-j} = \frac{-j}{\omega C}$$ > *Logic: Multiplying numerator and denominator by $-j$ uses the identity $j(-j) = -j^2 = +1$, and $-1/j = -j$.* **Step 4 — Identify the phase angle.** Since $Z_C = 0 - j/(\omega C)$, we have $Z' = 0$ and $Z'' = 1/(\omega C)$. Therefore: $$\phi_C = \arctan\!\left(\frac{1/\omega C}{0}\right) = -90°$$ > *Logic: The current **leads** the voltage by 90° in a capacitor, so the voltage lags — hence the negative phase.* **Final boxed result:** $$\boxed{\,Z_C(\omega) = \frac{1}{j\omega C} = -\frac{j}{\omega C} \quad ; \quad \phi_C = -90°\,}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total Impedance of the Randles Circuit (No-Diffusion Case) **Starting premise:** The Randles circuit without Warburg element is $R_s$ in series with the parallel combination of $R_{ct}$ and $C_{dl}$. **Step 1 — Compute the impedance of the parallel $R_{ct} \parallel C_{dl}$ branch.** For two impedances in parallel, the rule is: $$Z_{parallel} = \frac{Z_1 \cdot Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2}$$ Substituting $Z_1 = R_{ct}$ and $Z_2 = 1/(j\omega C_{dl})$: $$Z_{p} = \frac{R_{ct} \cdot \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_{dl}}}{R_{ct} + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_{dl}}}$$ > *Logic: The standard parallel-formula gives the equivalent impedance of the two elements shunting the interface.* **Step 2 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $j\omega C_{dl}$** to clear the inner fraction. $$Z_{p} = \frac{R_{ct}}{1 + j\omega R_{ct} C_{dl}}$$ > *Logic: The factor $j\omega C_{dl}$ cancels from top and bottom, leaving a clean one-pole transfer function.* **Step 3 — Add $R_s$ in series** (since the solution resistance is in the path of the current before it reaches the interface). $$Z_{Randles}(\omega) = R_s + \frac{R_{ct}}{1 + j\omega R_{ct} C_{dl}}$$ > *Logic: Series impedances add algebraically. The real, frequency-independent term $R_s$ shifts the entire spectrum to the right on the real axis.* **Final boxed result:** $$\boxed{\,Z_{Randles}(\omega) = R_s + \frac{R_{ct}}{1 + j\omega R_{ct} C_{dl}}\,}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Separating Real and Imaginary Parts to Get the Nyquist Equation **Starting premise:** We need to show that the Randles expression produces a **semicircle** in the $Z''$ vs. $Z'$ plane. **Step 1 — Rationalize the complex fraction.** Multiply numerator and denominator by the complex conjugate of the denominator $(1 - j\omega R_{ct} C_{dl})$: $$Z_p = \frac{R_{ct}(1 - j\omega R_{ct} C_{dl})}{(1 + j\omega R_{ct} C_{dl})(1 - j\omega R_{ct} C_{dl})}$$ **Step 2 — Expand the denominator** using the difference of squares: $$(1)^2 - (j\omega R_{ct} C_{dl})^2 = 1 - (j)^2 \omega^2 R_{ct}^2 C_{dl}^2 = 1 + \omega^2 R_{ct}^2 C_{dl}^2$$ > *Logic: $j^2 = -1$, so subtracting gives $+1$.* **Step 3 — Separate real and imaginary parts.** $$Z_p = \frac{R_{ct}}{1 + \omega^2 R_{ct}^2 C_{dl}^2} \; - \; j\,\frac{\omega R_{ct}^2 C_{dl}}{1 + \omega^2 R_{ct}^2 C_{dl}^2}$$ > *Logic: The real part comes from the in-phase product; the imaginary part from the $-j$ term. The negative sign confirms the capacitive (negative imaginary) nature.* **Step 4 — Add $R_s$ to the real part only.** $$Z' = R_s + \frac{R_{ct}}{1 + \omega^2 R_{ct}^2 C_{dl}^2} \qquad ; \qquad Z'' = \frac{\omega R_{ct}^2 C_{dl}}{1 + \omega^2 R_{ct}^2 C_{dl}^2}$$ **Step 5 — Identify the geometric locus.** Compute $Z' - R_s - R_{ct}/2$ and $Z''$, then verify that: $$\left(Z' - R_s - \frac{R_{ct}}{2}\right)^2 + (Z'')^2 = \left(\frac{R_{ct}}{2}\right)^2$$ > *Logic: This is the standard Cartesian equation of a **circle** with center $\left(R_s + R_{ct}/2,\; 0\right)$ and radius $R_{ct}/2$ — a **semicircle** because the imaginary part $Z''$ is restricted to non-negative values for a capacitor.* **Final boxed result — the Nyquist equation:** $$\boxed{\,\left(Z' - R_s - \frac{R_{ct}}{2}\right)^2 + (Z'')^2 = \left(\frac{R_{ct}}{2}\right)^2\,}$$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Frequency at the Apex of the Semicircle **Starting premise:** The maximum imaginary part $|Z''|_{max}$ occurs at the apex of the semicircle, where the derivative $dZ''/d\omega = 0$. **Step 1 — Differentiate $Z''(\omega) = \omega R_{ct}^2 C_{dl} / (1 + \omega^2 R_{ct}^2 C_{dl}^2)$ with respect to $\omega$** using the quotient rule: $$\frac{dZ''}{d\omega} = \frac{R_{ct}^2 C_{dl}(1 + \omega^2 R_{ct}^2 C_{dl}^2) - \omega R_{ct}^2 C_{dl} \cdot 2\omega R_{ct}^2 C_{dl}^2}{(1 + \omega^2 R_{ct}^2 C_{dl}^2)^2}$$ **Step 2 — Set the numerator to zero.** $$R_{ct}^2 C_{dl}(1 + \omega^2 R_{ct}^2 C_{dl}^2) - 2\omega^2 R_{ct}^4 C_{dl}^3 = 0$$ $$1 + \omega^2 R_{ct}^2 C_{dl}^2 = 2\omega^2 R_{ct}^2 C_{dl}^2$$ $$1 = \omega^2 R_{ct}^2 C_{dl}^2$$ > *Logic: Cancelling the common positive factor $R_{ct}^2 C_{dl}$ and rearranging gives the optimum frequency.* **Step 3 — Solve for the angular frequency** and convert to ordinary frequency $f = \omega/(2\pi)$: $$\omega_{top} = \frac{1}{R_{ct} C_{dl}} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad f_{top} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{ct} C_{dl}}$$ **Final boxed result:** $$\boxed{\,f_{top} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{ct} C_{dl}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \tau}\,}$$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Trick Question:** "The Nyquist semicircle for a freshly polished iron sample in 0.5 M H₂SO₄ has its apex at 1.59 kHz and $C_{dl} = 40\;\mu\text{F/cm}^2$. Find $R_{ct}$." > Just invert: $R_{ct} = 1/(2\pi f C_{dl}) = 1/(2\pi \cdot 1590 \cdot 40 \times 10^{-6}) \approx 2.5\;\Omega\cdot\text{cm}^2$. --- ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Stern–Geary Conversion of $R_{ct}$ to $i_{corr}$ **Starting premise:** Near the corrosion potential $E_{corr}$, the Butler–Volmer equation linearizes to: $$i = i_{corr}\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{2.303\eta}{\beta_a}\right) - \exp\!\left(-\frac{2.303\eta}{\beta_c}\right)\right]$$ **Step 1 — Linearize the exponentials** for small overpotential $\vert \eta \vert \ll \beta$. Using $e^x \approx 1 + x$: $$i \approx i_{corr}\left(\frac{2.303\eta}{\beta_a} + \frac{2.303\eta}{\beta_c}\right) = \frac{2.303\,i_{corr}(\beta_a + \beta_c)}{\beta_a \beta_c}\,\eta$$ > *Logic: Near equilibrium, the net current is linearly proportional to the overpotential — this is the definition of polarization resistance.* **Step 2 — Identify the slope $d\eta/di$ as $R_p$.** $$R_p = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303\,i_{corr}(\beta_a + \beta_c)}$$ **Step 3 — Invert to obtain the corrosion current density.** $$i_{corr} = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303\,R_p (\beta_a + \beta_c)} = \frac{B}{R_p}$$ where $B = \dfrac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)}$. **Final boxed result:** $$\boxed{\,i_{corr} = \frac{B}{R_p} = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303\,R_p(\beta_a + \beta_c)}\,}$$ --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > Per the V10 protocol, all node IDs are alphanumeric and free of reserved keywords. Labels are pure uppercase alphanumeric text to prevent Mermaid parser errors. ## 4.1 Block Diagram — EIS Measurement Setup ```mermaid graph LR POT["POTENTIOSTAT
FREQUENCY RESPONSE ANALYZER"] -- "SINE WAVE V AC 5 TO 10 MV" --> WE["WORKING ELECTRODE
CORRODING METAL SAMPLE"] WE -- "CURRENT I RESPONSE" --> FRA["FRA DETECTOR
LOCK IN AMPLIFIER"] FRA -- "Z PRIME AND Z DOUBLE PRIME" --> PC["PC DATA ACQUISITION
NYQUIST AND BODE PLOT"] RE["REFERENCE ELECTRODE
SCE OR AG AGCL"] -. "HIGH IMPEDANCE PROBE" .-> POT CE["COUNTER ELECTRODE
PT MESH"] -- "CARRIES I" --> POT ``` > *Reading the diagram:* The Potentiostat + Frequency Response Analyzer (FRA) injects a small AC voltage at the working electrode, measures the resulting AC current, and computes the complex ratio. The reference electrode (RE) senses potential without carrying current, and the counter electrode (CE) closes the circuit. ## 4.2 Equivalent Circuit — Randles Cell (No Diffusion) ```mermaid graph LR A["TERMINAL A
WORKING ELECTRODE"] -- RS["RS
SOLUTION RESISTANCE"] --> B["NODE B
METAL ELECTROLYTE INTERFACE"] B -- RCT["RCT
CHARGE TRANSFER RESISTANCE"] --> C["TERMINAL C
REFERENCE"] B -- CDL["CDL
DOUBLE LAYER CAPACITANCE"] --> C ``` > *Reading the diagram:* Current arriving at node B from the bulk splits into two parallel paths — one through $R_{ct}$ (the faradaic corrosion reaction) and one through $C_{dl}$ (the non-faradaic charging of the double layer). The values of these two elements are extracted by fitting the measured spectrum. ## 4.3 Equivalent Circuit — Randles Cell with Warburg Diffusion ```mermaid graph LR A["TERMINAL A"] -- RS["RS"] --> B["NODE B"] B -- RCT["RCT"] --> D["NODE D"] B -- CDL["CDL"] --> D D -- ZW["ZW
WARBURG IMPEDANCE
45 DEG LINE"] --> C["TERMINAL C"] ``` > *Reading the diagram:* When mass-transport of reactive species (e.g., dissolved O₂ or H⁺) is rate-limiting, the faradaic branch is no longer a pure resistor but includes a **Warburg element** in series with $R_{ct}$. The Warburg has a characteristic **45° straight line** at low frequencies on the Nyquist plot. ## 4.4 Nyquist Plot — Visual Semicircle Construction ```mermaid graph LR O["ORIGIN 0 0"] -- ZPRIME["Z PRIME REAL AXIS"] --> RS["RS
HIGH FREQ INTERCEPT"] RS -- DIAMETER["SEMICIRCLE
DIAMETER = RCT"] --> RPRS["RS PLUS RP
LOW FREQ INTERCEPT"] APEX["APEX OF SEMICIRCLE
FREQ = F TOP"] --- RS APEX --- RPRS ZDOUBLE["Z DOUBLE PRIME
IMAG AXIS"] --- APEX ``` > *Reading the diagram:* The Nyquist plot of a simple Randles cell is a single **semicircle** in the fourth quadrant (capacitive). The high-frequency intercept on the real axis gives $R_s$; the diameter of the semicircle gives $R_p \approx R_{ct}$; the apex frequency $f_{top}$ yields the time constant $\tau = R_{ct} C_{dl}$. ## 4.5 Process Flow — From Raw Data to Corrosion Rate ```mermaid graph TD S1["STEP 1
PREPARE 3 ELECTRODE CELL"] --> S2["STEP 2
WAIT FOR STABLE OCP"] S2 --> S3["STEP 3
APPLY 10 MV AC FROM 100 KHZ TO 10 MHZ"] S3 --> S4["STEP 4
RECORD Z PRIME AND Z DOUBLE PRIME"] S4 --> S5["STEP 5
PLOT NYQUIST AND BODE"] S5 --> S6["STEP 6
FIT TO EQUIVALENT CIRCUIT
EXTRACT RS RCT CDL"] S6 --> S7["STEP 7
APPLY STERN GEARY
COMPUTE I CORR"] S7 --> S8["STEP 8
CONVERT TO MPY OR MM PER YEAR"] ``` > *Reading the diagram:* EIS is not a one-click measurement; the analyst must fit the spectrum to a physically justified equivalent circuit, then apply the Stern–Geary relation to translate the electrical measurement into an engineering corrosion rate. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pattern:** Three questions, each carrying **3 marks**, typically testing definitions, identifications, or short derivations. Answers should fit in **3 to 5 lines**. ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define electrochemical impedance. How is it different from DC resistance?** **Model Answer:** Electrochemical impedance $Z(\omega)$ is the frequency-dependent complex ratio of the sinusoidal voltage perturbation to the sinusoidal current response at an electrochemical interface, $Z(\omega) = V_0 e^{j\omega t} / I_0 e^{j(\omega t - \phi)}$. Unlike DC resistance, which is a single real number measured at one bias point, impedance is a **complex, frequency-dependent** function that separately captures the energy-storing (capacitive) and energy-dissipating (resistive) behavior of the interface. A pure resistor has $Z = R$ independent of frequency, whereas $Z_C = 1/(j\omega C)$ varies inversely with frequency. ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What is a Nyquist plot? What information can be obtained from it?** **Model Answer:** A Nyquist plot is a graph of the **negative imaginary part** $-Z''(\omega)$ versus the **real part** $Z'(\omega)$ of the complex impedance, with frequency as an implicit parameter. From a Nyquist plot of a Randles cell, one can read: - The **high-frequency real-axis intercept** = solution resistance $R_s$. - The **diameter of the semicircle** = charge-transfer resistance $R_{ct}$ (≈ polarization resistance $R_p$). - The **deviation from a perfect semicircle** indicates non-ideal behavior (CPE, diffusion, adsorption). - The **presence of a 45° tail** at low frequencies indicates Warburg diffusion control. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. The student is given an internal choice (either Q-A or Q-B) and must answer one full question. ### ✅ QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — 14 Marks **Q. (a) With a neat sketch, explain the Randles equivalent circuit for a corroding metal–electrolyte interface. Identify each element and state its physical significance.** (7 marks) **Model Answer:** The Randles equivalent circuit models an electrochemical interface subjected to activation-controlled corrosion: - **$R_s$ (Solution Resistance):** Represents the ohmic resistance of the bulk electrolyte between the working electrode and the tip of the reference-electrode probe. It is in **series** with the interface and is read from the **high-frequency intercept** of the Nyquist plot. - **$C_{dl}$ (Double-Layer Capacitance):** Models the **non-faradaic** charging of the electrochemical double layer — a parallel-plate capacitor formed by the metal surface (one plate) and the layer of solvated ions (the other plate), separated by a distance of a few Angstroms. Its typical magnitude is **10 – 50 µF cm⁻²**. - **$R_{ct}$ (Charge-Transfer Resistance):** The **faradaic** resistance to electron transfer across the metal/solution interface. It is the direct electrical analogue of the activation energy barrier for the corrosion half-reactions. A **larger $R_{ct}$ implies better corrosion resistance**. The circuit places $R_{ct}$ in **parallel** with $C_{dl}$ (because the faradaic and capacitive currents are alternative paths across the interface) and this parallel combination is in **series** with $R_s$. (Equivalent circuit diagram — see Section 4.2 of these notes.) *Marking scheme (7 marks):* Sketch = 2, identification of 3 elements = 3, physical significance = 2. --- **Q. (b) Derive an expression for the total impedance of the Randles circuit (neglecting Warburg diffusion). Show that the Nyquist plot is a semicircle whose diameter equals $R_{ct}$.** (7 marks) **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — Combine the parallel branch.** The two parallel elements have impedances $Z_1 = R_{ct}$ and $Z_2 = 1/(j\omega C_{dl})$: $$Z_p = \frac{R_{ct}}{1 + j\omega R_{ct} C_{dl}}$$ **Step 2 — Add the series $R_s$:** $$Z(\omega) = R_s + \frac{R_{ct}}{1 + j\omega R_{ct} C_{dl}}$$ **Step 3 — Separate real and imaginary parts** by rationalising: $$Z' = R_s + \frac{R_{ct}}{1 + \omega^2 R_{ct}^2 C_{dl}^2} \qquad ; \qquad Z'' = \frac{\omega R_{ct}^2 C_{dl}}{1 + \omega^2 R_{ct}^2 C_{dl}^2}$$ **Step 4 — Eliminate the parameter $\omega$.** Substituting $\omega = Z'' / (R_{ct}^2 C_{dl} - \omega R_{ct}^2 C_{dl} \cdot Z')$ and simplifying, one obtains the circle equation: $$\left(Z' - R_s - \frac{R_{ct}}{2}\right)^2 + (Z'')^2 = \left(\frac{R_{ct}}{2}\right)^2$$ This is the **Cartesian equation of a circle** with centre at $\left(R_s + R_{ct}/2,\; 0\right)$ and radius $R_{ct}/2$. Therefore the Nyquist locus is a **semicircle** in the fourth quadrant (capacitive, $Z'' > 0$), with: - Left intercept: $Z' = R_s$ (high frequency, $\omega \to \infty$), - Right intercept: $Z' = R_s + R_{ct}$ (low frequency, $\omega \to 0$). The **diameter** of the semicircle, measured along the real axis, is exactly $R_{ct}$. Hence the Nyquist plot directly yields the charge-transfer resistance — and therefore, via Stern–Geary, the corrosion current density. *Marking scheme (7 marks):* Derivation of $Z$ = 2, separation of real/imaginary = 2, geometric identification of semicircle = 2, statement of diameter = 1. --- ### ✅ QUESTION B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) **Q. (a) Explain the construction and use of a Bode plot. How does it complement the Nyquist plot?** (7 marks) **Model Answer:** A **Bode plot** presents EIS data as two separate graphs versus $\log f$ (frequency): 1. **$\log \vert Z \vert$ vs. $\log f$** — the magnitude plot. 2. **Phase angle $\phi$ vs. $\log f$** — the phase plot. **Construction:** The instrument sweeps frequency (typically 100 kHz → 1 mHz, 5–10 points/decade) and records the modulus and phase at each frequency. Both are plotted on logarithmic frequency axes. **Reading a Bode plot for a Randles cell:** - At **high frequency**, $\vert Z \vert \to R_s$ and $\phi \to 0°$ (purely resistive behaviour). - At **intermediate frequency**, a slope of $-1$ (i.e., $\vert Z \vert \propto 1/f$) and a phase near $-90°$ indicate capacitive control. - At **low frequency**, $\vert Z \vert \to R_s + R_{ct}$ and $\phi \to 0°$ again. **Complement to Nyquist:** - Nyquist plots **suppress frequency information** — the same shape can come from different frequency ranges. Bode plots preserve it. - Bode plots are **easier to read for capacitive systems** with very large $R_{ct}$ because a Nyquist semicircle can become too big to fit on paper. - Bode plots allow easy identification of **multiple time constants** (e.g., coating + substrate) that overlap on a Nyquist plot. *Marking scheme (7 marks):* Construction = 2, Bode plot interpretation = 3, comparison with Nyquist = 2. --- **Q. (b) For a steel specimen in 0.5 M H₂SO₄, an EIS experiment yields a Nyquist semicircle with high-frequency intercept at 1.2 Ω·cm², low-frequency intercept at 28.4 Ω·cm², and apex frequency at 318 Hz. The Tafel slopes are $\beta_a = 60$ mV/decade and $\beta_c = 120$ mV/decade. Calculate (i) the polarization resistance, (ii) the double-layer capacitance, and (iii) the corrosion current density.** (7 marks) **Model Answer:** **Given:** - $R_s = 1.2\;\Omega\cdot\text{cm}^2$ - $R_s + R_p = 28.4\;\Omega\cdot\text{cm}^2$ - $f_{top} = 318\;\text{Hz}$ - $\beta_a = 60\;\text{mV/dec}$, $\beta_c = 120\;\text{mV/dec}$ **Step (i) — Polarization resistance $R_p$:** $$R_p = (R_s + R_p) - R_s = 28.4 - 1.2 = 27.2\;\Omega\cdot\text{cm}^2$$ **Step (ii) — Double-layer capacitance $C_{dl}$:** Using $f_{top} = 1/(2\pi R_p C_{dl})$: $$C_{dl} = \frac{1}{2\pi f_{top} R_p} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 318 \cdot 27.2} = 1.84 \times 10^{-5}\;\text{F/cm}^2 = 18.4\;\mu\text{F/cm}^2$$ This value is in the expected range (10 – 50 µF/cm²) for a clean metal/aqueous interface, confirming the validity of the Randles model. **Step (iii) — Corrosion current density $i_{corr}$:** Apply the Stern–Geary equation: $$B = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)} = \frac{0.060 \times 0.120}{2.303 \times (0.060 + 0.120)} = \frac{7.2 \times 10^{-3}}{0.4145} = 1.736 \times 10^{-2}\;\text{V} = 17.36\;\text{mV}$$ $$i_{corr} = \frac{B}{R_p} = \frac{17.36 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{V}}{27.2\;\Omega\cdot\text{cm}^2} = 6.38 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{A/cm}^2 = 638\;\mu\text{A/cm}^2$$ **Final answers:** - $R_p = 27.2\;\Omega\cdot\text{cm}^2$ - $C_{dl} = 18.4\;\mu\text{F/cm}^2$ - $i_{corr} = 6.38 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{A/cm}^2$ (or $638\;\mu\text{A/cm}^2$) *Marking scheme (7 marks):* (i) = 1, (ii) = 3, (iii) = 3. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Mixing up $R_{ct}$ and $R_p$:** For a **simple activation-controlled** Randles cell, $R_p \approx R_{ct}$, but the moment a Warburg element appears, $R_{ct} < R_p$. Always state your assumption explicitly. > 2. **Forgetting to subtract $R_s$:** When asked for "$R_p$", the value you read directly from the low-frequency intercept is $R_s + R_p$, not $R_p$ alone. This single error costs **2 marks** in numerical problems. > 3. **Using degrees vs. radians in the phase-angle equation:** $\arctan(Z''/Z')$ gives a result in **radians** by default in most calculators — for KTU, write the answer in **degrees** with the ° symbol. > 4. **Confusing Nyquist and Bode axes:** A Nyquist plot has $Z''$ (imaginary) on the y-axis; a Bode magnitude plot has $\log \vert Z \vert$ on the y-axis. Drawing them backwards is an instant **1-mark penalty**. > 5. **In Stern–Geary, the Tafel slopes must be in volts (not mV):** Convert **60 mV/dec → 0.060 V/dec** before substituting. Forgetting this gives an $i_{corr}$ off by a factor of 1000. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-revision checklist — read this 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.** - **EIS = AC Impedance Spectroscopy.** A small (5–10 mV) sinusoidal perturbation is applied over a wide frequency range and the complex ratio $Z = V/I$ is recorded. - **Impedance is complex:** $Z = Z' - jZ''$. Real part = resistance; imaginary part = reactance (capacitive if negative). - **Pure element responses:** $Z_R = R$, $Z_C = 1/(j\omega C)$, $Z_L = j\omega L$. Phases: $0°$, $-90°$, $+90°$. - **The Randles circuit** = $R_s$ (series) + [$R_{ct}$ || $C_{dl}$] (parallel). Add $Z_W$ in series with $R_{ct}$ if diffusion-limited. - **Nyquist plot** = $Z''$ vs. $Z'$. For a Randles cell, it is a **semicircle** with **diameter = $R_{ct}$**, centred at $R_s + R_{ct}/2$. - **Bode plot** = $\log \vert Z \vert$ and $\phi$ vs. $\log f$. Preserves frequency information, easier for multi-time-constant systems. - **Frequency at the apex of the Nyquist semicircle** gives the time constant: $f_{top} = 1/(2\pi R_{ct} C_{dl})$. - **CPE (Constant Phase Element)** $Z = 1/[Q(j\omega)^n]$ replaces ideal $C_{dl}$ for rough, inhomogeneous, or coated surfaces; $n \to 1$ recovers a true capacitor. - **Warburg impedance** $Z_W = \sigma(1-j)/\sqrt{\omega}$ gives the characteristic **45° straight-line tail** at low frequencies on the Nyquist plot, indicating diffusion control. - **Stern–Geary relation** bridges EIS to corrosion rate: $i_{corr} = B/R_p$, with $B = \beta_a \beta_c / [2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)]$. Typical $B$ = 25 – 52 mV. - **Linearity requirement:** The 5–10 mV amplitude ensures the system stays in the linear regime; the Kramers–Kronig transform can be used to check this *a posteriori*. - **KTU-favorite numericals:** "Given a Nyquist plot, find $R_s$, $R_{ct}$, $C_{dl}$, and $i_{corr}$." Master the geometry of the semicircle. - **Standard 3-electrode cell:** Working Electrode (corroding metal), Reference Electrode (SCE/Ag-AgCl), Counter Electrode (Pt mesh). - **Kramers–Kronig causality test:** Verifies that the measured impedance satisfies linear systems theory — KTU may ask for a one-line statement of what it checks. ---

topics.title: Thermogravimetric technique
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT527 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Thermogravimetric technique

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA)** is a branch of thermal analysis in which the **mass change** of a specimen is measured as a function of temperature and/or time under a controlled atmospheric environment. The instrument used to perform this measurement is called a **thermo balance** (or thermogravimetric analyzer), and the resulting plot of mass versus temperature (or time) is termed a **thermogram** or **thermal degradation curve**.

In the specific context of **Corrosion Engineering (PECHT527)**, TGA is the primary experimental technique used to quantify the **kinetics of high-temperature oxidation** and **hot corrosion** of metals and alloys. Since oxide scale formation at elevated temperatures involves a measurable gain in mass (as metal M + O₂ → MO), TGA provides a direct, continuous, and non-destructive record of the corrosion reaction progress.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Thermogravimetric technique is taught as a *quantitative* experimental method to determine oxidation/corrosion rate laws (parabolic, linear, logarithmic) and to extract kinetic parameters such as activation energy (Eₐ) and pre-exponential factor (A) from isothermal mass-gain experiments.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Measurement Quantity:** The dependent variable in TGA is **mass (m)**, typically recorded in milligrams (mg) or micrograms (µg), with a modern microbalance sensitivity of **±0.1 µg** (i.e., 10⁻⁷ g). The independent variable may be temperature (°C or K) under a defined heating rate, or time (s or min) at a constant set-point temperature.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you place a small iron nail on a precision kitchen scale inside a hot oven. As the oven heats up, you notice the reading on the scale slowly **creeps upward** even though nothing has been added to the pan. The reason? Oxygen from the air is continuously reacting with the iron to form a layer of rust (Fe₂O₃) on the surface, and that oxygen has *weight* — it is literally being added to the nail.

A **thermo balance** is just a vastly more sensitive, automated, and rigorously controlled version of this kitchen-scale-plus-oven setup:

- The **oven** heats the sample at a precisely programmed rate (or holds it at a fixed temperature).
- The **balance** weighs the sample continuously, with microgram accuracy.
- The **computer** plots weight versus time/temperature, producing the famous "staircase" or "smooth curve" of the thermogram.

By analyzing the **shape** of this curve, a corrosion engineer can deduce:

1. *How fast* the oxide scale grows (the rate constant).
2. *What mechanism* controls the growth (diffusion, reaction at interface, or breakdown of a protective film).
3. *How much energy* the corrosion process requires (the activation energy).

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Mass sensitivity | Δm | **±0.1 µg** (10⁻⁷ g) |
| Temperature range | T | **RT to 1600 °C** (furnace dependent) |
| Heating rates | β = dT/dt | **1 to 100 °C/min** (typical) |
| Atmospheric control | — | **Inert (Ar, N₂), oxidizing (O₂, air), reducing (H₂)** |
| Sample mass | m₀ | **1 to 100 mg** (typical) |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU numerical problems, the **gaseous environment** in TGA is critical. If air or oxygen is used, the mass *increases* (oxidation). If a reducing gas like H₂ is used, mass may *decrease* (reduction of oxides to metal). The student must always read the atmosphere stated in the problem before interpreting the thermogram.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — The Two Modes of TGA

TGA experiments in corrosion studies are conducted in **two fundamental modes**, and the choice dictates the entire data analysis strategy.

#### Mode 1 — Isothermal TGA (Constant Temperature)
- The sample is brought rapidly to a target temperature (e.g., 700 °C, 800 °C, 900 °C) and held there.
- The mass gain Δm is recorded as a function of time **t**.
- This mode is **preferred for kinetic studies** because the rate constants do not depend on a (changing) temperature.
- Multiple isothermal runs at different temperatures yield the **Arrhenius parameters** Eₐ and A.

#### Mode 2 — Non-Isothermal (Dynamic) TGA
- The sample is heated at a constant rate β (°C/min), typically from RT to 1000 °C or higher.
- The mass is recorded as a function of temperature.
- This mode is useful for identifying the **onset temperature of oxidation**, the formation of intermediate oxides, and the stability of pre-formed scales.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A 14-mark Part B question on this topic almost always involves an **isothermal** mass-gain–versus–time data set, asking the student to (a) identify the rate law, (b) compute the rate constant, and (c) determine the activation energy from an Arrhenius plot.

---

### 2.2 High-Temperature Oxidation Rate Laws (The Heart of TGA in Corrosion)

When a metal M is exposed to oxygen at high temperature, the mass gain per unit area (ΔW = Δm / A, in mg·cm⁻²) follows one of several empirical rate laws. The **shape of the isothermal TGA curve** identifies which law is operative.

| Rate Law | Integrated Form | Controlling Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Linear** | $\Delta W = k_l \, t$ | Reaction at gas/scale interface; non-protective porous scale; linear rate constant $k_l$ |
| **Parabolic** | $(\Delta W)^2 = k_p \, t$ | Solid-state diffusion through a growing protective scale (Wagner's theory); $k_p$ is the parabolic rate constant |
| **Cubic** | $(\Delta W)^3 = k_c \, t$ | Observed in some systems at intermediate temperatures; less common |
| **Logarithmic** | $\Delta W = k_{\log} \, \log(t + t_0)$ | Low-temperature oxidation; very thin films (< 100 nm); ion-tunnelling control |
| **Inverse Logarithmic** | $1/\Delta W = B - k_{il} \log t$ | Very low temperatures; field-assisted transport |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wagner's Parabolic Oxidation Theory (1933):** For most engineering alloys and structural metals at high temperatures, the dominant rate law is **parabolic**. The parabolic rate constant $k_p$ is theoretically related to the diffusivities of cations and anions through the oxide scale, and the thickness of the scale grows as $x \propto \sqrt{t}$.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mass gain per unit area | $\Delta W = \dfrac{\Delta m}{A}$ | mg·cm⁻² or g·m⁻² | A = exposed surface area of specimen |
| Parabolic rate law | $(\Delta W)^2 = k_p \, t$ | (mg·cm⁻²)²·s⁻¹ | Linear plot of (ΔW)² vs t gives slope $k_p$ |
| Linear rate law | $\Delta W = k_l \, t$ | mg·cm⁻²·s⁻¹ | Linear plot of ΔW vs t gives slope $k_l$ |
| Logarithmic rate law | $\Delta W = k_{\log} \log(t+t_0)$ | mg·cm⁻² | $t_0$ corrects for initial transient |
| Arrhenius equation | $k = A \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | — | k = rate constant ($k_p$, $k_l$, etc.) |
| Linearised Arrhenius | $\ln k = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T}$ | — | Slope of $\ln k$ vs $1/T$ = $-E_a/R$ |
| Activation energy | $E_a = -R \cdot \text{slope}$ | J·mol⁻¹ or kJ·mol⁻¹ | R = **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** |
| Universal gas constant | $R$ | **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** | — |
| Rate of mass change | $\dfrac{d(\Delta W)}{dt} = \dfrac{k_p}{2\,\Delta W}$ | mg·cm⁻²·s⁻¹ | Differential form of parabolic law |
| Parabolic-to-linear threshold | $\Delta W_{crit} = \dfrac{k_l}{k_p}$ | mg·cm⁻² | Marks transition where protective scale fails |
| Instantaneous rate from TGA | $r = \dfrac{1}{A}\dfrac{dm}{dt}$ | g·m⁻²·s⁻¹ | Read directly from derivative thermogram (DTG) |
| Fraction reacted (α) | $\alpha = \dfrac{\Delta m_t}{\Delta m_{total}}$ | dimensionless | 0 ≤ α ≤ 1; useful in kinetic modelling |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** In the parabolic law, students often confuse the **rate constant** $k_p$ with the **rate**. The rate (in mg·cm⁻²·s⁻¹) is $k_p / (2\,\Delta W)$ and *decreases* with time, whereas $k_p$ itself is a **constant** for a given temperature.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The thermogravimetric technique is the workhorse of high-temperature materials selection in:

- **Aerospace & Gas Turbines** — Ranking Ni-based superalloys, bond coats (NiAl, NiCrAlY) by their $k_p$ values at 900–1100 °C.
- **Boiler & Power Plant Design** — Evaluating fireside corrosion and oxidation resistance of ferritic and austenitic steels in steam at 600–650 °C.
- **Nuclear Reactors** — Zircaloy cladding oxidation by steam (Zr + 2H₂O → ZrO₂ + 2H₂) is a textbook TGA experiment.
- **Chemical Process Industries** — Sulphidation attack in petrochemical reactors (V₂O₅, Na₂SO₄ molten salt-induced hot corrosion).
- **Coating Evaluation** — Mass-gain measurements of aluminide, MCrAlY, and thermal-barrier coatings under cyclic oxidation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Standard Reference:** The **ASTM E2550** standard ("Standard Test Method for Thermal Stability by Thermogravimetry") and **ASTM G54** (for isothermal oxidation) formalize TGA procedures for corrosion studies.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Parabolic Rate Constant from Isothermal Mass-Gain Data

**Statement:** For a metal oxidizing according to Wagner's parabolic law, the mass gain per unit area ΔW at time t is governed by $(\Delta W)^2 = k_p \, t$. Derive an expression for the rate constant $k_p$ from a set of experimental data.

**Derivation (Step-by-Step):**

Step 1: Begin with the empirical parabolic law as the constitutive equation.

$$\Delta W^2 = k_p \cdot t$$

Step 2: This is a straight-line equation of the form $Y = mX$, where the independent variable is $t$ and the dependent variable is $\Delta W^2$. Therefore, plotting $\Delta W^2$ (y-axis) against $t$ (x-axis) should yield a straight line passing through the origin.

Step 3: The slope $m$ of this line equals the parabolic rate constant $k_p$.

$$k_p = \frac{\Delta W^2}{t} = \text{slope of } (\Delta W^2) \text{ vs } t \text{ plot}$$

Step 4: For a more accurate least-squares fit through *N* experimental data points $(t_i, \Delta W_i)$:

$$k_p = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} t_i \, \Delta W_i^{\,2}}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} t_i^{\,2}}$$

Step 5: The instantaneous oxidation rate (in mg·cm⁻²·s⁻¹) is obtained by differentiating:

$$\frac{d(\Delta W)}{dt} = \frac{k_p}{2 \cdot \Delta W}$$

This shows that the rate is *high* at short times (thin scale, easy diffusion) and *low* at long times (thick scale, sluggish diffusion). ∎

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Activation Energy from Arrhenius Plot

**Statement:** If isothermal TGA runs are performed at three (or more) temperatures $T_1$, $T_2$, $T_3$ and the parabolic rate constants are $k_{p,1}$, $k_{p,2}$, $k_{p,3}$, derive the activation energy $E_a$.

**Derivation (Step-by-Step):**

Step 1: At each temperature, the rate constant obeys the Arrhenius equation:

$$k_p = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R T}\right)$$

where $A$ is the pre-exponential factor and $E_a$ is the activation energy.

Step 2: Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln k_p = \ln A - \frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T}$$

Step 3: This is a straight-line equation of the form $y = c + m x$, with:
- $y = \ln k_p$
- $x = 1/T$
- $c = \ln A$ (intercept)
- $m = -E_a/R$ (slope)

Step 4: Plot $\ln k_p$ on the y-axis and $1/T$ on the x-axis. A straight line is obtained. The slope $m$ is calculated from two data points:

$$m = \frac{\ln k_{p,2} - \ln k_{p,1}}{(1/T_2) - (1/T_1)}$$

Step 5: Since $m = -E_a / R$, we obtain:

$$E_a = -m \cdot R = -R \cdot \frac{\ln k_{p,2} - \ln k_{p,1}}{(1/T_2) - (1/T_1)}$$

Step 6: Equivalently, using two temperatures directly:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{k_{p,2}}{k_{p,1}}\right) = -\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

Step 7: Solve explicitly for $E_a$:

$$E_a = \frac{R \cdot \ln(k_{p,2}/k_{p,1})}{(1/T_1) - (1/T_2)}$$

with $R = \mathbf{8.314 \; J \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot K^{-1}}$. ∎

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board-Style)

> **Problem:** A nickel specimen of surface area 5 cm² is oxidized in pure oxygen at 800 °C. The mass gain recorded by the thermobalance at various times is:
>
> | t (h) | 1 | 2 | 4 | 8 | 16 |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | Δm (mg) | 0.45 | 0.62 | 0.88 | 1.25 | 1.77 |
>
> (a) Identify the rate law.
> (b) Calculate the parabolic rate constant $k_p$ in (mg·cm⁻²)²·h⁻¹.
> (c) If the same experiment at 900 °C yields $k_p = 4.50 \times 10^{-2}$ (mg·cm⁻²)²·h⁻¹, determine the activation energy for nickel oxidation.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Identification of the rate law:**

Compute ΔW = Δm / A for each data point:

| t (h) | ΔW (mg·cm⁻²) | (ΔW)² (mg·cm⁻²)² |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.090 | 0.00810 |
| 2 | 0.124 | 0.01538 |
| 4 | 0.176 | 0.03098 |
| 8 | 0.250 | 0.06250 |
| 16 | 0.354 | 0.12532 |

The ratio (ΔW)² / t is approximately **constant** (≈ 0.0078–0.0081 for all points), indicating a **parabolic rate law**.

**Part (b) — Parabolic rate constant:**

Using the slope of (ΔW)² vs t. Using the best fit through origin:

$$k_p = \frac{\sum t_i \cdot (\Delta W_i)^2}{\sum t_i^2}$$

Numerator: $(1)(0.00810) + (2)(0.01538) + (4)(0.03098) + (8)(0.06250) + (16)(0.12532)$
$= 0.00810 + 0.03076 + 0.12392 + 0.50000 + 2.00512 = 2.66790$

Denominator: $1^2 + 2^2 + 4^2 + 8^2 + 16^2 = 1 + 4 + 16 + 64 + 256 = 341$

$$k_p = \frac{2.66790}{341} \approx 7.82 \times 10^{-3} \; (mg \cdot cm^{-2})^2 \cdot h^{-1}$$

**Part (c) — Activation energy:**

Given:
- $T_1 = 800 + 273 = 1073 \; K$, $\; k_{p,1} = 7.82 \times 10^{-3}$
- $T_2 = 900 + 273 = 1173 \; K$, $\; k_{p,2} = 4.50 \times 10^{-2}$

Apply the Arrhenius-derived formula:

$$E_a = \frac{R \cdot \ln(k_{p,2}/k_{p,1})}{(1/T_1) - (1/T_2)}$$

Compute the ratio:

$$\frac{k_{p,2}}{k_{p,1}} = \frac{4.50 \times 10^{-2}}{7.82 \times 10^{-3}} = 5.754$$

$$\ln(5.754) = 1.750$$

Compute the temperature term:

$$\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{1073} - \frac{1}{1173}$$

$$= 9.320 \times 10^{-4} - 8.525 \times 10^{-4} = 7.95 \times 10^{-5} \; K^{-1}$$

Substitute:

$$E_a = \frac{(8.314) \cdot (1.750)}{7.95 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$E_a = \frac{14.55}{7.95 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.830 \times 10^{5} \; J \cdot mol^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{E_a \approx 183 \; kJ \cdot mol^{-1}}$$

This value is in excellent agreement with the literature value for nickel oxidation (≈ 180–200 kJ·mol⁻¹). ∎

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Rate Law Fits My Data?

| Diagnostic Check | Linear | Parabolic | Logarithmic |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shape of ΔW vs t | Straight line | Parabolic curve flattening | Sharp early rise, then near-flat |
| Plot that linearises | ΔW vs t | (ΔW)² vs t | ΔW vs log t |
| Slope of (ΔW)² vs t | Increases with t | **Constant** | Decreases with t |
| Typical T range | Very high (T/Tₘ > 0.9) or porous scale | Intermediate-to-high | Low (T/Tₘ < 0.3) |
| Scale thickness | Non-protective, spalls | Protective, adherent | < 100 nm |
| Theoretical basis | Interface reaction control | Wagner diffusion control | Field-assisted ion transport |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Test:** To distinguish parabolic from linear in a problem, simply check whether the ratio ΔW/√t is **constant** (parabolic) or whether the ratio ΔW/t is **constant** (linear). This is the fastest diagnostic.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Modern Thermogravimetric Analyzer (Thermo balance)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CTRL["CONTROL AND DATA ACQUISITION"]
        PC1["Personal Computer with TGA Software"]
        TC1["Temperature Programmer Controller"]
    end

    subgraph GAS["ATMOSPHERE CONTROL UNIT"]
        GC1["Gas Cylinder N2 or Ar or O2"]
        FC1["Mass Flow Controller MFC"]
        PU1["Purge Gas Inlet"]
    end

    subgraph FURN["FURNACE ASSEMBLY"]
        FURN1["Programmable Tube Furnace RT to 1600 C"]
        TC2["Type S or Type K Thermocouple"]
        HT1["Heating Element SiC or Pt Rh"]
    end

    subgraph BAL["MICROBALANCE ASSEMBLY"]
        SS1["Sample Suspension Wire Pt or Al2O3"]
        MB1["High Precision Microbalance Sensitivity 0.1 microgram"]
        REF1["Reference Counterweight"]
        BS1["Balance Signal Output to PC"]
    end

    subgraph SP["SAMPLE HOLDER"]
        CR1["Crucible Al2O3 or Pt"]
        SM1["Specimen Metal Coupon mg scale"]
    end

    GC1 --> FC1 --> PU1 --> FURN1
    FURN1 --> CR1
    CR1 --> SM1
    SM1 --> SS1 --> MB1
    MB1 --- REF1
    MB1 --> BS1 --> PC1
    TC2 --> FURN1
    TC2 --> TC1 --> PC1
    FURN1 --> HT1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **gas train** (left) supplies a metered, controlled atmosphere.
- The **furnace** (centre) heats the **sample** (bottom) on a precision **balance** (right).
- The **thermocouple** near the sample reports temperature to the **PC** for plotting the thermogram.
- The **PC** simultaneously records mass (from balance) and temperature (from thermocouple) versus time, producing the final TGA curve.

---

### 4.2 Functional Flow of a TGA-Based Corrosion Kinetic Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Step 1 Specimen Preparation Polishing Cleaning Weighing"]
    A1["Step 2 Mount Sample in Thermobalance Record Initial Mass m0"]
    A2["Step 3 Set Furnace Parameters Atmosphere Temperature Program"]
    A3["Step 4 Run Experiment Record Mass vs Time or Temperature"]
    A4["Step 5 Convert Mass to Mass per Unit Area Delta W"]
    A5["Step 6 Diagnostic Plot Identify Linear or Parabolic or Logarithmic"]
    A6["Step 7 Compute Rate Constant k_p or k_l from Slope"]
    A7["Step 8 Repeat at Multiple Temperatures to obtain kp T1 kp T2 kp T3"]
    A8["Step 9 Arrhenius Plot ln kp vs 1 over T Compute Slope"]
    A9["Step 10 Calculate Activation Energy E_a equals minus R times Slope"]
    A10["Step 11 Report k_p and E_a and Propose Mechanism Diffusion or Interface"]

    A0 --> A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 --> A6 --> A7 --> A8 --> A9 --> A10
```

This **eleven-step protocol** mirrors the standard practice in corrosion research laboratories and is the exact sequence expected in a 14-mark KTU Part B question.

---

### 4.3 Thermogram Interpretation — Common Features

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TS["TYPICAL ISOTHERMAL OXIDATION THERMOGRAM"]
        P1["Region I Initial Transient 0 to t0"]
        P2["Region II Steady State Parabolic or Linear"]
        P3["Region III Scale Spallation or Breakaway"]
    end

    P1 --> P2 --> P3

    P1A["Due to surface adsorption and nucleation of oxide islands"]
    P2A["Diffusion controlled or interface controlled growth"]
    P3A["Loss of protective character mechanical failure"]

    P1 -.-> P1A
    P2 -.-> P2A
    P3 -.-> P3A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Interpretation Rule:** If a thermogram shows a **sudden mass loss** during an oxidation experiment, it indicates **spallation** of the oxide scale (a real concern in thermal-cycling service). If mass **continues to rise linearly** after parabolic behaviour, the scale has lost protectiveness.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**
*Define thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). What is a thermogram, and what are its two modes of operation?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) is a thermal analysis technique in which the **mass of a specimen is continuously measured** as a function of temperature or time under a controlled atmosphere, using an instrument called a thermo balance.

**Thermogram (1 mark):** The plot of mass (or mass change) versus temperature or time is called a **thermogram**. It is the primary output of a TGA experiment and contains all kinetic information.

**Two modes (1 mark):**
1. **Isothermal TGA** — sample held at a constant temperature; mass recorded vs time. Used for kinetic rate-law determination.
2. **Non-isothermal (Dynamic) TGA** — sample heated at a constant rate β (°C/min); mass recorded vs temperature. Used to identify onset temperatures and reaction stages.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
*List the three principal high-temperature oxidation rate laws and write the corresponding integrated equations. State one mechanism associated with each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Rate Law | Integrated Form | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Linear** | $\Delta W = k_l t$ | Reaction-controlled at gas-scale interface; porous, non-protective scale |
| 2 | **Parabolic** | $(\Delta W)^2 = k_p t$ | Diffusion-controlled (Wagner); cations/anions migrate through a dense, adherent protective scale |
| 3 | **Logarithmic** | $\Delta W = k_{\log} \log(t+t_0)$ | Low-temperature oxidation; very thin films (< 100 nm); field-assisted transport or quantum tunnelling |

*(1 mark for naming, 1 mark for the equations, 1 mark for the mechanisms.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024 (Model)]**

**(a)** Describe the construction and working of a thermogravimetric analyzer (thermo balance) used in high-temperature corrosion studies, with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of (i) the microbalance, (ii) the furnace, (iii) the thermocouple, and (iv) the gas-purge system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pure iron specimen of surface area 4 cm² is oxidised in air at 700 °C. The mass gain versus time is tabulated below. Determine (i) the governing rate law, (ii) the parabolic rate constant $k_p$ in (mg·cm⁻²)²·h⁻¹, and (iii) the thickness of the oxide (Fe₂O₃, density 5.24 g·cm⁻³) at t = 25 h. **(7 marks)**

| t (h) | 2 | 5 | 10 | 20 | 25 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Δm (mg) | 0.36 | 0.56 | 0.80 | 1.13 | 1.26 |

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 marks):**

A **thermo balance** is a precision instrument combining four critical sub-systems:

**(i) Microbalance (1.5 marks):** A high-sensitivity electronic microbalance (sensitivity ≈ 0.1 µg) continuously monitors the sample mass. It has a sample-weighing pan and a reference counterweight on a symmetric beam. Any mass change produces a deflection detected by a photodiode, which is converted into an electrical signal proportional to Δm. The sample is suspended from one arm of the balance by a thin platinum or alumina wire into the furnace hot zone.

**(ii) Furnace (1.5 marks):** A programmable resistance furnace (typically using SiC or Pt-Rh heating elements) surrounds the sample. It can be operated from room temperature up to 1600 °C with a controlled heating rate β = dT/dt (1–100 °C/min) or held isothermally. The furnace must provide a uniform hot zone (±1 °C) where the sample is located.

**(iii) Thermocouple (1 mark):** A Type-S (Pt/Pt-Rh) or Type-K (Chromel/Alumel) thermocouple is positioned as close as possible to the sample (without touching it) to provide an accurate measure of the specimen temperature. The thermocouple signal is fed to the temperature controller and to the data-acquisition system.

**(iv) Gas-purge system (1.5 marks):** A metered flow of high-purity gas (N₂, Ar, O₂, air, or even H₂) is passed through the furnace chamber at a controlled rate (typically 50–100 mL/min) using a mass flow controller. The atmosphere is critical: an oxidising atmosphere (O₂, air) produces mass *gain* (oxidation), whereas a reducing atmosphere (H₂) can cause mass *loss* (oxide reduction). The purge also removes volatile corrosion products.

**Working (1 mark):** The PC simultaneously logs time, temperature, and mass, plotting the **thermogram** in real time.

**Block diagram (0.5 marks):** Refer to the diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes.

---

**Part (b) — Data Analysis (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Convert Δm to ΔW = Δm / A (A = 4 cm²):**

| t (h) | ΔW (mg·cm⁻²) | (ΔW)² (mg·cm⁻²)² |
|---|---|---|
| 2 | 0.090 | 0.00810 |
| 5 | 0.140 | 0.01960 |
| 10 | 0.200 | 0.04000 |
| 20 | 0.2825 | 0.07981 |
| 25 | 0.315 | 0.09923 |

**Step 2 (i) Identify the rate law — Test (ΔW)² / t:**

| t | (ΔW)²/t |
|---|---|
| 2 | 0.00405 |
| 5 | 0.00392 |
| 10 | 0.00400 |
| 20 | 0.00399 |
| 25 | 0.00397 |

The ratio is essentially **constant ≈ 0.004**, confirming the **parabolic rate law**. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3 (ii) Compute k_p:** Use the least-squares formula:

$$k_p = \frac{\sum t_i (\Delta W_i)^2}{\sum t_i^2}$$

Numerator: $(2)(0.00810) + (5)(0.01960) + (10)(0.04000) + (20)(0.07981) + (25)(0.09923)$

$= 0.01620 + 0.09800 + 0.40000 + 1.59620 + 2.48075 = 4.59115$

Denominator: $4 + 25 + 100 + 400 + 625 = 1154$

$$k_p = \frac{4.59115}{1154} = 3.978 \times 10^{-3} \; (mg \cdot cm^{-2})^2 \cdot h^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{k_p \approx 3.98 \times 10^{-3} \; (mg \cdot cm^{-2})^2 \cdot h^{-1}} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 4 (iii) Oxide thickness at t = 25 h:** First compute ΔW at t = 25 h:

$$\Delta W = \sqrt{k_p \cdot t} = \sqrt{(3.978 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 25} = \sqrt{0.09945} = 0.3154 \; mg \cdot cm^{-2}$$

Convert to mass per unit area in g·cm⁻²:

$$\Delta W = 0.3154 \times 10^{-3} \; g \cdot cm^{-2} = 3.154 \times 10^{-4} \; g \cdot cm^{-2}$$

Convert mass to volume using density of Fe₂O₃ (5.24 g·cm⁻³):

$$V = \frac{m}{A \cdot \rho} = \frac{3.154 \times 10^{-4}}{5.24} = 6.020 \times 10^{-5} \; cm \; (= \text{thickness for unit area})$$

$$\boxed{x \approx 6.02 \times 10^{-5} \; cm = 0.602 \; \mu m} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**

**(a)** Explain **Wagner's theory of parabolic oxidation**. Discuss how the parabolic rate constant $k_p$ is related to the diffusion of ions through the growing oxide scale. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The parabolic rate constants for the oxidation of a Ni-Cr alloy at three different temperatures are:

| T (°C) | 700 | 800 | 900 |
|---|---|---|---|
| $k_p$ [(mg·cm⁻²)²·h⁻¹] | 1.20 × 10⁻⁴ | 9.50 × 10⁻⁴ | 6.10 × 10⁻³ |

Calculate the **activation energy** $E_a$ in kJ·mol⁻¹ using the Arrhenius approach. Comment on the value. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a) — Wagner's Theory of Parabolic Oxidation (7 marks):**

**Postulates (2.5 marks):**
1. A dense, adherent, and continuous oxide layer forms on the metal surface.
2. The oxide scale is a **mixed ionic-electronic conductor**, allowing both ions (cations and anions) and electrons to migrate.
3. **Thermodynamic equilibrium** is established at both the metal/scale and scale/gas interfaces.
4. The rate-determining step is the **solid-state diffusion** of ions (and electrons) through the growing oxide scale — *not* the interface reactions, which are considered fast.
5. The driving force for diffusion is the **gradient in electrochemical potential** of the migrating species across the scale.

**Mathematical formulation (2.5 marks):** Wagner derived that the parabolic rate constant is given by:

$$k_p = \int_{a_{O_2}^{\,I}}^{a_{O_2}^{\,II}} \left[ \frac{n_c \, n_e}{n} \cdot D_{ion} \cdot \frac{\sigma_e \, \sigma_c}{\sigma_e + \sigma_c} \right] d(\ln a_{O_2})$$

where:
- $n_c$, $n_e$ = charge numbers of the migrating cation and electron
- $D_{ion}$ = diffusion coefficient of the rate-limiting ionic species
- $\sigma_c$, $\sigma_e$ = partial ionic and electronic conductivities of the oxide
- $a_{O_2}^{I}$, $a_{O_2}^{II}$ = oxygen activities at the two interfaces
- $n$ = total charge number for the oxide

**Engineering significance (2 marks):** Wagner's theory explains why:
- *Doped* alloys (e.g., Cr in NiO) can dramatically reduce $k_p$ by altering ionic conductivity (Doping Principle — Wagner-Hauffe rules).
- The parabolic law gives a **self-limiting** behaviour: as the scale thickens, the rate drops, providing inherent protection — the basis of "stainless" steel and superalloy design.

---

**Part (b) — Activation Energy Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Convert temperatures to Kelvin:**

$T_1 = 700 + 273 = 973 \; K$
$T_2 = 800 + 273 = 1073 \; K$
$T_3 = 900 + 273 = 1173 \; K$

**Step 2: Compute 1/T and ln k_p:**

| T (K) | 1/T (K⁻¹) | k_p | ln k_p |
|---|---|---|---|
| 973 | 1.0278 × 10⁻³ | 1.20 × 10⁻⁴ | −9.028 |
| 1073 | 9.320 × 10⁻⁴ | 9.50 × 10⁻⁴ | −6.960 |
| 1173 | 8.525 × 10⁻⁴ | 6.10 × 10⁻³ | −5.099 |

**Step 3: Find the slope of ln k_p vs 1/T (Arrhenius plot):**

Using the first and third points:

$$m = \frac{(-5.099) - (-9.028)}{(8.525 \times 10^{-4}) - (1.0278 \times 10^{-3})}$$

$$m = \frac{3.929}{-1.753 \times 10^{-4}} = -2.241 \times 10^{4} \; K$$

(Check with middle point: slope between 973 K and 1073 K is $(-6.960 + 9.028)/(9.320 \times 10^{-4} - 1.0278 \times 10^{-3}) = 2.068/-9.58 \times 10^{-5} = -2.159 \times 10^4$. Close, confirming linearity.) **(2 marks)**

**Step 4: Calculate E_a:**

$$E_a = -m \cdot R = -(-2.241 \times 10^{4}) \cdot 8.314 = 1.863 \times 10^{5} \; J \cdot mol^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{E_a \approx 186 \; kJ \cdot mol^{-1}} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 5: Best-fit slope using all three points (1 mark):**

For a more rigorous least-squares fit through three points:
- Mean of 1/T: $\overline{x} = 9.708 \times 10^{-4} \; K^{-1}$
- Mean of ln k_p: $\overline{y} = -7.029$
- Sum of $(x_i - \overline{x})(y_i - \overline{y})$:
  - $(5.70 \times 10^{-5})(-1.999) = -1.140 \times 10^{-4}$
  - $(-3.88 \times 10^{-5})(0.069) = -2.68 \times 10^{-6}$
  - $(-1.82 \times 10^{-5})(1.930) = -3.51 \times 10^{-5}$
  - Sum = $-1.518 \times 10^{-4}$
- Sum of $(x_i - \overline{x})^2$:
  - $(5.70 \times 10^{-5})^2 + (-3.88 \times 10^{-5})^2 + (-1.82 \times 10^{-5})^2 = 3.249 \times 10^{-9} + 1.505 \times 10^{-9} + 0.331 \times 10^{-9} = 5.085 \times 10^{-9}$
- Slope = $-1.518 \times 10^{-4} / 5.085 \times 10^{-9} = -2.985 \times 10^{4}$

Using best-fit: $E_a = 2.985 \times 10^4 \times 8.314 = 2.482 \times 10^5 \; J \cdot mol^{-1} \approx 248 \; kJ \cdot mol^{-1}$

**Comment (1 mark):** A value of $E_a$ in the range 180–250 kJ·mol⁻¹ is characteristic of a **lattice-diffusion-controlled** oxidation process through a Cr₂O₃ or Al₂O₃-rich protective scale. This is consistent with the formation of a continuous, adherent, and protective oxide layer on Ni-Cr alloys — the basis of their use as heat-resistant engineering materials in gas-turbine and furnace applications.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in TGA Questions (Examiner's View):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to K** before computing 1/T in the Arrhenius equation. *Cost: 2 marks.*
> 2. **Using kg or g in some rows and mg in others** without unit consistency. Always pick one unit system and stick to it. *Cost: 1–2 marks.*
> 3. **Confusing the rate constant k_p with the rate** d(ΔW)/dt. The rate constant is constant; the rate decreases with time for parabolic kinetics.
> 4. **Not dividing by surface area A** — the rate law must be expressed in terms of mass *per unit area* (ΔW), not total mass (Δm).
> 5. **Misidentifying the rate law** — students often assume parabolic without testing ΔW/√t or (ΔW)²/t for constancy. Always perform a diagnostic test.
> 6. **Forgetting the sign in the Arrhenius equation** — the slope of ln k vs 1/T is *negative* (–Eₐ/R); using the wrong sign gives a *negative* activation energy, which is physically meaningless and immediately signals an error to the examiner.
> 7. **Not stating the units** of $k_p$ in the final answer. *Cost: 0.5 mark.*

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets for the Night Before the Exam:**

- ✅ **TGA = continuous weighing** of a sample vs temperature or time under controlled atmosphere; instrument = **thermo balance**; output = **thermogram**.
- ✅ **Isothermal** mode → constant T, mass vs t → used for **rate-law identification** and $k_p$ measurement.
- ✅ **Non-isothermal** mode → constant β (°C/min), mass vs T → used for **onset temperature** and reaction-stage identification.
- ✅ **Three rate laws:** Linear ($\Delta W = k_l t$), Parabolic ($(\Delta W)^2 = k_p t$), Logarithmic ($\Delta W = k_{\log} \log(t+t_0)$).
- ✅ **Parabolic** = Wagner diffusion-controlled = most common in engineering alloys at high T.
- ✅ **Diagnostic test:** If $\Delta W^2 / t$ is constant → **parabolic**; if $\Delta W / t$ is constant → **linear**.
- ✅ **Arrhenius form:** $\ln k_p = \ln A - E_a / (RT)$. Plot $\ln k_p$ vs $1/T$ → straight line of slope $-E_a/R$.
- ✅ **Compute $E_a$** as: $E_a = -R \cdot \text{slope}$ with $R = 8.314 \; J \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot K^{-1}$.
- ✅ **Always convert** °C to K before computing $1/T$ in the Arrhenius formula.
- ✅ **Always express** mass gain as $\Delta W = \Delta m / A$ in mg·cm⁻² or g·m⁻² — never use raw Δm in the rate law.
- ✅ **Use $k_p$ values at two temperatures** to compute $E_a$ via the two-point formula: $E_a = R \cdot \ln(k_2/k_1) / [(1/T_1) - (1/T_2)]$.
- ✅ **High $E_a$** (180–250 kJ/mol) → lattice-diffusion-controlled protective oxidation; **low $E_a$** (< 100 kJ/mol) → grain-boundary diffusion or short-circuit paths.
- ✅ **Atmosphere matters:** Oxidising gas → mass gain; reducing gas → mass loss; inert gas → no change.
- ✅ **Spallation** on a thermogram = sudden mass drop = mechanical failure of protective scale (cyclic oxidation problem).
- ✅ **ASTM standards** governing TGA: **ASTM E2550** (general TGA), **ASTM G54** (isothermal oxidation).
- ✅ **Industrial relevance:** Gas turbines (Ni-superalloys), boilers (ferritic steels), nuclear (Zircaloy), chemical reactors (hot corrosion).

> **Mnemonic to recall the parabolic equation:**
> *"**P**arabolic: **P**lot **P**ower 2 — $(\Delta W)^2$ vs $t$, slope = $k_p$."*

---

*End of Notes — Topic: Thermogravimetric Technique | Module 3 | PECHT527 Corrosion Engineering | KTU 2024 Scheme*

topics.title: Salt spray test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT527 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Salt spray test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

The **Salt Spray Test (SST)**, also universally known as the **Neutral Salt Spray (NSS) Test** or **Fog Test**, is a standardised, accelerated laboratory corrosion test method used to evaluate the relative corrosion resistance of metallic materials, metallic coatings, conversion coatings, organic coatings, and painted surfaces when exposed to a finely atomised chloride-rich saline atmosphere under strictly controlled environmental conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Governing Standards:**
> - **ASTM B117** – Standard Practice for Operating Salt Spray (Fog) Apparatus (USA)
> - **ISO 9227** – Corrosion tests in artificial atmospheres – Salt spray tests (International)
> - **JIS Z 2371** – Japanese Industrial Standard
> - **IS 5528** – Bureau of Indian Standards equivalent

The test is **not** designed to simulate real-service atmospheric corrosion, but to provide a **controlled, reproducible, and accelerated** corrosive environment for **quality control, ranking, and comparative screening** of protective coating systems.

## 1.2 Test Chamber Geometry and Test Solution Specification

| Parameter | Standard Condition (ASTM B117 / ISO 9227) |
|---|---|
| **NaCl solution concentration** | **5 ± 1 % by mass** in distilled or deionised water |
| **pH of collected solution** | **6.5 – 7.2** (at 25 ± 2 °C) |
| **Chamber temperature** | **35 ± 2 °C** (NSS) |
| **Spray collection rate** | **1.0 – 2.0 mL/hr per 80 cm²** of horizontal collecting area |
| **Air pressure to atomiser** | **69 – 172 kPa** (10 – 25 psi) |
| **Specimen inclination** | **15° – 30° from vertical**, supported on inert racks |
| **NaCl purity requirement** | **< 0.1 % total impurities** (no heavy metals like Cu, Ni) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Quality Gate for NaCl:** The salt used must be sodium chloride containing **less than 0.1 % of copper (Cu) and nickel (Ni)**, since even trace heavy-metal contamination can drastically skew the corrosion behaviour of the test specimen.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Day-in-a-Box" Idea

Imagine you want to know whether the paint on your bicycle can survive **5 years on the Kerala coast**, but you obviously cannot wait 5 years in the laboratory. So you place a painted panel inside a sealed, warm box and continuously mist it with a fine **5 % salt-water fog at 35 °C**.

In this box, the chloride ions that would normally attack the coating over months or years arrive at the metal surface within **hours**. This is exactly what the salt spray chamber does — it **compresses years of chloride exposure into days of testing**.

> [!TIP]
> **Student Mnemonic — "5-7-35-80":**
> **5**% NaCl, **pH 6.5–7.2**, **35**°C chamber, **80 cm²** collecting area for spray rate. Memorise this quartet — it appears in almost every KTU question on this topic.

## 1.4 Why 5 % NaCl and not Seawater?

Seawater contains roughly 3.5 % salts, but the standard prescribes **5 %** because:

1. It produces a **saturated, aggressive chloride concentration** that accelerates pitting initiation.
2. It maintains **stable solution chemistry** throughout the test duration (no precipitation).
3. It allows **worldwide laboratory reproducibility**, since the ionic strength is well-defined.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The salt solution is **not** an electrolyte bath. The specimen is **never immersed**; it is exposed only to the **falling mist**, and condensed droplets run off the surface. The corrosion mechanism is **atmospheric wet-dry chloride deposition**, not bulk-liquid electrochemistry.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Working Principle

The salt spray test functions by combining **three simultaneous corrosion-driving forces**:

1. **Chloride-ion attack** from the atomised 5 % NaCl fog.
2. **Elevated thermal energy (35 °C)** increasing reaction kinetics per the Arrhenius equation.
3. **Sustained thin-electrolyte film formation** on the specimen, where oxygen diffusion is rapid and corrosion is cathodically controlled.

### 2.1.1 Atomisation and Fog Generation

- Compressed air is first **humidified** by bubbling through a saturation tower containing deionised water (prevents salt-crystal blockage).
- The humidified air is then **pre-heated** to 46–49 °C and passed over the **atomising nozzle**, where it aspirates the **5 % NaCl solution** and breaks it into a **finely dispersed fog** of 5–20 µm droplet size.
- The fog rises due to its buoyancy, falls under gravity on the specimens, and **continuously refreshes the electrolyte film** on the metal.

### 2.1.2 Specimen Placement and Surface Wetting

- Specimens are placed on inert (plastic or glass-coated) racks at **15°–30° from vertical**.
- The inclined geometry ensures that droplets **do not pool** but **drain off**, simulating a rain-washed surface.
- Each specimen is positioned so that **condensed spray does not drip from one specimen onto another**, preventing cross-contamination.

### 2.1.3 Continuous Operation and Sampling

- The test runs **continuously, 24 hours a day**, with no dry cycles (for standard NSS).
- The chamber is opened **only for periodic inspection** (e.g., at 24, 48, 96, 168, 240, 500, 1000 h).
- Two **salt-spray collection funnels** continuously monitor spray rate and pH to verify chamber compliance.

## 2.2 The Three Standardised Variants of Salt Spray Testing

### 2.2.1 Neutral Salt Spray (NSS) — ASTM B117

- pH of solution: **6.5 – 7.2** (neutral).
- pH adjustment using **NaOH or HCl** (never buffer chemicals).
- Temperature: **35 ± 2 °C**.
- **Most widely used** variant, suitable for: zinc, cadmium, tin, aluminium, and stainless steel coatings; organic paint systems.

### 2.2.2 Acetic Acid Salt Spray (AASS) — ISO 9227

- pH of solution: **3.1 – 3.3** (acidic).
- Acidified by adding **glacial acetic acid** to the 5 % NaCl solution.
- Temperature: **35 ± 2 °C**.
- Used for: **decorative chromium plating on plastics and metals**, nickel-chromium coatings.

### 2.2.3 Copper-Accelerated Acetic Acid Salt Spray (CASS) — ISO 9227

- pH: **3.1 – 3.3** (acidic).
- Modified with addition of **(0.26 ± 0.02) g/L CuCl₂** to the 5 % NaCl solution.
- Temperature: **50 ± 2 °C**.
- Used for: **decorative Cu/Ni/Cr plating systems**, automotive bright trim.
- The Cu²⁺ ions act as a **cathodic depolariser**, dramatically accelerating the attack.

> [!NOTE]
> **CASS = Cupric + Acid + Salt + Spray.** A handy KTU-board mnemonic.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units / Standard Value | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Corrosion rate (mass loss) | $CR = \dfrac{87.6 \times W}{D \times A \times T}$ | mm/year | Quantifies metal loss |
| Corrosion rate (mils per year) | $mpy = \dfrac{534 \times W}{D \times A \times T}$ | mils/year | Imperial version |
| Spray rate (acceptance) | $R_{spray} = \dfrac{V_{collected}}{A_{funnel} \times t}$ | **1.0 – 2.0 mL/hr per 80 cm²** | Chamber quality control |
| Acceleration factor (approx.) | $AF \approx \dfrac{T_{service}}{T_{test}}$ | dimensionless | Roughly 50–500× real life |
| Salt concentration (mass %) | $C_{NaCl} = \dfrac{m_{salt}}{m_{solution}} \times 100$ | **5 ± 1 %** | Aggressive chloride level |
| Specimen inclination | $\theta$ | **15° – 30°** from vertical | Ensures drainage |
| Chamber temperature (NSS) | $T_{ch}$ | **35 ± 2 °C** | Kinetic acceleration |
| Chamber temperature (CASS) | $T_{ch}$ | **50 ± 2 °C** | Plus Cu acceleration |
| Exposure duration (typical) | $t$ | 24, 48, 96, 168, 500, 1000, 2000 h | Inspection intervals |
| Faraday corrosion rate | $CR = \dfrac{i_{corr} \times K \times EW}{D}$ | mm/year | Electrochemical cross-check |
| Arrhenius acceleration | $k = A e^{-E_a / RT}$ | — | Explains 35 °C choice |

> Where in the table above:
> - $W$ = mass loss (mg)
> - $D$ = metal density (g/cm³)
> - $A$ = exposed area (cm²)
> - $T$ = exposure time (hours)
> - $EW$ = equivalent weight
> - $i_{corr}$ = corrosion current density (µA/cm²)
> - $K$ = corrosion constant (typically $K = 3.27 \times 10^{-3}$ for mm/year)
> - $E_a$ = activation energy

## 2.4 Engineering Utility and Industrial Application

| Industry Sector | Typical Use of Salt Spray Test |
|---|---|
| **Automotive** | QA of zinc-nickel, e-coat, and powder-coat systems (target: 500–1000 h NSS) |
| **Marine & Offshore** | Pre-qualification of marine-grade coatings |
| **Fasteners & Hardware** | Zinc, zinc-flake, and Geomet coatings (e.g., 240–1000 h NSS) |
| **Electronics** | Tin-lead and gold plating qualification |
| **Aerospace** | Supplementary screening of primer + topcoat systems |
| **Appliances & White Goods** | Painted steel enclosures (96–500 h NSS) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A 1000 h NSS result is widely accepted in the fastener industry as a "correlate" of approximately 8–10 years of mild atmospheric exposure**, although KTU examiners emphasise that this correlation is **empirical and not scientifically universal**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Corrosion Rate Equation from Mass Loss

The corrosion rate in mm/year is derived from the **fundamental definition of linear metal penetration per unit time**.

### Step 1: Relating Mass Loss to Volume of Metal Dissolved

For a corroding metal of density $D$ (in g/cm³) undergoing uniform mass loss $W$ (in mg), the equivalent volume of metal dissolved is:

$$
V_{metal} = \dfrac{W}{D}
$$

where $W$ must be converted from mg to g (or $D$ to mg/cm³) for unit consistency.

### Step 2: Converting Volume to Average Linear Penetration Depth

If the corroded area is $A$ (in cm²), the average thickness of metal removed $x$ (in cm) is:

$$
x = \dfrac{V_{metal}}{A} = \dfrac{W}{D \times A}
$$

### Step 3: Converting Depth per Hour to Depth per Year

The exposure time is $T$ (in hours). The penetration rate in cm/hour is:

$$
r = \dfrac{x}{T} = \dfrac{W}{D \times A \times T}
$$

Since one year = 8760 hours, the annual penetration in cm/year is:

$$
r_{annual} = \dfrac{W \times 8760}{D \times A \times T}
$$

### Step 4: Converting cm/year to mm/year

Multiplying by 10 to convert cm to mm:

$$
CR \left(\dfrac{mm}{yr}\right) = \dfrac{87.6 \times W}{D \times A \times T}
$$

This is the canonical **ASTM G1 / G102 conversion** used universally. The constant **87.6** originates from $8760 \times 10 / 1000$, embedding the unit conversions from (mg→g), (cm→mm), and (hours→year) into a single multiplier.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 87.6?** Because $8760 \text{ h/yr} \times 10 \text{ mm/cm} \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/mg} = 87.6$. KTU examiners love asking "where does the 87.6 come from?"

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Board Style

> **Problem:** A mild-steel specimen of exposed area **30 cm²** is exposed in a salt spray chamber for **500 hours**. After cleaning, the mass loss is measured as **250 mg**. The density of mild steel is **7.86 g/cm³**. Calculate the corrosion rate in **(a) mm/year** and **(b) mpy**.

### Solution

**Step (a): Corrosion rate in mm/year**

Substituting into the standard formula:

$$
CR = \dfrac{87.6 \times W}{D \times A \times T}
$$

$$
CR = \dfrac{87.6 \times 250}{7.86 \times 30 \times 500}
$$

$$
CR = \dfrac{21900}{117900}
$$

$$
\boxed{CR = 0.1857 \text{ mm/year}}
$$

**Step (b): Corrosion rate in mpy**

Using the mpy version:

$$
mpy = \dfrac{534 \times W}{D \times A \times T}
$$

$$
mpy = \dfrac{534 \times 250}{7.86 \times 30 \times 500}
$$

$$
mpy = \dfrac{133500}{117900}
$$

$$
\boxed{mpy = 1.132 \text{ mils/year}}
$$

### Interpretation

| Result | Engineering Classification |
|---|---|
| 0.1857 mm/yr | Outstanding (> 0.5 mm/yr is poor) |
| 1.132 mpy | Excellent (< 5 mpy) |

A coating that protects mild steel to this level in NSS is generally regarded as **fit for indoor-to-mild-atmospheric service**.

## 3.3 Spray-Rate Compliance Calculation

> **Problem:** During a 4-hour quality check, **120 mL** of solution is collected from **two** standard collection funnels, each of horizontal area **80 cm²**. Verify whether the spray rate complies with **ASTM B117**.

### Step 1: Total collection area

$$
A_{total} = 2 \times 80 = 160 \text{ cm}^2
$$

### Step 2: Spray rate

$$
R_{spray} = \dfrac{V}{A_{total} \times t} = \dfrac{120}{160 \times 4} = \dfrac{120}{640}
$$

$$
R_{spray} = 0.1875 \text{ mL/hr per cm}^2
$$

### Step 3: Normalise to the standard 80 cm² basis

The standard measures "mL/hr per 80 cm²", so:

$$
R_{standardised} = 0.1875 \times 80 = 15.0 \text{ mL/hr per 80 cm}^2
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **This is far above the 1.0–2.0 mL/hr limit.** The chamber is **non-compliant** — the atomiser pressure is too high, or the nozzle is worn. The test result would be **invalid** and the experimenter must recalibrate the atomiser before continuing.

## 3.4 Acceleration Factor Derivation (Conceptual)

Using the Arrhenius relation, the rate enhancement at $T_{test} = 308$ K (35 °C) versus $T_{service} = 298$ K (25 °C) for a typical corrosion activation energy $E_a = 60$ kJ/mol is:

$$
\dfrac{k_{test}}{k_{service}} = \exp\left[ \dfrac{E_a}{R} \left( \dfrac{1}{T_{service}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{test}} \right) \right]
$$

$$
\dfrac{k_{test}}{k_{service}} = \exp\left[ \dfrac{60000}{8.314} \left( \dfrac{1}{298} - \dfrac{1}{308} \right) \right]
$$

$$
\dfrac{k_{test}}{k_{service}} = \exp\left[ 7216.7 \times (0.003356 - 0.003247) \right]
$$

$$
\dfrac{k_{test}}{k_{service}} = \exp\left[ 7216.7 \times 1.092 \times 10^{-4} \right]
$$

$$
\dfrac{k_{test}}{k_{service}} = \exp(0.788) \approx 2.20
$$

So the **thermal contribution alone** is only a factor of ~2. The remaining acceleration (often 50–500× total in practice) comes from the **aggressive chloride chemistry and continuous wet-dry cycling**, not from the 10 °C temperature rise.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Salt Spray Test Chamber

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Compressed Air Supply] --> B[Humidification Tower]
    B --> C[Pre-heater to 47 C]
    C --> D[Atomising Nozzle Assembly]
    E[5 Percent NaCl Reservoir] --> D
    D --> F[Salt Spray Fog Cloud]
    F --> G[Test Specimens on Inclined Racks]
    G --> H[Condensate Drain to Reservoir]
    I[Chamber Heater 35 C plus or minus 2] --> G
    J[Collection Funnels 80 cm squared] --> K[Spray Rate and pH Monitor]
    K --> L[Compliance Log]
    H --> M[Waste Treatment Unit]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow of a Standardised Salt Spray Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Specimen Preparation and Cleaning] --> S2[Step 2: Weighing and Area Measurement]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Masking and Edge Protection]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Mounting on Racks at 20 Degree Tilt]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Chamber Pre-conditioning to 35 C]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Start Continuous NSS Exposure]
    S6 --> S7{Periodic Inspection at 24, 48, 96, 168, 500, 1000 h}
    S7 --> S8[Visual Rating and Mass Loss Recording]
    S8 --> S9[Final Evaluation per ASTM B117 / ISO 9227]
    S9 --> S10[Report: Hours to First Rust, Blistering, Creep]
```

## 4.3 Comparative Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Variant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```
