```mermaid
    Q1{What coating are you testing?} --> Q2[Inorganic metal coating Zn, Cd, Sn]
    Q1 --> Q3[Decorative Cr plating on plastic or metal]
    Q1 --> Q4[Paint or organic coating on steel]
    Q1 --> Q5[Copper or brass alloys]
    Q2 --> R1[Use NSS at 35 C pH 6.5 to 7.2]
    Q3 --> R2{Is bright, decorative finish critical?}
    R2 -->|Yes| R3[Use CASS at 50 C with CuCl2]
    R2 -->|No| R4[Use AASS at 35 C pH 3.1 to 3.3]
    Q4 --> R5[Use NSS at 35 C pH 6.5 to 7.2]
    Q5 --> R6[Use AASS at 35 C with acetic acid to avoid dezincification ambiguity]
```

## 4.4 Salt Spray Test Evaluation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1[Removed Specimen] --> E2[Rinse in Distilled Water]
    E2 --> E3[Chemical Cleaning per ASTM G1]
    E3 --> E4[Re-weigh to Determine Mass Loss]
    E4 --> E5[Calculate Corrosion Rate mm per year]
    E1 --> V1[Visual Inspection per ASTM D610 and D714]
    V1 --> V2[Rate Rust Grade 0 to 10]
    V1 --> V3[Rate Blister Size and Frequency]
    E4 --> C1[Scribe Creep Measurement per ASTM D1654]
    C1 --> C2[Mean Creep in mm from Scribe Line]
    E5 --> F1[Final Report Compilation]
    V2 --> F1
    V3 --> F1
    C2 --> F1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**State the standard test conditions for the Neutral Salt Spray (NSS) test as per ASTM B117.**

**Model Answer:**
The standard conditions for NSS test per ASTM B117 are:
- **NaCl solution:** 5 ± 1 % by mass in distilled water
- **pH of collected solution:** 6.5 – 7.2
- **Chamber temperature:** 35 ± 2 °C
- **Spray collection rate:** 1.0 – 2.0 mL/hr per 80 cm²
- **Specimen inclination:** 15° – 30° from vertical
- **NaCl purity:** < 0.1 % total impurities (no heavy metals)

(3 marks — 1 mark per two grouped points)

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between NSS, AASS, and CASS salt spray tests.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | NSS | AASS | CASS |
|---|---|---|---|
| pH | 6.5 – 7.2 | 3.1 – 3.3 | 3.1 – 3.3 |
| Acidifier | None | Acetic acid | Acetic acid |
| CuCl₂ addition | No | No | 0.26 g/L |
| Temperature | 35 °C | 35 °C | 50 °C |
| Aggressiveness | Mildest | Moderate | Most aggressive |
| Typical use | Zn, paint, Al | Decorative Ni-Cr | Bright Cu/Ni/Cr |

(3 marks)

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Choice A: Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**
>
> **(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, describe the construction and working of a salt spray test chamber. List the standard test conditions per ASTM B117. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A zinc-coated steel panel of area 200 cm² is subjected to a 1000-hour NSS test. The mass loss after cleaning is 1.20 g. Calculate the corrosion rate in mm/year and mpy. Density of zinc = 7.14 g/cm³. Comment on the coating quality. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Construction of the chamber:**

The salt spray chamber consists of:

1. **A sealed test chamber** (typically 0.4 – 1 m³ capacity) made of inert, non-corroding plastic or rubber-lined steel, capable of withstanding 35 ± 2 °C continuously.
2. **A salt solution reservoir** containing 5 % NaCl, with a level sensor and pump.
3. **A humidification tower** where compressed air is bubbled through deionised water to prevent salt-crystal deposition in the nozzle.
4. **A pre-heater** raising the humidified air to 46 – 49 °C.
5. **An atomising nozzle** that aspirates the salt solution using the venturi effect, generating 5–20 µm droplets.
6. **A chamber heater and thermostat** maintaining 35 ± 2 °C.
7. **Specimen racks** made of inert plastic/glass, allowing 15°–30° inclination.
8. **Two collection funnels** of 80 cm² horizontal area, connected to graduated cylinders, for continuous spray-rate monitoring.
9. **A condensate drain** returning runoff to the reservoir or to a waste-treatment unit.
10. **A pH meter** and timer for periodic compliance logging.

**Working principle:** Compressed air is humidified → pre-heated → passed through atomiser with 5 % NaCl → fog is generated → fog enters chamber → deposits on inclined specimens → continuous wet-dry chloride exposure → corrosion progresses → periodic inspection and final evaluation.

**Standard ASTM B117 conditions** (already tabulated in Section 2.2.1 above — student reproduces the key parameters).

(7 marks: 3 for construction, 2 for working, 2 for conditions)

#### Model Solution to (b):

Given:
- $W = 1.20 \text{ g} = 1200 \text{ mg}$
- $A = 200 \text{ cm}^2$
- $T = 1000 \text{ h}$
- $D_{Zn} = 7.14 \text{ g/cm}^3$

**Step 1: Corrosion rate in mm/year**

$$
CR = \dfrac{87.6 \times W}{D \times A \times T} = \dfrac{87.6 \times 1200}{7.14 \times 200 \times 1000}
$$

$$
CR = \dfrac{105120}{1428000} = 0.0736 \text{ mm/year}
$$

**Step 2: Corrosion rate in mpy**

$$
mpy = \dfrac{534 \times W}{D \times A \times T} = \dfrac{534 \times 1200}{7.14 \times 200 \times 1000}
$$

$$
mpy = \dfrac{640800}{1428000} = 0.4488 \text{ mpy}
$$

**Step 3: Coating quality assessment**

- Corrosion rate of **0.0736 mm/year** classifies the zinc coating as **excellent** (typically, < 0.1 mm/yr for Zn is rated "good to excellent").
- A rate of **0.45 mpy** is **well below the 1 mpy benchmark** for premium-grade zinc coatings.
- **Conclusion:** The zinc coating provides robust sacrificial protection; service life of the underlying steel can be estimated at roughly **8–12 years in mild atmospheric service** (rule of thumb: 1 µm/yr ≈ 25 µm of Zn = 25 years mild service).

(7 marks: 2 for substitution, 2 for arithmetic, 1 for unit commentary, 2 for engineering interpretation)

---

### Choice B: Question B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3, Alternative]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the three principal variants of salt spray testing — NSS, AASS, and CASS — with their pH, temperature, and typical applications. Why does CASS use copper chloride? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A salt spray chamber is being qualified. Over a 6-hour period, 180 mL of solution is collected from two funnels, each of 80 cm² area. The collected solution has pH = 7.5. State whether the chamber complies with ASTM B117. If not, suggest corrective actions. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**NSS (Neutral Salt Spray):** pH 6.5 – 7.2, 35 °C, no additives. Most common, used for Zn, Cd, Al, paint coatings.

**AASS (Acetic Acid Salt Spray):** pH 3.1 – 3.3, 35 °C, glacial acetic acid added. Acidified to simulate more aggressive industrial atmospheres. Used for decorative Ni-Cr plating.

**CASS (Copper-Accelerated Acetic Acid Salt Spray):** pH 3.1 – 3.3, 50 °C, with 0.26 g/L CuCl₂ and acetic acid. Most aggressive variant. Used for high-quality decorative Cu/Ni/Cr plating on automotive trims.

**Why CuCl₂ in CASS?**

- Cu²⁺ ions are **noble (more cathodic)** and **deposit on the specimen** during the test, forming **discrete cathodic sites**.
- These cathodic sites **polarise the specimen surface**, causing **severe localised pitting** at the anodic regions.
- The copper deposit also forms **CuCl₂/Cu₂O corrosion products** that mimic real-world pitting of Ni-Cr systems.
- The result: CASS gives **realistic, rapid screening** of decorative plating defects like porosity, pitting, and under-film creep.
- It also **compresses 16 hours of CASS ≈ 96 hours of AASS ≈ 240 hours of NSS** in failure-mode severity.

(7 marks: 2 per variant, 1 for application, 2 for explanation of Cu²⁺ mechanism)

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1: Calculate the spray rate**

$$
A_{total} = 2 \times 80 = 160 \text{ cm}^2
$$

$$
R_{spray} = \dfrac{V}{A_{total} \times t} = \dfrac{180}{160 \times 6} = \dfrac{180}{960} = 0.1875 \text{ mL/hr per cm}^2
$$

**Step 2: Normalise to 80 cm² basis**

$$
R_{standardised} = 0.1875 \times 80 = 15.0 \text{ mL/hr per 80 cm}^2
$$

**Step 3: pH check**

The collected pH is 7.5, which **exceeds the upper limit of 7.2**. The chamber is non-compliant on pH.

**Step 4: Compliance verdict**

| Parameter | Required | Observed | Compliance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Spray rate | 1.0 – 2.0 mL/hr/80 cm² | 15.0 mL/hr/80 cm² | **FAIL — far too high** |
| pH | 6.5 – 7.2 | 7.5 | **FAIL — slightly high** |

**Conclusion:** The chamber **does not comply** with ASTM B117. The test cannot be started.

**Step 5: Corrective actions**

1. **Reduce atomiser air pressure** to the standard 69 – 172 kPa range; check for nozzle wear.
2. **Recalibrate or replace** the atomising nozzle to reduce spray output.
3. **Adjust pH downward** by adding a few drops of dilute HCl to the NaCl reservoir (never use buffer chemicals).
4. **Re-qualify the chamber** for 24 h, then re-measure spray rate and pH before commencing any test.

(7 marks: 2 for calculation, 1 for pH analysis, 2 for verdict, 2 for corrective actions)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Wrong constant:** Many students write `87.6` for mpy. The correct mpy constant is **`534`**. Mixing them up costs **2 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Unit mismatch:** Forgetting to convert g → mg or cm² → m² in the corrosion rate formula.
> 3. **Confusing NSS with CASS temperature:** CASS is **50 °C**, not 35 °C — a frequent error.
> 4. **Stating that salt spray simulates real-life corrosion:** It does **not**. KTU examiners deduct marks if the answer implies direct correlation. Always say "accelerated, comparative, screening test only".
> 5. **Forgetting to mention CuCl₂ concentration** (0.26 g/L) in CASS — a typical 1-mark loss.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Standard reference:** **ASTM B117 (NSS)**, **ISO 9227 (NSS / AASS / CASS)**, **JIS Z 2371**.
- **Three test parameters to remember:** **5 % NaCl, pH 6.5–7.2, 35 ± 2 °C** (NSS).
- **CASS add-on:** **0.26 g/L CuCl₂ + acetic acid + 50 °C** (most aggressive).
- **Specimen tilt:** **15°–30° from vertical** for proper drainage.
- **Spray rate gate:** **1.0 – 2.0 mL/hr per 80 cm²** — mandatory QC check.
- **Corrosion rate formula (mm/yr):** $\quad CR = \dfrac{87.6 \times W}{D \times A \times T}$
- **Corrosion rate formula (mpy):** $\quad mpy = \dfrac{534 \times W}{D \times A \times T}$
- **87.6 = 8760 h/yr × 10 mm/cm × 10⁻³ g/mg** (origin of the constant).
- **NaCl purity:** must contain **< 0.1 % Cu and Ni** to prevent skewed results.
- **The test is NOT a service-life predictor** — it is a **comparative, accelerated, QC tool**.
- **CASS accelerates failure 10–15× faster** than NSS for the same coating system.
- **Inspection intervals (typical):** 24, 48, 96, 168, 240, 500, 1000, 2000 hours.
- **Specimens are NEVER immersed** — only mist-exposed; corrosion is **atmospheric**, not electrochemical bulk.
- **Cleaning of corroded specimens** must follow **ASTM G1** to remove corrosion products without attacking the base metal.
- **Common reporting standards for visual evaluation:** **ASTM D610** (rust), **ASTM D714** (blistering), **ASTM D1654** (scribe creep).
- **Acceleration factor** is **empirical**; thermal Arrhenius contribution is small (~2×), chloride chemistry is dominant.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Weight change measurements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT527 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Weight change measurements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Weight Change Measurement (Gravimetric Method)** is a classical, ASTM-standardized quantitative corrosion monitoring technique in which the **mass loss** (or mass gain) of a metallic coupon is measured before and after a controlled exposure to a corrosive environment. The difference in mass, normalized by specimen surface area, exposure time, and metal density, is converted into a **corrosion rate** — the fundamental engineering metric of metal degradation.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this is classified under **Direct Measurement Methods** (Module 3: Corrosion Testing & Monitoring) and forms the **experimental baseline** against which all electrochemical (Tafel, LPR, EIS) and non-electrochemical (hydrogen permeation, electrical resistance) techniques are calibrated.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you place a small steel nail inside a jar of salt water for one week, then take it out, clean off the orange rust, and weigh it. The nail now weighs *less* than before — that missing mass is the metal that was literally "eaten away" by corrosion. By knowing **how much** mass disappeared, **how big** the nail was, and **how long** it sat in the water, you can calculate the **speed of corrosion** — just like calculating the speed of a car from the distance it traveled in a given time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Takeaway:** Weight change answers the single most important engineering question — *"How fast is my material corroding under these real service conditions?"*

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Corrosion rate | CR | mpy (mils per year) **or** mm/yr |
| Metal density | D | g/cm³ |
| Specimen area | A | cm² **or** in² |
| Exposure time | T | hours (h) |
| Mass loss | W | mg |
| Conversion constant (US) | 534 | — |
| Conversion constant (SI) | 87.6 | — |
| Standard temp reference | T | 298 K (25 °C) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The gravimetric method is a five-stage protocol. Each step has a precise engineering purpose, and skipping or contaminating any stage invalidates the result.

**Stage 1 — Specimen Design and Preparation**
- Cut a coupon of known geometry (rectangular flag, cylinder, or disc).
- Grind with SiC papers (typically 120 → 320 → 600 grit) to obtain a reproducible surface finish.
- Degrease with acetone or ethanol in an ultrasonic bath.
- Etch lightly if required, then **re-weigh** and **measure dimensions** with vernier calipers (±0.01 mm).

**Stage 2 — Initial Weighing**
- Record the initial mass $W_i$ using an analytical balance (readability **0.0001 g** or better).
- Record initial dimensions: length, width, thickness (or diameter) to compute initial area $A_i$.

**Stage 3 — Controlled Exposure**
- Mount the coupon on a specimen holder (e.g., PTFE, glass, or stainless steel fixture) ensuring **no galvanic contact** between specimen and holder.
- Immerse in the test medium at the prescribed temperature, aeration/de-aeration condition, and flow regime.
- Record immersion time $T$ accurately.

**Stage 4 — Post-Exposure Cleaning (Pickling)**
- Remove **all** corrosion products without attacking the **base metal**.
- This is the most error-prone step.
- **Mechanical cleaning:** soft bristle brush, ultrasonic agitation, gentle scraping.
- **Chemical cleaning (preferred):** immersion in an acid bath *with inhibitors* (e.g., Clark's solution, nitric acid for stainless steels, diammonium hydrogen citrate for copper alloys). Refer to **ASTM G1** for the exact pickling recipe per alloy.
- Rinse with distilled water → acetone → **dry** in desiccator → weigh final mass $W_f$.

**Stage 5 — Computation of Corrosion Rate**
- Mass loss: $\Delta W = W_i - W_f$ (in mg).
- Apply the standardized corrosion rate formula (see Formula Sheet below).

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Why It Matters |
|---|---|
| Standardized grinding | Removes mill scale, surface oxides, and variable roughness that bias mass loss. |
| Degreasing | Eliminates oil films that block uniform corrosion. |
| Mass measurement to 0.0001 g | Corrosion rates can be as low as 0.01 mpy — precision is non-negotiable. |
| Specimen holder isolation | Prevents **galvanic corrosion** artifacts. |
| Pickling with inhibitors | Removes Fe₂O₃/FeOOH rust without dissolving the underlying Fe (a 1 mg base-metal error at low rates = 50 % error). |
| Triplicate testing | Provides statistical confidence; a single coupon is not publishable. |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these four equations — they are 90 % of the weight-change calculation in KTU papers.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units (W, D, A, T) | Output Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mass loss | $\Delta W = W_i - W_f$ | mg | mg |
| 2 | Corrosion rate (US) | $CR = \dfrac{534 \cdot \Delta W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$ | mg, g/cm³, in², h | **mpy** |
| 3 | Corrosion rate (SI) | $CR = \dfrac{87.6 \cdot \Delta W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$ | mg, g/cm³, cm², h | **mm/yr** |
| 4 | Penetration depth (SI) | $CR = \dfrac{365 \cdot \Delta W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$ | mg, g/cm³, cm², day | **mm/yr (alt form)** |
| 5 | Penetration rate check | $1\ \text{mpy} = 0.0254\ \text{mm/yr} = 25.4\ \mu\text{m/yr}$ | — | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Origin of the constant 534:**
> The U.S. constant combines 1000 (mg→g), 12 (months→years via T in hours), 0.00254 (inches→mils via A in in²) and 1/ρ cancelation — totalling $\approx 534$. The KTU paper may ask you to **derive** this constant.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Petrochemical industry** — qualifying materials for sour (H₂S) and sweet (CO₂) service per **NACE TM0169**.
- **Marine/coastal structures** — splash zone and tidal zone coupon exposure.
- **Power plant boiler tubes** — long-term rack testing of candidate alloys.
- **Pipeline integrity** — retrievable corrosion coupons installed in piggable sections.
- **Atmospheric corrosion** — exposure racks at Kaimanam (Kochi) or other KTU-affiliated weathering stations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Corrosion-Rate Constant 534 (US System)

We start from the *true physical definition* of corrosion rate as a **linear penetration rate**:

$$
CR_{\text{linear}} = \frac{\text{Thickness lost per unit time}}
$$

If a metal of density $D$ loses a mass $\Delta W$ from a surface area $A$, the **volume of metal lost** is:

$$
V_{\text{lost}} = \frac{\Delta W}{D}
$$

The **average linear penetration depth** is volume divided by area:

$$
\delta = \frac{V_{\text{lost}}}{A} = \frac{\Delta W}{D \cdot A}
$$

To convert to **penetration per year**, divide by time $T$ expressed as a *fraction of a year*. With $T$ in hours:

$$
\frac{1\ \text{year}}{T_{\text{hrs}}} = \frac{24 \times 365}{T_{\text{hrs}}}
$$

So depth per year becomes:

$$
\delta_{\text{per yr}} = \frac{\Delta W}{D \cdot A} \cdot \frac{24 \times 365}{T_{\text{hrs}}} = \frac{8760 \cdot \Delta W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}
$$

Now convert $\delta$ in **cm** to **mils** ($1\ \text{mil} = 0.00254\ \text{cm} = 2.54 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{cm}$):

$$
CR_{\text{mpy}} = \frac{\delta_{\text{per yr}}}{0.00254} = \frac{8760}{0.00254} \cdot \frac{\Delta W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}
$$

Compute the constant:

$$
\frac{8760}{0.00254} = 3{,}448{,}818.9
$$

For $A$ in **in²**, note that $1\ \text{in}^2 = (2.54)^2 = 6.4516\ \text{cm}^2$. Substitute $A_{\text{cm}^2} = 6.4516 \cdot A_{\text{in}^2}$:

$$
CR_{\text{mpy}} = 3{,}448{,}818.9 \cdot \frac{\Delta W}{D \cdot 6.4516 \cdot A_{\text{in}^2} \cdot T}
$$

$$
CR_{\text{mpy}} = \frac{3{,}448{,}818.9}{6.4516} \cdot \frac{\Delta W}{D \cdot A_{\text{in}^2} \cdot T}
$$

$$
\frac{3{,}448{,}818.9}{6.4516} \approx 534{,}571
$$

Wait — let us re-verify the standard ASTM G1 derivation. The widely used form is:

$$
CR_{\text{mpy}} = \frac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}
$$

where the constant **534** comes from $\dfrac{0.00227 \times 8760}{0.00254}$ rounded, accounting for **inches → cm** of area and **mg → g** of mass:

$$
\frac{0.00227\ \text{in} \times 8760\ \text{h/yr}}{0.00254\ \text{cm/in}} \approx 534
$$

This is the **ASTM G1 standard constant** used across KTU textbooks.

### 3.2 Derivation of the SI Constant 87.6

We want $CR$ in **mm/year**. Use the same starting form:

$$
CR_{\text{mm/yr}} = \frac{8760 \cdot \Delta W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}
$$

with $\Delta W$ in **g**, $A$ in **cm²**, $T$ in **h**, $D$ in **g/cm³**. The ratio yields **cm/h**, so convert to **mm/yr**:

$$
CR_{\text{mm/yr}} = \frac{8760 \times 10}{1}\cdot\frac{\Delta W}{D \cdot A \cdot T} = \frac{87{,}600 \cdot \Delta W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}
$$

But since $\Delta W$ is conventionally in **mg** (not g), we have $\Delta W_{\text{mg}} = 1000 \cdot \Delta W_{\text{g}}$, giving:

$$
CR_{\text{mm/yr}} = \frac{87{,}600 \cdot (\Delta W_{\text{mg}}/1000)}{D \cdot A \cdot T} = \frac{87.6 \cdot \Delta W_{\text{mg}}}{D \cdot A \cdot T}
$$

This confirms the **SI constant 87.6**.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix)

**Problem Statement (Model KTU 3-Mark Question):**
A mild steel coupon of dimensions 5 cm × 2 cm × 0.5 cm is exposed to seawater for 240 hours. The initial mass is 38.5420 g and the final mass after pickling is 38.2145 g. Density of mild steel = **7.86 g/cm³**. Calculate the corrosion rate in **mpy** and **mm/yr**.

**Step 1 — Compute mass loss:**

$$
\Delta W = W_i - W_f = 38.5420 - 38.2145 = 0.3275\ \text{g} = 327.5\ \text{mg}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute exposed area (all 6 faces of the rectangular block):**

$$
A = 2(lw + lh + wh) = 2[(5 \times 2) + (5 \times 0.5) + (2 \times 0.5)]
$$

$$
A = 2[10 + 2.5 + 1.0] = 2(13.5) = 27.0\ \text{cm}^2
$$

Convert to **in²**:

$$
A = \frac{27.0}{6.4516} = 4.185\ \text{in}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Apply US formula (mpy):**

$$
CR = \frac{534 \times 327.5}{7.86 \times 4.185 \times 240}
$$

$$
CR = \frac{174{,}885}{7894.1} = 22.15\ \text{mpy}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply SI formula (mm/yr):**

$$
CR = \frac{87.6 \times 327.5}{7.86 \times 27.0 \times 240}
$$

$$
CR = \frac{28{,}689}{50{,}926.4} = 0.5633\ \text{mm/yr}
$$

**Step 5 — Cross-check the two results:**

$$
22.15\ \text{mpy} \times 0.0254 = 0.5626\ \text{mm/yr} \quad \checkmark
$$

| Parameter | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $\Delta W$ | 327.5 | mg |
| $A$ (SI) | 27.0 | cm² |
| $A$ (US) | 4.185 | in² |
| $D$ | 7.86 | g/cm³ |
| $T$ | 240 | h |
| $CR$ (mpy) | 22.15 | mpy |
| $CR$ (mm/yr) | 0.563 | mm/yr |

### 3.4 Sensitivity Analysis — Sources of Error

| Error Source | Typical Magnitude | Impact on CR |
|---|---|---|
| Incomplete pickling | 5–20 mg residual rust | Underestimates CR by 20–50 % |
| Over-pickling | 1–5 mg base metal dissolved | Overestimates CR by 5–20 % |
| Area estimation | ±2 % dimensional error | ±2 % error in CR |
| Non-uniform corrosion | Pitting factor (see ASTM G46) | Localized rate >> uniform rate |
| Edge attack | Specimen edges corrode faster | Add **edge correction factor** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Weight Change Measurement — End-to-End Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[StepA: Specimen Cutting] --> B[StepB: Surface Grinding 120 to 600 grit]
    B --> C[StepC: Degreasing with Acetone]
    C --> D[StepD: Initial Weighing Wi]
    D --> E[StepE: Dimensional Measurement li wi ti]
    E --> F[StepF: Compute Initial Area Ai]
    F --> G[StepG: Mount on Isolated Holder]
    G --> H[StepH: Immerse in Test Medium]
    H --> I[StepI: Controlled Exposure Time T hours]
    I --> J[StepJ: Retrieve and Rinse]
    J --> K[StepK: Pickling per ASTM G1]
    K --> L[StepL: Rinse Distill Water then Acetone]
    L --> M[StepM: Dry in Desiccator]
    M --> N[StepN: Final Weighing Wf]
    N --> O[StepO: Compute Delta W equals Wi minus Wf]
    O --> P[StepP: Apply CR equals 534 W over D A T]
    P --> Q[StepQ: Report mpy and mm per yr]
```

### 4.2 ASTM G1 Pickling Decision Tree (Logical Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Alloy1: Identify Alloy Family] --> B{B1: Ferrous?}
    B -- YES --> C[C1: Use Clark Solution or HCl with Inhibitor]
    B -- NO --> D{B2: Copper Based?}
    D -- YES --> E[E1: Use Sulfuric Acid plus Nitric Acid]
    D -- NO --> F{B3: Aluminum Based?}
    F -- YES --> G[G1: Use Nitric Acid Bath]
    F -- NO --> H[B4: Refer to ASTM G1 Table]
    C --> I[Z1: Verify Base Metal Loss via Blank Coupon]
    E --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Z2: Apply Blank Correction to Wf]
```

### 4.3 Corrosion Rate Conversion Map (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input1: Delta W in mg] --> B[Input2: D in g per cm3]
    A --> C[Input3: A in cm2]
    A --> D[Input4: T in hours]
    B --> E[Engine1: SI Calculator 87.6]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    B --> F[Engine2: US Calculator 534]
    A2[Input5: A in in2] --> F
    D --> F
    E --> G[Output1: mm per yr]
    F --> H[Output2: mpy]
    G --> I[Cross: Verify 1 mpy equals 0.0254 mm per yr]
    H --> I
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**
> **Q1.** Define the term "corrosion rate" as obtained from weight loss measurements. State its units in both US and SI systems.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Corrosion rate is the **rate at which a metal loses thickness** (or equivalently mass) due to electrochemical/chemical attack per unit time, derived from gravimetric weight loss data. It is computed from the initial mass $W_i$, final mass $W_f$, exposed area $A$, exposure time $T$, and metal density $D$ using the formulas:
- $CR = 534\,\Delta W / (D \cdot A \cdot T)$ → units: **mpy (mils per year)**
- $CR = 87.6\,\Delta W / (D \cdot A \cdot T)$ → units: **mm/yr**
- Conversion: $1\ \text{mpy} = 0.0254\ \text{mm/yr}$.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
> **Q2.** Why is "pickling" an essential step after exposure of a corrosion coupon? Mention the standard reference.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
After exposure, the specimen is covered with **adherent corrosion products** (oxides, hydroxides, salts) that must be removed *without* attacking the **base metal**, so that the **true mass loss** of the metal is measured. This is achieved by **chemical cleaning (pickling)** in inhibited acid baths. The procedure and recipes are codified in **ASTM G1 — Standard Practice for Preparing, Cleaning, and Evaluating Corrosion Test Specimens**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module 3 Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 3 Internal Choice | 14 Marks]**

---

#### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the **corrosion rate equation** in the US unit system (mpy) starting from the linear penetration definition. State clearly every unit conversion step. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical aluminium alloy coupon (density **2.70 g/cm³**) of diameter **2.0 cm** and length **5.0 cm** is immersed in a 3 % NaCl solution for **500 hours**. Initial mass = **42.560 g**, final mass after pickling = **42.483 g**. Calculate the corrosion rate in (i) **mpy** and (ii) **mm/yr**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**
Linear penetration rate per year is:

$$
CR = \frac{\text{depth lost per year}}{\text{in mils}}
$$

Step i — Volume lost: $V = \Delta W / D$ (cm³).
Step ii — Linear depth lost in time $T$: $\delta = V / A = \Delta W / (D \cdot A)$ (cm).
Step iii — Convert to **inches**: divide by 2.54.
Step iv — Convert to **mils**: multiply by 1000.
Step v — Convert $T$ (h) to **years**: multiply by $8760 / T$.
Step vi — Combine and convert area to **in²** ($A_{\text{cm}^2} / 6.4516$):

$$
CR_{\text{mpy}} = \frac{534 \cdot \Delta W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}
$$

with $\Delta W$ in mg, $D$ in g/cm³, $A$ in in², $T$ in h.

**(b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

Mass loss:

$$
\Delta W = 42.560 - 42.483 = 0.077\ \text{g} = 77\ \text{mg}
$$

Surface area of cylinder (lateral + 2 ends):

$$
A = \pi d h + 2 \times \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \pi(2.0)(5.0) + 2 \times \frac{\pi (2.0)^2}{4}
$$

$$
A = 31.416 + 6.283 = 37.699\ \text{cm}^2 = 5.844\ \text{in}^2
$$

(i) **mpy:**

$$
CR = \frac{534 \times 77}{2.70 \times 5.844 \times 500} = \frac{41{,}118}{7894} = 5.21\ \text{mpy}
$$

(ii) **mm/yr:**

$$
CR = \frac{87.6 \times 77}{2.70 \times 37.699 \times 500} = \frac{6745.2}{50{,}893.6} = 0.1325\ \text{mm/yr}
$$

Cross-check: $5.21 \times 0.0254 = 0.1323$ mm/yr ✓

---

#### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks)** — *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Explain the **gravimetric (weight-loss) method** of corrosion rate measurement with a neat flow diagram. List the **ASTM standards** relevant to it. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A brass coupon (Cu-30 % Zn, density **8.5 g/cm³**) of size 4 cm × 2 cm × 0.2 cm is exposed to an ammoniacal solution for 720 hours. The mass loss is found to be **120 mg**. Calculate (i) the corrosion rate in **mm/yr** and (ii) the expected **lifetime in years** if the allowable thickness loss is **1.0 mm**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Procedure (7 Marks):**
The gravimetric method involves:
1. **Specimen preparation** — cutting, grinding (120–600 grit), degreasing.
2. **Initial weighing** $W_i$ to 0.0001 g.
3. **Dimensional measurement** of length, width, thickness to compute area $A$.
4. **Exposure** in the test medium for a fixed time $T$.
5. **Post-exposure pickling** to remove corrosion products per **ASTM G1** (with blank-coupon correction).
6. **Final weighing** $W_f$ and calculation of $\Delta W = W_i - W_f$.
7. **Computation** of $CR$ via the standard formula.

Relevant ASTM standards:
- **ASTM G1** — Cleaning & evaluating specimens
- **ASTM G4** — Conducting plant corrosion tests
- **ASTM G31** — Laboratory immersion testing
- **ASTM G46** — Examination and evaluation of pitting corrosion
- **ASTM G50** — Atmospheric corrosion testing
- **ASTM G58** — Static immersion testing
- **NACE TM0169** — Laboratory testing for oilfield environments

**(b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

Compute area (all 6 faces):

$$
A = 2[(4 \times 2) + (4 \times 0.2) + (2 \times 0.2)] = 2[8 + 0.8 + 0.4] = 18.4\ \text{cm}^2
$$

(i) **mm/yr:**

$$
CR = \frac{87.6 \times 120}{8.5 \times 18.4 \times 720} = \frac{10{,}512}{112{,}608} = 0.0933\ \text{mm/yr}
$$

(ii) **Lifetime** for 1.0 mm thickness loss:

$$
\text{Life} = \frac{1.0}{0.0933} \approx 10.72\ \text{years}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the 1000 factor** when converting $g \to mg$ in the US formula → mark loss.
> 2. **Mixing in² and cm²** in the same equation (US expects in², SI expects cm²) → mark loss.
> 3. **Ignoring both ends** of a cylindrical coupon → area under-prediction → CR overstated.
> 4. **Using $T$ in days** without converting to hours in the standard formula → mark loss.
> 5. **Forgetting the blank-coupon correction** for pickling in high-mark descriptive answers.
> 6. **Reporting only one unit** when the question asks for "corrosion rate" — always give **both** mpy and mm/yr.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Weight change measurement = gravimetric method = ASTM G1 standard**.
- It is a **direct, destructive, off-line, time-averaged** method — the **gold standard** for corrosion rate.
- Formula: $CR_{\text{mpy}} = 534\,W / (D \cdot A \cdot T)$ — memorize and know the **derivation**.
- SI form: $CR_{\text{mm/yr}} = 87.6\,W / (D \cdot A \cdot T)$.
- Always report **both** mpy and mm/yr; conversion: $1\ \text{mpy} = 0.0254\ \text{mm/yr}$.
- Five stages: **cut → weigh → expose → pickle → re-weigh**.
- **Pickling with inhibitors** is the most critical step — refer to **ASTM G1**.
- Use **triplicate coupons** for statistical validity; report **mean ± std dev**.
- Apply **blank-coupon correction** to remove base-metal dissolution error.
- For **localized/pitting corrosion**, weight loss gives only the *uniform* component — supplement with **ASTM G46** pit-depth measurements.
- Densities to memorize (approximate): Fe = **7.86**, Cu = **8.96**, Al = **2.70**, Zn = **7.14**, brass (70/30) = **8.5**, stainless 304 = **7.93**, Ti = **4.51** g/cm³.
- Always quote **surface area units matching the formula** — in² for 534, cm² for 87.6.
- Time unit in the standard formula is **hours**, not days or months.
- The method is **incompatible with** conducting media needing real-time monitoring — use **LPR/EIS** instead.
- The method **cannot detect** corrosion rate changes during exposure — it is a *post-mortem* average.
- For **very low corrosion rates** (< 0.1 mpy), use **longer exposures** (≥ 30 days) and **higher precision balances** (0.00001 g).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Corrosion prevention: Materials selection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT527 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Corrosion prevention: Materials selection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Materials Selection for Corrosion Prevention** is a systematic engineering methodology that involves the deliberate identification, evaluation, and specification of engineering materials (metals, alloys, polymers, ceramics, and composites) whose inherent thermodynamic stability, kinetic resistance, and compatibility with the service environment ensure that the rate of material degradation remains within acceptable design limits over the intended service life of a component or system.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Corrosion Engineering syllabus (PECHT527, Module 3)**, materials selection is recognized as the **first and most fundamental line of defense** in a comprehensive corrosion-control strategy, preceding design modifications, protective coatings, cathodic/anodic protection, and the use of corrosion inhibitors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 3):** Materials selection is positioned as a *primary preventive strategy*. The examiner expects students to correlate alloy microstructure, environmental chemistry, and electrochemical potential when answering questions on this topic.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Right Shoe for the Right Terrain" Analogy

Imagine you are about to walk across three different terrains: a hot sandy beach, a slippery wet marble floor, and a thorny forest trail. Wearing the same pair of leather shoes for all three would result in:
- **Burned soles** on the beach (thermal + chemical attack analogous to high-temperature oxidation),
- **Slipping and falling** on marble (loss of mechanical integrity — analogous to stress-corrosion cracking or hydrogen embrittlement),
- **Torn shoes** in the forest (mechanical-chemical wear — analogous to erosion-corrosion).

Similarly, an engineer must select the **right material for the right environment**. Using mild steel in a chloride-rich marine environment is like wearing leather shoes in a thorny forest — failure is virtually guaranteed. The selection of, say, a super-duplex stainless steel or a fiber-reinforced polymer is the engineered equivalent of choosing hiking boots.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Materials Selection for Corrosion Resistance

> [!NOTE]
> Every materials selection decision for corrosion resistance is governed by **three interconnected pillars**:

1. **The Material Itself** — Its inherent composition, microstructure, and passivation behavior.
2. **The Environment** — Temperature, pH, chemical species (Cl⁻, SO₄²⁻, H₂S, O₂), flow velocity, and stress state.
3. **The Interface / Electrochemistry** — The corrosion potential (E_corr), polarization behavior, and thermodynamic tendency (ΔG) for the metal-environment reaction.

## 1.4 Defining Standard Metrics Used in Selection

- **Corrosion Rate (CR):** Expressed in **mils per year (mpy)** or **millimeters per year (mm/yr)**. KTU standard acceptable limit for most industrial applications: **< 0.1 mm/yr** (≈ **4 mpy**).
- **Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR):** A dimensionless number used to predict the protectiveness of an oxide layer.
- **Galvanic Series Position:** Determines the nobility of a metal relative to others in a given electrolyte.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of the Materials Selection Process

The selection of a corrosion-resistant material is a structured engineering decision. The KTU 2024 framework emphasizes the following sequential logic:

**Step 1 — Define the Service Environment**
Identify all chemical species (acids, alkalis, salts, dissolved gases), temperature range, pressure, velocity, and the presence of stray currents or biological agents (e.g., MICC — Microbiologically Influenced Corrosion).

**Step 2 — Establish Performance Requirements**
Specify the **maximum allowable corrosion rate**, the required **mechanical strength** (yield, UTS, fatigue), the **design life** (typically 15–30 years in process industries), and any **regulatory constraints** (e.g., FDA for food, ASME for pressure vessels).

**Step 3 — Screen Candidate Materials**
Use **iso-corrosion diagrams**, **Pourbaix diagrams**, and the **galvanic series** to shortlist 3–5 candidate materials whose Pourbaix stability domain overlaps with the operating E–pH window.

**Step 4 — Evaluate Compatibility**
- Check for **galvanic incompatibility** with adjoining components.
- Check for **specific forms of corrosion susceptibility**: SCC, pitting, crevice, dezincification, and intergranular corrosion.

**Step 5 — Conduct Laboratory and Field Validation**
Use **ASTM G48** (pitting), **ASTM G36** (SCC), and **ASTM G44** (alternate immersion) as standard qualification tests.

**Step 6 — Perform Economic Analysis**
Compare the **Life-Cycle Cost (LCC)** — not just initial material cost. A more expensive alloy with a 30-year life often beats a cheaper alloy needing replacement every 5 years.

## 2.2 Classification of Materials Used for Corrosion Prevention

| Class | Examples | Corrosion-Resistance Mechanism | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Carbon & Low-Alloy Steels** | AISI 1020, A516 | Forms porous Fe-oxide (poor protection) | Atmospheric, with coatings |
| **Stainless Steels (Austenitic)** | SS 304, SS 316, SS 316L | Cr₂O₃ passive film (self-healing) | Chemical, food, marine |
| **Stainless Steels (Duplex/Super-Duplex)** | SAF 2205, SAF 2507 | High Cr, Mo, N passive film | Offshore, oil & gas |
| **Nickel-Based Alloys** | Inconel 625, Monel 400, Hastelloy C-276 | Stable NiO / Cr₂O₃ film | Hot conc. acids, seawater |
| **Titanium Alloys** | Ti Grade 2, Ti-6Al-4V | TiO₂ passive film (extremely stable) | Aerospace, biomedical, chlor-alkali |
| **Aluminium Alloys** | AA 5083, AA 6061 | Amorphous Al₂O₃ film | Marine structures, automotive |
| **Polymers** | PTFE, PVC, HDPE, FRP | Inherently inert; no electrochemical reaction | Linings, tanks, piping |
| **Ceramics & Glass** | Al₂O₃, ZrO₂, borosilicate glass | Ionic/covalent bonds resist redox reactions | High-temp, aggressive chemicals |

## 2.3 The Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR) — A Key Selection Criterion

The PBR is **fundamental** to predicting whether the native oxide layer of a metal will protect it from further oxidation. The KTU examiner **frequently tests** this concept.

The Pilling–Bedworth Ratio is defined as:

$$R_{PB} = \frac{\text{Volume of oxide produced}}{\text{Volume of metal consumed}}$$

Mathematically:

$$R_{PB} = \frac{M_{oxide} \cdot \rho_{metal}}{n \cdot M_{metal} \cdot \rho_{oxide}}$$

Where:
- $M_{oxide}$ = molar mass of the metal oxide (g/mol)
- $M_{metal}$ = atomic mass of the metal (g/mol)
- $\rho_{metal}$ = density of the metal (g/cm³)
- $\rho_{oxide}$ = density of the oxide (g/cm³)
- $n$ = number of metal atoms in one molecule of the oxide

> [!NOTE]
> **Pilling–Bedworth Rule (KTU High-Yield):**
> - If $R_{PB} < 1$: oxide film is **non-protective** (tensile stress, cracks) → metal oxidizes continuously. *Examples: Alkali metals like Na, K, Mg.*
> - If $1 \leq R_{PB} \leq 2$: oxide film is **protective** (compressive stress, adherent). *Examples: Al ($R_{PB} \approx 1.28$), Cr ($R_{PB} \approx 2.07$), Ti ($R_{PB} \approx 1.73$).*
> - If $R_{PB} > 2$: oxide film is **excessively compressive**, tends to spall and flake off. *Example: Fe (Fe₂O₃, $R_{PB} \approx 2.15$, near limit).*

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Application / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Corrosion Rate (mm/yr) | $CR = \frac{87.6 \times W}{D \times A \times t}$ | $W$ = mass loss (mg), $D$ = density (g/cm³), $A$ = area (cm²), $t$ = time (h) |
| 2 | Corrosion Rate (mpy) | $CR_{mpy} = \frac{534 \times W}{D \times A \times t}$ | mils per year — U.S. standard |
| 3 | Pilling–Bedworth Ratio | $R_{PB} = \frac{M_{oxide} \cdot \rho_{metal}}{n \cdot M_{metal} \cdot \rho_{oxide}}$ | Predicts oxide film protectiveness |
| 4 | Nernst Equation | $E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0592}{n} \log \frac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}$ | Determines equilibrium corrosion potential |
| 5 | Pourbaix Stability | E vs. pH diagram | Predicts thermodynamic immunity, passivity, or corrosion zones |
| 6 | Faraday's Law | $W = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Links mass loss to current |
| 7 | Galvanic Corrosion Rate | $I_{cathode} = I_{anode}$ | Charge balance in galvanic couple |
| 8 | PREN (Pitting Resistance Equiv. No.) | $PREN = \%Cr + 3.3 \times \%Mo + 16 \times \%N$ | Higher PREN → better pitting resistance |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The materials selection methodology is the **backbone of design in**:
- **Oil & Gas:** Selecting **Inconel 625** or **super-duplex SS** for downhole tubing exposed to H₂S, CO₂, and chlorides at > 150 °C.
- **Chemical Processing:** Using **PTFE-lined steel** for 98% sulfuric acid storage at elevated temperatures.
- **Marine Engineering:** Specifying **AA 5083-H116** aluminium or **monel 400** for hulls and propeller shafts.
- **Biomedical Implants:** Choosing **Ti-6Al-4V ELI** for its TiO₂ passivity and biocompatibility.
- **Nuclear Power:** Selecting **Alloy 690** (high-Cr Ni-alloy) for steam generator tubes to resist SCC.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Calculating the Pilling–Bedworth Ratio for Aluminium

**Problem:** Calculate the PBR for aluminium, given the following data:
- Reaction: $4 Al + 3 O_2 \rightarrow 2 Al_2 O_3$
- $M_{Al_2O_3} = 101.96$ g/mol
- $M_{Al} = 26.98$ g/mol
- $\rho_{Al_2O_3} = 3.95$ g/cm³
- $\rho_{Al} = 2.70$ g/cm³
- $n = 2$ (Al atoms per molecule of Al₂O₃)

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the relevant variables and substitute into the PBR equation.

$$R_{PB} = \frac{M_{Al_2O_3} \cdot \rho_{Al}}{n \cdot M_{Al} \cdot \rho_{Al_2O_3}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the numerical values into the equation.

$$R_{PB} = \frac{(101.96) \cdot (2.70)}{(2) \cdot (26.98) \cdot (3.95)}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the numerator.

$$101.96 \times 2.70 = 275.292$$

**Step 4:** Compute the denominator.

$$2 \times 26.98 \times 3.95 = 213.142$$

**Step 5:** Divide numerator by denominator.

$$R_{PB} = \frac{275.292}{213.142} \approx 1.29$$

**Step 6:** Interpret the result.

$$\boxed{R_{PB} \approx 1.29 \in [1, 2] \Rightarrow \text{Protective Al}_2\text{O}_3 \text{ film}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The ratio lies comfortably in the protective range (1–2). The thin, adherent, self-healing Al₂O₃ film is the reason aluminium is corrosion-resistant in many environments — the scientific basis for its widespread use in aerospace, marine, and architectural applications.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Corrosion Rate from Mass-Loss Data

**Problem:** A carbon-steel coupon of dimensions 5 cm × 3 cm × 0.2 cm is exposed to a 3% NaCl solution for 30 days. The mass loss is 280 mg. The density of steel is 7.85 g/cm³. Calculate the corrosion rate in mm/yr and mpy, and decide whether the material is acceptable per KTU industrial norms (acceptable: < 0.1 mm/yr).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Calculate the exposed surface area (A). For a flat coupon, both faces and four edges are exposed. Thickness = 0.2 cm, so perimeter area = $2 \times (0.2 \times 5 + 0.2 \times 3)$ … for simplicity in KTU, take **A = 2 × (L × W) = 2 × (5 × 3) = 30 cm²** (neglecting edges).

$$A = 30 \ \text{cm}^2$$

**Step 2:** Convert the exposure time to hours.

$$t = 30 \times 24 = 720 \ \text{hours}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the SI-corrosion-rate formula.

$$CR = \frac{87.6 \times W}{D \times A \times t} = \frac{87.6 \times 280}{7.85 \times 30 \times 720}$$

**Step 4:** Compute numerator and denominator separately.

$$\text{Numerator} = 87.6 \times 280 = 24528$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 7.85 \times 30 \times 720 = 169560$$

**Step 5:** Divide to obtain the corrosion rate in mm/yr.

$$CR = \frac{24528}{169560} \approx 0.1446 \ \text{mm/yr}$$

**Step 6:** Convert to mpy.

$$CR_{mpy} = CR \times \frac{1000}{25.4} = 0.1446 \times 39.37 \approx 5.69 \ \text{mpy}$$

**Step 7:** Conclude.

$$\boxed{CR \approx 0.145 \ \text{mm/yr} \ \text{(slightly above the 0.1 mm/yr KTU threshold)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Decision:** The material **fails** the KTU acceptance criterion. **Recommended action:** Select SS 316L (PREN ≈ 25) or apply an epoxy coating + cathodic protection.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number (PREN) Comparison

**Problem:** Compare the pitting resistance of **SS 304** and **SS 316L** given:
- SS 304: 18% Cr, 0% Mo, 0.02% N
- SS 316L: 17% Cr, 2.5% Mo, 0.02% N

**Step 1:** Write the PREN formula.

$$PREN = \%Cr + 3.3 \times \%Mo + 16 \times \%N$$

**Step 2:** Substitute for SS 304.

$$PREN_{304} = 18 + (3.3 \times 0) + (16 \times 0.02) = 18 + 0 + 0.32 = 18.32$$

**Step 3:** Substitute for SS 316L.

$$PREN_{316L} = 17 + (3.3 \times 2.5) + (16 \times 0.02) = 17 + 8.25 + 0.32 = 25.57$$

**Step 4:** Compare.

$$\boxed{PREN_{316L} (25.57) \gg PREN_{304} (18.32)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** SS 316L has substantially superior pitting resistance, making it the preferred choice for chloride-rich environments such as marine and chemical-process industries. This single numerical comparison is a KTU favorite.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Selection Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Expected Skill:** Given a service environment, the student must be able to recommend a suitable material using the **Selection Matrix** below.

| Service Environment | Recommended Material | Selection Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Dilute H₂SO₄ (< 70%, < 70 °C) | Lead, Alloy 20 | Forms protective PbSO₄ or Cr-rich passive film |
| Hot conc. HCl | Hastelloy B / C-276 | Mo-rich passive film resists Cl⁻ attack |
| Seawater (ambient) | Super-duplex SS, Ti, Cu-Ni 90/10 | High PREN, no SCC susceptibility |
| NaOH (caustic) | Nickel, Monel 400 | Forms protective Ni(OH)₂ film |
| HNO₃ (conc., hot) | SS 304L | Strong oxidizer — promotes Cr₂O₃ film |
| H₂S + Cl⁻ (sour service) | Inconel 825, Alloy 625 | Resists SSC and SCC |
| 98% H₂SO₄ (hot) | PTFE-lined steel, Alloy 20 | Polymer/Alloy immune to direct attack |
| HF acid | Monel 400, Hastelloy C | Forms insoluble fluoride film |
| Food/pharma (sanitary) | SS 316L (electropolished) | Hygienic, low crevice, passivated |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Materials Selection Decision Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Define Service Environment] --> A1[Identify Chemicals, T, P, pH, Cl, Stress]
    A1 --> A2[Establish Performance Requirements]
    A2 --> A3[Set Max Corrosion Rate < 0.1 mm per year]
    A3 --> A4[Screen Candidate Materials]
    A4 --> A5{Consult Pourbaix Diagram}
    A5 --> A6{Zone: Immunity Passivity or Corrosion}
    A6 --> A7[Apply Pilling-Bedworth Ratio Check]
    A7 --> A8[Check Galvanic Compatibility]
    A8 --> A9{Candidate Passes All Criteria}
    A9 --> A10[Yes: Run ASTM Lab Tests G48 G36 G44]
    A9 --> A11[No: Return to Step 4 and Re-screen]
    A10 --> A12[Perform Life-Cycle Cost Analysis]
    A12 --> A13[Final Material Selection]
    A13 --> A14[Document in Material Spec Sheet]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of the Corrosion Selection Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ENVE[Environment Module]
        E1[Chemical Species: Cl SO4 H2S]
        E2[Physical: T, P, Velocity]
        E3[Electrochemical: pH, Dissolved O2]
    end

    subgraph MATL[Material Module]
        M1[Composition: Fe, Cr, Ni, Mo, Ti]
        M2[Microstructure: Austenitic, Ferritic, Duplex]
        M3[Surface State: Passive, Active, Coated]
    end

    subgraph SELECT[Selection Module]
        S1[Galvanic Series Lookup]
        S2[Pourbaix Stability Check]
        S3[PBR Evaluation]
        S4[PREN Computation]
    end

    subgraph OUTC[Output Module]
        O1[Ranked Candidate List]
        O2[ASTM Test Recommendation]
        O3[Final Specification]
    end

    ENVE --> SELECT
    MATL --> SELECT
    SELECT --> OUTC
```

## 4.3 Galvanic Series Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G0[Galvanic Series: Most Noble at Top] --> G1[Platinum Gold Graphite]
    G1 --> G2[Titanium SS 316L SS 304]
    G2 --> G3[Nickel Copper Brass]
    G3 --> G4[Lead Tin]
    G4 --> G5[Cast Iron Carbon Steel]
    G5 --> G6[Aluminium Zinc Magnesium]
    G6 --> G7[Most Active: Magnesium Alloys]
```

## 4.4 Form of Corrosion → Material Design Mitigation Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1[Uniform Attack] --> M1[Increase Alloy Cr Content / Use Coatings]
    F2[Pitting] --> M2[Increase Mo Content: SS 316L > 304]
    F3[Crevice] --> M3[Use Welded Joints: Avoid Bolted Seams]
    F4[SCC] --> M4[Avoid Austenitic SS in Hot Caustic: Use Duplex]
    F5[Galvanic] --> M5[Insulate Dissimilar Metals: Use Dielectric Unions]
    F6[Erosion] --> M6[Reduce Flow Velocity: Use Thicker Walls]
    F7[Intergranular] --> M7[Use L or H Grade SS: Low Carbon 0.03 percent max]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid Block Diagrams:** The KTU 2024 visual-pedagogy framework emphasizes **functional architecture mapping** of selection logic over hand-drawn schematics. These diagrams represent the same engineering knowledge in a format suitable for **digital note-taking, board examination flow-chart questions, and viva voce**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper, PECHT527]**

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**"What is the Pilling–Bedworth Ratio? Comment on the protectiveness of the oxide film for Aluminium and Iron."**

**Model Answer:**
The Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR) is the ratio of the volume of the metal oxide formed to the volume of the metal consumed during oxidation:

$$R_{PB} = \frac{M_{oxide} \cdot \rho_{metal}}{n \cdot M_{metal} \cdot \rho_{oxide}}$$

- For **Aluminium**, $R_{PB} \approx 1.28$, which lies in the range $[1, 2]$ — the Al₂O₃ film is **protective, adherent, and self-healing**.
- For **Iron**, $R_{PB} \approx 2.15$ (for Fe₂O₃) — at the upper limit. The oxide layer tends to **spall** under thermal cycling, exposing fresh metal — hence the familiar rusting of iron.

(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for Al, 1 for Fe.)

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**"Define the Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number (PREN). Why is SS 316L preferred over SS 304 in chloride environments?"**

**Model Answer:**
PREN is a numerical indicator of an alloy's resistance to pitting corrosion in chloride-containing media:

$$PREN = \%Cr + 3.3 \times \%Mo + 16 \times \%N$$

For SS 304: PREN ≈ **18.3**; for SS 316L: PREN ≈ **25.6**. The **higher PREN** of 316L (due to Mo addition) confers superior resistance to localized breakdown of the passive film by chloride ions, making it the material of choice in marine and chemical applications.

(3 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for calculation, 1 for explanation.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Format)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplemented for 2024 Scheme]**

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Alternative 1**

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the **Pourbaix diagram** for the Fe–H₂O system at 25 °C. Identify the regions of immunity, passivity, and corrosion. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **stainless steel selection methodology** for a chloride-containing environment. Compare 304, 316, and 2205 duplex grades with reference to PREN and SCC resistance. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The Pourbaix diagram is an **E vs. pH plot** that maps the thermodynamic stability domains of a metal and its compounds in aqueous solution.

**Key features for Fe–H₂O system:**

1. **Immunity Region** (low E, pH < 9): Fe remains as stable metal — no corrosion is thermodynamically possible. Cathodic protection exploits this region.
2. **Corrosion Region** (intermediate E, pH < 9): Fe²⁺ and Fe³⁺ ions are stable; metal dissolves actively.
3. **Passivity Region** (high E, pH > 9 or specific pH ranges): Stable Fe₂O₃ / Fe(OH)₃ / Fe₃O₄ films form on the surface, kinetically slowing the corrosion.
4. The diagonal lines correspond to the **hydrogen evolution** (lower) and **oxygen evolution** (upper) reactions of water.

**Sketch (verbal description):**
- Y-axis: Potential E (V vs. SHE), ranging from −1.0 to +1.5 V.
- X-axis: pH, ranging from 0 to 14.
- Three labeled zones: **Immunity (bottom-left)**, **Corrosion (mid, acidic)**, **Passivity (top, alkaline)**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

The selection of stainless steel for chloride service follows this logic:

**Step 1 — Identify the threat:** Chlorides initiate pitting by locally breaking down the Cr₂O₃ passive film. Susceptibility is quantified by PREN.

**Step 2 — Compare grades:**

| Grade | %Cr | %Mo | %N | PREN | SCC Resistance | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| SS 304 | 18 | 0 | 0.02 | 18.3 | Low | Lowest |
| SS 316L | 17 | 2.5 | 0.02 | 25.6 | Moderate | Medium |
| Duplex 2205 | 22 | 3.0 | 0.15 | 32.2 | High (no SCC in chloride < 80 °C) | High |

**Step 3 — Decision Logic:**
- For ambient-temperature marine: **SS 316L** is sufficient.
- For hot chloride (> 60 °C) or in the presence of H₂S: **Duplex 2205** is mandatory.
- For critical offshore structural components: **Super-duplex 2507** (PREN > 40).

**Step 4 — Verify:** Always validate using ASTM G48 (ferric chloride pitting test).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative 2**

**(a)** What is the **Galvanic Series**? How does it differ from the **Electromotive Force (EMF) Series**? Explain with examples how galvanic coupling influences materials selection. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A carbon-steel pipe of dimensions 50 cm × 10 cm × 0.5 cm (density 7.85 g/cm³) shows a mass loss of 1.2 g after 60 days in a 5% HCl solution at 40 °C. Calculate the corrosion rate in mm/yr and mpy. Comment on whether the material is suitable for this service. Suggest a suitable replacement material with justification. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Galvanic Series** is an **empirical ranking** of metals and alloys based on their **measured corrosion potentials** in a **specific electrolyte** (typically seawater). It orders metals from *most noble* (cathodic) to *most active* (anodic).

**EMF Series**, in contrast, is a **theoretical ranking** based on **standard electrode potentials** in a 1 M solution of their ions at 25 °C.

**Key Differences:**

| Feature | Galvanic Series | EMF Series |
|---|---|---|
| Basis | Measured potentials in given environment | Standard thermodynamic potentials |
| Environment | Specific (e.g., seawater) | Idealized, 1 M ionic concentration |
| Lists | Alloys + metals | Pure elements only |
| Practical Use | Predicts galvanic corrosion in real service | Predicts thermodynamic tendency only |

**Influence on Materials Selection:**
- **Avoid coupling metals far apart** in the galvanic series in the same electrolyte.
- If unavoidable, the **anode area must be much larger** than the cathode area to reduce current density.
- Use **dielectric unions, insulating gaskets, or sacrificial coatings** to break electrical continuity.

**Example:** Brass valve + steel pipe → steel corrodes preferentially. Replace with **all-brass** assembly or use **dielectric insulation**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given Data:**
- Dimensions: 50 cm × 10 cm × 0.5 cm
- Density of steel: $D = 7.85$ g/cm³
- Mass loss: $W = 1.2$ g = 1200 mg
- Time: $t = 60 \times 24 = 1440$ hours
- Exposed area (assuming both faces + edges): 

$$A = 2 \times (50 \times 10) + 2 \times (50 \times 0.5) + 2 \times (10 \times 0.5) = 1000 + 50 + 10 = 1060 \ \text{cm}^2$$

**Step 1 — Apply the corrosion rate formula in mm/yr:**

$$CR = \frac{87.6 \times W}{D \times A \times t}$$

$$CR = \frac{87.6 \times 1200}{7.85 \times 1060 \times 1440}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the numerator:**

$$87.6 \times 1200 = 105120$$

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator:**

$$7.85 \times 1060 \times 1440 = 11982240$$

**Step 4 — Divide to obtain the rate in mm/yr:**

$$CR = \frac{105120}{11982240} \approx 0.00877 \ \text{mm/yr}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to mpy:**

$$CR_{mpy} = 0.00877 \times 39.37 \approx 0.345 \ \text{mpy}$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion and Recommendation:**

$$\boxed{CR \approx 0.0088 \ \text{mm/yr} \approx 0.35 \ \text{mpy (Well within KTU < 0.1 mm/yr threshold)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Material Verdict:** Although the rate is technically acceptable, in a **5% HCl** service the carbon steel is **not recommended** for long-term service because:
> - HCl attacks most carbon steels aggressively once the protective iron oxide scale is dissolved.
> - The rate is likely to **accelerate** as corrosion proceeds.
> - Localized attack (pitting under deposits) is highly probable.

**Recommended Replacement: Hastelloy C-276 (Ni-Cr-Mo alloy)**
- **Justification:** Forms a passive Cr₂O₃ + MoO₃ film that is **immune to Cl⁻ attack**; resists HCl up to ~10% concentration at moderate temperatures; widely used in **chemical pickling lines and HCl storage tanks** as per NACE MR0175 standards.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert time to hours** (or mass to mg) — leads to 2–3 mark deduction.
> 2. **Using EMF series instead of galvanic series** in design questions — examiner deducts up to 4 marks as it shows lack of practical engineering judgment.
> 3. **Not stating the PBR range [1, 2] explicitly** when explaining protective oxides — always quote the numerical rule.
> 4. **Failing to justify material replacement with a quantified PREN or iso-corrosion chart** — vague answers lose marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 3 — Materials Selection: Final Rapid-Recap**

- ☐ **Materials selection is the FIRST line of defense** in corrosion prevention, before coatings or CP.
- ☐ The **Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR)** predicts oxide-film protectiveness:
  - $R_{PB} < 1$ → non-protective
  - $1 \leq R_{PB} \leq 2$ → protective
  - $R_{PB} > 2$ → spalling tendency
- ☐ **PREN** is the single most important metric for pitting resistance in stainless steel selection:
  $$PREN = \%Cr + 3.3 \cdot \%Mo + 16 \cdot \%N$$
- ☐ **Galvanic series** (not EMF) governs real-world galvanic compatibility.
- ☐ **Acceptable KTU corrosion rate** in most industrial services: **< 0.1 mm/yr** (≈ 4 mpy).
- ☐ **Stainless Steel Grades — Quick Hierarchy:**
  - 304 → 316L → 2205 Duplex → 2507 Super-Duplex → Alloy 625 → Ti Grade 2 → Hastelloy C-276
  - Increasing corrosion resistance ↔ Increasing cost.
- ☐ **Avoid coupling metals far apart** in the galvanic series; use **insulating gaskets** if unavoidable.
- ☐ For **hot HCl** → use **Hastelloy B/C-276** or PTFE-lined steel.
- ☐ For **H₂S + Cl⁻ (sour service)** → use **Inconel 825/625** (NACE MR0175 compliant).
- ☐ For **seawater** → **Super-duplex SS, Ti, Cu-Ni 90/10**.
- ☐ For **HF acid** → **Monel 400** (forms insoluble NiF₂ film).
- ☐ **Polymers (PTFE, HDPE, FRP)** are inherently corrosion-immune — no electrochemical reaction possible.
- ☐ Always validate final material choice with **ASTM G48, G36, G44** lab tests and **life-cycle cost (LCC) analysis**.
- ☐ In exam answers, **always quote the E vs. pH zones (Immunity, Passivity, Corrosion)** when referring to Pourbaix diagrams.
- ☐ **Cementite, ferrite, austenite** microstructures behave differently — austenitic SS is more prone to **SCC**; ferritic and duplex are not.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Alteration of environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT527 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Alteration of environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Alteration of Environment** is a corrosion control strategy that involves modifying the chemical, physical, or electrochemical nature of the corrosive medium itself, rather than altering the metal or the system design. The objective is to reduce or eliminate the aggressiveness of the surrounding environment contacting the metal surface, thereby reducing the corrosion rate to an economically and technically acceptable level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** Alteration of environment is one of the **four principal methods of corrosion control** (alongside material selection, proper design, and use of protective coatings). It is classified as a **secondary line of defense** because the environment — not the metal — is the variable being engineered.

The corrosive environment is characterized by a set of measurable parameters, each of which can potentially be altered:

- **Dissolved Oxygen (DO)** concentration
- **pH** of the medium
- **Concentration of aggressive ions** (e.g., $\text{Cl}^-$, $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$, $\text{H}^+$)
- **Temperature**
- **Velocity / flow regime**
- **Conductivity / Total Dissolved Solids (TDS)**
- **Oxidation–Reduction Potential (ORP)**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "Cleaning the Air You Breathe"**
> Imagine you are locked in a room where a candle is burning (a corrosive situation). You have two ways to prevent the candle from burning out:
> 1. **Replace the candle** (material selection — change the metal)
> 2. **Clean the air** (alteration of environment — remove the oxygen feeding the flame)
> In corrosion engineering, "cleaning the air" means removing dissolved $\text{O}_2$, neutralizing acids, removing chloride ions, or adding inhibitors. The metal stays the same, but the surroundings become less aggressive.

### 1.3 Primary Classification of Environmental Alteration Methods

| Sl. No. | Method | Principle | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Deaeration** | Removal of dissolved $\text{O}_2$ | Boiler feed water, cooling water |
| 2 | **Use of Inhibitors** | Addition of small quantities of chemicals to retard corrosion | Closed loops, oil & gas, pickling |
| 3 | **pH Adjustment** | Neutralization of acidic/basic media | Process industries, condensates |
| 4 | **Removal of Aggressive Ions** | Ion exchange, distillation, reverse osmosis | High-purity water systems |
| 5 | **Temperature & Velocity Control** | Reduction of kinetic driving force | Heat exchangers, pipelines |
| 6 | **Change in Oxidation State** | Shift of ORP via reducing/oxidizing agents | Chemical process vessels |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECHT527 Module 3):** The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically focuses on **Deaeration**, **Inhibitors**, and **pH Control** as the three principal sub-topics under alteration of environment.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Henry's Law Constant** for $\text{O}_2$ in water at 25 °C: $\text{K}_\text{H} \approx 769.2 \text{ L·atm/mol}$
- **Standard concentration** of $\text{O}_2$ in air-saturated water at 25 °C: $\approx \mathbf{8 \text{ mg/L} \text{ (8 ppm)}}$
- **Neutral pH** at 25 °C: $\mathbf{pH = 7.0}$
- **Passivity range** of iron: $\approx -0.05 \text{ V}$ to $+0.7 \text{ V}$ (SHE)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Method 1 — Deaeration of the Environment

Deaeration is the process of removing dissolved oxygen (DO) from water or aqueous solutions. Dissolved oxygen is the **primary cathodic reactant** in neutral and near-neutral aerated solutions, and its removal dramatically reduces the corrosion current.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cathodic reaction in aerated neutral water:**
> $$\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$$
> Removing $\text{O}_2$ eliminates this cathodic half-cell, suppressing the entire corrosion cell.

#### 2.1.1 Mechanical Deaeration (Thermal Deaeration)

- Water is heated close to saturation temperature (typically 100–110 °C) and sprayed through a deaerator.
- According to **Henry's Law**, solubility of a gas decreases with rising temperature.
- Final DO level achievable: $\leq \mathbf{0.005 \text{ mg/L}}$ (i.e., 5 ppb).

#### 2.1.2 Chemical Deaeration (Oxygen Scavengers)

Common oxygen scavengers and their reaction stoichiometries:

| Scavenger | Chemical Reaction with $\text{O}_2$ | Residual DO | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sodium sulfite ($\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3$) | $2\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$ | $\leq 0.01$ ppm | Most common, catalyzed by $\text{Co}^{2+}$ |
| Hydrazine ($\text{N}_2\text{H}_4$) | $\text{N}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{N}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | $\leq 0.01$ ppm | Used in boilers; toxic |
| Sulfite–Bisulfite | $2\text{NaHSO}_3 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{NaHSO}_4$ | — | Acidic systems |
| Catalyzed hydrazine | $\text{N}_2\text{H}_4$ + catalyst | — | Faster kinetics |

#### 2.1.3 Deaeration by Vacuum

- Pressure is reduced to lower the partial pressure of $\text{O}_2$.
- Achieves DO levels of $\leq 0.05$ ppm at room temperature.
- Common in marine and high-pressure boiler systems.

#### 2.1.4 Bohnenblust Equation (Residual Sulfite Estimation)

The minimum amount of sodium sulfite required to remove dissolved oxygen is given by:

$$C_{\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3} = \frac{7.88 \times C_{\text{O}_2}}{(F/100) - 4}$$

where:
- $C_{\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3}$ = required sodium sulfite concentration (mg/L)
- $C_{\text{O}_2}$ = dissolved oxygen concentration (mg/L)
- $F$ = purity of commercial sulfite (%)
- The factor **7.88** is the stoichiometric mass ratio: $\dfrac{2 \times 126}{32}$

### 2.2 Method 2 — Corrosion Inhibitors

A corrosion inhibitor is a chemical substance that, when added in small concentrations to an environment, effectively reduces the corrosion rate of the metal exposed to that environment.

#### 2.2.1 Classification of Inhibitors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Critical Classification:**
> Inhibitors are classified by their **mechanism of action** (anodic, cathodic, mixed) and by their **chemical nature** (inorganic, organic, vapor-phase).

**A. By Mechanism of Action:**

| Type | Mechanism | Example | Risk |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Anodic inhibitors** | Form protective oxide film on anode; shift corrosion potential in noble direction | Chromates, nitrites, molybdates, orthophosphates | **Dangerous** — can cause pitting if under-dosed |
| **Cathodic inhibitors** | Suppress cathodic reaction by forming insoluble precipitates at cathodic sites | Polyphosphates, zinc salts, calcium bicarbonate | Generally safe |
| **Mixed inhibitors** | Film formed over entire surface | Silicates, phosphates, organic amines | Moderate safety |
| **Vapor-phase (VPI) inhibitors** | Volatile; condense on metal surface forming protective film | Cyclohexylamine carbonate, dicyclohexylamine nitrite | Storage protection |

**B. By Chemical Nature:**

- **Inorganic inhibitors:** chromates, nitrites, molybdates, silicates, polyphosphates
- **Organic inhibitors:** amines, imidazolines, mercaptans, benzotriazole (for copper)
- **Vapor-phase inhibitors:** amine nitrites, amine carbonates

#### 2.2.2 Mechanism of Action of Anodic Inhibitors

Anodic inhibitors form a stable, adherent oxide layer. For example, sodium chromate reacts at anodic sites:

$$\text{2Fe} + \text{2Na}_2\text{CrO}_4 + \text{2H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + \text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3 + 4\text{NaOH}$$

The mixed $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$–$\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3$ film is highly passivating.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall — Pitting Risk:** If anodic inhibitors are added in **insufficient concentration**, they can cause localized attack (pitting) because uncovered anodic areas become highly active against large cathodic areas. The **minimum safe concentration** must always be maintained.

#### 2.2.3 Inhibitor Efficiency Formula

$$\text{IE (\%)} = \frac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0} \times 100$$

where:
- $CR_0$ = corrosion rate without inhibitor
- $CR_i$ = corrosion rate with inhibitor

### 2.3 Method 3 — pH Adjustment

For most metals (e.g., iron, zinc, copper), corrosion rate is **minimum** in the near-neutral pH range (6–9) due to formation of stable oxide films.

- **Increasing pH** (alkalization): reduces $\text{H}^+$ concentration, suppressing the cathodic hydrogen evolution reaction.
- **Decreasing pH** (acidification): increases hydrogen evolution; used only when necessary (e.g., pickling).

**Common alkalis for pH control:**
- $\text{NaOH}$, $\text{KOH}$, $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$, $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$, ammonia ($\text{NH}_3$)

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Equation | Description | Units / Constants |
|---|---|---|
| $2\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$ | Sulfite scavenger reaction | Stoichiometric ratio 7.88:1 |
| $\text{N}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{N}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | Hydrazine scavenger | 1:1 mol ratio |
| $C_{\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3} = 7.88 \, C_{\text{O}_2} / [(F/100) - 4]$ | Bohnenblust equation | mg/L |
| $\text{IE (\%)} = [(CR_0 - CR_i) / CR_0] \times 100$ | Inhibitor efficiency | % |
| $C = k \cdot P$ | Henry's Law (dissolved $\text{O}_2$) | $k$ depends on gas & T |
| $\eta_{\text{cathode}} = a + b \log i$ | Cathodic polarization (Tafel) | V vs. SCE |
| $\Delta E = E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}}$ | Driving EMF of corrosion cell | Volts |
| $\log P_{\text{O}_2} \propto -1/T$ | Solubility of $\text{O}_2$ vs T | Henry's constant form |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application | Method Used |
|---|---|---|
| Power Plants | Boiler feed water treatment | Mechanical + chemical deaeration |
| Oil & Gas | Sour service pipelines | $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ scavengers + film inhibitors |
| Cooling Towers | Recirculating water | Polyphosphate + zinc inhibitors |
| Marine | Bilge & ballast systems | Cathodic + anodic inhibitors |
| Chemical Pickling | Acid cleaning of scale | Mixed organic inhibitors (e.g., Rodine) |
| Storage | Long-term metal preservation | Vapor-phase inhibitors (VPI papers) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Sodium Sulfite Dose (Bohnenblust Equation)

**Given Problem:** A boiler feed water has dissolved oxygen of **8.0 mg/L**. The sodium sulfite available has a purity of **95%**. Calculate the minimum dose of sodium sulfite (in mg/L) required to completely scavenge the dissolved oxygen.

**Step 1 — Write the balanced chemical reaction:**

$$2\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the molar masses:**

- Molar mass of $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3$ = $(2 \times 23) + 32 + (3 \times 16) = 126 \text{ g/mol}$
- Molar mass of $\text{O}_2$ = $2 \times 16 = 32 \text{ g/mol}$

**Step 3 — Establish stoichiometric ratio:**

From the balanced equation, **2 moles** of $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3$ react with **1 mole** of $\text{O}_2$.

$$\text{Mass ratio} = \frac{2 \times 126}{32} = \frac{252}{32} = 7.88$$

So, theoretically, **7.88 mg of $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3$** is required to scavenge **1 mg of $\text{O}_2$**.

**Step 4 — Theoretical mass of pure sulfite required:**

$$m_{\text{pure}} = 7.88 \times C_{\text{O}_2} = 7.88 \times 8.0 = 63.04 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 5 — Account for purity of commercial sulfite:**

For a commercial sample of purity $F$ (in %), the **effective fraction** of pure $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3$ is $\frac{F}{100}$. However, an additional factor of **4 mg/L** is empirically retained as a safety residual in the Bohnenblust equation to ensure complete scavenging.

$$C_{\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3} = \frac{7.88 \times C_{\text{O}_2}}{(F/100) - 4 \times 10^{-2}}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the given values:**

$$C_{\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3} = \frac{7.88 \times 8.0}{(95/100) - 0.04}$$

$$= \frac{63.04}{0.95 - 0.04} = \frac{63.04}{0.91}$$

$$= 69.27 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 7 — Final Answer with Units:**

$$\boxed{C_{\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3} \approx 69.3 \text{ mg/L}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A residual of 4 mg/L of $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3$ is *always* maintained in the boiler water to ensure that **any leakage of oxygen** is immediately scavenged, preventing pitting corrosion of the boiler tubes.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Inhibitor Efficiency Calculation

**Given Problem:** A mild steel coupon in 1 M $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ shows a corrosion rate of **12.5 mm/year** without inhibitor. After adding 0.5% of a commercial inhibitor (Rodine-92), the rate drops to **0.85 mm/year**. Calculate the inhibitor efficiency (IE) in %.

**Step 1 — Identify the variables:**

- $CR_0 = 12.5$ mm/year (without inhibitor)
- $CR_i = 0.85$ mm/year (with inhibitor)

**Step 2 — Apply the IE formula:**

$$\text{IE (\%)} = \frac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$\text{IE (\%)} = \frac{12.5 - 0.85}{12.5} \times 100 = \frac{11.65}{12.5} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\text{IE (\%)} = 93.2\%}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Conclusion:**

> An IE > 90% is classified as a **"good inhibitor"** in industrial practice. Rodine-92 acts as a mixed-type organic inhibitor that adsorbs on the metal surface, blocking both anodic and cathodic reaction sites.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hydrazine Dose for Boiler Water

**Given Problem:** Determine the theoretical mass of hydrazine ($\text{N}_2\text{H}_4$) required to remove **4 mg/L of dissolved oxygen** in a high-pressure boiler.

**Step 1 — Reaction:**

$$\text{N}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{N}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 2 — Molar masses:**

- $M_{\text{N}_2\text{H}_4} = (2 \times 14) + (4 \times 1) = 32 \text{ g/mol}$
- $M_{\text{O}_2} = 32 \text{ g/mol}$

**Step 3 — Stoichiometric ratio:**

The reaction is **1:1** by mole. Since molar masses are equal (32 g/mol each):

$$\text{Mass ratio} = 1.0 \text{ mg N}_2\text{H}_4 \text{ per mg O}_2$$

**Step 4 — Mass required:**

$$m_{\text{N}_2\text{H}_4} = 1.0 \times 4.0 = 4.0 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 5 — With practical excess (2× stoichiometric):**

$$m_{\text{practical}} = 2 \times 4.0 = 8.0 \text{ mg/L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Hydrazine is preferred over sodium sulfite in **high-pressure boilers** because its reaction products are $\text{N}_2$ and $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ — both volatile, leaving **no dissolved solids** to scale the boiler tubes.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Deaeration Methods

| Parameter | Mechanical Deaeration | Chemical (Sulfite) | Chemical (Hydrazine) | Vacuum Deaeration |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Residual DO | $\leq 0.005$ ppm | $\leq 0.01$ ppm | $\leq 0.01$ ppm | $\leq 0.05$ ppm |
| Adds Solids | No | Yes ($\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$) | No (volatile products) | No |
| Cost | High (equipment) | Low | Moderate | High |
| Operating Temperature | 100–110 °C | Ambient–hot | 100–200 °C | Ambient |
| Use Case | Large boilers | Low-pressure systems | High-pressure boilers | Marine, condensate |
| Safety | Safe | Safe | Toxic (carcinogenic) | Safe |

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Inhibitor Selection Guide

| Metal | Recommended Inhibitor | Type | Concentration |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mild Steel (neutral) | Sodium benzoate, polyphosphate | Anodic/mixed | 0.1–1% |
| Copper | Benzotriazole (BTA) | Mixed | 10–100 ppm |
| Aluminum | Chromates, silicates | Anodic | 0.01–0.1% |
| Stainless Steel (acid) | Thiourea, propargyl alcohol | Cathodic/mixed | 0.01–0.5% |
| Steel in $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ (pickling) | Rodine, Armohib | Mixed organic | 0.1–0.5% |
| Boiler tubes | Hydrazine, morpholine | Mixed (VPI) | Trace |
