## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`

**Define a corrosion inhibitor. Classify inhibitors based on their mechanism of action, giving one example for each class.**

**Model Answer:**

A corrosion inhibitor is a chemical substance that, when added in small concentrations to a corrosive environment, effectively reduces the rate of corrosion of the metal exposed to that environment.

**Classification by mechanism:**

| Type | Function | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Anodic inhibitor | Forms protective film at anodic sites | Sodium chromate ($\text{Na}_2\text{CrO}_4$) |
| Cathodic inhibitor | Forms insoluble precipitate at cathodic sites | Zinc sulfate ($\text{ZnSO}_4$) |
| Mixed inhibitor | Forms barrier film over entire surface | Sodium silicate, amines |
| Vapor-phase inhibitor | Volatile; condenses on metal to form film | Cyclohexylamine carbonate |

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`

**Explain the principle of deaeration. Write the chemical reaction involved in the removal of dissolved oxygen using sodium sulfite.**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:** Deaeration is the removal of dissolved oxygen (DO) from water, since $\text{O}_2$ acts as the primary cathodic depolarizer in neutral aqueous corrosion. Removal of $\text{O}_2$ suppresses the cathodic half-reaction, reducing the overall corrosion current.

**Chemical Reaction:**

$$2\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$$

Sodium sulfite reacts with dissolved oxygen to form inert sodium sulfate. The reaction is catalyzed by cobalt ions ($\text{Co}^{2+}$) for faster kinetics at ambient temperatures.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the various methods of deaeration employed to remove dissolved oxygen from boiler feed water. Compare mechanical deaeration with chemical deaeration.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A boiler feed water contains **6 mg/L** of dissolved oxygen. Calculate the minimum dose of commercial sodium sulfite (purity **92%**) required for complete scavenging using the Bohnenblust equation.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A (a):**

The various methods of deaeration are:

**1. Mechanical (Thermal) Deaeration:**
- Water is heated to near saturation temperature (100–110 °C) and sprayed in a deaerator.
- Henry's Law: $\text{Solubility} \propto \text{Partial Pressure} / \text{Temperature}$
- As temperature rises, DO solubility decreases; oxygen is vented out.
- Achievable DO: $\leq 0.005$ ppm (5 ppb).
- Advantages: no chemical addition, continuous operation.

**2. Chemical Deaeration (Oxygen Scavengers):**
- **Sodium sulfite** is the most common scavenger:

$$2\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$$

- Catalyzed by $\text{Co}^{2+}$ ions (catalyzed sodium sulfite).
- **Hydrazine** is used in high-pressure boilers:

$$\text{N}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{N}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

- Products are volatile — no solid residue.

**3. Vacuum Deaeration:**
- Pressure is reduced to lower the partial pressure of $\text{O}_2$.
- Achievable DO: $\leq 0.05$ ppm at room temperature.
- Useful when heating is impractical (e.g., marine).

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Mechanical | Chemical (Sulfite) |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Thermal stripping | Chemical reaction |
| Residual DO | $\leq 0.005$ ppm | $\leq 0.01$ ppm |
| Solid residue | None | Adds $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$ |
| Cost | High initial | Low operating |
| Continuous | Yes | Depends on dosing |

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A (b):**

**Given:**
- $C_{\text{O}_2} = 6 \text{ mg/L}$
- $F$ = 92% (purity of commercial sulfite)
- Standard residual = 4 mg/L

**Bohnenblust equation:**

$$C_{\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3} = \frac{7.88 \times C_{\text{O}_2}}{(F/100) - 0.04}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute values:**

$$C_{\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3} = \frac{7.88 \times 6.0}{(92/100) - 0.04}$$

**Step 2 — Numerator:**

$$7.88 \times 6.0 = 47.28 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 3 — Denominator:**

$$0.92 - 0.04 = 0.88$$

**Step 4 — Final result:**

$$C_{\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3} = \frac{47.28}{0.88} = 53.73 \text{ mg/L}$$

$$\boxed{C_{\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3} \approx 53.73 \text{ mg/L}}$$

**Conclusion:** A minimum dose of **~53.7 mg/L** of 92% pure commercial sodium sulfite is required to completely scavenge 6 mg/L of dissolved oxygen from the boiler feed water, maintaining a residual of 4 mg/L for safety.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify corrosion inhibitors in detail. Explain the mechanism of action of (i) anodic inhibitors and (ii) cathodic inhibitors with suitable examples. Why is an under-dosing of anodic inhibitors dangerous?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A mild steel specimen in **3% NaCl solution** has a corrosion rate of **2.4 mm/year** without inhibitor. After addition of **500 ppm of a commercial inhibitor**, the rate drops to **0.18 mm/year**. Calculate the inhibitor efficiency. Is this inhibitor suitable for industrial application?

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B (a):**

**Classification of Inhibitors (as per KTU 2024 Module 3):**

**(i) Anodic Inhibitors — Mechanism:**

Anodic inhibitors form a thin, adherent, and protective oxide film on the **anodic regions** of the metal, blocking the anodic dissolution reaction:

$$\text{Fe} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^-$$

The inhibitor typically reacts with the dissolving metal ions to form an insoluble compound. For example, sodium chromate forms a mixed oxide film:

$$2\text{Fe} + 2\text{Na}_2\text{CrO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + \text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3 + 4\text{NaOH}$$

This $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$–$\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3$ film is highly passivating. **Examples:** Chromates, nitrites, molybdates, orthophosphates.

**(ii) Cathodic Inhibitors — Mechanism:**

Cathodic inhibitors act by suppressing the cathodic reduction reaction. They typically form insoluble precipitates at the cathodic sites, blocking oxygen diffusion. For example, in aerated neutral water:

$$\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$$

Zinc ions precipitate as zinc hydroxide at cathodic sites:

$$\text{Zn}^{2+} + 2\text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{Zn(OH)}_2 \, (\text{film})$$

**Examples:** Polyphosphates, $\text{ZnSO}_4$, $\text{Ca}(\text{HCO}_3)_2$.

**Why Anodic Inhibitor Under-Dosing is Dangerous:**

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Concept — Pitting Risk:**
> If the anodic inhibitor concentration falls **below the critical value**, only a *portion* of the anodic sites is passivated. The remaining active anodic areas become small, intense anodes paired with a large cathodic area (the rest of the surface), creating a **high local current density**. This results in severe **pitting corrosion**. Therefore, anodic inhibitors must always be dosed **above a minimum safe concentration**.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B (b):**

**Given:**
- $CR_0 = 2.4$ mm/year
- $CR_i = 0.18$ mm/year
- Inhibitor concentration = 500 ppm

**Step 1 — Apply Inhibitor Efficiency formula:**

$$\text{IE (\%)} = \frac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0} \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$\text{IE (\%)} = \frac{2.4 - 0.18}{2.4} \times 100 = \frac{2.22}{2.4} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\text{IE (\%)} = 92.5\%}$$

**Step 3 — Suitability Assessment:**

> An IE of **92.5%** is **excellent** and classifies this inhibitor as **"highly effective"** for industrial use (industry benchmark: IE > 90% = good; > 95% = excellent). The 500 ppm dose is also reasonably low, making it cost-effective. The inhibitor is **suitable for industrial application** in marine or cooling water systems.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in PECHT527 — Alteration of Environment:**
>
> 1. **Stoichiometric Error in Bohnenblust Equation (–2 marks):** Students often write the factor as **8** instead of **7.88** and forget the residual sulfite term (–4 in denominator). Always state both: the mass ratio and the residual.
>
> 2. **Confusing Anodic with Cathodic Inhibitors (–1 to 2 marks):** Mixing up the examples. Memorize: **Chromates/Nitrites = Anodic; Zinc/Polyphosphates = Cathodic; Silicates/Amines = Mixed**.
>
> 3. **Missing the Pitting Risk in Anodic Inhibitors (–2 marks):** When asked "Why anodic inhibitors are dangerous?" students often say "they are toxic" but forget the **localized pitting at under-dosing** — this is the *core* answer KTU examiners expect.
>
> 4. **No Units in Final Answer (–1 mark):** Always write mg/L, ppm, or mm/year. Numerical answers without units are penalized.
>
> 5. **Skipping the Reaction Equation (–2 marks):** Every deaeration or inhibitor question expects at least one **balanced chemical equation** to fetch full marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Alteration of Environment (Module 3)**

- ✅ Alteration of environment = modifying the **medium**, not the metal.
- ✅ Three principal sub-topics: **Deaeration**, **Inhibitors**, **pH Control**.
- ✅ **Mechanical deaeration** = thermal stripping; residual DO ≤ 0.005 ppm.
- ✅ **Sodium sulfite reaction:** $2\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$ (mass ratio **7.88**).
- ✅ **Hydrazine reaction:** $\text{N}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{N}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ (volatile products — used in high-pressure boilers).
- ✅ **Bohnenblust Equation:** $C_{\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3} = \dfrac{7.88 \cdot C_{\text{O}_2}}{(F/100) - 0.04}$
- ✅ **Anodic inhibitors** = passive film on anodes; examples: chromates, nitrites, molybdates. **Under-dosing = pitting.**
- ✅ **Cathodic inhibitors** = insoluble film on cathodes; examples: zinc salts, polyphosphates. **Safe at low doses.**
- ✅ **Mixed inhibitors** = film over entire surface; examples: silicates, amines.
- ✅ **Vapor-phase inhibitors (VPI)** = volatile amines for storage protection.
- ✅ **Inhibitor Efficiency (IE)** formula: $\text{IE} = \dfrac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0} \times 100$
- ✅ **pH control:** For Fe, Zn, Cu, optimum corrosion resistance is at **pH 6–9**.
- ✅ **Henry's Law:** Gas solubility ∝ Partial Pressure / Temperature — higher T = lower DO.
- ✅ Always quote **units (mg/L, ppm, mm/year)** in numerical answers.
- ✅ KTU 2024 examiner weightage: **Module 3 ≈ 15–20% of total marks** in Part B.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT527 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of "Design" in Corrosion Engineering

In the context of **Corrosion Engineering (PECHT527)**, **Design** refers to the proactive, systematic application of engineering principles during the planning, material selection, geometric configuration, and protection-strategy selection stages of a structure or component to **minimize, control, or eliminate corrosion degradation** throughout its intended service life.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, corrosion design is not a single activity but an **integrated decision-making framework** that combines:

1. **Material Selection** (corrosion-resistant alloys, polymers, composites)
2. **Geometric Design** (shape, drainage, accessibility, joint type)
3. **Environmental Modification** (inhibitors, coatings, dehumidification)
4. **Electrochemical Protection** (cathodic protection, anodic protection)
5. **Operational Control** (velocity limits, temperature, pressure)
6. **Corrosion Allowance** (extra wall thickness to compensate for material loss)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Corrosion design is the engineering discipline of integrating corrosion prevention into every phase of a project — from conceptual design to decommissioning — using principles of metallurgy, electrochemistry, fluid mechanics, and economics.*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Think of designing a structure the same way you would **build a house in a coastal village prone to heavy monsoon rains**.

If you simply pour concrete and paint it, the salt-laden sea breeze and rain will eat away the structure within a few years — just like a steel pipe without corrosion design will leak in months.

A smart builder (the **corrosion engineer**) instead does the following:

- Chooses **better building materials** (corrosion-resistant alloys / SS 316, FRP, coated rebar) — just like a builder uses weather-resistant bricks.
- Shapes the **roof with a slope** so rainwater drains off (avoiding crevices, pockets, and stagnant zones).
- Keeps **wooden beams away from damp concrete** (avoiding galvanic couples between dissimilar materials).
- Adds an **extra layer of paint + a waterproof membrane** (coatings + inhibitors).
- Installs a **lightning conductor** that safely grounds any stray electrical surges (cathodic protection).
- Keeps the walls **thicker than structurally required** by an extra inch (corrosion allowance) so that even if rain eats into the surface, the wall remains strong for its full 50-year life.

> [!NOTE]
> **In one line:** *Corrosion design is "**building smart, not just strong**" — anticipating every way the environment can attack your material, and engineering a defense before construction begins.*

---

## 1.3 Key Design Metrics & Constants (KTU High-Yield)

| Metric | Standard Value | Symbol | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Design service life of buried pipeline | 30 – 50 | $L$ | years |
| Typical corrosion allowance (carbon steel) | 1.5 – 6.0 | $CA$ | mm |
| Galvanic series potential difference (dangerous) | $> 0.25$ | $\Delta E$ | V |
| Maximum safe velocity for carbon steel in seawater | $< 1.0$ | $v$ | m/s |
| Pipe support spacing (vibration avoidance) | Function of $D$ | — | m |
| Cathodic protection potential for steel | $-0.85$ | $E_{cp}$ | V (vs CSE) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Faraday's Constant** $\mathbf{F = 96{,}485\ C/mol}$ and the **Galvanic Series** are the two single most important reference data for ALL corrosion design problems in KTU exams.

---

## 1.4 The Four Pillars of Corrosion Design (KTU Syllabus Map)

| Pillar | Core Question Answered | KTU Mark Weightage |
|---|---|---|
| **Material Selection** | *What should I build it from?* | ~25% |
| **Geometric Design** | *What shape should it be?* | ~25% |
| **Electrochemical Protection** | *How do I electrically defend it?* | ~30% |
| **Corrosion Allowance** | *How much extra thickness do I need?* | ~20% |

These four pillars form the spine of **Module 3 – Design** and will be the focus of every Part A and Part B question in the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Design Decision Flow (Logic Steps)

The KTU 2024 Scheme expects every student to follow this **structured design logic** for full marks:

**Step 1 — Identify the Environment**
- Determine the corrosive medium (seawater, soil, acid, alkaline, atmospheric).
- Quantify: $T$ (temperature), $pH$, $Cl^-$ concentration, dissolved $O_2$, flow velocity, presence of $H_2S$ / $CO_2$.

**Step 2 — Identify the Likely Corrosion Forms**
Use the environment to predict the dominant degradation mode:
- High $Cl^-$ → **Pitting / Crevice**
- Dissimilar metals + electrolyte → **Galvanic**
- Tensile stress + corrosive environment → **SCC**
- High velocity + suspended solids → **Erosion–Corrosion**
- Alternate wet/dry → **Atmospheric / Stray-current**

**Step 3 — Select Base Material**
Match the material's corrosion rate to the design life and economic constraints.

**Step 4 — Apply Geometric Design Rules**
Avoid crevices, ensure drainage, eliminate dead legs, round corners, butt-weld instead of lap-weld.

**Step 5 — Add Electrochemical Protection if Required**
Cathodic protection (CP) for buried/submerged structures; anodic protection for sulfuric acid tanks.

**Step 6 — Apply Coatings & Linings**
Surface preparation + primer + intermediate + topcoat (e.g., epoxy phenolic for marine).

**Step 7 — Calculate Corrosion Allowance**

**Step 8 — Plan Inspection, Monitoring & Maintenance**
Include access ports, coupon locations, reference electrode ports.

---

## 2.2 Why Each Step Matters (The "Why" Behind the Logic)

| Step | The "Why" | Engineering Consequence if Skipped |
|---|---|---|
| Environment ID | You cannot fight an enemy you have not identified | Wrong material, premature failure |
| Corrosion form ID | Each form has a different counter-measure | Galvanic couple in design → catastrophic bimetallic attack |
| Material selection | Sets the baseline corrosion rate | Wrong alloy → pitting in months |
| Geometric design | Shape controls fluid flow and stagnant zones | Crevice → oxygen differential cell → pitting |
| Electrochemical protection | Provides active defense for the design life | Unprotected steel in soil → failure in 5–10 yrs |
| Coatings | First line of passive defense | Bare steel in marine → 0.1–0.5 mm/yr loss |
| Corrosion allowance | Buys time before leak | No CA → no warning before rupture |
| Inspection plan | Detects failure before it becomes catastrophic | No monitoring → blowouts, explosions |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Module 3 – Design)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table.** It contains every equation that has appeared (or will appear) in the KTU 2024 ESE for this module.

### Table A: Corrosion Allowance & Wall Thickness

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $CA = CR \times L$ | Corrosion allowance = corrosion rate × design life | mm |
| 2 | $t_{design} = t_{struct} + CA$ | Total design thickness = structural + corrosion allowance | mm |
| 3 | $t = \dfrac{P R}{S E - 0.6 P} + CA$ | Pressure vessel / pipe wall thickness (ASME) | mm |
| 4 | $L_{safe} = \dfrac{t_{actual} - CA}{CR}$ | Remaining safe life calculation | years |

### Table B: Cathodic Protection (Sacrificial Anode)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 5 | $I_{req} = A_{total} \times i_{req}$ | Total current required | A |
| 6 | $M_{anode} = \dfrac{I_{req} \times T \times 8760}{C \times U}$ | Mass of anode metal required | kg |
| 7 | $N = \dfrac{M_{anode}}{m_{single}}$ | Number of anodes | — |
| 8 | $R_{anode} = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{2L}{r}\right) - 1\right]$ | Earth resistance of single vertical anode | $\Omega$ |
| 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{consumed}}$ | Anode utilization factor (typ. 0.75 – 0.90) | — |

### Table C: Cathodic Protection (Impressed Current)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 10 | $I_{req} = A_{total} \times i_{req}$ | DC current required | A |
| 11 | $P = V \times I_{req}$ | DC power required for rectifier | W |
| 12 | $N_{anode} = \dfrac{I_{req}}{I_{per\ anode}}$ | Number of impressed current anodes | — |
| 13 | $R_{total} = \dfrac{V_{driver}}{I_{req}}$ | Circuit resistance | $\Omega$ |

### Table D: Galvanic Corrosion Design (Area Effect)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 14 | $i_{cathode} \cdot A_{cathode} = i_{anode} \cdot A_{anode}$ | Charge balance (no polarization) | A |
| 15 | $i_{anode,actual} = i_{anode,eq} \times \dfrac{A_{cathode}}{A_{anode}}$ | Current density amplification due to small anode | A/m² |
| 16 | $CR = \dfrac{K \cdot i_{anode,actual}}{n \cdot \rho \cdot A_{year}}$ | Resulting accelerated corrosion rate | mm/yr |

### Table E: Galvanic Series Potential Difference

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 17 | $\Delta E = \vert E_{cathode} - E_{anode} \vert$ | Driving voltage for galvanic corrosion | V |
| 18 | $\Delta E < 0.25\ V$ → **Generally safe** | KTU thumb rule | V |
| 19 | $\Delta E > 0.25\ V$ → **Isolation required** | KTU thumb rule | V |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical formatting rule:** In the table above, $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes absolute value using the LaTeX `\vert` command — this is intentional to prevent breaking the markdown pipe `|` syntax used in KTU digital answer sheets.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Corrosion Allowance (CA) from Faraday's Law

We start from Faraday's law of electrolysis (a result students learned in Module 1):

$$m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

where:
- $m$ = mass of metal dissolved (g)
- $I$ = corrosion current (A)
- $t$ = time (s)
- $M$ = molar mass (g/mol)
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = 96,485 C/mol

**Step 1 — Convert mass to thickness.**
The volume of metal dissolved is $V = m / \rho$, where $\rho$ is density (g/cm³). The thickness lost is:

$$x = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{m}{\rho \cdot A}$$

**Step 2 — Express thickness in mm/yr using current density $i = I/A$.**
Substitute $m$ from Faraday's law:

$$x = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A} = \frac{i \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

**Step 3 — Convert time to years.**
Let $t = L \times 8760 \times 3600$ s (where $L$ is design life in years). For a 1-year period, $t = 3.1536 \times 10^7$ s.

**Step 4 — Express the corrosion rate $CR$ in mm/yr.**
Setting $A_{year} = 3.1536 \times 10^7$ s/yr, the engineering corrosion rate is:

$$CR = \frac{i \cdot M \cdot A_{year}}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho} \quad \text{(mm/yr)}$$

**Step 5 — Compute corrosion allowance.**
Over a design life of $L$ years, the metal lost is $CA = CR \times L$:

$$\boxed{\,CA = CR \times L \quad \text{(in mm)}\,}$$

This is the **single most important equation** for any Part-B design problem in KTU ESE.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cathodic Protection — Number of Sacrificial Anodes

We need to protect a buried pipeline of length $\ell$ and diameter $D$ for $L$ years.

**Step 1 — Compute surface area to be protected.**
For a pipeline, only the bottom and sides are in contact with soil. Conservatively, we use the full cylindrical area:

$$A_{total} = \pi \cdot D \cdot \ell$$

**Step 2 — Determine the protective current density.**
For bare carbon steel in typical soil: $i_{req} \approx 10$ to $20\ \text{mA/m}^2$. For coated steel: $i_{req} \approx 0.05$ to $0.5\ \text{mA/m}^2$.

**Step 3 — Compute total current required.**

$$I_{req} = A_{total} \times i_{req}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the charge to be delivered over the design life $L$.**

$$Q_{total} = I_{req} \times L \times 8760 \quad \text{(A-hr, since 1 year = 8760 hr)}$$

**Step 5 — Account for anode capacity and utilization.**
The anode capacity $C$ (A·hr/kg) is the total charge deliverable per kg of anode before it is exhausted. The utilization factor $U$ accounts for the fact that an anode cannot be 100% consumed. The required anode mass is:

$$M_{anode} = \frac{Q_{total}}{C \times U} = \frac{I_{req} \times L \times 8760}{C \times U}$$

**Step 6 — Compute number of anodes.**
If each anode weighs $m_{single}$ kg:

$$\boxed{\,N = \frac{M_{anode}}{m_{single}} = \frac{I_{req} \times L \times 8760}{C \times U \times m_{single}}\,}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Galvanic Corrosion — The Area Effect

Consider two dissimilar metals $M_1$ (cathode) and $M_2$ (anode) coupled in an electrolyte.

**Step 1 — Charge conservation (Kirchhoff for electrochemistry).**
The total anodic current must equal the total cathodic current (no charge accumulation):

$$i_a \cdot A_a = i_c \cdot A_c$$

**Step 2 — Anode current density amplification.**
Solving for the actual anodic current density:

$$i_{a,actual} = i_c \cdot \frac{A_c}{A_a}$$

**Step 3 — Engineering implication.**
If $A_c \gg A_a$ (a small anode in contact with a large cathode), $i_{a,actual}$ becomes enormous, and the small anode **dissolves very rapidly**. This is the **"small anode–large cathode" anti-pattern** in KTU design problems.

**Step 4 — Numerical example.**
Take a steel rivet ($A_a = 1\ \text{cm}^2$) holding two copper plates ($A_c = 200\ \text{cm}^2$) in seawater. The anodic current density is amplified by 200×. The corrosion rate increases by 200× compared to uncoupled steel — leading to rivet failure in weeks instead of years.

**Step 5 — Design remedy.**
Always make the **anode larger than the cathode** (e.g., steel bolt in copper plate — the steel bolt is the anode, and it is small → BAD). Reverse the design: use a copper bolt in a steel plate, OR use **dielectric insulation** at the joint.

$$\boxed{\,\text{Design rule:}\ \frac{A_{anode}}{A_{cathode}} \geq 1 \quad \text{to minimize galvanic attack}\,}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Design Strategy

| Corrosion Form | First-Choice Design Strategy | Second-Choice Strategy | Avoid |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Uniform** | Corrosion allowance + coating | Material upgrade | Bare steel exposed to acid |
| **Galvanic** | Avoid dissimilar metal contact | Insulate / use third-metal sacrificial washer | Small anode–large cathode |
| **Pitting** | Use $Mo$-bearing SS (SS 316) | Eliminate $Cl^-$ / lower $T$ | Stagnant $Cl^-$ solutions |
| **Crevice** | Butt-weld + dye-penetrate inspect | Use non-absorbent gaskets (PTFE) | Flanged joints in $Cl^-$ |
| **SCC** | Use low-carbon / stabilized grade | Compressive shot peening | Tensile residual stress + corrosive env. |
| **Erosion** | Reduce velocity, hard-face weld | Use erosion-resistant alloy (Stellite) | Sharp bends, sudden contractions |
| **Cavitation** | Improve inlet hydraulics, use tougher alloy | Increase back-pressure | High-velocity low-pressure regions |
| **Intergranular** | Solution-anneal (e.g., SS 304L) | Use stabilized grade (SS 321, 347) | Welding without post-weld heat treatment |
| **Dealloying** | Use inhibited brass (admiralty) | Avoid stagnant acidic water | High-zinc brass in hot acidic water |

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (Full KTU Style)

> **Problem (Q) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]:**
> A carbon steel pipe of **30 cm diameter** carries seawater at a velocity of **2.5 m/s**. The pipe length is **500 m**. The corrosion rate observed on a coupon is **0.5 mm/yr**. The design life required is **25 years**. Calculate:
> (a) The corrosion allowance to be added.
> (b) The total design thickness if the structural thickness from pressure calc is 8.0 mm.
> (c) The remaining safe life if the actual measured thickness after 10 years is 17 mm.

### Solution:

**Part (a): Corrosion Allowance**
Using Formula (1):

$$CA = CR \times L = 0.5\ \text{mm/yr} \times 25\ \text{yr}$$

$$\boxed{CA = 12.5\ \text{mm}}$$

**Part (b): Total Design Thickness**
Using Formula (2):

$$t_{design} = t_{struct} + CA = 8.0\ \text{mm} + 12.5\ \text{mm}$$

$$\boxed{t_{design} = 20.5\ \text{mm}}$$

**Part (c): Remaining Safe Life**
Actual thickness after 10 years: $t_{actual} = 17\ \text{mm}$
Metal consumed in 10 years: $8.0 + 12.5 - 17 = 3.5\ \text{mm}$
Observed rate: $3.5 / 10 = 0.35\ \text{mm/yr}$
Using Formula (4):

$$L_{safe} = \frac{t_{actual} - CA_{future}}{CR} = \frac{17 - 0}{0.35} = 48.6\ \text{years\ from\ now}$$

Wait — recompute carefully. $L_{safe} = (t_{actual} - t_{struct})/CR = (17 - 8)/0.35 = 25.7$ years remaining.

$$\boxed{L_{safe} \approx 25.7\ \text{years}}$$

This problem illustrates the **complete use of the corrosion-allowance formula family** and is a guaranteed 14-marker in the KTU 2024 ESE.

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: The Corrosion Design Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    B --> C[Predict Dominant Corrosion Form]
    C --> D{Material Sufficient?}
    D -- No --> E[Upgrade Material / Add Lining]
    D -- Yes --> F[Apply Geometric Design Rules]
    F --> G[Check Galvanic Compatibility]
    G --> H{Dissimilar Metals Present?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Add Dielectric Insulation]
    H -- No --> J[Select Coating System]
    I --> J
    J --> K{Cathodic Protection Needed?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Design CP System]
    K -- No --> M[Compute Corrosion Allowance]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Add Inspection and Monitoring Ports]
    N --> O[Final Design Review]
    O --> P[End: Issue for Construction]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    A3 --> A4{Anode Type?}

    A5 --> A6[Find C and U from Datasheet]

    A7 --> A8[Compute Number N = M / m_single]
    A8 --> A9[Check Anode Spacing and Resistance]
    A4 -- Impressed Current --> A10[Choose MMO or Scrap Anode]

```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Geometric Design Rules (Anti-Pattern vs Good Design)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph BAD[BAD DESIGN - Creates Corrosion]
        B1[Lap joint with crevice] --> B2[Stagnant fluid trapped]
        B2 --> B3[Oxygen differential cell]
        B3 --> B4[Crevice pitting corrosion]
    end
    subgraph GOOD[GOOD DESIGN - Prevents Corrosion]
        G1[Butt weld fully penetrated] --> G2[Smooth fluid flow]
        G2 --> G3[No oxygen gradient]
        G3 --> G4[No crevice attack]
    end
    BAD -. AVOID .-> GOOD
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Galvanic Series and Design Compatibility Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Most Active / Anodic] --> A2[Magnesium]
    A2 --> A3[Zinc]
    A3 --> A4[Aluminum Alloys]
    A4 --> A5[Carbon Steel]
    A5 --> A6[Cast Iron]
    A6 --> A7[Lead Tin]
    A7 --> A8[Brass Copper]
    A8 --> A9[Bronze]
    A9 --> A10[Nickel Copper Alloys]
    A10 --> A11[Stainless Steel 304]

    A12 --> A13[Nickel Alloys]
    A13 --> A14[Titanium]
    A14 --> A15[Graphite Most Noble Cathodic]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** When two metals are connected and immersed in an electrolyte, the **higher (more active) metal corrodes** preferentially. To prevent galvanic corrosion in design, choose metals that are **close together** in this vertical list, or use **dielectric isolation** at the joint.

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5: Corrosion Allowance Concept (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pipe Inner Surface] --> B[Original Wall]
    B --> C[Corroded Layer over Design Life]
    C --> D[Remaining Sound Metal]
    D --> E[Required Structural Thickness]
    F[Corrosion Allowance CA] --> G[Extra thickness added during manufacture]
    G --> B
```

> **Interpretation:** The figure represents a cross-section of a pipe. The **structural thickness** (the minimum required to hold pressure) is preserved throughout the design life. The **corrosion allowance** is the **extra thickness** added during fabrication that gradually gets consumed by corrosion. After $L$ years, the wall is just at the structural limit — the **safe end-of-life condition**.
