# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]

**Define corrosion allowance. Why is it important in the design of chemical process equipment?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Corrosion allowance ($CA$) is the **extra thickness of metal** deliberately added to a component beyond its calculated structural requirement, to compensate for the **anticipated material loss due to corrosion** over the design service life.

**Mathematical form (0.5 mark):** $CA = CR \times L$ (in mm), where $CR$ is the corrosion rate in mm/yr and $L$ is the design life in years.

**Importance (1 mark):**
- It provides a **safety margin** against unexpected corrosion.
- It allows the equipment to operate **safely for the full design life** without leakage or failure.
- It permits **planned inspection intervals** — operators know when thickness approaches the structural limit.
- It is the **simplest and cheapest** form of corrosion protection (no additional equipment needed).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Set B]

**List any SIX important design considerations to prevent corrosion in process equipment.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.5 mark each):**

1. **Avoid crevices** in joints, gaskets, and lap welds — use full-penetration butt welds.
2. **Ensure complete drainage** — slope piping at minimum 1:100 gradient, eliminate dead legs.
3. **Avoid galvanic couples** — keep dissimilar metals electrically isolated, or use the more noble metal as the fastener and the more active as the larger component.
4. **Round all corners** — avoid sharp internal angles that concentrate stress and trap fluids.
5. **Allow accessibility for inspection and maintenance** — provide manholes, coupon ports, and reference-electrode access.
6. **Select material compatible with the environment** — match alloy composition (e.g., $Mo$ for $Cl^-$ service) to the corrosion form expected.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Internal Choice Pattern:** *All Part B questions in the KTU 2024 ESE are split into two equal sub-parts of **7 marks each**, OR are offered as a choice between two full 14-mark questions. Both styles are presented below.*

---

### Q3. Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

**(a) Discuss in detail the various design considerations to prevent different forms of corrosion, with suitable engineering examples.** (7 marks)

**(b) A buried carbon steel pipeline of 25 cm diameter and 800 m length is to be protected by a sacrificial magnesium anode cathodic protection system. The design life is 30 years. The required protective current density is 15 mA/m². The magnesium anode has a capacity of 1230 A·hr/kg and a utilization factor of 0.85. Each anode weighs 5 kg. Calculate the number of anodes required.** (7 marks)

---

#### Solution to Q3(a) — Design Considerations (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Corrosion design is the proactive integration of material, geometric, electrochemical, and operational measures into a component or system to achieve a target service life with acceptable risk and cost.

**Design considerations — point-wise (6 × 1 mark each):**

1. **Material Selection** — Choose alloys that are inherently resistant to the environment. Example: Use **SS 316** instead of SS 304 in chloride-bearing seawater to prevent pitting. Use **Hastelloy C-276** in hot HCl service.

2. **Geometric Design — Avoid Crevices** — Crevices trap electrolyte and create oxygen-depleted zones, leading to **crevice corrosion**. Example: Replace **lap joints with full-penetration butt welds**; use **PTFE gaskets** instead of compressed fiber.

3. **Geometric Design — Ensure Drainage and Ventilation** — Stagnant liquid leads to under-deposit corrosion and MIC. Example: Slope process piping at **1:100 minimum**; install drain plugs at low points.

4. **Geometric Design — Avoid Sharp Corners and Eddies** — High local turbulence causes erosion–corrosion. Example: Use **long-radius elbows** ($R/D \geq 3$) instead of sharp 90° bends in slurry service.

5. **Avoid Galvanic Couples** — Connect only metals close in the galvanic series. Example: Do **not** use **copper piping directly connected to galvanized steel** in a water distribution system; use a **dielectric union** at the joint.

6. **Use Corrosion Allowance** — Add extra thickness to compensate for material loss. Example: In a refinery overhead condenser, add **3 mm CA** on top of the structural wall thickness for 20-year life.

7. **Cathodic / Anodic Protection** — For buried or submerged steel, install **CP**. For sulfuric acid storage tanks, use **anodic protection** with a controlled passivating potential.

8. **Apply Protective Coatings** — Epoxy phenolic for marine, FBE for buried pipelines, Zn-rich primer for atmospheric steel.

9. **Control Operating Conditions** — Limit velocity, reduce temperature, deaerate, add inhibitors.

10. **Provide Inspection Access** — Manholes, coupon holders, reference electrode ports, UT thickness monitoring points.

**Conclusion (built into points):** An integrated design that combines all ten considerations gives the lowest lifecycle cost.

---

#### Solution to Q3(b) — Number of Mg Anodes (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- $D = 25\ \text{cm} = 0.25\ \text{m}$
- $\ell = 800\ \text{m}$
- $L = 30\ \text{years}$
- $i_{req} = 15\ \text{mA/m}^2 = 0.015\ \text{A/m}^2$
- $C = 1230\ \text{A·hr/kg}$
- $U = 0.85$
- $m_{single} = 5\ \text{kg}$

**Step 1 — Compute the surface area (0.5 mark):**

$$A_{total} = \pi D \ell = \pi \times 0.25 \times 800 = 628.3\ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute total current required (0.5 mark):**

$$I_{req} = A_{total} \times i_{req} = 628.3 \times 0.015 = 9.425\ \text{A}$$

**Step 3 — Compute total charge over 30 years (1 mark):**

$$Q_{total} = I_{req} \times L \times 8760 = 9.425 \times 30 \times 8760 = 2{,}476{,}890\ \text{A·hr}$$

**Step 4 — Compute mass of magnesium required (1 mark):**

$$M_{anode} = \frac{Q_{total}}{C \times U} = \frac{2{,}476{,}890}{1230 \times 0.85} = 2369.4\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 5 — Compute number of anodes (1 mark):**

$$N = \frac{M_{anode}}{m_{single}} = \frac{2369.4}{5} = 473.9 \approx 474\ \text{anodes}$$

**Step 6 — Final statement (1 mark):**
A minimum of **474 magnesium anodes** of 5 kg each is required to protect the pipeline for 30 years. In practice, a **safety factor of 1.2** is applied → $N_{design} = 474 \times 1.2 \approx 569$ anodes.

---

### Q4. Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Set A]

**(a) Explain with neat sketches the principles of design to prevent galvanic corrosion and crevice corrosion. What is the "area effect" in galvanic corrosion?** (7 marks)

**(b) A carbon steel tank is to be coated with an epoxy coating. The required Dry Film Thickness (DFT) is 250 µm. The coating is to be applied in three coats of 85 µm, 90 µm, and 90 µm (wet film thickness, with 50% volume solids). Calculate the actual DFT obtained. If the design calls for 250 µm, is the coating adequate? Suggest a remedy if not.** (7 marks)

---

#### Solution to Q4(a) — Galvanic & Crevice Design Principles (7 marks)

**Galvanic Corrosion Design (3.5 marks):**

Galvanic corrosion occurs when two dissimilar metals are in electrical contact in an electrolyte. The more active (anodic) metal corrodes preferentially.

**Design rules to prevent galvanic corrosion:**

1. **Avoid dissimilar metal couples** — Choose metals close in the galvanic series (potential difference $< 0.25\ \text{V}$).
2. **Provide electrical isolation** — Use **dielectric gaskets, sleeves, and washers** at bolted joints. Example: rubber or PTFE insulator between a stainless steel bolt and a carbon steel flange.
3. **Control the area effect** — If coupling is unavoidable, make the **anode area larger than the cathode area**. (Never use a small steel bolt to fasten a large copper plate — the bolt will fail in weeks.)
4. **Apply coatings with care** — Coat **both** metals, but if only one can be coated, coat the **cathode** (more noble). A defect in the coating on the anode dramatically accelerates attack.
5. **Use a third (sacrificial) metal** — A zinc washer between a steel bolt and a copper plate acts as the anode, protecting both.
6. **Avoid threaded joints between dissimilar metals** — Use welded or insulated flanges instead.

**Sketch / Schematic (built into the points):**
Use a labelled cross-section showing: copper plate (cathode) — dielectric sleeve — steel bolt (anode) — electrolyte film — current flow arrows from anode to cathode.

**Crevice Corrosion Design (2.5 marks):**

Crevice corrosion is a localized form of attack in narrow gaps where the electrolyte becomes stagnant and oxygen-depleted.

**Design rules to prevent crevice corrosion:**

1. **Eliminate crevices** by using **butt welds** instead of lap welds; seal the weld root.
2. **Use non-absorbent gaskets** (PTFE, flexible graphite) instead of compressed asbestos or fiber.
3. **Ensure complete drainage** — no horizontal dead legs, all pockets drainable.
4. **Close gaps** by continuous welding, soldering, or use of sealants.
5. **Avoid threaded connections** in chloride service — weld instead.
6. **Use crevice-resistant alloys** (e.g., **SS 316 with $Mo$** for seawater) and **high-Cr / high-Mo super-austenitic** grades for severe service.

**Area Effect (1 mark):**
The "area effect" is the principle that the **corrosion current density on the anode is amplified** in proportion to the ratio of the cathode area to the anode area. A small anode coupled to a large cathode experiences a very high current density and fails rapidly. Mathematically:

$$i_{anode,actual} = i_{cathode} \times \frac{A_{cathode}}{A_{anode}}$$

---

#### Solution to Q4(b) — Coating DFT Calculation (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- Required DFT = 250 µm
- Three coats applied (wet film thickness, WFT): 85 µm, 90 µm, 90 µm
- Volume Solids (VS) = 50% = 0.50

**Concept (1 mark):**
The relationship between Wet Film Thickness (WFT) and Dry Film Thickness (DFT) is:

$$DFT = WFT \times VS$$

**Step 1 — Compute DFT of each coat (1.5 marks):**
- Coat 1: $DFT_1 = 85 \times 0.50 = 42.5\ \mu\text{m}$
- Coat 2: $DFT_2 = 90 \times 0.50 = 45.0\ \mu\text{m}$
- Coat 3: $DFT_3 = 90 \times 0.50 = 45.0\ \mu\text{m}$

**Step 2 — Total DFT achieved (1 mark):**

$$DFT_{total} = 42.5 + 45.0 + 45.0 = 132.5\ \mu\text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Comparison with design (1 mark):**
$$DFT_{required} = 250\ \mu\text{m} \quad \text{vs.} \quad DFT_{achieved} = 132.5\ \mu\text{m}$$

$$\text{Shortfall} = 250 - 132.5 = 117.5\ \mu\text{m}$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The coating is **NOT adequate** — it is **47% below** the design DFT.

**Step 4 — Remedy (1.5 marks):**
Several options can be applied:
1. **Apply an additional coat** of WFT = 235 µm (since $235 \times 0.5 = 117.5\ \mu\text{m}$ DFT).
2. **Use a higher-solids coating** (e.g., 100% solids epoxy or polyurethane at $VS = 0.80$): for the same WFT of 85+90+90 = 265 µm, the new DFT = $265 \times 0.80 = 212\ \mu\text{m}$ — still short. Need WFT = $250/0.8 = 312.5\ \mu\text{m}$.
3. **Use plural-component spray** with $VS \geq 95\%$ → WFT = 263 µm sufficient.
4. **Re-evaluate the design DFT** with the asset owner; in mild service, 132.5 µm may be acceptable.

**Final Recommendation:** Apply a fourth coat of WFT = 235 µm (with 50% VS) to meet the 250 µm design DFT.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 ESE Module 3 — Design:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — A huge number of students write $I = A \times i$ without checking if $i$ is in $A/m^2$ or $mA/m^2$. Always state the unit conversion explicitly in the solution.
> 2. **Mixing time units in Faraday's law** — Some students use $t$ in years directly in $Q = I \times t$ when $C$ is in A·hr/kg. Always multiply by 8760 hr/yr to convert.
> 3. **Confusing "small anode–large cathode" rule** — Many students incorrectly say "make the cathode small." The correct rule is **make the ANODE area $\geq$ cathode area** to *reduce* the current density on the anode.
> 4. **Omitting the safety factor in CP design** — KTU expects a 1.2–1.3 safety factor on anode mass or current. Without it, full marks are not awarded.
> 5. **Ignoring the area effect in galvanic corrosion** — Even if the metals are close in the galvanic series, a poor area ratio (small anode, large cathode) can cause rapid failure. Always mention both criteria.
> 6. **Using lap-welded joints in the diagrams** — In the KTU 2024 scheme, **lap joints are penalised** in design sketches; use full-penetration butt welds.
> 7. **Confusing WFT and DFT** — A 100 µm WFT of a 50% solids coating is **NOT 100 µm DFT**; it is 50 µm DFT. State the formula clearly.
> 8. **Forgetting to specify the reference electrode** — When stating the CP potential of $-0.85$ V, always specify "**vs. CSE (Copper–Copper Sulfate Reference Electrode)**." Without this, the answer is incomplete.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist. It is your last-minute revision sheet for KTU ESE Module 3 — Design.**

### Core Concepts to Memorize

- [ ] Corrosion Allowance formula: $CA = CR \times L$
- [ ] Pressure-vessel wall thickness with CA: $t = \dfrac{PR}{SE - 0.6P} + CA$
- [ ] Cathodic protection current: $I_{req} = A \times i_{req}$
- [ ] Sacrificial anode mass: $M = \dfrac{I \times L \times 8760}{C \times U}$
- [ ] Number of anodes: $N = M / m_{single}$
- [ ] Galvanic area effect: $i_{anode,actual} = i_{cathode} \times A_c / A_a$
- [ ] CP potential for steel: $-0.85\ \text{V}$ vs CSE
- [ ] Impressed current rectifier power: $P = V \times I$
- [ ] Coating DFT: $DFT = WFT \times VS$
- [ ] Galvanic safety threshold: $\Delta E < 0.25\ \text{V}$

### Design Rules (Geometric)

- [ ] **Butt-weld** > lap-weld
- [ ] **Drainage slope** ≥ 1:100
- [ ] **Long-radius elbows** ($R/D \geq 3$)
- [ ] **No dead legs** in piping
- [ ] **Round internal corners**, no sharp angles
- [ ] **Manholes and access** for inspection at every 6 m on tanks

### Material & Galvanic Pairing Quick Reference

- [ ] **SS 316** in chloride / marine service (resists pitting)
- [ ] **SS 304L / 316L** to avoid intergranular corrosion after welding
- [ ] **Hastelloy** in hot HCl
- [ ] **Titanium** in hot chloride + oxidizing conditions
- [ ] **FRP / GRP** in HCl and H₂SO₄ storage
- [ ] **CPVC** in hot water service
- [ ] **Dielectric union** between galvanized steel and copper piping
- [ ] **Zinc-rich primer** under epoxy topcoat for steel in marine atmosphere

### Numerical Constants to Keep Handy

- [ ] $F = 96{,}485\ \text{C/mol}$
- [ ] $1\ \text{year} = 8760\ \text{hr} = 3.1536 \times 10^7\ \text{s}$
- [ ] Density of steel = 7.85 g/cm³
- [ ] Density of zinc = 7.13 g/cm³
- [ ] Density of magnesium = 1.74 g/cm³
- [ ] Typical anode capacity: Zn = 780 A·hr/kg; Mg = 1230 A·hr/kg; Al = 2700 A·hr/kg
- [ ] Typical utilization factor: 0.75 to 0.90
- [ ] Typical $i_{req}$ for bare steel in soil: 10–20 mA/m²
- [ ] Typical $i_{req}$ for coated steel in soil: 0.05–0.5 mA/m²

### Common Traps / Exam Day Don'ts

- [ ] Do **not** use SI and CGS units in the same equation.
- [ ] Do **not** state $CA$ without specifying **units (mm)**.
- [ ] Do **not** ignore the **safety factor** in CP design.
- [ ] Do **not** use **lap joints** in design diagrams.
- [ ] Do **not** forget to specify the **reference electrode** in CP questions.
- [ ] Do **not** confuse **WFT and DFT** in coating problems.
- [ ] Do **not** couple **stainless steel** with **carbon steel** without insulation in chloride service.

### One-Sentence Summary of Module 3 — Design

> *Module 3 — Design is the engineering discipline of building-in corrosion resistance by **smart material selection, geometric hygiene, electrochemical protection, and a measured corrosion allowance** so that the structure survives its full design life with minimum lifecycle cost.*

---

**End of Module 3 — Design Notes | PECHT527 Corrosion Engineering | KTU 2024 Scheme**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cathodic protection and anodic protection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT527 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cathodic protection and anodic protection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Cathodic Protection (CP) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cathodic Protection (CP)** is an electrochemical corrosion-control technique in which the metal structure to be protected is forced to become the **cathode** of a deliberately engineered electrochemical cell, thereby suppressing the anodic (oxidation/dissolution) reaction on its surface. This is achieved either by supplying an external direct current from an inert anode (**Impressed Current Cathodic Protection – ICCP**) or by electrically connecting the structure to a more electrochemically active metal that preferentially corrodes (**Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection – SACP**, also called *Galvanic Cathodic Protection*).

The fundamental thermodynamic basis is derived from the **Nernst equation** and **Pourbaix diagrams**: by polarizing the protected metal in the **negative (cathodic) direction**, its open-circuit potential (E_corr) is shifted below the **equilibrium potential of the anodic reaction (E°_{M/M²⁺})**, making the net anodic current density → **0**, so corrosion is thermodynamically and kinetically halted.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Protection Criterion (NACE SP0169):** For carbon steel in soil/water, the structure-to-soil "instant-off" potential must be more negative than **−850 mV vs. Copper/Copper Sulfate Reference Electrode (CSE)**. An alternative criterion is a minimum of **100 mV** of cathodic polarization between the structure and a stable reference electrode.

### 1.2 Anodic Protection (AP) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anodic Protection (AP)** is an electrochemical technique applicable **only to metals/alloys that exhibit active–passive behavior** (e.g., stainless steels, Ni-based alloys, Ti, carbon steel in concentrated H₂SO₄). The protected structure is made the **anode** of an auxiliary cell, and a controlled **anodic current** is impressed to drive and maintain its potential within the **passive region** of its anodic polarization curve, where the metal spontaneously forms a thin, adherent, protective oxide film (e.g., Cr₂O₃ on stainless steel). A **potentiostat** continuously compares the structure's potential with a reference electrode and adjusts the current to hold the potential within the safe passive window.

The principle relies on the **Flade potential** (E_F) — the potential below which the passive film is reduced and above which it is stable. AP is unworkable for active metals like mild steel in neutral environments because they lack a passive region.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

**Cathodic Protection Analogy (The "Sacrificial Bodyguard"):**
Imagine a king (your steel pipeline) is constantly under attack by corrosion goblins. CP hires a **bodyguard** (the sacrificial anode) made of a more "popular" metal (zinc, magnesium, or aluminum). The goblins, being opportunistic, attack the bodyguard instead of the king. The bodyguard willingly "takes the hit" and dissolves in the king's place. Once the bodyguard is consumed, you simply replace it — the king remains untouched. In the **ICCP version**, instead of a bodyguard, you hire an *electric bouncer* (a DC power source) that electrocutes the goblins at the gate, preventing them from reaching the king.

**Anodic Protection Analogy (The "Magic Shield"):**
Some knights (stainless steel, Ti) have a magical self-healing invisible shield (passive oxide layer) that only appears when they are slightly "agitated" electrically. The potentiostat plays the role of a **trainer** who constantly pokes the knight with a controlled electric prod to keep the shield activated, but never pokes so hard that the shield breaks. The prod is cheap; rebuilding the knight from corrosion would be astronomically expensive.

### 1.4 Key Standards, Constants, and Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Standard | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Faraday's constant | F | **96,485** | C·mol⁻¹ |
| Density of zinc | ρ_Zn | **7.14** | g·cm⁻³ |
| Density of magnesium | ρ_Mg | **1.74** | g·cm⁻³ |
| Density of aluminum | ρ_Al | **2.70** | g·cm⁻³ |
| E° Zn/Zn²⁺ | — | **−1.10** | V vs SHE |
| E° Mg/Mg²⁺ | — | **−2.37** | V vs SHE |
| E° Fe/Fe²⁺ | — | **−0.44** | V vs SHE |
| Protection criterion (steel) | E_prot | **−0.850** | V vs CSE |
| CSE potential vs SHE | E_CSE | **+0.318** | V |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Cathodic Protection — The Electrochemical Logic

**Step 1 — The Problem (Mixed-Potential Theory of Evans):**
On a corroding metal surface, anodic half-cells (M → M²⁺ + 2e⁻) and cathodic half-cells (O₂ + 2H₂O + 4e⁻ → 4OH⁻) coexist at the **corrosion potential E_corr** with current density **i_corr**. The local action of these cells is the corrosion current.

**Step 2 — The Polarization:**
When an external current is imposed, the metal is **polarized cathodically**. The Evans diagram shows that as potential becomes more negative, the anodic dissolution current drops exponentially (Tafel behavior), while the cathodic current rises. At the **protective potential E_prot**, the anodic current is reduced to a negligible, safe level (typically < 1 μA/cm²).

**Step 3 — The Protection Current Density (i_cp):**
Every structure has a current demand:

$$I_{cp} \;=\; i_{cp} \times A_{surface}$$

where:
- $I_{cp}$ = total cathodic protection current (Amperes)
- $i_{cp}$ = required current density (typically **10–30 mA/m²** for coated steel in soil, **50–150 mA/m²** for bare steel in seawater, **>300 mA/m²** for hot, aggressive, low-resistivity soils)
- $A_{surface}$ = wetted/exposed surface area (m²)

**Step 4 — Anode Selection & Lifetime Calculation:**

For **Sacrificial Anodes**, the lifetime is governed by the **electrochemical capacity** C (Ah·kg⁻¹) of the anode material:

$$C \;=\; \frac{n \cdot F}{M \times 3.6}$$

where:
- n = number of electrons (e.g., 2 for Zn, 2 for Mg)
- M = molar mass (kg·mol⁻¹)
- F = Faraday's constant
- 3.6 = conversion factor (3600 s/h ÷ 1000 g/kg)

The **anode life** (T) in years:

$$T \;=\; \frac{m_{anode} \times C \times U}{I_{cp} \times 8760}$$

where:
- $m_{anode}$ = mass of anode (kg)
- $U$ = **utilization factor** (typically 0.85 for Zn, 0.50 for Mg due to non-uniform corrosion)
- 8760 = hours per year

**Step 5 — Circuit Design (Ohm's Law for ICCP):**

$$V_{DC} \;=\; I_{cp} \times R_{circuit} \;+\; \Delta E_{anode/cathode}$$

where $R_{circuit}$ includes the **anode-to-electrolyte resistance** (often dominant). For a single vertical anode in infinite homogeneous soil:

$$R_v \;=\; \frac{\rho}{2 \pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{4L}{r}\right) - 1 \right]$$

For a horizontal anode:

$$R_h \;=\; \frac{\rho}{2 \pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{2L}{r}\right) + \ln\left(\frac{L}{\sqrt{2 d \cdot r}}\right) - 0.5 \right]$$

where ρ = soil resistivity (Ω·m), L = anode length, r = anode equivalent radius, d = burial depth.

### 2.2 Anodic Protection — The Passive Window Logic

**Step 1 — Identify the Anodic Polarization Curve:**
A typical active–passive metal shows three regions:
1. **Active region** (E < E_p): metal dissolves actively, current rises with potential.
2. **Active–Passive transition** (E ≈ E_p): critical current density **i_cc** peak.
3. **Passive region** (E_p < E < E_T): current drops to low **i_p** (often 100–1000× lower than i_cc).
4. **Transpassive region** (E > E_T): pitting, oxygen evolution, or transpassive dissolution (e.g., Cr⁶⁺ formation).

**Step 2 — Maintain Potential in the Passive Window:**
The potentiostat reads the structure's potential (vs. reference electrode) and adjusts current to keep E_str within [E_p, E_T]. The current consumed is the small passive current i_p × A.

**Step 3 — Why AP is Energy-Efficient in Concentrated Acids:**
In concentrated H₂SO₄ (>70%), active dissolution of carbon steel would consume enormous currents. But in the passive region, i_p is tiny (~few μA/cm²), so power consumption is negligible. AP is the standard for sulfuric acid coolers, alkylation units in refineries, and titanium tanks for HNO₃.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Application / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_{cp} = i_{cp} \times A$ | Total CP current requirement |
| 2 | $m_{corr} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Mass of metal dissolved by stray/galvanic current (Faraday's Law) |
| 3 | $C = \dfrac{n \cdot F}{M \times 3.6}$ | Electrochemical capacity of sacrificial anode (Ah/kg) |
| 4 | $T = \dfrac{m_{anode} \cdot C \cdot U}{I_{cp} \cdot 8760}$ | Anode design life in years |
| 5 | $R_v = \dfrac{\rho}{2 \pi L}\left[\ln\left(\dfrac{4L}{r}\right) - 1\right]$ | Vertical anode-to-soil resistance (Dwight's formula) |
| 6 | $R_h = \dfrac{\rho}{2 \pi L}\left[\ln\left(\dfrac{2L}{r}\right) + \ln\left(\dfrac{L}{\sqrt{2dr}}\right) - 0.5\right]$ | Horizontal anode-to-soil resistance |
| 7 | $V_{DC} = I_{cp} \cdot R_{circuit} + \Delta E$ | ICCP rectifier sizing |
| 8 | $E_{CSE} = E_{SHE} + 0.318$ V | Potential conversion CSE ↔ SHE |
| 9 | $\eta = \beta_a \log\left(\dfrac{i_a}{i_{corr}}\right)$ | Anodic activation overpotential (Tafel) |
| 10 | $E_{prot} = E_{corr} - \Delta E_{pol}$ | Cathodic polarization criterion (100 mV shift) |
| 11 | $P_{AP} = i_p \times A \times E_{cell}$ | Anodic protection power requirement |
| 12 | $i_{corr} = \dfrac{i_{cc} \cdot i_p}{i_{cc} + i_p}$ | Corrosion current estimate (3-point approximation) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** CP is the workhorse for **subsea pipelines, ship hulls, offshore wind foundations, underground tank bottoms, reinforced concrete bridge decks, and water utility mains**. AP is the niche, energy-efficient choice for **sulfuric/nitric acid storage, fertilizer reactors, and titanium-based chemical process equipment**.

### 2.4 Critical Comparison Matrix — CP vs. AP

| Feature | Cathodic Protection | Anodic Protection |
|---|---|---|
| **Role of protected metal** | Cathode | Anode |
| **Mechanism** | Suppresses anodic reaction | Stabilizes passive oxide film |
| **Required external power** | Only for ICCP (yes); SACP (no) | Yes (potentiostat mandatory) |
| **Applicable metals** | All metals (universal) | Only active–passive metals (limited) |
| **Typical environment** | Soil, seawater, freshwater | Concentrated H₂SO₄, HNO₃, fertilizer solutions |
| **Current direction** | Electrons flow *into* structure | Electrons flow *out of* structure |
| **Reference electrode needed?** | For monitoring only | Essential for control (potentiostat loop) |
| **Failure consequence** | Localized pitting if under-protected | Catastrophic — film breakdown → rapid dissolution |
| **Power cost** | Moderate (W to kW range) | Low (i_p is tiny) but precision-critical |
| **Anode material** | Zn, Mg, Al (sacrificial) or Fe–Si, MMO/Ti, graphite (inert) | Auxiliary cathode: Pt, Hastelloy, stainless steel |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Electrochemical Capacity of a Sacrificial Anode

**Objective:** Show that 1 kg of zinc theoretically delivers ~825 Ah of protective current.

**Step 1 — Write the anodic dissolution half-reaction:**

$$\text{Zn} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^-$$

**Step 2 — Identify the molar mass and valence:**

- M(Zn) = 65.38 g/mol
- n = 2 electrons per atom

**Step 3 — Compute charge per mole using Faraday's Law:**

$$Q_{mol} \;=\; n \cdot F \;=\; 2 \times 96{,}485 \;\text{C/mol} \;=\; 192{,}970 \;\text{C/mol}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to ampere-hours (divide by 3600 s/h):**

$$Q_{mol}^{Ah} \;=\; \frac{192{,}970}{3600} \;\approx\; 53.6 \;\text{Ah/mol}$$

**Step 5 — Compute capacity per kilogram:**

$$C_{Zn} \;=\; \frac{Q_{mol}^{Ah}}{M_{Zn}} \;\times\; 1000 \;\text{(g/kg)} \;=\; \frac{53.6 \times 1000}{65.38} \;\approx\; 820 \;\text{Ah/kg}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the utilization factor U = 0.85 for practical service:**

$$C_{Zn,\,practical} \;=\; 820 \times 0.85 \;\approx\; 697 \;\text{Ah/kg}$$

> **Engineering takeaway:** Published Zn alloy anodes list **~780 Ah/kg** (theoretical) and **~700 Ah/kg** practical, matching this derivation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Cathodic Protection Current for a Buried Pipeline

**Problem statement:** A 5 km long buried steel pipeline of 60 cm diameter is to be protected. Soil resistivity ρ = 50 Ω·m. Required cathodic current density i_cp = 20 mA/m². Design a SACP system using Zn anodes (each anode mass = 12 kg, U = 0.85) and compute the number of anodes and anode spacing.

**Step 1 — Compute the wetted external surface area of the pipeline:**

$$A \;=\; \pi \cdot D \cdot L \;=\; \pi \times 0.60 \times 5000 \;\text{m}^2$$

$$A \;\approx\; 9424.8 \;\text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute the total cathodic current requirement:**

$$I_{cp} \;=\; i_{cp} \times A \;=\; 0.020 \;\text{A/m}^2 \times 9424.8 \;\text{m}^2$$

$$I_{cp} \;\approx\; 188.5 \;\text{A}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the useful charge per Zn anode (12 kg, U = 0.85):**

$$Q_{anode} \;=\; m_{anode} \times C_{Zn} \times U \;=\; 12 \times 820 \times 0.85 \;\text{Ah}$$

$$Q_{anode} \;\approx\; 8364 \;\text{Ah}$$

**Step 4 — Assume each anode delivers a constant current of about 0.5 A (typical for 12 kg Zn in 50 Ω·m soil):**

$$N_{anodes} \;=\; \frac{I_{cp}}{I_{anode}} \;=\; \frac{188.5}{0.5} \;\approx\; 377 \;\text{anodes}$$

**Step 5 — Compute anode spacing along the pipeline:**

$$s \;=\; \frac{L}{N_{anodes}} \;=\; \frac{5000}{377} \;\approx\; 13.3 \;\text{m}$$

**Step 6 — Compute service life (replace when 85% consumed):**

$$T \;=\; \frac{Q_{anode}}{I_{anode} \times 8760} \;=\; \frac{8364}{0.5 \times 8760} \;\approx\; 1.91 \;\text{years}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical refinement:** Designers adjust for **coating breakdown factor**, **dielectric shields**, **anode-to-soil resistance** (via Dwight's formula), and **temperature derating**. This is why the actual design uses iterative computation rather than the simple geometry above.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Rectifier (DC Source) Sizing for an ICCP System

**Problem:** An ICCP station protects a 2 km subsea pipeline. Required current = 25 A. Anode-to-electrolyte resistance R_a = 1.2 Ω, structure-to-electrolyte (cathode) resistance R_c = 0.4 Ω, cable resistance R_cable = 0.3 Ω, back-EMF counter-potential of the auxiliary anode reaction = 2.0 V (oxygen evolution on MMO).

**Step 1 — Total circuit resistance (using Kirchhoff's Voltage Law):**

$$R_{total} \;=\; R_a + R_c + R_{cable} \;=\; 1.2 + 0.4 + 0.3 \;\Omega$$

$$R_{total} \;=\; 1.9 \;\Omega$$

**Step 2 — IR drop across the circuit:**

$$V_{IR} \;=\; I \times R_{total} \;=\; 25 \times 1.9 \;\text{V} \;=\; 47.5 \;\text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Add counter-EMF (anode overpotential):**

$$V_{DC} \;=\; V_{IR} + \Delta E_{anode} \;=\; 47.5 + 2.0 \;\text{V}$$

$$V_{DC} \;=\; 49.5 \;\text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Rectifier rating with 20% safety margin:**

$$V_{rated} \;\geq\; 49.5 \times 1.20 \;\approx\; 60 \;\text{V}$$

**Step 5 — Power consumption:**

$$P \;=\; V_{DC} \times I \;=\; 49.5 \times 25 \;\text{W} \;\approx\; 1.24 \;\text{kW}$$

> **Engineering takeaway:** Standard rectifiers for ICCP are typically rated **30–60 V / 20–100 A** with **automatic potential control** using a buried CSE reference.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flowchart for Choosing CP vs. AP

| Question | If YES → | If NO → |
|---|---|---|
| Does the metal show a clear passive region? | Consider **AP** first | Use **CP** |
| Is the electrolyte a strong concentrated acid? | **AP** is highly efficient | **CP** (in soil/water) |
| Is the structure geometrically complex with many crevices? | **CP (ICCP)** is more uniform | Either is fine |
| Is the project budget for ongoing power low? | **SACP** uses no power | **ICCP or AP** |
| Are there nearby stray-current sources (e.g., HVDC, rail)? | **ICCP with IR drop correction** | Standalone SACP or AP |
| Does the structure undergo high-velocity electrolyte flow? | **CP** (AP film may erode) | Both possible |
| Is the temperature > 250 °C? | **AP** (passive film is heat-stable) | CP current demand rises sharply |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cathodic Protection — Sacrificial Anode (Galvanic) System

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SACP_Circuit["SACRIFICIAL ANODE CP SYSTEM"]
        ZnAnode["SACRIFICIAL ANODE Zn / Mg / Al"]
        Backfill["BACKFILL Gypsum + Bentonite + Sodium Sulfate"]
        Cable1["INSULATED COPPER CABLE"]
        Pipe["STEEL PIPELINE CATHODIC"]
        Soil["ELECTROLYTE MOIST SOIL"]
        CSEref["CSE REFERENCE ELECTRODE MONITORING"]
    end

    ZnAnode ---|"IONIC PATH via SOIL"| Pipe
    Pipe ---|"ELECTRONS via CABLE"| ZnAnode
    Backfill --- ZnAnode
    CSEref --- Pipe
    ZnAnode -.->|"PREFERENTIALLY DISSOLVES"| ZnAnode
```

**Operating Mechanism (Read with diagram):**
- The Zn anode (E° = −1.10 V vs SHE) is **more active** than the steel pipe (E°_{Fe/Fe²⁺} = −0.44 V vs SHE).
- Electrons flow **internally** through the cable from Zn → pipe.
- Zn dissolves: Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻ (anodic half-cell).
- On the pipe surface, O₂ reduction occurs: O₂ + 2H₂O + 4e⁻ → 4OH⁻ (cathodic half-cell).
- The pipe is **protected** because no Fe²⁺ leaves its surface.

### 4.2 Cathodic Protection — Impressed Current System

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph ICCP_Circuit["IMPRESSED CURRENT CP SYSTEM"]
        Rect["DC RECTIFIER AC MAINS TO DC"]
        MMOAnode["IMPRESSED ANODE MMO / Ti / Graphite / Fe-Si"]
        Backfill2["CARBONACEOUS BACKFILL Coke Breeze"]
        Pipe2["STEEL STRUCTURE PROTECTED CATHODIC"]
        Soil2["ELECTROLYTE"]
        CSE2["CSE / Ag-AgCl REFERENCE"]
        Ctrl["POTENTIAL CONTROLLER AUTO MODE"]
    end

    Rect ---|"POSITIVE TERMINAL"| MMOAnode
    MMOAnode ---|"IONIC CURRENT through SOIL"| Soil2
    Soil2 --- Pipe2
    Pipe2 ---|"NEGATIVE TERMINAL CABLE"| Rect
    CSE2 --- Pipe2
    CSE2 ---|"FEEDBACK SIGNAL"| Ctrl
    Ctrl ---|"ADJUSTS DC OUTPUT"| Rect
```

**Operating Mechanism:**
- The rectifier drives DC current **from the MMO anode, through the soil, onto the pipeline, and back to the rectifier's negative terminal**.
- The pipe is forced to a potential **more negative than −0.85 V vs CSE**.
- The MMO/Ti anode is **inert** and does not dissolve (only the oxygen/chlorine evolution reaction occurs at its surface).
- The controller continuously monitors pipe potential via the CSE and modulates the DC output to maintain the setpoint.

### 4.3 Anodic Protection System

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph AP_Circuit["ANODIC PROTECTION SYSTEM"]
        Struct["PASSIVATABLE METAL STRUCTURE ANODE e.g., SS304 in H2SO4"]
        AuxCath["AUXILIARY CATHODE Pt / Hastelloy C / SS"]
        RE["REFERENCE ELECTRODE Ag-AgCl OR Pt"]
        Pot["POTENTIOSTAT REFERENCE COMPARATOR + CURRENT ADJUSTER"]
        Electrolyte["CONCENTRATED ELECTROLYTE H2SO4 > 70 percent"]
    end

    Struct ---|"CONNECTED TO POSITIVE OUTPUT"| Pot
    AuxCath ---|"CONNECTED TO NEGATIVE OUTPUT"| Pot
    RE --- Struct
    RE ---|"MEASURES E_STR"| Pot
    Pot ---|"INJECTS CONTROLLED I_AP"| Struct
    AuxCath ---|"CONTACT"| Electrolyte
    Struct ---|"CONTACT"| Electrolyte
    Pot -.->|"HOLD E_STR IN PASSIVE WINDOW E_p to E_T"| Struct
```

**Operating Mechanism:**
- The structure is connected to the **positive** terminal of the potentiostat (it is the **anode**).
- The reference electrode continuously reads E_struct.
- If E_struct falls below E_p, the potentiostat increases I_ap to drive it back into the passive region.
- If E_struct rises above E_T, the potentiostat reduces I_ap to prevent transpassive pitting or oxygen evolution.

### 4.4 Evans Diagram — Cathodic vs. Anodic Protection Shown Visually

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Evans["EVANS DIAGRAM for ACTIVE-PASSIVE METAL"]
        A1["ACTIVE PEAK i_cc Critical Current Density"]
        A2["PASSIVE PLATEAU i_p Very Low"]
        A3["TRANSPASSIVE OXYGEN EVOLUTION"]
        A4["ANODIC PROTECTION WINDOW Hold E_p LESS THAN E LESS THAN E_T"]
        A5["CATHODIC PROTECTION Push E below E_corr Anodic Reaction Suppressed"]
        Eaxis["POTENTIAL E increases right"]
        iaxis["LOG CURRENT DENSITY log i"]
    end

    A1 --- A2
    A2 --- A3
    A4 -.-> A2
    A5 -.-> A1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this Evans diagram:** Moving **right** means more positive (anodic) potential. CP pushes the operating point **to the left** (more negative), reducing the anodic current i_a to near zero. AP holds the operating point **on the flat passive plateau** (low i_p), which is far below the active peak i_cc.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Distinguish between **sacrificial anode** and **impressed current** cathodic protection systems. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key Pattern):**

| Feature | Sacrificial Anode CP (SACP) | Impressed Current CP (ICCP) |
|---|---|---|
| Power source | **None** — uses galvanic coupling | **External DC rectifier** required |
| Anode material | Active metals (Zn, Mg, Al) | Inert (Fe–Si, graphite, MMO/Ti) |
| Current capacity | Limited (~0.5–5 A per anode) | High (tens to hundreds of A) |
| Driving voltage | Galvanic potential difference (≤ 0.5 V) | Adjustable (0–60 V typical) |
| Typical use | Short pipelines, small vessels, marine buoys | Long pipelines, large tank farms |
| Maintenance | Periodic anode replacement | Rectifier + reference electrode checks |

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** State and explain the **criteria for cathodic protection** of a buried steel structure. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
1. **−850 mV criterion (primary, NACE SP0169):** The structure-to-electrolyte "instant-off" potential shall be **more negative than −0.850 V vs CSE**, with the IR drop removed.
2. **100 mV polarization criterion:** A minimum of **100 mV of cathodic polarization** between the structure and a stable reference electrode (i.e., the polarized potential must be at least 100 mV more negative than the native E_corr).
3. **Tafel slope / E-log i criterion:** The net anodic current density must fall below a tolerable value (typically < 1 μA/cm²), verified by the Tafel extrapolation of the polarization curve.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 3 Choice A]**
>
> **Question A (14 marks):**
> **(a)** Explain the **principle, components, and design** of an **impressed current cathodic protection (ICCP)** system with a neat schematic. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** A 4 km buried steel pipeline of 80 cm diameter is to be protected. Soil resistivity ρ = 30 Ω·m. Design current density i_cp = 25 mA/m². Compute: (i) the total protection current, (ii) the number of 20 kg Mg anodes (C_Mg = 1230 Ah/kg, U = 0.55) assuming each delivers 1.0 A, and (iii) the anode spacing. *(7 marks)*

### **Model Solution to A(a):**

**Principle (2 marks):**
Impressed Current Cathodic Protection uses an **external DC source** to drive current from an **inert (impressed) anode** through the **electrolyte** to the structure to be protected, which is made the **cathode**. By forcing the structure's potential below −0.85 V vs CSE, the anodic dissolution reaction is suppressed.

**Components (3 marks):**
1. **DC Rectifier (TR Unit)** — silicon or thyristor-controlled, converts AC mains to low-voltage DC (typically 0–60 V, 0–100 A), with adjustable output and **automatic potential control** based on reference-electrode feedback.
2. **Impressed Anodes** — inert, high-silicon cast iron, graphite, or **MMO/Ti** (mixed-metal-oxide coated titanium). They must resist aggressive anodic reactions (Cl⁻ → Cl₂, O₂ evolution).
3. **Backfill** — carbonaceous coke breeze (for buried anodes) to lower anode-to-soil resistance and extend the active surface.
4. **Cables** — stranded copper, XLPE-insulated, with HMWPE sheathing for direct burial; color-coded (red = positive/anode lead, black = negative/cathode lead).
5. **Reference Electrode** — buried **CSE** (Cu/CuSO₄) for permanent monitoring.
6. **Junction Boxes, Test Stations, Surge Protection, and Lightning Arrestors.**

**Schematic (2 marks):** Refer to the ICCP Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2 (reproduced here in plain text):

```
   [AC Mains] → [Rectifier +ve] → [Impressed Anode] → (Electrolyte) → [Pipe −ve] → [Rectifier −ve]
                                            ↑                                            ↑
                                       [CSE feedback] ←——— [Potential Controller] ←——┘
```

### **Model Solution to A(b):**

**(i) Total protection current:**

$$I_{cp} \;=\; i_{cp} \times (\pi D L) \;=\; 0.025 \times (\pi \times 0.80 \times 4000)$$

$$I_{cp} \;=\; 0.025 \times 10{,}053 \;\text{A} \;\approx\; 251.3 \;\text{A}$$

**(ii) Number of anodes:**

$$N \;=\; \frac{I_{cp}}{I_{anode}} \;=\; \frac{251.3}{1.0} \;\approx\; 252 \;\text{Mg anodes}$$

**(iii) Anode spacing:**

$$s \;=\; \frac{L}{N} \;=\; \frac{4000}{252} \;\approx\; 15.9 \;\text{m}$$

> **Cross-check (anode life, optional):**
> $T = \dfrac{m \times C \times U}{I \times 8760} = \dfrac{20 \times 1230 \times 0.55}{1.0 \times 8760} \approx 1.55 \;\text{years}$
> So anodes must be replaced approximately every 1.5 years.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 3 Choice B]**
>
> **Question B (14 marks):**
> **(a)** Explain the **principle, components, applications, and limitations of anodic protection (AP)**. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** Compare **cathodic protection and anodic protection** in a tabulated form, citing at least **eight** distinguishing parameters. *(7 marks)*

### **Model Solution to B(a):**

**Principle (2 marks):**
Anodic protection is applicable **only to metals that exhibit active-passive behavior** (e.g., stainless steel, Ni alloys, Ti, carbon steel in concentrated H₂SO₄). The protected structure is the **anode** of a controlled auxiliary cell. By impressing a small anodic current, the structure's potential is driven and maintained **inside the passive region** of its anodic polarization curve, where a thin protective oxide film (Cr₂O₃, TiO₂, etc.) is stable. A **potentiostat** is mandatory — it compares the structure's potential with a reference electrode and modulates the current to hold the setpoint.

**Components (2 marks):**
1. **Auxiliary cathode** (Pt, Hastelloy C, or 18Cr–8Ni SS) — completes the circuit, must resist the cathodic environment.
2. **Reference electrode** (Ag/AgCl, Hg/HgSO₄, or Pt) — feeds back the structure's potential to the controller.
3. **Potentiostat / electronic controller** — continuously adjusts current; trips on signal loss.
4. **Cabling and instrumentation** — including surge protection, alarms, and remote monitoring.

**Applications (2 marks):**
- **Sulfuric acid** storage tanks (carbon steel, ≥ 70% H₂SO₄).
- **Alkylation units** and **sulfuric acid coolers** in petroleum refineries.
- **Nitric acid** storage (stainless steel tanks).
- **Fertilizer** solutions (NH₄NO₃, urea-ammonium nitrate).
- **Stainless steel pickling baths** and **pharmaceutical reactors**.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Applicable **only to passivating metals**.
- Requires **precise potential control**; any deviation causes either active corrosion (if E < E_p) or pitting/transpassive attack (if E > E_T).
- **Not suitable for low-conductivity electrolytes** (insufficient ionic path).
- **Cannot protect complex geometries** with shielding/crevices as uniformly as CP.
- **Power-failure consequences** are more severe than in CP (film dissolves and may not re-form easily).

### **Model Solution to B(b) — Comparison Table:**

| # | Parameter | Cathodic Protection | Anodic Protection |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Role of protected structure** | Cathode | Anode |
| 2 | **Applicable metals** | All metals | Only active-passive metals (SS, Ni, Ti) |
| 3 | **External power** | Optional (ICCP needs it; SACP does not) | Always required (potentiostat) |
| 4 | **Reference electrode** | For monitoring only | Essential for control loop |
| 5 | **Cost of operation** | Moderate (anode replacement or kW-scale power) | Low power, high instrumentation cost |
| 6 | **Failure mode** | Localized pitting | Catastrophic transpassive/active attack |
| 7 | **Common environments** | Soil, seawater, freshwater | Concentrated H₂SO₄, HNO₃, fertilizers |
| 8 | **Current magnitude** | High (10–500 mA/m²) | Low passive i_p (μA/m² range) |
| 9 | **Efficiency** | High for long, distributed structures | Best for compact, acidic process vessels |
| 10 | **Design complexity** | Moderate (anode layout) | High (potential window control) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Mixing up current direction:** A frequent error is stating that current flows *from* the structure *to* the anode in CP. The truth is the opposite — in CP, conventional current flows **from** the anode **through the electrolyte to** the structure; electrons flow **from** the anode **to** the structure through the wire.
> 2. **Confusing the role of the protected structure in CP vs. AP:** In CP, the protected structure is the **cathode**; in AP, it is the **anode**. Examiners award full marks only if this distinction is explicit.
> 3. **Forgetting units in Faraday's law calculations:** $I \times t$ must be in **coulombs**, $M$ in **kg·mol⁻¹** (or g·mol⁻¹ with consistent F), and the answer in **kg** (or g). Showing units earns a mark.
> 4. **Missing the −0.85 V vs CSE criterion:** KTU examiners expect students to know the **NACE −850 mV criterion**; omitting the reference electrode (CSE) loses 0.5 mark.
> 5. **Failing to mention the utilization factor U** in sacrificial anode design — many students write $T = mC/I$ without the U term, which makes the design non-conservative in real engineering.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (High-Yield KTU Points):**

- ✅ **Cathodic Protection (CP)** = forces the structure to be the **cathode** → suppresses anodic dissolution.
- ✅ **Anodic Protection (AP)** = forces the structure to be the **anode** → stabilizes passive film.
- ✅ Two CP variants: **Sacrificial Anode (galvanic)** — uses Zn/Mg/Al; **Impressed Current (ICCP)** — uses inert anode + DC source.
- ✅ AP is **only for active-passive metals** (SS, Ni, Ti, C-steel in conc. H₂SO₄).
- ✅ NACE **−850 mV vs CSE** is the primary CP protection criterion for steel in soil.
- ✅ **100 mV cathodic polarization** is the alternative criterion.
- ✅ Faraday's Law: $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ (verify units!).
- ✅ **Electrochemical capacity** of Zn ≈ **820 Ah/kg** (theoretical), Mg ≈ **1230 Ah/kg**, Al ≈ **2980 Ah/kg** (all theoretical, derated by U).
- ✅ **Utilization factor U**: Zn ≈ 0.85, Mg ≈ 0.50, Al ≈ 0.85–0.95.
- ✅ Anode life: $T = \dfrac{m_{anode} \cdot C \cdot U}{I \cdot 8760}$ **years**.
- ✅ **Potentiostat** is mandatory for AP — open-loop failure → catastrophic corrosion.
- ✅ **CSE** = +0.318 V vs SHE; **Ag/AgCl (seawater)** = +0.222 V vs SHE; **SCE (lab)** = +0.241 V vs SHE.
- ✅ Dwight's formula for vertical anode-to-soil resistance: $R_v = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln(4L/r) - 1\right]$.
- ✅ Current direction in CP: **electrons from anode → cathode (via wire)**; **conventional current from anode → cathode (via electrolyte)** — they are opposite in sign.
- ✅ AP applied to **sulfuric acid coolers, alkylation reactors, nitric acid tanks, ammonium nitrate storage**.
- ✅ CP applied to **pipelines, ship hulls, offshore jackets, tank bottoms, rebar in concrete**.
- ✅ Failure of AP = **transpassive pitting or active dissolution** (high i_corr resumes).
- ✅ Failure of CP = **under-protection** (still anodic, pitting) or **over-protection** (alkaline attack, H₂ embrittlement on high-strength steel).
- ✅ **Over-protection hazard:** Cathodic potentials more negative than −1.2 V vs CSE may cause **hydrogen embrittlement** in high-strength steels and **cathodic blistering** of coatings.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coatings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT527 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Coatings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Coatings

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **coating** is a thin layer of material (metallic, inorganic, or organic) deliberately applied onto the surface of a substrate (typically a metal) to act as a **physical barrier**, **sacrificial anode**, or **electrochemical inhibitor** against the ingress of corrosive species from the environment. According to KTU PECHT527 (Corrosion Engineering) Module 3, coatings represent the **most industrially deployed corrosion-mitigation strategy**, accounting for an estimated **> 80% of all corrosion-protection expenditure globally**.

Coatings are broadly classified into three families:

| Family | Examples | Primary Protection Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **Metallic Coatings** | Zn, Al, Sn, Cr, Ni, Cd | Barrier + Galvanic (Sacrificial) or Noble Barrier |
| **Inorganic Coatings** | Phosphate, Chromate, Anodized Al₂O₃, Enamels, Ceramics | Conversion film (barrier) |
| **Organic Coatings** | Paints, Varnishes, Lacquers, Polymers, Epoxies | Barrier + Inhibitive Pigment Action |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 explicitly covers *Types of metallic coatings*, *Methods of application*, and *Paint coatings (constituents and mechanisms)*. Students must master both the **electrochemical theory** and the **industrial process engineering** of each technique.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a coating as the **"raincoat"** of a metal:

- If you wrap a vulnerable metal in a coat that **dissolves preferentially** (e.g., zinc on steel), the coat takes the beating and the steel survives — this is **sacrificial (galvanic) protection**, exactly like a bodyguard absorbing punches.
- If the coat is **chemically inert and just blocks water/oxygen** (e.g., a tin can or an epoxy paint), the metal underneath is simply **physically isolated** from the corrosive environment.
- If the coat is **porous**, water creeps through the holes and rusts the metal from underneath — this is why **porosity, adhesion, and film thickness** are the three holy grails of coating inspection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy (galvanizing):** A galvanized iron fence rusts the zinc layer first over decades, while the steel core remains intact. The moment the zinc is consumed, however, the steel rusts rapidly. This is a direct application of the **electrochemical series** (E°_Zn < E°_Fe).

## 1.3 Key Metrics and Standards

- **Coating thickness:** measured in **microns (µm)** or **mils** (1 mil = 25.4 µm). Typical industrial standards require **25–250 µm** for heavy-duty corrosion protection.
- **Adhesion strength:** measured in **MPa** via pull-off tests (ASTM D4541).
- **Porosity:** measured as **% area of pores** or via **porosity test (ASTM B765)**.
- **Standard reference electrode (SRE) potential difference (ΔE°):** must be **> 0.15 V** for reliable galvanic protection in neutral environments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always remember: a coating is **only as good as its weakest defect**. Even a single pinhole can become the initiation site for severe localized (crevice or pitting) corrosion.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Metallic Coatings — Sub-Classification

### 2.1.1 Sacrificial (Anodic) Coatings
- **Definition:** Coating metal is **more electrochemically active** (more negative E°) than the base metal.
- **Examples:** **Zn** (galvanizing), **Al** (aluminizing), **Cd** on steel.
- **Operating principle:** When the coating is scratched, it dissolves preferentially, cathodically protecting the exposed base metal.
- **Design rule:** Galvanic anode efficiency is governed by the **anode-to-cathode area ratio**. A small scratch (cathode) on a large coated panel (anode) → very safe geometry.

### 2.1.2 Noble (Cathodic) Coatings
- **Definition:** Coating metal is **less active** (more positive E°) than the base metal.
- **Examples:** **Sn** (tinplate on steel), **Cr**, **Ni**, **Ag**, **Au** on steel.
- **Operating principle:** Coating is purely a **physical barrier**. If breached, the underlying steel becomes a small anode against a large cathodic coating → **catastrophic pitting**.
- **Critical consequence:** Any porosity or scratch in a noble coating accelerates corrosion of the base metal (this is why **tinplate food cans** must be lacquered internally).

## 2.2 Methods of Applying Metallic Coatings

### A. Hot-Dip Process (Galvanizing)
1. **Surface preparation:** degreasing → acid pickling (H₂SO₄ or HCl) → fluxing (ZnCl₂ + NH₄Cl).
2. **Immersion** of steel in molten metal bath (Zn at **~450 °C**, Al at **~700 °C**).
3. **Withdrawal** and cooling → formation of intermetallic alloy layers (e.g., **Fe-Zn phases**: Γ, δ, ζ, η).
4. **Layer structure (galvanized steel):** η (pure Zn) / ζ (FeZn₁₃) / δ (FeZn₇) / Γ (Fe₃Zn₁₀) / Steel substrate.

### B. Electroplating (Electrodeposition)
- Substrate is made the **cathode** in an electrolytic cell; coating metal ions (e.g., Zn²⁺, Ni²⁺, Cr³⁺) are reduced from solution.
- **Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis** govern the mass deposited.
- Advantages: precise thickness control, uniform deposits, room-temperature operation.

### C. Electroless Plating
- **No external current**; deposition occurs via **autocatalytic chemical reduction** (e.g., Ni²⁺ reduced by NaH₂PO₂ in presence of Pd nuclei).
- **Advantage:** uniform thickness on complex geometries (no "dog-boning" effect).
- **Disadvantage:** slow (typically 10–25 µm/hr).

### D. Metal Spraying (Thermal Spray)
- Molten or semi-molten metal particles are projected onto a grit-blasted surface via a flame, arc, or plasma jet.
- **Processes:** Flame spray (Zn, Al), Arc spray (Zn-Al), Plasma spray (ceramics, MCrAlY).
- Coating is **mechanically bonded** (not metallurgically) with inherent **porosity 5–15%** → sealants are mandatory.

### E. Diffusion Coatings (Sherardizing, Chromizing, Calorizing)
- Base metal is packed in coating-metal powder and heated (**< melting point** of either) → atoms diffuse into the substrate forming a **solid solution or intermetallic**.
- Example: Sherardizing = Zn powder + steel + 370 °C → Zn-Fe alloy layer.

### F. Cladding
- Two metals are **roll-bonded** (hot/cold rolling with surface cleaning) under high pressure.
- Example: Al-clad steel, stainless-clad mild steel.

## 2.3 Organic Coatings (Paints) — Constituents

A paint is a **heterogeneous, multi-phase system** consisting of:

| Constituent | Function | Typical Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Pigment** | Color, opacity, corrosion inhibition | Fe₂O₃ (red), Cr₂O₃ (green), Zn-chromate, **Zn-rich primers** |
| **Binder (Vehicle/Resin)** | Film formation, adhesion, mechanical strength | Alkyd, Epoxy, Polyurethane, Vinyl, Acrylic, Phenolic |
| **Solvent/Thinner** | Viscosity control for application | Mineral spirits, Toluene, Xylene, Water |
| **Additives** | Specific performance properties | Driers, Anti-settling agents, UV stabilizers, Plasticizers |
| **Extender/Filler** | Cost reduction, rheology | Talc, CaCO₃, Mica |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pigment Volume Concentration (PVC)** is the single most important formulation parameter. When **PVC > CPVC** (Critical Pigment Volume Concentration), the binder is insufficient to fill inter-pigment voids → film becomes **porous, permeable, and electrically conductive** → protection fails.

## 2.4 Mechanisms of Paint Protection

1. **Barrier Effect:** Resin film physically blocks H₂O, O₂, and ionic species.
2. **Electrochemical Inhibition:** Active pigments (Zn chromate, red lead Pb₃O₄) act as anodic/cathodic inhibitors.
3. **Sacrificial Action:** **Zinc-rich primers** (> 95% Zn dust in dry film) provide cathodic protection to steel.
4. **Chemical Passivation:** Phosphate and chromate pigments form protective conversion films on the substrate.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mass deposited (Faraday) | $m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$ | m in g, I in A, t in s, F = **96,485 C mol⁻¹** |
| 2 | Coating thickness from mass | $t = \dfrac{m}{\rho \cdot A}$ | t in cm, ρ in g cm⁻³, A in cm² |
| 3 | Current efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | Dimensionless % |
| 4 | Mean coating thickness (Sauter) | $t_{mean} = \dfrac{W_{coating}}{\rho_{coating} \cdot A_{substrate}}$ | W in g, ρ in g cm⁻³ |
| 5 | Pigment Volume Concentration | $PVC = \dfrac{V_{pigment}}{V_{pigment} + V_{binder}} \times 100$ | Dimensionless % |
| 6 | Cathodic protection current | $I_{req} = i_{corr} \cdot A_{bare}$ | A; i_corr in A m⁻² |
| 7 | Galvanic series potential difference | $\Delta E = E_{cathode} - E_{anode}$ | Must be > **0.15 V** for effective protection |
| 8 | Coating porosity (Ferroxyl test) | $\%P = \dfrac{N_{blue\,spots}}{A_{sample}} \times 100$ | Spots per unit area |
| 9 | Pull-off adhesion strength | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{\pi r^{2}}$ | F in N, r in m, σ in **MPa** |
| 10 | Thermal spray deposition rate | $R = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho \cdot A}$ | m s⁻¹ |
| 11 | Pilling-Bedworth Ratio | $PBR = \dfrac{V_{oxide}}{V_{metal}}$ | PBR between **1 and 2** = protective oxide |
| 12 | Corrosion rate from coating loss | $CR = \dfrac{87.6 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$ | CR in mm yr⁻¹, W in mg, D in g cm⁻³, T in hr |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Pilling-Bedworth Ratio (PBR)** is a key KTU concept: if PBR < 1, the oxide is non-protective (e.g., MgO, PBR ≈ 0.8); if PBR > 2, the oxide spalls (e.g., Fe₂O₃, PBR ≈ 2.15). The "Goldilocks zone" is **1 < PBR < 2** — examples: Al₂O₃ (1.28), Cr₂O₃ (1.99), TiO₂ (1.78).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive industry:** Electro-galvanized steel bodies + electrophoretic (e-coat) primer + base coat + clear coat.
- **Offshore structures:** Thermal-sprayed Al (TSA) + epoxy sealant + polyurethane topcoat.
- **Food packaging:** Electrolytic tinplate (ETP) on steel, internally lacquered to avoid Sn-Fe galvanic couple upon scratching.
- **Aerospace:** Cd-plating on high-strength steel (now phased out) → replaced by IVD-Al (Ion Vapour Deposition).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mass of Coating Deposited (Faraday's First Law)

**Statement:** The mass *m* of a substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of charge *Q = I·t* passed through the electrolyte.

**Step 1 — Define molar relationships.**
For a metal ion M^(n+) being reduced:
$$\text{M}^{n+} + n e^{-} \longrightarrow \text{M}$$

One mole of metal atoms (atomic mass **M** grams) requires **n** moles of electrons.

**Step 2 — Express charge per mole of metal.**
Charge required for 1 mole of metal = n × F coulombs, where F = **96,485 C mol⁻¹** (Faraday's constant).

**Step 3 — Relate mass to charge passed.**
For charge Q = I·t coulombs, the mass deposited is:

$$\begin{aligned}
m &= \text{(charge passed)} \times \dfrac{\text{atomic mass}}{\text{charge per mole}} \\
m &= I \cdot t \times \dfrac{M}{n \cdot F} \\
\therefore \quad m &= \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **fundamental Faraday equation** used in all electroplating calculations.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mean Coating Thickness from Mass Deposited

**Step 1 — Assume uniform thickness *t* and area *A*.**
Volume of coating = $V = A \cdot t$.

**Step 2 — Express volume via mass and density.**
$V = \dfrac{m}{\rho}$, where ρ is the density of the coating metal (e.g., Zn: 7.14 g cm⁻³; Ni: 8.90 g cm⁻³).

**Step 3 — Equate and solve for t.**
$$\begin{aligned}
A \cdot t &= \dfrac{m}{\rho} \\
\therefore \quad t &= \dfrac{m}{\rho \cdot A}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Combine with Faraday's law.**
$$\begin{aligned}
t &= \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t_{time}}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A} \\
t &= \dfrac{M \cdot i}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}
\end{aligned}$$

where $i = \dfrac{I}{A}$ is the **current density** (A m⁻²) and *t_time* is the plating time. This shows thickness is **linearly proportional to current density and time**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Pilling-Bedworth Ratio (PBR)

**Step 1 — Definition.**
PBR is the ratio of the molar volume of the oxide to the molar volume of the parent metal from which the oxide formed.

$$PBR = \dfrac{V_{oxide}}{V_{metal}} = \dfrac{\dfrac{M_{oxide}}{n_{oxide} \cdot \rho_{oxide}}}{\dfrac{M_{metal}}{n_{metal} \cdot \rho_{metal}}}$$

**Step 2 — Worked numerical: Aluminium → Al₂O₃.**
- Molar mass Al₂O₃ = 102 g mol⁻¹, density = 3.99 g cm⁻³ → V_oxide per O = 102/(3 × 3.99) = **8.52 cm³ mol⁻¹ O**
- Molar mass Al = 27 g mol⁻¹, density = 2.70 g cm⁻³ → V_metal per O = 2 × 27/(3 × 2.70) = **6.67 cm³ mol⁻¹ O**

**Step 3 — Compute.**
$$PBR_{Al} = \dfrac{8.52}{6.67} = 1.28$$

This is in the **protective range (1–2)** → explains why Al forms a self-healing, adherent oxide → excellent corrosion resistance.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Current Efficiency in Electroplating

**Step 1 — Real deposits vs. theoretical deposits.**
In practice, side reactions (H₂ evolution at cathode, O₂ at anode) consume charge. Define:
$$\eta_{cathode} = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 2 — Worked example.**
A steel panel of area **0.01 m²** is Zn-plated at **2 A** for **30 min** in acidic sulphate bath. Density Zn = 7.14 × 10³ kg m⁻³, M = 65.4 g mol⁻¹, n = 2.

Theoretical mass (Faraday):
$$\begin{aligned}
m_{th} &= \dfrac{65.4 \times 2 \times 30 \times 60}{2 \times 96{,}485} \\
&= \dfrac{235{,}440}{192{,}970} \\
&= 1.220 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}$$

Theoretical thickness:
$$\begin{aligned}
t_{th} &= \dfrac{1.220 \times 10^{-3}}{7140 \times 0.01} \\
&= 1.71 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 17.1 \text{ µm}
\end{aligned}$$

If actual measured mass is **1.10 g**, then:
$$\eta = \dfrac{1.10}{1.22} \times 100 = 90.2\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU numerical problems almost always require a **multi-step calculation** combining Faraday's law + density + area. Always show units explicitly to gain full marks.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Coating Selection Decision Tree

| Engineering Requirement | Recommended Coating | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Atmospheric corrosion (rural) | Galvanized Zn (85 µm) | ΔE° = 0.76 V; sacrificial; cheap |
| Marine immersion | Zn-rich primer + epoxy + PU | Barrier + cathodic backup |
| High-temperature (> 500 °C) | Diffusion aluminide | Forms stable Al₂O₃ scale |
| Food contact (acidic) | Tinplate + lacquer | Inert + barrier |
| Decorative + wear resistance | Electroplated Cr (decorative) over Ni | Hard, bright, noble barrier |
| Substrate = Mg alloy | Chromate conversion coating | MgO is non-protective (PBR < 1) |
| Complex internal surfaces (tubes) | Electroless Ni-P | Uniformity independent of geometry |
| Repair of galvanized structures | Zn-rich paint (cold galvanizing) | Field-applicable, no molten bath |
| Stainless steel (304) | Passivation (HNO₃) | Restore Cr-depleted layer |
| Aerospace fasteners | IVD-Al or Cd (legacy) | Galvanic compatibility with Al alloys |

> [!NOTE]
> A common KTU viva question: *"Why is cadmium plating banned in aerospace today?"* Answer: Cd is highly toxic (carcinogenic, ITAR-restricted) and produces toxic fumes in fires. The replacement is **IVD-Al (Ion Vapour Deposition of Aluminium)**, which provides equivalent galvanic compatibility with Al-alloy structures.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] 
**"Distinguish between anodic and cathodic protective coatings with one example each."**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Anodic (Sacrificial) | Cathodic (Noble) |
|---|---|---|
| Coating metal potential | More negative E° | More positive E° |
| Example | Zn on steel (galvanizing) | Sn on steel (tinplate) |
| Function when scratched | Dissolves to protect Fe | Base Fe corrodes preferentially |
| Failure mode | Coating dissolves uniformly | Severe localized pitting of base |
| E° difference | ΔE° = E°Fe − E°Zn ≈ **0.76 V** | ΔE° = E°Sn − E°Fe ≈ **0.30 V** |

> Mnemonic: **"Anode Attacks, Cathode Cowers"** — the anode is always the one that gets eaten.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] 
**"What is CPVC? Why is it significant in paint formulation?"**

**Model Answer:**
- **CPVC (Critical Pigment Volume Concentration)** is the pigment volume concentration at which the binder (resin) **just fills the inter-pigment voids** in the dry film.
- Below CPVC → continuous binder matrix → intact barrier film.
- Above CPVC → interconnected voids → film becomes **porous, permeable, electrically conductive** → corrosion protection fails catastrophically.
- A common KTU answer tip: cite the numerical CPVC of **Zn-rich primers ≈ 65%** and **conventional primers ≈ 30–40%**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option Set 1

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** 
**(a)** Explain in detail the **hot-dip galvanizing process** for protecting steel, including surface preparation, bath chemistry, and the resulting metallurgical layer structure. (7 marks)

**(b)** A steel sheet of area **200 cm²** is to be zinc-coated by electroplating to a thickness of **25 µm**. Given: density of Zn = 7.14 g cm⁻³, atomic mass = 65.4 g mol⁻¹, n = 2, F = 96,485 C mol⁻¹. Calculate the **time required** if plating is carried out at a current of **3 A** with a cathode current efficiency of **90%**. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **Definition:** Hot-dip galvanizing is the process of immersing cleaned steel into a bath of molten zinc (≈ 450 °C) to form a metallurgically bonded Zn-Fe alloy coating.
2. **Surface preparation sequence (3 steps):**
   - **Degreasing** in hot alkaline solution (NaOH, ~80 °C) to remove oils/grease.
   - **Pickling** in dilute H₂SO₄ (10–20%) or HCl to remove mill scale and rust.
   - **Fluxing** in ZnCl₂ + NH₄Cl solution at ~60 °C to prevent re-oxidation before immersion.
3. **Bath chemistry:** Molten Zn at **445–455 °C**; optional additions of **0.03–0.1% Al** to suppress brittle Fe-Zn intermetallic growth (suppresses ζ and δ layers).
4. **Layer structure (top → bottom):**
   - **η (eta):** pure Zn, soft, ductile, sacrificial.
   - **ζ (zeta):** FeZn₁₃, brittle.
   - **δ (delta):** FeZn₇, hard.
   - **Γ (gamma):** Fe₃Zn₁₀, very hard, thin, bonded to steel.
5. **Applications:** Transmission towers, guard rails, structural fasteners, automotive chassis.
6. **Advantages vs. limitations:** Long life (50+ years rural), self-healing at cut edges; not suitable for continuous high-temp service > 200 °C (Zn melts at 420 °C).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Mass of Zn required for 25 µm thickness on 200 cm²:**
$$\begin{aligned}
V &= A \cdot t = 200 \text{ cm}^2 \times 25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm} = 0.5 \text{ cm}^3 \\
m &= V \cdot \rho = 0.5 \times 7.14 = 3.57 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Faraday's law with efficiency correction:**
$$\begin{aligned}
m &= \eta \cdot \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t_{time}}{n \cdot F} \\
3.57 &= 0.90 \times \dfrac{65.4 \times 3 \times t_{time}}{2 \times 96{,}485} \\
3.57 &= 0.90 \times \dfrac{196.2 \cdot t_{time}}{192{,}970} \\
3.57 &= 9.151 \times 10^{-4} \cdot t_{time} \\
\therefore \quad t_{time} &= \dfrac{3.57}{9.151 \times 10^{-4}} = 3901 \text{ s} \\
t_{time} &\approx 65.0 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** Time required ≈ **65 minutes (1 hr 5 min)**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option Set 2 (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** 
**(a)** Discuss the **constituents of a paint system** and explain the four principal **mechanisms by which paints protect** metallic substrates. (7 marks)

**(b)** A galvanized steel pipe (85 µm Zn, ρ = 7.14 g cm⁻³) is exposed to a neutral aerated NaCl environment. The corrosion current density measured on the Zn coating is **i_corr = 15 µA cm⁻²**. Estimate the **time to first perforation of the steel substrate**, assuming Zn is consumed uniformly. (Atomic mass Zn = 65.4 g mol⁻¹, n = 2, F = 96,485 C mol⁻¹). (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **Paint Constituents (4 components):**
   - **Pigment:** Provides colour, opacity, UV shielding, and active corrosion inhibition. Examples: Red oxide (Fe₂O₃), Zn chromate, metallic Zn dust, TiO₂.
   - **Binder (Resin/Vehicle):** The polymer matrix that forms the continuous film. Examples: Alkyd, Epoxy, Polyurethane, Vinyl, Phenolic, Acrylic.
   - **Solvent/Thinner:** Adjusts viscosity for application. Volatile, evaporates during curing. Examples: Xylene, toluene, mineral spirits, water.
   - **Additives:** Specialty chemicals for rheology, drying, anti-settling, anti-foaming, biocides.

2. **Four Protection Mechanisms:**
   - **(i) Barrier Effect:** Resin film physically impedes H₂O, O₂, Cl⁻ transport to substrate. Most common mechanism. Effectiveness depends on **PVC/CPVC ratio**, **film thickness**, and **resin permeability**.
   - **(ii) Inhibitive (Chemical) Action:** Active pigments (e.g., **Zn chromate, red lead, phosphate**) release inhibitive anions that passivate the substrate → form conversion film.
   - **(iii) Sacrificial (Galvanic) Action:** **Zinc-rich primers** (Zn dust > 95% in dry film) provide cathodic protection to underlying steel. Coating conductivity is essential.
   - **(iv) Adhesion + Surface Passivation:** Strong adhesion + chemical bonding to substrate prevents underfilm migration.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Mass of Zn per unit area:**
$$\begin{aligned}
m_{Zn} &= \rho \cdot t = 7.14 \text{ g cm}^{-3} \times 85 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm} \\
m_{Zn} &= 0.0607 \text{ g cm}^{-2} = 60.7 \text{ mg cm}^{-2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Total charge required to consume this mass:**
$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dfrac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{M} = \dfrac{0.0607 \times 2 \times 96{,}485}{65.4} \\
Q &= \dfrac{11{,}713}{65.4} = 179.1 \text{ C cm}^{-2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Time to consumption at i_corr = 15 µA cm⁻²:**
$$\begin{aligned}
i_{corr} &= 15 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A cm}^{-2} \\
t &= \dfrac{Q}{i_{corr}} = \dfrac{179.1}{15 \times 10^{-6}} \\
t &= 1.194 \times 10^{7} \text{ s} \\
t &\approx 138 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** Approximate service life ≈ **138 days** before the zinc is fully consumed and steel perforation begins (assuming uniform corrosion and the steel substrate is then exposed).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students frequently forget to convert **µA to A** and **days to seconds**, losing 2 marks per unit error. Always use **SI base units consistently** (A, s, g). Also, do NOT confuse coating thickness units: 1 µm = 10⁻⁴ cm = 10⁻⁶ m.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] Coatings are classified as **Metallic, Inorganic, Organic, Conversion** — know one industrial process for each.
- [ ] **Galvanizing** is a *sacrificial (anodic)* coating; **Tinplate** is a *noble (cathodic)* coating — opposites in behavior at scratches.
- [ ] **ΔE°** between coating and substrate must be **> 0.15 V** for reliable galvanic protection.
- [ ] **Faraday's Law:** $m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$ — this is the **#1 formula** for KTU electroplating problems.
- [ ] **Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR):** protective only when **1 < PBR < 2**.
- [ ] **Hot-dip Zn layer phases** in order (top → bottom): η → ζ → δ → Γ → Steel.
- [ ] **Paint = Pigment + Binder + Solvent + Additives.** Mnemonic: **"Please Bring Snacks Always"**.
- [ ] **CPVC** is the breakpoint between protective and non-protective paint films.
- [ ] **Zinc-rich primers** (> 95% Zn dust) provide **cathodic (sacrificial)** protection to steel.
- [ ] **Porosity, adhesion, and thickness** are the three critical quality-control parameters.
- [ ] **Cathode efficiency** $\eta = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100\%$ — always needed for real plating time.
- [ ] **Aluminizing** is preferred over galvanizing for high-temperature service (> 420 °C).
- [ ] **Electroless plating** gives uniform thickness even on complex geometries (no edge build-up).
- [ ] **PBR of Al₂O₃ ≈ 1.28** (protective) — explains aluminium's atmospheric corrosion resistance.
- [ ] **Cd-plating** is restricted in aerospace due to toxicity → replaced by **IVD-Al**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Exam Tip (2024 Scheme):** When asked to "explain a coating process," always include **(i) surface preparation, (ii) process parameters (T, t, current), (iii) layer/microstructure formed, (iv) advantages and limitations, and (v) one industrial application.** Structured 5-point answers consistently score **> 90% marks** under the KTU marking scheme.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
