# MODULE 4: CORROSION AND ITS CONTROL IN CONCRETE STRUCTURES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (PECHT527 — Module 4):**
> *Corrosion in concrete structures* refers to the electrochemical degradation of embedded steel reinforcement (rebar) and the cementitious matrix of concrete caused primarily by the ingress of aggressive species — chiefly carbon dioxide (CO₂) and chloride ions (Cl⁻) — into the porous concrete cover. The deterioration follows an electrochemical cell mechanism with anodic metal dissolution, cathodic oxygen reduction, and ionic conduction through the pore solution.

### 1.1 The Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English View)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Rusting Skeleton of a Building":**
> Think of reinforced concrete as a **human skeleton wrapped in skin**. The *rebar* is the rigid bone that gives strength, while the *concrete cover* is the protective skin that shields it from the environment. Concrete is naturally **alkaline (pH ≈ 12.5–13.5)** because of calcium hydroxide, Ca(OH)₂, in the cement paste — this alkalinity is like an "invisible protective ointment" that passivates the steel and prevents rusting. However, when **CO₂ (carbonation)** or **salt water (chlorides)** slowly penetrate the skin, they neutralize this ointment. Once the protection is gone, the steel begins to rust. Rust occupies **2 to 6 times** the volume of the original steel, which is like a tumor swelling inside the bone — it cracks the surrounding skin (concrete), causing **spalling** and structural failure.

### 1.2 The Two-Way Deterioration Pathway

Corrosion in concrete is a **dual attack** — it targets *both* the steel and the concrete:

| Target | Primary Attacker | Visible Symptom |
|---|---|---|
| **Steel Rebar** | Carbonation, Cl⁻ ingress | Rust staining, section loss |
| **Concrete Matrix** | Sulfate attack, ASR, leaching | Cracking, spalling, efflorescence |

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Engineering Constants used in concrete corrosion analysis (KTU syllabus standard):**
> - **Standard reference electrode potential of Fe/Fe²⁺ : E° = −0.440 V vs SHE**
> - **Oxygen reduction potential in alkaline concrete: E° = +0.401 V vs SHE**
> - **pH of fresh concrete pore solution ≈ 12.5 – 13.5** (saturated Ca(OH)₂)
> - **Critical chloride threshold for rebar depassivation: 0.4 % – 1.0 % by mass of cement** (ACI 222R standard)
> - **Carbonation depth relation: x = k·√t** (mm), where k = carbonation coefficient
> - **Density of steel: ρ = 7.85 g/cm³**
> - **Molar volume expansion from Fe → Fe(OH)₃ → Fe₂O₃ : roughly 2× to 6×**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Mechanism of Rebar Corrosion in Concrete (Step-by-Step)

Reinforced concrete corrosion is fundamentally an **electrochemical phenomenon** that proceeds in three simultaneous half-reactions across a thin electrolyte film on the steel surface:

**Step 1 — Passivity of Steel in Fresh Concrete**
- The highly alkaline pore solution (saturated Ca(OH)₂ + KOH + NaOH) forms a thin, invisible, **ferric oxide passive film** (γ-Fe₂O₃) on the rebar.
- This film is **stable between pH 9.5 and 13.5** and reduces the corrosion current density to **≤ 0.1 μA/cm²** (negligible).

**Step 2 — Depassivation by Two Aggressive Agents**
- (a) **Carbonation:** CO₂ diffuses inward, reacts with Ca(OH)₂ to form CaCO₃, drops pH below ~9, and destroys the passive film.
- (b) **Chloride Ingress:** Cl⁻ ions penetrate and locally break down the passive film, even at high pH, by forming soluble Fe-Cl complexes.

**Step 3 — Active Electrochemical Cell Formation**
- Anode (rebar):  Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻
- Cathode (adjacent rebar):  O₂ + 2H₂O + 4e⁻ → 4OH⁻
- Electrolyte: pore solution in concrete
- External path: electrons flow through steel

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the concrete cover thickness so important?**
> The **clear cover** (typically 25–75 mm in KTU-relevant IS 456:2000 design) is the *primary* defense. It controls both the rate of CO₂/Cl⁻ penetration (Fick's second law) and provides the electrical resistance of the electrolyte path. Doubling the cover roughly *quadruples* the time to depassivation.

### 2.2 Carbonation — Detailed Chemistry

$$\text{CO}_2 + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CaCO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

The depth of carbonation at any time $t$ follows the **square-root-time law**:

$$x_c = k_c \sqrt{t}$$

where:
- $x_c$ = depth of carbonation (mm)
- $k_c$ = carbonation coefficient (mm/year^0.5) — depends on w/c ratio, cement type, exposure
- $t$ = exposure time (years)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** $k_c$ values typically range from **1 mm/√yr (excellent quality concrete)** to **>10 mm/√yr (poor, porous concrete)**. KTU examiners commonly quote $k_c$ values and ask for service-life prediction.

### 2.3 Chloride-Induced Corrosion — Fick's Second Law

Chloride diffusion through saturated concrete is modelled by **Fick's Second Law of Diffusion**:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D_c \frac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}}$$

For a semi-infinite medium with constant surface concentration $C_s$ and initial concentration $C_0$, the closed-form solution is:

$$C(x,t) = C_0 + (C_s - C_0)\left[1 - \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{D_c\, t}}\right)\right]$$

where:
- $C(x,t)$ = chloride concentration at depth $x$ after time $t$ (wt % of cement)
- $C_s$ = surface chloride concentration
- $C_0$ = initial chloride content (typically 0)
- $D_c$ = chloride diffusion coefficient (m²/s or cm²/s)
- $\text{erf}$ = error function (Gaussian)
- $x$ = depth from concrete surface (mm)
- $t$ = exposure time (seconds or years)

### 2.4 Corrosion Rate — Faraday's Law in Concrete

The mass loss of steel per unit area per unit time is given by **Faraday's Law**:

$$\text{CR} = \frac{M \cdot i_{\text{corr}}}{n \cdot F}$$

For iron in concrete, the equivalent *penetration depth* (mm/year) is:

$$d_{\text{pen}} = \frac{0.0116 \cdot i_{\text{corr}}}{n}$$

where:
- CR = corrosion rate (g/m²·yr)
- $M$ = atomic mass of Fe = **55.85 g/mol**
- $n$ = electrons transferred = **2**
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96 485 C/mol**
- $i_{\text{corr}}$ = corrosion current density (μA/cm²)
- $0.0116$ = unit conversion constant (from μA/cm² to mm/yr for Fe)

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All key formulas for the KTU ESE — Module 4 (Concrete Corrosion).**

| # | Formula / Relation | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $x_c = k_c \sqrt{t}$ | $x_c$ in mm; $t$ in years; $k_c$ in mm·yr⁻⁰·⁵ | Carbonation depth prediction |
| 2 | $C(x,t) = C_0 + (C_s - C_0)\left[1 - \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{D_c t}}\right)\right]$ | $C$ in % by cement mass; $D_c$ in cm²/s | Chloride profile / service life |
| 3 | $\text{CR} = \frac{M\, i_{\text{corr}}}{n\, F}$ | CR in g/m²·yr; $i_{\text{corr}}$ in μA/cm² | Faraday's mass-loss rate |
| 4 | $d_{\text{pen}} = \frac{0.0116\, i_{\text{corr}}}{n}$ | $d_{\text{pen}}$ in mm/yr | Penetration rate from $i_{\text{corr}}$ |
| 5 | $W = \frac{M\, I\, t}{n\, F}$ | $W$ = mass lost (g); $I$ = current (A) | Total mass loss in service |
| 6 | $\text{CR} = \frac{K \cdot I_{\text{corr}}}{A \cdot \rho}$ | $K$ = constant 3272 mm·A·yr⁻¹·cm⁻¹; $A$ = area (cm²) | Alternative KTU-board form |
| 7 | $R = \rho_e \cdot \frac{L}{A}$ | $\rho_e$ = concrete resistivity (Ω·cm); $L/A$ = cell geometry (cm⁻¹) | Macrocell resistance calculation |
| 8 | $\text{SR} = \frac{C_s - C_t}{C_t} \times 100$ | $C_s$ = surface; $C_t$ = threshold | Severity index (chloride) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Error Function Values (KTU Exam Hot-List):**
> - $\text{erf}(0) = 0$
> - $\text{erf}(0.5) = 0.5205$
> - $\text{erf}(1.0) = 0.8427$
> - $\text{erf}(1.5) = 0.9661$
> - $\text{erf}(2.0) = 0.9953$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Faraday's Law for Corrosion Rate in Concrete (Full Board-Style Proof)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Objective:** Show that the corrosion penetration depth of reinforcing steel $d_{\text{pen}}$ (mm/yr) is directly proportional to the measured corrosion current density $i_{\text{corr}}$ (μA/cm²).

**Starting Physical Fact (Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis):**
The mass $W$ of metal dissolved at the anode is proportional to the total electric charge $Q$ that has passed:

$$W \propto Q$$

Including the proportionality constant, and substituting $Q = I \cdot t$:

$$W = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 1 — Convert mass loss to equivalent volume loss.**
Using density $\rho$ of iron (7.85 g/cm³):

$$V_{\text{loss}} = \frac{W}{\rho} = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

**Step 2 — Convert volume loss to linear penetration depth.**
If the corroding area is $A$ (cm²) and the penetration is uniform, then:

$$d_{\text{pen}} = \frac{V_{\text{loss}}}{A} = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A}$$

**Step 3 — Express using current density $i_{\text{corr}} = I / A$.**

$$d_{\text{pen}} = \frac{M \cdot i_{\text{corr}} \cdot t}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the numerical constant for iron over 1 year.**

$$\frac{M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho} = \frac{55.85}{2 \times 96\,485 \times 7.85} = \frac{55.85}{1\,514\,813.5} \approx 3.687 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{cm·cm}^2/(\mu\text{A·yr})$$

**Step 5 — Convert to mm/year (multiply by 10):**

$$3.687 \times 10^{-5} \times 10 = 3.687 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{mm·cm}^2/(\mu\text{A·yr})$$

After inserting the empirical "Andrade" constant adjustment (0.0116 is the standard, well-validated KTU-board constant for field application), the working formula becomes:

$$\boxed{\,d_{\text{pen}} \ (\text{mm/yr}) = \frac{0.0116 \cdot i_{\text{corr}}\ (\mu\text{A/cm}^2)}{n}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Sanity Check:** If $i_{\text{corr}} = 1\ \mu\text{A/cm}^2$ and $n = 2$, then $d_{\text{pen}} = 0.0058\ \text{mm/yr}$. For a 16 mm rebar (typical in slabs), that means **section loss of about 1 mm every 172 years** — but with chlorides, $i_{\text{corr}}$ jumps to 5–10 μA/cm², and the same rebar loses 1 mm in just **17–35 years**.

### 3.2 KTU Board-Style Solved Numerical

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example (typical 7-mark KTU ESE problem):**
> A 16 mm diameter rebar embedded in a marine-exposed bridge pier has been instrumented with a linear polarization probe that records an average $i_{\text{corr}} = 4.5\ \mu\text{A/cm}^2$ over 5 years. Estimate the **percentage reduction in cross-sectional area** of the rebar. Assume uniform corrosion.

**Step 1 — Compute penetration depth.**

$$d_{\text{pen}} = \frac{0.0116 \times 4.5}{2} = \frac{0.0522}{2} = 0.0261\ \text{mm/yr}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by exposure time.**

$$d_{\text{total}} = 0.0261 \times 5 = 0.1305\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute original radius and area.**

$$r_0 = \frac{16}{2} = 8\ \text{mm}, \quad A_0 = \pi r_0^{2} = \pi (8)^2 = 201.06\ \text{mm}^2$$

**Step 4 — Compute reduced radius and area.**

$$r_{\text{new}} = 8 - 0.1305 = 7.8695\ \text{mm}$$

$$A_{\text{new}} = \pi (7.8695)^2 = 194.57\ \text{mm}^2$$

**Step 5 — Compute percentage area loss.**

$$\%\ \text{loss} = \frac{201.06 - 194.57}{201.06} \times 100 = \frac{6.49}{201.06} \times 100 \approx 3.23\ \%$$

**Final Answer:** The rebar has lost approximately **3.23 % of its cross-sectional area** in 5 years.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Severity Classification (KTU Exam Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standardized $i_{\text{corr}}$ Severity Table (from CEB / RILEM and adopted in KTU practicals):**

| $i_{\text{corr}}$ (μA/cm²) | Corrosion State | $d_{\text{pen}}$ (mm/yr) | Service Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\leq 0.1$ | **Passive / Negligible** | $\leq 0.0006$ | No action |
| $0.1$ – $0.5$ | **Low** | $0.0006$ – $0.003$ | Monitor |
| $0.5$ – $1.0$ | **Moderate** | $0.003$ – $0.006$ | Plan repair |
| $1.0$ – $5.0$ | **High** | $0.006$ – $0.029$ | Repair within 2–5 yr |
| $> 5.0$ | **Very High** | $> 0.029$ | Emergency repair |

### 3.4 Solved Carbonation-Depth Numerical (KTU 7-Mark Type)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example:**
> A column cast with OPC concrete (w/c = 0.55) shows a carbonation coefficient $k_c = 4.0\ \text{mm/yr}^{0.5}$. After 9 years, the clear cover to the rebar is 25 mm. Will carbonation have reached the rebar?

**Step 1 — Apply the carbonation depth equation.**

$$x_c = k_c \sqrt{t} = 4.0 \times \sqrt{9} = 4.0 \times 3 = 12\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compare with cover depth.**

$$x_c = 12\ \text{mm} \quad < \quad \text{cover} = 25\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute time to reach cover.**

$$t_{\text{crit}} = \left(\frac{\text{cover}}{k_c}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{25}{4.0}\right)^{2} = (6.25)^{2} = 39.06\ \text{years}$$

**Final Answer:** At 9 years, carbonation has reached only **12 mm** — well short of the 25 mm cover. The column is **safe for 39 years** from carbonation-induced corrosion.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Figure 1 — Mechanism of Rebar Corrosion in Concrete (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Concrete Cover\n25 to 75 mm] --> B[Aggressive Agents\nCO2 and Chloride Ions]
    B --> C[Pore Solution Diffusion\nElectrolyte Pathway]
    C --> D[Depassivation of Steel\npH drops below 9]
    D --> E1[Anode Reaction\nFe to Fe2+ plus 2e-]
    D --> E2[Cathode Reaction\nO2 plus 2H2O plus 4e- to 4OH-]
    E1 --> F1[Fe2 plus migrates into pore solution]
    E2 --> F2[OH minus forms at cathode site]
    F1 --> G[Formation of Iron Oxides\nFe OH 2 then Fe2O3]
    F2 --> G
    G --> H[Volume Expansion\n2 to 6 times]
    H --> I[Internal Tensile Stress]
    I --> J[Concrete Cracking and Spalling]
    J --> K[Structural Deterioration]
```

### 4.2 Figure 2 — Hierarchy of Corrosion-Control Strategies (KTU Board-Ready Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Corrosion Control in Concrete] --> S1[Material Selection]
    S --> S2[Design Strategies]
    S --> S3[Surface Protection]
    S --> S4[Electrochemical Methods]
    S --> S5[Chemical Inhibitors]

    S1 --> S1a[Low w/c Ratio Concrete]
    S1 --> S1b[Sulfoaluminate or Pozzolanic Cements]
    S1 --> S1c[Stainless Steel Rebar]
    S1 --> S1d[FRP Composite Rebar]
    S1 --> S1e[Epoxy Coated Rebar]

    S2 --> S2a[Increased Clear Cover]
    S2 --> S2b[Low Permeability Mix Design]
    S2 --> S2c[Proper Curing]
    S2 --> S2d[Drainage and Geometry Control]

    S3 --> S3a[Silane or Siloxane Sealers]
    S3 --> S3b[Acrylic and Polyurethane Coatings]
    S3 --> S3c[Membranes for New Construction]

    S4 --> S4a[Cathodic Protection\nImpressed Current]
    S4 --> S4b[Cathodic Protection\nSacrificial Anode]
    S4 --> S4c[Electrochemical Chloride Removal]
    S4 --> S4d[Realkalization]

    S5 --> S5a[Calcium Nitrite Inhibitor]
    S5 --> S5b[Organic Migrating Inhibitors]
    S5 --> S5c[Amine and Ester Based Admix]
```

### 4.3 Figure 3 — Time-Line of Concrete Deterioration (Phases of Service Life)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Phase 1 Initiation\nDepassivation Time t0] --> P2[Phase 2 Propagation\nActive Corrosion]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3 Deterioration\nCracking and Spalling]
    P3 --> P4[Phase 4 Failure\nLoss of Serviceability]

    P1 --- A1[Carbonation reaches cover\nor Cl- exceeds threshold]
    P2 --- A2[i_corr grows from 0.1 to over 1.0 microA per cm2]
    P3 --- A3[Crack width exceeds 0.3 mm\nDelamination begins]
    P4 --- A4[Rebar section loss exceeds 10 to 15 percent]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read these figures for the exam:**
> - *Figure 1* should be reproduced whenever the question asks "explain the mechanism" or "with a neat sketch".
> - *Figure 2* is the master answer chart for any "discuss corrosion control methods" question (full 14-mark).
> - *Figure 3* is the **time-line concept** examiners love to test in 7-mark service-life questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Question Bank Tagging Convention:** Each question is tagged with a simulated KTU past-paper style label. Marks are distributed as per KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Evaluation) pattern — Part A = 3 marks each; Part B = 14 marks each (with internal choice between two 7-mark sub-parts).

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define carbonation of concrete. How does it initiate corrosion of reinforcing steel?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 m):** Carbonation is the chemical reaction between atmospheric CO₂ and the calcium hydroxide Ca(OH)₂ in the hydrated cement paste, producing calcium carbonate CaCO₃ and water.
- **Initiation (1.5 m):** The reaction lowers the pore-solution pH from ≈ 12.5 to below 9, destroying the passive ferric oxide film on the rebar and exposing active iron to oxygen and moisture. With the passive film gone, an electrochemical cell forms, and active corrosion begins.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*What is the "critical chloride threshold" for rebar depassivation? State its typical value as per ACI 222R.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **critical chloride threshold** is the minimum chloride concentration at the rebar surface above which the passive film is locally broken down and active corrosion initiates, even in highly alkaline concrete. As per **ACI 222R**, the threshold is typically **0.4 % to 1.0 % chloride ion by mass of cement**, depending on cement composition, w/c ratio, and concrete quality.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> *On this topic, students commonly lose marks for the following reasons — avoid them!*
> 1. **Forgetting to state units** in Fick's law numerical problems (always specify cm²/s for $D_c$ and years for $t$).
> 2. **Confusing the carbonation constant $k_c$** with the chloride diffusion coefficient $D_c$ — they are *different* parameters used in *different* equations.
> 3. **Writing the error function as `erf` without parentheses**, or omitting the argument $\frac{x}{2\sqrt{D_c t}}$.
> 4. **Omitting the (0.0116) constant** when converting $i_{\text{corr}}$ to penetration depth.
> 5. **Listing control methods without grouping them** (material / design / surface / electrochemical / chemical) — examiners *explicitly reward* a structured hierarchy.
> 6. **Saying "concrete corrodes"** — concrete itself does *not* corrode in the metallic sense; the correct phrasing is *steel reinforcement corrodes while concrete degrades by chemical attack*.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Carbonation and Chloride Attack**

**(a) [7 marks]**
*With a neat diagram, explain the mechanism of carbonation-induced corrosion of steel in concrete. Derive the carbonation depth relation $x_c = k_c \sqrt{t}$ and discuss the factors affecting the carbonation coefficient $k_c$.*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Diagram (2 m):** Draw a labelled sketch showing:
- Concrete cover with CO₂ diffusion arrows
- Carbonation front (pH = 9 contour)
- Passive and depassivated zones of the rebar
- Anode and cathode sites with electron flow

**2. Mechanism (2.5 m):**
- CO₂ from air diffuses through pore structure (Fick's law).
- Reaction: $\text{CO}_2 + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow \text{CaCO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$.
- pH drops from 12.5 to ~8.5; γ-Fe₂O₃ passive film dissolves.
- Anode: $\text{Fe} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^-$.
- Cathode: $\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$.
- Fe²⁺ + 2OH⁻ → Fe(OH)₂ → further oxidised to Fe₂O₃ (rust).

**3. Derivation of $x_c = k_c \sqrt{t}$ (1.5 m):**
The depth of carbonation follows Fick's first law with the assumption of a *sharp* carbonation front moving into a semi-infinite medium. The mass of CO₂ that diffuses through unit area in time $t$ is $m = c_s \cdot x_c$, and Fick's first law gives $m = D \cdot c_s \cdot t / x_c$. Equating: $c_s x_c = D c_s t / x_c$, so $x_c^{2} = D t$, i.e., $x_c = \sqrt{D t}$. Defining $k_c = \sqrt{D}$ yields:

$$x_c = k_c \sqrt{t}$$

**4. Factors affecting $k_c$ (1 m):**
- w/c ratio (↑ ⇒ ↑ $k_c$)
- Cement type (pozzolanic cements reduce $k_c$)
- Curing quality (good curing reduces $k_c$)
- Relative humidity and CO₂ concentration of environment

**(b) [7 marks]**
*A reinforced concrete beam has a clear cover of 40 mm and is exposed to a marine environment. The surface chloride concentration is 0.8 % and the critical chloride threshold is 0.4 % by mass of cement. The chloride diffusion coefficient is $D_c = 1.5 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$. Estimate the time required for corrosion to initiate. Use $\text{erf}^{-1}(0.5) = 0.477$.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 m):** Set up Fick's second-law solution with $C_0 = 0$:

$$C(x,t) = C_s \left[1 - \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{D_c t}}\right)\right]$$

**Step 2 (1 m):** Substitute at the critical condition $C = 0.4\ \%$ at $x = 0.040\ \text{m}$:

$$0.4 = 0.8 \left[1 - \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{0.040}{2\sqrt{D_c t}}\right)\right]$$

**Step 3 (1.5 m):** Simplify:

$$0.5 = 1 - \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{0.040}{2\sqrt{D_c t}}\right) \Rightarrow \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{0.040}{2\sqrt{D_c t}}\right) = 0.5$$

**Step 4 (1.5 m):** Take the inverse error function:

$$\frac{0.040}{2\sqrt{D_c t}} = \text{erf}^{-1}(0.5) = 0.477$$

**Step 5 (2 m):** Square both sides and solve for $t$:

$$D_c t = \left(\frac{0.040}{2 \times 0.477}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{0.040}{0.954}\right)^{2} = (0.04193)^{2} = 1.758 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2$$

$$t = \frac{1.758 \times 10^{-3}}{1.5 \times 10^{-12}} = 1.172 \times 10^{9}\ \text{seconds}$$

**Step 6 (1 m):** Convert to years:

$$t = \frac{1.172 \times 10^{9}}{3.154 \times 10^{7}} \approx 37.2\ \text{years}$$

**Final Answer:** Corrosion will initiate at the rebar in approximately **37 years**.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Cathodic Protection and Control Methods**

**(a) [7 marks]**
*Explain the principle of impressed current cathodic protection (ICCP) for reinforced concrete structures. With a neat sketch, describe its components and discuss its design parameters.*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Principle (2 m):**
ICCP supplies a small DC current from an external rectifier through the concrete to the rebar, making the entire rebar network **cathodic**. This suppresses the anodic dissolution reaction: $\text{Fe} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^-$. The negative potential shift of ~300 mV (E ≈ −850 mV vs Cu/CuSO₄) ensures the steel is fully immune to corrosion.

**2. Components (2 m):**
- **DC Rectifier / Power Source** — controlled current supply
- **Inert Anode** — typically MMO (mixed metal oxide) Ti mesh or conductive coating on the concrete surface
- **Reference Electrode** — Ag/AgCl or Cu/CuSO₄ for monitoring
- **Concrete Electrolyte** — pore solution conducts ionic current
- **Rebar Network** — acts as the cathode, connected to the negative terminal

**3. Design Parameters (1.5 m):**
- Protection current density: **5–20 mA/m²** of rebar surface for typical structures
- Protection potential: **−850 mV vs Cu/CuSO₄** (with 100 mV depolarization criterion)
- Anode current density limit
- Anode-to-cathode geometry and zone spacing

**4. Advantages (1 m):**
- Can be applied to existing structures
- Long protection life (20–40 years)
- Continuously adjustable
- Most effective for chloride-contaminated structures

**5. Limitations (0.5 m):**
- Requires continuous power supply
- Risk of hydrogen embrittlement at over-protection (< −1100 mV)

**(b) [7 marks]**
*Discuss the various methods of controlling corrosion in reinforced concrete structures. Group them under (i) material selection, (ii) design strategies, (iii) coatings and sealers, and (iv) chemical inhibitors.*

**Model Solution (grouped, KTU board-format):**

**(i) Material Selection (2 m):**
- **Low w/c ratio (≤ 0.40)** for dense, low-permeability concrete
- **Supplementary cementitious materials** — fly ash, silica fume, GGBS reduce permeability and chloride diffusion
- **Stainless steel rebar** (e.g., 304, 316, or duplex grades) for highly aggressive environments
- **Epoxy-coated rebar (ECR)** — fusion-bonded epoxy coating (debonding in chlorides is a known concern)
- **FRP (fibre-reinforced polymer) rebar** — non-metallic, immune to chloride attack (used in MRI rooms and coastal bridges)

**(ii) Design Strategies (2 m):**
- **Adequate clear cover** — ≥ 50 mm in marine, ≥ 75 mm in severe exposure (IS 456:2000)
- **Proper curing** — at least 7–14 days of moist curing reduces surface permeability
- **Drainage and detailing** — avoid water ponding; slope surfaces; use drip grooves
- **Crack-width control** — limit design crack width to ≤ 0.2 mm in aggressive exposure

**(iii) Coatings and Sealers (1.5 m):**
- **Silane / siloxane water repellents** — hydrophobic surface treatment
- **Acrylic and polyurethane elastomeric coatings** — bridge-deck applications
- **Epoxy / polyurethane top-coats** on the concrete surface
- **Membranes** for new construction (bituminous, PVC)

**(iv) Chemical Inhibitors (1.5 m):**
- **Calcium nitrite** (anodic inhibitor) — competitive adsorption with Cl⁻; commercial as DCI® or Rheocrete®
- **Amine-carboxylate and amino-alcohol** based migrating corrosion inhibitors (MCI®)
- **Organic ester admixtures** for new construction
- These work by *passivating* the steel surface or *sequestering* chloride ions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 — Concrete Structures: Last-Minute Revision Bullets**

- [x] **Concrete does NOT corrode — the embedded steel does.** Concrete degrades by chemical attack (sulfate, ASR, carbonation); steel corrodes electrochemically.
- [x] **Initial pH of concrete pore solution = 12.5–13.5** (saturated Ca(OH)₂). This is what *passivates* the rebar with a γ-Fe₂O₃ film.
- [x] **Two main depassivation agents:** (1) **Carbonation** (CO₂ + Ca(OH)₂ → CaCO₃, drops pH < 9), and (2) **Chlorides** (Cl⁻ > 0.4–1.0 % by mass of cement).
- [x] **Carbonation depth:** $x_c = k_c \sqrt{t}$ — typical $k_c$ ranges 1–10 mm/√yr.
- [x] **Chloride ingress:** Fick's second law with error function solution — master the form $C(x,t) = C_s[1 - \text{erf}(x/2\sqrt{D_c t})]$.
- [x] **Faraday's Law for corrosion rate:** $d_{\text{pen}} = (0.0116 \cdot i_{\text{corr}})/n$ in mm/yr; for Fe, $n = 2$.
- [x] **Severity table (memorize):** $i_{\text{corr}} \leq 0.1$ = passive; 0.1–0.5 = low; 0.5–1.0 = moderate; 1.0–5.0 = high; > 5.0 μA/cm² = very high.
- [x] **Control methods (5 groups):** Material selection • Design strategies • Surface coatings • Electrochemical (CP) • Chemical inhibitors.
- [x] **Cathodic protection:** Sacrificial (Zn, Al, Mg anodes) for small jobs; **Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP)** with MMO-Ti anodes for large bridges/piers.
- [x] **Protection criterion:** Re-bar potential ≤ **−850 mV vs Cu/CuSO₄** (with 100 mV depolarization).
- [x] **Volume expansion of rust** = 2× to 6× the parent steel — *this* is what causes spalling.
- [x] **Critical chloride threshold** for ACI 222R: **0.4 % to 1.0 %** by mass of cement.
- [x] **IS 456:2000 cover rules:** Moderate exposure ≥ 30 mm; severe ≥ 45 mm; very severe / marine ≥ 50–75 mm.
- [x] **For exams: ALWAYS draw the Mermaid mechanism diagram** and the **hierarchy chart of control methods** — they convert 7-mark answers into 14-mark answers.
- [x] **Tip for ESE Day:** Keep the **error function values table** on your formula sheet (0, 0.5205, 0.8427, 0.9661, 0.9953 for arguments 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Duplex
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT527 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Duplex

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Duplex Stainless Steels (DSS)** constitute a distinct family of corrosion-resistant alloys characterized by a balanced, two-phase (*duplex*) microstructure comprising approximately equal volumetric fractions of **body-centered cubic (BCC) ferrite (α-phase)** and **face-centered cubic (FCC) austenite (γ-phase)**. The phase balance is typically maintained in the range of **30–70% of either phase**, with the optimum ratio being **~50:50**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The defining feature of DSS is *not* its chemistry alone, but its **two-phase crystallographic architecture**, which uniquely combines the mechanical strength of ferritic stainless steels with the corrosion resistance and toughness of austenitic stainless steels.

**Typical Chemical Composition Window of DSS:**

| Element | Function | Typical Range (% by mass) |
|---|---|---|
| Chromium (Cr) | Ferrite stabilizer; passive film former | 18 – 28 |
| Nickel (Ni) | Austenite stabilizer | 4 – 8 |
| Molybdenum (Mo) | Pitting resistance | 1 – 5 |
| Nitrogen (N) | Austenite stabilizer; pitting resistance | 0.1 – 0.3 |
| Carbon (C) | Trace (kept low) | ≤ 0.03 |
| Manganese (Mn), Silicon (Si), Tungsten (W), Copper (Cu) | Minor alloying/balancing | Trace |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **chocolate chip muffin** 🍫🧁. The soft, porous muffin body represents the **ferrite phase (BCC)** — strong, magnetic, and resistant to chloride stress corrosion cracking but less tough. The embedded chocolate chips represent the **austenite phase (FCC)** — ductile, tough, non-magnetic, and rich in nickel and nitrogen.

Neither ingredient alone gives you the perfect muffin — the **ferrite alone is strong but brittle**, and the **austenite alone is soft**. However, by carefully *blending* them, you obtain an alloy that is **simultaneously strong, tough, and corrosion-resistant**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Just as a master baker balances flour and chocolate to achieve the perfect texture, the metallurgist balances **ferrite stabilizers (Cr, Mo, Si)** against **austenite stabilizers (Ni, N, C, Mn)** to lock the microstructure at the sweet ~50:50 ratio.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Density of DSS:** **~7.8 g/cm³**
- **Magnetic Permeability:** Ferromagnetic (due to ferrite) — a key distinguishing feature from fully austenitic grades
- **Yield Strength (2205):** **~450 MPa** — roughly **double** that of standard austenitic SS 304 (~215 MPa)
- **Operating Temperature Limit (to avoid embrittlement):** **−50 °C to +300 °C** for most grades

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The "Why" and "How" Behind Duplex Stainless Steel Design

The conceptual logic behind DSS can be deconstructed into **five engineering pillars**:

1. **Strength without Sacrificing Toughness**
   - The BCC ferrite phase provides high yield strength (solid-solution strengthening by Cr and Mo).
   - The FCC austenite phase delivers ductility, low-temperature toughness, and impact resistance.
   - The composite effect yields a yield strength **2× higher** than 300-series austenitic SS.

2. **Resistance to Chloride Stress Corrosion Cracking (Cl⁻-SCC)**
   - Austenitic SS (e.g., 304, 316) are highly susceptible to SCC in chloride environments.
   - The continuous ferrite phase in DSS **breaks up the slip planes** and interrupts crack propagation, giving DSS a **critical threshold stress** for SCC an order of magnitude higher.

3. **Superior Pitting and Crevice Corrosion Resistance**
   - Cr, Mo, and N synergistically reinforce the passive Cr₂O₃ film.
   - Quantitative ranking is performed using the **Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number (PREN)**.

4. **Lower Nickel Content (Cost-Effectiveness)**
   - DSS uses only 4–8% Ni versus 8–12% in 300-series austenitic SS, making it **less sensitive to Ni-price volatility**.

5. **Weldability with Phase-Balance Retention**
   - With proper thermal control, DSS can be welded while maintaining the 50:50 phase ratio, though improper heat input causes **sigma (σ) phase** precipitation.

### 2.2 Phase Stabilization Theory

The phase balance in DSS is governed by the **competition between ferrite and austenite stabilizers**. This is quantified using the **Schaeffler Diagram** (legacy) or the more accurate **WRC-1992 Diagram**.

**Schaeffler Equivalent Equations (Foundational):**

$$\text{Cr}_{eq} = \%\text{Cr} + \%\text{Mo} + 1.5 \times \%\text{Si} + 0.5 \times \%\text{Nb}$$

$$\text{Ni}_{eq} = \%\text{Ni} + 30 \times \%\text{C} + 0.5 \times \%\text{Mn}$$

**WRC-1992 Equivalent Equations (Modern, more accurate):**

$$\text{Cr}_{eq} = \%\text{Cr} + \%\text{Mo} + 0.7 \times \%\text{Nb} + 0.7 \times \%\text{W}$$

$$\text{Ni}_{eq} = \%\text{Ni} + 35 \times \%\text{C} + 20 \times \%\text{N} + 0.25 \times \%\text{Cu}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Conceptual Bridge:** A higher **Cr_eq / Ni_eq ratio** shifts the structure toward ferrite; a higher **Ni_eq** shifts toward austenite. The WRC-1992 equation is preferred because it explicitly accounts for the strong austenite-stabilizing effect of **nitrogen**, which the original Schaeffler equation ignored.

### 2.3 Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number (PREN)

The PREN is the **single most important metric** in DSS selection for corrosive service.

$$\boxed{\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \times \%\text{Mo} + 16 \times \%\text{N}}$$

A more conservative variant used for **super-austenitic** and highly alloyed grades:

$$\text{PREN}_{\text{conservative}} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \times \%\text{Mo} + 30 \times \%\text{N}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** Do not blindly add the 30-coefficient. Use **16** for conventional duplex/standard austenitic SS, and **30** only for high-nitrogen super-austenitic grades.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Metric** | **Equation** | **Application / Engineering Use** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| PREN (Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number) | $\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \cdot \%\text{Mo} + 16 \cdot \%\text{N}$ | Rank DSS by pitting/crevice resistance in chloride media | Dimensionless |
| PREN (conservative) | $\text{PREN}_c = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \cdot \%\text{Mo} + 30 \cdot \%\text{N}$ | Used for super-austenitic / high-N grades | Dimensionless |
| Schaeffler Chromium Equivalent | $\text{Cr}_{eq} = \%\text{Cr} + \%\text{Mo} + 1.5 \cdot \%\text{Si} + 0.5 \cdot \%\text{Nb}$ | Predicts ferrite tendency; plot on Schaeffler diagram | Dimensionless |
| Schaeffler Nickel Equivalent | $\text{Ni}_{eq} = \%\text{Ni} + 30 \cdot \%\text{C} + 0.5 \cdot \%\text{Mn}$ | Predicts austenite tendency; plot on Schaeffler diagram | Dimensionless |
| WRC-1992 Chromium Equivalent | $\text{Cr}_{eq} = \%\text{Cr} + \%\text{Mo} + 0.7 \cdot \%\text{Nb} + 0.7 \cdot \%\text{W}$ | Modern ferrite prediction in welds | Dimensionless |
| WRC-1992 Nickel Equivalent | $\text{Ni}_{eq} = \%\text{Ni} + 35 \cdot \%\text{C} + 20 \cdot \%\text{N} + 0.25 \cdot \%\text{Cu}$ | Modern austenite prediction (includes N) | Dimensionless |
| Critical Pitting Temperature (CPT) | $\text{CPT} \approx a + b \cdot \text{PREN}$ (empirical) | Threshold temperature for stable pitting | °C |
| Phase Ratio Target | $\text{Vol}\%_{\alpha} \approx \text{Vol}\%_{\gamma} \approx 50$ | Optimum for balanced DSS properties | % |

### 2.5 Classification of Duplex Stainless Steels

| **Family** | **Example UNS** | **Cr (%)** | **Ni (%)** | **Mo (%)** | **N (%)** | **Typical PREN** | **Typical Application** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Lean Duplex** | UNS S32304 (2304) | 23 | 4 | 0.3 | 0.10 | **~25** | Storage tanks, structural |
| **Standard Duplex** | UNS S32205 (2205) | 22 | 5 | 3.0 | 0.15 | **~34** | Oil & gas, chemical tanks |
| **Super Duplex** | UNS S32750 (2507) | 25 | 7 | 4.0 | 0.30 | **~43** | Hot seawater, heat exchangers |
| **Hyper Duplex** | UNS S32707 (2707) | 27 | 6.5 | 5.0 | 0.40 | **~50** | Aggressive chemical/HCl service |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil & Gas (downhole tubing, manifolds):** DSS resists H₂S/CO₂ and chlorides simultaneously.
- **Marine & Desalination:** Used in pumps, valves, propeller shafts; resists seawater and brine.
- **Chemical Process Industry:** Handles hot chlorides, organic acids, and oxidizing media.
- **Pulp & Paper Digesters:** Withstands alkaline sulfide liquors.
- **Heat Exchangers & Pressure Vessels:** Replaces austenitic SS where SCC and weight are concerns.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 PREN Derivation and Worked Examples (Exhaustive)

The PREN is a **weighted linear combination** of the three most influential alloying elements for pitting resistance, calibrated empirically from electrochemical data and ASTM G48 testing.

**Theoretical Basis:**
- Chromium forms the passive Cr₂O₃ film and is the **primary** pitting resistor → weight = 1.
- Molybdenum enhances passive film stability in chloride solutions → experimentally, **Mo is ~3.3× more effective than Cr per %**, hence weight = 3.3.
- Nitrogen strengthens the passive film and forms ammonium ions (NH₄⁺) inside pits, which buffer the local pH → experimentally, **N is ~16× more effective than Cr per %**, hence weight = 16.

**The Final Operational Form:**

$$\boxed{\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \times \%\text{Mo} + 16 \times \%\text{N}}$$

---

**Worked Example 1: Standard Duplex UNS S32205 (2205)**
- Composition: 22% Cr, 5% Ni, 3% Mo, 0.15% N

Substituting into the PREN formula:

$$\text{PREN} = 22 + (3.3 \times 3) + (16 \times 0.15)$$

$$= 22 + 9.9 + 2.4$$

$$= 34.3$$

**Interpretation:** A PREN of 34.3 places UNS S32205 in the **Standard Duplex** category, suitable for moderately aggressive chloride environments (e.g., brackish water, mild chemical service).

---

**Worked Example 2: Super Duplex UNS S32750 (2507)**
- Composition: 25% Cr, 7% Ni, 4% Mo, 0.30% N

Substituting into the PREN formula:

$$\text{PREN} = 25 + (3.3 \times 4) + (16 \times 0.30)$$

$$= 25 + 13.2 + 4.8$$

$$= 43.0$$

**Interpretation:** A PREN of 43.0 places UNS S32750 in the **Super Duplex** category, making it ideal for **hot seawater, aggressive chemical media, and offshore process equipment**.

---

**Worked Example 3: Lean Duplex UNS S32304 (2304)**
- Composition: 23% Cr, 4% Ni, 0.3% Mo, 0.10% N

Substituting into the PREN formula:

$$\text{PREN} = 23 + (3.3 \times 0.3) + (16 \times 0.10)$$

$$= 23 + 0.99 + 1.6$$

$$= 25.6$$

**Interpretation:** A PREN of 25.6 places UNS S32304 in the **Lean Duplex** category, suitable for **structural and less-corrosive service**, often used as a cost-effective substitute for 304/316.

---

**Worked Example 4: Hyper Duplex UNS S32707 (2707)**
- Composition: 27% Cr, 6.5% Ni, 5.0% Mo, 0.40% N

Substituting into the PREN formula:

$$\text{PREN} = 27 + (3.3 \times 5.0) + (16 \times 0.40)$$

$$= 27 + 16.5 + 6.4$$

$$= 49.9$$

**Interpretation:** A PREN of 49.9 places UNS S32707 in the **Hyper Duplex** category, suitable for **extreme service** such as hot HCl, aggressive halide brines, and severe oilfield conditions.

---

### 3.2 Schaeffler / WRC-1992 Phase Prediction — Worked Example

A 2205 weld deposit has the following chemistry: **22% Cr, 5% Ni, 3% Mo, 0.15% N, 0.02% C, 0.6% Mn, 0.5% Si**.

**Step 1: Compute Cr_eq (Schaeffler)**

$$\text{Cr}_{eq} = 22 + 3 + (1.5 \times 0.5) + (0.5 \times 0)$$

$$= 22 + 3 + 0.75 + 0 = 25.75$$

**Step 2: Compute Ni_eq (Schaeffler)**

$$\text{Ni}_{eq} = 5 + (30 \times 0.02) + (0.5 \times 0.6)$$

$$= 5 + 0.60 + 0.30 = 5.90$$

**Step 3: Compute Cr_eq (WRC-1992) — no Nb or W present**

$$\text{Cr}_{eq, WRC} = 22 + 3 + 0 + 0 = 25$$

**Step 4: Compute Ni_eq (WRC-1992)**

$$\text{Ni}_{eq, WRC} = 5 + (35 \times 0.02) + (20 \times 0.15) + (0.25 \times 0)$$

$$= 5 + 0.70 + 3.00 + 0 = 8.70$$

**Step 5: Plot on the Schaeffler/WRC Diagram and Read Ferrite Content**

- For the WRC-1992 system, plotting (Cr_eq = 25, Ni_eq = 8.7) lands in the **~50% ferrite / ~50% austenite** zone — confirming phase balance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The WRC-1992 equation gives a *higher* Ni_eq than the Schaeffler equation because of the **strong austenite-stabilizing influence of nitrogen** (coefficient of 20 vs Schaeffler's omission). This is why WRC-1992 is the modern industry standard for predicting weld microstructure.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: DSS vs Other Stainless Steel Families

| **Property** | **Ferritic SS (430)** | **Austenitic SS (304)** | **Duplex SS (2205)** | **Super Duplex (2507)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Microstructure | 100% α (BCC) | 100% γ (FCC) | 50% α + 50% γ | 50% α + 50% γ |
| Yield Strength (MPa) | ~275 | ~215 | ~450 | ~550 |
| Tensile Strength (MPa) | ~450 | ~505 | ~620 | ~800 |
| PREN (typical) | ~18 | ~19 | ~34 | ~43 |
| Cl⁻-SCC Resistance | High | Low | **High** | **Very High** |
| Magnetic? | Yes | No | Yes | Yes |
| Weldability | Limited | Excellent | Good (with care) | Good (with care) |
| Cost Factor (relative to 304) | ~0.7 | 1.0 | ~1.4 | ~1.9 |
| Common Use | Automotive trim | Food, kitchen | Oil & gas, marine | Offshore, hot brine |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Duplex Stainless Steel Microstructure

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeA[Duplex Stainless Steel Alloy]
    nodeA --> nodeB[Ferrite Phase alpha BCC]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Austenite Phase gamma FCC]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Cr and Mo Rich]
    nodeB --> nodeE[High Yield Strength]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Magnetic Behavior]
    nodeB --> nodeG[Resists Cl SCC]
    nodeC --> nodeH[Ni and N Rich]
    nodeC --> nodeI[High Toughness]
    nodeC --> nodeJ[Non Magnetic Islands]
    nodeC --> nodeK[Cr2O3 Passive Film Stability]
    nodeA --> nodeL[Target Phase Ratio 50 alpha to 50 gamma]
    nodeA --> nodeM[Stabilizers Ferrite Cr Mo Si]
    nodeA --> nodeN[Stabilizers Austenite Ni N C Mn]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block-flow model captures the **two-phase architecture** and the **synergistic stabilization** by alloying elements. The ferrite and austenite phases are interpenetrating, with the ferrite often forming the *matrix* and austenite forming *islands* (or vice versa) depending on thermomechanical history.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Classification Tree by PREN and Application

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeStart[Duplex Stainless Steels DSS]
    nodeStart --> nodeLean[Lean Duplex PREN 25 to 30]
    nodeStart --> nodeStd[Standard Duplex PREN 30 to 38]
    nodeStart --> nodeSup[Super Duplex PREN 38 to 45]
    nodeStart --> nodeHyp[Hyper Duplex PREN more than 45]
    nodeLean --> nodeLean1[UNS S32304 2304]
    nodeLean1 --> nodeLean2[Storage tanks structural]
    nodeStd --> nodeStd1[UNS S32205 2205]
    nodeStd1 --> nodeStd2[Oil and gas chemical tanks]
    nodeSup --> nodeSup1[UNS S32750 2507]
    nodeSup1 --> nodeSup2[Hot seawater heat exchangers]
    nodeHyp --> nodeHyp1[UNS S32707 2707]
    nodeHyp1 --> nodeHyp2[Aggressive chemicals hot HCl]
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: PREN Calculation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Identify Alloy Composition]
    node1 --> node2[Read Cr percent]
    node1 --> node3[Read Mo percent]
    node1 --> node4[Read N percent]
    node2 --> node5[Apply PREN Formula]
    node3 --> node5
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6[PREN equals Cr plus 3.3 times Mo plus 16 times N]
    node6 --> node7{PREN Range}
    node7 -->|25 to 30| node8[Lean Duplex]
    node7 -->|30 to 38| node9[Standard Duplex]
    node7 -->|38 to 45| node10[Super Duplex]
    node7 -->|more than 45| node11[Hyper Duplex]
    node8 --> node12[Select Grade UNS S32304]
    node9 --> node13[Select Grade UNS S32205]
    node10 --> node14[Select Grade UNS S32750]
    node11 --> node15[Select Grade UNS S32707]
```

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Failure / Embrittlement Pathways in DSS

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Improper Heat Treatment]
    nodeA --> nodeB[Sigma Phase 600 to 900 C]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Chi Phase 700 to 900 C]
    nodeA --> nodeD[475 C Embrittlement]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Cr2N Precipitation 600 to 900 C]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Loss of Toughness]
    nodeB --> nodeG[Preferential Corrosion of Ferrite]
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeH[Intergranular Corrosion]
    nodeF --> nodeI[Service Failure]
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Corrective Action Solution Anneal 1050 to 1100 C and Water Quench]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** The **sigma (σ) phase** is the single most damaging precipitate in DSS. It is **Cr and Mo rich**, hard, brittle, and forms in the **600–900 °C** window. Solution annealing at **1050–1100 °C** followed by **rapid water quenching** redissolves σ and restores the 50:50 phase balance.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**
*Define Duplex Stainless Steels. Mention the phase composition and two key advantages over austenitic stainless steels.*

**Model Answer:**
Duplex Stainless Steels (DSS) are a class of corrosion-resistant steels with a two-phase microstructure consisting of approximately equal proportions (~50:50) of ferrite (BCC) and austenite (FCC) phases. The composition typically includes 18–28% Cr, 4–8% Ni, 1–5% Mo, and 0.1–0.3% N.

Two key advantages over austenitic SS:
1. **Yield strength is roughly double** (e.g., 2205 has ~450 MPa vs 304's ~215 MPa), enabling thinner sections and weight reduction.
2. **Superior chloride stress corrosion cracking (Cl⁻-SCC) resistance** due to the BCC ferrite phase interrupting slip-band crack propagation.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
*What is the Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number (PREN)? Write its formula and state the typical PREN range for standard duplex stainless steel (2205).*

**Model Answer:**
PREN is an empirical, dimensionless number used to rank the pitting corrosion resistance of stainless steels. It is a weighted sum of the alloying elements that most influence passive film stability: chromium, molybdenum, and nitrogen.

**Formula:**

$$\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \times \%\text{Mo} + 16 \times \%\text{N}$$

For standard DSS grade 2205 (22% Cr, 3% Mo, 0.15% N), the PREN is approximately **34**, placing it in the **30–38 standard duplex range**.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Essay / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
*(a)* Explain the microstructure of Duplex Stainless Steels with a labeled block diagram. Discuss the role of major alloying elements (Cr, Ni, Mo, N) in stabilizing the two phases. **(7 Marks)**
*(b)* Compare Standard Duplex, Super Duplex, and Hyper Duplex grades based on **composition, PREN, and applications**. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

The microstructure of DSS consists of two interpenetrating phases:
- **Ferrite (α, BCC):** rich in Cr and Mo, magnetic, contributes high strength and SCC resistance.
- **Austenite (γ, FCC):** rich in Ni and N, non-magnetic, contributes toughness and ductility.

The aim is to maintain a **~50:50 phase ratio** for optimal property balance.

**Role of major alloying elements:**
- **Chromium (Cr):** Primary ferrite stabilizer; forms the passive Cr₂O₃ film → essential for corrosion resistance. Range: 18–28%.
- **Nickel (Ni):** Primary austenite stabilizer; lowers the ferrite content and promotes γ-phase. Range: 4–8%.
- **Molybdenum (Mo):** Ferrite stabilizer; improves resistance to pitting and crevice corrosion (especially in Cl⁻ media). Range: 1–5%.
- **Nitrogen (N):** Strong austenite stabilizer; enhances pitting resistance, improves weldability. Range: 0.1–0.3%.

The balance between ferrite-stabilizers (Cr, Mo) and austenite-stabilizers (Ni, N) is quantified using the **WRC-1992 equivalent equations**.

> **Mark Distribution Note (a):** *Microstructure sketch – 2 marks; role of each element – 1.25 marks × 4 = 5 marks.*

---

**Model Solution (b):**

A comparative table is presented:

| **Property** | **Standard DSS (2205)** | **Super DSS (2507)** | **Hyper DSS (2707)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cr (%) | 22 | 25 | 27 |
| Ni (%) | 5 | 7 | 6.5 |
| Mo (%) | 3 | 4 | 5 |
| N (%) | 0.15 | 0.30 | 0.40 |
| **PREN** | **~34** | **~43** | **~50** |
| **Application** | Chemical tanks, oil & gas piping, pressure vessels | Hot seawater systems, heat exchangers, offshore platforms | Aggressive halide brines, hot HCl, severe oilfield service |

**Discussion:**
- Increasing Cr, Mo, and N progressively enhances pitting/crevice resistance.
- The price premium also increases: Super DSS is ~1.5× the cost of Standard DSS, and Hyper DSS is ~2×.
- Selection is based on the **critical pitting temperature (CPT)** of the service environment.

> **Mark Distribution Note (b):** *Composition + PREN comparison – 4 marks; Applications – 3 marks.*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
*(a)* Discuss the **forms of corrosion that affect Duplex Stainless Steels**, with emphasis on **sigma phase embrittlement and pitting corrosion**. **(7 Marks)**
*(b)* Calculate the PREN of a duplex steel with composition **25% Cr, 4% Mo, 0.25% N**. Identify the family it belongs to. If the environment has a chloride concentration of 60,000 ppm and temperature 80 °C, comment on the suitability of the alloy. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

Duplex stainless steels can suffer from several forms of corrosion:

1. **Pitting Corrosion:**
   - Localized breakdown of the passive film at weak points (inclusions, MnS, or ferrite–austenite grain boundaries).
   - In DSS, pitting usually initiates in the **ferrite phase** (which is Mo-rich but sometimes depleted in N at grain boundaries).
   - Resistance is quantified by **PREN** and **Critical Pitting Temperature (CPT)**.

2. **Sigma (σ) Phase Embrittlement:**
   - Forms when DSS is held in the **600–900 °C** range (e.g., during improper welding or slow cooling).
   - σ is hard, brittle, and **Cr + Mo rich**.
   - Causes **loss of toughness, drop in impact energy, and preferential corrosion** of the ferrite phase.
   - Prevention: **solution annealing at 1050–1100 °C** followed by **water quenching**.

3. **Intergranular Corrosion:**
   - Caused by **chromium nitride (Cr₂N) or Cr₂₃C₆** precipitation at grain boundaries in the 600–900 °C range, which depletes adjacent regions of Cr.
   - Controlled by **low carbon content** and **nitrogen stabilization**.

4. **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC):**
   - Although DSS has far higher resistance than austenitic SS, very high Cl⁻ + H₂S + temperature can still cause SCC. Hot chloride environments above ~150 °C must be evaluated.

5. **Crevice Corrosion:**
   - Common in gasketed joints; the **critical crevice temperature (CCT)** is the limiting design parameter.

> **Mark Distribution Note (a):** *Pitting – 2 marks; Sigma embrittlement – 2 marks; Intergranular + SCC + Crevice – 3 marks.*

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1: Calculate PREN**

$$\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \times \%\text{Mo} + 16 \times \%\text{N}$$

$$= 25 + (3.3 \times 4) + (16 \times 0.25)$$

$$= 25 + 13.2 + 4.0$$

$$= 42.2$$

**Step 2: Identify the family**

A PREN of 42.2 falls in the **38–45** range → **Super Duplex Stainless Steel** family (e.g., similar to UNS S32750).

**Step 3: Comment on suitability**

- **Service condition:** 60,000 ppm Cl⁻ (~6% NaCl) at 80 °C is a *moderately aggressive marine* environment.
- **Critical Pitting Temperature (CPT) for Super DSS:** typically **~70–90 °C** in 6% FeCl₃.
- **Conclusion:** Super DSS with PREN ~42 is **marginally suitable** for this service.
  - It is acceptable for **continuous service at ≤ 80 °C** in 60,000 ppm chloride.
  - For **temperatures approaching or exceeding 90 °C**, a **Hyper DSS (PREN ≥ 49)** or a **super-austenitic** (e.g., 254 SMO) is recommended.
  - For **crevice conditions** or stagnant brine, the **CCT (typically ~40–50 °C for Super DSS)** becomes the design-limiting parameter, and the alloy may not be suitable.

> **Mark Distribution Note (b):** *PREN calculation – 3 marks; Family identification – 1 mark; Application comment with reasoning – 3 marks.*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Traps in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting the PREN coefficient of 16 for N** (or worse, omitting N entirely). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 2. **Conflating DSS with Super-Austenitic SS** (e.g., 254 SMO). DSS is magnetic, super-austenitic is not — a key distinguishing feature.
> 3. **Ignoring the 50:50 phase ratio constraint.** DSS performance is *highly sensitive* to phase imbalance; if the answer does not discuss this, expect a 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Not mentioning sigma phase embrittlement** when asked about DSS corrosion. This is a KTU board examiner favorite.
> 5. **Using absolute-value bars in formula tables** — examiners expect clean, properly formatted LaTeX.

---

### 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ **DSS = two-phase (ferrite + austenite) stainless steel, ~50:50 phase ratio.**
- ✅ **Composition window:** 18–28% Cr, 4–8% Ni, 1–5% Mo, 0.1–0.3% N.
- ✅ **PREN formula:** $\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \cdot \%\text{Mo} + 16 \cdot \%\text{N}$.
- ✅ **PREN ranges:** Lean (25–30) → Standard (30–38) → Super (38–45) → Hyper (>45).
- ✅ **2205 is the workhorse grade (PREN ~34).**
- ✅ **Yield strength of DSS is ~2× that of 300-series austenitic SS.**
- ✅ **DSS is magnetic** (due to ferrite) — distinguishes it from austenitic SS.
- ✅ **DSS is highly resistant to Cl⁻-SCC** because the BCC ferrite phase blocks crack propagation.
- ✅ **Sigma phase (600–900 °C)** is the most damaging precipitate; eliminated by **solution annealing at 1050–1100 °C + water quench**.
- ✅ **Nitrogen is a strong austenite stabilizer** (captured in WRC-1992, missed by Schaeffler).
- ✅ **WRC-1992 is preferred over Schaeffler** for modern phase prediction in welds.
- ✅ **Major applications:** Oil & gas, marine/desalination, chemical processing, heat exchangers, structural.
- ✅ **Selection metric:** CPT for pitting, CCT for crevice — both correlate with PREN.
- ✅ **Welds of DSS require low heat input** to avoid σ phase and Cr₂N precipitation.
- ✅ **CPT of Super DSS ≈ 70–90 °C in 6% FeCl₃**; CCT is typically 20–30 °C lower.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Super Duplex Stainless Steels (SDSS)** are a sub-class of duplex stainless steels (DSS) defined by a highly balanced ferritic-austenitic microstructure (≈ 50% ferrite / 50% austenite) and an enhanced alloying content of **chromium (25–27 wt%)**, **molybdenum (3.5–5.0 wt%)**, **nickel (6.0–8.0 wt%)**, and **nitrogen (0.24–0.32 wt%)**. The combination gives a **Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number (PREN) > 40**, mechanical yield strength ≥ 550 MPa, and outstanding resistance to chloride-induced pitting, crevice corrosion, and stress corrosion cracking.

The widely commercial grades — **UNS S32750 (SAF 2507)** and **UNS S32760 (Zeron 100)** — are the *de facto* references in the oil-and-gas, desalination, and chemical process industries.

| Grade | UNS | Cr | Ni | Mo | N | PREN (typ.) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2205 (Std. Duplex) | S31803 | 22 | 5.5 | 3.0 | 0.15 | ≈ 34 |
| **2507 (Super Duplex)** | **S32750** | **25** | **7** | **4** | **0.30** | **≈ 43** |
| **Zeron 100 (Super Duplex)** | **S32760** | **25** | **7** | **3.5** | **0.25** | **≈ 41** |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hybrid Athlete"

Think of a Super Duplex Stainless Steel as a **"hybrid athlete"** combining two distinct physiologies:

- The **ferritic phase (α-FCC → BCC)** is like a *marathon runner* — lean, strong, immune to chloride stress corrosion cracking, but somewhat brittle and not great at tackling acidic pitting.
- The **austenitic phase (γ-FCC)** is like a *sprinter* — ductile, tough, excellent in cryogenic conditions, but vulnerable to chloride SCC.

By micro-alloying the runner and the sprinter into a single team, the SDSS inherits the **strength of the ferritic phase** (yield strength roughly **double** that of Type 316L) *and* the **corrosion toughness of the austenitic phase** (pitting and crevice resistance comparable to high-nickel austenitics like Alloy 825).

> [!NOTE]
> The "Super" prefix is not marketing fluff — it is metallurgically justified because the alloy contains significantly **higher Cr, Mo, and N** than standard 2205 duplex, pushing PREN above the **critical 40 threshold** required for severe seawater and sour-service (H₂S, CO₂, Cl⁻) environments.

## 1.3 Why SDSS Exists — The Engineering Motivation

The chronological evolution of stainless steels for chloride service is:

$$\text{Austenitic 304} \xrightarrow{\text{Cl}^- \text{ failure}} \text{Austenitic 316L} \xrightarrow{\text{still pits}} \text{Duplex 2205} \xrightarrow{\text{not enough in hot seawater}} \text{Super Duplex 2507} \xrightarrow{\text{extreme service}} \text{Super-Austenitic / Ni-alloys}$$

SDSS fills the **cost-performance gap** between standard 2205 duplex (good but limited to ~70 °C seawater) and the much more expensive nickel-base alloys (Alloy 625, C-276). For most offshore and chemical applications, SDSS offers **~80 % of the corrosion performance at ~40 % of the cost** of nickel-base alternatives.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Physical/Mechanical Metrics to Remember (KTU syllabus-mandated):**
> - Density: **7800 kg/m³**
> - Thermal Expansion Coefficient: **13 × 10⁻⁶ /°C** (lower than austenitic — design advantage)
> - Thermal Conductivity: **15 W/m·K** (higher than austenitic)
> - Yield Strength (0.2 % PS): **≥ 550 MPa**
> - Tensile Strength: **≥ 750 MPa**
> - Elongation: **≥ 25 %**
> - Hardness: **≤ 28 HRC** (mandatory upper limit to avoid HISC/SCC)

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Microstructure: The 50/50 Phase Balance

The two-phase (α + γ) microstructure is the *defining* feature of SDSS. The phase balance is **not arbitrary** — it is engineered by controlling the ratio of ferrite-stabilizers (Cr, Mo, Si) to austenite-stabilizers (Ni, N, C, Mn).

### 2.1.1 The Schaeffler Equivalent Concept

The empirical **Schaeffler (1949) / DeLong equivalent** approach quantifies the phase balance:

$$Cr_{eq} = \%Cr + \%Mo + 1.5 \cdot \%Si + 0.5 \cdot \%Nb$$

$$Ni_{eq} = \%Ni + 30 \cdot \%C + 0.5 \cdot \%Mn + 30 \cdot \%N$$

For SDSS, the **Cr_eq / Ni_eq ratio is engineered to fall within the (α + γ) envelope of the Schaeffler diagram**, targeting a **Ferrite Number (FN) of 40–60** in the as-welded condition (FN measured by Feritscope per AWS A4.2).

> [!WARNING]
> A phase balance drifting **above FN 60** (ferrite-rich) embrittles the alloy and lowers impact toughness. A balance drifting **below FN 40** (austenite-rich) compromises SCC resistance. **KTU examiners love testing this trade-off.**

## 2.2 The Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number (PREN)

PREN is the most important single metric in duplex/super-duplex engineering. The widely accepted formula (per ASTM A923 / G48) is:

$$\boxed{\text{PREN} = \%Cr + 3.3 \cdot \%Mo + 16 \cdot \%N}$$

Some specifications (e.g., NORSOK M-601) use a **tungsten (W) term** for grades like S32760:

$$\text{PREN}_W = \%Cr + 3.3 \cdot (\%Mo + 0.5 \cdot \%W) + 16 \cdot \%N$$

### 2.2.1 PREN Interpretation Table

| PREN Range | Corrosion Class | Typical Service |
|---|---|---|
| 18 – 22 | Basic austenitic (304, 304L) | Indoor, mild atmosphere |
| 23 – 28 | Mo-bearing austenitic (316, 316L) | Marine atmospheric, low-Cl⁻ |
| 30 – 35 | Lean / standard duplex (2304, 2205) | Warm seawater, chemical tanks |
| 38 – 45 | **Super duplex (2507, S32760)** | **Hot seawater, sour service, FGD** |
| > 45 | Super-austenitic / Ni-base (254 SMO, 625) | Extreme halide, HCl, H₂SO₄ |

## 2.3 Critical Pitting Temperature (CPT) and Critical Crevice Temperature (CCT)

CPT and CCT are the temperature thresholds above which pitting/crevice corrosion initiates in a standard **6 % FeCl₃ test (ASTM G48 Practice A/B)**. For SDSS:

$$\text{CPT} \ (\!^\circ\text{C}) \approx 2.5 \cdot \text{PREN} - 50$$

For PREN = 43 → CPT ≈ **57.5 °C** (vs. ≈ 22 °C for 2205 and ≈ 15 °C for 316L).

The empirical relationship for crevice corrosion is stricter:

$$\text{CCT} \ (\!^\circ\text{C}) \approx 1.5 \cdot \text{PREN} - 30$$

For PREN = 43 → CCT ≈ **34.5 °C** (a critical design value for flanged joints and gasketed assemblies in seawater).

## 2.4 Sigma (σ) Phase and Intermetallic Precipitation

SDSS is **highly susceptible to brittle intermetallic phases** when held in the critical temperature range **600 °C – 950 °C** for even a few minutes. The most critical precipitates are:

- **σ phase (Fe-Cr-Mo)** — tetragonal, hard, Cr/Mo-rich → depletion of adjacent matrix in Cr/Mo → loss of corrosion resistance.
- **χ phase (Fe-Cr-Mo)** — similar effect, forms earlier.
- **α′ (475 °C embrittlement)** — spinodal decomposition of ferrite into Cr-rich and Fe-rich BCC zones; occurs at 300–500 °C.
- **Cr₂N / Cr₂₃C₆ carbides** — intergranular precipitation; tied to improper cooling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Time-Temperature-Transformation (TTT) curve for σ-phase in 2507 has its **"C-curve nose" at ≈ 850 °C** with an incubation time of **< 2 minutes**. This is why **post-weld rapid cooling and solution annealing at 1080–1120 °C** are absolutely non-negotiable in KTU-correct answers.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Application | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{PREN} = \%Cr + 3.3 \cdot \%Mo + 16 \cdot \%N$ | Pitting resistance ranking | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\text{PREN}_W = \%Cr + 3.3 \cdot (\%Mo + 0.5 \cdot \%W) + 16 \cdot \%N$ | W-bearing grades (S32760) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $Cr_{eq} = \%Cr + \%Mo + 1.5 \cdot \%Si + 0.5 \cdot \%Nb$ | Ferrite-stabilizer index | wt% |
| 4 | $Ni_{eq} = \%Ni + 30 \cdot \%C + 0.5 \cdot \%Mn + 30 \cdot \%N$ | Austenite-stabilizer index | wt% |
| 5 | $\text{CPT} \approx 2.5 \cdot \text{PREN} - 50$ | Pitting onset temperature | °C |
| 6 | $\text{CCT} \approx 1.5 \cdot \text{PREN} - 30$ | Crevice onset temperature | °C |
| 7 | $FN_{target} = 40 - 60$ (as-welded) | Phase balance control | FN units |
| 8 | Solution anneal: 1080 – 1120 °C / 30 min + water quench | Mandatory heat treatment | °C |
| 9 | Hardness cap: ≤ 28 HRC | HISC prevention | HRC |
| 10 | TTT nose for σ: 850 °C / 2 min | Welding interpass limit | °C / min |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

SDSS is the *workhorse alloy* in:
- **Offshore Oil & Gas:** subsea manifolds, christmas trees, flowlines (NACE MR0175 compliant).
- **Seawater Systems:** MSF desalination evaporators, reverse osmosis pressure vessels, fire-water piping.
- **Flue Gas Desulfurization (FGD):** absorber towers, outlet ducts (Cl⁻-laden, acidic condensates).
- **Pulp & Paper:** digesters, bleach plant equipment.
- **Chemical Tankers:** cargo tanks carrying hot sulfuric / phosphoric acid mixtures.
- **Geothermal:** downhole tubulars in Cl⁻-rich brines at 200–300 °C.

> [!TIP]
> The **lower thermal expansion coefficient (13 × 10⁻⁶/°C)** of SDSS versus austenitic SS (~17 × 10⁻⁶/°C) makes it ideal for **heat exchanger tube sheets** where thermal fatigue dominates the failure mode.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section delivers **complete, exam-style derivations** for the three highest-yield KTU problems on SDSS. Every algebraic step is shown; nothing is abbreviated.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — PREN Computation for SAF 2507

**Given (typical SAF 2507 composition, wt%):**

| Cr | Ni | Mo | N | C | Mn | Si | W |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 25.0 | 7.0 | 4.0 | 0.30 | 0.02 | 0.50 | 0.50 | — |

**Find:** (a) PREN, (b) estimated CPT, (c) estimated CCT.

**Step 1 — State the PREN formula:**

$$
\text{PREN} = \%Cr + 3.3 \cdot \%Mo + 16 \cdot \%N
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$
\text{PREN} = 25.0 + (3.3 \times 4.0) + (16 \times 0.30)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each term separately:**

- Chromium term: $25.0$
- Molybdenum term: $3.3 \times 4.0 = 13.2$
- Nitrogen term: $16 \times 0.30 = 4.8$

**Step 4 — Sum the terms:**

$$
\text{PREN} = 25.0 + 13.2 + 4.8 = 43.0
$$

**Step 5 — State the interpretation:**

> [!NOTE]
> PREN = 43.0 places SAF 2507 firmly in the **"super duplex"** class (PREN > 40). It is suitable for hot chloride-bearing environments up to its CPT/CCT limits.

**Step 6 — Compute CPT using the empirical relation:**

$$
\text{CPT} = 2.5 \cdot \text{PREN} - 50 = 2.5 \times 43.0 - 50 = 107.5 - 50 = 57.5 \ \text{°C}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute CCT using the empirical relation:**

$$
\text{CCT} = 1.5 \cdot \text{PREN} - 30 = 1.5 \times 43.0 - 30 = 64.5 - 30 = 34.5 \ \text{°C}
$$

**Step 8 — Engineering conclusion:**

A SDSS heat exchanger operating in seawater should be designed such that **the metal temperature at the gasket/crevice never exceeds 34.5 °C**, while the **bulk surface temperature must stay below 57.5 °C** to avoid pitting initiation.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Phase Balance Verification via Schaeffler Equivalents

**Given:** The same SAF 2507 composition.

**Find:** Cr_eq, Ni_eq, ratio, and the predicted ferrite fraction.

**Step 1 — Cr_eq formula:**

$$
Cr_{eq} = \%Cr + \%Mo + 1.5 \cdot \%Si + 0.5 \cdot \%Nb
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values (Nb ≈ 0 in 2507):**

$$
Cr_{eq} = 25.0 + 4.0 + (1.5 \times 0.50) + 0 = 25.0 + 4.0 + 0.75 = 29.75
$$

**Step 3 — Ni_eq formula:**

$$
Ni_{eq} = \%Ni + 30 \cdot \%C + 0.5 \cdot \%Mn + 30 \cdot \%N
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute values:**

$$
Ni_{eq} = 7.0 + (30 \times 0.02) + (0.5 \times 0.50) + (30 \times 0.30)
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate each term:**

- Nickel: $7.0$
- Carbon: $30 \times 0.02 = 0.60$
- Manganese: $0.5 \times 0.50 = 0.25$
- Nitrogen: $30 \times 0.30 = 9.00$

**Step 6 — Sum:**

$$
Ni_{eq} = 7.0 + 0.60 + 0.25 + 9.00 = 16.85
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notice the dominant role of nitrogen in austenite stabilization for SDSS.** 30 × N term alone (9.0) exceeds the metallic Ni contribution (7.0). This is why modern super-duplex grades use **interstitial N (0.30 wt%)** as a deliberate, cheap austenite stabilizer.

**Step 7 — Compute the ratio:**

$$
\frac{Cr_{eq}}{Ni_{eq}} = \frac{29.75}{16.85} \approx 1.77
$$

**Step 8 — Compare to Schaeffler diagram (α + γ envelope):**

The (α + γ) envelope of the Schaeffler diagram lies between Cr_eq/Ni_eq ≈ **1.4 and 2.6** at room temperature. A value of 1.77 places 2507 **comfortably in the duplex region** with a predicted ferrite volume fraction of **≈ 50 %** (austenite forms the other 50 %, predominantly at prior ferrite grain boundaries during cooling through 1100–950 °C).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Sigma-Phase Time Constraint During Welding

**Given:** A 12 mm thick SAF 2507 pipe is being welded using GTAW with an interpass temperature controlled at 100 °C. The cooling time between passes from 1100 °C to 600 °C is **75 seconds**.

**Find:** Is there a risk of σ-phase precipitation?

**Step 1 — Identify the critical TTT zone:**

The σ-phase "C-curve nose" for SDSS lies at **T_nose ≈ 850 °C**. The hazardous temperature band is **600 °C – 1000 °C**.

**Step 2 — Estimate the time spent in the hazardous band:**

The heat input per pass (E) is given by:

$$
E = \frac{V \cdot I}{v \times 1000} \ \text{(kJ/mm)}
$$

For a typical hot-pass on 12 mm 2507: V = 12 V, I = 180 A, travel speed v = 100 mm/min.

$$
E = \frac{12 \times 180}{100} = 21.6 \ \text{kJ/mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the cooling-rate approximation (Ion-Szekeres):**

The cooling time from 800 °C to 500 °C (Δt₈/₅) is given by:

$$
\Delta t_{8/5} = \frac{(E / \delta)^2}{2 \cdot \lambda \cdot \rho \cdot c \cdot (1 / (T_1 - T_0) + 1 / (T_2 - T_0))}
$$

A simpler empirical form for SDSS gives the **t₈/₅ per kJ/mm**:

$$
t_{8/5} \approx 4 \cdot \left(\frac{E}{\delta}\right)^2 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{T_{interpass}}\right)
$$

Substituting E = 21.6 kJ/mm, plate thickness δ = 12 mm, T_interpass = 100 °C:

$$
t_{8/5} = 4 \times (21.6 / 12)^2 \times (1 / 100) = 4 \times (1.8)^2 \times 0.01 = 4 \times 3.24 \times 0.01 \approx 0.13 \ \text{min} = 7.8 \ \text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with the TTT nose:**

The minimum incubation time for σ at 850 °C in 2507 is **≈ 90 seconds (1.5 min)**. The cooling time through the dangerous band is only **≈ 7.8 s**, which is **one order of magnitude shorter** than the incubation time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** With proper interpass temperature control (≤ 150 °C) and modest heat input (~20 kJ/mm), the weld HAZ passes through the σ-precipitation band too quickly to nucleate the phase. **Risk is acceptable.**

**Step 5 — Failure-mode check:**

If a welder inadvertently allows the interpass temperature to rise to **250 °C** (a common on-site mistake), t₈/₅ nearly doubles, and the cumulative effect of multiple passes pushes the HAZ into the σ-formation envelope. **This is the #1 KTU-exam pitfall.**

---
