# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Never write "PREN > 40" without showing the substitution.** Examiners award 1 mark each for: (i) formula statement, (ii) numerical substitution, (iii) arithmetic, (iv) interpretation.
> 2. **Confusing CPT and CCT** is a recurring 2-mark deduction. CPT > CCT always (because crevices are more aggressive than free surfaces).
> 3. **Forgetting the 28 HRC hardness cap** for HISC prevention is the single most common 1-mark error in welding questions.
> 4. **Using "stainless" generically** instead of naming the exact grade (SAF 2507, Zeron 100) loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 5. **Stating "σ-phase forms at high temperature" without quantifying (600–950 °C, ≈ 850 °C nose)** is treated as incomplete answer.

---

## Part A — 3 Mark Questions (with Model Answers)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PECHT527, Module 4]`
**Define the term "Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number" (PREN). What is the minimum PREN value required to classify a stainless steel as "super duplex"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> PREN is an empirical, dimensionless index used to rank stainless steels by their resistance to pitting corrosion in chloride-bearing environments.
>
> $$\text{PREN} = \%Cr + 3.3 \cdot \%Mo + 16 \cdot \%N$$
>
> For W-bearing grades: $\text{PREN}_W = \%Cr + 3.3 \cdot (\%Mo + 0.5 \cdot \%W) + 16 \cdot \%N$.
>
> A stainless steel is classified as a **super duplex** when its **PREN > 40** (typically 40–45). This threshold is set by industry specifications such as NORSOK M-601 and ASTM A923 and reflects the alloy's ability to withstand hot seawater and sour-service environments. (3 marks)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECHT527, Module 4]`
**List the four critical microstructural features of super duplex stainless steels and explain why the 50/50 ferrite–austenite balance is so important.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The four critical microstructural features of SDSS are:
> 1. **Phase balance (FN 40–60):** a near-equal volume fraction of ferrite (α-BCC) and austenite (γ-FCC).
> 2. **Absence of intermetallic phases:** no σ, χ, α′ or Cr₂N within the as-supplied/welded metal.
> 3. **Absence of coarse grain boundaries:** fine-grained structure, especially in the HAZ.
> 4. **Low intergranular carbide precipitation:** due to low C content (<0.02 wt%) and N-stabilization.
>
> The 50/50 balance is crucial because:
> - The **ferritic phase** provides **strength** (grain refinement, high YS) and **Cl⁻ SCC resistance** (no FCC slip-step dissolution mechanism).
> - The **austenitic phase** provides **toughness**, **ductility**, and **general corrosion resistance**.
> - Deviation from the balance causes **embrittlement** (ferrite-rich) or **SCC susceptibility** (austenite-rich). (3 marks)

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice: Q-A or Q-B)

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of suitable equations, compute the **PREN, CPT, and CCT** for an SAF 2507 super duplex stainless steel having the composition: 25 % Cr, 7 % Ni, 4 % Mo, 0.30 % N. Comment on its suitability for a heat-exchanger application in **80 °C seawater**.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the **sigma (σ) phase embrittlement** phenomenon in super duplex stainless steels. Include a sketch of the TTT behaviour, identify the temperature range, and outline the heat-treatment procedure to avoid it.

---

#### Model Solution — Q-A (a)

**Step 1 — PREN formula:**

$$
\text{PREN} = \%Cr + 3.3 \cdot \%Mo + 16 \cdot \%N
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute and evaluate:**

$$
\text{PREN} = 25.0 + (3.3 \times 4.0) + (16 \times 0.30) = 25.0 + 13.2 + 4.8 = 43.0
$$

**Step 3 — CPT estimation:**

$$
\text{CPT} = 2.5 \cdot \text{PREN} - 50 = 2.5 \times 43.0 - 50 = 57.5 \ \text{°C}
$$

**Step 4 — CCT estimation:**

$$
\text{CCT} = 1.5 \cdot \text{PREN} - 30 = 1.5 \times 43.0 - 30 = 34.5 \ \text{°C}
$$

**Step 5 — Suitability comment for 80 °C seawater:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Operating temperature (80 °C) is **greater than both the CPT (57.5 °C) and CCT (34.5 °C)** of SAF 2507.
> **Conclusion: UNSUITABLE for direct seawater service at 80 °C on free surfaces** (pitting) and **definitely unsuitable in creviced joints** (gaskets, tube-to-tubesheet) where the local metal temperature is also 80 °C.
>
> **Recommendation:** Either (i) cool the seawater below 30 °C, (ii) upgrade to Alloy 625 (CPT ≈ 75 °C) or Alloy C-276, or (iii) apply cathodic protection with a Zn/Al sacrificial anode. **(7 marks full solution with comment)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q-A (b)

**Definition (1 mark):** Sigma (σ) phase is a hard, brittle, tetragonal Fe-Cr-Mo intermetallic compound (topologically close-packed) that precipitates in the **ferrite matrix** of SDSS when held in the temperature range **600 °C – 950 °C**, with a maximum rate of formation at the **TTT nose ≈ 850 °C**.

**Mechanism (2 marks):** σ phase forms by **eutectoid transformation of δ-ferrite → σ + γ₂ (secondary austenite)**. The reaction is **diffusion-controlled**, requiring the partitioning of Cr and Mo. Because both Cr and Mo are α-stabilizers, their depletion from the matrix reduces the PREN locally to < 30, leading to **catastrophic loss of pitting resistance** in the immediate vicinity of the σ particle. Simultaneously, the σ particles act as **crack-initiation sites**, reducing Charpy impact energy from ≈ 300 J (solution-annealed) to < 50 J in the embrittled state.

**TTT sketch description (1 mark):** A "C-curve" with the nose at (T = 850 °C, t = 90 s). The lower shelf starts at ≈ 600 °C (≈ 1000 s incubation), the upper shelf terminates at ≈ 950 °C (≈ 300 s). Outside this band (T < 600 °C or T > 1000 °C), the incubation time exceeds 10⁴ s and the phase does not form in service-relevant times.

**Heat-treatment remedy (3 marks):**

1. **Solution Anneal:** Heat uniformly to **1080–1120 °C**, hold for **30 min per 25 mm of section thickness** to dissolve all σ and χ particles back into the matrix.
2. **Rapid Quench:** Water-quench within **≤ 60 seconds** of leaving the furnace — this passes the HAZ through the 600–950 °C window too fast to re-precipitate σ.
3. **Avoid Re-heating:** Do not subsequently expose the part to 600–950 °C (e.g., during post-weld stress relief, which is **NOT recommended** for SDSS — use solution anneal instead).
4. **Welding Controls:** Limit heat input to **≤ 25 kJ/mm** and interpass temperature to **≤ 150 °C** to keep the HAZ cooling rate above the σ nose. **(Total 7 marks)**

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare the **corrosion resistance, mechanical properties, and weldability** of super duplex stainless steel (SAF 2507) with standard austenitic stainless steel (AISI 316L). Tabulate your answer.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss in detail the **chloride stress corrosion cracking (Cl-SCC)** behaviour of super duplex stainless steels. Why are they superior to austenitic SS in this regard? What are the operating limits?

---

#### Model Solution — Q-B (a)

**Comparative table (5 marks for thorough tabulation):**

| Property | AISI 316L (Austenitic) | SAF 2507 (Super Duplex) | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cr / Mo / N content | 17 / 2.5 / 0.06 | 25 / 4.0 / 0.30 | **2507** |
| PREN | ≈ 26 | ≈ 43 | **2507** |
| 0.2 % Yield Strength | 220 MPa | **550 MPa** | **2507** (2.5× stronger) |
| Tensile Strength | 500 MPa | **750 MPa** | **2507** |
| Elongation (%) | 40 | 25 | 316L (more ductile) |
| Pitting (CPT) | ≈ 15 °C | **≈ 57 °C** | **2507** |
| Cl-SCC resistance | Poor (sensitive ≥ 60 °C) | **Excellent (sensitive ≥ 150 °C)** | **2507** |
| Thermal expansion | 17 × 10⁻⁶/°C | **13 × 10⁻⁶/°C** | **2507** |
| Thermal conductivity | 15 W/m·K | **15 W/m·K** | Tie |
| Weldability | Excellent, no PWHT needed | Good, **strict procedure control** (heat input, interpass) | 316L (easier) |
| Magnetic response | Non-magnetic | **Magnetic** | App-dependent |
| Cost (relative) | 1.0× | **2.0×** | 316L (cheaper) |

**Engineering summary comment (2 marks):**

> SAF 2507 is the *superior alloy* for **chloride-bearing, high-pressure, and high-temperature** service (subsea, FGD, hot brine), where its 2.5× higher strength allows **thinner wall, lighter-weight** design and its PREN-43 corrosion resistance eliminates the need for cathodic protection. 316L remains the *economical choice* for low-chloride, low-temperature, cryogenic, or post-weld-fabrication-heavy service where its superior formability and weldability outweigh the corrosion-performance deficit. **(7 marks total)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q-B (b)

**Mechanism of Cl-SCC in austenitic SS (2 marks):** In austenitic stainless steels (304, 316), chloride ions penetrate the passive film at slip-step emergence points on {111} planes. The combination of **tensile stress + Cl⁻ + temperature ≥ 60 °C** initiates transgranular SCC. The cracks propagate via film-rupture/anodic-dissolution at the crack tip, leading to sudden brittle failure at stresses far below the yield strength.

**Why SDSS resists Cl-SCC (3 marks):**

1. **BCC ferrite phase lacks the {111} slip-step mechanism** that drives austenitic transgranular SCC. The crack cannot propagate easily through a ferritic matrix.
2. **High Cr and Mo in solid solution** stabilise the passive film, raising the threshold Cl⁻ concentration required for film breakdown by an order of magnitude.
3. **Nitrogen enrichment (0.30 wt%)** of the austenite phase shifts the austenite to a more noble corrosion potential, and N itself forms NH₄⁺ at the crack tip, **buffering the local pH drop** and inhibiting H⁺-driven crack growth.
4. **Duplex grain structure** deflects and bifurcates cracks, dissipating the stress intensity at the crack tip.

**Operating limits (2 marks):**

- SDSS is **immune to Cl-SCC at temperatures below ≈ 150 °C** in the absence of H₂S.
- Above 150 °C, susceptibility to **Hydrogen-Induced Stress Cracking (HISC)** under cathodic protection must be evaluated. **NACE MR0175 / ISO 15156** limits SDSS use in sour service to **< 60 °C** and imposes the **≤ 28 HRC hardness cap** to prevent HISC.
- For **subsea cathodically-protected SDSS components**, the maximum design stress is limited to **≤ 80 % of SMYS** and the operating temperature to **≤ 60 °C in sour (H₂S) service** per NORSOK. **(7 marks total)**

---

## 5.X Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **SDSS = 25Cr-7Ni-4Mo-0.3N**, ferritic-austenitic ≈ 50/50, **PREN > 40**.
- **UNS S32750 (SAF 2507)** and **UNS S32760 (Zeron 100)** are the two reference super-duplex grades.
- **PREN formula (must memorise):** $\text{PREN} = \%Cr + 3.3 \cdot \%Mo + 16 \cdot \%N$.
- **CPT > CCT always**; empirical relations: $\text{CPT} \approx 2.5 \cdot \text{PREN} - 50$, $\text{CCT} \approx 1.5 \cdot \text{PREN} - 30$.
- **Schaeffler equivalent:** $Cr_{eq} = \%Cr + \%Mo + 1.5 \cdot \%Si + 0.5 \cdot \%Nb$; $Ni_{eq} = \%Ni + 30 \cdot \%C + 0.5 \cdot \%Mn + 30 \cdot \%N$.
- **Phase balance (Ferrite Number):** **FN 40–60** in the as-welded condition is the universally accepted acceptance window.
- **σ-phase danger window:** **600–950 °C**, with the **TTT nose at ≈ 850 °C / 90 s** in 2507. Avoid prolonged exposure.
- **Mandatory heat treatment:** **Solution anneal at 1080–1120 °C** (≥ 30 min) + **water quench within 60 s**.
- **Welding interpass temperature:** **≤ 150 °C**; **heat input:** **≤ 25 kJ/mm**; **no PWHT** (post-weld heat treatment) — it is detrimental.
- **Hardness cap: ≤ 28 HRC** — non-negotiable for HISC prevention in sour / cathodically protected service.
- **Nitrogen (0.30 wt%)** is the *intentional* cheap austenite-stabiliser in modern SDSS; it dominates the Ni_eq term ($30 \times \%N$).
- **CPT and CCT** of SAF 2507: **57.5 °C and 34.5 °C respectively** — for direct seawater service design, CCT is the binding constraint.
- **Lower thermal expansion (13 × 10⁻⁶/°C)** makes SDSS ideal for heat-exchanger tube sheets, where thermal-fatigue life scales inversely with CTE.
- **Cl-SCC resistance:** SDSS is **immune below ≈ 150 °C**; in sour service the limit drops to **≤ 60 °C** per NACE MR0175.
- **Mechanical strength:** YS ≈ **550 MPa** (≈ 2.5× that of 316L) → enables thinner walls, weight savings, higher design pressures.
- **Common KTU mistake to avoid:** never confuse "phase balance" (metallurgical, FN units) with "PREN" (corrosion, dimensionless). They are *different metrics* measuring *different attributes*.
- **NORSOK M-601, NACE MR0175, ASTM A923, ASTM G48** are the four key industry specifications to cite in descriptive answers for full credit.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ceramics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT527 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ceramics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Ceramics** are inorganic, non-metallic solids composed of metallic and non-metallic elements (e.g., Al, Si, O, N, C) held together primarily by **ionic bonds** and/or **covalent bonds**. In the context of corrosion engineering, ceramics are classified as one of the three principal families of engineering materials (alongside metals and polymers) and are prized for their exceptional resistance to chemical attack, high-temperature stability, and hardness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECHT527 Module 4):**
> Ceramics are polycrystalline inorganic non-metallic materials, generally compounds of a metal with a non-metal (typically oxygen, nitrogen, or carbon), which exhibit a combination of high hardness, high melting point, chemical inertness, and excellent corrosion resistance in aggressive media (acids, alkalis, molten salts, and oxidizing atmospheres).

### 1.2 Classification of Ceramics

| Class | Examples | Key Characteristic |
|---|---|---|
| **Traditional Ceramics** | Silica (SiO₂), Alumina (Al₂O₃), Magnesia (MgO), Porcelain, Fireclay | Made from natural raw materials (clay, silicates) |
| **Advanced / Engineering Ceramics** | SiC, Si₃N₄, ZrO₂, B₄C, AlN | Synthesized from high-purity chemicals; tailored properties |
| **Glasses** | Soda-lime glass, Borosilicate, Fused silica | Non-crystalline (amorphous) ceramics |
| **Glass-Ceramics** | Zerodur®, Pyroceram® | Partially crystallized glasses |
| **Cermets** | WC-Co, TiC-Ni, Al₂O₃-Cr | Ceramic-metal composites |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:**
> Imagine a brick wall — the bricks are tightly interlocked and bonded with strong mortar. That's what a ceramic is at the atomic level: positively charged metal ions (like Al³⁺) and negatively charged non-metal ions (like O²⁻) packed tightly together by strong electrostatic "mortar." Because the bonds are so strong, hostile chemicals (acids, salts, oxygen) have a very hard time prying the ions apart — which is exactly why ceramics **don't rust** the way iron does.
> 
> The trade-off? That same tight, rigid bonding makes ceramics **brittle** — like a wall that doesn't corrode, but shatters if you hit it with a hammer. Metals, by contrast, are like a chain-link fence (ductile but prone to rusting).

### 1.4 Bonding and Structure — The Foundation of Ceramic Behavior

Ceramics exhibit two primary bonding types:

1. **Ionic Bonding** — Electron transfer between metal and non-metal (e.g., NaCl, MgO, Al₂O₃). This gives ceramics high melting points and poor electrical conductivity in the solid state.
2. **Covalent Bonding** — Electron sharing (e.g., SiC, Si₃N₄, diamond). Yields directional bonds, extreme hardness, and high thermal stability.

**Crystallographic Implications:**
- Most ceramics are **polycrystalline** (many small crystals/grains bonded at grain boundaries).
- Some are **amorphous** (glasses — no long-range atomic order).
- Grain boundaries in ceramics are regions of slight disorder but generally *less reactive* than in metals — contributing to corrosion resistance.
- Common crystal structures: **Rock salt (NaCl-type)**, **Fluorite (CaF₂-type)**, **Perovskite (CaTiO₃-type)**, **Spinel (MgAl₂O₄-type)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for Corrosion Engineers:**
> The strong directional covalent/ionic bonds in ceramics mean there are essentially **no free electrons** to participate in the electrochemical reactions that drive metallic corrosion. This is *the* fundamental reason ceramics are corrosion-resistant.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Bonding Mechanics — Why Ceramics Resist Corrosion

The corrosion behavior of ceramics is governed by the **thermodynamic stability** of the metal-oxygen (or metal-nitrogen/carbon) bond. For an oxide ceramic MₐOᵦ:

$$
\Delta G_f^\circ(\text{M}_a\text{O}_b) = \text{strongly negative}
$$

- A **large negative Gibbs free energy of formation (ΔG°f)** implies the ceramic is thermodynamically stable and resistant to further oxidation or chemical attack.
- This is quantified by the **Ellingham Diagram**, where ceramic oxides (Al₂O₃, SiO₂, MgO, Cr₂O₃) lie far below the lines for most metals — confirming their superior stability.

### 2.2 Mechanism of Ceramic "Corrosion"

Unlike metals, ceramics do **not** undergo electrochemical corrosion (no electron transfer, no anodes/cathodes). Instead, ceramic degradation occurs via:

| Mode | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Chemical Attack / Dissolution** | Direct reaction with acids, alkalis, or molten salts | SiO₂ attacked by HF; Al₂O₃ attacked by concentrated H₃PO₄ |
| **Oxidative Degradation** | High-temperature oxidation in O₂ atmosphere | SiC → SiO₂ (protective scale forms) |
| **Molten Salt Corrosion** | Reaction with fused salts at high T | Al₂O₃ attacked by molten Na₂SO₄ |
| **Thermal Shock Failure** | Cracking due to rapid temperature change (not chemical, but functional failure) | Si₃N₄ cracking on rapid quenching |
| **Stress Corrosion Cracking (limited)** | Static fatigue in glass due to water vapor (slow crack growth) | Optical fibers, glass vessels |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:**
> For ceramic materials, the relevant "corrosion rate" is typically expressed in **mg/cm²·hr** or **μm/year** of material loss — orders of magnitude lower than for metals in the same environment.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All quantities in SI units. Mark these as **board-exam essentials**.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Gibbs Free Energy of Formation | $\Delta G_f^\circ = \Delta H_f^\circ - T\Delta S_f^\circ$ | $\Delta G_f^\circ$ (J/mol), $\Delta H_f^\circ$ (J/mol), $T$ (K), $\Delta S_f^\circ$ (J/mol·K) | Predicts thermodynamic stability of ceramic |
| 2 | Linear Corrosion Rate (Ceramic) | $CR = \frac{\Delta m}{\rho \cdot A \cdot t}$ | $CR$ (μm/yr), $\Delta m$ (g), $\rho$ (g/cm³), $A$ (cm²), $t$ (hr) | Mass-loss method for ceramic dissolution |
| 3 | Pilling-Bedworth Ratio (PBR) | $PBR = \frac{V_{oxide}}{V_{metal}} = \frac{M_{oxide} \cdot \rho_{metal}}{n \cdot M_{metal} \cdot \rho_{oxide}}$ | $M$ = molar mass (g/mol), $\rho$ = density (g/cm³), $n$ = moles of metal per mole oxide | Protective vs. non-protective oxide scale |
| 4 | Thermal Shock Resistance Parameter | $R = \frac{\sigma_f (1-\nu)}{E \alpha}$ | $\sigma_f$ = fracture strength (Pa), $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio, $E$ = Young's modulus (Pa), $\alpha$ = CTE (K⁻¹) | Predicts ceramic resistance to thermal shock |
| 5 | Activation Energy (Corrosion) | $k = A \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | $k$ = rate constant, $A$ = pre-exponential, $E_a$ (J/mol), $R$ = 8.314 J/mol·K | Arrhenius form for ceramic dissolution kinetics |
| 6 | Density of Ceramic (theoretical) | $\rho = \frac{n M}{N_A V_{cell}}$ | $n$ = atoms/unit cell, $M$ = molar mass, $N_A$ = 6.022×10²³, $V_{cell}$ (cm³) | Crystal structure calculation |
| 7 | Porosity Effect on Strength | $\sigma = \sigma_0 \exp(-bP)$ | $\sigma$ = strength with porosity $P$, $b$ = constant, $P$ = volume fraction porosity | Ryshkewitch-Duckworth equation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE (for KTU answer scripts):**
> In your exam, write the **Ellingham diagram analysis** with at least two oxide examples (e.g., Al₂O₃ and SiO₂) and link their ΔG° position to corrosion resistance. This is a **recurring 7-mark question pattern**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Sector | Ceramic Used | Corrosion-Resistance Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Chemical Process Industry** | Al₂O₃, SiC | Pump seals, valve seats, heat-exchanger tubes handling HCl, H₂SO₄ |
| **Aerospace & Turbine Blades** | Si₃N₄, ZrO₂ (YSZ) | Combustion environments up to 1400 °C; thermal barrier coatings |
| **Nuclear Reactors** | UO₂, SiC, ZrO₂ | Fuel pellets, cladding, control rods |
| **Electronics** | AlN, Al₂O₃ (substrates) | Hermetic packaging resistant to moisture & chemicals |
| **Medical Implants** | Al₂O₃, ZrO₂ (bioceramics) | Biocompatible, corrosion-proof hip & dental implants |
| **Cutting Tools** | Al₂O₃, Si₃N₄, cermets (WC-Co) | Machining fluids, hot chip environment |
| **Protective Coatings** | ZrO₂, Cr₂O₃, Al₂O₃ | Thermal-sprayed or CVD coatings on metals |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Pilling-Bedworth Ratio (PBR) for Protective Oxide Scales

The **Pilling-Bedworth Ratio (PBR)** is the cornerstone of high-temperature oxidation analysis — it tells us whether the oxide scale formed on a metal (or analogously, the stability of a ceramic oxide) will be **protective** or **non-protective**.

**Starting Equation:**

$$
PBR \;=\; \frac{V_{\text{oxide produced per mole of metal}}}{V_{\text{metal consumed per mole of metal}}
$$

**Step 1 — Express volumes in terms of molar mass and density:**

$$
V_{\text{oxide}} \;=\; \frac{M_{\text{oxide}}}{\rho_{\text{oxide}}}, \qquad V_{\text{metal}} \;=\; \frac{M_{\text{metal}}}{\rho_{\text{metal}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Account for stoichiometry. For a general reaction:**

$$
a\,\text{M}_{(s)} \;+\; b\,\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{M}_a\text{O}_b
$$

The number of moles of metal consumed per mole of oxide = $a$. Thus the volume of metal consumed per mole of oxide is:

$$
V_{\text{metal (per mole oxide)}} \;=\; \frac{a \cdot M_{\text{metal}}}{\rho_{\text{metal}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the PBR definition:**

$$
PBR \;=\; \frac{V_{\text{oxide}}}{V_{\text{metal (per mole oxide)}}} \;=\; \frac{M_{\text{oxide}} \,/\, \rho_{\text{oxide}}}{a\, M_{\text{metal}} \,/\, \rho_{\text{metal}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Final Working Formula:**

$$
\boxed{\,PBR \;=\; \frac{M_{\text{oxide}} \cdot \rho_{\text{metal}}}{a \cdot M_{\text{metal}} \cdot \rho_{\text{oxide}}}\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation Rule (must memorize for KTU):**

| PBR Range | Scale Behavior | Example |
|---|---|---|
| PBR < 1 | **Non-protective** — tensile stress, porous, scale cracks off | Mg (PBR ≈ 0.81), Na, K |
| 1 < PBR < 2 | **Protective** — compressive stress, adherent, blocking | Al (PBR ≈ 1.28), Cr (1.99), Si (2.15) |
| PBR > 2 | **Non-protective** — compressive spalling, scale buckles | Fe (PBR ≈ 2.15, borderline), Ni |

> [!NOTE]
> **Ceramic Connection:**
> The PBR concept explains *why* Al₂O₃, Cr₂O₃, and SiO₂ are the **most common protective ceramic scales** on heat-resistant alloys — all have PBR values in the protective 1–2 range.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Thermal Shock Resistance Parameter

Ceramics used in high-temperature service (e.g., turbine components) must survive rapid heating/cooling. The **thermal shock resistance parameter R** is derived from basic stress-strain considerations.

**Step 1 — A temperature change ΔT causes a free thermal strain:**

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{th}} \;=\; \alpha \cdot \Delta T
$$

where $\alpha$ is the coefficient of thermal expansion (K⁻¹).

**Step 2 — If the ceramic is constrained (e.g., bonded to a substrate or internally by cooler regions), this strain is converted into elastic stress:**

$$
\sigma \;=\; E \cdot \varepsilon_{\text{th}} \;=\; E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T
$$

where $E$ is Young's modulus (Pa).

**Step 3 — The ceramic survives as long as $\sigma$ does not exceed its fracture strength $\sigma_f$. Equating the two:**

$$
\sigma_f \;=\; E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T_{\text{crit}}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the maximum allowable temperature change (critical thermal shock):**

$$
\Delta T_{\text{crit}} \;=\; \frac{\sigma_f}{E \cdot \alpha}
$$

**Step 5 — The standard "first thermal shock resistance parameter" R is defined for biaxial stress state, introducing Poisson's ratio $\nu$:**

$$
\boxed{\,R \;=\; \frac{\sigma_f\,(1-\nu)}{E \cdot \alpha}\,}
$$

**Step 6 — A second, more practical parameter accounts for thermal conductivity $k$ (W/m·K), which allows heat to dissipate before stress builds up:**

$$
\boxed{\,R' \;=\; \frac{\sigma_f\,(1-\nu)}{E \cdot \alpha} \cdot k\,}
$$

**Step 7 — Engineering Interpretation:**

- **High $\sigma_f$** → better resistance (stronger material).
- **Low $E$** → better resistance (less stiff → less stress for a given strain).
- **Low $\alpha$** → better resistance (less dimensional change with temperature).
- **High $k$** → better heat dissipation → less thermal gradient → less stress.

| Ceramic | $\sigma_f$ (MPa) | $E$ (GPa) | $\alpha$ (×10⁻⁶/K) | $R$ (°C, approx.) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Al₂O₃ | 300 | 380 | 8.0 | ~90 |
| SiC | 500 | 410 | 4.5 | ~244 |
| Si₃N₄ | 700 | 310 | 3.2 | ~595 |
| ZrO₂ (YSZ) | 600 | 200 | 10.0 | ~240 |

> Si₃N₄ scores highest — explaining its dominance in **high-shock ceramic applications** like automotive engine components and gas turbines.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Linear Corrosion Rate from Mass Loss

For a ceramic coupon exposed to a corrosive medium, the rate is computed by the **mass-loss method**.

**Step 1 — Mass loss per unit area per unit time:**

$$
\text{Mass loss rate} \;=\; \frac{\Delta m}{A \cdot t}
$$

Units: g/(cm²·hr) or kg/(m²·s).

**Step 2 — Convert mass loss to thickness loss using density $\rho$:**

$$
\text{Thickness loss} \;=\; \frac{\Delta m}{\rho \cdot A}
$$

Units: cm or μm.

**Step 3 — Convert to a rate (per year) using a unit-conversion factor $K$:**

$$
CR \;=\; \frac{\Delta m \cdot K}{\rho \cdot A \cdot t}
$$

**Step 4 — Standard KTU value of K (mm/yr conversion):**

If $\Delta m$ is in mg, $A$ in cm², $\rho$ in g/cm³, and $t$ in hr:

$$
K \;=\; 8.76 \times 10^4 \quad \text{(to convert cm/hr → mm/yr)}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Working Formula:**

$$
\boxed{\,CR \;(\text{mm/yr}) \;=\; \frac{8.76 \times 10^4 \,\cdot\, \Delta m}{\rho \cdot A \cdot t}\,}
$$

where $\Delta m$ is in mg, $\rho$ in g/cm³, $A$ in cm², $t$ in hr.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Ceramic Selection for Corrosive Environments

| Environment | Recommended Ceramic | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **HF (Hydrofluoric Acid)** | SiC, Si₃N₄ (NOT silica) | HF attacks SiO₂; SiC forms protective SiO₂ but is kinetically slower |
| **HCl, H₂SO₄ (hot)** | Al₂O₃, SiC | Stable in reducing acid environments |
| **HNO₃ (hot concentrated)** | Al₂O₃, ZrO₂ | Excellent oxidative acid resistance |
| **Hot NaOH / KOH** | SiC, ZrO₂ (NOT SiO₂, Al₂O₃) | Al₂O₃ & SiO₂ dissolve in alkalis |
| **Molten Metals** | Al₂O₃, BN | Wetting resistance |
| **Molten Salts (sulfates, chlorides)** | MgO, Al₂O₃ (above 700 °C) | Inertness to ionic melts |
| **Oxidizing Atmospheres (1000 °C+)** | SiC, Si₃N₄, Al₂O₃ | Form protective SiO₂ or Al₂O₃ scale |
| **Reducing Atmospheres (high T)** | SiC, Si₃N₄ | Stable; some oxide ceramics reduce |
| **Steam / High-Pressure Water** | Al₂O₃, ZrO₂ | Used in nuclear and boiler applications |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Take-Away for KTU:**
> **NO single ceramic is universally corrosion-proof.** Selection must be based on the specific environment. This is a frequent board question.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Classification of Ceramics in Corrosion Engineering

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CERAMIC MATERIALS"] --> B["Traditional Ceramics"]
    A --> C["Advanced Engineering Ceramics"]
    A --> D["Glasses"]
    A --> E["Glass Ceramics"]
    A --> F["Cermets Ceramic Metal Composites"]

    B --> B1["Silica SiO2"]
    B --> B2["Alumina Al2O3"]
    B --> B3["Magnesia MgO"]
    B --> B4["Porcelain and Fireclay"]

    C --> C1["Silicon Carbide SiC"]
    C --> C2["Silicon Nitride Si3N4"]
    C --> C3["Zirconia ZrO2"]
    C --> C4["Boron Carbide B4C"]
    C --> C5["Aluminum Nitride AlN"]

    D --> D1["Soda Lime Glass"]
    D --> D2["Borosilicate Glass"]
    D --> D3["Fused Silica"]

    E --> E1["Zerodur"]
    E --> E2["Pyroceram"]

    F --> F1["Tungsten Carbide Cobalt WC Co"]
    F --> F2["Titanium Carbide Nickel TiC Ni"]
    F --> F3["Alumina Chromium Al2O3 Cr"]

    A --> G["Bonding Types"]
    G --> G1["Ionic Bonds Al2O3 MgO"]
    G --> G2["Covalent Bonds SiC Si3N4"]
    G --> G3["Mixed Ionic Covalent"]

    A --> H["Corrosion Mechanism"]
    H --> H1["Chemical Dissolution in Acids or Alkalis"]
    H --> H2["High Temperature Oxidation"]
    H --> H3["Molten Salt Attack"]
    H --> H4["Thermal Shock Fracture"]

    H1 --> I1["Low corrosion rate due to absence of free electrons"]
    H2 --> I2["Forms protective oxide scales PBR between 1 and 2"]
    H3 --> I3["Resistant but not immune at very high temperatures"]
    H4 --> I4["Governed by Thermal Shock Parameter R"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow — Mechanism of Ceramic Degradation in Acidic Media

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Step 1: Acidic species H plus diffuse to ceramic surface"] --> S2["Step 2: Chemisorption on active surface sites grain boundaries or pores"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: Selective leaching of one cation Example Mg2 plus from MgO leaves porous residue"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4: Layer-by-layer dissolution mass loss measurable in mg per cm2 per hour"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5: Possible formation of a protective reaction product film Example SiO2 from SiC"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 6: Steady state corrosion rate governed by slowest of diffusion through boundary layer OR reaction at interface"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Block — Ceramics vs. Metals vs. Polymers (Corrosion Perspective)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1["Material Family"] --> M2["Metals Fe Cu Al Ti"]
    M1 --> M3["Ceramics Al2O3 SiC ZrO2"]
    M1 --> M4["Polymers PVC PTFE PE"]

    M2 --> P2a["Mechanism: Electrochemical anodic dissolution plus cathodic reduction"]
    M2 --> P2b["Vulnerability: Susceptible to pitting crevice and stress corrosion cracking"]
    M2 --> P2c["Typical Rate: 0.1 to 10 mm per year in aggressive media"]

    M3 --> P3a["Mechanism: Direct chemical attack or dissolution no electron transfer"]
    M3 --> P3b["Vulnerability: Brittle fracture and selective leaching"]
    M3 --> P3c["Typical Rate: less than 0.001 mm per year often considered inert"]

    M4 --> P4a["Mechanism: Swelling oxidation and solvent penetration"]
    M4 --> P4b["Vulnerability: UV degradation and chemical permeation"]
    M4 --> P4c["Typical Rate: Variable highly environment dependent"]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block — Selection Logic for a Ceramic Lining in a Corrosive Service

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["Identify Corrosive Medium"] --> Q2{"Aqueous Acidic or Alkaline"}
    Q1 --> Q3{"High Temperature Oxidative"}
    Q1 --> Q4{"Molten Salt or Metal"}

    Q2 --> Q5["Pick Al2O3 for acid except HF or hot H3PO4"]
    Q2 --> Q6["Pick SiC for HF containing streams"]
    Q2 --> Q7["Pick ZrO2 or SiC for strong hot alkalis"]

    Q3 --> Q8["Pick SiC or Si3N4 up to 1400 C with SiO2 scale"]
    Q3 --> Q9["Pick YSZ ZrO2 as thermal barrier coating"]

    Q4 --> Q10["Pick MgO or Al2O3 for molten chloride and sulfate salts"]
    Q4 --> Q11["Pick BN for molten aluminum and zinc non wetting"]

    Q5 --> R1["Verify with mass loss coupon test"]
    Q6 --> R1
    Q7 --> R1
    Q8 --> R1
    Q9 --> R1
    Q10 --> R1
    Q11 --> R1
    R1 --> R2["Final Specification Including Thermal Shock Parameter R and Pilling Bedworth Ratio"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define ceramics. Why are they considered corrosion-resistant compared to metals?*

**Model Answer:**

Ceramics are inorganic, non-metallic materials composed of metallic and non-metallic elements (typically oxides, nitrides, or carbides) bonded primarily by ionic and/or covalent bonds. Examples include Al₂O₃, SiC, ZrO₂, and Si₃N₄.

**Reasons for superior corrosion resistance over metals:**

1. **No free electrons** — ceramics lack the mobile electron cloud required for electrochemical corrosion (anodic dissolution + cathodic reduction). Hence the classical galvanic/pitting/crevice mechanisms do not apply.
2. **High bond strength** — strong ionic/covalent bonds must be broken to release ions into solution; this requires very high activation energy.
3. **Thermodynamic stability** — most ceramic oxides have large negative ΔG°f values, indicating spontaneous formation and resistance to further reaction (refer Ellingham diagram).
4. **Stable passive scale** — the ceramic itself *is* a stable oxide scale; the protective layer is the bulk material.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is the Pilling-Bedworth Ratio? State its significance for ceramic oxide scales.*

**Model Answer:**

The **Pilling-Bedworth Ratio (PBR)** is defined as the ratio of the volume of oxide formed to the volume of metal consumed during oxidation:

$$
PBR \;=\; \frac{V_{\text{oxide}}}{V_{\text{metal}}} \;=\; \frac{M_{\text{oxide}} \cdot \rho_{\text{metal}}}{a \cdot M_{\text{metal}} \cdot \rho_{\text{oxide}}}
$$

**Significance for ceramics:**

- **PBR < 1** → scale is porous, non-protective (e.g., MgO on Mg).
- **1 < PBR < 2** → **protective scale** — compressive stress, adherent, blocks further oxidation. This range includes the most useful ceramic scales: Al₂O₃ (PBR ≈ 1.28), Cr₂O₃ (≈ 1.99), SiO₂ (≈ 2.15).
- **PBR > 2** → scale spalls off due to high compressive stress (e.g., Fe₂O₃ on Fe).

The principle is used to **design heat-resistant alloys** by promoting the formation of Al₂O₃, Cr₂O₃, or SiO₂ ceramic scales.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Provided — Answer ANY ONE of the following:**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Classify ceramic materials used in corrosion-resistant applications. Discuss the role of **bonding type (ionic vs. covalent)** in determining their corrosion behavior. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **mechanism of corrosion of ceramics** in aggressive environments. Compare and contrast it with the corrosion of metals. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of ceramics and role of bonding:**

**Classification:**

| Category | Examples | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| Traditional | SiO₂, Al₂O₃, MgO, porcelain | Refractories, insulators |
| Advanced | SiC, Si₃N₄, ZrO₂, B₄C | Aerospace, chemical, nuclear |
| Glasses | Soda-lime, borosilicate | Labware, optical |
| Glass-ceramics | Zerodur, Pyroceram | Cooktops, telescope mirrors |
| Cermets | WC-Co, TiC-Ni | Cutting tools, rocket nozzles |

**Role of bonding type:**

- **Ionic ceramics** (e.g., NaCl-type MgO, Al₂O₃): The bond is non-directional and electrostatic. The high lattice energy confers high melting point and chemical inertness. However, ionic bonds are susceptible to **polar attack** by water and polar solvents (e.g., hydrolysis: Al₂O₃ + H⁺ → Al³⁺ + H₂O). Al₂O₃ is stable in acidic oxidizing media but dissolves in strong alkalis.
- **Covalent ceramics** (e.g., SiC, Si₃N₄, diamond): The bond is highly directional, conferring extreme hardness and chemical inertness. They are less susceptible to polar attack but are attacked by specific reagents (e.g., SiO₂ + 6 HF → H₂SiF₆ + 2 H₂O; SiC + 2 NaOH + O₂ → Na₂SiO₃ + CO₂).
- **Mixed bonding** (e.g., Si₃N₄: ~70% covalent, 30% ionic) provides a balance of properties.

The **absence of free electrons** in both cases means the **electrochemical corrosion mechanism is impossible** — this is the unifying corrosion-resistance principle for all ceramics.

**(b) Mechanism of ceramic corrosion and comparison with metals:**

**Ceramic Corrosion Mechanisms:**

1. **Pure Chemical Dissolution:**
   - Example: MgO + 2 H⁺ → Mg²⁺ + H₂O. The oxide lattice dissolves ion-by-ion.
   - Rate controlled by: surface reaction kinetics or diffusion through the boundary layer.
   - Often follows Arrhenius behavior: $k = A \exp(-E_a/RT)$.

2. **Selective Leaching:**
   - One cation is preferentially removed, leaving a porous, weakened skeleton.
   - Example: leaching of Ca²⁺ from calcium-aluminate cements by acid.

3. **High-Temperature Oxidation:**
   - Ceramics like SiC, Si₃N₄ oxidize to form **SiO₂** protective scale:
     $$ \text{SiC} + \tfrac{3}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{SiO}_2 + \text{CO}_2 $$
   - The SiO₂ layer is viscous, adherent, and self-healing — providing excellent protection.

4. **Molten Salt Attack:**
   - Molten Na₂SO₄ dissolves the protective Al₂O₃ scale on alloys via acid-base fluxing:
     $$ \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow 2\,\text{NaAlO}_2 + \text{SO}_3 $$

5. **Thermal Shock Failure:**
   - A purely mechanical failure mode driven by thermal stress (not chemical), characterized by the thermal shock parameter R.

**Comparison with Metallic Corrosion:**

| Aspect | Metals | Ceramics |
|---|---|---|
| Driving force | Electrochemical (anode + cathode) | Pure chemical reaction |
| Free electrons | Abundant — enable electron transfer | Absent — no electrochemical pathway |
| Anode/cathode sites | Required (grain boundaries, inclusions, etc.) | Not applicable |
| Typical rate | 0.1–10 mm/yr in aggressive media | < 0.001 mm/yr (often considered inert) |
| Failure mode | Pitting, SCC, intergranular, uniform | Dissolution, leaching, thermal shock |
| Protective strategy | Add inhibitors, coatings, cathodic protection | Select inherently resistant ceramic; control T |
| Role of environment | Electrolyte is essential | Solid-state reactions, molten salts, hot gases |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** What are **cermets**? Explain the role of the metallic binder phase in **WC-Co** cermet with respect to its corrosion and mechanical behavior. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the application of **ceramic coatings** (especially thermal-barrier coatings, TBCs) in protecting metals from high-temperature corrosion. Explain why **yttria-stabilized zirconia (YSZ)** is the preferred TBC material. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Cermets — Definition and WC-Co analysis:**

**Definition:**
Cermets (ceramic + metal) are composite materials combining a hard ceramic phase (e.g., carbide, oxide, nitride) with a ductile metallic binder phase. The ceramic provides hardness, wear resistance, and chemical inertness; the metal provides toughness, thermal shock resistance, and sintering aid.

**WC-Co System:**

- **Ceramic phase:** Tungsten Carbide (WC) — hexagonal, extremely hard (~2200 HV), chemically inert, refractory (melts above 2700 °C).
- **Binder phase:** Cobalt (Co, typically 6–15 wt%) — acts as a glue, dissolves WC slightly at sintering T (~1400 °C) and re-precipitates on cooling, forming a strong metallurgical bond.

**Role of Co binder in corrosion behavior:**

1. **Galvanic vulnerability:** Co is the **weak link** in corrosion — it is the preferred site for electrochemical attack because:
   - Co is anodic compared to WC in many electrolytes.
   - Galvanic microcells form: WC (cathode) – Co (anode).
   - Result: **selective dissolution of Co**, leaving behind a porous WC skeleton.
2. **Binder leaching in acidic media:**
   $$ \text{Co} + 2\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{CoCl}_2 + \text{H}_2 $$
3. **Mitigation strategies:**
   - Use of **Ni or Fe** binders (more corrosion-resistant in some media).
   - Adding **Cr** to the binder to form a passive Cr₂O₃ layer.
   - Using **TiC-Ni** or **Ti(C,N)-Ni** cermets for better corrosion resistance in chemical industry.
   - Applying **PVD/CVD coatings** (TiN, CrN, Al₂O₃) on the cermet surface.

**Mechanical role of Co binder:**

- Co absorbs crack-tip energy via **plastic deformation**, providing fracture toughness (K_IC ≈ 10–15 MPa·m^(1/2), vs. pure ceramic ~3–5).
- Allows WC-Co to be used in **impact-loaded** applications (rock drilling, cutting tools interrupted cuts).

**(b) Ceramic Coatings and Thermal Barrier Coatings (TBCs):**

**Definition:**
A **ceramic coating** is a thin (50 µm – 1 mm) layer of ceramic applied to a metal substrate to provide thermal insulation, wear resistance, oxidation resistance, or corrosion resistance — without changing the bulk properties of the underlying metal.

**Types of ceramic coatings:**

| Type | Method | Thickness | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Thermal Barrier Coating (TBC)** | Electron-beam PVD, plasma spray | 100–500 µm | YSZ on turbine blades |
| **Wear-resistant coating** | CVD, PVD | 1–10 µm | TiN, CrN, DLC on tools |
| **Corrosion-resistant coating** | Plasma spray, sol-gel | 50–300 µm | Al₂O₃ on chemical reactor walls |
| **Bio-ceramic coating** | Plasma spray, electrophoretic deposition | 50–200 µm | Hydroxyapatite on Ti implants |

**Application of TBCs in High-Temperature Corrosion Protection:**

A typical TBC system on a gas-turbine blade has **four layers**:

1. **Superalloy substrate** — Ni-based (e.g., Inconel 718) — provides mechanical strength.
2. **Bond coat** (50–125 µm) — MCrAlY (M = Ni, Co) — provides oxidation resistance and adhesion.
3. **Thermally grown oxide (TGO)** — Al₂O₃ — forms naturally between bond coat and top coat.
4. **Ceramic top coat** (100–500 µm) — YSZ — the actual thermal insulator.

**Functions of the TBC:**

- Reduces metal surface temperature by **100–300 °C**, allowing higher turbine inlet temperatures (TIT) → improved efficiency.
- Blocks hot gas (containing O₂, SO₂, Na, V from fuels) from reaching the metal.
- Allows engines to run at **TIT > 1300 °C** (well above superalloy melting point).

**Why YSZ (Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia) is preferred:**

1. **Very low thermal conductivity** (~2.5 W/m·K at 1000 °C, vs. ~25 W/m·K for the superalloy) — best among refractory oxides.
2. **High coefficient of thermal expansion** (~10–11 × 10⁻⁶/K) — closely matches the bond coat / superalloy, minimizing thermal stress.
3. **Phase stabilization** — Pure ZrO₂ undergoes a destructive tetragonal → monoclinic phase transformation at ~1170 °C with ~4–5% volume expansion (causes cracking). Adding 6–8 wt% Y₂O₃ (yttria) **stabilizes the cubic/tetragonal phases** and prevents this transformation (a phenomenon called **transformation toughening**).
4. **Chemical inertness** in combustion atmospheres (O₂, CO₂, H₂O, SO₂) at high T.
5. **Sintering resistance** — maintains porous microstructure and insulating properties even after thousands of hours.
6. **Adequate hardness and erosion resistance** to survive particle impact in the gas stream.

> **Drawbacks of YSZ:** Long-term operation above 1200 °C causes sintering and destabilization → newer candidates include **Gd₂Zr₂O₇**, **La₂Zr₂O₇**, and **YSZ + Al₂O₃ composites**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make on "Ceramics" Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "corrosion" with "degradation":** Ceramics do not undergo *electrochemical* corrosion. Examiners deduct marks if you write "ceramics undergo pitting corrosion" or "galvanic corrosion of ceramics." Use the correct terminology: *chemical dissolution*, *selective leaching*, *molten salt attack*.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the PBR formula derivation:** A 7-mark question on Pilling-Bedworth Ratio will require you to **derive** the formula $PBR = \frac{M_{\text{oxide}} \cdot \rho_{\text{metal}}}{a \cdot M_{\text{metal}} \cdot \rho_{\text{oxide}}}$ — not just quote the result. Practice the derivation.
> 
> 3. **Mislabelling thermal shock parameter:** Students often write R = σ_f / (Eα) without the **Poisson's ratio term (1−ν)**. The factor (1−ν) is essential for the biaxial stress state of a thin coating/thermal shock scenario. **−1 mark** for omitting it.
> 
> 4. **Missing the "Why" of YSZ:** When asked "Why is YSZ used as a TBC?", students list properties but don't explain the **phase stabilization** mechanism (Y₂O₃ prevents the destructive t → m transformation). Examiners specifically check for this — **−2 marks** if missing.
> 
> 5. **Ignoring environment-specific limitations:** Writing "ceramics are corrosion-proof" without qualification is wrong. Always mention that **SiO₂ is attacked by HF**, **Al₂O₃ is attacked by hot alkalis**, etc. Examiners reward nuance.
> 
> 6. **Units and significant figures in numerical problems:** The conversion factor $K = 8.76 \times 10^4$ in the corrosion-rate formula is a favorite trap. If you use $K = 8.76$ (no power of 10), you will be off by 4 orders of magnitude. **Practice numericals.**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Ceramics (Module 4)**

- **Definition:** Inorganic, non-metallic, ionic/covalent bonded compounds of metals with O, N, or C.
- **Five classes:** Traditional, Advanced, Glasses, Glass-ceramics, Cermets.
- **Why corrosion-resistant:**
  - No free electrons → no electrochemical corrosion
  - Strong ionic/covalent bonds → high activation energy to break
  - Large negative ΔG°f → thermodynamically stable
  - Often *are* the protective oxide scale (Al₂O₃, Cr₂O₃, SiO₂)

- **Pilling-Bedworth Ratio (PBR):**
  - Formula: $PBR = \dfrac{M_{\text{oxide}} \cdot \rho_{\text{metal}}}{a \cdot M_{\text{metal}} \cdot \rho_{\text{oxide}}}$
  - 1 < PBR < 2 → protective; outside → non-protective

- **Thermal Shock Parameter:**
  - $R = \dfrac{\sigma_f(1-\nu)}{E\alpha}$; $R' = R \cdot k$
  - Si₃N₄ has the best R among common engineering ceramics.

- **Corrosion rate formula:**
  - $CR = \dfrac{8.76 \times 10^4 \,\cdot\, \Delta m}{\rho A t}$ (mm/yr)

- **Cermets:** Ceramic + metal binder; WC-Co is the prototype; Co binder is the corrosion weak link → galvanic attack; mitigated by Ni, Cr additions, or PVD coatings.

- **TBC / YSZ:**
  - YSZ = 6–8 wt% Y₂O₃ in ZrO₂
  - Low k (~2.5 W/m·K), high α (~10⁻⁵/K), phase-stabilized
  - Reduces metal T by 100–300 °C in turbines

- **Environment-Ceramic compatibility (must memorize):**
  - HF attacks SiO₂ → use SiC
  - Hot alkali attacks Al₂O₃, SiO₂ → use ZrO₂, SiC
  - Molten metals → use BN, Al₂O₃
  - Molten salts → use MgO, Al₂O₃

- **Ellingham diagram:** ceramic oxides lie very low (most stable) → confirms thermodynamic corrosion resistance.

- **Comparison with metals:** no anodes/cathodes, no pitting/SCC, but susceptible to brittle fracture and selective leaching.

- **Common exam trap questions:** PBR derivation, thermal shock parameter, "why YSZ?", environment-specific ceramic selection, cermet corrosion mechanism.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Composites and polymers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT527 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Composites and polymers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Composite Materials

A **composite material** is a heterogeneous, multiphase engineering material formed by the intentional combination of two or more distinct constituents — a **matrix** (continuous phase) and a **reinforcement** (dispersed phase) — at a macroscopic or microscopic level. The resulting material exhibits properties that are **superior** to those of the individual constituents acting alone. No new chemical compound is formed; instead, the components remain chemically distinct but act in synergy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A composite is *a material system comprising two or more physically distinct and mechanically separable phases, suitably arranged or distributed, to obtain properties and structural characteristics unattainable with any of the individual constituents.*

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Concrete of the Modern Age"
Imagine **reinforced concrete**. The **concrete** itself is strong in compression but weak in tension. However, when you embed **steel rebar** inside it, the steel handles tensile loads while concrete handles compressive loads. The final structure can resist both types of stress — a property neither material possesses individually. This is exactly how a composite works: the **matrix** carries and transfers loads to the **reinforcement**, which provides stiffness and strength. In corrosion engineering, composites are vital because they often combine the **chemical resistance of a polymer matrix** with the **mechanical strength of fibers**, giving us materials that resist both **chemical attack and mechanical stress** simultaneously.

## 1.2 Introduction to Polymers

A **polymer** is a high-molecular-weight organic compound (typically $\geq 10^4$ g/mol) composed of a large number of repeating structural units called **monomers**, joined together by covalent **C–C**, **C–H**, **C–O**, or **C–N** bonds through a process called **polymerization**.

> [!NOTE]
> The word *polymer* is derived from the Greek words *poly* (many) and *meros* (parts). The term was coined by **Jöns Jacob Berzelius** in 1833, although the concept of macromolecules was rigorously established much later by **Hermann Staudinger** in 1920, for which he received the Nobel Prize in Chemistry (1953).

### Conceptual Analogy — "A Chain of Paper Clips"
Think of a polymer as a long **chain made by linking thousands of identical paper clips** end-to-end. Each paper clip is a *monomer*, the linking process is *polymerization*, and the final long chain is the *polymer*. If the chains are loosely tangled (like a bowl of spaghetti), the polymer is flexible and soft — this is how **thermoplastics** behave. If the chains are chemically cross-linked at many points (like a fishing net), the polymer becomes rigid and strong — this is how **thermosets** behave.

## 1.3 Why Composites and Polymers in Corrosion Engineering?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this module matters in PECHT527:** Metallic corrosion costs the global economy an estimated **US$ 2.5 trillion annually** (NACE International IMPACT Study, ~3.4% of global GDP). The most elegant engineering solution is often to **replace the metal entirely** with a material that does not corrode — and that is precisely the role of **polymers and composite materials** in modern corrosion-resistant design (CRD).

| Property | Metals | Polymers | Composites (FRP) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Corrosion rate | High (in aggressive media) | **Negligible** | **Negligible** |
| Density (g/cm³) | 7.8 (steel), 2.7 (Al) | 0.9 – 1.4 | 1.5 – 2.0 |
| Tensile strength (MPa) | 250 – 1500 | 30 – 100 | 500 – 3000 |
| Chemical resistance | Low–Medium | **High** | **High** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Composites

Composites are broadly classified based on the **type of matrix** used:

### (a) Polymer Matrix Composites (PMC)
- Matrix: Thermosetting (epoxy, polyester, vinyl ester) or thermoplastic (PA, PEEK, PPS)
- Reinforcement: Glass, carbon, aramid, natural fibers
- **Most widely used** in corrosion engineering due to low cost and ease of fabrication
- Examples: FRP tanks, GRP pipes, epoxy-coated rebars

### (b) Metal Matrix Composites (MMC)
- Matrix: Aluminum, magnesium, titanium, copper
- Reinforcement: SiC, Al₂O₃, B, C fibers
- Used where **high-temperature strength and stiffness** are required
- Example: Al/SiC automotive brake rotors

### (c) Ceramic Matrix Composites (CMC)
- Matrix: Al₂O₃, SiC, Si₃N₄, ZrO₂
- Reinforcement: SiC whiskers, carbon fibers
- Used in **extreme thermal and chemical environments**
- Example: SiC/SiC turbine components, spacecraft nose cones

### (d) Classification by Reinforcement Geometry

| Geometry | Aspect Ratio | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Particle** | ≈ 1 | Cermets, filled polymers |
| **Whisker/Short fiber** | 10 – 1000 | SiC whiskers in Al |
| **Continuous fiber** | > 1000 | Glass/carbon/epoxy laminates |
| **Laminate** | — | Plywood, CFRP sandwich panels |

## 2.2 Classification of Polymers

### (a) Thermoplastic Polymers
- **Linear or branched** chains, no crosslinks
- **Reversible** softening on heating, hardening on cooling
- Can be **re-melted and reprocessed** → recyclable
- Examples: Polyethylene (PE), Polypropylene (PP), PVC, PTFE, PET, Nylon

### (b) Thermosetting Polymers
- Heavily **cross-linked** three-dimensional network
- **Irreversible** curing — once set, cannot be remelted
- Higher strength, chemical resistance, and thermal stability
- Examples: Epoxy, Phenol-formaldehyde, Urea-formaldehyde, Polyester, Melamine

### (c) Elastomers
- **Lightly cross-linked** polymers with high elastic recovery
- Exhibit very large reversible elongations (200% – 1000%)
- Examples: Natural rubber, Neoprene, Silicone, EPDM, Nitrile rubber

## 2.3 Polymerization Mechanisms

**Step 1 — Chain-growth (Addition) Polymerization:**
$$\text{Monomer (C=C)} + \text{Initiator (R}^{\bullet}) \rightarrow \text{R–M}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{R–(M)}_n\text{–M}^{\bullet}$$

**Step 2 — Step-growth (Condensation) Polymerization:**
$$n\,\text{HO–R–COOH} \longrightarrow \text{H–[O–R–CO]}_n\text{–OH} + (n-1)\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In **addition polymerization**, no by-product is released and the empirical formula of the polymer equals that of the monomer. In **condensation polymerization**, a small molecule (commonly **H₂O, HCl, or CH₃OH**) is eliminated at each step.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Composites & Polymers

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_f + V_m = 1$ | Volume fractions of fiber & matrix (assuming no voids) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\rho_c = \rho_f \, V_f + \rho_m \, V_m$ | **Rule of mixtures (density of composite)** | g/cm³ |
| 3 | $E_c^{\parallel} = E_f \, V_f + E_m \, V_m$ | Young's modulus — **iso-strain (Voigt)**, fibers aligned with load | GPa |
| 4 | $\dfrac{1}{E_c^{\perp}} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ | Young's modulus — **iso-stress (Reuss)**, fibers transverse to load | GPa |
| 5 | $\sigma_c^{\parallel} = \sigma_f \, V_f + \sigma_m \, V_m$ | Tensile strength — iso-strain | MPa |
| 6 | $W_f = \dfrac{\rho_f \, V_f}{\rho_f \, V_f + \rho_m \, V_m}$ | Weight fraction of fiber | dimensionless |
| 7 | $W_m = 1 - W_f$ | Weight fraction of matrix | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\overline{M}_n = \dfrac{\sum N_i \, M_i}{\sum N_i}$ | **Number-average molecular weight** | g/mol |
| 9 | $\overline{M}_w = \dfrac{\sum N_i \, M_i^2}{\sum N_i \, M_i}$ | **Weight-average molecular weight** | g/mol |
| 10 | $\text{PDI} = \dfrac{\overline{M}_w}{\overline{M}_n}$ | **Polydispersity Index** (always $\geq 1$) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\text{DP} = \dfrac{\overline{M}_n}{M_0}$ | **Degree of Polymerization** (number of repeat units) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{K \, W}{\rho \, A \, t}$ | Corrosion rate (mass loss method) | mm/yr |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering utility of these equations:** Equations 2–7 are used in **design of FRP pressure vessels, aircraft composites, and corrosion-resistant tanks**. Equations 8–11 govern the **mechanical strength, viscosity, and processability** of polymer coatings and linings used in corrosion protection. Equation 12 is the universal ASTM G-102 corrosion rate formula.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Rule of Mixtures (Density)

Consider a composite of total volume $V_c$ containing only two phases — a reinforcement (subscript $f$) and a matrix (subscript $m$).

**Step 1 — Express total volume:**
$$V_c = V_f + V_m$$

**Step 2 — Express total mass (mass is additive):**
$$m_c = m_f + m_m$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $m = \rho V$ for each phase:**
$$\rho_c \, V_c = \rho_f \, V_f + \rho_m \, V_m$$

**Step 4 — Divide throughout by $V_c$:**
$$\rho_c = \rho_f \, \frac{V_f}{V_c} + \rho_m \, \frac{V_m}{V_c}$$

**Step 5 — Recognize volume fractions $V_f / V_c = V_f$ and $V_m / V_c = V_m$** (when expressed as fractional values, dimensionless):

$$\boxed{\rho_c = \rho_f \, V_f + \rho_m \, V_m}$$

**Real-world check:** If $V_f = 0.5$, $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³ (E-glass), $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³ (polyester resin), then $\rho_c = (2.55)(0.5) + (1.20)(0.5) = 1.875$ g/cm³ — matches the experimentally measured density of standard glass-fiber reinforced plastic (GFRP).

## 3.2 Derivation of the Voigt (Iso-Strain) Modulus

**Step 1 — Geometric assumption:** For continuous fibers aligned *parallel* to the loading axis, both phases experience the **same strain** $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m$.

**Step 2 — Constitutive relation for each phase:**
$$\sigma_f = E_f \, \varepsilon_f \quad ; \quad \sigma_m = E_m \, \varepsilon_m \quad ; \quad \sigma_c = E_c^{\parallel} \, \varepsilon_c$$

**Step 3 — Force balance (load shared between the two phases):**
$$F_c = F_f + F_m \implies \sigma_c \, A_c = \sigma_f \, A_f + \sigma_m \, A_m$$

**Step 4 — Divide throughout by $A_c$ and substitute stress–strain relations:**
$$E_c^{\parallel} \, \varepsilon_c = E_f \, \varepsilon_f \, V_f + E_m \, \varepsilon_m \, V_m$$

**Step 5 — Apply iso-strain condition $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m$ and cancel:**
$$\boxed{E_c^{\parallel} = E_f \, V_f + E_m \, V_m}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Reuss (Iso-Stress) Modulus

**Step 1 — Geometric assumption:** For fibers aligned *perpendicular* to the load, both phases experience the **same stress** $\sigma_c = \sigma_f = \sigma_m$.

**Step 2 — Compatibility (total strain = sum of strain contributions):**
$$\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f \, V_f + \varepsilon_m \, V_m$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\varepsilon = \sigma / E$ for each phase:**
$$\frac{\sigma_c}{E_c^{\perp}} = \frac{\sigma_f}{E_f} V_f + \frac{\sigma_m}{E_m} V_m$$

**Step 4 — Apply iso-stress and cancel $\sigma$:**
$$\boxed{\frac{1}{E_c^{\perp}} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation of Polydispersity Index (PDI)

**Step 1 — Start with definitions of $\overline{M}_n$ and $\overline{M}_w$:**
$$\overline{M}_n = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i}{\sum_i N_i} \quad ; \quad \overline{M}_w = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^2}{\sum_i N_i M_i}$$

**Step 2 — Form the ratio PDI:**
$$\text{PDI} = \frac{\overline{M}_w}{\overline{M}_n} = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^2 / \sum_i N_i M_i}{\sum_i N_i M_i / \sum_i N_i}$$

**Step 3 — Algebraic simplification (cross-multiplying the sums):**
$$\boxed{\text{PDI} = \frac{\left(\sum_i N_i\right)\left(\sum_i N_i M_i^2\right)}{\left(\sum_i N_i M_i\right)^2}}$$

**Step 4 — Statistical bound (proof by Cauchy–Schwarz inequality):**
$$\left(\sum_i N_i M_i\right)^2 \leq \left(\sum_i N_i\right)\left(\sum_i N_i M_i^2\right)$$

**Step 5 — Therefore:**
$$\boxed{\text{PDI} \geq 1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering meaning of PDI:** PDI $= 1$ corresponds to a **monodisperse** sample (all chains of identical length — practically unattainable in synthetic polymers). For commercial polymers, PDI typically ranges from **1.5 to 5** for thermoplastics and can reach **10–20** for elastomers. A *narrow* PDI (close to 1) implies a more uniform molecular weight distribution, which leads to predictable mechanical and rheological behavior — a key quality control parameter in coating-grade resins.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Standard

**Problem:** A glass-fiber reinforced epoxy composite contains 60% by volume E-glass fibers ($E_f = 72$ GPa, $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³) in an epoxy matrix ($E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³). Calculate: (i) composite density, (ii) longitudinal modulus, (iii) transverse modulus, (iv) weight fraction of fibers.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify volume fractions:**
$$V_f = 0.60, \quad V_m = 0.40$$

**Step 2 — Composite density (Rule of mixtures):**
$$\rho_c = (2.55)(0.60) + (1.20)(0.40) = 1.53 + 0.48 = \mathbf{2.01 \ g/cm^3}$$

**Step 3 — Longitudinal modulus (Voigt model):**
$$E_c^{\parallel} = (72)(0.60) + (3.5)(0.40) = 43.2 + 1.4 = \mathbf{44.6 \ GPa}$$

**Step 4 — Transverse modulus (Reuss model):**
$$\frac{1}{E_c^{\perp}} = \frac{0.60}{72} + \frac{0.40}{3.5} = 0.00833 + 0.11429 = 0.12262$$
$$E_c^{\perp} = \frac{1}{0.12262} = \mathbf{8.16 \ GPa}$$

**Step 5 — Weight fraction of fibers:**
$$W_f = \frac{(2.55)(0.60)}{(2.55)(0.60) + (1.20)(0.40)} = \frac{1.53}{2.01} = \mathbf{0.761 \ \text{or} \ 76.1\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation from results:** The longitudinal modulus (44.6 GPa) is more than **5× the transverse modulus** (8.16 GPa). This **anisotropy** is a defining feature of fiber-reinforced composites and dictates how FRP laminates must be stacked (cross-ply, quasi-isotropic) in pressure vessel design.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A1, 3 Marks]**
> **Q1. Differentiate between thermoplastic and thermosetting polymers. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**
Thermoplastic polymers consist of **linear or branched chains** with only weak secondary bonding (van der Waals / hydrogen bonds) between chains. They **soften on heating and harden on cooling reversibly**, can be remelted and reprocessed, and are generally recyclable. Examples: *Polyethylene (PE), Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC), Polypropylene (PP), Nylon (PA 6,6)*.

Thermosetting polymers, on the other hand, possess a **heavily cross-linked three-dimensional network** formed irreversibly during curing. Once hardened, they **do not soften on reheating** (instead, they char or decompose), cannot be remelted, and exhibit superior strength, rigidity, and chemical resistance. Examples: *Epoxy resin, Phenol-formaldehyde (Bakelite), Polyester resin, Urea-formaldehyde*.

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q2. What is a polymer matrix composite (PMC)? Mention its matrix and reinforcement constituents.**

**Model Answer:**
A **Polymer Matrix Composite (PMC)** is a two-phase engineering material in which a **polymer resin** acts as the continuous matrix phase and a **fibrous or particulate material** acts as the dispersed reinforcement phase. Typical **matrix materials** include thermosetting resins such as *epoxy, unsaturated polyester, vinyl ester, and phenolic resins*; thermoplastic matrices such as *PEEK, PPS, and Nylon* are also used. Typical **reinforcement materials** include *E-glass, S-glass, carbon (graphite), aramid (Kevlar), and boron fibers*. The matrix binds the reinforcement, transfers applied loads to the fibers, protects them from environmental damage, and provides shape and surface finish, while the reinforcement carries the principal mechanical load.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question B-A, 14 Marks]**
> **(a)** With neat sketches, explain the classification of composite materials based on the **type of matrix**. Discuss in detail the role of matrix and reinforcement with reference to Polymer Matrix Composites (PMCs). **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Derive the **rule of mixtures** for estimating the density and longitudinal Young's modulus of a unidirectional fiber-reinforced composite. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

Composites are classified into three broad categories based on the matrix phase:

**(1) Polymer Matrix Composites (PMCs):** The matrix is a polymer resin — either a thermoset (epoxy, polyester, vinyl ester) or a thermoplastic (PEEK, PPS). PMCs are the most widely produced composites (≈ 65% of total composite production) due to their **low density, ease of fabrication, good chemical resistance, and low cost**. Examples: GRP pipes, FRP tanks, helicopter rotor blades, printed circuit boards.

**(2) Metal Matrix Composites (MMCs):** The matrix is a light metal — typically *Al, Mg, Ti, or Cu alloys*. Reinforcement is usually SiC, Al₂O₃, B, or C. MMCs offer **high specific stiffness, high-temperature strength, and good thermal conductivity**. Examples: Al/SiC brake rotors, Al/Al₂O₃ pistons, Ti/SiC aerospace components.

**(3) Ceramic Matrix Composites (CMCs):** The matrix is a technical ceramic — *Al₂O₃, SiC, ZrO₂, Si₃N₄*. Reinforcement is typically SiC whiskers, C fibers, or other ceramics. CMCs combine the **high-temperature stability and hardness of ceramics with improved fracture toughness** (toughness is the principal weakness of monolithic ceramics). Examples: SiC/SiC jet engine components, space-shuttle thermal protection tiles.

**Role of the Matrix (in PMCs):**
- Binds the reinforcement and **maintains the geometric distribution** of fibers
- **Transfers applied loads** to the reinforcement through shear at the interface
- **Protects fibers** from surface abrasion, moisture, and chemical attack
- Provides **shape, surface finish, and dimensional stability**
- Contributes to **toughness, ductility, and impact resistance** of the laminate

**Role of the Reinforcement (in PMCs):**
- Carries the **principal mechanical load** (typically ≥ 70% of tensile load in well-designed laminates)
- Provides **stiffness, strength, and high-temperature performance**
- Controls **thermal expansion**, electrical conductivity, and density
- Imparts **tailorable anisotropy** (e.g., unidirectional vs. cross-ply layups)

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Density derivation** — See SECTION 3.1 for the full step-by-step derivation. Result:
$$\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$$
subject to $V_f + V_m = 1$.

**Longitudinal modulus derivation** — See SECTION 3.2. Result:
$$E_c^{\parallel} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$$

**Assumptions stated:**
1. Fibers are **continuous, uniformly distributed, and perfectly aligned** with the loading axis
2. **Perfect bonding** exists at the fiber–matrix interface (no debonding or slip)
3. Both phases experience the **same strain** (iso-strain assumption — Voigt model)
4. The composite is **void-free** ($V_f + V_m = 1$)
5. The material is **linearly elastic** with constant $E_f$ and $E_m$
6. Loads are applied **parallel to the fiber direction** (uniaxial)

---

### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question B-B, 14 Marks]**
> **(a)** Explain the mechanisms of **(i) addition (chain-growth) polymerization** and **(ii) condensation (step-growth) polymerization** with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** A unidirectional GFRP composite contains 55% by volume of E-glass fibers in epoxy. Given $E_f = 72$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³, calculate the **density, longitudinal modulus, transverse modulus, and weight fraction of fibers**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**(i) Addition (Chain-Growth) Polymerization:**
This mechanism involves the **successive addition of monomer molecules** to a growing chain end carrying an active center (radical, ion, or metal-complex site). No small molecule is eliminated. The process proceeds in three stages:

- **Initiation:** A small amount of initiator (e.g., benzoyl peroxide) decomposes to form free radicals, which attack the double bond of the monomer:
$$\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{CH}_2{=}\text{CHX} \rightarrow \text{R–CH}_2\text{–CH}^{\bullet}\text{X}$$
- **Propagation:** The active chain end successively adds monomer units at high speed:
$$\text{R–(CH}_2\text{–CHX)}_n\text{–CH}_2\text{–CH}^{\bullet}\text{X} + \text{CH}_2{=}\text{CHX} \rightarrow \text{R–(CH}_2\text{–CHX)}_{n+1}\text{–CH}_2\text{–CH}^{\bullet}\text{X}$$
- **Termination:** Two active chains combine (**coupling**) or undergo **disproportionation**, terminating the chain. The molecular weight is controlled by **chain-transfer** agents.

**Example:** Polymerization of **ethylene to polyethylene**, **vinyl chloride to PVC**, **styrene to polystyrene**. The empirical formula of the polymer is the same as the monomer.

**(ii) Condensation (Step-Growth) Polymerization:**
This mechanism involves the **stepwise reaction between bi- or poly-functional monomers**, with each step **eliminating a small molecule** (H₂O, HCl, NH₃, or CH₃OH). Polymer builds up slowly, and long chains form only at very high conversion (> 99%).

- A typical example is the formation of **Nylon 6,6** from hexamethylenediamine and adipic acid:
$$n\,\text{H}_2\text{N–(CH}_2)_6\text{–NH}_2 + n\,\text{HOOC–(CH}_2)_4\text{–COOH} \rightarrow \text{H–[NH–(CH}_2)_6\text{–NH–CO–(CH}_2)_4\text{–CO]}_n\text{–OH} + (2n-1)\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$
- Other examples: **Terylene (PET)** from ethylene glycol + terephthalic acid, **Bakelite** from phenol + formaldehyde, **silicones** from chlorosilanes.

**Key distinction:** Condensation polymers often have **lower molecular weight** than addition polymers because of the strict requirement of high conversion. The molecular weight is governed by the **Carothers equation**:
$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{1}{1 - p}$$
where $p$ is the extent of reaction and $\overline{X}_n$ is the number-average degree of polymerization.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $V_f = 0.55$, $V_m = 0.45$, $E_f = 72$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³.

**(i) Density of composite:**
$$\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m = (2.55)(0.55) + (1.20)(0.45)$$
$$\rho_c = 1.4025 + 0.5400 = \mathbf{1.9425 \ g/cm^3}$$

**(ii) Longitudinal modulus (Voigt model — iso-strain):**
$$E_c^{\parallel} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m = (72)(0.55) + (3.5)(0.45)$$
$$E_c^{\parallel} = 39.6 + 1.575 = \mathbf{41.175 \ GPa}$$

**(iii) Transverse modulus (Reuss model — iso-stress):**
$$\frac{1}{E_c^{\perp}} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m} = \frac{0.55}{72} + \frac{0.45}{3.5}$$
$$\frac{1}{E_c^{\perp}} = 0.007639 + 0.128571 = 0.136210$$
$$E_c^{\perp} = \frac{1}{0.136210} = \mathbf{7.341 \ GPa}$$

**(iv) Weight fraction of fibers:**
$$W_f = \frac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_c} = \frac{(2.55)(0.55)}{1.9425} = \frac{1.4025}{1.9425} = \mathbf{0.722 \ \text{or} \ 72.2\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not mix up Voigt and Reuss models.** A common mistake is using the Reuss formula for longitudinal loading or the Voigt formula for transverse loading. *Always* use Voigt (iso-strain) when fibers are **aligned with the load** and Reuss (iso-stress) when fibers are **perpendicular to the load**.
> 2. **Units matter in the final answer.** If $E$ is in GPa, the answer is in GPa; do not write 41175 MPa without correct decimal placement. KTU evaluators check unit consistency strictly.
> 3. **Volume fractions must sum to unity** ($V_f + V_m = 1$). If the problem gives weight fractions, *convert to volume fractions first* using the densities — this is where 70% of students lose 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Show the substitution step** before the numerical evaluation. Writing only the final answer without intermediate algebra is penalized even if the answer is numerically correct.
> 5. **In polymerization questions**, students frequently confuse addition and condensation by giving the wrong example — e.g., citing Nylon (a condensation polymer) as an example of addition polymerization.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✦ Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Composites**
> - A composite has **two chemically distinct phases** (matrix + reinforcement) with a **distinct interface** between them.
> - Classification by matrix: **PMC, MMC, CMC**. Classification by reinforcement geometry: **particle, whisker, fiber, laminate**.
> - **Rule of mixtures (density):** $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$.
> - **Voigt (iso-strain, longitudinal):** $E_c^{\parallel} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$.
> - **Reuss (iso-stress, transverse):** $1/E_c^{\perp} = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$.
> - Voigt gives the **upper bound**; Reuss gives the **lower bound** of the actual modulus.
> - Weight fraction: $W_f = \rho_f V_f / \rho_c$ and $W_f + W_m = 1$.
> - Always state assumptions: **continuous fibers, perfect bonding, void-free, linear elastic, uniaxial load, uniform distribution**.
> - **FRP** = Fiber-Reinforced Plastic = the most widely used PMC in corrosion engineering (tanks, pipes, gratings, rebars).
> - The **gelcoat + structural + liner** architecture of an FRP tank is the canonical KTU diagram question.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Polymers**
> - Polymer = **many repeat units** (monomers) joined by **covalent bonds**.
> - **Thermoplastics**: linear/branched, reversible softening, recyclable. Examples: *PE, PP, PVC, PTFE, Nylon, PET*.
> - **Thermosets**: cross-linked 3D network, irreversible curing, higher strength and chemical resistance. Examples: *Epoxy, Polyester, Vinyl Ester, Phenol-formaldehyde*.
> - **Elastomers**: lightly cross-linked, high elastic recovery (200%–1000% elongation). Examples: *Natural rubber, Neoprene, Silicone, EPDM, Nitrile*.
> - **Addition (chain-growth) polymerization**: no by-product; initiated by radical/ion/catalyst; high MW achievable.
> - **Condensation (step-growth) polymerization**: by-product eliminated (H₂O, HCl, NH₃); Carothers equation: $\overline{X}_n = 1/(1-p)$.
> - **Number-average MW** ($\overline{M}_n$) is sensitive to **low-MW chains**; **weight-average MW** ($\overline{M}_w$) is sensitive to **high-MW chains**.
> - **PDI = $\overline{M}_w / \overline{M}_n \geq 1$** (equality only for monodisperse sample).
> - **Degree of Polymerization (DP) = $\overline{M}_n / M_0$** — where $M_0$ is the molecular weight of one repeat unit.
> - **Mechanical strength** of a polymer generally **increases with increasing $\overline{M}_w$** up to a saturation point (typically $\overline{M}_w \approx 10^5$ g/mol).
> - **Corrosion resistance ranking (broad, in oxidizing acids):** PTFE > PVDF > PP > PVC > PE > Polyester > Epoxy > Phenolic (highly dependent on acid type, concentration, and temperature).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hooks for the KTU Exam Hall:**
> 1. **"V = V in Voigt"** — both phases share the **same strain** (V for **V**olume-additive stiffness).
> 2. **"1/E in Reuss"** — the **reciprocal** of modulus is volume-additive (think of springs in **series**).
> 3. **"M_n sees the many small chains; M_w sees the few large chains"** — remember the weighting: $\overline{M}_n$ counts molecules equally, $\overline{M}_w$ counts mass equally.
> 4. **"Addition = Anhydride-free"** (no by-product); **"Condensation = condense out a small molecule"**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Corrosion and its control in industries: Power
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT527 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Corrosion and its control in industries: Power

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Corrosion in the Power Industry** refers to the irreversible deterioration of metallic components across the entire power generation cycle — from fuel handling, boiler/steam generation, high-temperature turbine sections, condensers, and cooling water circuits — caused by electrochemical, chemical, and mechanical-environmental interactions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Power plant corrosion is a multidisciplinary phenomenon encompassing aqueous corrosion (in condensers, feedwater lines, cooling towers), high-temperature gaseous oxidation (in superheaters, reheaters, gas turbine blades), and hot corrosion (Type I & Type II attack on gas turbine components due to molten salt deposits of Na₂SO₄, V₂O₅, and NaCl).

In the KTU 2024 framework, the power industry is segmented into three corrosion regimes:

| Regime | Location | Dominant Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Aqueous / Wet Corrosion** | Condenser, economizer, feedwater, cooling tower | Electrochemical, pitting, SCC |
| **High-Temperature Oxidation** | Superheater, reheater, boiler tubes, turbine blades | Diffusion-controlled oxide scale growth |
| **Hot Corrosion** | Gas turbine blades, boiler fireside | Molten salt accelerated attack |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pressure Cooker with a Wound"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a pressure cooker (the boiler) working non-stop 24×7 at 540 °C and 170 bar. Now imagine that one small scratch on its inner wall allows steam to slowly creep in and react with the metal underneath — much like a wound on your skin that, if untreated, spreads infection. The "blood" of the power plant (steam, water, combustion gases) is corrosive by nature, and every metallic surface is essentially fighting a continuous battle. **Corrosion control in power plants is the equivalent of bandaging, disinfecting, and rebuilding the wounded skin** — through water treatment, alloy selection, coatings, and inhibitors.

## 1.3 Why Power Industry Corrosion is a KTU Priority Topic

- Power plant downtime losses can exceed **₹1 crore per hour** in large thermal stations.
- A single boiler tube failure can cost **₹50 lakh to ₹2 crore** in repairs + lost generation.
- Corrosion accounts for **~30 % of forced outages** in coal-fired plants globally (EPRI estimates).
- Nuclear plant corrosion failures have direct safety implications (Fukushima, 2011 — partial SCC contribution).
- KTU 2024 emphasises this in CO4: *"Apply corrosion control strategies in industrial environments."*

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants to Remember for KTU 2024:**
> - **Faraday's constant (F)** = **96,485 C/mol** (≈ 96,500 C/mol in most exam answers)
> - **Universal gas constant (R)** = **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**
> - **Pilling–Bedworth threshold** = **1 < PBR < 2** (protective); PBR < 1 (non-protective); PBR > 2 (spalls)
> - **Acceptable corrosion rate (power plant steel)** = **< 0.10 mm/year** (industrial benchmark)
> - **Boiler feedwater pH** = **8.5 – 9.5** (sweet corrosion control range)
> - **Conductivity of pure feedwater** = **< 0.2 µS/cm**
