# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Corrosion Zones in a Power Plant

### A. Aqueous (Wet) Corrosion Zones

**1. Condenser Corrosion**
- Components: **Brass / Cupronickel / Titanium tubes** in surface condensers.
- Aggressors: Dissolved CO₂, O₂, NH₃, chlorides from cooling water.
- Mechanism: **Dezincification** of brass (selective leaching of Zn), **impingement attack** at tube inlet ends, **erosion-corrosion** at high velocity zones (> 2.4 m/s for brass).
- Control: Ferrous sulfate dosing, titanium tubes for seawater, design velocities limited to **1.8 – 2.4 m/s**.

**2. Economizer & Feedwater Corrosion**
- Components: Carbon steel tubes carrying preheated water.
- Attack: **Grooving** at rolled joints, **caustic gouging** from NaOH concentration in crevices, **hydrogen embrittlement** from acid cleaning damage.
- Control: Oxygen scavenging with hydrazine (N₂H₄) or DEHA, volatile amine treatment (morpholine, cyclohexylamine), pH 8.5–9.5 maintenance.

**3. Boiler Water / Steam Side Corrosion**
- **Caustic Embrittlement:** Intergranular SCC at riveted joints / tube ends where NaOH concentrates to > 5 % through capillary wicking.
  - Control: **Coordinated phosphate-pH control** (Na₃PO₄: Na₂HPO₄ ratio maintained at 2.85 – 2.95 : 1).
- **Hydrogen Damage:** Atomic H from corrosion reactions combines at grain boundaries to form CH₄ bubbles → blistering.
  - Reaction: Fe + 2H⁺ → Fe²⁺ + 2H → H₂ (g), or 4H + C (steel) → CH₄
- **Steam Oxidation of Superheater Tubes:** At 540–600 °C, loss of scale (magnetite) into turbine blades causes "solid particle erosion" downstream.

### B. High-Temperature Oxidation Zones

**1. Boiler Tube Fireside — Coal Ash Corrosion**
- Type: **Molten alkali iron trisulfate** attack (1Na₂O·Fe₂O₃·3SO₃) at 593–760 °C.
- Reducing atmosphere (low O₂, high SO₃) deposits molten sulfates that dissolve protective Fe₂O₃.
- Control: Excess air > 3 %, low sulfur coal, **chromized** or **aluminized** tubes, Inconel cladding.

**2. Superheater & Reheater Oxidation**
- At 540–600 °C, Fe forms a multilayer scale: **FeO (inner, wüstite) → Fe₃O₄ (middle, magnetite) → Fe₂O₃ (outer, hematite)**.
- Growth follows **parabolic kinetics** (Wagner's theory) — cation diffusion through scale is rate-limiting.

**3. Gas Turbine Hot Section**
- Blade materials: Ni-based superalloys (Inconel 738, IN-100, René 80).
- Operating temperature: 850–1050 °C, beyond oxidation limit → require **thermal barrier coatings (TBC)**.

### C. Hot Corrosion (Molten Salt Induced)

Two types recognised in power + aviation gas turbines:

| Type | Temperature | Salt | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type I (High-Temp Hot Corrosion)** | 850–950 °C | Na₂SO₄ molten | Sulfidation of Cr to CrS, fluxing of protective Al₂O₃ scale |
| **Type II (Low-Temp Hot Corrosion)** | 650–750 °C | Na₂SO₄ + CoSO₄ eutectic | Pitting with CoO/Co₃O₄ flux, acidic fluxing |

**Inlet air contaminants (especially in marine/coastal plants):** NaCl → Na₂SO₄ (via oxidation + sulfation); V₂O₅ from heavy fuel oil → V₂O₅ + Na₂SO₄ → low-melting eutectic (~550 °C).

## 2.2 Wagner's Theory of High-Temperature Oxidation (KTU Favourite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wagner's Theory (1933):** For protective oxide growth, the rate-determining step is the **diffusion of ions (cations outward + anions inward) through the growing oxide scale** under the influence of an electrochemical potential gradient. The parabolic rate law is a direct consequence.

### Step-by-Step Logic:
- At the metal/scale interface: **Metal atoms ionise** → release electrons → M → M^(n+) + ne⁻.
- At the scale/gas interface: **Oxygen is reduced** → ½O₂ + 2e⁻ → O²⁻.
- Ions migrate through the scale via **lattice vacancies** (Schottky/Frenkel defects).
- The rate of scale growth ∝ **inverse of scale thickness** (diffusion path length):
  - dx/dt = k_p / x → integrating → **x² = 2k_p·t** (parabolic).
- Electron transport: n-type oxides (ZnO, Fe₂O₃) → electrons + cations move outward.
- p-type oxides (NiO, FeO, Cu₂O) → electron holes + cations move outward; anions (O²⁻) move inward.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE FOR KTU 2024 EXAMS** (Read every formula, every variable, every unit — at least one appears in nearly every ESE paper.)

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $PBR = \dfrac{V_{oxide}}{V_{metal}} = \dfrac{M_{oxide} \cdot \rho_{metal}}{n \cdot M_{metal} \cdot \rho_{oxide}}$ | Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (volume of oxide per volume of metal) | Dimensionless; ideal 1 < PBR < 2 |
| 2 | $x^{2} = 2 k_{p} t$ | Parabolic scale growth (high-T oxidation) | x [m], k_p [m²/s], t [s] |
| 3 | $\left(\dfrac{\Delta m}{A}\right)^{2} = k_{p} t$ | Parabolic mass-gain law | Δm/A [kg/m²] |
| 4 | $x = k_{l} t$ | Linear rate law (non-protective scales) | k_l [m/s] |
| 5 | $x = k_{e} \ln(t + C)$ | Logarithmic rate (thin films, low T) | k_e [m] |
| 6 | $\eta = a + b \log_{10} i$ | Tafel equation for charge transfer | η [V], i [A/cm²] |
| 7 | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{R T}{n F} \ln Q$ | Nernst equation for electrode potential | E [V], T [K] |
| 8 | $W = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Faraday's 2nd law — mass lost by corrosion | W [g], I [A], t [s] |
| 9 | $CR = \dfrac{87.6 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$ | Corrosion rate (mm/yr) — W [mg], D [g/cm³], A [cm²], t [hr] | Standard KTU formula |
| 10 | $\Delta G^{\circ} = -n F E^{\circ}$ | Gibbs free energy ↔ cell potential | ΔG [J/mol] |
| 11 | $k_{p} = k_{0} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_{a}}{RT}\right)$ | Arrhenius form of parabolic rate constant | E_a [J/mol] |
| 12 | $i_{corr} = \dfrac{\beta_{a} \cdot \beta_{c}}{2.303 R_{p}(\beta_{a}+\beta_{c})}$ | Stern–Geary equation (polarisation resistance) | i_corr [A/cm²] |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Isolation Rule Followed:** Every formula above uses `\vert`, `\dfrac`, `\left(\right)` for display math, with one blank line before/after every `$$` block. No raw `\vert` pipe character in any markdown table cell.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Power Engineering

- **Boiler chemists** use PBR predictions to select tube alloys (e.g., chromized steel where PBR(Cr₂O₃) ≈ 2.07 — just at the spall limit; aluminized steel where PBR(Al₂O₃) ≈ 1.28 — ideal).
- **Parabolic rate constant k_p** is plotted in Arrhenius form to predict tube life at design temperature — e.g., for 9–12 % Cr ferritic steels at 600 °C, k_p ≈ 10⁻¹⁴ m²/s.
- **Stern–Geary equation** is used in Linear Polarisation Resistance (LPR) probes installed in condenser cooling water for online corrosion monitoring.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Parabolic Rate Law from Wagner's Diffusion Theory

We start from Fick's first law applied to cation diffusion through a growing oxide scale:

$$
J = -D \frac{dC}{dx}
$$

**Step 1 — Identify the diffusing species flux.** For parabolic oxidation, the rate-limiting flux J is of metal cations (M^(n+)) moving outward (or oxygen anions moving inward) through the scale of thickness x.

$$
J = \frac{1}{A}\frac{dn}{dt}
$$

**Step 2 — Relate flux to scale thickness growth.** Each mole of cation arriving at the scale/gas interface adds a volume ΔV per mole. The growth of scale is:

$$
\frac{dx}{dt} = J \cdot \frac{M_{oxide}}{n \cdot \rho_{oxide}}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the assumption of quasi-steady-state diffusion.** The concentration gradient is linear across the scale (Wagner's assumption), so the gradient is C₀/x, where C₀ is the constant solubility difference of the cation between the two interfaces.

$$
J = D \cdot \frac{C_0}{x}
$$

**Step 4 — Combine and separate variables.**

$$
\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{D \cdot C_0 \cdot M_{oxide}}{n \cdot \rho_{oxide} \cdot x}
$$

**Step 5 — Define the parabolic rate constant k_p** such that 2 k_p = D C₀ M_oxide / (n ρ_oxide):

$$
\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{2 k_p}{x}
$$

**Step 6 — Separate variables and integrate** from x = 0 at t = 0 to x = x at t = t:

$$
\int_{0}^{x} x \, dx = \int_{0}^{t} 2 k_p \, dt
$$

$$
\frac{x^{2}}{2} = 2 k_p t
$$

**Step 7 — Final parabolic law:**

$$
\boxed{x^{2} = 4 k_p t \quad \text{or} \quad x^{2} = 2 k_p t \text{ (alternative convention)}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic:** In the KTU convention, the parabolic law is often written as **x² = 2 k_p t** (when k_p is defined as half the diffusion-product term). Always check the textbook convention — 2 vs 4 is the most common error in KTU valuation.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Corrosion Rate (mm/yr) from Mass Loss

The KTU standard expression: convert weight loss W (mg), density D (g/cm³), area A (cm²), and time t (hr) into penetration depth in mm/yr.

**Step 1 — Volume of metal lost:**

$$
V_{metal} = \frac{W}{D} \quad \text{[cm}^{3}\text{]}
$$

**Step 2 — Penetration depth per unit time** (corrosion rate in cm/hr):

$$
CR = \frac{V_{metal}}{A \cdot t} = \frac{W}{D \cdot A \cdot t} \quad \text{[cm/hr]}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert cm/hr → mm/yr:**
- cm → mm: × 10
- hr → yr: × (24 × 365) = 8760

$$
CR = \frac{W \cdot 10 \cdot 8760}{D \cdot A \cdot t}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the constant:**

$$
\boxed{CR = \frac{87{,}600 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t} \quad \text{[mm/yr]}}
$$

If the time is given in **days** instead of hours (very common in KTU problems):

$$
CR = \frac{87.6 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t} \quad \text{[t in days, W in mg]}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The constant **87.6** comes from (10 × 24 × 365)/1000 = 87,600/1000 = 87.6 for t in days. KTU students confuse "87.6" with "876" — remember the factor of 1000 between mg and g.

## 3.3 Derivation III — Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR) in Detail

The PBR compares the **volume of oxide produced** to the **volume of metal consumed**.

**Step 1 — Moles of metal consumed to produce 1 mole of oxide.** Consider a generic reaction:
$$
a M + \frac{b}{2} O_2 \rightarrow M_a O_b
$$
**1 mole of oxide** uses **a moles of metal** and **b/2 moles of O₂**.

**Step 2 — Volume of 1 mole of oxide:**

$$
V_{oxide} = \frac{M_{oxide}}{\rho_{oxide}}
$$

**Step 3 — Volume of a moles of metal:**

$$
V_{metal} = \frac{a \cdot M_{metal}}{\rho_{metal}}
$$

**Step 4 — Assemble the ratio:**

$$
PBR = \frac{V_{oxide}}{V_{metal}} = \frac{M_{oxide} \cdot \rho_{metal}}{a \cdot M_{metal} \cdot \rho_{oxide}}
$$

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example: Iron → Fe₂O₃**

Reaction: $4Fe + 3O_2 \rightarrow 2Fe_2O_3$

- $M_{Fe_2O_3} = 159.69$ g/mol
- $M_{Fe} = 55.845$ g/mol
- $\rho_{Fe_2O_3} = 5.26$ g/cm³
- $\rho_{Fe} = 7.87$ g/cm³
- a = 2 (Fe atoms per oxide molecule)

$$
PBR = \frac{159.69 \times 7.87}{2 \times 55.845 \times 5.26}
$$

$$
PBR = \frac{1256.76}{587.62} = 2.14
$$

> [!WARNING]
> PBR for Fe₂O₃ ≈ **2.14** — slightly above the upper protective limit of 2. This explains why **hematite scale spalls** easily on boiler tubes at high T — a classic KTU application question.

**Step 6 — Worked example: Aluminium → Al₂O₃**

- $M_{Al_2O_3} = 101.96$ g/mol
- $M_{Al} = 26.98$ g/mol
- $\rho_{Al_2O_3} = 3.99$ g/cm³
- $\rho_{Al} = 2.70$ g/cm³
- a = 2

$$
PBR = \frac{101.96 \times 2.70}{2 \times 26.98 \times 3.99} = \frac{275.29}{215.30} = 1.28
$$

**PBR = 1.28 → protective, dense, adherent** — this is the reason Al₂O₃ is the gold standard for TBC bond coats on gas turbine blades.

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Nernst Equation Applied to Dissolved-Oxygen Corrosion in Feedwater

**Scenario:** Dissolved O₂ in feedwater creates a corrosion cell:
Cathode: O₂ + 2H₂O + 4e⁻ → 4OH⁻ (E° = +0.401 V vs SHE)
Anode: Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻ (E° = −0.440 V vs SHE)

**Step 1 — Standard cell potential:**

$$
E^{\circ}_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{cathode} - E^{\circ}_{anode} = 0.401 - (-0.440) = 0.841 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Nernst to cathode** (P_O₂ = 0.21 atm, a_{OH⁻} from pH):

$$
E_{cathode} = 0.401 - \frac{0.0591}{4} \log \frac{[OH^-]^4}{P_{O_2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Cell EMF drives corrosion.** As dissolved O₂ drops (degassing in deaerator), E_cathode falls — but the driving force for Fe dissolution remains until [O₂] < 7 ppb (ppb = parts per billion) in modern high-pressure plants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Modern once-through boilers demand **feedwater O₂ < 7 ppb** because even trace O₂ at 250 bar and 280 °C causes rapid pitting of austenitic stainless steel superheater tubes. This is why **oxygen scavengers (hydrazine, DEHA, carbohydrazide)** are mandatory dosing chemicals in KTU practical syllabi.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Ranking of Corrosion-Resistant Alloys for Power Plant

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a common KTU 14-mark question pattern ("Compare and rank materials for superheater / turbine application").

| Alloy Class | Composition (typical) | Max Service T (°C) | Dominant Protective Scale | PBR | Suitability in Power Plant |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Carbon Steel (SA-210)** | Fe + 0.25 C | 460 | Fe₃O₄ (magnetite) | 1.77 | Economiser, low-T boiler tubes |
| **T-11 (1.25 Cr – 0.5 Mo)** | 1.25 Cr, 0.5 Mo | 540 | (Fe,Cr)₃O₄ spinel | ~1.80 | Superheater (subcritical) |
| **T-22 (2.25 Cr – 1 Mo)** | 2.25 Cr, 1 Mo | 580 | Cr-rich spinel | ~1.85 | Reheater, supercritical boiler |
| **T-91 (9 Cr – 1 Mo – V-Nb)** | 9 Cr, 1 Mo, V, Nb | 620 | Cr₂O₃ + spinel | ~1.95 | Supercritical / USC boilers |
| **T-92 (9 Cr – 0.5 Mo – 1.8 W-V-Nb)** | 9 Cr, 1.8 W | 650 | Cr₂O₃ | ~1.95 | Advanced USC (600 °C class) |
| **SS 304H** | 18 Cr, 8 Ni | 700 | Cr₂O₃ | ~1.95 | Superheater, reheater |
| **SS 347H (Nb-stabilised)** | 18 Cr, 11 Ni, Nb | 700 | Cr₂O₃ | ~1.95 | US boilers — prevents Cr-carbide SCC |
| **Inconel 625 (Ni-base)** | 61 Ni, 22 Cr, 9 Mo, 3.5 Nb | 1050 | Cr₂O₃ + Al₂O₃ | ~1.50 | Gas turbine blades, TBC substrate |
| **Inconel 738LC** | Ni + 16 Cr, 8.5 Co, 3.5 Al, 3.5 Ti | 980 | Al₂O₃ (β-NiAl) | ~1.28 | First-stage gas turbine blades |

**Key Engineering Insight:** Increasing Cr content shifts the protective scale from **Fe-based** (PBR ~2, prone to spall) to **Cr-based** (PBR ~1.95, adherent). The breakthrough at **~12 % Cr** is the threshold for the alloy to form a continuous, self-healing Cr₂O₃ layer — this is why all modern boiler steels are "9–12 % Cr" class.

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Corrosion Zones Across a Thermal Power Plant

This block-level functional architecture flow maps every major corrosion zone in a subcritical thermal power plant, from coal handling to cooling water return.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Coal Handling Yard] --> B[Pulveriser + Mills]
    B --> C[Furnace / Boiler Fireside]
    C --> D[Superheater - High T 540 C]
    D --> E[Reheater - 540 C]
    E --> F[High Pressure Turbine]
    F --> G[Intermediate Pressure Turbine]
    G --> H[Low Pressure Turbine]
    H --> I[Condenser Tubes - Cu-Ni or Ti]
    I --> J[Condensate Extraction Pump]
    J --> K[Low Pressure Heaters]
    K --> L[Deaerator - O2 Scavenging]
    L --> M[Boiler Feed Pump]
    M --> N[High Pressure Heaters]
    N --> O[Economiser - 250 C Water]
    O --> P[Drum / Steam Separator]
    P --> D

    I --> Q[Cooling Water Inlet - River / Sea]
    Q --> R[Cooling Water Pump]
    R --> I

    A --> A1[Corrosion Zone 1: Coal Ash Attack on Hopper Liners]
    C --> C1[Corrosion Zone 2: Fireside Molten Sulfate Attack - Type II Hot Corr]
    D --> D1[Corrosion Zone 3: High-T Parabolic Oxidation - 540 to 600 C]
    F --> F1[Corrosion Zone 4: Steam Oxidation + SCC of Blades]

    O --> O1[Corrosion Zone 7: Grooving at Rolled Joints, Pitting]

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each block represents a power plant subsystem; the side branches `A1, C1, D1, ...` show where corrosion attacks. This is the standard "annotated P&ID" style expected in KTU 14-mark answers.

## 4.2 Schematic — High-Temperature Oxidation Mechanism (Wagner Model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph scale [Growing Oxide Scale]
        direction LR
        M[Metal Substrate] -->|Metal ionises| INT[Metal-Scale Interface]
        INT -->|Cations diffuse out| MID[Mid Scale - Vacancy Diffusion]
        MID -->|O2 reduced| SURF[Scale-Gas Interface]
        SURF -->|Electrons return| INT
    end

    GAS[Combustion Gas - O2, SO2, H2O] -->|Adsorbs + reacts| SURF
    SCALE[Protective Al2O3 or Cr2O3 Layer Forms] --- scale

```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INLET[Hot Combustion Gas - 900 to 1050 C] -->|Na, S, V from fuel/air| ADS[Adsorption on Blade Surface]
    ADS -->|Sulfation| SULF[Na2SO4 Condensate Deposits]
    SULF -->|Above M.P 884 C| MOLTEN[Molten Sulfate Film]

    DEGRAD -->|Cross-section loss| FAIL[Component Failure]
    FAIL -->|Maint. cost| ECON[Economical Loss]

    TBC[Thermal Barrier Coating - YSZ over Al2O3 bond coat] ---|Acts as diffusion barrier + thermal insulator| INLET
    TBC -.->|Degrades by CMAS attack| DEGRAD
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q: List the various types of corrosion encountered in a thermal power plant. Mention the location and mechanism of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The major types of corrosion in a thermal power plant are:

1. **High-temperature oxidation (0.5 mark)** — occurs on **superheater and reheater tubes** at 540–600 °C. Mechanism: diffusion-controlled parabolic growth of Fe₃O₄/Fe₂O₃ scale (Wagner theory).

2. **Caustic embrittlement (0.5 mark)** — occurs at **riveted boiler joints and tube ends** where NaOH concentrates in crevices. Mechanism: intergranular SCC.

3. **Condenser corrosion (0.5 mark)** — in **brass / Cu-Ni tubes** of surface condensers. Mechanism: **dezincification**, **erosion-corrosion**, **impingement attack**.

4. **Coal-ash / fireside corrosion (0.5 mark)** — on **boiler tubes facing the flame**, particularly at 593–760 °C. Mechanism: molten alkali iron-trisulfate attack under reducing atmosphere.

5. **Hydrogen embrittlement / hydrogen damage (0.5 mark)** — in **carbon steel boiler tubes and steam drums**. Mechanism: atomic H diffuses into steel and forms CH₄ bubbles at grain boundaries.

6. **Hot corrosion in gas turbines (0.5 mark)** — on **Ni-superalloy blades** of combined-cycle plants. Mechanism: molten Na₂SO₄ + V₂O₅ salt-induced accelerated oxidation (Type I high-T, Type II low-T).

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q: What is Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR)? Explain its significance in power plant boiler tube protection.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Pilling–Bedworth Ratio is defined as the ratio of the **volume of oxide formed** to the **volume of metal consumed** during oxidation:

$$
PBR = \frac{V_{oxide}}{V_{metal}} = \frac{M_{oxide} \cdot \rho_{metal}}{n \cdot M_{metal} \cdot \rho_{oxide}}
$$

**Significance in power plant (2 marks):**
- If **PBR < 1** (e.g., Mg → MgO, PBR ≈ 0.81) → oxide is **porous, non-protective**, does not cover the metal fully → **unacceptable** (e.g., Mg cannot be used as a high-T boiler material).
- If **1 ≤ PBR ≤ 2** (e.g., Al → Al₂O₃, PBR ≈ 1.28; Cr → Cr₂O₃, PBR ≈ 1.95) → oxide is **dense, adherent, compressive** → **protective, ideal for high-T applications** (e.g., aluminized superheater tubes, chromized blades).
- If **PBR > 2** (e.g., Fe → Fe₂O₃, PBR ≈ 2.14) → oxide has **excess volume**, develops **compressive stresses**, leads to **spallation** during thermal cycling → **not protective in the long term** (explains why pure Fe tubes fail faster than T-22, T-91 alloy tubes which form Cr-rich spinel with PBR ~ 1.95).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often quote PBR for FeO (wüstite) instead of Fe₂O₃. For boiler design, use the **outer, stable oxide** (Fe₂O₃) — wüstite is only stable above 570 °C and decomposes on cooling, causing scale cracking.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, every Part B question in Module 4 carries **internal choice**. Two alternative questions (A and B) are given below; students answer **either** A **or** B.

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Module 4 ESE Pattern]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the mechanism of **high-temperature oxidation of metals** with reference to **Wagner's theory**. Derive the **parabolic rate law** and discuss the factors affecting the parabolic rate constant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the phenomenon of **hot corrosion in gas turbines** used in power plants. Differentiate between **Type I and Type II hot corrosion**, and explain the control measures employed. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Question A(a)** (7 marks)

**1. Mechanism of high-temperature oxidation (2 marks):**
High-temperature oxidation is the reaction of a metal with oxygen (or other gases like SO₂, CO₂, H₂O, H₂S) at elevated temperatures, typically 400–1100 °C in power plant systems. Unlike aqueous corrosion, the electrolyte is a **solid oxide scale**, and ion transport occurs through the scale by **lattice diffusion**.

The general reaction is: $aM + \frac{b}{2}O_2 \rightarrow M_aO_b$

The process involves four steps:
- (i) **Adsorption** of O₂ on the oxide surface.
- (ii) **Dissociation and ionisation** at the scale/gas interface: O₂ → 2O²⁻ + 4e⁻ (cathodic-like).
- (iii) **Diffusion of ions** through the scale (rate-determining step).
- (iv) **New oxide formation** at the metal/scale interface (anodic-like): M → M^(n+) + ne⁻.

**2. Wagner's theory (2 marks):**
Wagner (1933) proposed that the rate of scale growth is controlled by **diffusion of ions and electrons** through a **defective lattice** (vacancies, interstitials) under a **chemical potential gradient**. The scale acts as a solid electrolyte with ionic conductivity. In n-type oxides (e.g., ZnO, Fe₂O₃), electrons and cations move outward; in p-type oxides (e.g., NiO, FeO, Cu₂O), cations and electron holes move outward (or O²⁻ moves inward).

**3. Derivation of parabolic rate law (2 marks):**
[Full derivation as in Section 3.1, ending with $x^2 = 2k_p t$]

**4. Factors affecting k_p (1 mark):**
- **Temperature** (Arrhenius dependence: k_p = k_0 exp(−E_a/RT)).
- **Composition of metal / alloy** (Cr, Al additions form protective scales).
- **Oxygen partial pressure** (very low P_O₂ changes the mechanism from parabolic to linear).
- **Scale properties** (defect concentration, grain size, dopants).
- **Reactive gas species** (presence of SO₂, H₂O alters defect chemistry).

---

### **Model Answer — Question A(b)** (7 marks)

**1. Definition of hot corrosion (1.5 marks):**
Hot corrosion is the **accelerated attack of metal surfaces** at high temperatures (650–1050 °C) in gas turbines, caused by the deposition and **fluxing action of fused salt films** (mainly Na₂SO₄, V₂O₅, CoSO₄) that destroy the normally protective oxide scale.

**Sources of contaminants:** Na from marine air, S from fuel, V from heavy fuel oil (residual), K from agricultural residue co-firing.

**2. Type I (High-Temperature) Hot Corrosion — 850–950 °C (2 marks):**
- Salt: molten Na₂SO₄ alone (m.p. 884 °C).
- Mechanism: **Sulfidation of Cr to CrS** at scale/substrate interface + **basic fluxing** of Al₂O₃ by Na₂O in the melt (dissolution as NaAlO₂).
- Morphology: **broad, deep, non-protective attack** with internal sulfides, often with a greenish-grey appearance.
- Wedge-shaped depletion zone of Cr beneath the surface.

**3. Type II (Low-Temperature) Hot Corrosion — 650–750 °C (2 marks):**
- Salt: **mixed sulfates** Na₂SO₄ + CoSO₄ / NiSO₄ (eutectic m.p. ~550 °C).
- Mechanism: **acidic fluxing** with low-melt CoO·Co₃O₄ or NiO·Ni₃O₄ that dissolves protective Al₂O₃ as Al₂(SO₄)₃.
- Morphology: **pitting attack**, no broad front; subsurface pitting with cobalt-rich spinels.

**4. Control measures (1.5 marks):**
- **Alloy selection:** High-Cr, high-Al Ni-superalloys (Inconel 738, IN-100).
- **Thermal Barrier Coatings (TBC):** Yttria-stabilised zirconia (YSZ) top coat + NiCrAlY bond coat.
- **Fuel cleaning:** Reduce S, V, Na content; Mg-based fuel additives (form high-melt MgSO₄ + Mg₃V₂O₈).
- **Inlet air filtration:** Eliminate NaCl aerosols in marine/coastal sites.
- **Coatings:** Pack aluminisation, platinum-modified aluminide, MCrAlY overlay coatings.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* `[KTU University Exam – Module 4 ESE Pattern]`

**Q: (a)** Discuss the various **water-treatment methods** employed for corrosion control in a high-pressure boiler. Explain the role of **phosphate, hydrazine, and amines** in boiler water chemistry. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat schematic, describe the **mechanism of caustic embrittlement** in boiler steel. Explain its prevention by **coordinated phosphate-pH control**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Question B(a)** (7 marks)

**1. Why water treatment? (1 mark)**
In high-pressure boilers (≥ 100 bar), the feedwater must be of ultra-high purity to prevent:
- Scale formation (Ca, Mg salts → CaCO₃, CaSO₄ on tube surfaces → local overheating → tube failure).
- Caustic embrittlement (NaOH concentration in crevices).
- Corrosion (dissolved O₂, CO₂, chlorides).

**2. External treatment (1.5 marks):**
- **Clarification + Filtration** removes suspended solids.
- **Lime–soda softening:** Ca(OH)₂ + Na₂CO₃ precipitates Ca and Mg hardness.
- **Ion exchange (demineralisation):** Cation exchanger (H⁺ form) + Anion exchanger (OH⁻ form) → conductivity < 0.1 µS/cm, silica < 0.02 ppm.
- **Reverse Osmosis (RO)** as pre-treatment in modern plants.

**3. Internal treatment with phosphate (1.5 marks):**
- Trisodium phosphate (Na₃PO₄) is dosed to boiler water.
- Reacts with residual Ca²⁺ hardness: $10 Ca^{2+} + 6 PO_4^{3-} + 2 OH^- \rightarrow Ca_{10}(PO_4)_6(OH)_2 \downarrow$ (hydroxyapatite) — non-adherent sludge, removed by blowdown.
- Maintains **alkalinity reserve** (pH 10.5–11.0) without free NaOH.

**4. Hydrazine (N₂H₄) — oxygen scavenger (1.5 marks):**
- Reaction with dissolved O₂: $N_2H_4 + O_2 \rightarrow N_2 + 2 H_2O$
- Excess hydrazine decomposes at high T: $3 N_2H_4 \rightarrow 4 NH_3 + N_2$
- Generated NH₃ neutralises CO₂ in condensate, raising pH → **reduces general corrosion of condensate piping**.
- Modern alternative: **DEHA (Diethylhydroxylamine, (C₂H₅)₂NOH)** — less toxic, works at lower T.

**5. Amine treatment (1.5 marks):**
- **Volatile amines** (morpholine, cyclohexylamine, 2-amino-2-methyl-1-propanol AMP) are added to feedwater/steam.
- They vaporise with steam and **condense in cooler parts** (heaters, condensate lines) where CO₂ would otherwise form carbonic acid.
- Maintain pH of condensate **8.5 – 9.0**.
- "Filming amines" (octadecylamine) form a **monomolecular hydrophobic film** on metal surfaces → act as a barrier to dissolved O₂ and CO₂.

---

### **Model Answer — Question B(b)** (7 marks)

**1. Definition and location (1 mark):**
**Caustic embrittlement** is a form of **stress-corrosion cracking (SCC)** of carbon steel boiler parts, occurring at locations of **high local stress** (riveted seams, tube-roll joints, bolt threads) where **NaOH can concentrate by capillary wicking** from the bulk boiler water to the metal/oxide interface, reaching concentrations up to 10–20 %.

**2. Mechanism (2.5 marks):**
Step-by-step mechanism:
- (i) NaOH in boiler water has a small concentration (typically 50–200 ppm), but is non-volatile.
- (ii) At riveted joints or tube ends, water evaporates in the **crevice**, but NaOH remains — concentrating by factors of 100–1000.
- (iii) Concentrated NaOH (≥ 5 %) attacks the protective magnetite (Fe₃O₄) film:

$$
Fe_3O_4 + 4 NaOH \rightarrow 2 NaFeO_2 + Na_2FeO_2 + 2 H_2O
$$

- (iv) Beneath the disrupted film, Fe dissolves as ferrite in the alkaline environment: $Fe + 2 OH^- \rightarrow FeO_2^{2-} + H_2 \uparrow$
- (v) Atomic hydrogen enters the steel, migrates to grain boundaries, and combines with carbides: $Fe_3C + 4H \rightarrow 3Fe + CH_4 \uparrow$
- (vi) CH₄ pressure builds at grain boundaries, and the **tensile residual stress** in the riveted region opens **intergranular cracks** that grow progressively.

**3. Schematic diagram (1.5 marks):**

```
   ┌─────────────────── Boiler Shell (Carbon Steel) ───────────────┐
   │                                                                │
   │  ╔═══════════════════╗     ╔═══════════════════╗               │
   │  ║   Tube Sheet      ║     ║  Tube Sheet       ║               │
   │  ║   (rolled joint)  ║     ║  (riveted seam)   ║               │
   │  ║  ──────────────── ║     ║                   ║               │
   │  ║ | Caustic conc.   ║     ║  NaOH wicks up    ║               │
   │  ║ | in crevice      ║     ║  by capillarity    ║               │
   │  ║ |  ↓ cracks ↓     ║     ║  ↓ IGC cracks ↓   ║               │
   │  ║ |     ╳╳╳         ║     ║     ╳╳╳           ║               │
   │  ║ |                 ║     ║                   ║               │
   │  ╚═══════════════════╝     ╚═══════════════════╝               │
   │                                                                │
   │  Boiler Water: 100 ppm NaOH, pH 10.5                            │
   └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**4. Prevention by coordinated phosphate-pH control (2 marks):**
- Replaces **free NaOH** (the cracking agent) with a **sodium phosphate buffer** system.
- The three phosphates — **Na₃PO₄ (trisodium), Na₂HPO₄ (disodium), NaH₂PO₄ (monosodium)** — buffer boiler water in the **pH range 9.0 – 11.0** without any free NaOH.
- The **Na : PO₄ molar ratio** is maintained at **2.85 : 1** (using the formula: $Na/PO_4 = \frac{2[Na_3PO_4] + [Na_2HPO_4]}{[Na_3PO_4] + [Na_2HPO_4] + [NaH_2PO_4]}$).
- At this ratio, free NaOH = 0, eliminating the driving force for caustic wicking.
- Additional safeguards:
  - Replace riveted joints with **welded joints** wherever possible.
  - Use **stress-relieved** plates.
  - Add **sodium nitrate (NaNO₃)** as an SCC inhibitor (10 : 1 to 30 : 1 ratio of NaNO₃ to total alkalinity).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often confuse "**Na : PO₄ ratio 2.85 : 1**" with "**Na₃PO₄ : Na₂HPO₄ ratio 2.85 : 1**". The correct KTU definition (from **Pfeiffer & Becker, 1931**) is the **Na-to-phosphate molar ratio**, not the ratio of two phosphate species. Lose 2 marks easily here.

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY KTU 2024 REVISION SUMMARY** — Read this list 3 times the night before the exam.

### A. Core Definitions (Memorise Word-for-Word)
- ✅ **Caustic Embrittlement** — Intergranular SCC of carbon steel boiler parts by concentrated NaOH in crevices.
- ✅ **Hot Corrosion** — Accelerated high-T attack by molten salt deposits (Na₂SO₄, V₂O₅).
- ✅ **Coal-ash corrosion** — Fireside attack by molten alkali iron trisulfate.
- ✅ **Dezincification** — Selective leaching of Zn from brass condenser tubes, leaving porous Cu.
- ✅ **Pilling–Bedworth Ratio** — $V_{oxide} / V_{metal}$; protective if 1 < PBR < 2.
- ✅ **Wagner's Theory** — Parabolic oxidation governed by ionic diffusion through defective scale.

### B. Key Numbers to Memorise
- ✅ Parabolic law: $x^2 = 2 k_p t$
- ✅ Faraday's constant: 96,485 C/mol
- ✅ Feedwater pH target: 8.5 – 9.5
- ✅ Boiler water pH: 10.5 – 11.0
- ✅ Dissolved O₂ limit: < 7 ppb (once-through boilers)
- ✅ Conductivity limit: < 0.2 µS/cm
- ✅ Corrosion rate constant: 87,600 (for t in hours) or 87.6 (for t in days)
- ✅ Na : PO₄ coordinated ratio: 2.85 : 1
- ✅ Condenser tube velocity: 1.8 – 2.4 m/s

### C. Alloys & Their PBR (Memorise the Trend)
- ✅ Mg → MgO: PBR ≈ 0.81 (non-protective)
- ✅ Al → Al₂O₃: PBR ≈ 1.28 (highly protective — best)
- ✅ Cr → Cr₂O₃: PBR ≈ 1.95 (protective)
- ✅ Fe → Fe₃O₄: PBR ≈ 1.77 (reasonably protective)
- ✅ Fe → Fe₂O₃: PBR ≈ 2.14 (marginally non-protective, spalls)
- ✅ Ni → NiO: PBR ≈ 1.65 (protective)

### D. Corrosion Control Hierarchy (Always Write This Order in Answers)
1. **Material selection** (use 9–12 % Cr steels, austenitic SS, superalloys).
2. **Surface coatings** (TBC, MCrAlY, chromizing, aluminizing).
3. **Water/steam chemistry control** (phosphate, hydrazine, amines, O₂ scavengers).
4. **Cathodic protection** (impressed current, sacrificial anodes in condensers/cooling water).
5. **Inhibitor dosing** (Zn-phosphate, tolyltriazole, molybdate, nitrite for closed loops).
6. **Design improvements** (avoid crevices, lower velocities, eliminate galvanic couples).
7. **Monitoring** (LPR probes, ER probes, hydrogen probes, online chemistry analysers).

### E. Two-Minute Comparison Tables to Practise Drawing

| Feature | Type I Hot Corrosion | Type II Hot Corrosion |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | 850 – 950 °C | 650 – 750 °C |
| Salt | Na₂SO₄ alone | Na₂SO₄ + CoSO₄/NiSO₄ |
| Mechanism | Basic fluxing + sulfidation | Acidic fluxing + pitting |
| Morphology | Broad front attack | Localised pitting |
| Source | Marine Na + fuel S | Co/Ni from corroded alloys |

| Feature | Carbon Steel | T-91 (9 Cr) | Inconel 625 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Max T (°C) | 460 | 620 | 1050 |
| Protective scale | Fe₃O₄ | Cr-rich spinel | Cr₂O₃ + Al₂O₃ |
| Application | Economiser | Supercritical tubes | Turbine blades |
| Cost (relative) | 1× | 4× | 50× |

### F. Three KTU "Trick" Questions to Watch For
1. ❓ "Why is pure iron not used for superheater tubes despite being cheap?" → PBR(Fe₂O₃) > 2 → spalls; only 9–12 % Cr steels form Cr₂O₃ with PBR ~ 1.95.
2. ❓ "Why are titanium tubes preferred in seawater-cooled condensers?" → Ti forms passive TiO₂ (PBR ≈ 1.95), immune to Cl⁻ pitting, no dezincification, biofouling-resistant.
3. ❓ "Why is DEHA replacing hydrazine in modern plants?" → DEHA is **less carcinogenic**, works at lower T (saves fuel in cold startups), and the by-products (DEA, acetic acid) are more environmentally acceptable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Examiner Tip:** Always **start your 14-mark answer with a one-line definition**, then move to mechanism, then derivation, then examples, then control — in that exact order. KTU examiners reward **structured, diagram-supported answers** with **bullet points and tables** over dense paragraphs. Maximum marks go to students who cite the **specific alloy (T-91, IN-738), specific number (Na:PO₄ = 2.85:1), and specific equation ($x^2 = 2k_p t$)** — never write vague statements like "oxides form on the surface."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT527 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Corrosion Control Processes — The Foundational Definition

In the context of **KTU PECHT527 (Corrosion Engineering, 2024 Scheme)**, **Module 4: Process** refers to the systematic, engineered **electrochemical and surface-engineering methods employed to control, suppress, or arrest the corrosion of metals and alloys in industrial service environments**. These "processes" are the practical interventions that convert a reactive metal surface into a thermodynamically stable, kinetically protected state.

The principal corrosion control processes covered under this module are:

1. **Cathodic Protection (CP)** – Making the metal the cathode of an electrochemical cell.
2. **Anodic Protection (AP)** – Making the metal the anode but passivating it.
3. **Protective Coatings** – Barrier and sacrificial metallic/non-metallic layers.
4. **Corrosion Inhibitors** – Chemical additives that reduce corrosion rate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 of PECHT527 primarily emphasizes the **electrochemical protection processes** — namely **Cathodic Protection** and **Anodic Protection** — including their design parameters, impressed current systems, sacrificial anode systems, and the use of reference electrodes.

---

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a busy highway junction with a single narrow bridge that keeps collapsing every monsoon. The "corrosion" here is the bridge crumbling. You have **four** ways to fix it:

- **Cathodic Protection** → You build a parallel "decoy" bridge (sacrificial anode) that rusts away first, sparing the main bridge. *OR* you connect the main bridge to a power source (rectifier) that pushes protective current into it.
- **Anodic Protection** → You paint the bridge with a special "smart" coating (passive film) controlled by a tiny electronics box, which forms a tough oxide skin only when the bridge is in a specific state.
- **Coatings** → You wrap the bridge in a waterproof tarpaulin so rain never touches the steel.
- **Inhibitors** → You add a chemical to the rainwater (environment) that neutralizes the acid, slowing rust formation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** All four processes aim to shift the **corrosion potential ($E_{corr}$)** away from the active corrosion region, either by **polarization** (CP/AP) or by **physical/chemical isolation** (coatings/inhibitors).

---

## Key Definitions (Syllus-Aligned)

| Term | Formal Definition |
|------|-------------------|
| **Cathodic Protection** | An electrochemical technique to prevent corrosion by making the metal structure the **cathode** of an electrochemical cell. |
| **Anodic Protection** | An electrochemical technique that maintains a metal in the **passive region** by controlled anodic polarization using an external power source. |
| **Galvanic (Sacrificial) Anode** | A more electrochemically active metal (e.g., Zn, Mg, Al) that corrodes preferentially to protect the structure. |
| **Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP)** | A CP system using an external DC power source and an inert anode to drive protective current. |
| **Reference Electrode** | A stable, reproducible electrode (e.g., Cu/CuSO₄, Ag/AgCl, SCE) used to measure the potential of the protected structure. |
| **Passivation** | Formation of a thin, adherent, protective oxide film on the metal surface (e.g., Cr₂O₃ on stainless steel). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric:** Corrosion rate in CP design is typically expressed in **mils per year (mpy)** or **millimeters per year (mm/yr)**, where **1 mpy = 0.0254 mm/yr**. Current density is in **mA/m²** or **μA/cm²**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cathodic Protection (CP) — Deep Theory

Cathodic Protection is the most widely deployed electrochemical corrosion control process for **buried pipelines, marine structures, storage tanks, and reinforced concrete**. It works on Faraday's principle: **if you supply electrons to the metal faster than they are lost by anodic dissolution, the net anodic reaction is suppressed.**

### The Polarization Principle

In an actively corroding metal, the local anodes (where metal dissolves: $M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^-$) and local cathodes (where reduction occurs: $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$) operate at the **corrosion potential $E_{corr}$** and the **corrosion current $I_{corr}$**.

By applying an external cathodic current:

- The metal is **polarized cathodically**.
- The local anode current is reduced to zero (or near zero).
- The metal becomes uniformly a cathode — hence the name.

### The Two Methods of Cathodic Protection

**Method A: Galvanic (Sacrificial) Anode Cathodic Protection (GACP)**

- The protected structure is connected to a more **active** metal (Zn, Mg, or Al alloys).
- The active metal acts as the anode and dissolves preferentially.
- **No external power source** is required.
- Best for: small, well-coated structures in low-resistivity soils or seawater.

**Method B: Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP)**

- An **external DC rectifier** drives current from an inert anode (e.g., graphite, Ti/MMO, scrap iron) to the structure.
- The structure is polarized to a more negative potential.
- **Adjustable output** makes it suitable for large structures and high-resistivity environments.
- Best for: long pipelines, offshore platforms, tank farms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two methods?** GACP is **self-regulating** but limited by the driving voltage (~0.2–0.5 V). ICCP can deliver high current over long distances, but requires a power source and maintenance.

---

## 2.2 Anodic Protection (AP) — Deep Theory

Anodic Protection is conceptually the **opposite** of CP. It is applicable **only to metals that exhibit passivity** in the service environment (e.g., stainless steels, carbon steel in concentrated H₂SO₄, Ti, Ni alloys).

The metal is connected to the **positive terminal** of a DC source, making it the anode. By carefully controlling the potential within the **passive region** of the anodic polarization curve, a stable passive film is maintained.

### The Anodic Polarization Curve — The Heart of AP

The anodic polarization curve has three key regions:

1. **Active Region** — Metal dissolves actively (high current).
2. **Passive Region** — A protective oxide film forms; current drops sharply to a low, stable value ($I_{pass}$).
3. **Transpassive Region** — Film breaks down; current rises again (often with oxygen evolution).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Anodic Protection holds the metal within the **passive region** — between the **primary passive potential ($E_{pp}$)** and the **transpassive breakdown potential ($E_{b}$)**.

### Critical Design Parameter

- **Protection Potential ($E_{prot}$):** Maintained within the passive window, typically referenced to a reference electrode.
- **Reference Electrodes in AP:** Often **Ag/AgCl** or **SCE** for sulfuric acid service; **Mo** or **Pt** for some specialized services.

---

## 2.3 Comparison: Cathodic vs. Anodic Protection

| Parameter | Cathodic Protection | Anodic Protection |
|-----------|---------------------|-------------------|
| Polarization Direction | Cathodic | Anodic |
| Applicable Metals | All metals | Only passivating metals |
| Power Source | Optional (GACP) or required (ICCP) | Always required (potentiostat/rectifier) |
| Typical Application | Pipelines, marine, tanks | H₂SO₄ storage tanks, steel in conc. acids |
| Hazard | Hydrogen embrittlement, overprotection | Acidification, pitting if mistuned |
| Reference Electrode | Cu/CuSO₄ (soil/water), Ag/AgCl (sea) | Ag/AgCl, SCE, Mo |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA CHEAT SHEET — Master These for KTU Board Exam**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $W = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | **Faraday's Law** — mass of metal consumed | W (g), I (A), t (s) |
| 2 | $I_{prot} = i_{prot} \cdot A$ | **Protection current** = current density × area | I (A), i (A/m²), A (m²) |
| 3 | $CR = \dfrac{0.00327 \cdot i \cdot EW}{\rho}$ | **Corrosion rate** from current density | CR (mm/yr), i (μA/cm²), ρ (g/cm³) |
| 4 | $R_{anode} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L}{A}$ | **Anode-to-soil resistance** (for ground bed) | R (Ω), ρ (Ω·m) |
| 5 | $I = \dfrac{\Delta E}{R_{total}}$ | **Ohm's Law** for CP circuit | I (A), E (V), R (Ω) |
| 6 | $W_{anode} = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{C \cdot u}$ | **Anode lifetime calculation** | W (kg), t (h), C (A·h/kg), u (utilization) |
| 7 | $\eta = \vert E_{prot} - E_{corr} \vert$ | **Polarization (overpotential)** | η (V) |
| 8 | $E_{Ag/AgCl} = E_{SCE} - 0.045\ V$ | **Reference electrode conversion** | V |
| 9 | $E_{Cu/CuSO_4} = E_{SCE} + 0.318\ V$ | **Reference electrode conversion** | V |
| 10 | $P = I \cdot V$ | **Rectifier power** for ICCP | P (W) |

Where:
- **$F$** = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol**
- **$n$** = number of electrons transferred
- **$EW$** = Equivalent weight of metal (g/eq)
- **$\rho$** = Density of metal (g/cm³)
- **$C$** = Anode capacity (A·h/kg)

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Offshore Platforms:** ICCP with Ti/MMO anodes protects the submerged hull and jacket.
- **Buried Pipelines:** Combination of coating + ICCP — coating reduces the current demand, CP handles coating holidays.
- **Cross-Country Oil/Gas Pipelines:** GACP with Mg anodes for short sections; ICCP stations every 30–60 km.
- **Sulfuric Acid Plants:** Anodic protection of carbon steel storage tanks at 90–98% H₂SO₄ concentration.
- **Reinforced Concrete Bridges:** Cathodic prevention using Ti-mesh anodes embedded in concrete overlays.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cathodic Protection Current Requirement (Faraday + Area Method)

### Problem Statement
A buried steel pipeline (length $L = 5\ km$, diameter $D = 0.5\ m$) is to be protected by ICCP. Determine the protection current required, given:
- Required current density: $i_{prot} = 10\ mA/m^2$
- Soil resistivity considered in anode bed (not needed for this step)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Calculate the surface area of the pipeline exposed to soil.**

$$
A = \pi \cdot D \cdot L
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \pi \times 0.5\ m \times 5000\ m \\
A &= 3.1416 \times 0.5 \times 5000 \\
A &= 7854\ m^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the protection current formula.**

$$
I_{prot} = i_{prot} \cdot A
$$

**Step 4: Convert $i_{prot}$ to consistent units (A/m²).**

$$
i_{prot} = 10\ mA/m^2 = 0.010\ A/m^2
$$

**Step 5: Final computation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{prot} &= 0.010\ A/m^2 \times 7854\ m^2 \\
I_{prot} &= 78.54\ A
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** $I_{prot} = 78.54\ A$ — this is the total DC current that the rectifier must deliver to fully cathodically protect the pipeline.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Anode Lifetime Calculation (ICCP Design)

### Problem Statement
An ICCP system uses a graphite anode bed. Determine the **anode lifetime** for the following:
- Anode current output: $I = 30\ A$
- Anode consumption rate: $C = 4.0\ A \cdot h/kg$ (graphite)
- Anode mass: $W = 50\ kg$ per anode
- Utilization factor: $u = 0.80$ (80% of anode can be consumed before replacement)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Write the anode lifetime equation.**

$$
t = \dfrac{W \cdot C \cdot u}{I}
$$

Where:
- $t$ = lifetime in hours
- $W$ = anode mass in kg
- $C$ = theoretical capacity in A·h/kg
- $u$ = utilization factor (dimensionless)
- $I$ = current output in A

**Step 2: Substitute numerical values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \dfrac{50\ kg \times 4.0\ A \cdot h/kg \times 0.80}{30\ A} \\
t &= \dfrac{50 \times 4.0 \times 0.80}{30} \\
t &= \dfrac{160}{30} \\
t &= 5.333\ hours
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Convert to years (engineering-relevant unit).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{years} &= \dfrac{5.333\ h}{8760\ h/yr} \\
t_{years} &= 6.09 \times 10^{-4}\ years \\
t_{days} &= \dfrac{5.333\ h}{24\ h/day} = 0.222\ days
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** The lifetime is **5.33 hours** — clearly indicating that the anode mass is severely undersized. **This demonstrates why ICCP anode beds use hundreds of kilograms of material in practice.**

### Practical Recalculation
For a **20-year design life**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{required} &= 20\ yr \times 8760\ h/yr = 175,200\ h \\
W_{required} &= \dfrac{I \cdot t}{C \cdot u} = \dfrac{30 \times 175,200}{4.0 \times 0.80} \\
W_{required} &= \dfrac{5,256,000}{3.2} = 1,642,500\ kg
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Corrosion Rate from Current Density (Faraday's Law)

### Problem Statement
For a carbon steel sample in aerated water, the measured corrosion current density is $i_{corr} = 25\ \mu A/cm^2$. Calculate the **corrosion rate in mpy**.

Given:
- Equivalent weight of Fe: $EW = 27.92\ g/eq$ (since $M_{Fe} = 55.85$, $n = 2$)
- Density of Fe: $\rho = 7.87\ g/cm^3$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Faraday's Law for corrosion rate.**

$$
CR = \dfrac{K \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho}
$$

where $K$ is a conversion constant. For $CR$ in **mpy** and $i_{corr}$ in **μA/cm²**:

$$
K = 0.00327\ \dfrac{mpy \cdot cm^3}{\mu A \cdot g}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CR &= \dfrac{0.00327 \times 25 \times 27.92}{7.87} \\
CR &= \dfrac{0.00327 \times 698.0}{7.87} \\
CR &= \dfrac{2.2825}{7.87} \\
CR &= 0.2900\ mpy
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Convert to mm/yr.**

$$
CR = 0.2900\ mpy \times 0.0254\ mm/mpy = 0.00737\ mm/yr
$$

> **Result:** $CR \approx 0.29\ mpy$ — this is an **excellent** corrosion rate, typical of well-protected systems.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection of CP Method

| Application | Environment | Recommended CP Method | Reason |
|-------------|-------------|------------------------|--------|
| Short pipeline (<1 km), coated | Low-resistivity soil | GACP (Zn or Mg) | No power needed, simple |
| Long pipeline (>10 km) | Variable soil | ICCP | Long-range current distribution |
| Marine hull | Seawater | ICCP with Ti/MMO | High current demand, adjustable |
| Submerged offshore jacket | Seawater | GACP (Al anodes) | Simple, reliable, no power |
| Ship ballast tank | Marine/atmospheric | ICCP or GACP | Confined space, accessibility |
| Underground storage tank | Soil | GACP (Mg) | Small, well-coated |
| Internal surface of H₂SO₄ tank | Concentrated H₂SO₄ | **Anodic Protection** | Only method that works for passivating metal in conc. acid |

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Logic:** Use the **4-selection criteria** — **(1)** Metal passivates? → Consider AP. **(2)** Power available? → ICCP. **(3)** Size of structure? → Large = ICCP, Small = GACP. **(4)** Environment resistivity? → High ρ = ICCP (GACP has limited driving voltage).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Cathodic Protection System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Rectifier / DC Power Source] --> B[Anode Ground Bed]
    B -->|Current through soil| C[Buried Pipeline or Structure]
    C -->|Negative cable back| A
    R[Reference Electrode Cu/CuSO4] -->|Measures potential| C
    R -->|Signal| A
    S[Structure to be Protected] --> C
```

**Description of Flow:**
- The **rectifier** (A) converts AC to DC and supplies the protective current.
- The **anode ground bed** (B) — typically graphite or Ti/MMO anodes — disperses current into the soil.
- Current flows through the **soil electrolyte** to the **protected structure** (C).
- The structure is connected back to the rectifier's negative terminal, completing the circuit.
- The **reference electrode** (R) continuously monitors the structure's potential and feeds back to the rectifier for automatic control.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection (GACP)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sacrificial Anode Zn / Mg / Al] -->|Electron flow| B[Protected Steel Structure]
    B -->|Ionic current through electrolyte| A
    E[Electrolyte Soil or Seawater] -.-> A
    E -.-> B
```

**Description of Flow:**
- The **sacrificial anode** (A) is more active (more negative $E^\circ$) than steel.
- It dissolves: $Zn \rightarrow Zn^{2+} + 2e^-$
- Electrons flow through the metallic path to the protected structure (B).
- The structure is polarized cathodically — no corrosion.
- The electrolyte (E) completes the ionic circuit.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Anodic Protection System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[DC Power Source Potentiostat] -->|Positive lead| M[Metal to be Protected]
    M -->|Cathode of AP cell| C[Cathode Stainless or Pt]
    C -->|Back to negative lead| P
    RE[Reference Electrode AgAgCl or SCE] -->|Feedback signal| P
    RE -->|Potential measurement| M
    F[Passive Oxide Film e.g. Cr2O3] --> M
```

**Description of Flow:**
- The **DC power source / potentiostat** (P) maintains the metal (M) at a precise potential within the **passive region**.
- The **cathode** (C) completes the electrochemical circuit.
- The **reference electrode** (RE) provides closed-loop feedback.
- A **passive film** (F) — typically Cr₂O₃ for stainless steel or FeSO₄-derived film for steel in H₂SO₄ — forms and is maintained.

---

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Corrosion Process Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Metal to be protected] --> Q1{Does metal show passive behavior?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is electrolyte aggressive and uniform?}
    Q1 -->|No| CP[Use Cathodic Protection]
    Q2 -->|Yes conc H2SO4| AP[Use Anodic Protection]
    Q2 -->|No| CP
    CP --> Q3{Power source available?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| ICCP[Impressed Current CP]
    Q3 -->|No| GACP[Galvanic Anode CP]
    AP --> Q4{Reference electrode stable?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| APDesign[Design AP System with Potentiostat]
    Q4 -->|No| CP
```

**Description of Decision Flow:**
1. **First Decision:** Does the metal passivate? If **no** → CP only. If **yes** → consider AP.
2. **Second Decision (if passivating):** Is the environment suited to AP? (Concentrated acids, oxidizing conditions). If **no** → fall back to CP.
3. **Third Decision (CP path):** Is external power available? If **yes** → ICCP. If **no** → GACP.

---

## 4.5 Schematic — Anodic Polarization Curve (Reference)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AnodicPolarization[E vs log i Anodic Polarization Curve]
        A1[Active Region] -->|Increasing E| A2[Critical Passive Current Icrit]
        A2 -->|Epp| A3[Passive Region Low Ipass]
        A3 -->|Eb| A4[Transpassive Region]
    end
    AP_Window[Anodic Protection operates here] --> A3
    CP_Region[Cathodic Protection polarizes to left of Ecorr] --> A1
```

**Description:**
- The **active region** (A1) is where unprotected metals corrode rapidly.
- The **passive region** (A3) — between the **primary passive potential ($E_{pp}$)** and the **breakdown potential ($E_{b}$)** — is where Anodic Protection holds the metal.
- The **transpassive region** (A4) is to be avoided (oxygen evolution, pitting).
- **Cathodic Protection** polarizes the metal to potentials **more negative than $E_{corr}$** (i.e., to the left of the active region), suppressing anodic dissolution.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Define Cathodic Protection. List the two methods of applying it.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Cathodic Protection (CP)** is an electrochemical method of corrosion control in which the metal to be protected is made the **cathode** of an electrochemical cell, thereby suppressing its anodic dissolution reaction.
>
> The two methods of applying cathodic protection are:
> 1. **Galvanic (Sacrificial) Anode Cathodic Protection (GACP)** — using a more active metal such as Zn, Mg, or Al.
> 2. **Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP)** — using an external DC power source (rectifier) and an inert anode.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is Anodic Protection? Name two metals/environments where it is applied.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Anodic Protection (AP)** is an electrochemical corrosion control method in which the metal is maintained in the **passive region** of its anodic polarization curve by controlled anodic polarization using an external DC power source.
>
> **Applications:**
> 1. **Carbon steel in concentrated sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄)** — typically 90–98% H₂SO₄ storage tanks.
> 2. **Stainless steel (e.g., 304, 316) in oxidizing acid environments** — e.g., nitric acid, chromic acid.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students commonly lose **2–3 marks** by **confusing the direction of polarization** in CP vs. AP. Remember: **CP polarizes the metal cathodically (negative); AP polarizes the metal anodically (positive).**
> - Another frequent mistake: **forgetting the units in the anode lifetime formula** ($A \cdot h/kg$ vs. $A \cdot s/kg$).
> - For ICCP numericals, students often **omit the rectifier power requirement** ($P = I \times V$).
> - **Reference electrode sign convention is critical**: $E_{Cu/CuSO_4} = +0.318\ V$ vs. SCE, $E_{Ag/AgCl} = +0.222\ V$ vs. SHE (saturated KCl). Always specify the reference!

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a) With a neat sketch, explain the Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP) system for a buried pipeline. What are the essential components? (7 marks)**

**(b) An ICCP system protects a buried steel pipeline of length 8 km and diameter 0.6 m. The required protection current density is 15 mA/m². The rectifier efficiency is 70% and the rectifier delivers DC at 30 V. Calculate: (i) Total protection current, (ii) Power consumption of the rectifier, (iii) Cost of electricity per month at ₹7 per kWh if the system operates continuously. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) ICCP System Description (7 marks):**

An **Impressed Current Cathodic Protection system** is a CP system that uses an external DC source (rectifier) to drive protective current through the soil to a buried pipeline.

**Essential Components:**

1. **DC Rectifier (Power Source):** Converts AC mains to DC; typically 0–50 V, 0–100 A; often equipped with automatic potential control.
2. **Anode Ground Bed:** Inert anodes (graphite, high-silicon cast iron, Ti/MMO) buried in a low-resistivity soil/coke breeze backfill, connected to the **positive** terminal of the rectifier.
3. **Protected Structure (Cathode):** The buried pipeline, connected to the **negative** terminal.
4. **Reference Electrode:** Cu/CuSO₄ saturated reference electrode placed near the pipeline to monitor the **pipe-to-soil potential** (target: $-850\ mV$ for steel, with respect to Cu/CuSO₄).
5. **Cables and Junction Boxes:** For interconnection and surge protection.
6. **Test Stations:** For periodic measurement of pipe-to-soil potential.

**Working Principle:**
The rectifier impresses a DC current from the **anode** (ground bed) through the **soil electrolyte** to the **pipeline**. The pipeline is polarized cathodically, suppressing anodic dissolution. The reference electrode provides feedback for the rectifier to maintain the desired potential.

> **Key Criterion:** Pipe-to-soil potential more negative than **$-850\ mV$ (vs. Cu/CuSO₄)** is generally accepted as full protection (per NACE / ISO 15589 standards).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $L = 8\ km = 8000\ m$
- $D = 0.6\ m$
- $i_{prot} = 15\ mA/m^2 = 0.015\ A/m^2$
- Rectifier efficiency $\eta_r = 70\% = 0.70$
- $V = 30\ V$ (DC output voltage)
- Electricity cost = ₹7 per kWh

**(i) Total Protection Current:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \pi \cdot D \cdot L = 3.1416 \times 0.6 \times 8000 \\
A &= 15,079.6\ m^2 \\
I_{prot} &= i_{prot} \times A \\
I_{prot} &= 0.015 \times 15,079.6 \\
I_{prot} &= 226.19\ A
\end{aligned}
$$

> **$I_{prot} \approx 226.2\ A$** ✓

**(ii) Power Consumption of the Rectifier:**

DC power delivered to the system:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{DC} &= V \times I_{prot} = 30 \times 226.2 = 6786\ W = 6.786\ kW
\end{aligned}
$$

Rectifier input power (accounting for efficiency):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{AC} &= \dfrac{P_{DC}}{\eta_r} = \dfrac{6.786}{0.70} = 9.694\ kW
\end{aligned}
$$

> **$P_{AC} \approx 9.69\ kW$** ✓

**(iii) Monthly Electricity Cost:**

Energy per month (continuous operation, 30 days):

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= 30 \times 24 = 720\ hours \\
E &= P_{AC} \times t = 9.694 \times 720 = 6979.7\ kWh
\end{aligned}
$$

Cost:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost} &= 6979.7 \times 7 = ₹48,857.9
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Monthly cost ≈ ₹48,858** ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 — Alternative]

**(a) Explain the principle and design of Anodic Protection with a suitable anodic polarization curve. What are its advantages and limitations? (7 marks)**

**(b) A carbon steel storage tank containing 98% sulfuric acid is to be protected by anodic protection. The passive current density is 0.05 mA/cm², the passive potential range is from +0.4 V to +1.2 V (SCE), and the tank's wetted area is 50 m². Calculate: (i) The total current required, (ii) The power required if the AP rectifier output is 2.5 V, (iii) Comment on the energy efficiency compared to cathodic protection of a similar structure. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Anodic Protection — Principle and Design (7 marks):**

**Principle:**
Anodic protection is based on the phenomenon of **passivity**. When a metal (that exhibits passivation in a given environment) is held at a potential within the **passive region** of its anodic polarization curve, a thin, adherent, and protective oxide film forms on its surface. This film drastically reduces the corrosion current to a low, stable value ($I_{pass}$).

**Design Steps:**

1. **Determine the anodic polarization curve** of the metal in the service environment (experimentally using a potentiostat).
2. **Identify the passive region:** Locate $E_{pp}$ (primary passive potential) and $E_{b}$ (breakdown / transpassive potential).
3. **Select a set potential** within the passive window, typically in the middle or lower passive region for safety.
4. **Choose a reference electrode** compatible with the environment:
   - For H₂SO₄: Ag/AgCl or SCE (with salt bridge) or Hg/Hg₂SO₄.
5. **Use a potentiostat or a feedback-controlled DC source** to maintain the set potential automatically.
6. **Size the cathode** (auxiliary electrode) — usually stainless steel or platinum-clad material.
7. **Verify** that the measured current stabilizes at $I_{pass}$.

**Advantages:**
- Very low current requirement (often $\mu A$ to $mA$ levels).
- Highly effective in aggressive media like concentrated H₂SO₄.
- Excellent for metals that passivate (stainless steels, Ti, Ni alloys, carbon steel in conc. H₂SO₄).
- Corrosion rates can be reduced to < 0.1 mpy.

**Limitations:**
- **Only applicable to passivating metals** — cannot be used for carbon steel in dilute acids or for non-passivating metals (e.g., Cu in HCl).
- Requires **precise potential control** — outside the passive window leads to accelerated corrosion.
- **Cannot be used where pitting or SCC is a concern** without careful monitoring.
- Requires **reliable reference electrode** — corrosion of the reference can lead to system failure.
- Higher initial instrumentation cost.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $i_{pass} = 0.05\ mA/cm^2$
- $A = 50\ m^2$
- $V_{AP} = 2.5\ V$

**(i) Total Current Required:**

Convert units: $i_{pass} = 0.05\ mA/cm^2 = 0.05 \times 10^{-3}\ A / 10^{-4}\ m^2 = 0.5\ A/m^2$

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{pass} &= i_{pass} \times A \\
I_{pass} &= 0.5 \times 50 \\
I_{pass} &= 25\ A
\end{aligned}
$$

> **$I_{pass} = 25\ A$** ✓

**(ii) Power Required:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= V \times I = 2.5 \times 25 = 62.5\ W = 0.0625\ kW
\end{aligned}
$$

> **$P = 62.5\ W$** ✓

**(iii) Energy Efficiency Comparison:**

Compare with CP for a similar 50 m² structure:
- CP current density: ~$10\ mA/m^2$ (typical)
- CP current: $0.010 \times 50 = 0.5\ A$
- CP rectifier output: typically 20–30 V → CP power: $0.5 \times 25 = 12.5\ W$

> **Observation:** For this specific case, CP would use **less power** (0.5 A vs. 25 A), BUT CP is **not applicable** here because **carbon steel in 98% H₂SO₄ actively corrodes when made the cathode** (hydrogen evolution + acid attack). Therefore, **Anodic Protection is the only viable electrochemical method** for this service. AP's high current is justified by **near-elimination** of the corrosion rate (from > 100 mpy unprotected to < 1 mpy protected).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Module 4: Process**

- ✅ **Cathodic Protection (CP)** = Make the metal the **cathode** → polarize in the **negative** direction → suppress anodic dissolution $M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^-$.
- ✅ **Anodic Protection (AP)** = Make the metal the **anode** → polarize in the **positive** direction → maintain within the **passive region** ($E_{pp} < E < E_b$).
- ✅ Two CP methods: **GACP** (Sacrificial Anode: Zn/Mg/Al, no power needed) and **ICCP** (Impressed Current: rectifier + inert anode, requires power).
- ✅ Three protection criteria for CP: (1) $-850\ mV$ vs. Cu/CuSO₄, (2) $-850\ mV$ vs. CSE with IR drop consideration, (3) $300\ mV$ polarization shift.
- ✅ **Reference electrodes:** Cu/CuSO₄ (soil/sea), Ag/AgCl (sea/concrete), SCE (lab), Mo (high-temp/concrete), Zn (seawater portable).
- ✅ **Anode materials for ICCP:** Graphite, high-silicon cast iron, Ti/MMO (Mixed Metal Oxide), scrap iron.
- ✅ **Sacrificial anode materials:** Magnesium (most active, soil), Aluminum alloys (most efficient, seawater), Zinc (general, warm seawater).
- ✅ **Faraday's Law:** $W = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ — underpins all CP current calculations.
- ✅ **Protection current density** for steel: ~$10\text{–}20\ mA/m^2$ in soil, ~$150\text{–}300\ mA/m^2$ in seawater (bare), ~$0.1\text{–}1\ mA/m^2$ (well-coated).
- ✅ **Anode lifetime** = $W \cdot C \cdot u / I$ — design for **20–40 years** in practice.
- ✅ **AP is restricted to:** stainless steels, Ti, Ni alloys, carbon steel in conc. H₂SO₄ (>78%), conc. HNO₃, etc.
- ✅ **AP hazards:** (1) acid attack if pH drops at anode surface, (2) transpassive dissolution if $E > E_b$, (3) pitting if chlorides present.
- ✅ **CP hazards:** (1) Hydrogen embrittlement of high-strength steels, (2) coating blistering (alkaline generation), (3) stray current interference on neighboring structures, (4) overprotection causing calcareous deposit issues.
- ✅ **Combined systems:** Coating + CP = "**belt and suspenders**" approach — coating reduces CP current demand by 90–99%, CP handles coating holidays.
- ✅ **NACE/ISO Standards:** ISO 15589 (CP for pipelines), NACE SP0169 (CP for buried/external), NACE SP0108 (CP for internal).
- ✅ **KTU Exam Focus Areas:** (1) Difference between CP and AP, (2) Numerical problems on Faraday's law and anode life, (3) Sketch of ICCP system, (4) Anodic polarization curve labeling.

> **🎯 Final Exam Tip:** Always specify the **reference electrode** in any potential-related answer. A statement like "$-850\ mV$" without "vs. Cu/CuSO₄" will lose marks!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 4
