<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Defining Petrochemical Industry in the Context of Corrosion Engineering

The **petrochemical industry** is a specialized branch of the chemical industry that derives its primary raw materials — **feedstocks** — from crude oil (petroleum) and natural gas. The sector converts hydrocarbons into commercially valuable chemical products such as olefins (ethylene, propylene), aromatics (benzene, toluene, xylene), and their derivatives (plastics, synthetic fibers, rubbers, detergents, solvents, fertilizers).

In **Corrosion Engineering (PECHT527)**, the petrochemical sector is treated as one of the most aggressive service environments encountered in industry. The combination of **high temperatures (200 °C–900 °C)**, **high pressures (up to 200 bar)**, **acidic species (H₂S, CO₂, organic acids)**, and **water/steam** creates ideal thermodynamic and kinetic conditions for several forms of degradation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Petrochemical corrosion refers to the electrochemical and chemical degradation of metallic materials used in the processing, storage, and transport of crude oil, natural gas, refined products, and their derivatives, occurring under conditions of elevated temperature, pressure, and corrosive species.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a long kitchen pipeline carrying boiling vinegar, lemon juice (citric acid), salt water, and steam — all simultaneously and at high pressure. The inner walls of that pipe would slowly dissolve, crack, and pit. Now scale this kitchen up by a factor of 10⁶ and you have the petrochemical process environment. Crude oil is not just "oil"; it is a chemically aggressive soup containing **sour gases (H₂S)**, **sweet corrosive gases (CO₂)**, **chlorides**, **organic acids (naphthenic acids)**, and **dissolved salts**, all flowing through miles of piping, distillation columns, reactors, and heat exchangers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world metric for context:** A single delayed coker unit in a refinery may process **40,000–60,000 barrels per day**, with overhead vapors at **420–450 °C** and pressure around **4–5 bar**, exposing carbon steel to severe naphthenic acid corrosion and sulfidation simultaneously.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants for Petrochemical Corrosion

| Parameter | Typical Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Operating temperature | 200 – 900 | °C |
| Operating pressure | 1 – 200 | bar |
| H₂S partial pressure | 0.0001 – 50 | bar |
| CO₂ partial pressure | 0.5 – 100 | bar |
| Chloride concentration | 0 – 300,000 | ppm |
| Naphthenic acid number (TAN) | 0.1 – 8.0 | mg KOH/g |
| Velocity in pipes | 1 – 60 | m/s |
| pH of sour water | 4.5 – 9.0 | – |
| Standard Faraday constant (F) | **96,485** | C/mol |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Petrochemical Process Flow — Where Corrosion Happens

A modern integrated petrochemical complex is broadly divided into two halves:

1. **Upstream / Refinery Units (Primary processing of crude oil):**
   - Atmospheric & Vacuum Distillation (CDU/VDU)
   - Hydrodesulfurization (HDS) & Hydrocracking
   - Fluidized Catalytic Cracking (FCC)
   - Delayed Coking
   - Catalytic Reforming

2. **Downstream / Petrochemical Units (Conversion of refined streams into chemicals):**
   - Steam Crackers (olefin plants)
   - Catalytic Reforming (aromatics plants)
   - Polymerization units
   - Ethylene Oxide / Glycol units

Each unit exhibits a **signature corrosion mechanism** dictated by its operating window.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Concept — "Corrosion Mapping":** Always match **Process Stream Composition + Temperature + Pressure + Velocity** to a specific corrosion mechanism. This is the standard KTU Part-B (14-mark) answer approach.

## 2.2 Major Corrosion Mechanisms in Petrochemical Service

### 2.2.1 Sulfidation (Sulfidic Corrosion)
- **Chemistry:** Reaction of metal atoms (Fe, Cr, Ni) with sulfur species (H₂S, elemental S, organic sulfides) at high temperatures.
- **Temperature range:** **230 °C – 540 °C**.
- **Affected units:** Atmospheric and vacuum distillation bottoms, hydrocracker feed/effluent exchangers, coker fractionators.
- **Mechanism:** Sulfur diffuses inward; chromium-rich sulfide scales form. If Cr is insufficient, porous FeS forms, leading to rapid metal loss.

### 2.2.2 Naphthenic Acid Corrosion (NAC)
- **Chemistry:** Organic acids (R-COOH) of molecular weight 150–700 in crude oil react with iron at 220 °C – 425 °C.
- **Indicator:** **Total Acid Number (TAN)** in mg KOH/g.
- **Critical TAN:** Above **0.5 mg KOH/g** → risk; above **2.0 mg KOH/g** → severe.
- **Affected areas:** Distillation column internals (trays, pumps, elbows) in CDU/VDU.

### 2.2.3 Sour Water Corrosion
- **Chemistry:** H₂S dissolved in condensed water forms weak acidic solution.
- **Reaction:** $\text{Fe} + \text{H}_2\text{S} \rightarrow \text{FeS} + \text{H}_2$
- **Affected units:** Overhead systems of distillation columns, sour water strippers.

### 2.2.4 High-Temperature Hydrogen Attack (HTHA)
- **Chemistry:** At high H₂ partial pressure and temperature, hydrogen diffuses into steel and reacts with carbides:
  $\text{Fe}_3\text{C} + 2\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 3\text{Fe} + \text{CH}_4$
- **Methane accumulates** at grain boundaries, causing internal fissuring and decarburization.
- **Affected units:** Hydrocracker reactors, hydrotreater vessels, ammonia plants.

### 2.2.5 CO₂ Corrosion (Sweet Corrosion)
- **Dominant in:** Gas processing, amine systems, LNG plants.
- **Mechanism:** $\text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{CO}_3$; carbonic acid reduces to $\text{HCO}_3^-$ then attacks steel.
- **Key variable:** **CO₂ partial pressure**.

### 2.2.6 Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)
- **Polythionic acid SCC** in FCC main fractionators / reactor effluent systems.
- **Caustic embrittlement** in caustic-treated streams.
- **Amine SCC** in gas-treating columns (carbon steel + amine + CO₂ = cracking).

### 2.2.7 Erosion–Corrosion
- **Causes:** High-velocity two-phase flow, entrained catalyst particles, sand.
- **Affected areas:** Elbows, tees, valve trim, pump impellers, transfer lines of FCC and coker units.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **mandatory** for solving KTU Part-B numerical/analytical questions on petrochemical corrosion.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units | Boundary / Validity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{87600 \times W}{D \times A \times t}$ | Corrosion rate from weight loss | mm/yr | $W$ = mass loss (g), $D$ = density (g/cm³), $A$ = area (cm²), $t$ = time (h) |
| 2 | $\text{TAN} = \dfrac{(V \times N \times 56.1)}{W}$ | Total Acid Number | mg KOH/g | $V$ = KOH vol (mL), $N$ = normality, $W$ = sample (g) |
| 3 | $\dfrac{1}{\text{TAN}_{\text{blend}}} = \sum x_i \cdot \dfrac{1}{\text{TAN}_i}$ | TAN of crude blend (mixing rule) | mg KOH/g | $x_i$ = mass fraction of crude $i$ |
| 4 | $\text{NAC Rate} \propto (\text{TAN})^{0.5} \times V^{0.5}$ | Empirical Naphthenic Acid corrosion | mm/yr | $V$ = velocity; valid for TAN 0.5–8 |
| 5 | $P_{\text{H}_2\text{S}} = y_{\text{H}_2\text{S}} \times P_{\text{total}}$ | Partial pressure of H₂S | bar | Used in **NACE MR0175** sour service |
| 6 | $\log(\text{CR}) = a + b\cdot \log(P_{\text{CO}_2})$ | de Waard–Milliams CO₂ corrosion model | mm/yr | $a, b$ are empirical; classic for sweet service |
| 7 | $\text{API RP 571 Number} \rightarrow \text{damage factor}$ | API damage factor lookup | – | Used in RBI studies |
| 8 | $E = E^\circ + \dfrac{0.0592}{n}\log\dfrac{[\text{Ox}]}{[\text{Red}]}$ | Nernst equation | V | Electrochemistry of aqueous corrosion |
| 9 | $\text{LMP} = \dfrac{\Delta m}{(\rho \times A)}$ | Local Metal Penetration / Was test | – | Used in pitting analysis |
| 10 | $D_c = D \times \dfrac{\rho_{\text{actual}}}{\rho_{\text{lab}}}$ | Temperature density correction for CR | – | For hot section CR conversion |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** A corrosion engineer at a refinery uses formula (1) for **chemical cleaning audits**, formula (2) for **crude-blending decisions**, and formula (6) coupled with API RP 571 for **Risk-Based Inspection (RBI)** planning — all of which are exam-favorite KTU applications.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Corrosion Rate from Weight-Loss Data (Standard NACE Method)

**Given data:** A carbon steel coupon (density $D = 7.85\ \text{g/cm}^3$) exposed in a crude overhead system for 720 hours loses 0.482 g. Exposed surface area = 26.6 cm². Calculate the corrosion rate in **mm/yr** and **mpy (mils per year)**.

**Formula (re-stated):**

$$\text{CR (mm/yr)} = \dfrac{87600 \times W}{D \times A \times t}$$

### Step-by-step logical expansion

Let us identify the role of every constant in the numerator 87600:

$$
\begin{aligned}
87600 &= \dfrac{365\ \text{days/yr} \times 24\ \text{h/day}}{10} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{8760\ \text{h/yr}}{0.1\ \text{cm/mm}} \times \text{(conversion factors)} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{\text{annual hours}}{\text{depth conversion}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CR} &= \dfrac{87600 \times 0.482}{7.85 \times 26.6 \times 720} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{42223.2}{150343.2} \\[4pt]
&= 0.2808\ \text{mm/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to mpy (mils per year; 1 mm = 39.37 mil):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CR (mpy)} &= 0.2808 \times 39.37 \\[4pt]
&= 11.06\ \text{mpy}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation (KTU exam language):** The carbon steel exhibits a corrosion rate of **0.28 mm/yr**, which exceeds the NACE acceptable limit of **0.20 mm/yr** for crude overhead service. A material upgrade to **AISI 410 SS or 5Cr-0.5Mo** is recommended.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Blending Rule for TAN of Mixed Crudes

**Scenario:** A refinery blends 60% (mass) of Crude A (TAN = 3.0 mg KOH/g) with 40% of Crude B (TAN = 0.5 mg KOH/g). Find the resulting TAN.

The blending rule derives from **mass conservation of acidic species**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total KOH consumed (blend)} &= m_A \cdot \text{TAN}_A \cdot k + m_B \cdot \text{TAN}_B \cdot k \\[4pt]
\text{where } k &= \text{stoichiometric constant} \\[4pt]
\text{Dividing by total mass } m_A + m_B: \\[4pt]
\text{TAN}_{\text{blend}} &= \dfrac{m_A}{m_T}\text{TAN}_A + \dfrac{m_B}{m_T}\text{TAN}_B
\end{aligned}
$$

Numerical evaluation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TAN}_{\text{blend}} &= (0.6)(3.0) + (0.4)(0.5) \\[4pt]
&= 1.80 + 0.20 \\[4pt]
&= 2.00\ \text{mg KOH/g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The blend is now in the **"high NAC risk" zone** (TAN ≥ 2.0). The plant must employ **316L stainless steel cladding** in the 220 °C–400 °C distillation zone.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Naphthenic Acid Corrosion Rate (Couper–Gorman Empirical Model)

The widely cited empirical NAC rate equation:

$$
\log(\text{CR}) = \log(C) + n \log(\text{TAN}) + m \log(V) - \dfrac{E_a}{2.303RT}
$$

For a carbon steel at 320 °C in a 30 m/s stream, fitted parameters are $C = 0.034$, $n = 0.5$, $m = 0.5$, $E_a = 18{,}000\ \text{J/mol}$, and $R = 8.314\ \text{J/mol·K}$:

- Temperature in Kelvin: $T = 320 + 273 = 593\ \text{K}$.
- Activation term:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{E_a}{2.303 R T} &= \dfrac{18000}{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 593} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{18000}{11349.9} \\[4pt]
&= 1.586
\end{aligned}
$$

Assuming TAN = 2.0, V = 30 m/s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log(\text{CR}) &= \log(0.034) + 0.5 \log(2.0) + 0.5 \log(30) - 1.586 \\[4pt]
&= -1.469 + 0.151 + 0.739 - 1.586 \\[4pt]
&= -2.165
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CR} &= 10^{-2.165} \\[4pt]
&= 6.84 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{mm/yr}\ (\text{for carbon steel baseline})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Note:** Empirical NAC rate is normally multiplied by a **sulfur correction factor** (1.0–0.2) and **Cr-content factor** (e.g., 316 SS gives ~1/10 the rate of CS). This is the **complete KTU-style answer approach**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Mechanism vs. Process Unit

| Process Unit | Temperature | Dominant Corrosion Type | Recommended Material |
|---|---|---|---|
| Atmospheric Distillation overhead | 120 – 180 °C | Sour water / HCl | Monel, 316L, Inconel |
| Atmospheric Distillation bottom | 350 – 400 °C | Naphthenic acid + sulfidation | 316L SS, 317L SS |
| Vacuum Distillation bottom | 380 – 420 °C | Naphthenic acid (high TAN) | 317L SS, alloy 825 |
| Hydrocracker reactor | 350 – 450 °C, 150 bar | HTHA | 2.25Cr-1Mo, 3Cr-1Mo, 347 SS |
| FCC main fractionator | 200 – 350 °C | Polythionic acid SCC | 304H / 321H austenitic SS |
| Coker fractionator | 400 – 480 °C | Sulfidation + NAC | 5Cr-0.5Mo, 9Cr-1Mo |
| Steam cracker (furnace tubes) | 800 – 1050 °C | Carburization, oxidation | HP-40, HK-40, 35Cr-45Ni |
| Sour water stripper | 100 – 130 °C | H₂S wet corrosion | FRP, 316L SS |
| Amine contactor | 40 – 70 °C | Amine SCC, CO₂ corrosion | 304L SS, alloy 400 |
| Ethylene oxide reactor | 230 – 290 °C | Stress corrosion | Carbon steel + inhibition |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Petrochemical Corrosion Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Crude Oil Feedstock] --> B[Process Unit Identification]
    B --> C{Operating Regime?}
    C -- HT above 230 C --> D[High Temp Corrosion]
    C -- LT below 230 C --> E[Low Temp Aqueous Corrosion]
    D --> D1[Sulfidation]
    D --> D2[Naphthenic Acid Attack]
    D --> D3[High Temp H Attack]
    D --> D4[Carburization]
    D --> D5[Metal Dusting]
    E --> E1[Sour Water H2S Attack]
    E --> E2[CO2 Sweet Corrosion]
    E --> E3[Stress Corrosion Cracking]
    E --> E4[Microbial Induced Corrosion]
    D1 --> F[Select Cr Mo Steel or SS]
    D2 --> G[Select 316L / 317L SS]
    D3 --> H[Select 2.25Cr 1Mo or higher]
    D4 --> I[Select HP Alloy or Aluminized]
    D5 --> J[Select High Cr Ni Alloys]
    E1 --> K[Apply Chemical Inhibition]
    E2 --> L[De Waard Model Application]
    E3 --> M[Avoid CS Use Apply PWHT]
    E4 --> N[Biocide Treatment Program]
    F --> O[Material Selection Report]
    G --> O
    H --> O
    I --> O
    J --> O
    K --> O
    L --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow: NAC Decision Logic for Refinery Blending

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Crude Receipt TAN Analysis] --> Q{Is TAN greater than 0.5?}
    Q -- No --> R[Standard CS Acceptable]
    Q -- Yes --> S{Velocity greater than 30 m/s?}
    S -- No --> T[Monitor CR Quarterly]
    S -- Yes --> U{Stream temperature in 220 to 425 C?}
    U -- No --> V[Use 5Cr 0.5Mo]
    U -- Yes --> W[Apply 316L SS Cladding]
    W --> X[Validate with Pilot Loop Test]
    V --> X
    T --> X
    R --> X
    X --> Y[Issue Material Specification]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Sulphur–Temperature Corrosion Zone Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Z1[Sulphur Content low TAN low] --> ZONE1[Zone 1 CS Safe]
    Z2[Sulphur Content low TAN high] --> ZONE2[Zone 2 NAC Risk Use SS]
    Z3[Sulphur Content high TAN low] --> ZONE3[Zone 3 Sulfidation Use Cr Mo]
    Z4[Sulphur Content high TAN high] --> ZONE4[Zone 4 Combined Attack Use 317L or Alloy 825]
    ZONE1 --> OUT[Material Recommendation]
    ZONE2 --> OUT
    ZONE3 --> OUT
    ZONE4 --> OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of these diagrams:** In KTU Part-B 14-mark questions, examiners award full marks only when students present a **flowchart-style decision logic** along with the supporting text. The above diagrams give a ready-made schematic for an answer on *"Discuss corrosion mechanisms in petroleum refinery overhead systems."*

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define naphthenic acid corrosion. What is the significance of TAN in predicting NAC?**

**Model Answer:**
Naphthenic acid corrosion (NAC) is the high-temperature (220 °C – 425 °C) attack on ferrous metals by organic carboxylic acids (R–COOH, MW 150–700) present in crude oil. **Total Acid Number (TAN)** is the measure of these acids expressed in mg KOH required to neutralize 1 g of crude. A TAN above **0.5 mg KOH/g** signals the onset of NAC risk, and above **2.0 mg KOH/g** the corrosion is severe, necessitating upgrade to **316L/317L stainless steel** in the affected distillation zones. (3 marks)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between sulfidation and high-temperature hydrogen attack.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Sulfidation | HTHA |
|---|---|---|
| Corrodent | H₂S / organic sulfides | Molecular H₂ |
| Mechanism | Sulfur inward diffusion → metal sulfide scale | H₂ reacts with carbides → methane at grain boundaries |
| Temperature | 230 – 540 °C | 200 – 595 °C |
| Pressure sensitivity | Less pressure-sensitive | Strongly pressure-sensitive (H₂ partial pressure) |
| Affected material | Low-Cr carbon steel | Carbon and low-alloy steel with carbides |
| Prevention | Increase Cr, Mo; use 5Cr–9Cr steels | Use 2.25Cr-1Mo or austenitic SS with stable carbides |

(3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Petrochemical Module**
> 1. Students often **confuse "sour" and "sweet" corrosion** — remember: H₂S = sour, CO₂ = sweet.
> 2. NAC is **not** an aqueous corrosion mechanism — it is **chemical (not electrochemical)**.
> 3. Always quote the **API RP 571** damage mechanism number in the answer.
> 4. In HTHA questions, examiners expect the **Nelson Curve** reference (post-1999 revision).

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model Paper)]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the mechanism of **high-temperature sulfidic corrosion** in a petroleum refinery, with a discussion of the role of chromium and molybdenum in alloy resistance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A carbon steel coupon exposed in a hydrocracker feed line for 1500 h lost 1.32 g. Area = 28.0 cm²; density = 7.85 g/cm³. Calculate the corrosion rate in **mm/yr** and **mpy**, and comment on whether the material is acceptable per NACE guidelines. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *Sulfidation mechanism*:
- Above 230 °C, sulfur-bearing species (H₂S, S, RSH) thermally decompose on the steel surface, releasing atomic sulfur.
- Sulfur diffuses inward; the metal diffuses outward, forming a sulfide scale (FeS initially).
- FeS is **non-protective** and porous, leading to linear (not parabolic) kinetics.
- **Role of Cr:** Above 12% Cr, a Cr-rich sulfide (CrS, spinel-type) forms, which is dense and adherent. Hence **5Cr-0.5Mo, 9Cr-1Mo, 12Cr (410/420 SS)** are used progressively at higher temperatures.
- **Role of Mo:** Mo stabilizes carbides, reduces sensitization, and improves sulfidation resistance up to ~540 °C.
- **Operating rule of thumb (API RP 571):** For crude units, every 1% Cr added ≈ increases the safe temperature by **15–20 °C**. (7 marks)

**(b)** *Numerical solution*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CR (mm/yr)} &= \dfrac{87600 \times 1.32}{7.85 \times 28.0 \times 1500} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{115632}{329700} \\[4pt]
&= 0.3507\ \text{mm/yr} \\[4pt]
\text{CR (mpy)} &= 0.3507 \times 39.37 = 13.81\ \text{mpy}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** NACE acceptable limit for hydrocracker service = **0.20 mm/yr (~8 mpy)**. The observed rate of **0.35 mm/yr** is **excessive**; recommended upgrades are **2.25Cr-1Mo (P22)** or **austenitic SS (347/321)** with proper welding PWHT. (7 marks)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]`

**(a)** Discuss the various forms of **stress corrosion cracking (SCC)** encountered in petrochemical units, with examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A refinery blends 70% Crude-X (TAN = 4.0) and 30% Crude-Y (TAN = 0.4). The combined stream enters the vacuum distillation column at 380 °C. Will the material of construction (carbon steel) be acceptable? Justify with calculations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *SCC in petrochemical units*:

1. **Polythionic Acid SCC** — occurs in FCC and hydrocracker reactors when sensitized 304/321 SS is exposed to H₂S + O₂ + moisture during shutdown. Prevention: use stabilized grades (321, 347) or low-carbon (304L, 316L).
2. **Caustic Embrittlement** — caustic (NaOH) concentrated in riveted boiler/heat-exchanger joints causes intergranular cracking. Prevention: use welded joints, add NaNO₃ inhibitor.
3. **Amine SCC** — in gas-treating units, carbon steel + amine + CO₂ produces carbonate stress cracking. Prevention: use **alloy 400 (Monel)** or 304L SS, and limit lean amine temperature to **< 120 °C**.
4. **H₂S SCC (Wet Sour)** — in NACE sour service environments when hardness > HRC 22 and pH < 4.5. Prevention: NACE MR0175 compliance.
5. **Chloride SCC** — austenitic SS at > 60 °C in chloride media. Prevention: use duplex SS, Ni alloys, or upgrade water chemistry. (7 marks)

**(b)** *TAN blending calculation*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TAN}_{\text{blend}} &= (0.70)(4.0) + (0.30)(0.4) \\[4pt]
&= 2.80 + 0.12 \\[4pt]
&= 2.92\ \text{mg KOH/g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Justification:** The blended TAN of **2.92 mg KOH/g** is far above the **0.5 mg KOH/g** threshold and well into the **severe NAC regime (TAN > 2.0)**. At 380 °C, the operating temperature lies in the **peak NAC window of 220–425 °C**. Therefore, **carbon steel is NOT acceptable**; the engineer must specify **316L or 317L stainless steel cladding** for the vacuum column internals, trays, pump bodies, and piping in the 220–400 °C zone. (7 marks)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Petrochemical Corrosion**

- **Petrochemical corrosion** is a *combination* of high-T chemical attack and low-T electrochemical attack, NOT just one or the other.
- The **4 master variables** that decide mechanism: **Temperature, Pressure, Stream chemistry (H₂S/CO₂/TAN/Cl⁻), and Velocity**.
- **Sulfidation zone:** 230–540 °C; rule: *more Cr = more protection*.
- **NAC zone:** 220–425 °C; rule: *high TAN + high velocity = severe attack*; upgrade to **316L / 317L / Alloy 825**.
- **HTHA zone:** high H₂ partial pressure + T > 200 °C; refer to **Nelson curves** for safe operation.
- **Sweet corrosion:** CO₂ dominant → use **de Waard–Milliams** model and **NACE CO₂ corrosion charts**.
- **Sour service:** H₂S dominant → follow **NACE MR0175 / ISO 15156** strictly.
- **Amine SCC** + **Polythionic SCC** + **Caustic embrittlement** are the *three* most-tested SCC variants.
- **Materials progression for refinery service:**
  Carbon steel → 1.25Cr-0.5Mo → 2.25Cr-1Mo → 5Cr-0.5Mo → 9Cr-1Mo → 12Cr (410) → 304/316 SS → Alloy 825/625.
- **Always state TAN threshold:** 0.5 mg KOH/g (low risk), 2.0 mg KOH/g (severe).
- **Always state NACE acceptable CR:** ≤ **0.20 mm/yr (~8 mpy)** for refinery liquid service.
- **Conversion factor to memorize:** **1 mm/yr = 39.37 mpy**.
- **Faraday constant:** **F = 96,485 C/mol** — required for any electrochemical sub-question.
- **API RP 571** is the standard damage mechanism reference — quote the mechanism number wherever possible.
- **Inspection techniques** for petrochemical units: **ultrasonic thickness (UT), radiography (RT), eddy current, phased-array UT, AE monitoring**.
- **Mitigation triad:** *(1) Material upgrade, (2) Process control (T, V, chemistry), (3) Chemical inhibition + cathodic protection.*

> **End of Module 4 — Petrochemical Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ship building
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT527 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ship building

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Ship Building Corrosion Engineering** is the specialised branch of materials and corrosion science that deals with the systematic identification, prediction, mitigation, and control of metallic degradation phenomena occurring on marine vessels, offshore platforms, submarines, and auxiliary shipboard structures exposed to the **marine environment** (seawater, marine atmosphere, splash zone, and seabed mud).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework (PECHT527 - Corrosion Engineering, Module 4), ship building is treated as an **integrated case study** where the student must apply the full spectrum of corrosion knowledge—electrochemical theory, passivity, cathodic protection, coating science, and material selection—to a real, large-scale engineering application. A ship is essentially a **floating corrosion cell** containing multiple dissimilar metals, welds, riveted joints, and crevices, all simultaneously attacked by an aggressive, conductive electrolyte (seawater with resistivity ~ **20 Ω·cm**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Approved):**
> *"The application of corrosion science principles to the design, fabrication, and maintenance of marine vessels, encompassing material selection, protective coatings, cathodic protection, corrosion allowance, and biofouling control across distinct marine exposure zones."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a ship as a **giant metal sandwich left in salty lemon juice (seawater)**. The lemon juice (chloride ions) eats away the metal, just like rust eats an old iron gate—but much faster, because seawater is **electrically conductive** (so corrosion currents flow freely) and **continuously replenished** (waves, tides, splash).

Now imagine the ship's hull contains **two different metals touching each other**—say, a bronze propeller and a steel hull. Just like a battery, one metal becomes the **anode** (gets eaten) and the other the **cathode** (protected). To stop this, shipbuilders do three smart things:
1. **Paint the metal** (barrier protection — like raincoat on the sandwich)
2. **Attach zinc blocks** to the hull (sacrificial anodes — the zinc "takes the hit" instead of the steel)
3. **Choose the right metal alloys** from the start (like choosing stainless steel cutlery instead of iron for daily use)

**Key Standard Metrics in Bold:**
- Seawater resistivity: **ρ ≈ 20 Ω·cm** (vs. freshwater ≈ 18,000 Ω·cm)
- Salinity of seawater: **~35 g/L (3.5%)**
- Dissolved oxygen: **~5–8 mL/L**
- Typical ship design life: **25–30 years**
- Pitting corrosion rate for unprotected mild steel in seawater: **0.25–0.50 mm/year**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Ship Building is a Critical KTU Topic:**
> - It integrates **all four core corrosion mechanisms** (galvanic, pitting, crevice, SCC) in one system.
> - It tests the student's ability to **choose between CP methods** (sacrificial vs. ICCP).
> - It is a **favourite Part B (14-mark) question** in KTU University Exams, often combined with offshore structures.

### 1.3 Classification of Marine Exposure Zones (KTU High-Yield)

A ship's hull experiences **five distinct corrosion zones**, each with different severity and protection requirements:

| Zone | Location | Environment | Corrosion Severity | Typical Protection |
|------|----------|-------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| **Atmospheric Zone** | Superstructure, mast | Marine air, salt spray | Moderate (cyclic wet/dry) | Paints (alkyd, epoxy) |
| **Splash Zone** | Just above waterline | Alternating wet/dry, UV, wave action | **Highest** | Thick neoprene, epoxy, Monel sheathing |
| **Tidal Zone** | Between high/low tide | Periodic immersion | High | CP + coatings |
| **Submerged Zone** | Underwater hull | Continuous immersion, biofouling | High (uniform + pitting) | ICCP / sacrificial anodes + antifouling paint |
| **Mud Zone** | Below seabed (for fixed structures) | Anaerobic, sulfate-reducing bacteria | Severe (MIC) | CP + coating |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Primary Corrosion Mechanisms Active on a Ship

#### 2.1.1 Galvanic (Bimetallic) Corrosion — *Most Common on Ships*
- **Why it occurs:** Ships contain many dissimilar metals in electrical contact via seawater (a perfect electrolyte).
- **Classic example on a ship:** Steel hull (+) + Bronze propeller (–) + Monel (Ni-Cu alloy) shaft → forms a **tri-metallic cell**.
- **Driving force:** Difference in galvanic series position measured as **E° (V vs. SHE)**.

**Galvanic series in seawater (from active to noble):**
$$\text{Mg} \rightarrow \text{Zn} \rightarrow \text{Al} \rightarrow \text{Steel} \rightarrow \text{Cu} \rightarrow \text{Brass} \rightarrow \text{Bronze} \rightarrow \text{Monel} \rightarrow \text{Ni} \rightarrow \text{SS (passive)} \rightarrow \text{Ti} \rightarrow \text{Pt}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Rule:** The metal **higher (more active)** in the series becomes the **anode** and corrodes. The **lower (more noble)** metal is protected. Hence **zinc and aluminium sacrificial anodes** are placed strategically on the hull.

#### 2.1.2 Pitting and Crevice Corrosion
- **Pitting:** Caused by Cl⁻ ions (concentration in seawater ≈ **19,000 ppm**) breaking down passive films on stainless steel propellers, pump shafts, and valve seats.
- **Crevice corrosion:** Common at **riveted joints, gasket surfaces, threaded fasteners, and propeller-shaft sealing areas** where stagnant seawater accumulates, becomes oxygen-depleted, and becomes acidic (OCP drops from +0.2 V to –0.4 V vs. SCE).

#### 2.1.3 Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)
- **Susceptible alloys in marine service:** Austenitic SS (especially 304 in warm seawater → chloride SCC), brass condenser tubes (season cracking—ammonia + residual stress).
- **Critical threshold:** Temperature > **60 °C** dramatically accelerates SCC of austenitic SS in Cl⁻.

#### 2.1.4 Cavitation and Erosion-Corrosion
- Occurs on **propeller blades, pump impellers, and rudder surfaces** where high-velocity water (> **30 m/s**) creates collapsing vapour bubbles.
- Mechanism: Mechanical disruption of passive film → fresh metal exposure → rapid anodic dissolution → rough, pitted, sponge-like surface.

#### 2.1.5 Microbiologically Influenced Corrosion (MIC)
- **Anaerobic sulfate-reducing bacteria (SRB)** in seabed mud beneath offshore platforms and in stagnant ballast tanks.
- Cathodic reaction: $\text{SO}_4^{2-} + 8\text{H}^+ + 8e^- \rightarrow \text{S}^{2-} + 4\text{H}_2\text{O}$
- Produces black, foul-smelling iron sulfide deposits.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbol / Unit | Application in Ship Building |
|---|---------------------|---------------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | **Faraday's Law (mass loss)** | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | m = mass lost (g), I = corrosion current (A), t = time (s), M = molar mass, n = electrons transferred, F = 96,485 C/mol |
| 2 | **Corrosion rate (penetration depth)** | $CR = \dfrac{3.27 \times 10^{-3} \cdot I_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho}$ | CR in mm/year, $I_{corr}$ in μA/cm², EW = equivalent weight, ρ in g/cm³ |
| 3 | **Corrosion current from weight loss** | $I_{corr} = \dfrac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{A \cdot t}$ | Convert weight-loss data into anodic current |
| 4 | **Cathodic protection design (sacrificial anode life)** | $L = \dfrac{m_{anode} \cdot u \cdot \eta}{I_{req} \cdot 8760}$ | L = design life (years), $m_{anode}$ = mass, u = utilisation factor (~0.85 for Zn), η = current efficiency, $I_{req}$ = required current (A) |
| 5 | **Required CP current density (steel in seawater)** | $i_{req}$ = **10–30 mA/m²** (submerged) up to **150 mA/m²** (hot tropical hull) | Used in ICCP anode sizing |
| 6 | **Mixed potential (Evans diagram)** | $E_{corr}$ where $i_a = i_c$ | Predicts galvanic couple behaviour |
| 7 | **Nernst equation (oxygen reduction in seawater)** | $E = E^\circ - \dfrac{0.059}{n}\log \dfrac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}$ | Used for calculating $\text{O}_2/\text{H}_2\text{O}$ half-cell at +0.815 V vs. SHE |
| 8 | **Seawater resistivity at 25 °C** | $\rho = \dfrac{1}{\Lambda \cdot C}$ | $\rho \approx 20\ \Omega\cdot\text{cm}$ for 3.5% NaCl equivalent |
| 9 | **Pitting resistance equivalent number (PREN)** | $\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \times \%\text{Mo} + 16 \times \%\text{N}$ | **PREN > 40** required for marine SS (e.g., super-duplex) |
| 10 | **Sacrificial anode capacity** | Zn ≈ 780 Ah/kg, Al ≈ 2,500 Ah/kg, Mg ≈ 1,100 Ah/kg | Selects anode type for hull |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Conversion Constant:** $1\ \text{mpy} = 0.0254\ \text{mm/year}$. KTU problems frequently flip between these units—memorise this!

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The above principles are applied in:
- **Naval shipyards (Cochin Shipyard, Mazagon Dock):** Designing CP retrofitting for in-service warships.
- **Commercial shipbuilding (Hyundai Heavy Industries):** Specifying ICCP systems for VLCCs (Very Large Crude Carriers) with 25-year design life.
- **Offshore platforms (ONGC Mumbai High):** Combined splash-zone greases, CP, and corrosion-resistant alloy (CRA) piping.
- **Submarines:** Special titanium hulls + CP to withstand deep-sea pressure and corrosion.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Corrosion Rate from Weight-Loss Data (KTU Favourite)

A steel hull specimen of dimensions 5 cm × 5 cm × 0.3 cm is immersed in seawater for 30 days. Mass loss = 0.85 g. Density of steel ρ = 7.85 g/cm³. Equivalent weight EW = 27.92. Calculate the corrosion rate in **mm/year**.

**Step 1 — Identify the area exposed:**
$$A = 2(lb + lh + bh)$$
$$A = 2(5 \times 5 + 5 \times 0.3 + 5 \times 0.3)$$
$$A = 2(25 + 1.5 + 1.5)$$
$$A = 2 \times 28 = 56\ \text{cm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Convert exposure time to seconds (for SI consistency in Faraday's equation):**
$$t = 30\ \text{days} \times 24 \times 3600 = 2.592 \times 10^6\ \text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Faraday's Law to obtain corrosion current:**
$$I = \frac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{A \cdot t \cdot EW / M} \quad \text{(using compact form)}$$

More conveniently, use the direct ASTM G102 formula for corrosion current density from weight loss:
$$i_{corr} = \frac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{A \cdot t \cdot M}$$

Using $M = 55.85\ \text{g/mol}$ for Fe, $n = 2$ (Fe → Fe²⁺):
$$i_{corr} = \frac{0.85 \times 2 \times 96485}{56 \times 2.592 \times 10^6 \times 55.85}$$

Numerator = $0.85 \times 2 \times 96485 = 1.6402 \times 10^5$

Denominator = $56 \times 2.592 \times 10^6 \times 55.85 = 8.1067 \times 10^9$

$$i_{corr} = \frac{1.6402 \times 10^5}{8.1067 \times 10^9}$$
$$i_{corr} = 2.024 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{A/cm}^2 = 20.24\ \mu\text{A/cm}^2$$

**Step 4 — Apply the penetration-rate formula:**
$$CR = \frac{3.27 \times 10^{-3} \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho}$$
$$CR = \frac{3.27 \times 10^{-3} \times 20.24 \times 27.92}{7.85}$$
$$CR = \frac{1.8495}{7.85}$$
$$\boxed{CR = 0.2356\ \text{mm/year}}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** This is a typical value for unprotected mild steel in quiescent seawater. To reduce it to < **0.05 mm/year** (acceptable for ship hull), CP + coating is mandatory.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Cathodic Protection Design for a Ship Hull (14-Mark Standard Problem)

**Problem statement:** A ship hull of total wetted surface area **A = 3500 m²** is to be protected using **zinc sacrificial anodes**. Required current density $i_{req} = 12\ \text{mA/m}^2$, current efficiency η = 0.90, utilisation factor u = 0.85, design life L = **5 years** between dry-dockings. Calculate (a) total current required, (b) total mass of zinc needed, (c) number of anodes if each anode is 6 kg.

**Step 1 — Total current required:**
$$I_{req} = i_{req} \times A$$
$$I_{req} = 12 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{A/m}^2 \times 3500\ \text{m}^2$$
$$I_{req} = 42\ \text{A}$$

**Step 2 — Total anode mass required:**

Using the anode life equation:
$$L = \frac{m_{anode} \cdot u \cdot \eta \cdot C_{Zn}}{I_{req} \cdot 8760}$$

where $C_{Zn}$ = electrochemical capacity of zinc = **780 Ah/kg** and 8760 h/year.

Rearranging for $m_{anode}$:
$$m_{anode} = \frac{L \cdot I_{req} \cdot 8760}{u \cdot \eta \cdot C_{Zn}}$$
$$m_{anode} = \frac{5 \times 42 \times 8760}{0.85 \times 0.90 \times 780}$$

Numerator = $5 \times 42 \times 8760 = 1.8396 \times 10^6$

Denominator = $0.85 \times 0.90 \times 780 = 596.7$

$$m_{anode} = \frac{1.8396 \times 10^6}{596.7}$$
$$\boxed{m_{anode} \approx 3083\ \text{kg of zinc}}$$

**Step 3 — Number of anodes:**
$$N = \frac{3083}{6} \approx 514\ \text{anodes}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering sanity check (KTU marker looks for this):**
- Distribute 514 anodes on a 3500 m² hull → spacing ≈ **2.6 m** along the hull, consistent with the empirical rule of anode spacing = **2–5 m**.
- Verify voltage drop via seawater resistivity (20 Ω·cm) — typically < 0.3 V, acceptable.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Cathodic Protection Methods

| Parameter | Sacrificial (Galvanic) Anode CP | Impressed Current CP (ICCP) |
|-----------|--------------------------------|-----------------------------|
| **Driving force** | Galvanic potential difference (Zn, Al, Mg) | External DC rectifier + inert anode |
| **Typical anode material** | Zn, Al-Zn-In alloy | Pt/Ti, mixed-metal oxide (MMO/Ti), lead-silver |
| **Current output** | 1–5 A per anode (limited by galvanic EMF) | Up to 200 A per anode |
| **Voltage source** | None (self-driven) | Transformer-rectifier (TR) unit |
| **Best suited for** | Small boats, low-resistivity seawater, in-service retrofits | Large ships, offshore platforms, ships in port with shore power |
| **Drawback** | Anode mass consumption, frequent replacement | Risk of over-protection → hydrogen embrittlement, paint blistering |
| **Protection criterion** | E ≤ –0.80 V vs. Ag/AgCl (for steel) | E ≤ –0.80 V vs. Ag/AgCl (for steel) |
| **Cost over 25 years (relative)** | Higher (replacement labour) | Lower (anode lasts longer) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Ship Corrosion Protection System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship Hull Mild Steel ASTM A36] --> B{Corrosion Zone Classifier}
    B --> C1[Atmospheric Zone Superstructure]
    B --> C2[Splash Zone Hull above WL]
    B --> C3[Submerged Zone Underwater Hull]
    B --> C4[Internal Tanks Ballast Fuel]

    C1 --> P1[Barrier Coating Alkyd or Epoxy Primer]
    P1 --> P1A[Topcoat for UV resistance]

    C2 --> P2A[Neoprene Rubber Sheath 6 to 10 mm]
    C2 --> P2B[Monel Alloy 400 Cladding]
    C2 --> P2C[Thick Epoxy Mastic Coating]

    C3 --> CP1{Protection Type Decision}
    CP1 --> S1[Sacrificial Anode CP Zn or Al Anodes]
    CP1 --> S2[Impressed Current CP ICCP MMO Ti Anode]
    CP1 --> S3[Antifouling Paint Cu2O or Tributyltin Biocide Free]
    CP1 --> S4[Fuel Cathodic Protection IC for Internal Tanks]

    C4 --> T1[Internal Ballast Tank Epoxy Lining]
    C4 --> T2[Corrosion Allowance 2 to 3 mm Extra Plate Thickness]

    S1 --> M1[Monitoring Reference Electrode Ag or AgCl or Zn]
    S2 --> M1
    M1 --> CB[Control Rectifier and Junction Box]
    CB --> X[Potential Held at minus 0.80 V vs AgAgCl]

    A --> D[Material Selection Input]
    D --> D1[CuNi 90 10 for Piping]
    D --> D2[Super Duplex SS for Propeller Shaft]
    D --> D3[Al Bronze for Propeller Hub]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Corrosion Mechanism Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    I[Marine Exposure Input] --> J{Identify Aggressive Agent}
    J --> K1[Chloride Ions Pitting Risk]
    J --> K2[Dissolved O2 Uniform Attack]
    J --> K3[SRB Microbiologically Influenced]
    J --> K4[Stress plus Chloride SCC]
    J --> K5[Bimetallic Contact Galvanic]

    K1 --> L1[Apply PREN greater than 40 Super Duplex]
    K2 --> L2[Apply Cathodic Protection]
    K3 --> L3[Biocide plus Coatings]
    K4 --> L4[Avoid Austenitic SS Use Inconel or Ti]
    K5 --> L5[Isolate using Gaskets plus Insulating Bushes]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Note for Students:** In the KTU Board Exam, a labelled Mermaid block diagram (≥ 6 functional nodes) carries **3–4 marks by itself** within a 14-mark question. Always show the **direction of protection logic** (hull → zone → protection → monitoring).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Question Paper]**
*Define the term "splash zone" in the context of ship corrosion. Why is it the most severely corroded zone? List two protective measures.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) The splash zone is the region of the ship's hull located just above and at the waterline, which is alternately wetted by waves and dried by sun and wind.
- (1 mark) It suffers the highest corrosion rate because of (a) **continuous wet–dry cycling** causing differential aeration cells, (b) high concentration of chloride ions, and (c) mechanical impact of waves and suspended sand that removes protective films.
- (1 mark) Two protective measures: **(i)** Monel (Ni–Cu) alloy sheathing of 1–2 mm thickness; **(ii)** thick-film neoprene or epoxy-mastic coating (200–500 µm) reinforced with glass flake.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*Distinguish between sacrificial anode cathodic protection and impressed current cathodic protection as applied to ship hulls.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Sacrificial Anode CP | Impressed Current CP (ICCP) |
|---------|---------------------|------------------------------|
| Power source | Self-driven (galvanic EMF) | External DC rectifier |
| Anode material | Zn, Al, Mg (active metals consumed) | Pt/Ti, MMO/Ti (inert, not consumed) |
| Current output | Limited (~1–5 A/anode) | High (~50–200 A/anode) |
| Application | Small vessels, retrofit | Large ships, offshore platforms |

(1½ marks for correct distinction; 1½ marks for application example.)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]

**(a)** Discuss in detail the various **corrosion mechanisms** encountered in marine ship structures, with specific reference to galvanic corrosion, pitting corrosion, and stress corrosion cracking. Give at least two ship-board examples of each. (7 marks)

**(b)** A naval ship with wetted hull area of **4200 m²** is to be protected using **aluminium-zinc-indium sacrificial anodes** (capacity = 2500 Ah/kg, utilisation factor = 0.80, current efficiency = 0.95). Required current density = 15 mA/m². Calculate (i) total current, (ii) total anode mass required for 4-year dry-dock interval, and (iii) number of anodes if each anode weighs 8 kg. Verify the spacing. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 marks (1 mark per mechanism concept + 0.5 mark per example + 1 mark for ship relevance summary):**

1. **Galvanic corrosion (2 marks):**
   - Definition: Preferential corrosion of the more active metal when two dissimilar metals are in electrical contact in an electrolyte.
   - Ship examples: (i) Steel hull (anode) coupled to Monel shaft (cathode); (ii) Bronze propeller coupled to steel hull via seawater.

2. **Pitting corrosion (2 marks):**
   - Definition: Localised attack producing deep, narrow cavities in passive metals when Cl⁻ ions breakdown the passive film.
   - Ship examples: (i) Stainless steel pump impellers in seawater; (ii) Aluminium bronze valve seats in engine cooling system.

3. **Stress corrosion cracking (2 marks):**
   - Definition: Brittle fracture under combined tensile stress and corrosive environment.
   - Ship examples: (i) Austenitic SS 304 propeller shaft in warm seawater (>60 °C); (ii) Brass condenser tubes in steam turbines ("season cracking" from NH₃).

4. **Engineering summary (1 mark):** Ship designers mitigate these through material selection (super-duplex SS with PREN > 40), CP, insulation gaskets at flange joints, and stress-relief heat treatment of brass components.

---

**(b) — 7 marks (full derivation shown):**

**Step 1: Total current required (2 marks):**
$$I_{req} = i_{req} \times A = 15 \times 10^{-3} \times 4200 = 63\ \text{A}$$

**Step 2: Total mass of Al-Zn-In anodes (3 marks):**
$$m_{anode} = \frac{L \cdot I_{req} \cdot 8760}{u \cdot \eta \cdot C_{Al}}$$
$$m_{anode} = \frac{4 \times 63 \times 8760}{0.80 \times 0.95 \times 2500}$$
$$m_{anode} = \frac{2.2075 \times 10^6}{1900}$$
$$m_{anode} \approx 1161.8\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 3: Number of anodes (1 mark):**
$$N = \frac{1161.8}{8} \approx 145\ \text{anodes}$$

**Step 4: Spacing verification (1 mark):**
$$\text{Average spacing} = \sqrt{\frac{4200}{145}} \approx 5.4\ \text{m}$$

This is within the empirical safe range of 2–5 m (slightly high; may require adding 10–15% extra anodes for edge-effect coverage). Acceptable answer.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a **neat schematic**, explain the **impressed current cathodic protection (ICCP) system** used for a ship's hull. Mention the role of each component: DC source, impressed current anodes, reference electrode, and the controlled potential criterion. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare the performance of **zinc, aluminium, and magnesium sacrificial anodes** for marine CP of a ship hull. Justify the choice of **aluminium alloy anodes** as the most preferred material in modern shipbuilding. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 marks (Schematic 2 marks + explanation 5 marks):**

**Schematic (describe since drawing is required):**
*(ASCII equivalent of the ICCP circuit for the examiner's reference)*

```
   AC mains → [Transformer-Rectifier] → (+) → MMO/Ti Anodes (mounted on hull)
                                            ↓
                                 [Reference Electrode (Ag/AgCl)] — feedback →
                                            ↓
                                 Hull (cathode) — at -0.80 V vs Ag/AgCl
```

**Explanation (5 marks):**
1. **DC source (1 mark):** Transformer-rectifier converts ship-board AC (440 V, 60 Hz) to low-voltage DC (typically 0–24 V). It has automatic potential-control circuitry.
2. **Impressed current anodes (1.5 marks):** MMO (Mixed Metal Oxide) coated titanium, platinised titanium, or lead–silver alloy. They are **inert** and do not dissolve; they simply inject current into the seawater.
3. **Reference electrode (1.5 marks):** High-purity zinc or Ag/AgCl/sea-water half-cell permanently mounted on the hull. It senses the hull-to-seawater potential and feeds the signal back to the rectifier (on/off or proportional control).
4. **Protection criterion (1 mark):** Hull potential maintained between **–0.80 V and –1.05 V vs. Ag/AgCl**. Below –1.05 V → over-protection → hydrogen evolution, paint blistering, and possible hydrogen embrittlement of high-strength propeller shafts.

---

**(b) — 7 marks:**

**Comparison table (4 marks):**

| Property | Zinc Anode | Aluminium Anode (Al-Zn-In) | Magnesium Anode |
|----------|-----------|---------------------------|-----------------|
| Theoretical capacity (Ah/kg) | 820 | 2980 | 2200 |
| Practical capacity (Ah/kg) | 780 | 2500 | 1100 |
| Density (g/cm³) | 7.13 | 2.71 | 1.74 |
| Current efficiency (%) | 90–95 | 85–95 | 50–55 (wastes in freshwater) |
| Open-circuit voltage vs. SCE (V) | –1.10 | –1.15 | –1.55 |
| Best medium | Seawater | Seawater | Freshwater / soil |
| Mass required (per A·year) | ~11.2 kg | ~3.5 kg | ~8.0 kg |

**Justification for aluminium being preferred (3 marks):**
1. **Higher electrochemical capacity** (~3× Zn), so a much smaller anode mass protects the same hull area → less drag and payload loss.
2. **Lower density** (2.71 vs 7.13 g/cm³ for Zn) → larger volume per kg, distributed better along the hull.
3. **Stable performance in aerated seawater** with current efficiency > 90% when alloyed with Zn (5%) and In (0.02%) to prevent oxide passivation.
4. **Lower replacement frequency** in 5-year dry-docking cycles.
5. *(Optional 1 mark)* Modern Cu-free, Cd-free, Hg-free Al-Zn-In alloys comply with **IMO Resolution A.600(15)** anti-pollution regulations.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (As Observed in KTU Valuation):**
> 1. **Confusing PREN and pitting potential** — PREN is a *number* (Cr + 3.3Mo + 16N); pitting potential $E_{pit}$ is a *voltage*. Do NOT interchange in the same answer.
> 2. **Forgetting units in cathode-protection calculations** — Examiner will deduct **1 full mark** if "8760 hours/year" is not explicitly shown when converting L to hours.
> 3. **Writing "aluminium corrodes because it is active"** — wrong; the correct statement is "aluminium acts as a sacrificial anode because its corrosion potential in seawater is more negative than steel's". Be precise.
> 4. **Ignoring biofouling** — A 14-mark shipbuilding question without a mention of *antifouling paint* or *marine growth* is considered incomplete (loses 1–2 marks).
> 5. **Mixing up SCC of austenitic SS (chloride) with hydrogen embrittlement of high-strength steel** — these are **different mechanisms**; examiners test this distinction.
> 6. **Forgetting the protection criterion range** — Always cite **–0.80 V to –1.05 V vs. Ag/AgCl** as the *safe operating window*; anything outside risks over- or under-protection.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] A ship's hull is divided into **5 corrosion zones**: atmospheric, splash (worst), tidal, submerged, and mud — each requires a tailored protection strategy.
- [x] **Splash zone** is the most aggressively attacked region due to wet-dry cycling and oxygen differential cells.
- [x] **Seawater resistivity ≈ 20 Ω·cm** (vs. freshwater ≈ 18,000 Ω·cm) — makes it an excellent electrolyte that supports large galvanic currents.
- [x] **Galvanic series order in seawater:** Mg → Zn → Al → Steel → Cu → Bronze → Monel → SS → Ti (the further apart, the greater the corrosion rate of the anode).
- [x] **Sacrificial anodes** preferred for small vessels, retrofits, and in-service hulls; **ICCP** preferred for large warships and offshore platforms.
- [x] **Aluminium alloy anodes (Al-Zn-In)** are the modern preferred choice — 3× the capacity of Zn at one-third the weight.
- [x] **PREN > 40** is required for marine-grade stainless steels (super-duplex 2507) to resist pitting/crevice corrosion in seawater.
- [x] **Cathodic protection criterion for steel in seawater:** E = –0.80 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) for protection; avoid dropping below –1.05 V (over-protection).
- [x] **Cathodic protection current density for steel hulls:** 10–30 mA/m² (submerged), rising to 150 mA/m² in tropical/coated-but-painted hulls.
- [x] **Five corrosion mechanisms** to memorise for ships: galvanic, pitting, crevice, SCC, and erosion-cavitation (plus MIC in tanks and mud).
- [x] **SCC of austenitic SS** requires the **triad**: chloride + temperature > 60 °C + tensile stress — eliminate any one to prevent failure.
- [x] **Biofouling** + corrosion = **synergistic degradation**; antifouling paints (Cu₂O-based, modern silicone-fouling-release) are mandatory below the waterline.
- [x] **Corrosion allowance:** Ship hull plates are typically specified **1–3 mm thicker** than the strength calculation requires, to absorb uniform corrosion over 25-year life.
- [x] **IMO Resolution A.600(15)** governs sacrificial anode composition — restricts Cd, Hg, and Pb content for environmental safety.
- [x] **Key Faraday constant** to remember: **F = 96,485 C/mol** (sometimes rounded to 96,500 in KTU problems — check the digits of the given data).
- [x] **Conversion factor:** 1 mpy = 0.0254 mm/year. Always state which unit you are using in the final answer.
- [x] **Standard cell potential for O₂ reduction in aerated seawater:** $E = +0.815\ \text{V}$ vs. SHE at pH 8.2.
- [x] **Cathodic reaction in deaerated/mud zones:** $\text{SO}_4^{2-} + 8\text{H}^+ + 8e^- \rightarrow \text{S}^{2-} + 4\text{H}_2\text{O}$ (SRB-driven MIC).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy Tip:** When a 14-mark question asks about ship building, structure your answer in this order: (1) List the five zones → (2) List the corrosion mechanisms with **one ship-specific example each** → (3) Discuss **CP design** (include numerical problem if asked) → (4) Discuss **coatings + antifouling** → (5) Conclude with **material selection recommendations** (Cu-Ni, super-duplex, Al-bronze). This structure consistently scores 12–14 marks in KTU Board valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Marine and fertilizer industries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT527 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Marine and fertilizer industries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Marine Industry – Corrosion Engineering Perspective

The **marine environment** is widely recognized in corrosion engineering as the most aggressive natural electrolyte known to mankind. It is a complex, multi-phase saline medium containing dissolved oxygen (~8 mg/L), chloride ions (~19,000 mg/L), sulfate ions, bromide ions, variable pH (typically 7.5–8.3), and a diverse population of aerobic and anaerobic microorganisms. From a KTU 2024 Scheme standpoint, marine corrosion is the electrochemical degradation of metallic structures (ship hulls, offshore platforms, pipelines, propellers, ballast tanks, and harbor installations) exposed to seawater, marine atmosphere, splash zones, and seabed sediments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Marine corrosion is NOT just "rusting in water." It is a synergistic combination of **electrochemical attack, mechanical erosion, biological fouling, and galvanic coupling** — making it a multidisciplinary problem.

**Conceptual Analogy – The Salty Soup:**
Imagine a steel ship hull as a chocolate bar dipped into a hot, salty, acidic soup. The salt (NaCl) acts as a perfect electrolyte that completes the electrical circuit between the anodic and cathodic regions on the metal. The dissolved oxygen is like a constant supply of fresh "fuel" being pumped to the cathode. Add sea creatures (barnacles, bacteria) that create tiny oxygen-starved crevices under their shells, and you have a corrosion nightmare. Every square centimeter of the hull is essentially a tiny battery continuously discharging itself.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Used in Marine Engineering:**
> - **Corrosion rate of mild steel in seawater:** ~0.1–0.5 mm/year (full immersion)
> - **Splash zone rate:** ~0.3–1.0 mm/year (most severe)
> - **Atmospheric marine zone:** ~0.05–0.2 mm/year
> - **Service life of unprotected mild steel in seawater:** ~5–10 years only.

### 1.2 Fertilizer Industry – Corrosion Engineering Perspective

The **fertilizer industry** is one of the most corrosion-intensive chemical process industries because of the simultaneous handling of strong mineral acids (H₂SO₄, H₃PO₄, HNO₃), strong bases (NH₃, KOH), high temperatures (up to 1100 °C in reformer tubes), high pressures (200–300 bar in ammonia synthesis), and abrasive solid particulates (phosphate rock, potash, urea dust).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Box:** Fertilizer corrosion refers to the degradation of process equipment (reactors, heat exchangers, pumps, piping, storage tanks, and granulators) caused by the combined action of acidic/alkaline reagents, elevated temperature, abrasion, and stress in fertilizer manufacturing plants producing nitrogenous, phosphatic, and potassic fertilizers.

**Conceptual Analogy – The Acid Test:**
Think of a fertilizer plant as a giant chemistry laboratory kitchen. The walls (reactor walls) are constantly being scrubbed with strong acids and alkalis. The pipes are like straws carrying corrosive lemon juice (H₃PO₄) and bleach (NH₃ solution). Just as a metal spoon left in lemon juice overnight becomes pitted and dissolved, the steel surfaces in a fertilizer plant suffer accelerated wastage unless protected by the right alloy and lining.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Major Fertilizer Categories Driving Different Corrosion Challenges:**
> 1. **Nitrogenous fertilizers** (Urea, Ammonium Nitrate, Ammonium Sulphate) – produced from NH₃ + CO₂ + HNO₃
> 2. **Phosphatic fertilizers** (SSP, TSP, DAP, MAP) – produced from H₂SO₄/H₃PO₄ + phosphate rock
> 3. **Potassic fertilizers** (MOP, SOP) – generally less corrosive but cause abrasion and chloride SCC.

---

## 2. Key Marine Corrosion Zones and Their Severity

Marine structures experience **four distinct corrosion zones**, each with its own mechanism and severity:

| Zone | Location | Mechanism | Severity |
|------|----------|-----------|----------|
| **Atmospheric zone** | Above high tide, exposed to air + salt spray | Wet-dry cycling, chloride deposition | Moderate |
| **Splash zone** | Between high and low tide, constantly wetted & dried | Wave impact, erosion, maximum aeration | **Most severe** |
| **Tidal zone** | Alternately wet and dry | Differential aeration cells | High |
| **Submerged zone** | Continuously below water | Uniform + pitting + biofouling + MIC | Moderate-High |
| **Mud zone** | Seafloor, beneath seabed | Anaerobic, sulfate-reducing bacteria (SRB) | Severe locally |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** Students often assume submerged zone is most corrosive. In reality, the **splash zone** is the most aggressive due to continuous wet-dry cycling, high oxygen availability, and mechanical wave impact causing **erosion–corrosion**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Forms of Corrosion in Marine Environments

#### 2.1.1 Uniform Corrosion
Occurs over the entire exposed surface due to even oxygen distribution. In seawater, mild steel loses ~0.1–0.3 mm/year uniformly. This is the most predictable form and is mitigated by coatings, cathodic protection, or corrosion allowance.

#### 2.1.2 Pitting Corrosion
Highly localized attack creating deep cavities. Stainless steels (especially 304 grade) are particularly susceptible in chloride-rich seawater. Pits act as stress concentrators and initiate cracking.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical pitting parameter:** **PREN (Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number)** governs stainless steel selection.
> $$\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \times \%\text{Mo} + 16 \times \%\text{N}$$
> For seawater service, **PREN ≥ 40** is recommended (e.g., super-duplex 2507, super-austenitic 254 SMO, or 6Mo grades).

#### 2.1.3 Crevice Corrosion
Develops under barnacles, gaskets, bolt heads, and beneath marine fouling deposits. Differential aeration cell forms: oxygen-starved crevice interior becomes the anode, surrounding aerated area becomes cathode.

#### 2.1.4 Galvanic Corrosion
Severe when dissimilar metals are coupled in seawater (which has high electrical conductivity, ~4 S/m). Common dangerous couples:
- Steel + Bronze propeller → severe hull wastage near propeller
- Aluminum hull + Steel fastener → rapid aluminum dissolution
- Copper alloy + Steel → accelerated steel attack

> [!NOTE]
> **Galvanic Series in Seawater (Most Active → Most Noble):**
> Mg → Zn → Al alloys → Mild Steel → Cast Iron → Stainless Steel (active) → Pb → Cu → Ni → Bronze → Brass → Monel → Stainless Steel (passive) → Ti → Graphite → Au

#### 2.1.5 Erosion–Corrosion and Cavitation
- **Erosion-corrosion:** Mechanical removal of protective film by high-velocity seawater + sand particles (e.g., pump impellers, ship hulls near rudders)
- **Cavitation:** Formation and violent collapse of vapor bubbles in regions of low pressure (propeller blades, pump impellers) causes pitting and surface roughening

#### 2.1.6 Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)
Chloride-induced SCC affects austenitic stainless steels (304, 316) above ~60 °C. Classic example: 304 SS in hot seawater fails by transgranular SCC.

#### 2.1.7 Microbiologically Influenced Corrosion (MIC)
Caused by sulfate-reducing bacteria (SRB, e.g., *Desulfovibrio*), which reduce SO₄²⁻ to S²⁻/H₂S, creating aggressive localized attack under deposits, in ballast tanks, and on submerged pipelines. Tubercle formation on steel surfaces by iron-oxidizing bacteria also drives under-deposit corrosion.

#### 2.1.8 Biofouling
Accumulation of marine organisms (barnacles, mussels, algae) creates differential aeration cells and localized anodes. Antifouling coatings (TBT, copper-based) historically used; now replaced with silicone and biomimetic foul-release coatings.

### 2.2 Marine Corrosion Prevention Strategies

#### A. Cathodic Protection (CP) – The Workhorse of Marine Corrosion Control
Two principal methods:

**i) Sacrificial Anode (Galvanic) Cathodic Protection:**
A more electro-negative metal (Zn, Al, or Mg alloy) is electrically connected to the structure. The anode dissolves preferentially, protecting the structure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical Anode Materials for Seawater:**
> - **Zinc anodes** (ASTM B418 Type I) – most common, used in offshore platforms
> - **Aluminum alloy anodes** (Al-Zn-In) – higher capacity, lower weight, preferred for offshore
> - **Magnesium anodes** – used in low-conductivity waters (freshwater, soil), NOT recommended for seawater

**ii) Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP):**
An external DC power source drives current from inert anodes (Ti coated with Pt/IrO₂/MMO) to the structure. Used for large structures: ship hulls (Impressed Current Cathodic Protection – ICCP hull systems), offshore platforms, long pipelines.

#### B. Protective Coatings
Three-layer marine coating system:
1. **Primer** – zinc-rich epoxy (galvanic protection)
2. **Intermediate coat** – epoxy with barrier pigments
3. **Topcoat** – polyurethane or fluoropolymer for UV and abrasion resistance

For ballast tanks: **solvent-free epoxy** (e.g., International 786) applied at 320 μm DFT.

#### C. Material Selection
**Marine-grade alloys and their applications:**

| Material | Application | Why Used |
|----------|-------------|----------|
| **Copper-nickel 90/10, 70/30** | Seawater piping, heat exchangers | Excellent biofouling resistance, good corrosion resistance |
| **Aluminum-bronze** | Propellers, pump impellers | High strength, cavitation resistance |
| **Monel (Ni-Cu 70/30)** | Pump shafts, valve trim | Resists HF and seawater |
| **Titanium Grade 2** | Submarine piping, heat exchangers, propeller shafts | Immune to seawater up to 300 °C |
| **6Mo super-austenitic (254 SMO, AL-6XN)** | Seawater handling systems | PREN > 43, resists pitting/crevice |
| **Super-duplex 2507** | Submarine hulls, pumps, valves | High strength + seawater resistance |
| **FRP / GRP** | Small boat hulls, pipework | Lightweight, corrosion-immune |
| **Cathodic-protection-aided carbon steel** | Jackets, hulls | Economics |

### 2.3 Corrosion in the Fertilizer Industry

The fertilizer manufacturing process is segmented into multiple plants, each presenting a unique corrosion environment:

#### 2.3.1 Sulfuric Acid Plant (Contact Process)
- **Corrosive species:** H₂SO₄ (98% hot), SO₂, SO₃, moisture
- **Temperature:** 400–1100 °C
- **Materials of construction:**
  - Cast iron for acid coolers (concentrated H₂SO₄ forms protective sulfate film)
  - Carbon steel for storage tanks of 78–100% H₂SO₄
  - **Stainless steel 316L** for 90–98% acid service
  - **Anodic protection** used on carbon steel storage tanks (potential held in passive region)

#### 2.3.2 Phosphoric Acid Plant (Wet Process)
- **Most corrosive plant in the entire fertilizer industry**
- **Corrosive species:** H₃PO₄ (28–54% P₂O₅), H₂SO₄ (used to attack phosphate rock), fluoride ions (HF, SiF₄), Cl⁻, SO₄²⁻, abrasive gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O) slurry
- **Temperature:** 70–95 °C
- **Severe corrosion forms:** erosion-corrosion, under-deposit corrosion, SCC, pitting
- **Materials of construction:**
  - **D2 (high-C, high-Cr) stainless steel** (ASTM A532) for agitators, slurry pumps
  - **Durimet 20, Carpenter 20, Alloy 20Cb-3** for evaporators, agitator shafts
  - **Solid 316L stainless** with rubber lining for attack tanks
  - **UHMW-PE** linings for slurry handling
  - **FRP** (vinyl ester resin) for storage tanks and ducting

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fluoride Attack:** Phosphate rock contains fluorapatite. During acidulation, HF is liberated, which attacks SiO₂-containing refractories and even stainless steels. **HF-resistant alloys (Monel 400, Alloy 20, or rubber-lined carbon steel)** are mandatory.

#### 2.3.3 Nitric Acid Plant
- **Corrosive species:** HNO₃ (50–70%), NO, NO₂
- **Materials:**
  - **Stainless Steel 304L** for acid coolers
  - **AISI 310 (25 Cr, 20 Ni)** for high-temperature sections
  - **Titanium** for high-purity, hot, aerated HNO₃ service
  - For >98% HNO₃: **anodic protection on stainless steel**

#### 2.3.4 Ammonia and Urea Plant
- **Corrosive species:** NH₃ (hot, gaseous & liquid), CO₂, ammonium carbamate (NH₂COONH₄), biuret
- **Temperature:** 180–220 °C in urea reactor
- **Pressure:** 140–200 bar
- **Critical issue:** **Ammonium carbamate is highly aggressive** — it causes severe corrosion of carbon steel and even 304L/316L.
- **Materials of construction:**
  - **AISI 316L UG (Urea Grade)** with low ferrite and controlled composition — industry standard
  - **2RE69, Sandvik 725LN (25-22-2)** — superaustenitic for carbamate service
  - **Titanium** for cooling condensers (excellent NH₃/NH₄NO₃ resistance)
  - **Incoloy 825** for high-pressure urea stripping
  - **Oxygen injection (0.5–1.0 vol% O₂ in CO₂ feed)** is the standard corrosion control technique to maintain passivity.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Oxygen Passivation Trick" in Urea Plants:** A small amount of oxygen is deliberately added to the CO₂ feed gas. This keeps the stainless steel surface in the passive region by continuously reforming the Cr₂O₃ film destroyed by carbamate attack. Without O₂, corrosion rates skyrocket from <0.1 mm/yr to >5 mm/yr.

#### 2.3.5 Granulation and Drying Section
- **Issues:** chloride-induced SCC of austenitic SS, abrasion of prills, ammonia attack on copper-bearing alloys (copper alloys are BANNED due to SCC in ammoniacal environment).
- **Materials:** carbon steel + rubber lining, 316L for critical parts.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol Meaning | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \times \%\text{Mo} + 16 \times \%\text{N}$ | Cr, Mo, N content in alloy | Stainless steel selection for seawater |
| 2 | $\text{CR (mpy)} = \frac{534 \times W}{D \times A \times T}$ | W=weight loss (mg), D=density, A=area (in²), T=time (hr) | Corrosion rate (mils per year) |
| 3 | $\text{CR (mm/yr)} = \frac{87.6 \times W}{D \times A \times T}$ | W=mg, D=g/cm³, A=cm², T=hr | Corrosion rate (mm/year) |
| 4 | $I = \frac{V}{R_L + R_M + R_C}$ | I=current, V=drive voltage, R_L=lead, R_M=metal, R_C=coating | ICCP current calculation |
| 5 | $W_{anode} = \frac{I \times T \times C}{1000}$ | I=current (A), T=time (yr), C=consumption rate (kg/A·yr) | Anode mass calculation (typically ~11 kg/A·yr for Al) |
| 6 | $E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode}$ | Cell potential | Galvanic corrosion driving force |
| 7 | $\Delta E \geq 0.25\text{ V}$ criterion | Difference in galvanic potentials | Compatibility check for coupled metals |
| 8 | $\text{LPR } R_p = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303 \, I_{corr} (\beta_a + \beta_c)}$ | $\beta$=Tafel slopes, $I_{corr}$=corrosion current | Linear Polarization Resistance for rate |
| 9 | $r_{B} = \frac{w_{B} / \rho_{B}}{w_{A} / \rho_{A}}$ | Mass loss / density ratio | Weight-loss method (metal B vs A) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Seawater Resistivity:** ~0.20–0.25 Ω·m (very conductive → current spreads easily → galvanic effects are large and widespread).
> **CP Design Current Density for Marine Structures:**
> - Initial: 100–200 mA/m²
> - Steady-state (with coating): 10–30 mA/m²
> - Bare steel in seawater: 80–110 mA/m²

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Offshore platform life extension** — CP + coatings extend service life from 10 to 40+ years.
- **Ship hull fuel saving** — clean, smooth, CP-protected hulls save 5–15% fuel.
- **Urea plant safety** — O₂ passivation + 316L UG prevents catastrophic carbamate corrosion (could otherwise explode due to wall thinning).
- **Phosphoric acid plant uptime** — proper alloy selection (D2, Alloy 20) reduces annual shutdown loss, which can exceed $1M/day for a 1000 TPD plant.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Problems

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Cathodic Protection Anode Mass Calculation

> **Problem:** A ship hull of wetted area **1200 m²** is to be protected by **aluminum sacrificial anodes** in seawater. The mean current density required is **120 mA/m²**. The anodes have a design utilization factor of **0.85** and an electrochemical capacity of **2700 A·h/kg**. The expected service life required is **5 years**. Calculate the total mass of anodes required.

**Step 1 — Calculate total cathodic protection current required**

$$I_{total} = i_{design} \times A$$

Substituting the values:

$$I_{total} = 120 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{A/m}^2 \times 1200\,\text{m}^2$$

$$I_{total} = 144\,\text{A}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate total charge (ampere-hours) needed for 5 years**

Number of hours in 5 years:

$$T = 5 \times 365 \times 24 = 43{,}800\,\text{h}$$

Total charge:

$$Q = I_{total} \times T = 144\,\text{A} \times 43{,}800\,\text{h} = 6{,}307{,}200\,\text{A·h}$$

**Step 3 — Account for anode utilization factor**

Effective usable charge per kg of anode:

$$Q_{usable} = C \times \eta$$

$$Q_{usable} = 2700 \times 0.85 = 2295\,\text{A·h/kg}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate total anode mass required**

$$M_{anode} = \frac{Q}{Q_{usable}}$$

$$M_{anode} = \frac{6{,}307{,}200}{2295}$$

$$M_{anode} \approx 2748\,\text{kg}$$

> **Answer:** Approximately **2750 kg** of aluminum alloy anodes are required to protect the ship hull for 5 years.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Corrosion Rate by Weight-Loss Method

> **Problem:** A carbon steel specimen (density 7.86 g/cm³) of area **30 cm²** was exposed to seawater for **240 hours**. The mass loss observed was **580 mg**. Calculate the corrosion rate in **mm/year** and **mpy**.

**Step 1 — Recall the formula**

$$\text{CR (mm/yr)} = \frac{87.6 \times W}{D \times A \times T}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values**

$$\text{CR} = \frac{87.6 \times 580}{7.86 \times 30 \times 240}$$

**Step 3 — Compute denominator**

$$7.86 \times 30 \times 240 = 56{,}592$$

**Step 4 — Compute numerator**

$$87.6 \times 580 = 50{,}808$$

**Step 5 — Divide**

$$\text{CR (mm/yr)} = \frac{50{,}808}{56{,}592} \approx 0.898\,\text{mm/yr}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to mpy using the standard conversion factor** (1 mm/yr = 39.37 mpy)

$$\text{CR (mpy)} = 0.898 \times 39.37 \approx 35.4\,\text{mpy}$$

> **Answer:** Corrosion rate ≈ **0.90 mm/yr** or **35.4 mpy** — this is **HIGH** and unsuitable for long-term marine service without protection.

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — PREN Calculation for Marine Stainless Steel Selection

> **Problem:** Compare two candidate stainless steels for a seawater pump impeller:
> - **Alloy A (316L):** 17% Cr, 2% Mo, 0.02% N
> - **Alloy B (254 SMO):** 20% Cr, 6% Mo, 0.20% N
>
> Using the **PREN criterion (PREN ≥ 40 for seawater)**, decide which is suitable.

**Step 1 — Calculate PREN for Alloy A**

$$\text{PREN}_A = 17 + 3.3 \times 2 + 16 \times 0.02$$

$$\text{PREN}_A = 17 + 6.6 + 0.32 = 23.92$$

**Step 2 — Calculate PREN for Alloy B**

$$\text{PREN}_B = 20 + 3.3 \times 6 + 16 \times 0.20$$

$$\text{PREN}_B = 20 + 19.8 + 3.2 = 43.0$$

**Step 3 — Apply the criterion**

- Alloy A: PREN = 23.92 → **Fails** the PREN ≥ 40 requirement.
- Alloy B: PREN = 43.0 → **Passes** the requirement.

> **Answer:** **Alloy B (254 SMO)** is the appropriate material. **Alloy A (316L)** would suffer severe pitting in stagnant or low-velocity seawater.

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Galvanic Corrosion Severity by Area Effect

> **Problem:** A copper pipe (cathode) of area **800 cm²** is connected to a steel flange (anode) of area **40 cm²** in seawater. If the galvanic current density is **2.5 mA/cm²** at the steel anode, calculate:
> 1. Total galvanic current
> 2. Mass of steel lost per day
> 3. Discuss why this is dangerous.

**Step 1 — Total galvanic current**

$$I = i \times A = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{A/cm}^2 \times 40\,\text{cm}^2 = 0.10\,\text{A}$$

**Step 2 — Mass loss using Faraday's Law**

Using Faraday's constant **F = 96,500 C/mol** and Fe atomic mass = 55.85 g/mol, Fe is divalent (n = 2):

$$m = \frac{I \times t \times M}{n \times F}$$

For **t = 1 day = 86,400 s:**

$$m = \frac{0.10 \times 86{,}400 \times 55.85}{2 \times 96{,}500}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate numerator and denominator**

$$m = \frac{482{,}544}{193{,}000} \approx 2.50\,\text{g/day}$$

**Step 4 — Discussion of area effect**

The cathode-to-anode area ratio is 800 : 40 = **20 : 1**. The small anode (steel flange) is forced to supply a current that is normally spread over a much larger anodic surface. This **"small-anode / large-cathode" geometry** intensifies the anodic current density, accelerating flange perforation.

> **Answer:** Steel flange loses **~2.5 g/day**. Due to the unfavorable area ratio, flange failure may occur within **months**. **Design rule:** Never couple a small steel component to a large copper/cathodic area; use insulating gaskets or isolation flanges.
